# Master GDD

> Vite + React + PixiJS · WebGL renderer · Real-Time ARPG · 50 players/room · 4-player dungeon parties.
> Priority: Clarity > Flexibility > Balance > Implementation.
> **Scope:** Systems only. Content (specific weapons, bosses, zones, quests) lives in JSON data files.
> For creative direction (visual, audio, narrative tone) → `creative-reference.md`. For technical architecture (stack, state, server) → `architecture.md`.
> Version 14.0.6 · Updated April 2026 · Design-ship changelog inline below; process/hygiene/sync history in `CHANGELOG.md`.
>
> **v14.0.6 Appendix A enum-registry cleanup (hygiene ship, no mechanics):** Appendix A is updated to reflect the v13 four-register alignment rework and the v14.0.5 five-ending + five-pole additions. Prior enum values are preserved in HISTORICAL RECORD blocks per Editing Protocol step 6. Changes: (1) `Alignment` (Radiant/Good/Neutral/Dark/Shadow) and `MoralPath` (Good/Neutral/Evil) superseded by `AlignmentRegister` (responsible/mischievous/cool/ruthless) — the v13 four-register model with independent per-register counters, no ranking, no aggregate label; (2) `EndingAlignment` extended from the pre-v13 three-axis (benevolent/neutral/evil) to the v14.0.5 five-value (hero/trickster/arbiter/sovereign/weaver) per §33.4; (3) `EarnedTitle` extended to include Trickster (v13 register title), Weaver (v14.0.5 fifth ending), Three-Faced and Complete Bro (v13 compound titles per §54); (4) `Pole` enum added explicitly with the five canonical values (light/dark/eclipse_warm/eclipse_reserved/convergence) per §57.12. Zero gameplay changes, zero formula changes, zero new invariants, zero new mechanics — this ship closes the Appendix A drift that was tracked in the v14.0.3 CHANGELOG "deferred to separate hygiene pass" bullet. Companion ships: creative-reference.md v5.42 → v5.43 (straggler parity pass) and architecture.md v2.28.2 → v2.29 (scheduled follow-up for the v14.0.3 and v14.0.5 data-model additions).
>
> **v14.0.5 thesis-alignment corrections (four targeted changes):** Four corrections to sections where the existing spec was working against the §0.4 unification thesis rather than expressing it. No scope change, no production-cost rationale, no feature removal for scope reasons. Each correction names a place where the doc's design was thesis-violating and replaces it with a thesis-aligned alternative. **(1) Fifth ending for balanced characters (§33.4, §53.4, §54.1, §57.7a).** The v13 tiebreaker for balanced registers (1/1/1/1 or 3+ tied) routed them to the Hero ending via the ordering `Responsible > Mischievous > Cool > Ruthless`. This encoded exactly the moral hierarchy §0.3 spent its length rejecting. Balanced characters are now given a fifth ending — **Weaver**, pole **Convergence** — matching the Convergence zone's existing role as "the geography where Dark and Light coexist." The Weaver's Naming sentence is canonical: *They held all four until the four remembered they had never been separate.* The four-register thesis is now fully expressed at the ending layer: four dominant-register endings plus one balanced ending, none ranked, the balanced case being the most thesis-aligned of the five. The Convergence pole completes the pole taxonomy (Light / Dark / Eclipse-warm / Eclipse-reserved / Convergence). **(2) Complete Eclipse visual reworked (§33.5).** Prior spec: "all four variants layered together, prismatic." This is synthesis, not unification — the four registers blend into a composite that subsumes them, violating §0.4's "permanent holding of opposites." Revised spec: the Complete Eclipse *cycles sequentially* through the four pure variants across its 5-second duration (1.25s per variant), each fully itself, held, released, replaced by the next fully itself. The balanced player's Eclipse is now the one that refuses to collapse the registers into a blend — the visual embodiment of holding four registers without resolving them. **(3) Journey Log prose self-rewriting removed (§27.4).** Prior spec: entries older than 200 in-game hours self-rewrite during off-peak server time into more reflective voice. The feature was beautiful in its example text but violated the Journey Log's own truth-contract — the Log's emotional premise is that the game remembers what actually happened to you; generated prose evolution replaces the player's memory of a moment with a machine's reflection on it, and makes factual claims about the player's inner state the game cannot know. Rereading an entry and having it gain new meaning as the player changes around it is what rereading *is*; auto-evolution does the rereading for the player. Cut entirely. All other Journey Log features retained — prose format at entry-creation, tonal variants, inspect-card excerpts, pin-as-highlight, milestone non-pruning. `display_text_evolved` field removed from the data model; `read evolved version (if available)` action removed from per-entry UI. **(4) Overwhelmed Fragment historical rationale trimmed (§57.HIST.8).** Prior spec: full preservation of the v14.0.1 Overwhelmed Fragment mechanic plus its phase-at-failure metadata, migration path, and rationale for deprecation. The deprecation is correct and preserved. The preserved full rationale was an attractive nuisance — the Overwhelmed concept is the specific kind of thesis-violating "consolation kindness" that reads well in isolation and is one changelog rotation from re-emergence every time a designer re-reads the historical record. Trimmed to a four-sentence acknowledgment plus the migration path (still load-bearing for pre-v14.0.3 character save data); the extended design-rationale prose that argued the deprecation's logic moved to a separate design-archive reference. §57.1 reference updated. No mechanical change; the deprecation is unchanged, only the preserved record is trimmed. **What this ship is not.** This ship makes no scope reductions, no production-cost-motivated cuts, no feature trims for simplification. The 20-tier material ladder is preserved intact (the flatter XP curve means each tier is lived in long enough to preserve crafter attribution emotional weight against obsolescence; the ladder is thesis-aligned when read alongside the XP progression). Life skills, Guardian zones, four-register voice variants, 90 collisions, the Convergence perceptual layer, Transcendent-rank crafter mythology, and every other "big-scope" system is preserved unchanged — these are thesis-serving, not scope-bloat. This ship corrects four specific thesis-violations and otherwise leaves v14.0.4 untouched. Creative-reference.md §0.4 / §8.15a / Eclipse variants subsection expected to follow in a v5.42 parity ship (fifth ending creative direction, Complete Eclipse sequential-cycle direction, and Weaver ending sequence authoring). Architecture changes: none required; `endingAlignment` enum gains one value, Fragment pole enum gains one value, both additive.
>
> **v14.0.4 thesis vocabulary reframe (no mechanical changes):** §0.4 and §57.1 are revised to name **collision as the game's core verb** and to reframe the unification thesis as collision grammar at the self-scale. No mechanical spec changes, no execution-quality gate changes, no asset or authoring-scope changes from v14.0.3. The ritual's shape is unchanged; only the vocabulary used to describe it. Rationale: v14.0.3 correctly stated the unification thesis but described §57 in unification-vocabulary ("the ritual is the unification thesis made physical") when the ritual's native vocabulary is collision — two elements meeting, a third state emerging, which is the grammar the game has taught for a hundred hours across combat, Eclipse, alignment, and the Convergence. Collision is the engine that produces unification's pattern; unification is what collision looks like when the holding is permanent. The v14.0.4 reframe names the engine explicitly and locates §57 within it: the ending is the first collision in the game whose both elements are the player (the pole the Bro has become, and the seed that pole grew from), with the twenty-second hold as the collision window stretched to ritual duration, the Fragment as the resulting third state, and the sky flash as the world's atmospheric register of the event. This reframe answers design questions v14.0.3 could not (why collision-shaped ritual grammar, why this specific access condition, why early-game Bros cannot attempt it) and preserves v14.0.3's mechanical and creative-direction discipline unchanged. §0.4 gains one new paragraph naming collision as the core verb and the ending as the first self-scale collision; §57.1 is rewritten to lead with collision framing before reaching for unification vocabulary, with the three original paragraphs preserved. Walkout reframed as *collision deferred* rather than *refusal of commitment* — the two poles are present, the window opened, it closed before the third state emerged; the grip in inventory is the unresolved collision in portable form. Section 57.1's "five elements carry the ritual" line retains its five elements with updated parenthetical roles (grip = seed the pole grew from; eight lights = the pole itself, as accumulated collision signature; hold = collision window sustained; Fragment construction = third state made permanent; sky flash = world's collision register). Creative-reference.md §0.4 and §8.15 opening paragraph expected to follow in a v5.42 parity ship. No architecture changes.
>
> **v14.0.3 thesis and ending revision:** Elevates the **unification thesis** from an implicit pattern across systems to an explicit canonical principle (§0.4). The game's thesis is unification through the *permanent holding of opposites* — not synthesis, not resolution, but the refusal to release either pole. The thesis was always expressed in combat collisions (two elements held until a third state emerges), Eclipse's dual destroy-and-heal action, the four-register alignment system's non-hierarchy, the Convergence's coexistent Dark/Light geography, and the ritual's two-object hold — but prior docs presented these as independent design decisions rather than expressions of one coherent claim. §0.4 now states the thesis explicitly with a test for future design work (*does this hold opposites or resolve them?*). §57 is revised to v14.0.3 in alignment: the three-phase painful recombination is collapsed to a **universal 20-second chest-cradle hold** of two objects (rusted first-weapon grip in off-hand against chest, current weapon in dominant hand), preceded by the Rise (eight elements in canonical elemental-wheel order, intensity-scaled 30-100% by lifetime usage share) and followed by a 4-second Fragment construction sequence in which both objects dissolve together and reassemble as grip-at-floor with crystalline form growing upward in the player's pole color. Three whimsy anchors are added explicitly per §0.3 tonal rule: the Ferryman's register-matched dry threshold line (§57.2a NEW), the grip's gentle descent animation (§57.5), and explicit naming of Mayor Bro's return line and the Hall's pristine-grip detail as architectural whimsy. The Unfinished Fragment collapses into the grip itself — the grip IS the walkout record, IS the inventory presence, IS the resume token. v14.0.1 Overwhelmed Fragment is formally deprecated (§57.HIST.8). Four execution-quality gates named at §57.13 (chest-cradle animation, voice line content, Fragment construction VFX, Naming timing). Sky flash unchanged but explicitly named as unification thesis at social layer. Architecture changes deferred to follow-up spec pass.
>
> **v14.0.2 ending revision (hygiene ship):** §57 gains structural revisions following design review. The 30-second gesture hold is restructured as a three-phase painful recombination (Phase A: The Eight, Phase B: The Band, Phase C: The Closing, + Explosion), with active input-layer physics resistance during Phases B and C. The rising sequence teaches the ritual's own grammar — eight inner elements rising individually, then Darkness, then Light, with canonical audio signatures. **Input loss during Phases B or C resolves to the walkout path (Unfinished Fragment, §57.2c), not a distinct outcome** — the ritual has two honest outcomes, not three, because a separate "overwhelmed" artifact would create a consolation-participation Fragment and undermine walkout's genuine-choice framing. The chamber's authorship lore (built by past Guardians across server history) is canonicalized at §57.13, with the Keeper of the Hall expanded as a former Guardian with per-server pole variation. Item preservation routes to the farm's home chest (§19.2 canonical v13 home storage) rather than the town Bank, making the walk-home-to-farm the new life's opening beat. The four-register-to-cosmological-pole mapping (Responsible→Light, Ruthless→Dark, Mischievous→Eclipse-warm, Cool→Eclipse-reserved) is confirmed as non-hierarchical resonance. One `crossed_persistent` achievement added for walkout-then-completion paths. See §57.3 for the three-phase structure and §57.13 for chamber authorship lore; all v14.0 initial content preserved where structurally compatible.
>
> **v14.0 ending rework:** §57 is fully rewritten. The canonical Primordial encounter — through all prior iterations (pre-v13 cosmic-duality, v12.44 Stability Fragments, v13 unchosen-self, v13 acknowledgment dialogue, v13 post-completion witnessing) — is superseded by the **Last Recombination** ritual. The ritual is a sacrificial cosmological ceremony in which the player unifies with Darkness, Light, or Eclipse, dies in the white flash of recombination, and wakes on the opening beach — stripped to tutorial state, carrying the **Crossed property** (permanent ambient world-alteration in their presence) and producing a permanent **Soul Fragment** visible to every player forever. The ending is designed for continuous-play multiplayer: the player does not leave, the world is changed. Changes marked `(v14.0 REPLACEMENT)`, `(v14.0 NEW)`, or `(v14.0 REVISED)`. Full v14.0 ship reasoning in the v14.0 inline changelog entry; v13 content preserved as historical record per §57 subsections §57.HIST.1 through §57.HIST.7 and Editing Protocol step 6.
>
> **v13 integration:** Three cross-cutting design principles (tonal range, progressive UI revelation, four-register alignment) plus ~95 scoped changes across systems. See §0 "v13 Cross-Cutting Principles" below and v13 tags on affected sections. Changes marked `(v13 ADDITIVE)`, `(v13 REPLACEMENT)`, `(v13 REVISED)`, or `(v13 DEFINE)`.

---

**Status tag meanings:**

- **[shipped]** — system is in the codebase and playable; editing the GDD here should be kept in sync with code.
- **[partial]** — some implementation exists; specific sub-features may be spec-only. Check before assuming.
- **[spec-only]** — described in the GDD, not yet implemented. Edits here are design work, not code-facing.
- **[unknown]** — implementation status not confidently determinable from the docs alone. Verify against the codebase before treating as shipped.
- **—** — navigation/meta row, not a game system.

**Important caveat on these tags.** The tags were seeded from the Development Phases tiering, architecture.md file metrics, and explicit "planned"/"implemented"/"stub" signals in the docs. They are a *starting point*, not an authoritative inventory. The tags should be reviewed and corrected by someone with current knowledge of the codebase. An AI assistant editing a section tagged [unknown] should treat that as a cue to verify against the code (or ask) rather than assume either direction. When a feature ships, its row should be updated and a changelog entry should note the status change.

### Authority Hierarchy

When two parts conflict, lower number wins:

1. **Canonical pseudocode** (e.g. §10.8 multi-actor collision)
2. **Canonical formulas** (per the Reference Map above and Appendix B)
3. **Data model tables** (e.g. §6.7 monster forms, §6.8 monster sizes)
4. **Tuning tables** (§20 and per-system tables)
5. **Prose descriptions**
6. **Reference builds** (§24 — validation, not definitions)
7. **DESIGN INTENT blocks** — rationale only, zero implementation weight

### Editing Protocol (CANONICAL)

This document is large (~5,800 lines) and dense with cross-system references. To prevent edits from drifting from the doc's existing state, **any AI assistant editing this file must follow the Editing Protocol below before making any change**. This is a process invariant, not a runtime invariant — it governs how the doc is modified, not how the game behaves. It exists because the doc has surfaced multiple instances of state-drift across the v12.4–v12.9 sequence, and the cheapest defense is a discipline of "read before edit."

**The Editing Protocol (mandatory):**

1. **Read the §-Reference Map first.** Before any edit, the assistant must read the §-Reference Map (above, lines 14–91) and identify which sections are relevant to the change. The Reference Map is the authoritative index of where each topic lives, and it is the single most important navigation aid in this document. Skipping the Reference Map produces edits that miss the canonical location of the system being edited and creates duplicate or contradictory content.

2. **Read the relevant Reference Map rows in full.** For each row identified as relevant, the assistant must `view` the section the row points to, in full, before editing. Partial reads (grep, line-range views of just the immediate edit point) are not sufficient — the section's surrounding context, cross-references, and prior version notes are part of what determines whether an edit is consistent with the doc's existing state.

3. **Read the most recent changelog entries.** The changelog at the bottom of the document tells the story of how each section reached its current state. Before editing a section that has been changed in any of the last three versions, the assistant must read the relevant changelog paragraphs to understand the *reasons* the section looks the way it does. Many of the doc's invariants exist because earlier versions had problems the changelog documents — editing without that context risks recreating problems the doc has already solved.

4. **Verify file state before assuming.** The doc evolves between editing sessions. The assistant must `md5sum` or `wc -l` the working file at the start of any editing turn and compare against expected state. If the file has changed since the last known checkpoint, the assistant must read whatever has changed before making new edits. **State drift between turns is the most common source of inconsistent edits**, and it is preventable through verification.

5. **Use the Authority Hierarchy when sections conflict.** If two sections appear to disagree (e.g., a tuning value in §4.6d doesn't match a formula in §4.4), the Authority Hierarchy above determines which wins. The assistant must not silently reconcile conflicts by changing the lower-authority section to match the higher-authority section without flagging the discrepancy explicitly in the response.

6. **Preserve historical record on resolved issues.** When a previously-failing validator pass or audit gate is fixed, the failure record stays in the doc as part of the historical context (see §23.6a.1 for an example — the v12.8 FAIL results are preserved alongside the v12.9 PASS results). The assistant must not delete or rewrite past version notes when shipping fixes; the next version's documentation goes alongside the prior version's, not on top of it.

7. **Surface uncertainty rather than hide it.** When an edit involves estimates, modeling assumptions, or unmeasured rates, the assistant must explicitly label them as such (with confidence levels where possible) rather than presenting them as facts. This is the operational expression of the v12.8/v12.9 honesty principle: "the validator's job is to find problems, not to solve them," and the same principle applies to the editing assistant — the doc's job is to surface uncertainty, not to project false certainty.

**Why this protocol exists.** Across the v12.4–v12.9 sequence, the doc has repeatedly surfaced cases where changes were made without full context: the v12.6 changelog flagged a §6.5 rebalance that wasn't actually needed (caught in v12.8), the v12.5 changelog referenced INV-29 and INV-30 without actually adding them to §22 (caught in v12.6), the v12.8 v6.1 validator pass used per-hour rates that should have been amortized (caught in v12.9), and several minor cross-section inconsistencies have been repaired turn-by-turn. **Every one of these would have been caught at edit time if the editing assistant had read the relevant sections in full before making the change.** The Editing Protocol formalizes the discipline that catches these cases at the source.

**This protocol is binding for AI assistants editing this file**, including future sessions of the same assistant. It is not a guideline. Any edit that does not follow steps 1–7 above is a protocol violation and should be flagged in the response if it occurred.

`[DESIGN INTENT: This is a process invariant rather than a runtime invariant, but it is no less important than the runtime invariants in §22. The doc is large enough that an editing assistant who skips the read-first discipline will produce inconsistent edits, and inconsistent edits accumulate into doc-quality drift. The v12.8 audit pass found that v12.5 was always failing its own §6.5 audit gates and nobody noticed for two versions — that is the canonical example of what happens when edits are made without the read-first discipline. The Editing Protocol is the operational defense against that failure mode. Placing it near the top of the doc (after the §-Reference Map and Authority Hierarchy, before Development Phases) is intentional: an assistant opening this file for the first time will encounter the Reference Map first (showing them where things live), the Authority Hierarchy second (showing them which version wins when conflicts arise), and this Editing Protocol third (showing them how to read before editing). The three sections together form the doc's editing constitution.]`

### Versioning Convention (CANONICAL)

Design docs use **unversioned stable filenames**. The GDD is `gdd.md`, the Creative Reference is `creative-reference.md`, the architecture doc is `architecture.md`. These filenames never change. Version information lives in three places, in this priority order:

1. **The `Document version:` line** inside each file's bottom metadata block. This is the authoritative current version.
2. **Git tags** on releases — one tag per doc per release (e.g., `gdd-v12.20`, `creative-ref-v5.6.4`, `arch-v2.4`). Git history is the long-term audit trail; tags let you `git show <tag>:<filename>` to retrieve any historical version.
3. **The changelog block** at the bottom of each file. Every ship appends one entry, preserving all prior entries verbatim per Editing Protocol step 6.

**Cross-references between docs use bare filenames.** Inside the GDD, `architecture.md` and `creative-reference.md` are referenced by stable name without version suffixes. A version bump in `architecture.md` does not invalidate any cross-reference in the GDD because the GDD's cross-references don't encode a version.

**Companion metadata is informational, not enforced.** Each doc's bottom metadata block may include a `Companion to:` line recording which versions of the other docs were current at ship time. This is useful historical metadata ("when v12.20 shipped, the companion docs were at these versions") but it is no longer load-bearing. Under the earlier convention (filenames with version suffixes, strict cross-doc lockstep), a version bump in any doc required coordinated bumps in every referring doc, which created a cascade where one bump triggered three. Under the current convention, one bump affects one doc; the others don't need to update just because a companion's version number changed.

**To retrieve a historical version of any doc:**
```
git log <filename>                              # see history
git tag --list                                  # see available release tags
git show <tag>:<filename>                       # retrieve the file at that tag
git diff <tag-old> <tag-new> -- <filename>      # compare versions
```

**The `tools/` directory enforces this convention mechanically.** `tools/check_cross_doc_refs.py` verifies every cross-document filename reference resolves to a real file in the project root, which is now the primary cross-doc consistency check. The older "version lockstep" check was retired when the convention changed. See `tools/README.md` for the full audit suite and what each script catches.

`[DESIGN INTENT: The earlier versioned-filename convention (gdd-v12_15_0.md, etc.) served two purposes — preserve historical snapshots as distinct files, and make the current version visible in directory listings. Both purposes can be served more cleanly by git tags + the in-file Document version line. The convention change happened in the GDD v12.20 ship (see the v12.20 changelog for full reasoning, including the cascade problem that motivated the switch) and is formalized here so future edits don't regress to the earlier convention.]`

### Development Phases

Three tiers. Lower tier ships first. Systems within a tier are unordered — build in whatever sequence produces the tightest feedback loop.

#### Phase 1 — MVP (Playable Solo + Multiplayer Core)

The game must be fun for one player in one room before anything else matters.

| System | §Section | Gate Reason |
|--------|:--------:|-------------|
| Two-tier stat system | §1–§2 | Everything derives from stats. Build first. |
| Resource framework (HP/stamina/mana) | §3 | Can't take or deal damage without pools. |
| Gear system (5 slots, 4 weapon types, tiers) | §4 | Gear is identity. Drops are the reward loop. |
| Actions, timing, state machine, death | §5.1–§5.6 | Core input→output. The game doesn't exist without this. |
| Monster scaling and archetypes (7 types) | §6 | Need something to fight. Zone-leveled, tri-phase. |
| Damage pipeline (interceptor chain) | §8 | How numbers become HP loss. Non-negotiable. |
| Status effects and ICD | §9 | Statuses are the setup half of every collision. |
| Elemental collisions (90 pairs) | §10 | The skill ceiling. The reason the combat system exists. |
| Collision mana restore | §3.5 | Without this, mana runs out and collisions stop. Sustain loop. |
| World design (town + 3 spokes, depths 1–4) | §14 | Enough zones to teach the element loop. Ship 3 of 8 spokes. |
| Mobile controls (dual joystick + swipe) | §15.1–§15.4 | Primary platform. |
| Networking (20Hz tick, WebSocket, room broadcast) | §16.1–§16.9 | 50-player rooms need authoritative server from day one. |
| Loot and drop rules | §4.11 | Players need gear progression to continue. |
| Death and respawn | §5.5 | Death must have stakes. Scatter + recovery window. |
| XP and leveling (to cap 100) | §6.2 | Progression. |
| Town (marketplace vendor, bank, basic NPCs) | §14.3 | Social hub + gold sink baseline. |
| Basic economy (kill gold, vendor, crafting costs) | §23.1–§23.3 | Gold must flow in and out. |
| Blacksmithing + woodworking (weapon crafting) | §18 | Gear loop needs crafting, not just drops. |
| Learning curve tiers 0–1 | §12 | Move → attack → first element → first collision. Guided. |

**Phase 1 exit criteria:** A player can create a character, fight monsters in 3 elemental zones through 4 depth tiers (levels 1–40), trigger collisions, craft weapons, die and recover items, level up, and play alongside 49 other players in the same room. Combat feels good. The collision loop sustains. Kill times hit the §6.5 audit gates.

#### Phase 2 — Core Complete (Full Progression + Social + Identity)

The game has a beginning, middle, and end. Players have reasons to stay.

| System | §Section | Gate Reason |
|--------|:--------:|-------------|
| Resonance window | §5.7 | Timing reward layer. Raises skill ceiling after collision basics land. |
| Contextual dodge (lunge + retreat shot) | §5.8 | Engagement verbs. Zero new inputs, high feel improvement. |
| Combo chain | §5.9 | Makes auto-attacks inhabited. Now/Wide/Next vocabulary. |
| Remaining 5 elemental spokes (depths 1–8) | §14 | Full 8-element world. 64 standard zones. |
| Darkness + Light zones (depths 9–10) | §14.1 | Endgame elements. Eclipse unlocked. |
| Monster forms (Imp/Skeleton/Eye evolution) | §6.7 | Visual progression across depths. |
| Monster sizes (16×16 → 176×176, dramatic jump at depth 8, v12.40) | §6.8 | Monsters grow smoothly, endgame-entry is the signature visual transition. |
| Dungeon bosses | §6.9 | Depth-gating content. 4-player instanced. |
| Quest-gated unlock system (25 base quests) | §25 | Every quest unlocks a real capability. Guided progression. |
| Moral branching (8 capstone quests) | §25.1 | Alignment system needs decision points. |
| Narrative + alignment system | §33 | Echo Stones, Zone Keepers, alignment score. The game's soul. |
| Desktop controls | §15.5, §34 | Second platform. Keyboard+mouse. |
| PvP and reputation (duels + threats) | §19 | Player interaction beyond co-op. |
| Lawless Land + Ferryman | §26.4 | Full-loot PvP zone. High-stakes endgame. |
| Multiplayer contribution XP | §7 | Party play must feel fair. |
| Threat and aggro | §11 | Tank role needs aggro. Party play needs roles. |
| Anti-cheat (behavior detection + velocity limits) | §17 | Must ship before economy matures. |
| Remaining life skills (fishing, farming, cooking, etc.) | §18 | Full crafting loop. 10 skills. |
| Enchanting + gem cutting | §18 | Element customization on gear. Build diversity. |
| Pets (capture + loot vacuum) | §18.1 | Collection loop. Loot QoL. |
| Clan system | §29 | Social identity groups. |
| Social systems (friend/block/mute/inspect) | §30 | Multiplayer hygiene. |
| Personal farm + housing | §31 | Home base. Well Rested buff. Cooking station. |
| Server marketplace (order book) | §39 | Cross-room trading. Economy needs liquidity. |
| Resistance and immunity | §13 | Monster diversity at higher depths. |
| Zone-specific mechanics (frost/tidal/hollows) | §26 | Mario Principle. Zones must feel different. |
| Encyclopedia (bestiary/codex/materials/zones) | §35 | Discovery tracking. Completionist hook. |
| Journey Log | §27.4 | Narrative memory. Re-onboarding tool. |
| Comprehensive stats + achievement points | §27 | Player identity in numbers. |
| Tuning tables (all §20 subsections) | §20 | Balance pass requires all knobs exposed. |
| Cross-system invariants | §22 | CI checks prevent regression. |
| Reconciliation + PvP lag compensation | §16.4, §16.12 | Combat must feel fair at scale. |
| Cosmetic monetization (trails, auras, names) | §32 | Revenue. Ships before first anniversary. |

**Phase 2 exit criteria:** A player can progress from level 1 to 100 across all 66 zones, make moral choices that shape their identity, trigger all 90 collision pairs, complete the Ferryman quest, reach Ascendant title, trade on the marketplace, join a clan, decorate a farm, and feel like the game has a complete arc. Resonance, lunge, retreat shot, and combo chain are integrated. The economy is stable. Revenue flows.

#### Phase 3 — Endgame + Expansion (Post-Launch Content)

Long-term retention. Prestige. Community. These systems are valuable but the game ships and sustains without them.

| System | §Section | Priority Notes |
|--------|:--------:|----------------|
| Guardian zone system (8 zones) | §55 | First post-launch content drop. Apex bosses. |
| The Convergence | §56 | Alignment-driven endgame. Requires Guardian completion. |
| The Last Recombination ritual + Hall of Fragments | §57 | Narrative capstone. Solo ritual with continuous-play integrity. Ships after Convergence. |
| Three endings + title progression | §53–§54 | Ending sequence. Requires Guardian + alignment. |
| Level uncap (infinite progression) | §38 | Post-100 prestige. Leaderboard fuel. |
| Gladiator arena | §43 | Competitive PvP. Requires stable combat balance. |
| Spectator betting | §44 | Social layer on arena. Ships with arena. |
| Clan wars | §42 | Guild vs guild PvP. Requires clan maturity. |
| Leaderboards | §37 | Prestige tracking. Ships with level uncap. |
| Life skill guilds (10 guilds, 9 ranks) | §45 | Deep life skill engagement. 80 guild quests. |
| Dungeon Workshop (custom creator) | §36 | UGC. Requires stable dungeon system. |
| Expressive pet evolution (v12.47, visual trait inheritance, pure cosmetic) | §40 | Personal-identity collection endgame. Non-combat. |
| Gift-economy furniture (v12.46, crafter-attributed house decoration, no stat buffs) | §41 | Housing as social map. Woodworking social-recognition endgame. |
| Farm minigames (v12.45, fishing/cooking/matching hangout activities) | §47 | Social hangout material. Farm-local. |
| Cosmetic masks | §48 | Gold sink. Social expression. |
| Stat-based visuals | §49 | Build identity at a glance. Visual polish. |
| Contemplative underwater diving (v12.50, underwater Echo Stones, Tidal Caves narrative layer) | §46 | Zone memory legibility. Primary reward is lore, not treasure. |
| Anniversary items | §50 | Time-gated cosmetics. |
| Community feedback system | §51 | Structured player input. |
| New achievements (24) | §52 | Ships alongside their parent systems. |
| Gambling system | §28 | Gold sink. Entertainment. |

**Phase 3 cadence:** Guardian zones ship as the first major update (target: 1 month post-launch). Convergence and the Last Recombination ritual (§57) follow (months 2–3). Arena, clan wars, and workshop ship quarterly. Life skill guilds, minigames, and cosmetic systems fill gaps between major drops. Each Phase 3 system ships when its prerequisites are stable, not on a fixed schedule.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Phase 1 is ~60% of the engineering effort but 100% of the "is this game fun?" question. Phase 2 completes the game. Phase 3 extends it. If Phase 1 doesn't feel good, nothing else matters. If Phase 2 ships incomplete, the game still works — players just hit the ceiling sooner. Phase 3 is gravy. Ship Phase 1 tight, ship Phase 2 complete, ship Phase 3 when it's ready.]`

### System Dependency Graph

**Phase 1 Critical Path** (longest sequential chain — determines minimum Phase 1 duration):

```
Stat System (§1-2)
  → Resource Pools (§3)
    → Damage Pipeline (§8)
      → Status Effects (§9)
        → Elemental Collisions (§10)
          → Collision Mana Restore (§3.5)
            → [CORE COMBAT LOOP COMPLETE]
```

**Phase 1 Parallel Tracks** (can be built simultaneously with the critical path):

```
Track A: Gear System (§4) → Loot Rules (§4.11)
         [Depends on: Damage Pipeline for weapon damage formula]

Track B: Actions / State Machine (§5.1-5.6)
         [Depends on: Resource Pools for stamina/mana costs]

Track C: Monster Scaling (§6) → Kill-Time Validation (§6.5)
         [Depends on: Damage Pipeline for TTK calculation]

Track D: Networking (§16) → Mobile Controls (§15)
         [Independent — build from day one alongside everything else]

Track E: World Design (§14) → Town (§14.3)
         [Depends on: Monster Scaling for zone-level assignment]

Track F: Basic Economy (§23.1-§23.3) → Blacksmithing + Woodworking (§18)
         [Depends on: Gear System for crafting output, Loot Rules for drop integration]

Track G: Learning Curve Tiers 0-1 (§12)
         [Depends on: Core Combat Loop + Controls]
```

**Phase 2 Non-Obvious Dependencies** (cross-section chains that aren't visible from the §Reference Map):

```
Marketplace (§39) ──REQUIRES──→ Anti-Cheat (§17)
  You cannot ship a player-to-player economy without economic velocity checks.

Enchanting (§18) → Gem Cutting (§18) → Mining (§18) → Zone Access → Dungeon Completion (§18.4)
  5-step chain for a single crafting system. Enchanting cannot ship before the dungeon system.

PvP Reputation (§19) → Social Block System (§30.2) → Lawless Zone (§26.4)
  Block behavior changes in lawless zones. The block system needs the lawless zone flag.

Ferryman Quest (§33.6) → All 8 NPC Chains (§25) → Moral Branching (§25.1) → Alignment System (§33)
  The narrative capstone requires the full quest and alignment infrastructure.

Convergence (§56) → Guardian Zones (§55) → Level 100 Clear → All 66 Standard Zones (§14)
  Post-launch content depends on the entire Phase 2 world being complete.
```

**Phase 3 Dependencies** (each system lists its prerequisite):

```
Guardian Zones (§55)      ← All 8 standard spokes complete
Convergence (§56)         ← Guardian Zones
Last Recombination Ritual (§57)  ← Convergence + alignment (v14.0 replaces Primordial encounter)
Level Uncap (§38)         ← XP system (§6.2)
Leaderboards (§37)        ← Level Uncap + Durable Object
Arena (§43)               ← PvP balance (§19) + Anti-cheat (§17)
Spectator Betting (§44)   ← Arena (§43)
Clan Wars (§42)           ← Clan system (§29) + PvP (§19)
Dungeon Workshop (§36)    ← Dungeon system (§18.4) + Farm (§31)
Life Skill Guilds (§45)   ← All 10 life skills (§18) + Level Uncap for Rank 8
Pet Evolution (§40)       ← Pet capture (§18.1) + Gem cutting (§18)
Furniture (§41)           ← Woodworking (§18) + Farm (§31)
Minigames (§47)           ← Farm (§31)
Masks (§48)               ← Character rendering + Gold economy
Diving (§46)              ← Tidal Caves zone (§14) + Swimming (§26.2)
```

### Speculative Value Notation

Throughout this document, tuning values fall into three categories:

**Validated values** have been checked against at least one invariant (§22), audit gate (§6.5), or numerical model (e.g., the economy validation script for §23). They appear without any suffix: `DODGE_IFRAMES_BASE(0.25s)`, `POWER_DMG_COEFF(0.8)`, the §23.6 sink table.

**Edge-case values** are validated and pass core checks but sit at the edge of acceptable ranges. They appear with a `†` suffix and are noted in the relevant section's validation notes (e.g., §23.6a). These values function correctly but should be revisited during playtesting for refinement.

**Placeholder values** are reasonable estimates that have NOT been validated against any model or invariant. They appear with a `*` suffix: `DIVE_TREASURE_CHANCE(0.002*)`. Placeholder values should be treated as starting points for playtesting, not as design commitments. Phase 3 stub systems contain exclusively placeholder values.

---

## §0. v13 Cross-Cutting Design Principles (v13 CANONICAL)

Three principles apply across every system in this document. Where any individual section conflicts with these principles, the principles take precedence.

### §0.1 Tonal Rule — Every Somber Line Has a Sibling That's Funny

Brotown's native voice is epic *and* absurd. The Unlikely Hero frame (§33.3, §33.7) works because it's self-aware about its own unlikeliness. Content authoring across every system must include both registers. For every somber line, write a sibling funny one. For every grave NPC reaction, write a dry or warm or wry alternative. Humor is part of Brotown's thesis, not a separate tonal department.

Specific registers per alignment register (see §0.3 four-register system below) carry distinct humor flavors:

- **Responsible** voice: earnest-epic with self-deprecating edge ("I probably shouldn't have done that, but I did")
- **Mischievous** voice: warm wit, affectionate trouble, quick asides
- **Cool** voice: dry observation, deadpan delivery, understated
- **Ruthless** voice: dark comedy, sharp edges, uncomfortable truths

Every dialogue system, flavor text surface, Journey Log entry, Echo Stone, NPC line, and system acknowledgment carries both grave and funny variants where the situation allows. The grave register has its place (the ritual Ferryman framing per §57.2 and §57.3, Ferryman crossing per §33.6, Soul Fragment sentence per §57.5). Most other surfaces live in both registers simultaneously.

**Content authoring guidance.** When writing any new text content (NPC lines, journal entries, flavor text, tooltip descriptions, system messages), author at least two variants per line — one grave/observational and one playful/wry. The game's selection system picks variants based on context (player's dominant alignment register, recent events, random selection for variety). This is a standing content rule; all content pipelines per §21.4 include tonal-variant authoring as a default requirement.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Brotown's charm comes from holding epic and absurd at once. Uniform melancholy is a misreading of the game's voice. Humor is the universal language that brings players together; surfacing it everywhere makes the game feel alive in ways pure gravity never can. The four alignment-register voices ensure humor expresses character rather than floating as generic jokes — a Ruthless Bro's dark comedy is a different register from a Mischievous Bro's warm wit, and the game distinguishes them.]`

### §0.2 Progressive UI Revelation

UI elements appear as they become relevant to the player's experience. A new Bro washing up does not see quest log icons, clan interfaces, arena tabs, marketplace advanced filters, guild progression UI, alignment display, or title showcase — none of which matter yet. Each UI element unlocks at a specific qualifying moment.

**UI revelation schedule (CANONICAL):**

| UI Element | Appears When |
|------------|--------------|
| Quest Log icon | First quest is offered |
| Alignment display (§33.3) | After first capstone choice (first alignment-registered action) |
| Clan tab (§29) | When Clan Hall is first visited (as guest) or upon creating a clan |
| Marketplace basic (§39) | Level 5 |
| Marketplace advanced filters (§39) | After first 5 marketplace transactions |
| Title showcase (§54.1) | When second title is earned (first title auto-displays) |
| Guild progression UI (§45) | When first life skill enters Apprentice rank |
| Arena tab (§43) | When Training Mode is first visited |
| Diving tab (§46) | When first dive location is discovered |
| Inspect card — deeper fields (§30.4) | As viewer earns qualifying experiences per field-specific unlock rules |
| Journey Log icon (§27.4) | At character creation; initial entry: "You washed up" |
| Encyclopedia Bestiary tab (§35.1) | Already discovery-based — CANONICAL preserved |
| Encyclopedia Zones tab (§35.4) | Already discovery-based — CANONICAL preserved |
| Codex (§35.2) | Already zone-paced — CANONICAL preserved |
| Stat derivatives (§2.7) | Per-threshold reveal — already CANONICAL |

**Implementation notes.** UI element rendering is gated at the React component level in `src/ui/BroTown.jsx` — each top-level UI element checks its qualification condition before rendering. Conditions evaluate against `rpg._uiRevelation` state tracking per-element unlock timestamps. Once unlocked, elements remain visible permanently (no hiding regression).

**First-appearance moment.** When a UI element first unlocks, a brief non-intrusive animation draws attention — the icon fades in with a soft pulse, accompanied by a single-line contextual hint that fades out after 3 seconds. Examples:
- Quest Log first unlock: *"A quest has been offered. Your journey has a first ask."*
- Alignment first unlock: *"You've chosen. The game is watching who you're becoming."*
- Title showcase first unlock: *"A second title earned. You may display a pair now."*

Hint lines follow the tonal rule from §0.1 — variants include both sincere and wry voices per element.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Canonical UI exposes all features from character creation. New players are overwhelmed with information they don't yet need. Progressive revelation paces complexity to match player experience; each UI arrival becomes a moment of the game acknowledging something earned. The interface breathes with the character rather than standing static. The first-appearance moment for each element is small but load-bearing — it tells the player what the new element means and frames the moment as a small form of progression. Over a character's arc, UI growth is its own quiet reward.]`

### §0.3 Four-Register Alignment System

**Canonical Good / Neutral / Evil is replaced by four action registers.** See §33.3 for full alignment system specification; this section establishes the framing principle.

The four registers are:

- **Responsible** — takes care, protects, follows through on commitments. The Bro who shows up for others.
- **Mischievous** — teases, pranks, bends rules playfully without harm. The Bro who makes trouble with warmth.
- **Cool** — detached, reserved, doesn't get involved. The Bro who maintains distance.
- **Ruthless** — chooses hard efficiency, willing to harm. The Bro who takes the hard path.

None is "good" or "bad." Each is an identity players can inhabit. Four registers capture the moral space better than binary good/evil with neutral fallback — in particular, the Mischievous register gives Brotown's humor a place to live as moral identity, which the prior system lacked.

**Why four registers.** Canonical Good/Evil framing implicitly ranks morality: Good is "correct," Evil is "transgressive," Neutral is "failure to commit." Four action registers, none of which maps to "correct," turn alignment into character expression rather than moral evaluation. A Ruthless player is not playing the "wrong" way; they are playing a specific way. The moral system becomes character expression rather than moral test.

**Systems that reference this principle.** §25.1 capstone moral branching expands to four paths per capstone. §33.3 Alignment system replaces the -8 to +8 axis with four separate counters. §33.7 NPC tone gets four-register variants. §53.1 seven-act structure remains unchanged, but act-transition acknowledgments (§53.6) gain alignment-register-aware framing. §53.4 endgame is, under v14.0.5, **five endings** — four dominant-register endings (Hero / Trickster / Arbiter / Sovereign) plus one balanced-register ending (Weaver). §54.1 titles expand to include four dominant-register titles, the v14.0.5 Weaver title, and many compound titles. §56 Convergence perception expands to four register-specific appearances. §57 Last Recombination ritual maps registers to five cosmological poles (Light / Dark / Eclipse-warm / Eclipse-reserved / Convergence), with the Convergence pole (v14.0.5) corresponding to the balanced-register Weaver ending. Per §0.4, no register and no ending is ranked — five valid outcomes, five valid identities.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Binary Good/Evil with Neutral fallback flattens moral identity into a correctness test. Four action registers, none of which is "correct," turn alignment into character expression rather than moral evaluation. The Mischievous register specifically gives Brotown's humor a place to live as moral identity — the mischievous-warmth Bro currently has no path that fits them. Each register produces distinct titles, distinct NPC reactions, distinct endgames, and distinct ritual gestures that unify with distinct cosmological poles (per §57). Character expression replaces moral hierarchy.]`

### §0.4 The Unification Thesis (v14.0.4 CANONICAL)

Brotown's core thesis is **unification through the permanent holding of opposites.**

**Collision is the game's core verb.** Two elements meet; a third state emerges that is neither reducible to its sources nor synthesized away from them. This grammar operates at every scale — combat collisions (§10), Eclipse's dual destroy-and-heal action (§33.5), four-register alignment's refusal to rank (§33.3), the Convergence's coexistent geography (§56), and the ritual (§57). The ending is the first collision whose both elements are the player: the pole the Bro has become across a hundred hours of play, and the seed that pole grew from. Unification is what collision looks like at the self-scale, where the two elements are internal and the third state is the player themselves, made permanent as a Fragment.

Every system in this game expresses this thesis at a different scale. It is the throughline that connects combat mechanics, alignment systems, endgame zones, and the ritual.

**The thesis at each scale:**

- **Combat collisions (§10).** Two elements meet and produce a third state that is neither. Flame and Frost become Steam. Flame and Water become Quench. The collision is not the destruction of either element — it is the moment both are *held together* until a new state emerges that is neither reducible to its sources nor synthesized away from them. The third state is the opposition itself, crystallized briefly into effect.

- **Eclipse (§33.5).** Dark and Light, the two primordial poles, collide into the only collision in the game that simultaneously destroys and heals. Every other collision is violent. Eclipse is violent AND restorative — both at once, held in the same moment. The game's grammar has taught the player for a hundred levels that elemental combination is destruction. Eclipse is the revelation that at the highest scale, the combination is both.

- **Four-register alignment (§33.3).** The alignment system holds four possible identities (Responsible, Mischievous, Cool, Ruthless) simultaneously without ranking them. A player can be any of the four, or a mix, and no register is superior. The game refuses to resolve the opposition between identities into a single correct answer. All four are held, permanently, as equally valid.

- **The Convergence (§56).** The endgame zone is the geography where Dark and Light coexist. Not adjacent, not alternating — coexistent. The player walks through a zone that holds both poles at once as physical space. The Convergence is the unification thesis made traversable.

- **The Ritual (§57).** The ending is the thesis made physical at the personal scale. A Bro, who begins as an address with no identity, performs the act of holding their origin and their present simultaneously for twenty seconds. This performance — refusing to release either pole, refusing to resolve the opposition, refusing to let one pole subordinate the other — is what identity actually is in this game. Unification is not synthesis. It is the permanent refusal to let go of either side.

**The starting condition.** Brotown Bros begin as addresses with no identity. The NFT is a token for a not-yet-person. The character's face is a composite, their stats are zero, their inventory is empty, their Journey Log is blank. The hundred hours of play accumulate specificity — actions taken, choices made, registers inhabited, weapons wielded, zones lived in. At the ritual, the Bro performs the final act of holding their entire accumulated arc simultaneously with their origin. The Fragment that results is the crystallized record of that holding. The sky flash is the world's acknowledgment that one more address has become one more specific identity.

**Unification vs. synthesis.** The distinction is load-bearing. Synthesis is Hegelian — two things merge into a third that subsumes both. Brotown's unification is the opposite pattern: two things are held together permanently, without either being resolved into the other. The Bro at ritual completion does not stop being the origin-Bro and become the identity-Bro. They become the Bro who *holds* both — whose identity is constituted by the simultaneous presence of who they were at the beach and who they became after a hundred hours, never collapsed into a single resolved self. The grip and the weapon do not become one object at the ritual's climax; they dissolve together into the Fragment because they were always two poles of a single act of holding, and the Fragment is that act made permanent.

**The tonal corollary.** Per §0.1, Brotown's voice is epic AND absurd simultaneously. This is not a stylistic preference — it is the unification thesis expressed at the tonal layer. A game that is only grave fails the thesis because it has let one pole subordinate the other. A game that is only absurd fails the thesis for the same reason. Brotown holds both, at every layer, in every moment that can carry both. The ending's ritual (§57) is the clearest application: the grave center (the twenty-second hold, the Fragment's construction, the Naming sentence) is framed by absurd beats (the Ferryman's dry threshold line, the grip's gentle descent, Mayor Bro's "Huh. You're still here," the untouched-grip's pristine detail in the Hall). Neither register is suspended. The ritual's tonal structure embodies its mechanical structure.

**What this thesis rules out.** Several design moves that might have been attractive are ruled out by this thesis:

- *Progression-to-a-goal.* The game is not about reaching a destination where opposition is resolved. The player does not "complete" their identity by becoming maximally responsible or maximally ruthless. They complete it by holding whatever opposition their specific arc produced.
- *Synthesis endings.* The ritual does not merge the player's origin and present into a third unified thing that replaces them. Both are held; both dissolve together; the Fragment carries the signature of both in visible form (rust at base, crystal above).
- *Grave-only ceremonial surfaces.* Any beat that could carry epic weight must also admit its absurd counterpart. The ritual is cosmological *and* domestic. The Hall is memorial *and* quietly roasting. The sky flash is the world's acknowledgment *and* brief enough that most observers miss it.
- *Moralized alignment.* No register is "correct." The four-register system refuses to rank. The Ferryman, as between-figure, refuses to judge.
- *Single-identity frames.* A Bro with mixed registers is not "confused" or "incomplete." They are holding more oppositions simultaneously, which is more of the thesis, not less.

**Authoring discipline.** All future design work should be tested against this thesis before shipping. A proposed system that would resolve an opposition rather than hold it violates the thesis. A proposed mechanic that makes one pole of a pair mechanically superior to the other violates the thesis. A proposed tonal register that is purely grave or purely absurd violates the thesis. These violations are not always obvious — good-looking design moves can fail the thesis in subtle ways (e.g., a consolation mechanic that softens walkout's refusal register undermines the walkout/completion opposition that is load-bearing for two-honest-outcomes). When in doubt, return to this thesis and ask: *does this design hold opposites, or does it resolve them?*

`[DESIGN INTENT (v14.0.3): The v14.0.3 ship elevates the unification thesis from an implicit pattern to an explicit canonical principle. The thesis was always present in the design — combat collisions produce third states, Eclipse is the only dual-action collision, the four-register system refuses to rank, the Convergence holds both poles geographically, and the ritual's structure (twenty-second hold of two objects) embodies the thesis directly — but the earlier docs presented these as independent design decisions rather than as expressions of a single core thesis. Stating the thesis explicitly has three consequences. First, it gives future design work a clear test (does this hold opposites or resolve them?) that can catch thesis-violating proposals before they ship. Second, it clarifies why certain rejected alternatives were correctly rejected — the Overwhelmed Fragment (v14.0.1) was a consolation mechanic that would have created three outcomes where two-honest-outcomes was load-bearing for the opposition-holding structure; the Fragment-as-buff-dispenser (Tide hybrid) would have resolved the "memorial vs. mechanic" opposition in the wrong direction. Third, it allows the tonal rule (§0.1) to be understood as a specific application of the unification thesis rather than as a standalone stylistic preference. The epic-AND-absurd rule is what unification looks like at the voice layer. This §0.4 completes the foundation work — §0.1 identifies the tonal register (epic AND absurd), §0.2 identifies the surface discipline (progressive revelation), §0.3 identifies the identity framework (four registers), and §0.4 names the thesis that unifies the three. The GDD's cross-cutting principles are now coherent as expressions of a single claim about what this game is.]`

---

## PART I — CORE RPG SYSTEMS (§1–§7)

### §1. Two-Tier Stat System

#### §1.1 Stat Acquisition — Use-Trained

Stats are not allocated. They are **trained through use**. Every action in combat contributes stat experience (`stat_xp`) to the stats it exercises. A character's stat sheet is a record of how the player has played, not a forecast of how they intend to play.

- **Level 1 baseline:** Functional floor on all stats. A Level 1 character can move, dodge, attack, and trigger elements without any training. The baseline ensures every starting character is playable across every weapon type.
- **Level cap:** Infinite (see §38). Stat acquisition is bounded by total character level — see §1.4 budget rules. Stat training continues post-100 at the same per-level rate, but the stat ceilings (495 per tier) cap accumulation regardless of level.
- **Total at cap:** 495 Tier 1 points across 5 stats. 495 Tier 2 points across 5 specializations. Identical to prior versions — only the acquisition mechanism has changed.
- **No allocation menu.** No respec NPC. No reroll. The character you have is the character you played.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Identity is *earned through behavior*, not declared through paperwork. A player who spends 40 levels as a sword duelist and then picks up a staff gradually becomes a hybrid. They don't lose their Power — Power doesn't decay — they accumulate Mind alongside it. The "build" is the shape of the player's playtime. The §4.1 stat gates and the §4.11 loot filter couple cleanly with this: the play that drops your gear is the play that trains the stats to wield it, so a player's main-weapon loop is self-sustaining. Switching weapon types becomes a deliberate transition the marketplace exists to mediate — the new system's natural use case for player-to-player trade.]`

#### §1.2 Tier 1 — Capacity

Tier 1 stats train through **any combat engagement** that exercises them. They are foundational — every player accumulates Tier 1 stats simply by playing. Tier 1 trains faster than Tier 2 (see §1.4) so that players reach a recognizable Tier 1 identity ("a Power build") before they reach a refined Tier 2 specialization ("a Power-Ferocity crit build").

| Stat | Gives Exclusively | Trained By |
|------|-------------------|------------|
| **Power** | Base weapon damage scaling (universal — all weapon types benefit) | Landing damage with melee or ranged weapons against valid threats |
| **Vitality** | Health pool size | Taking damage and surviving (any source) |
| **Endurance** | Stamina pool size (dodge, block, sprint fuel) | Spending stamina on dodge, block, or sprint during combat |
| **Agility** | Move speed, dodge distance, dodge i-frame quality, base attack speed | Successful dodges (i-frames consumed against incoming damage) and movement under threat |
| **Mind** | Mana pool size (swipe trigger fuel) | Spending mana on swipe triggers and absorbing collision mana restore |

**v13 ADDITIVE — Somatic stat signaling.** Stats above specific thresholds produce subtle idle-animation changes that other players can read. High Power: weapons hang heavier in the swing and in sheathe. High Vitality: character breathes deeper at rest. High Agility: idle weight-shifting more frequent, lighter stance. High Endurance: holds breath during active combat, shorter recovery breaths between actions. High Mind: occasional faint aura around hands at rest. Thresholds align with §2.7 progressive derivative visibility — somatic signals appear at the same thresholds that surface the derivatives on the character sheet. No mechanical effect; pure visual reinforcement of who the player has become through play.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13 additive): Stats already train invisibly per §1.1. Somatic signaling makes the training readable on the character's body — a Power-trained Bro looks heavier; a Mind-trained Bro feels ethereal. Other players reading a character in town get a sense of what that Bro has been training without needing to inspect stats. Identity-in-body per §0.3 framing.]`

#### §1.3 Tier 2 — Technique

Tier 2 specs train through the **kind** of engagement, not just any engagement. They are technical — they reflect a *style* of play within a Tier 1 identity. Tier 2 trains slower than Tier 1 (see §1.4) so that specialization is the second layer of identity, layered on top of capacity.

| Spec | Style | Scales | Trained By |
|------|-------|--------|------------|
| **Ferocity** | ATTACK | Crit chance, crit damage multiplier | Landing critical hits |
| **Elemental Mastery** | ELEMENTS | Status damage, collision damage, status duration | Triggering collisions and applying status damage |
| **Fortification** | DEFEND | Block reduction, Thorns, poise, Counter Resonance, partial block floor | Mitigating incoming damage through block, partial block, or Thorns retaliation |
| **Restoration** | SUSTAIN | All regen rates, collision mana restore, Bless healing, cleanse | Healing self or allies, restoring mana through collisions, cleansing statuses |
| **Influence** | CONTROL | CC durations, debuff potency, Slow/Curse/Fracture/Shock/Reveal scaling | Applying CC and debuff statuses that successfully land on valid threats |

#### §1.4 Stat XP Budget and Distribution (CANONICAL)

**The acquisition rate is bounded by character level, not by playtime.** A Level 50 character has earned exactly the same total stat budget as the equivalent allocation-based character at Level 50: 250 Tier 1 points and 250 Tier 2 points. What changes is *which* stats hold those points — and that is determined by play.

**Per-level budget:**

```
TIER1_POINTS_PER_LEVEL = 5
TIER2_POINTS_PER_LEVEL = 5
```

Each level grants 5 Tier 1 stat-points-worth of stat XP and 5 Tier 2 stat-points-worth of stat XP, distributed across the stats the player has been training during that level. Total per-level acquisition matches the prior allocation system exactly. All downstream formulas in §2, all invariants in §22, and all audit gates in §6.5 remain valid because the totals are unchanged.

**Distribution rule:**

```
func distribute_stat_xp(player, tier, contributions):
    # contributions: map<stat_id, float> — relative training contributions during this level
    # tier: TIER1 or TIER2
    
    budget = TIER1_POINTS_PER_LEVEL if tier == TIER1 else TIER2_POINTS_PER_LEVEL
    
    # Filter out locked stats — they consume budget but earn nothing
    unlocked_contributions = {s: c for s, c in contributions.items() if not player.is_locked(s)}
    locked_share = sum(c for s, c in contributions.items() if player.is_locked(s))
    
    # Locked share is BURNED. It does not redistribute to unlocked stats.
    # This is the cost of locking: actions toward locked stats are wasted budget.
    effective_budget = budget × (1 - locked_share / total_share(contributions))
    
    # Normalize unlocked contributions and distribute
    total_unlocked = sum(unlocked_contributions.values())
    if total_unlocked == 0: return  # No unlocked training this level
    
    for stat, contribution in unlocked_contributions.items():
        share = contribution / total_unlocked
        gained = effective_budget × share
        
        # Cap at the per-tier ceiling (99 per Tier 1 stat, 99 per Tier 2 spec)
        # Excess is also burned — same principle as locking
        player.stats[stat] = min(player.stats[stat] + gained, STAT_CAP_PER_STAT(99))
```

**Why the budget is global per level rather than infinite per action:** The per-level budget caps total stat XP at the same rate as the old allocation system, which means *no amount of grinding within a level produces more stats than a level provides*. Players cannot bank a level's worth of dungeon runs into 250 extra Power. They get exactly 5 Tier 1 points worth of training per level, distributed across whatever they did during that level. This preserves the §22 invariants and the §6.5 kill-time gates without requiring per-action diminishing returns or anti-grind heuristics. The level is the budget. Play allocates it.

**Valid threat requirement:** Stat XP only accrues from actions against enemies within ±5 levels of the player, or against any boss enemy regardless of level. Hitting a Level 1 slime at Level 80 contributes nothing. This single rule eliminates the grind-on-trivial-enemies exploit class without further machinery. The §6.5 kill-time gates already validate that real fights take real time — if an encounter passes those gates, it counts as a real fight for stat XP purposes.

`[DESIGN INTENT: The level-bounded budget is what makes use-trained stats safe. Without it, the system invites infinite grinding. With it, the math is identical to allocation — same totals, same ceilings, same downstream formulas — and only the acquisition path changes. A player who plays optimally and a player who plays poorly reach the same total stats at Level 50; the difference is the distribution. This is the property that lets us change the stat acquisition mechanism without re-validating any other system in the GDD.]`

**v13 ADDITIVE — Prose training record.** The training record is accessible on demand from the character sheet, shown as prose descriptors with tonal range per §0.1:

- Observational: *"Power: You struck hard against fair opponents today."*
- Wry: *"Agility: You moved well under threat. Mostly."*
- Dry: *"Restoration: You healed Bro Tessa exactly once. She thanked you exactly once."*
- Self-deprecating: *"Mind: You tried a complex combo. It did not work. You learned something."*

The numerical stat-XP breakdown remains accessible via an expandable technical view for players who want accounting precision. The prose view is the default — training expressed in voice rather than number.

#### §1.5 Stat Locks (CANONICAL)

Players can **lock** any stat at its current value. Locked stats do not train, regardless of how much the player exercises them. Locking is the mechanism that preserves player agency and supports pure builds.

**Lock rules:**

- A lock can be applied at any time, on any stat (Tier 1 or Tier 2), at the stat's current value.
- A locked stat **does not gain stat XP**, even if the player performs actions that would normally train it.
- A locked stat **still consumes its share of the per-level budget** when the player performs training actions that target it. Those points are *burned* — they do not redistribute to unlocked stats. (See §1.4 distribution rule.)
- A lock can be removed at any time. Unlocking does **not** retroactively grant the training the stat would have received while locked. Locked time is lost time for that stat.
- Locks have **no gold cost, no cooldown, and no penalty** beyond the burned budget. They are a free expression of player intent.

**Why burning the locked share matters:** If locked stats redistributed their share of the budget to unlocked stats, locking would become a *focusing* mechanism — players would lock four stats and funnel all training into the fifth, reaching maximum efficiency in one stat with minimum effort. By burning the locked share, locks become a *commitment to a ceiling*. The pure-build player who locks four stats accepts that their training during combat is partially wasted; the discipline is the design. This preserves the integrity of the per-level budget and prevents lock exploitation.

**Level lock:**

A player can also lock their character level. A level-locked character does not gain XP — XP that would have been earned is converted to a small currency reward (LEVEL_LOCK_XP_REFUND, tunable, recommended 1g per 10 XP) so that play is still rewarded, but progression halts. Level locking enables low-level competitive play and self-imposed difficulty modes. A level-locked character can still train stats up to their level's budget — locking the level does not lock the stats. If the player wants a fully frozen character, they lock the level *and* all relevant stats independently.

**Inspect card:** Lock state is visible to other players via the inspect card (§30), and the inspect card additionally displays the player's permanent **Vow history** — a persistent list of every lock and unlock ceremony the player has performed, each with a stat name and timestamp. Active vows appear first with a "Vow:" prefix ("Vow: Vitality locked, Day 43"); broken vows appear below with a "Vow Broken:" prefix and the break date. A character with all stats locked at deliberate values reads as a craftsman who knows exactly what they're doing — pure builds are honored, not hidden — and the vow history gives ongoing visible testimony to that discipline across the character's lifetime. Leaderboards (§37) include separate categories for locked-level achievements.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Pure builds are a real playstyle that the use-trained system would otherwise erase. The lock primitive preserves them at zero cost to the rest of the system. The "burned share" rule is the load-bearing piece — without it, locks become a min-max tool; with it, locks become a commitment. The framing matters: a player locking four stats should *feel* like they're sculpting deliberate constraint, not gaming the system. The inspect card visibility and the dedicated leaderboards are what convert the lock from a workaround into a recognized way to play.]`

##### §1.5.1 Distributed NPC-Witnessed Commitments (v13 REPLACEMENT of Vows Shrine)

**The Vows Shrine as a single town building is removed.** Stat locks are instead performed at **meaningful NPC locations** tied to the stat's domain. The mechanical rules of §1.5 are unchanged — what changes is where and how the lock is enacted.

**NPC assignment per stat:**

| Stat | Locking NPC | Location |
|------|-------------|----------|
| Power | The Blacksmith | Forge (town) |
| Vitality | The Mayor | Town Hall |
| Endurance | The Gymkeeper | Training Yard (town) |
| Agility | The Courier | Courier's Post (town) |
| Mind | The Ferryman | Wall gate |
| Ferocity | The Blacksmith | Forge (town) |
| Elemental Mastery | The Enchanter | Enchanting Shop |
| Fortification | The Mayor | Town Hall |
| Restoration | The Herbalist | Apothecary |
| Influence | The Tavern Keeper | Tavern |

Where multiple stats share an NPC (Power + Ferocity at the Blacksmith), the NPC handles both with distinct ceremonies — the player selects which stat during the interaction.

**The ceremony.** A player with stats to lock walks to the assigned NPC and interacts. A short ceremony plays appropriate to that NPC's register — the Blacksmith nods gravely and strikes the anvil once (Power lock), the Ferryman looks at you without speaking and marks a symbol on his oar (Mind lock), the Tavern Keeper pours a single drink and sets it down untouched (Influence lock). Each NPC's lock ceremony is authored for that character's register and the specific stat.

A fixed affirmation line displays — *"This is who I am."* The affirmation is shared across all lock ceremonies regardless of NPC; its singularity is part of the moment's weight. The stat being locked animates briefly on the inspect card's stat list to mark the new state.

**Locked stats produce subtle posture change.** Characters with locked stats carry themselves slightly differently — a locked-Power character stands with weapon-ready weight, a locked-Mind character is subtly more still. The posture change is continuous visual state tied to lock inventory, not a ceremony artifact.

**Public visibility.** Each NPC location has a small visible acknowledgment when a lock ceremony begins — the forge glows briefly during a Power/Ferocity ceremony, the Ferryman's symbol briefly illuminates during a Mind ceremony, and so on. Other players in town can see the NPC location activate from anywhere with visible sight line. They do not see which player is at the NPC unless within interact-range.

**The Recent Vows display per NPC.** Each locking NPC maintains a per-NPC Recent Vows display at their location — the last 10 ceremonies performed with that NPC, server-wide. This replaces the canonical single Shrine Recent Vows panel. A player visiting the Blacksmith's forge reads the last 10 Power/Ferocity vows; visiting the Ferryman reads the last 10 Mind vows. Each entry displays player name, specific stat locked, and timestamp (relative). Tapping a name opens that player's inspect card.

**Unlocking ceremony.** Removing a lock also requires visiting the assigned NPC. The unlock ceremony is deliberately different in register per NPC — the Blacksmith silently sets down his hammer (Power unlock), the Ferryman turns to face the Lawless gate rather than the player (Mind unlock). No affirmation line plays. A "Vow Broken" entry appears on that NPC's Recent Vows display. Unlock remains free per §1.5.

**Tonal range in NPC reactions (per §0.1).** Each NPC's lock/unlock ceremony includes both grave and wry variants. Blacksmith Power lock grave variant: *"You've chosen. The steel remembers."* Wry variant: *"Good. Commit. I was getting tired of your indecision."*

**Zone restrictions.** Lock and unlock actions are town-only. They cannot be performed in combat zones, Guardian zones, the Convergence, or Lawless Land. This preserves town as identity-commitment geography.

**Accessibility note.** Locking NPCs are visible from Level 1 and can be interacted with by any character. New players walking past see the Recent Vows displays and learn the mechanic through the server's ongoing activity — no tutorial needed. Per §0.2 progressive UI revelation, no special UI element appears; the feature is discovered through the NPCs themselves.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): A single Shrine centralizes commitments in a location that is architecturally efficient but locationally generic. Distributing commitments across meaningful NPCs makes each lock a specific ritual tied to the domain of the commitment. Locking Power at the Blacksmith's forge feels different from locking Mind with the Ferryman at the wall — the ceremony's character matches the stat's character. The town becomes a geography of commitment decisions rather than a menu of them. The shared affirmation line ("This is who I am") preserves the canonical singularity across distributed ceremonies; what varies is the *place* and the *witness*. Per-NPC Recent Vows displays give each locking location its own rotating lore. Per §0.1 tonal rule, every NPC ceremony authored gains both grave and wry variants, so the Blacksmith's gravity coexists with the Tavern Keeper's dry humor across the full locking landscape.]`

#### §1.6 Training Visibility (UI Requirement)

The use-trained system fails as a player experience if the player cannot see what they are training. The character sheet must display, alongside current stat values, a **training pane** showing:

- Current stat value and the stat's hard cap (99 per stat).
- Active stat XP gain rate per stat (points per minute, smoothed over the last combat minute).
- The single most recent action that contributed to each stat (e.g., "Power +0.03 — landed greatsword hit on Frost Imp").
- Lock toggle per stat.
- A breakdown of "this level's budget so far" — how much of the per-level Tier 1 and Tier 2 budgets the player has consumed, and which stats have received how much.

**Combat micro-feedback:** During combat, small floating "+stat" indicators appear on stat-gain events at the screen edge or near the resource HUD. Visible but not intrusive. The micro-feedback is what makes the system feel like *agency* rather than *drift*. Without it, players experience their character growing behind their back. With it, they see themselves becoming what they're playing as in real time.

**Journey Log integration:** The Journey Log (§27.4) gains a session recap on logout: "In this session you trained: Power +2.4, Ferocity +1.1, Mind +0.6. You moved from a melee specialist toward a hybrid duelist." This narrative recap is one of the use-trained system's biggest payoffs — it gives the Journey Log a concrete job (tracking the player's evolving identity) and gives the stat system a place to be legible across sessions.

**Session letter format (v13 ADDITIVE).** The session recap is delivered as a short letter from the character to themselves, not a stat readout. Per §0.1 tonal rule, the letter is authored across multiple registers:

- Grave: *"Today I swung hard. My arms remember it. The imps in Ember Fields know my rhythm now."*
- Self-deprecating: *"Today I died three times. Not my best day. Tomorrow."*
- Wry: *"Killed the Depth 5 boss. I'm pretending I meant to dodge that second hit."*
- Observational: *"Today I moved between Frost and Flame. My gear is confused. So am I, a little."*

The letter is chosen by context (dominant register per §33.3, session event highlights, accumulated stat drift). Numerical stat diff remains accessible via an expandable section below the letter for players who want accounting; the letter is the default logout view.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): Numerical session recap is informational. Letter format makes ending a session reflective in the Unlikely Hero voice. Tonal variety keeps the letters from becoming monotonous across hundreds of sessions. The numerical diff remains available for players who want it — the letter is the default, not the only option.]`

`[DESIGN INTENT: Allocation systems are satisfying partly because the player presses a button and a number goes up. Use-trained systems can feel passive unless training is *visible at the moment it happens*. The training pane, the floating combat indicators, and the Journey Log recap are the three legibility layers — moment-to-moment, session, and long-term. All three are required. Cutting any of them undermines the system's core experience.]`

#### §1.6.1 Training Moment Feedback (v12.48 CANONICAL)

The §1.6 Training Visibility infrastructure establishes that players must *see* their stats train. v12.48 extends this with two complementary legibility layers operating at different time scales: **continuous-fill bars** on the inspect card (moment-to-moment training visible as sub-integer fill progress) and **threshold celebration** on first productive use of a new stat tier (discrete milestone punctuation).

**Why the two-layer approach.** The combat micro-feedback (floating "+stat" indicators) and session recap cover the immediate and long-term scales, but the *process between combat sessions and between thresholds* was invisible. A player whose Power ticks from 149 to 150 over several minutes of combat previously saw only the integer change. v12.48 fills this gap: continuous-fill bars make the sub-integer progression visible in real time on the inspect card, and threshold celebration punctuates the moment when the new tier actually matters (the first time it gets used productively).

**Continuous-fill bars.** Each stat on the inspect card displays a small fill bar between integer values. A player with Power 149.7 sees a bar that is ~70% filled under the integer "149." During active combat, the bar visibly fills as use-training accrues — each combat hit that contributes Power XP animates the fill percentage smoothly. When the bar completes, it ticks over to the next integer (150) and resets. The fill rate during combat is not literal XP accumulation; it is a smoothed render of the underlying continuous XP counter that the training pipeline already tracks.

**Threshold celebration.** When a stat crosses a round-number threshold and the player subsequently takes an action that *benefits from the new tier*, a brief visual effect fires:

- Character glows in the stat's accent color for ~0.5 seconds (accent colors per §1.7 CR direction match archetype accents).
