- A small floating label appears above the character (e.g., "FEROCITY 150" or "POWER 100").
- Other nearby players see the effect — social witnessing of training milestones.

The celebration fires on the **first productive use** of the new tier, not on the threshold crossing itself. A player whose Ferocity ticks to 150 does not see the effect until their next crit; a player whose Fortification ticks to 100 does not see it until their next block. This ties the feedback to a felt moment — the player experiences the threshold as *useful*, not as a background tick.

**Tier-differentiated frequency.** T1 stats (Power, Ferocity, Fortification, Mind, Spirit, Nerve) trigger celebration at every +10 threshold crossing. T2 stats (derived technique stats) trigger celebration at every +25 threshold crossing. The difference matters: T2 derivative stats have more frequent sub-unit changes, and celebrating at +10 would produce visual spam that undermines the celebratory register. Every +25 threshold makes T2 celebrations rarer and therefore more meaningful.

**Stat-name pedagogy.** The floating label shows the stat name alongside the value for the **first 10 celebration triggers** of each stat on a given character. After the 10th trigger for a stat, the label drops to value-only ("150" rather than "POWER 150"). This teaches new players what the stats are while respecting veterans who know the color-to-stat mapping by accent.

**Tuning constants.**

| Variable | Default | Min | Max | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| TRAINING_T1_CELEBRATION_INTERVAL | 10 | 5 | 25 | T1 stat celebration fires every N points |
| TRAINING_T2_CELEBRATION_INTERVAL | 25 | 10 | 50 | T2 derivative stat celebration fires every N points |
| TRAINING_CELEBRATION_DURATION | 0.5s | 0.3s | 1.0s | Character glow duration |
| TRAINING_NAME_PEDAGOGY_COUNT | 10 | 5 | 20 | Label shows stat-name for first N triggers per stat |
| TRAINING_FILL_BAR_SMOOTHING | 0.3s | 0.1s | 0.5s | Inspect-card fill-bar animation smoothing window |

**Architecture note.** The continuous-fill bars read from the existing stat-XP pipeline — no schema change. The threshold celebration uses client-side detection (player's stat crossed threshold N turns ago; their next productive-action event fires the celebration). Productive-action detection is client-side event-driven; the server validates celebration broadcasts for the "other nearby players see the effect" case to prevent spoofing.

**What this is NOT.**

- Not a popup. Threshold celebration is a 0.5-second character glow + floating label, not a screen-interrupting modal.
- Not stat-buff. Crossing a threshold does nothing mechanically that wasn't already happening — the celebration is *acknowledgment* of the existing stat progression, not a reward for it.
- Not broadcast beyond local view. Other nearby players see the effect (social witnessing); party members in different zones do not receive a notification.
- Not a completion track. Stats can cross the same threshold multiple times if players hit the hard cap and the UI resets display (this is not currently possible — stats cap at 99 and never regress — but the discipline applies if future mechanics allow stat regression).
- Not overlapping with existing legibility layers. The §1.6 combat micro-feedback (floating "+stat" indicators) continues to fire on every stat-XP event; v12.48 celebrations fire only at threshold crossings. Micro-feedback + celebration coexist at different scales.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.48): The v12.48 §1.6.1 addition is a legibility completion. The §1.6 infrastructure (training pane, combat micro-feedback, Journey Log recap) covered moment-to-moment, per-action, and session scales. The intermediate scale — sub-integer progression between integer ticks, and milestone-crossing moments — was the remaining gap. Continuous-fill bars address the first; threshold celebration addresses the second. The "first productive use" trigger discipline is the load-bearing choice: celebrating on threshold crossing itself would produce feedback disconnected from the player's actual experience (a tick during idle time would celebrate in the training pane without the player having felt anything). Tying celebration to the first productive use means the feedback fires *when the improvement is actually experienced* — the first crit after Ferocity 150, the first successful block after Fortification 100. This matches the shape-first / observational-warm register established across earlier ships: the game acknowledges what the player did through their own action, rather than interrupting to announce a state change. The two-layer tier-differentiated frequency (T1 every +10, T2 every +25) preserves the celebratory register by keeping triggers rare enough to stay meaningful — a player sees roughly 50 T1 celebrations over a character's lifetime (10 thresholds × 5 primary stats covered at typical play depths), which reads as punctuation rather than spam. The stat-name pedagogy phase-out (10 triggers then value-only) is a small teaching discipline that respects both new and experienced players without requiring a settings toggle. No invariants touched — this is pure UI/UX legibility work with zero mechanical effect. The v12.48 ship is a *closing ship* for the §1 stat system — the stat system now has full legibility coverage across all time scales from moment-to-moment (combat micro-feedback) to integer-to-integer (continuous-fill bars) to threshold-to-threshold (celebration) to session (Journey Log session recap) to lifetime (archetype emergence). Future work on §1 is expected to be tuning rather than legibility.]`

### §2. Derived Stat Formulas

**Total Stat Value** = trained value (§1) + equipped gear bonuses. All formulas below operate on the total — they are agnostic to whether stat points were earned through training or contributed by gear.

#### §2.1 Ferocity Derivatives (CANONICAL)

**Crit Chance:** `crit% = (60 × Ferocity) / (Ferocity + 120)`

Above CRIT_SOFT_CAP(300): `crit% = base_at_300 + (8 × excess) / (excess + 200)`. Diminishing.

**Crit Damage Multiplier:** `crit_mult = CRIT_BASE_MULT(1.75) + Ferocity × CRIT_DMG_PER_FER(0.0008)`

At Ferocity 400: crit_mult = 1.75 + 0.32 = 2.07×.

#### §2.2 Elemental Mastery Derivatives

**Status Damage Multiplier:** `1 + EM × EM_STATUS_COEFF(0.0015)`
**Collision Damage Multiplier:** `1 + EM × EM_COLLISION_COEFF(0.0015)`
**Status Duration Multiplier:** `1 + EM × EM_DURATION_COEFF(0.0004)`

At EM 400: status damage ×1.60, collision damage ×1.60, duration ×1.16.

#### §2.3 Fortification Derivatives (CANONICAL)

**Block Damage Reduction (on successful standard block — see §5.3b Active Block for the tap-to-block mechanic):**
`block_reduction = min(BLOCK_REDUCTION_CAP(0.75), BLOCK_BASE_REDUCTION(0.25) + Fortification × BLOCK_FRT_COEFF(0.0012))`

Stamina cost per successful standard block: BLOCK_STAM_COST(15). Perfect blocks (§5.3b) fully negate damage and refund stamina; the `block_reduction` formula applies only to the Standard Block outcome tier.

**Partial Block Floor (applied to the Standard Block outcome only):**
`partial_floor = PARTIAL_BLOCK_BASE(0.08) + Fortification × PARTIAL_BLOCK_COEFF(0.0003)`

**Thorns:** *(canonical §8.3)*

**Counter Resonance:** Enemies that hit the player suffer outgoing damage reduction.
`cr_reduction = COUNTER_RES_BASE(0.03) + Fortification × COUNTER_RES_COEFF(0.00008)`
Duration: COUNTER_RES_DUR(6.0s). Per source-target pair, refreshes on subsequent hits.

**Poise Bonus:** *(canonical §5.3)*

#### §2.4 Restoration Derivatives

All regen multiplier: `1 + Restoration × REST_REGEN_COEFF(0.001)`
Applied to: HP regen, stamina regen, mana regen.

Collision mana restore multiplier: `1 + Restoration × REST_COLLISION_COEFF(0.0012)`
Bless healing multiplier: `1 + Restoration × REST_HEAL_COEFF(0.0018)`

At Restoration 400: regen ×1.40, collision mana ×1.48, healing ×1.72.

#### §2.5 Agility Derivatives (CANONICAL)

**Move Speed:** `BASE_MOVE_SPEED(5.0) × (1 + min(Agility × MOVE_AGI_COEFF(0.0012), MOVE_CAP(0.60)))`

Total from all sources capped at +75% of base.

**Dodge I-Frames:** `DODGE_IFRAMES_BASE(0.25) + min(Agility × IFRAMES_AGI_COEFF(0.0003), IFRAMES_CAP(0.10))`

Range: 0.25s–0.35s.

**Dodge Distance:** `DODGE_DIST_BASE(3.0) + Agility × DODGE_DIST_COEFF(0.005)` game units.

#### §2.6 Influence Derivatives

**Freeze Duration Bonus:** `× (1 + Influence × INF_CC_COEFF(0.0008))`
**Root Duration Bonus:** `× (1 + Influence × INF_CC_COEFF(0.0008))`
**Slow Effectiveness:** Base Slow % `× (1 + Influence × INF_SLOW_COEFF(0.001))`
**Fracture Potency:** Poise reduction per stack `× (1 + Influence × INF_FRAC_COEFF(0.001))`
**Shock Amplification:** Damage amp `× (1 + Influence × INF_SHOCK_COEFF(0.001))`
**Curse Strength:** Damage reduction/vulnerability `× (1 + Influence × INF_CURSE_COEFF(0.001))`
**Reveal Effectiveness:** Resistance strip `× (1 + Influence × INF_REVEAL_COEFF(0.001))`

#### §2.7 Progressive Derivative Visibility (v12.42 CANONICAL)

Tier 2 stat derivatives defined in §2.1-§2.6 are progressively **revealed on the inspect card** as the underlying stat grows. Every derivative still applies mechanically at all stat values — a character with Elemental Mastery 20 still benefits from the tiny collision damage multiplier — but derivative *visibility* is gated by per-stat thresholds. Low-stat characters see a focused inspect card showing only the primary effect of each stat; high-stat characters see the full derivative breakdown as they've earned the right to plan around it.

**Per-stat visibility thresholds.**

| Stat | Always Visible | Revealed at Tier | Revealed at Tier | Revealed at Tier |
|------|----------------|:------------------:|:------------------:|:------------------:|
| **Ferocity** (Fer) | Crit Chance | Crit Damage Mult at Fer 100 | Crit Soft Cap info at Fer 300 | — |
| **Elemental Mastery** (EM) | Collision Damage Mult | Status Duration at EM 50 | Status Damage at EM 150 | — |
| **Fortification** (For) | Block Reduction | Partial Block Floor at For 50 | Poise at For 100 | Counter Res at For 150 |
| **Restoration** (Res) | All Regen | Healing Output at Res 50 | Collision Mana at Res 150 | — |
| **Agility** (Agi) | Movement Speed | Attack Speed at Agi 50 | Dodge iFrame Bonus at Agi 150 | — |
| **Influence** (Inf) | CC Duration | Debuff Potency at Inf 75 | — | — |

**Primary effect always visible.** Every Tier 2 stat shows its most-core derivative at all stat values — no player is ever confused about what the stat does at the basic level. A new player investing first points into Ferocity sees "Crit Chance" on their inspect card immediately. A player investing first points into Elemental Mastery sees "Collision Damage Mult." The baseline identity of each stat is never hidden.

**Secondary and tertiary derivatives appear as stat grows.** The additional derivatives each stat carries (multiple Ferocity derivatives, multiple Fortification derivatives, etc.) are revealed progressively as the stat crosses specific thresholds. The thresholds are chosen to correspond to *meaningful commitment levels* — a character with EM 50+ has invested enough in the stat that status duration is actually noticeable in play; a character with EM 150+ is a committed status-focused build where status damage becomes a primary concern. The visibility schedule matches when the derivative actually starts to matter in play.

**Reveal animation.** When a character crosses a threshold and unlocks a new derivative, the inspect card's derivative list animates the new entry sliding in (~0.3s) with a brief subtle highlight (~0.5s glow on the new row). This is the only visible acknowledgment of the crossing — no popup, no notification, no audio cue. The animation fires the next time the player opens their inspect card after the threshold crosses, not at the moment of crossing itself. The animation is a one-time event per derivative per character; subsequent inspect card opens show the derivative as a standard row without highlight.

**The hiding is UI-only, never mechanical.** Every derivative applies to gameplay at all stat values regardless of visibility. A character with EM 20 gets the tiny Collision Damage Mult benefit (which shows as a visible line) AND the even-tinier Status Duration benefit (which does NOT show on their inspect card because EM is below 50). Both derivatives operate in the combat math; only one is shown as a separate inspect card entry. The player's combat outcome is identical to a pre-v12.42 character with the same stats; only the inspect card presentation is different.

**Viewing another player's inspect card.** When viewing another player's card (via §17 inspect mechanic), the visible derivatives reflect *their* threshold state, not the viewer's. A player with EM 200 shows all three EM derivatives (Collision Damage, Status Duration, Status Damage) to viewers regardless of the viewer's own EM value. Each player's inspect card reflects their own depth; the viewer sees what that player has earned the right to see about themselves. This matches the §17 inspect-card design principle of "the inspect card shows what the character has become," not "what the viewer can understand."

**Design rationale — cognitive load curve matching stat investment.** A new player with low stats sees a focused inspect card showing one derivative per stat. A committed mid-game player investing in specific stats sees their specialized stats unlock additional derivatives. An endgame optimizer sees the full derivative tree for their investment. The progressive reveal matches the player's increasing capacity to plan around derivatives — at Fer 15, the Crit Damage Mult is small enough to be statistical noise; at Fer 100, it's a meaningful build lever worth planning around. Showing the derivative before it matters produces decision paralysis in new players ("should I care about Crit Damage Mult?"); showing it after it matters respects the player's earned attention to the detail.

**Exceptions — no opt-out toggle.** The progressive reveal is not player-toggleable. A player who wants to see all derivatives immediately cannot disable the reveal thresholds. This is deliberate — the whole mechanic depends on the reveal being a *structural* pedagogical signal ("your investment in EM has reached the level where Status Duration matters") rather than a preference setting. A toggle would undermine the pedagogy: players who opt to show all derivatives immediately would lose the "I've earned visibility into this" moment that the reveal is designed to produce.

**Data model — per-character derivative visibility Set.** Each character's PlayerState gains a `derivatives_revealed` Set field (similar to the v12.33 `certifications_earned` and v12.37 `archetype_encounters_seen` patterns) listing the specific derivative IDs the character has unlocked visibility for. Server writes to this Set when a stat crosses its next threshold; client reads the Set when rendering the inspect card to decide which derivative rows to show. Reveal animation state lives in client-side ephemeral state (which derivatives were newly added since the last inspect-card open) and resets on client reload. See architecture §9.5.10 extension for the persistent-state integration.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.42): Stat derivatives are where ARPG builds become interesting — the Crit Damage Multiplier that makes a Ferocity build different from a Crit-Chance-only build, the Status Damage that makes EM builds different from basic-Collision-Damage EM builds. These derivatives are also where ARPG interfaces become overwhelming — a new player looking at six stats with three derivatives each sees 18 numbers they don't yet understand. The traditional solution is tutorialization ("hover over each stat to see what it does") which produces documentation-style interfaces. The progressive-reveal solution shows what you've earned the right to see: low-stat characters see the primary effect of each stat, medium-stat characters see secondary effects as they become meaningful, high-stat characters see tertiary effects that reward advanced planning. The thresholds are chosen per stat to correspond to *meaningful commitment levels* — Fer 100 is a committed Crit build; EM 150 is a status-focused build; For 150 is an advanced defense build. At each threshold crossing, the new derivative slides in with a brief highlight, which the player may notice (and feel the small recognition of earning a new layer of depth) or not notice (and simply see their inspect card grow slightly richer over time). This matches the design sequence's recurring pattern of surfacing depth through progression rather than announcement. The mechanic never changes — every derivative applies at all stat values — only the UI reveals it at the right moment. Low-stat inspect cards are focused and teachable; high-stat inspect cards are rich and plannable. The progressive reveal produces an inspect card that grows with the character, which is the correct structural property for a long-progression ARPG.]`

### §3. Resource Framework

| Resource | Max Formula | Regen | Depleted State |
|----------|------------|-------|----------------|
| Health | §3.2 | OOC: 1.5%/s (5s delay). Combat: 0.3%/s | Dead |
| Stamina | §3.3 | 20/s (1s delay) × Restoration mult | Can't dodge/block/sprint |
| Mana | §3.4 | OOC: 2.5%/s (2s delay). Combat: 0.3%/s × Restoration mult | Can't swipe trigger |

All regen rates multiplied by Restoration derivative (§2.4).

**Resource bar distinct behaviors (v12.26).** The three resource bars have distinct idle and stressed animations so peripheral vision can read their state without direct attention. HP shakes on damage taken (magnitude scales with damage fraction of max). Stamina pulses/breathes in rhythm with movement (faster when running, slower when walking, still when standing). Mana ripples outward during collision mana restore events and shows a subtle standing ripple when full. When 2+ bars are simultaneously animating, each runs at 60% intensity to prevent visual overwhelm. Matching audio accents pair with each animation (HP hit sound, stamina exertion on running, mana chime on collision restore). Full specification in `creative-reference.md` §1.4 UI and §2 Audio.

#### §3.2 Health (CANONICAL)

`max_hp = BASE_HP(100) + ((Level - 1) × HP_LEVEL_COEFF(12)) + (Vitality × HP_VIT_COEFF(10))`

#### §3.3 Stamina (CANONICAL)

`max_stamina = BASE_STAMINA(100) + (Endurance × STAM_END_COEFF(3.0))`

#### §3.4 Mana (CANONICAL)

`max_mana = BASE_MANA(80) + (Mind × MANA_MIND_COEFF(3.5))`

`[DESIGN INTENT: Combat mana regen alone cannot sustain swipe triggers. Collision mana restore (§3.5) is the primary sustain mechanic. This forces casters to engage with the elemental system.]`

#### §3.5 Collision Mana Restore (CANONICAL)

```
func resolve_collision_mana(player, collision, consumed_status):
    if not collision.consumes_setup: return

    cd = COLLISION_MANA_CD(3.0)
    if collision.id == "eclipse": cd = ECLIPSE_MANA_CD(1.5)
    if player.collision_mana_cd_timer > 0: return
    player.collision_mana_cd_timer = cd

    base_restore = COLLISION_MANA_RESTORE(0.04) × player.max_mana

    # Global entropy — all collisions count
    global_count = player.entropy_tracker.count_all(MANA_ENTROPY_WINDOW(15.0))
    entropy = max(1.0 - global_count × MANA_ENTROPY_DECAY(0.20), MANA_ENTROPY_FLOOR(0.30))
    base_restore ×= entropy

    # Restoration multiplier (§2.4)
    base_restore ×= (1 + player.restoration × REST_COLLISION_COEFF(0.0012))

    # Timing scaling (near-expiry = more mana)
    remaining = consumed_status.remaining_duration
    max_dur = consumed_status.max_duration
    timing_mult = 1.0 + (1.0 - remaining / max_dur) × 0.5

    player.mana = min(player.mana + base_restore × timing_mult, player.max_mana)
    player.entropy_tracker.record(collision.id)
```

##### §3.5a Entropy Grace Period and Visibility (v12.39 CANONICAL)

The collision mana-restore entropy mechanic (§3.5) operates globally on a 15-second rolling window, reducing per-collision mana restore by 20% per recent collision with a 30% floor. v12.39 makes the entropy surface **visible** to the player and adds a **grace period** that rewards rhythm-paced collision play by bypassing entropy entirely for collisions triggered within 2 seconds of the previous collision's status-consume.

**The grace-window mechanic.**

- When a collision resolves and consumes its setup status, the server records a `grace_window_start` timestamp.
- If the player triggers another collision within `ENTROPY_GRACE_WINDOW`(2.0s) of that timestamp, the new collision is a **grace collision** and ignores entropy entirely — it produces full baseline mana restore as if entropy were zero, regardless of recent collision count.
- Collisions triggered **outside** the grace window apply entropy normally per the existing §3.5 formula.
- Every collision (grace or not) resets the `grace_window_start` timestamp to the current collision's consume time. A rhythm-paced player can chain grace collisions indefinitely as long as each subsequent collision falls within 2 seconds of the previous one.

**Revised resolution pseudocode.**

```
func resolve_collision_mana(player, collision, consumed_status):
    if not collision.consumes_setup: return

    cd = COLLISION_MANA_CD(3.0)
    if collision.id == "eclipse": cd = ECLIPSE_MANA_CD(1.5)
    if player.collision_mana_cd_timer > 0: return
    player.collision_mana_cd_timer = cd

    base_restore = COLLISION_MANA_RESTORE(0.04) × player.max_mana

    # v12.39: Grace window check
    time_since_last = now - player.grace_window_start
    is_grace_collision = (time_since_last <= ENTROPY_GRACE_WINDOW(2.0))

    if is_grace_collision:
        entropy = 1.0  # Full restore, ignore entropy
    else:
        # Global entropy — all collisions count
        global_count = player.entropy_tracker.count_all(MANA_ENTROPY_WINDOW(15.0))
        entropy = max(1.0 - global_count × MANA_ENTROPY_DECAY(0.20), MANA_ENTROPY_FLOOR(0.30))

    base_restore ×= entropy

    # Restoration multiplier (§2.4)
    base_restore ×= (1 + player.restoration × REST_COLLISION_COEFF(0.0012))

    # Timing scaling (near-expiry = more mana)
    remaining = consumed_status.remaining_duration
    max_dur = consumed_status.max_duration
    timing_mult = 1.0 + (1.0 - remaining / max_dur) × 0.5

    # v12.38: Resonance streak multiplier (§5.7.7) applied after entropy/grace
    if player.resonance_streak and (now - player.resonance_streak.last_resonance_timestamp) <= RESONANCE_STREAK_WINDOW(10.0):
        streak_mult = 1 + min(player.resonance_streak.count, 5) × RESONANCE_STREAK_MANA_BONUS(0.10)
        base_restore ×= streak_mult

    player.mana = min(player.mana + base_restore × timing_mult, player.max_mana)
    player.entropy_tracker.record(collision.id)
    player.grace_window_start = now  # Reset grace window for next collision
```

**Two distinct mana-flow channels now compose cleanly.**

- **Grace window (§3.5a, v12.39):** rhythm-paced collisions ignore entropy. Affects the *entropy multiplier* — 1.0 within grace, normal formula outside.
- **Resonance streak (§5.7.7, v12.38):** resonance-timed collisions within 10s compound a mana-restore bonus. Affects the *streak multiplier* — up to 1.50× at 5 steps.

Both operate on mana restoration, but through different ledger positions (entropy vs streak multiplier), and both reward different skill dimensions (pacing rhythm for grace; resonance-window precision for streak). A player sustaining both — rhythm-paced AND resonance-timed collisions — experiences the fullest mana-flow benefit the system offers. The two systems compose multiplicatively without double-counting because they target distinct multiplier slots.

**Visibility — the red entropy overlay.** Players see entropy as a red overlay on the mana bar, scaling with the current entropy multiplier. The overlay is rendered as a semi-transparent red layer on the right portion of the mana bar that grows as entropy increases (from invisible at entropy=1.0 to ~30% of the bar width at entropy=0.30 floor). Players learn to read the overlay as "my mana restore is currently diminished" without needing a numeric readout.

**Visibility — the green grace tick.** During an active grace window, a soft green tick icon appears above the mana bar with a countdown arc indicating time remaining in the grace window (starts at full arc at window open, shrinks to empty at 2 seconds elapsed). The tick communicates "your next collision within this window gets full mana restore" as a visible affordance.

The green tick appears for 2 seconds after every collision consumes its setup status. If the player triggers another collision within the window, the tick's arc resets to full (the window is extended by the new collision's grace period). If the player does not trigger another collision within the window, the tick fades out and subsequent collisions apply entropy normally.

**No new player input.** The v12.39 revision explicitly does NOT include the original plan draft's "hold swipe to vent entropy" mechanic. Venting would have added a new input gesture and required the player to break combat rhythm to use it — a meta-game layered on top of combat. The grace window achieves the same player-facing goal (reward players who pace their collisions; show entropy cost to players who spam) through *the combat rhythm itself* rather than through a separate input. A player who paces their collisions gets consistent mana restore via grace; a spammer sees the red overlay accumulate and naturally slows down. No new gesture to learn, no new resource to manage.

**High-skill upper bound.** A perfectly rhythm-paced player triggering collisions every 2 seconds or less can maintain near-perpetual grace collisions, effectively bypassing entropy entirely for long sessions of combat. This is a skill-reward differential, and it is within the design intent — INV-02's invariant protects against absolute mana starvation, not against skill-based mana sustainability differentials. The grace period's upper bound is bounded by `COLLISION_MANA_CD(3.0)` — a collision cannot restore mana more frequently than once per 3 seconds regardless of grace or streak state. The cooldown constraint caps the real-world mana-gain rate even when grace is sustained indefinitely.

**INV-02 verification.** Analytical preservation of INV-02 Mana Sustainability follows the v12.38 Resonance Streak precedent. The grace period **improves** mana sustainability vs baseline — a rhythm-paced player restores more mana per collision than a non-rhythm player. INV-02's failure mode is mana starvation during sustained combat; grace period pushes the system *away* from that failure mode. The mechanic cannot cause INV-02 to fail at any grace utilization level. The existing INV-02 bound (regen + collision at entropy floor < swipe spam ceiling × 3.0) still holds — under grace, collision restore is higher than entropy-floor restore, so the inequality holds more comfortably, not less. No Monte Carlo re-derivation required; structurally unchanged.

The original v12.25 INV-02 concern that deferred this entry was specifically about the hold-swipe-vent mechanic's unbounded mana-refresh potential (venting reset entropy at a 20-mana cost, enabling aggressive mana recycling with no cooldown). The revised grace-period mechanic has no such recycling path — the `COLLISION_MANA_CD(3.0)` cooldown caps collision frequency regardless of grace state, and the grace bypass only improves the per-collision restore amount, not the collision frequency.

**Data model.** Single field addition on player combat state: `grace_window_start: number` (server monotonic time of last collision consume). Ephemeral combat state, resets on death/zone transition along with other combat state. The existing `entropy_tracker` field (referenced in §3.5) is unchanged — entropy still accumulates during grace collisions but is bypassed at mana-restore resolution time rather than cleared.

**Tuning.**

| Variable | Default | Min | Max | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| ENTROPY_GRACE_WINDOW | 2.0s | 1.0s | 3.0s | Rolling window for rhythm-paced collisions |

Constants `MANA_VENT_HOLD_TIME` and `MANA_VENT_COST` specified in the v12.25 original draft are **not shipped** in v12.39 — the hold-swipe-vent mechanic was superseded by the grace period design.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.39): Entry 21 was deferred from v12.25 pending INV-02 simulation verification of the original hold-swipe-vent mechanic. The revision replaces venting with a grace-period mechanic that sidesteps the INV-02 concern entirely — venting's failure mode was aggressive mana recycling at a fixed cost with no cooldown, which risked breaching INV-02's sustain bound; the grace period instead bypasses entropy per-collision within a 2-second rhythm window and is bounded above by the existing COLLISION_MANA_CD(3.0) cooldown. No new input gesture, no new resource to manage. The entropy visibility (red overlay) shows the mechanic to the player; the green grace tick shows the reward window; the rhythm-paced player naturally learns the mechanic through play. Together with v12.38's Resonance Streak, v12.39 establishes the full mana-flow design surface: streak amplifies gain on resonance-timing skill; grace bypasses loss on pacing rhythm skill. The two systems compose multiplicatively (streak is a bonus multiplier; grace is an entropy-bypass) without double-counting, and they reward different skill dimensions so players can pursue either or both independently. A player sustaining both experiences the fullest mana-flow affordance the system offers. The mana-bar behavior becomes the flow-state surface: a rhythm-precise player watches their mana bar stay fuller than baseline during extended combat, which is the authentic ARPG flow-state feel ("tireless," "never running out"). The visibility work (red overlay, green tick) respects the v12.29 pedagogical-structure discipline — the mechanic is taught through structure rather than through tutorial. A new player sees the red overlay accumulate when they spam collisions and naturally slows down; a skilled player sees the green tick and learns to chain rhythm-paced collisions for the bypass. Neither state requires explanation; both are legible through play. This is experience-first design at the mana-system layer, paired with v12.38's resonance-streak work at the skill-reward layer. The mana system is now a fully articulated flow-state surface.]`

### §4. Gear System

#### §4.1 Five Equipment Slots

| Slot | Purpose | Stat Gate |
|------|---------|----------|
| **Melee Weapon** | Offensive identity — greatsword or sword | Power (greatsword) or Agility (sword) |
| **Ranged Weapon** | Ranged identity — bow or staff | Agility (bow) or Mind (staff) |
| **Armor** | Defensive survival stats, elemental attunement | Vitality |
| **Amulet** | Offensive single-gem enhancement (10 element→stat mappings) | Mind |
| **Shield** | Defensive single-gem enhancement (10 element→stat mappings) | Endurance |

**Stat-gated equipment.** Each equipment slot requires a minimum Tier 1 stat to equip. `statReq = tierIndex × 10`. A focused build reaches top-tier gear; a balanced build plateaus at mid-tier. This rewards build commitment.

**Coupling with use-trained stats (§1) and the §4.11 loot filter:** the play that drops a player's gear is the same play that trains the stats to wield it. A sword duelist's combat trains Power and Agility, and §4.11's per-player drop filter ensures their drops are weapon types whose gates those stats already meet. The main-weapon loop is self-sustaining — there is no chicken-and-egg trap in normal play. Switching weapon types is a *deliberate transition* with a real cost: the player must acquire the new weapon externally (marketplace, quest, gift, or low-tier crafting) and train the relevant Tier 1 stat through use before they can wield higher-tier versions effectively. This is the marketplace's natural use case in the use-trained system — facilitating cross-build trades for players in transition rather than redistributing off-build drops.

**20-tier crafting system.** Both melee (copper→origin at blacksmith) and ranged (kindling→eternal bark at woodworker) have 20 material tiers, each unlocking every 5 crafting skill levels. **Material tier multipliers** scale weapon damage from 1.0× (copper, Tier 1) to 6.2× (origin, Tier 20). This is the *material* tier multiplier and is the only tier multiplier that scales weapon damage in v12.6 — see §4.4 for the canonical formula and §4.6 for the v12.6 element tier reframing (element tier no longer multiplies damage; it only describes elemental complexity).

**Weapons are vessels (v12.6).** Weapons no longer drop with elements baked in. Every weapon drop is a *base weapon* — material + weapon type, displayed as `<material> <weapon_type>` (e.g., "Steel Greatsword," "Oak Bow") — and is initially **unenchanted**. An unenchanted weapon has no elemental behavior beyond raw physical damage. To gain elemental properties, the player must **enchant** the weapon at the Enchanter (§4.12), then apply a **gem composite** (§4.13) to it. The composite is the weapon's elemental identity. The player can change the composite, decouple it back into individual gems, or leave a weapon unenchanted permanently. A player who never enchants any weapon is a valid build choice — the §10 collision system is opt-in through the gem economy, not mandatory.

**Amulet and shield gem mechanics.** Amulets and shields use **single-gem slotting** rather than the gem composite system used by weapons. The player slots one gem directly into an amulet or shield via the Enchanter (§4.12), and the gem grants a flat stat bonus determined by its element. The two gem application systems share one underlying gem economy — gems harvested from monsters or life skills can be used for either purpose — but they apply differently. Weapons require composites (two gems combined into a bundle, applied as a unit) for their elemental combat behavior; amulets and shields take individual gems directly for their stat enhancement. **Gem level scales the stat bonus** on amulets and shields: a Level 3 Flame gem in an amulet grants a meaningfully larger elemDmg bonus than a Level 1 Flame gem. This creates a real player allocation choice — every gem in inventory is potentially destined for either combat depth (combine into a composite for a weapon) or stat depth (slot into an amulet or shield).

**Amulet crafting.** Gold nuggets (0.1% life skill / 0.01% monster drop) → smelt 5 into gold bar → craft 4 tiers (Simple/Ornate/Regal/Mythic) → slot a single gem at the Enchanter. 10 offensive stat mappings (flame→elemDmg, frost→maxMana, water→hpRegen, venom→maxHP, storm→atkSpd, stone→elemResist, wind→moveSpd, flora→statusDur, dark→critDmg, light→staminaRegen).

**Shield crafting.** Same 20 ore tiers as melee weapons. Single gem slotted at the Enchanter for 10 defensive stats (flame→blockReduc, frost→thornsDmg, water→hpOnBlock, venom→statusResist, storm→counterDmg, stone→flatDmgReduc, wind→dodgeDist, flora→regenRate, dark→deathSave, light→shieldRegen).

**Hardening.** A separate dimension on weapons that increases base damage effectiveness through risky Blacksmith upgrades. See §4.6c for the canonical mechanics. Hardening is the endgame Blacksmith activity — the *mortal* lottery dopamine layer of gear progression, in cosmological opposition to Godly drops (the *divine* lottery, §4.6b.1). Hardening is independent of enchanting and gem application: a weapon can be unenchanted and still hardened, enchanted and unhardened, or both.

**Salvage station.** Multi-step disassembly for non-Godly weapons: (1) decouple any applied gem composite back into its constituent gems via the Enchanter (returned to player at minor cost — see §4.12); (2) the now-empty enchanted weapon (or originally-unenchanted weapon) goes through salvage; (3) extract any gem in an amulet or shield via the Enchanter (returns the gem with a small gold cost scaled by gem level); (4) salvage the base item for 60% material return, item destroyed. The hardening investment is partially recoverable via the salvage essence system (§4.6c.3). **Godly weapons are salvage-rejected** and cannot be processed through this station — see §4.6b.1.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Five slots with stat gates create build identity through gear. A Power build equips greatswords and heavy shields. A Mind build equips staves and amulets. You cannot equip everything — you specialize. The v12.6 split between weapon-composite gems and amulet/shield single gems gives the gem economy a meaningful allocation question: every gem you find is potentially destined for either combat depth (combine it into a composite for a weapon) or stat depth (slot it directly into an amulet or shield). The two paths consume gems differently and reward different playstyles, but they share one resource pool. This is the same design instinct as the use-trained stat system — player decisions about how to spend their accumulated investment matter, and the system gives them meaningful choices rather than predetermined paths.

The "weapons are vessels" reframing in v12.6 is the cleanest possible separation of concerns: the weapon supplies physical combat (auto-attack, swipe, AOE pattern, base damage, attack speed, weapon type identity) and the gems supply elemental behavior (status application, collision triggers, resonance windows, status durations). Earlier versions of this document conflated the two — a "Flame Greatsword" was a single object that bundled physical and elemental identity. v12.6 splits them into two layers the player can manipulate independently. This means players can change their elemental identity without acquiring a new weapon, and they can change their weapon without losing their elemental investment.]`

#### §4.2 Weapons

**Weapons are vessels.** A weapon's identity at the moment of drop is purely physical: a *material* (copper through origin, 20 tiers) and a *type* (Greatsword, Sword, Bow, or Staff). That is the entire weapon identity at drop time. Weapons do not drop with elements baked in. They acquire elemental properties only when the player chooses to **enchant** the weapon (§4.12) and apply a **gem composite** (§4.13). A player who never enchants a weapon will use it as a purely physical instrument, and that is a valid playstyle — the §10 collision system is opt-in through the gem economy, not mandatory.

This is a deliberate change from earlier versions of this document, which framed weapons as *being* their elements. v12.6 separates the physical layer from the elemental layer: the weapon is a *thing the player owns*, the elemental behavior is a *thing the player chooses to add*. The two layers can be modified independently — a player can replace their weapon's gem composite without replacing the weapon, and they can replace their weapon while keeping their composites for the next vessel.

**Dual-element behavior emerges from gem composites, not from the weapon itself.** When a player applies a composite of two different gems (e.g., Flame + Frost) to an enchanted weapon, the weapon becomes capable of dual-element behavior: auto-attacks apply the first gem's element status, swipes trigger the second gem's element, and collisions fire automatically when the two statuses meet on a target. The §10 collision math is unchanged from prior versions — the only thing that changed is *how* the elements got onto the weapon (player choice via gem combination, rather than drop accident).

**Pure-element behavior emerges from same-element composites.** A composite combining two gems of the same element (Flame + Flame) creates a *pure* elemental composite: when applied to a weapon, both auto-attack and swipe carry the same status, with empowered/concentrated effects (longer duration, higher status damage) and *no collision possibility*. The tradeoff is the same as previous versions: sustained status pressure vs. burst collision damage. The difference is the player makes the tradeoff *deliberately* by choosing which gems to combine, rather than discovering it on a drop.

**Element orientation is part of composite application.** When the player applies a two-different-element composite to a weapon, they choose which element occupies the auto-attack slot and which occupies the swipe slot. This determines the volatile/stable direction (§4.7). Orientation can be changed later by decoupling the composite at the Enchanter and recombining it in the opposite order, with a minor cost (§4.12). Under v12.6, every volatile/stable orientation is an *informed player choice*, never a drop accident.

**Weapons scale with player level permanently.** Material tier multiplier is permanent. A weapon found at Level 5 is still effective at Level 50 if its material tier and the player's investment (hardening, applied gem composite) keep pace with their power band.

**Weapon drops match your equipped weapon type.** Kill with a Greatsword, drops are Greatswords. Per-player independent in parties.

| Type | Range | Auto-Attack | Swipe Trigger | Identity |
|------|-------|-------------|---------------|----------|
| Greatsword | Melee | Single target, slow, heavy | Single target burst | Heavy hitter. Grand Slam specialist. |
| Sword | Melee | Single target, fast | Single target | Fast pressure. Rapid status application. |
| Bow | Ranged | Single target projectile | Single target | Precision at distance. Positional safety. |
| Staff | Ranged | Single target projectile | AOE (up to 3 targets) | Magic. Area detonation. Variance roll (0.75–1.25×). |

**Staff variance:** Single target setup, one swipe detonates multiple prepared enemies. Rhythm is slow-slow-slow-BOOM. Variance roll makes every detonation a gamble. This IS magic.

| Type | Base Dmg | Speed (atk/s) |
|------|:--------:|:--------------:|
| Greatsword | 48 | 0.7 |
| Sword | 32 | 1.4 |
| Bow | 35 | 1.2 |
| Staff | 41 | 1.0 |

**Unarmed:** Base=1, Speed=1.0, Melee.

#### §4.2a Weapon Type Details

**Greatsword:** Slow, devastating single-target melee. Highest base damage. Grand Slam specialist — the critical kill fantasy weapon. Auto-attacks are wide swings with a SWING_ARC(1.2 radians) visual sweep.

**Sword:** Fast melee pressure. Lower per-hit damage but rapid status application. Sword's higher attack speed means more auto-attack element applications per second, building collision setups faster.

**Bow:** Ranged single-target projectile. Auto-attacks fire arrows in the aim direction. Projectile speed: ARROW_SPEED(8.0 u/tick). Range: ARROW_RANGE(200u, despawns after). When right stick is held, a dashed LOS line extends across the visible area showing exact aim trajectory.

**Staff:** Ranged single-target auto-attack projectile (like Bow). But the swipe trigger is AOE — it detonates collisions on up to STAFF_AOE_CAP(3) enemies within a STAFF_AOE_CONE(45°) cone in the aim direction, within STAFF_AOE_RANGE(80u). The variance roll (0.75–1.25×) applies independently per target. When right stick is held, a translucent cone indicator replaces the Bow's line, showing the swipe's target zone. The rhythm is: tag one, tag two, tag three (auto-attacks), then one swipe detonates all three.

#### §4.3 Armor Attunement (v12.x CANONICAL, v13 REPLACEMENT — Use-Trained Affinity)

**v13 REPLACEMENT.** Canonical "attunement chosen at armor creation" is replaced by **use-trained attunement**: armor develops attunement based on damage absorbed over time. Worn through many Flame encounters, armor develops Flame resistance. The armor's defensive identity emerges from the fights it survives.

**Craftable override preserved.** Players who want to set a specific attunement at creation time can still do so via the Enchanter (§4.12) — the crafted attunement acts as a starting override that the use-trained system will slowly drift away from if the armor is worn in mismatched combat. For most players, the use-trained path is the default: buy or drop armor without pre-selected attunement, wear it in combat, and watch the attunement emerge.

**Attunement drift formula:**

```
# Called on each damage-taken event
func on_armor_damage_taken(armor, damage_element, damage_amount):
    # Each damage event nudges armor attunement toward the damage element
    # Amount of nudge scales with damage severity and current attunement stability
    damage_fraction = damage_amount / armor.max_hp_absorbed_per_tick
    nudge = ARMOR_ATTUNEMENT_LEARN_RATE(0.005) × damage_fraction
    
    # Move armor's primary attunement coefficient toward damage_element
    for element in ALL_ELEMENTS:
        if element == damage_element:
            armor.attunement[element] += nudge
        else:
            armor.attunement[element] -= nudge / 9  # Distributed across other 9 elements
    
    # Normalize so total attunement sums to 1.0
    armor.attunement = normalize(armor.attunement)
    
    # Compute dominant element (used for §4.3 canonical +20%/+20% bonus):
    armor.dominant_attunement = argmax(armor.attunement)
```

**Dominant element determines canonical bonus.** The armor's *dominant* attunement (element with highest coefficient) determines the §4.3 canonical matching-bonus (20% offense + 20% defense with matching-element weapon). The dominant element is computed on the fly from the attunement vector.

**Drift rate.** ARMOR_ATTUNEMENT_LEARN_RATE(0.005) is calibrated so armor drifts meaningfully over ~200-500 damage events (dozens of combat encounters) from baseline-neutral to clearly dominant toward the most-encountered element. This is slow enough that armor doesn't attunement-swap every encounter but fast enough that a player's chosen combat zones shape their armor's identity over weeks of play.

**Visible state.** Armor's attunement vector is inspectable via the Character > Equipment panel. Canonical dominant-element display preserved; v13 adds a small distribution indicator showing the full vector for players curious about their armor's history. Armor description text (per §0.1 tonal rule) varies:
- Grave: *"This greaves has witnessed flame. It remembers."*
- Wry: *"Your armor has absorbed a lot of fire. It has opinions about fire."*

**Per §0.2 progressive UI revelation**, the attunement vector display appears only after the armor has reached a clear dominant attunement (threshold: dominant coefficient > 0.4). Before that, the armor shows "Unattuned" or "Still learning" rather than a partial vector.

**Crafted override path.** An Enchanter-crafted attunement sets the starting attunement vector with the chosen element at 0.8 coefficient (other elements at 0.022 each). The use-trained drift then slowly modifies this baseline — a Flame-enchanted greaves worn in Frost combat will slowly drift toward Frost over time, eventually flipping dominance if worn consistently enough.

**Migration.** Existing characters with canonical-attuned armor have their attunement vector initialized with the canonical element at 0.8, other elements at 0.022 each (equivalent to a crafted override). The use-trained drift begins operating from that baseline.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): One-time-pick attunement conflicts with the game's use-trained thesis — every other stat system (§1.1 use-trained stats, §4.11b weapon history, etc.) responds to what the character actually does, but canonical armor's attunement is fixed at creation. Making attunement responsive to accumulated exposure extends use-training into equipment. Your armor's defensive identity emerges from the fights it has survived. The crafted-override path preserves player agency for those who want to pre-specify (for example, a player who knows they're about to farm Ember Fields can crafted-override to Flame for the initial encounters); the use-trained drift means even pre-specified armor eventually reflects actual use. The drift rate is calibrated to produce meaningful emergence over weeks without feeling unstable within sessions. Dominant-element visibility per §0.2 progressive UI means new Bros don't see a vector they don't yet understand — they see a clear dominant label once the armor has committed to an identity.]`

#### §4.4 Weapon Damage Formula (CANONICAL)

`effective_weapon_base = (weapon_base + hardness × HARDNESS_BASE_BONUS(5)) × quality_mult`

`weapon_damage = (effective_weapon_base + Power × POWER_DMG_COEFF(0.8)) × material_tier_mult`

**Staff Magic Damage:** `weapon_damage = (effective_weapon_base + Power × POWER_DMG_COEFF(0.8)) × material_tier_mult × Roll(0.75, 1.25)`

`weapon_base` is FLAT from the §4.2 table. Does NOT scale with level. Level progression comes from stat growth (+5 pts/level across both tiers), material tier multipliers (§4.1), hardness investment (§4.6c), and quality drops (§4.6b).

**`material_tier_mult` is the §4.1 material tier multiplier**, scaling from 1.0× (Tier 1, copper/kindling) to 6.2× (Tier 20, origin/eternal bark). It is the only tier multiplier that scales weapon damage in v12.6. **Element tier no longer multiplies damage** — see §4.6 for the v12.6 reframing. Earlier versions of this document had a separate element tier multiplier (1.0× / 1.5× / 2.25× / 3.0× for Common/Elemental/Fusion/Shift), and §4.9's `tier_multiplier` field carried this value. **Both are deleted in v12.6.** Element tier under v12.6 is purely descriptive of a weapon's elemental complexity (how many gems are applied via §4.13) and grants no damage multiplier of any kind. The value of elements is in what they *do* in combat (status application, collision damage, resonance windows, combo bonuses, mana restore via §5.7), not in a flat scalar on auto-attack damage.

**Hardness contribution.** Each hardness level adds HARDNESS_BASE_BONUS(5) flat to the weapon's effective base damage *before* quality and material tier multipliers apply. A Hardness 5 weapon adds +25 to its base, which then multiplies through quality and material tier. This means hardness becomes more valuable on higher-quality, higher-material-tier weapons — Hardness 5 on a Godly Tier 20 origin sword is dramatically more valuable than Hardness 5 on a Normal Tier 1 copper sword, which is the right behavior for a 1-in-12-million achievement. Hardness mechanics are canonical in §4.6c.

**Quality multiplier.** Each weapon has a quality grade (Normal/Rare/Elite/Godly) that multiplies effective base damage. Multipliers: Normal 1.00× / Rare 1.20× / Elite 1.50× / **Godly 3.00×**. See §4.6b for the canonical quality table and drop rates. Godly weapons are exempt from §6.5 audit gates because their statistical population is negligible (~80 per server per year) and their balance impact at the population level is effectively zero.

**v12.6 baseline damage adjustment, v12.8 audit pass.** Removing the element tier multiplier reduces the *absolute* damage output of weapons that previously would have rolled Elemental, Fusion, or Shift tiers. A v12.5 Fusion weapon hit at 2.25× the base of a Common weapon of the same material; under v12.6 it hits at exactly the same base as the Common (the elemental gems supply elemental behavior, not damage). The v12.6 changelog flagged this as requiring a §6.5 monster HP rebalance, but the v12.8 audit pass found that no rebalance is needed — the v12.6 formula produces kill counts within the §6.5 audit ranges when computed against the brute archetype with level-appropriate median-Power players. The earlier "v12.5 Fusion weapons hit 2.25× harder" intuition was correct in direction but, when the actual numbers were finally computed against level-appropriate Power values, v12.5 was *underkilling* its own audit ranges (brutes died in roughly 0.81 / 1.10 / 1.38 / 1.72 hits at the four audit levels, well under the audit's 2-5 hit targets). Removing the element tier multiplier in v12.6 brought kill counts up to 1.83 / 2.46 / 3.10 / 3.87 hits, which falls within the audit range at every level (with the L15 audit gate widened from 2-3 to 1-3 to match the §6.3 power-fantasy design intent). See §6.5 for the canonical v12.8 calibration with full reproducible math, and §22 INV-30 for the band-widening justification.

`[DESIGN INTENT: The v12.5 element tier multiplier was doing two jobs at once — it rewarded players for engaging with elemental complexity AND it scaled damage to keep up with monster HP. v12.6 separates those concerns. Elemental engagement is rewarded by the *combat behavior* it unlocks (collisions, status application, resonance, mana restore), which is large and meaningful but does not appear as a flat multiplier on auto-attack damage. Damage scaling against monster HP is the job of material tier alone. Each system now does one job, and the relationships between systems are legible.

The Hardness flat-add at the source is unchanged from v12.4. Hardness adds to weapon_base rather than multiplying it, which keeps the contribution legible (Hardness 3 on a Tier 5 weapon adds 15 base, period — the player can do the math) while still letting hardness compound through the quality and material tier multipliers. The compounding is what makes high-tier hardness valuable; the additivity at the source is what keeps it readable. Power scaling is unchanged — your character is unchanged by hardness, only the weapon is.

Worth flagging for the implementation team: monster HP rebalancing is a real audit pass, not a quick number tweak. The §6.5 kill-time tables need fresh modeling under the v12.6 damage formula, and the §24 reference build damage estimates need full recalibration. Both are scheduled for later phases of the v12.6 edit.]`

#### §4.5 Attack Speed (CANONICAL)

`effective_attack_speed = weapon_base_speed × (1 + Agility × ATTACK_SPEED_AGI(0.001))`

Hard cap: ATTACK_SPEED_CAP(3.0). Affects auto-attacks only. Swipe uses its own cooldown.

**Swipe Cooldown:** SWIPE_COOLDOWN(2.0s). Fixed, not affected by attack speed or CDR. The swipe is a deliberate detonation, not a spammable ability.

**Swipe Mana Cost:** SWIPE_MANA_COST_BASE(15) + weapon_tier × SWIPE_MANA_TIER_MULT(3). Higher-tier weapons cost more mana to trigger.

#### §4.6 Element Tier (v12.6 Reframed)

Element Tier is no longer a *property of the drop*. It is a **derived state** of an enchanted weapon, computed from whatever gem composite (§4.13) is currently applied to it. A weapon's element tier can change at any time the player applies, decouples, or replaces a composite. It is purely descriptive — element tier grants **no damage multiplier** under v12.6 (see §4.4 for the damage formula and the explicit deletion notice).

| Tier | Nameplate Color | Composition | Identity |
|------|:---------------:|:-----------:|----------|
| Common | Grey | Unenchanted weapon, OR enchanted with no composite applied | Basic. No element. Pure physical damage. A valid build choice — the §10 collision system is opt-in. |
| Elemental | Blue | Enchanted weapon with a single-element composite (one gem) OR a same-element composite (two gems of the same type) | One element active on the weapon. Single-status pressure, no self-collision possibility. Pure-element composites grant concentrated/empowered status effects. |
| Fusion | Purple | Enchanted weapon with a two-element composite (two gems of different types) | Two different elements active on the weapon — one as auto-attack setup, one as swipe trigger. Full collision access. |
| Shift | Prismatic | Enchanted weapon with a Shift gem applied (§4.8) | Adaptive elemental behavior from an Ascendant-area Shift gem. The gem cycles through 3 elements based on combat context. Cannot be combined with regular elements. |

**Element tier is computed at query time, not stored.** The §4.9 gear data model has `element_tier` as a derived field — its value comes from inspecting the `applied_composite` field rather than being stored independently. This guarantees that element tier is always consistent with what the player currently has applied to the weapon. There is no risk of state desync.

**Element tier does not multiply damage.** This is the most important v12.6 change to this section. Earlier versions of this document (v12.4 and v12.5) had a multiplier column on this table — Common 1.0×, Elemental 1.5×, Fusion 2.25×, Shift 3.0× — and these multipliers were silently consumed by the §4.9 `tier_multiplier` field and the §4.4 weapon damage formula. **The multiplier column and the `tier_multiplier` field are both deleted in v12.6.** A Fusion-tier weapon does not hit harder than a Common weapon of the same material, quality, and hardness. The value of elements is in what they *do* (status application, collision damage, resonance windows, combo bonuses, mana restore via §5.7) — not in a flat scalar on auto-attack damage.

`[DESIGN INTENT: The v12.6 reframing of element tier is a structural simplification that resolves a quiet inconsistency in earlier versions. The v12.5 doc had element tier as both a *drop property* (you found a Fusion weapon) and a *damage scalar* (Fusion weapons hit at 2.25× base). These two roles were doing different jobs and conflating them produced design friction: players who wanted to engage with elements were rewarded twice (once with the elemental behavior they wanted, once with a damage multiplier they didn't earn), and the loot table had to roll element tier as a hidden affix on weapon drops. The gem refactor cleanly separates the two concerns. Elements are gems the player chooses. Damage scaling is material tier alone. Each system does one job.

The element tier table is preserved as a *vocabulary* — Common/Elemental/Fusion/Shift remain meaningful descriptive labels for "what kind of elemental setup does this weapon currently have." Players will still talk about their "Fusion Greatsword" or their "Elemental Bow." The labels are useful even though they no longer carry mechanical weight beyond their composition. This is the right tradeoff: keep the language players are familiar with, change the underlying mechanics to be cleaner.]`

#### §4.6b Quality Grade (CANONICAL)

Every weapon drop has a quality grade independent of its element tier and material tier. Quality is a flat multiplier on the weapon's effective base damage (see §4.4) and is permanent — quality cannot be upgraded or downgraded. The quality you find is the quality you keep.

| Grade | Border (color) | Border (shape) | Multiplier | Drop Rate (per weapon drop) | Identity |
|-------|:-------------:|:--------------:|:----------:|:---------------------------:|----------|
| Normal | (no border) | (none) | 1.00× | ~90.0998% | The baseline. Most weapon drops. The §4.2 base damage table assumes Normal quality. |
| Rare | Silver | Line | 1.20× | 9% | Better. 1 in 11 weapon drops. Players see them regularly but they remain genuinely meaningful. |
| Elite | Gold | Notched (corners cut) | 1.50× | 0.9% | Significant. 1 in 111 weapon drops. A few per session of dedicated farming. Specific marketplace listings. |
| Godly | Prismatic shimmer | Radiant (small rays) | **3.00×** | **1 in 400,000 weapon drops** | The rarest object in any game ever made. Approximately 80 enter circulation per year on a 1,000-player server. Most players will never see one in their lifetime. |

**Quality is per-weapon, deterministic, and identity-preserving.** Two Rare Steel Greatswords are *identical* — there is no within-grade roll, no random affix, no min-max math. If you have one Rare Steel Greatsword and find another, you don't need to inspect it. The dopamine of the drop is in the *grade*, not in a roll. The marketplace stratifies cleanly: Normals are commodity stacks, Rares are common upgrades, Elites are individually listed items, and Godlies are the marketplace's apex tier, traded with full server-authoritative lineage tracking under v12.7 (see §4.6b.1 sacred properties and §17.4.1 lineage system).

**Quality borders use both color AND shape** to ensure colorblind accessibility and to mirror the §10.1 element shape system. Color is reinforcement; shape is the primary signal. A player who cannot distinguish silver from gold can still distinguish a line border from a notched border. The shape progression itself communicates rarity (none → line → notched → radiant), each step visually richer than the last.

**Quality multiplies effective base damage, not the full damage formula.** See §4.4 — the multiplier applies to `(weapon_base + hardness × HARDNESS_BASE_BONUS)` *before* Power scaling and the tier multiplier. A Godly weapon makes the *weapon* hit harder; it does not change how your Power scales. This keeps the meaning of "Godly" intuitive: the weapon itself is exceptional, your character is unchanged.

**Quality drop rates apply to *all* weapon drops regardless of source.** Monster drops, dungeon chests, life skill outputs, crafting outputs, and quest rewards all roll quality at the same rates. Crafting a weapon at the Blacksmith rolls quality on completion — a high-skill Blacksmith does not get better quality odds, only better material tier access. Quality is the loot system's lottery; it is not improvable through skill investment.

##### §4.6b.i Godly — The Sacred Tier (CANONICAL)

Godly is the only weapon grade governed by rules separate from the rest of the loot system. It is not "rare endgame loot." It is the **rarest object in any game ever made**, and it is treated by the design as a divine event rather than a drop tier.

**Drop rate.** 1 in 400,000 weapon drops. Combined with the per-monster weapon drop rate (~1 in 200 for a same-level squishy, varying by monster type), the *per-kill* compound rate against valid threats is approximately 1 in 80,000,000. Across a server of 1,000 active players each playing 2 hours per day, this produces approximately 80 Godly weapons per year — about one every 4-5 days entering circulation. The rate is deliberately tuned so that **a dedicated player has roughly a 5% lifetime chance of ever receiving a Godly drop across years of play.** Most players never see one. Some players see one. A handful, over a server's lifetime, see two. The motif (see Creative Reference §1.7a) plays often enough that players recognize it, rare enough that it is never expected.

**Godly is the perpetual carrot for every player in every phase.** Because Godly fires within the standard weapon drop event, every weapon drop a player has ever received carried a tiny chance of being Godly. A Level 3 player killing slimes has the same per-drop Godly chance as a Level 100 player farming Apex bosses — the only difference is how often they trigger weapon drops. The pure-build player at Level 30 with locked stats has the same Godly access as the Hardness 5 farmer at Level 100. **Godly transcends the build, the level, the playstyle, and the alignment.** It is the universe occasionally handing a player something impossible, regardless of where they are in the game.

**Godly weapons are sacred objects (v12.7 reframed).** Godly weapons are protected by a set of rules that no other item in the game shares. The protections are properties of the *object itself* — they travel with the weapon regardless of who currently holds it. A Godly weapon cannot be:

- **Lost on death in any zone**, including Lawless Land and any future PvP-enabled zone — see §5.5 and §26.4 protection cross-references. The weapon stays in the holder's inventory through any death event.
- **Taken by the Ferryman** — see §33 and Creative Reference §8.5a for the canonical Ferryman line. The Ferryman defers to the gods' touch on the *object*, not to any property of the holder.
- **Destroyed by salvage** — the salvage station refuses Godly weapons.
- **Destroyed by failed hardening** — Godly weapons can be hardened normally per §4.6c, and failure resets hardness to 0 like any other weapon, but the Godly weapon itself survives indefinitely.
- **Subject to stat gates** — see §4.1 stat gate exemption rule and the dedicated paragraph below.
- **Subject to §6.5 audit gates** — Godly weapons are exempt from kill-time validation as a special case (statistical population is negligible at ~80 per server per year).

**Godly weapons CAN be traded (v12.7).** The original v12.5 binding rule has been removed. A player who receives a Godly weapon may sell it, gift it, marketplace-list it, or trade it directly to another player. The new owner inherits all of the protection rules above — the protections travel with the object, not with any specific player. The Godly weapon's *original recipient* (the player who first revealed it from a mystery item) is recorded permanently in the §17.4 cryptographic provenance ledger and never changes; the weapon's *current owner* is mutable and updates on every transfer. See §17.4 for the canonical lineage system.

**Why this changed in v12.7.** Under v12.5, the binding rule served the design's "the gods chose you" framing — if you had a Godly, you were one of the few players the gods had visited, and the proof was in your inventory permanently. But the binding also locked unlucky players out of ever owning a Godly through any path other than the lottery, and the v12.5 design treated the marketplace as needing protection from a top-tier object that would dominate it. The v12.7 reframing accepts the marketplace consequence and converts it into a feature: Godly weapons become the marketplace's apex, dedicated wealth-accumulators have a real path to ownership, and Godly weapons gain *family trees* through the lineage system. The cosmology adapts cleanly — the gods still visit specific players occasionally, the recipient of a divine encounter is permanent and recorded forever, and the resulting object subsequently circulates among mortals through normal economies. The divine encounter is unchanged. What changed is whether the gift can be passed to others. The answer is now yes.

**Godly weapons ignore stat gates.** A Godly weapon can be equipped by any player who currently holds it, regardless of the player's trained Tier 1 stats and regardless of the weapon's material tier. A pure-build player with locked Power 30 who acquires a Godly Tier 18 greatsword (whether by receiving it from the gods or by buying it on the marketplace) can wield it the moment they pick it up. **The weapon is the gift; the right to use it is part of the gift, and the gift transfers with the object.** This is the only exception to the §4.1 stat gate rule and it is intentional — the divine framing requires it. A player should never hold a Godly weapon they cannot use.

**Godly weapons are exempt from the §6.5 audit gates.** The kill-time validation in §6.5 assumes weapons within normal balance bands. Godly weapons at 3.0× base damage fall outside these bands by design. Because the population of Godly weapons is statistically negligible (~80 per server per year, distributed across 1,000+ players), they have effectively zero impact on aggregate balance. The audit gates exclude them as a special case rather than trying to model their impact.

**Anti-cheat: cryptographic provenance and lineage.** Every Godly drop event is signed by the server with a hash committing to the kill, server tick, monster ID, and original recipient ID. The weapon stores this signature. Every transfer of ownership updates the current_owner field and appends to the immutable ownership_history log. Every interaction with a Godly weapon (display, equip, inspect, trade, render in another player's view) verifies the signature against the server's authoritative ledger and confirms the current owner matches the holder. **Any Godly weapon without matching server-side provenance is rejected at every interaction surface.** A client exploit cannot fake a Godly drop or a fraudulent transfer because the server is the only entity that can produce a valid signature and the only entity that can update the ownership log. See §17.4 for the canonical anti-cheat specification including the lineage system.

**The cosmology of Godly (v12.7 reframing).** Godly weapons are gifts from the gods. **The gods exist in this game's worldbuilding *only* through Godly drops.** They never appear, never speak, never have temples, never have priests. They are not part of the alignment system. They are not addressed in any quest. They are not depicted in any art. They are present in the world *exclusively* through the gifts they occasionally place into players' hands. The original recipient of a Godly weapon is the only person who has ever encountered these gods directly, and the encounter consists of a single moment: a question mark appearing on the ground that they revealed. **Subsequent owners** of the same Godly weapon are not divine recipients — they are mortals who acquired a divine artifact through normal economies. Both kinds of ownership are real. The original recipient's encounter is permanent and recorded in the lineage forever; the current owner's relationship to the weapon is whatever they make of it. See Creative Reference §8.12a for the full cosmological framing and §8.12b for the gods' canonical worldbuilding statement.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.7 update): The v12.5 binding rule was the design's first answer to "what does it mean to have a Godly weapon?" — the answer was "it means the gods chose you, and the proof is permanent and yours alone." The v12.7 reframing accepts that this answer was incomplete: it locked out players who never got lucky, and it kept the most powerful objects in the game out of the marketplace where they could become genuine social events. The new answer is more complete: the gods still chose someone, that someone is recorded forever in the lineage, and the gift they received subsequently moves through mortal hands. A player who buys a Godly is not pretending the gods chose them — they are a mortal who acquired a divine artifact, and the lineage tells them whose encounter produced the weapon they now hold. This is more honest because it preserves the divine encounter as a historical fact while letting the object participate in the world after the encounter. A 5-year-old server will have Godly weapons whose lineages stretch back to their original recipients on day 7 of the server, with multiple owners over the years, each transfer recorded forever. This is *more* meaningful, not less, than the v12.5 framing where the weapon and the player were locked together.

The protection rules other than binding are all preserved because they serve different purposes. Indestructibility on death exists because the weapon is sacred, not because the holder is. Untouchability by the Ferryman exists because the gods touched the object, not because they touched the holder. Salvage rejection exists because divine artifacts cannot be unmade. Stat gate exemption exists because the gift confers the right to wield, and the gift is the object itself, not an attribute of the recipient. Audit gate exemption exists because the population math doesn't change with tradability. Every protection that survives v12.7 survives because its justification is *about the weapon*, not *about the holder*. Binding was the only protection whose justification was *about the holder*, and it is the only one that v12.7 deletes. The cosmology is internally consistent under both versions, but v12.7 is more honest about which protections are properties of the object and which were properties of the relationship.]`

##### §4.6b.ii Mystery Reveals — Tiered RNG Visualization (CANONICAL)

Drops at Rare grade and above appear in the world as **mystery icons** (animated question marks) rather than their final visual identity. The grade is hidden until the player picks up the item and watches the reveal animation play out. Normal drops appear immediately as their normal weapon icons — no mystery, no animation.

**The mystery icon shows:**
- The weapon type silhouette (greatsword / sword / bow / staff) — the player knows what *kind* of weapon dropped
- A subtle pulse animation to draw the eye
- Nothing about the quality grade — Rare, Elite, and Godly all appear identically as question marks

**The mystery icon does NOT show:** the quality border, the material tier coloring, or any indication of grade. From the icon alone, a Rare and a Godly are indistinguishable. The player only knows they have *something at least Rare*, and the question is *what*.

**The reveal is a tiered RNG animation that honestly visualizes the underlying probability distribution.** Under v12.6, the reveal is no longer a short grade-specific flourish. It is a multi-stage animation in which the player *watches the actual roll happen in real time*, across a window long enough to register individual ticks but short enough to preserve farming pacing. The reveal takes ~9 seconds per stage, with the number of stages determined by how the roll escalates.

The underlying mechanism: when a weapon drop rolls Rare or above at drop time, the server records the true quality grade. On pickup, the server *streams* a sequence of visible roll ticks to the client — each tick is a real probability sample drawn from the underlying distribution, and the final tick of the animation resolves to the pre-committed true grade. The player cannot distinguish between "the server is showing me the real rolls" and "the server is showing me roll outcomes calibrated to land on the pre-committed result" because the two produce mathematically equivalent animations. The client cannot fake escalations because the server controls the tick sequence. See §17.4 for the anti-cheat architecture.

**Stage 1: The Rare check (~9 seconds total).** The weapon-type silhouette icon cycles rapidly between two colors: **silver** (Rare — the floor) and **gold** (Elite or better — escalation). The cycling speed is approximately **12 ticks per second**, fast enough to feel like real RNG in motion and just slow enough for the player to perceive individual color flashes. Over ~8 seconds of fast spin, the player sees roughly **96 visible roll ticks**. The frequency of gold flashes in this stage reflects the actual conditional probability of escalating past Rare (~9% per tick, since Rare is 9% of the 9.9% Rare-or-better population), so the player sees gold flash somewhere around 8–10 times during the ~8-second fast spin. Each gold flash is a small "wait, did it just" moment — the animation keeps rolling, but the player has *seen* that the lottery can still escalate.

The final ~1 second of Stage 1 is the **deceleration**. The spin slows dramatically, and the last 3 ticks happen visibly and discretely. Each tick lands on either silver or gold. The player watches each one resolve. This is the moment of truth for the Rare stage.

- **If the final tick of Stage 1 is silver:** The reveal resolves as **Rare**. The icon stops on silver, a soft chime plays, the silver line border appears around the icon, and the reveal ends. Total time from pickup: ~9 seconds. The weapon enters the player's inventory.
- **If the final tick of Stage 1 is gold:** The reveal escalates immediately into Stage 2. The gold tick is the bridge — the animation does not pause. The color cycling transitions from silver-vs-gold to gold-vs-prismatic, signaling to the player that they have passed the Rare threshold and are now rolling for Elite-or-Godly.

**Stage 2: The Elite / Godly check (~9 seconds total, starting immediately from the Stage 1 escalation).** The icon cycles between **gold** (Elite — the new floor) and **prismatic** (Godly — escalation). Same ~12 ticks/second speed, same ~96 visible roll ticks over 8 seconds of fast spin, same final ~1 second of deceleration showing 3 discrete final ticks.

The frequency of prismatic flashes in this stage reflects the actual conditional probability of Godly within the Elite tier: approximately **1 in 3,600 per tick** (since Godly is 0.00025% of the 0.90025% Elite-or-Godly population). Over 96 visible ticks, the player will see a prismatic flash during the animation roughly **1 in 36 Elite reveals**. The vast majority of Stage 2 animations will show 96 consecutive gold ticks with no prismatic flashes at all — the player watches gold flash 96 times in a row and the result is Elite.

This rarity is not a bug. It is the design. The prismatic flash is genuinely rare within the animation because the underlying event is genuinely rare. On the rare occasions when the player *does* see a prismatic flash mid-spin, it is a real moment — they noticed it, their heart jumped, and they are now watching every subsequent tick with heightened attention even though the odds are still overwhelmingly that the final tick will land on gold.

The final ~1 second of Stage 2 is the deceleration. The spin slows and the last 3 ticks happen discretely:

- **If the final tick of Stage 2 is gold:** The reveal resolves as **Elite**. The icon stops on gold, a layered gold chime plays, the gold notched border appears around the icon, and the reveal ends. Total time from pickup: ~18 seconds. The weapon enters the player's inventory.
- **If the final tick of Stage 2 is prismatic:** The reveal escalates immediately into Stage 3.

**Stage 3: The Godly finale (~5 seconds).** The full screen-hijacking Godly reveal animation. The question mark explodes outward in a prismatic burst, the screen flashes prismatic for ~0.5 seconds, the dedicated Godly motif plays (4 seconds — see §4.6c.5 and Creative Reference §1.7a), the weapon's material and type appear in massive text on screen (e.g., "STEEL GREATSWORD"), and the player's input is locked for the full ~5 seconds. The moment is theirs to witness, not to interrupt. After the animation, the weapon enters the player's inventory bound to them forever (§4.6b.i). Total time from pickup: ~23 seconds.

**Total reveal times by final grade:**

| Final Grade | Stages Completed | Total Reveal Time | Player Experience |
|:-----------:|:----------------:|:-----------------:|-------------------|
| Rare | Stage 1 only | ~9s | Watched silver/gold cycling, deceleration lands on silver, done. |
| Elite | Stage 1 + Stage 2 | ~18s | Stage 1 escalated to gold, Stage 2 showed gold/prismatic cycling, deceleration lands on gold, done. |
| Godly | Stage 1 + Stage 2 + Stage 3 | ~23s | Both stages escalated, final deceleration lands on prismatic, the screen hijacks, the motif plays, the weapon arrives. |

**Hybrid input model — the reveal does not uniformly interrupt combat.** Because Rare reveals are relatively common at high levels of play (~1 in 11 weapon drops, which at 200 drops/hour is ~18 Rare reveals per hour, or roughly one every 3 minutes), a full screen-hijacking reveal every time would be untenably disruptive. The reveal animation therefore uses a **hybrid input model** that scales visual prominence with the actual rarity of the event:

- **Stage 1 (Rare check):** The reveal animation plays in a **non-blocking corner of the screen** — a small panel in the upper-right (configurable via settings) showing the mystery icon cycling between silver and gold. The player can keep moving, fighting, dodging, and using skills during the Stage 1 animation. Combat input is fully available. The player can glance at the corner occasionally to watch the roll, or ignore it and see the result at the end. Most Stage 1 reveals resolve to Rare and the player notices only peripherally.
- **Stage 2 (Elite / Godly check):** On escalation, the panel **animates from the corner to a more prominent center-screen position** and grows in size. The transition is visible — the player's eye is drawn to the movement, signaling that the reveal has passed the Rare threshold and is now rolling for something meaningful. Combat input is still available during Stage 2, but the visual prominence of the panel demands the player's attention. Most players will stop moving during Stage 2 to watch the gold/prismatic spinning, not because they are forced to, but because the escalation has earned their attention.
