- **Stage 3 (Godly finale):** Full screen hijack with **input lock**. The player cannot move, cannot fight, cannot dodge. The game pauses the player's combat state (applying brief invulnerability for the duration of the animation if the player is currently in combat, to prevent unfair deaths during the moment). The 5-second animation plays uninterrupted. This is the only stage that fully hijacks the screen, and it is reserved for the divine moment — a player will see this, if at all, perhaps once in a lifetime.

**The escalation in visual prominence matches the escalation in player interest.** Rare reveals happen in the periphery because they happen frequently. Elite reveals pull attention because they are meaningful events. Godly reveals own the screen because they are the moment. Players will learn this correspondence after their first few reveals and will know, from the prominence of the panel alone, roughly what tier the animation is currently rolling for.

**Server-streamed roll progression.** The reveal animation is streamed from the server to the client frame-by-frame (or tick-by-tick — a single WebSocket message per tick is sufficient). The client does not know in advance what the final grade will be; it only knows what the server tells it on each tick. This prevents the client from faking escalations, fast-forwarding to the result, or displaying a fake Godly reveal that didn't actually happen. The architectural cost is small — roughly 100 tiny messages per reveal, only on Rare-or-better drops (which are less than 10% of all weapon drops) — and the security benefit is significant. See §17.4 for the anti-cheat integration.

**Drop despawn timer.** Unrevealed question mark drops despawn after a window:
- **Standard zones:** 90 seconds
- **Lawless Land:** 60 seconds
- **Dungeons:** Persist until the dungeon is exited or completed (no timer)

The shorter timer in Lawless Land creates the high-stakes decision: the player has to commit to picking up the question mark *before* knowing what it resolves to, while exposed to PvP risk. This is one of the most dramatic player decisions in the game — gamble on grabbing something that might be a Godly while exposed to Lawless Land's full-loot PvP, or move to safety and lose the drop forever.

**Why the tiered RNG reveal exists.** Under a system where the quality is determined instantly at pickup (the v12.5 model), the dopamine fires when the reveal animation plays — the player sees silver/gold/prismatic and knows immediately what they got. This is fine, but it loses something: the moment of *watching the roll happen*. The v12.6 tiered reveal puts the player inside the probability distribution. They see the actual RNG ticking by. They see gold flashes occasionally during the Rare stage, and they know what those flashes mean. They see (very rarely) prismatic flashes during the Elite stage, and they know what *those* mean. Every reveal is a small slot-machine moment where the player watches real odds play out, and the final deceleration is a shared moment with the RNG where they see the last three ticks resolve.

The slot-machine analogy is intentional. Slot machines are satisfying not because of the result but because of the *moment of uncertainty before the result*. The reels spin. You don't know yet. You're hoping. The longer the reels stay in motion, the more invested you are in what they'll land on. The v12.6 reveal makes the animation *be* the result — the player is watching the lottery play out, not watching a fixed animation that confirms a pre-computed outcome. The result is the same mathematically, but the *experience* is honest: the player is inside the RNG, not watching it from outside.

`[DESIGN INTENT: The tiered reveal is the design's answer to a specific failure mode in loot animations. Most games fire a dramatic animation on rare drops and a quiet animation on common drops, which means the player knows what they got the moment the animation starts — the animation is a confirmation, not a lottery. The v12.6 reveal inverts this: the animation *is* the lottery, and the player watches it resolve in real time. The anticipation extends all the way from pickup through to the final deceleration tick, and every tick carries a real chance of escalation. This is a different kind of dopamine than "oh, I got a rare drop" — it is the slot-machine dopamine of "I am watching the outcome unfold and I don't know yet what it will be." The fact that the underlying RNG is honestly visualized (prismatic flashes appear at their actual conditional rate) makes the system *trustworthy* in a way that pre-computed animations are not. The player learns, over time, that prismatic flashes are rare because Godly is rare, and the one time they see a prismatic flash the system has earned the right to make them care.]`

#### §4.6c Hardness (CANONICAL)

Hardness is a per-weapon level (0–5) that increases the weapon's effective base damage. It is the endgame Blacksmith activity — a player chooses to risk a weapon they love on a series of upgrade attempts with cumulative failure odds and full reset on failure. Hardness is the game's "lottery dopamine carrot on a stick" by player commitment, paralleling Godly drops as the lottery dopamine by luck.

**The hardness ladder:**

| Level | Success Chance | Gold Cost (×base) | Effect |
|:-----:|:--------------:|:----------------:|--------|
| 0 → 1 | 80% | 1× | +5 effective base damage |
| 1 → 2 | 20% | 4× | +10 effective base damage |
| 2 → 3 | 5% | 16× | +15 effective base damage |
| 3 → 4 | 1% | 64× | +20 effective base damage |
| 4 → 5 | 0.5% | 256× | +25 effective base damage |

**Failure consequence (pre-v12.28 behavior, modified by Temper — see §4.6c.6):** On a failed hardening attempt, the weapon's hardness **resets to 0** under canonical baseline behavior. The weapon itself is *not* destroyed — the player keeps their Steel Greatsword. They lose the accumulated hardness investment, not the item. A player attempting Hardness 4 → 5 has a 99.5% chance of watching four successful upgrades evaporate in one click. That is the design.

**v12.28 refinement (Temper):** A weapon's Temper value modifies the failure-reset depth. At Temper 100+, a failed attempt preserves the weapon's current Hardness entirely (gold and materials still consumed). The raw success rates (80/20/5/1/0.5%) are unchanged — Temper does not affect the probability of success, only the penalty of failure. See §4.6c.6 for the full Temper specification.

**Expected ladder cost.** The compound success rate to reach Hardness 5 from scratch with no failures is 0.80 × 0.20 × 0.05 × 0.01 × 0.005 = 4 × 10^-7, or roughly 1 in 2.5 million attempts. Hardness 5 remains emphatically server-rare — on a 1,000-player server with active endgame hardening participation, new Hardness 5 weapons are expected to enter the world roughly 5-10 times per year, rather than the 1-2 per year under the prior 0.1% rate. Hardness 5 weapons still create individual mythology around their owners; the rate adjustment preserves the named-individual framing while avoiding the "lottery-by-wealth" failure mode where only players with bulk-attempt resources can realistically reach it. See §22 INV-27 for the per-90-day-window creation rate invariant.

**Practical hardness distribution at the population level (rough estimates):**

| Level | Approx Player Population at Endgame |
|:-----:|:-----------------------------------:|
| Hardness 0 | All players starting any weapon |
| Hardness 1 | ~80% of endgame players have at least one |
| Hardness 2 | ~30% of endgame players have at least one |
| Hardness 3 | ~5% of endgame players have at least one |
| Hardness 4 | ~0.5% of endgame players have at least one |
| Hardness 5 | A handful of players, server-wide, ever |

**Gold cost scaling.** Each hardness attempt costs `BASE_HARDEN_COST × 4^level` gold and consumes a tier-appropriate amount of crafting materials. The 4× compounding makes late-tier attempts dramatically more expensive — a Hardness 4 → 5 attempt costs 256× the base, which means the player is risking serious resources on a 0.5% roll (v12.24; was 0.1% pre-v12.24). This is the gold sink that balances the system at the endgame economic level (see §23.6 v6 economy update).

**Blacksmith skill gates which weapon material tiers can be hardened, not the success rate.** A Blacksmith with skill level N can attempt to harden any weapon up to material tier `floor(N / 5)`. So:

| Blacksmith Skill | Max Hardenable Material Tier |
|:----------------:|:----------------------------:|
| 5 | Tier 1 |
| 25 | Tier 5 |
| 50 | Tier 10 |
| 75 | Tier 15 |
| 100 | Tier 20 (all weapons) |

This mirrors the existing crafting unlock pattern in §4.1 (one new material tier every 5 Blacksmith skill levels) — hardening unlocks naturally as the player levels their life skill, with no separate progression to track. **Blacksmith skill does not modify success rates.** A novice Blacksmith and a master Blacksmith hardening a Tier 1 weapon both face the same 80% / 20% / 5% / 1% / 0.5% ladder. The gate is access, not advantage.

##### §4.6c.6 Temper — Persistence Record (v12.28 CANONICAL)

Every weapon has a **Temper** value: an integer counter that tracks the number of failed hardening attempts that weapon has accumulated. Temper starts at 0 for every new weapon and increments by 1 on each failed hardening attempt. **Temper does not affect success rates.** The raw odds of the Hardness ladder (80% / 20% / 5% / 1% / 0.5%) are preserved exactly under Temper. What Temper changes is the *failure penalty* — how far the weapon's Hardness resets when an attempt fails.

**The revised failure-reset behavior (replacing pre-v12.28's always-reset-to-0):**

| Weapon's Temper | Failure behavior |
|:---------------:|------------------|
| 0 – 19 | Hardness resets to 0 (canonical pre-v12.28 behavior) |
| 20 – 49 | Hardness resets to `max(0, current − 2)` |
| 50 – 99 | Hardness resets to `max(0, current − 1)` |
| 100 + | Hardness preserved at current value (no reset); gold and materials still consumed |

Gold cost and material consumption for each attempt are unchanged by Temper — every attempt costs resources regardless of outcome.

**Temper reset on success.** When a hardening attempt *succeeds* and the weapon's Hardness increases, the weapon's Temper resets to 0. Temper is per-Hardness-level accumulation — a weapon building Temper 80 at the 3→4 step loses that Temper once it reaches Hardness 4, and starts fresh at Temper 0 if it attempts 4→5.

**Temper persistence.** Temper persists through:
- Trades and gifts (the receiving player gets the weapon with its current Temper)
- Marketplace sales (buyer sees Temper before purchase)
- Salvage and re-acquire cycles — actually, salvage destroys the weapon entirely (Temper is lost with it, consistent with §18.3's attribution destruction on salvage)
- The weapon's lifetime in general

Temper never decreases through time, inactivity, or any action other than a successful hardening attempt (which resets it to 0). A weapon sitting in a bank for a year keeps its Temper value.

**Temper visibility.** Temper is visible on the weapon's inspect card alongside the Hardness badge. Example display: *"Hardness 3, Temper 47"* — shows the weapon has accumulated 47 failed attempts at the 3→4 step. Temper is **inspect-only** visibility; it is not visible across a room like the Hardness 5 prismatic numeral. Hardness level remains the public-identity signal; Temper is the weapon's private story readable only on close examination.

**Temper and marketplace.** The §39 Marketplace treats Temper as a searchable/sortable field on weapon listings. Listings display Temper prominently alongside Hardness. Buyers can filter by minimum Temper values (e.g., "show weapons with Temper ≥ 50 at Hardness 3" — finding weapons where the next attempt won't catastrophically reset on failure). This creates a distinct marketplace category: *tempered weapons* sold at premium prices specifically because they fail more gracefully rather than because they succeed more often. See §39.8 for marketplace integration detail.

**Why Temper preserves the Hardness 5 mythology.** The pre-revision draft of Temper had it *boost success rate* at high Temper values (e.g., guaranteed success at Temper 200). That approach would have converted Hardness 5 from server-rare lottery into grind-achievable certainty, destroying the named-individual mythology the Hardness ladder is designed to produce. The revised Temper mechanic preserves the mythology intact — raw odds are unchanged, the expected Hardness 5 creation rate per 90-day window is unchanged (INV-27 holds without modification), and most Hardness 5 weapons will still come from early-attempt lucky hits rather than from persistence paths. What Temper changes is the *psychological* failure penalty — a player who has accumulated Temper 100+ on a weapon attempting Hardness 4→5 can keep attempting indefinitely without watching the weapon cascade back to Hardness 0 on each failure. Each attempt still costs resources; each attempt still has only 0.5% raw chance to succeed; but the catastrophic-loss failure mode is softened.

**Tuning (§4.6d):**

| Variable | Default | Range | Description |
|----------|:-------:|:-----:|-------------|
| TEMPER_THRESHOLD_T1 | 20 | 10–30 | Partial reset threshold (−2 on fail) |
| TEMPER_THRESHOLD_T2 | 50 | 30–80 | Near-preservation threshold (−1 on fail) |
| TEMPER_THRESHOLD_T3 | 100 | 80–200 | No-reset threshold (preserve on fail) |
| TEMPER_RESET_DEPTH_T1 | −2 | fixed | Reset depth at T1 threshold |
| TEMPER_RESET_DEPTH_T2 | −1 | fixed | Reset depth at T2 threshold |

**Invariant impact.** INV-27 (Hardness 5 Creation Rate) is **unchanged** under the revised Temper mechanic. Success rates are not modified; only the failure penalty is softened. The compound rate for a fresh Hardness 5 from a fresh weapon remains 4 × 10⁻⁷ per attempt, producing the same 5-10 per year / 1,000-player projection that v12.24's ladder produces. The ≤10 per 90-day window cap holds with the same headroom it had pre-v12.28. **No Monte Carlo re-derivation required.** This is the load-bearing outcome of the revision — a more forgiving mechanic that does not require retuning the rest of the system.

**Creative framing** — see `creative-reference.md` §8.12a for the named-craftsman mythology extension (Tempered weapons as "the weapon remembers each attempt," crafters known for producing high-Temper weapons that fail more gracefully).

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.28): The pre-v12.28 Hardness ladder was mathematically satisfying (rare lottery producing server-rare content) but psychologically brutal at the endgame tiers. A player attempting 4→5 faces a 99.5% chance of watching four successful upgrades evaporate in one click, and this is the failure mode that makes Hardness 5 pursuit feel like masochism rather than commitment. The experience-first integration plan originally proposed boosting success rate with Temper as a mercy, but that approach would have destroyed the mythology by making Hardness 5 grindable. The revision — softening the failure penalty without touching success rates — threads the needle cleanly. The lottery feel is entirely preserved (raw odds unchanged, most Hardness 5s still come from early-attempt luck, INV-27 holds without modification). The catastrophic-loss failure mode is softened for players who have committed to a weapon. Each tier of the failure-reset reduction (0→−2, −2→−1, −1→no-reset) is an *achievement* of persistence — a player who has built Temper 100 on a weapon has genuinely invested in it, and that investment now buys them a specific kind of safety (attempts no longer catastrophically reset) rather than a general advantage (success rate boost). The marketplace implication is that Tempered weapons become a legitimately-distinct category: buyers pay a premium not for "fails less" but for "fails more gracefully." The economics shift from probability manipulation to risk management, which is both more honest to the system and more interesting as an emergent market dynamic. The Hardness 5 mythology — "Bro Mason spent three months working a single weapon and finally got one" — now explicitly includes the Tempered path as a legitimate way to reach it without changing the server-rare frequency. Temper is the mechanic that converts "the Hardness ladder is cruel" into "the Hardness ladder is rigorous.".]`

`[DESIGN INTENT: Hardness is the player-agency mirror of the Godly quality drop. Both are lottery dopamine. Both produce roughly server-wide-rare items. The difference is *which* dimension of luck the player engages with: Godly rewards farming volume (find more drops, eventually find a Godly), while Hardness 5 rewards committing to a specific weapon and pushing it (climb the ladder, fail, climb again). They converge in expected effort but feel completely different to play. A player who has both — a Godly Fusion weapon hardened to Level 5 — owns one of the most valuable objects on the server. There may only be one such weapon in existence. The economy and the social mythology around that weapon are part of the game's content. This is content created by player effort, not by content designers, and it scales to the player base for free.

The Blacksmith skill gate (which-tier, not what-rate) is what gives the life skill an endgame role without breaking the lottery design. A maxed Blacksmith does not have better Hardness 5 odds than anyone else — but they are the only players who can offer Tier 20 hardening services. The marketplace will form around these players: "Bro Mason will harden your Tier 18 weapon for 50,000 gold, materials provided by you, success not guaranteed." High-skill blacksmiths become known by name, like high-rank Final Fantasy XIV crafters. This is social mythology around the *crafter*, where the Hardness 5 weapon itself is social mythology around the *owner*. Two layers of named-individual content emerge naturally from one mechanic.]`

#### §4.6d Hardness and Quality Tuning

| Variable | Default | Min | Max | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| HARDNESS_BASE_BONUS | 5 | 3 | 8 | Flat base damage per hardness level |
| HARDNESS_0_TO_1 | 0.80 | 0.70 | 0.90 | Success chance |
| HARDNESS_1_TO_2 | 0.20 | 0.15 | 0.30 | Success chance |
| HARDNESS_2_TO_3 | 0.05 | 0.03 | 0.08 | Success chance |
| HARDNESS_3_TO_4 | 0.01 | 0.005 | 0.02 | Success chance |
| HARDNESS_4_TO_5 | 0.005 | 0.0005 | 0.005 | Success chance — keeps Hardness 5 mythological. **v12.24: raised from 0.001 default to 0.005** to shift Hardness 5 from lottery-by-wealth toward lottery-by-commitment. Default now sits at the upper bound of the tunable range; future loosening would require raising the max. See §22 INV-27 for creation rate invariant. |
| BASE_HARDEN_COST | 500g | 250g | 1,000g | Tier-1 base cost; scales by `4^level` and weapon tier |
| QUALITY_NORMAL_MULT | 1.00 | 1.00 | 1.00 | Baseline (do not change) |
| QUALITY_RARE_MULT | 1.20 | 1.15 | 1.30 |  |
| QUALITY_ELITE_MULT | 1.50 | 1.40 | 1.65 |  |
| QUALITY_GODLY_MULT | 3.00 | 2.50 | 3.50 | Sacred tier — exempt from §6.5 audit gates |
| QUALITY_NORMAL_RATE | 0.900998 | 0.88 | 0.93 | Residual rounds into Normal |
| QUALITY_RARE_RATE | 0.09 | 0.07 | 0.11 | 1 in 11 weapon drops |
| QUALITY_ELITE_RATE | 0.009 | 0.007 | 0.012 | 1 in 111 weapon drops |
| QUALITY_GODLY_RATE | 0.0000025 | 0.0000010 | 0.0000050 | 1 in 400,000 weapon drops — DO NOT loosen without explicit cosmological review |
| MYSTERY_DESPAWN_STANDARD | 90s | 60s | 120s | Unrevealed Rare+ drop despawn time, standard zones |
| MYSTERY_DESPAWN_LAWLESS | 60s | 45s | 90s | Unrevealed Rare+ drop despawn time, Lawless Land |
| GODLY_REVEAL_DURATION | 4.5s | 3.0s | 6.0s | Total length of Godly reveal animation including motif |
| TEMPER_THRESHOLD_T1 | 20 | 10 | 30 | §4.6c.6 Temper — partial reset (−2) threshold |
| TEMPER_THRESHOLD_T2 | 50 | 30 | 80 | §4.6c.6 Temper — near-preservation (−1) threshold |
| TEMPER_THRESHOLD_T3 | 100 | 80 | 200 | §4.6c.6 Temper — no-reset threshold |
| TEMPER_RESET_DEPTH_T1 | −2 | fixed | fixed | §4.6c.6 Temper — reset depth at T1 |
| TEMPER_RESET_DEPTH_T2 | −1 | fixed | fixed | §4.6c.6 Temper — reset depth at T2 |

#### §4.7 Volatile vs Stable Fusions

**Volatile:** Two elements adjacent on effectiveness circle. One beats the other. Internal conflict = crackling visual boundary on weapon.

**Volatile collision bonus is directional.** The VOLATILE_COLLISION_BONUS(0.30) (+30% collision damage) applies only when the **strong** element is the **trigger** consuming the **weak** element's setup status. The strong element actively overcoming the weak element is what makes the collision volatile. The reverse direction (weak triggering strong) is a standard stable collision — no bonus.

**Stable:** Two elements non-adjacent, OR the reverse direction of a volatile pair. Harmonious coexistence. Normal collision damage. Smooth blended visual.

**9 Volatile relationships** (circle-adjacent element pairs). Each relationship produces exactly **1 volatile collision** (strong→weak direction) and **1 stable collision** (weak→strong direction):

| Volatile Relationship | Strong→Weak Direction (Volatile, +30%) | Weak→Strong Direction (Stable) |
|-----------------------|:--------------------------------------:|:------------------------------:|
| Flame–Frost | Meltwater (Freeze + Flame trigger) | Steam (Burn + Frost trigger) |
| Frost–Storm | Static Freeze (Shock + Frost trigger) | Hailstorm (Freeze + Storm trigger) |
| Storm–Stone | Tremor (Fracture + Storm trigger) | Seismic Pulse (Shock + Stone trigger) |
| Stone–Wind | Sandstorm (Slow + Stone trigger) | Erosion (Fracture + Wind trigger) |
| Wind–Venom | Miasma (Root + Wind trigger) | Plague Wind (Slow + Venom trigger) |
| Venom–Flora | Blight Garden (Thorn + Venom trigger) | Decompose (Root + Flora trigger) |
| Flora–Water | Overgrowth (Soak + Flora trigger) | Bloom (Thorn + Water trigger) |
| Water–Flame | Quench (Burn + Water trigger) | Scald (Soak + Flame trigger) |
| Dark↔Light (mutual) | Eclipse (Curse + Light trigger) **+** Umbra (Reveal + Dark trigger) | *(both volatile — mutual exception)* |

**Total: 10 volatile collisions** (8 directional + 2 mutual Dark↔Light). **80 stable collisions.**

**What this means for weapon orientation.** Under the v12.6 gem system, a player who wants a volatile weapon does the following: (1) acquire the two element gems they want (Flame and Frost, for example), (2) combine them into a Flame+Frost composite at the Enchanter (§4.13), (3) apply the composite to an enchanted weapon, and (4) toggle the orientation to place the *strong* element (Flame) as the swipe trigger and the *weak* element (Frost) as the auto-attack setup. The result: a Flame/Frost weapon where the collision fires as Meltwater at +30%. If the player had the same two gems but oriented Frost as the swipe trigger and Flame as the auto-attack, they'd produce Steam at standard damage. **The player makes this choice consciously, with full knowledge of both directions, because the player *chose* both gems.** Volatile direction is no longer partially an accident of which weapon dropped — it is entirely an informed decision made at the Enchanter.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Under the v12.4 system, volatile-vs-stable emerged partially from drops — players who found a Flame/Frost Fusion weapon might or might not understand the volatile direction, and orientation was a choice made *after* seeing what the game gave them. Under the v12.6 gem system, the player makes *every* elemental decision consciously: which gems to acquire, which to combine, which orientation to apply. This raises both the floor and the ceiling of the system. Floor: new players cannot accidentally hit volatile damage without understanding why, so they won't get carried by lucky orientations. Ceiling: experienced players have full agency over their volatile pairings and can plan collision optimization as part of their build identity. The use-trained stat system and the gem composition system parallel each other thematically — both move identity from "the game declares it" to "the player builds it from raw material." Elemental identity is earned through gem acquisition and combination decisions, not through drop luck on weapon tier.]`

**Visual distinction:** Volatile composites crackle at the element boundary when applied to a weapon. Stable composites blend smoothly. The player can tell at a glance whether a weapon's current elemental identity is volatile or stable. The crackle direction subtly favors the strong element's side — a Flame/Frost weapon crackles more on the Flame side. This hints at the volatile direction without requiring UI explanation.

#### §4.8 Shift Gems (CANONICAL)

Shift is no longer a weapon tier. It is a **special gem type** that drops only from Ascendant areas — the Guardian zones (§55), the Convergence (§56), and the ritual chamber of the Last Recombination (§57 — not farmable, see note). Shift gems are rare within those zones: not Godly-rare, but uncommon enough that even players who farm Ascendant areas regularly will see them only occasionally.

**Ritual chamber note.** The v14.0 ritual chamber (§57.2b) is not a farmable source of Shift gems — the chamber can only be entered once per character, and the ritual consumes the character's visible identity. Shift gem generation at the ritual chamber is not canonical in v14.0. The Shift gem Ascendant-area reference is preserved for Guardian zones and Convergence.

**Shift gems cannot be combined with other gems.** When applied to an enchanted weapon, a Shift gem occupies the weapon alone — no second gem, no composite partner. The weapon becomes a Shift weapon by virtue of the Shift gem being applied. Shift gems grant the weapon adaptive elemental behavior (prismatic visual, element-shifting combat properties — the exact mechanic is specified in §4.13.5).

**Shift gem levels and combination.** Like regular element gems, Shift gems have levels and can be upgraded via Gem Cutting — combining multiple Level N Shift gems produces one Level N+1 Shift gem, subject to the Gem Cutting skill threshold. Higher-level Shift gems produce stronger shift effects. But Shift gems can *only* combine with other Shift gems — they cannot be mixed with any regular element gem.

**Shift gem drop rarity within Ascendant areas.** The exact rate is tuned in the loot table data, but as a reference baseline: approximately 1 in 50 weapon drops in Ascendant areas are Shift gems rather than standard drops. A dedicated Ascendant-area farmer can expect to see Shift gems a few times per session. This is frequent enough that players who commit to Ascendant-area farming have a real path to a Shift build, rare enough that Shift weapons remain a meaningful endgame identity.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Shift is preserved as a rare endgame gem type rather than deleted entirely because the Ascendant areas needed a unique loot anchor distinct from standard gem drops. Making Shift a gem type rather than a weapon tier keeps it consistent with the rest of the v12.6 system (all elements come from gems, no exceptions) while giving the Ascendant areas their own rare reward. The rule that Shift gems cannot be combined with regular elements is the design's way of saying "Shift is a different *mode* of elemental operation, not another element to mix in." A weapon with a Shift gem applied is a Shift weapon, full stop — the player is opting into adaptive behavior instead of fixed-element combinations. The level-up path for Shift gems mirrors the level-up path for regular gems, keeping the Gem Cutting skill's value consistent across all gem types.]`

#### §4.9 Gear Data Model (v12.6 Revised)

| Field | Type | Req | Desc |
|-------|------|:---:|------|
| gear_id | String | Y | Unique ID |
| slot | Weapon/Armor | Y | Slot |
| weapon_type | Greatsword/Sword/Bow/Staff/null | Weapon only | Attack style |
| material_tier | 1–20 | Y | Crafting material tier (copper=1, origin=20). Drives stat gate via §4.1. |
| quality | Normal/Rare/Elite/Godly | Y | Quality grade (§4.6b). Permanent. Multiplies effective base damage. |
| quality_mult | Float | Y | Damage multiplier from quality (1.00 / 1.20 / 1.50 / 3.00) |
| godly_origin_signature | Hash | Godly only | Server-side cryptographic signature over the immutable origin record (kill, tick, monster, original_recipient). Never changes. See §17.4.1. |
| godly_current_owner_signature | Hash | Godly only | Server-side signature over (weapon_id, current_owner, history_length). Re-signed on every transfer. See §17.4.1. |
| godly_original_recipient | UUID | Godly only | The player who first picked up and revealed this weapon. Permanent, never changes. |
| godly_current_owner | UUID | Godly only | The player who currently holds the weapon. Updates on every transfer via the §17.4.1 protocol. |
| godly_ownership_history | List<OwnershipEvent> | Godly only | Append-only log of all transfers. Publicly inspectable. See §17.4.1. |
| hardness | 0–5 | Y | Per-weapon hardness level (§4.6c). Default 0. Modified by Blacksmith hardening. |
| enchanted | Bool | Y | True once the weapon has been enchanted (§4.12). Required before any gem composite can be applied. Permanent once set. |
| applied_composite | CompositeId/null | Weapon only, if enchanted | Reference to a gem composite (§4.13) that is currently applied to the weapon. Null if no composite applied. |
| element_tier | Common/Elemental/Fusion/Shift | Y (derived) | **Derived** from `applied_composite` state. Computed at query time, not stored. Common if no composite, Elemental if single-element composite, Fusion if two-element composite, Shift if a Shift gem is applied. |
| stat_bonuses | Map<Stat, Int> | N | Flat additive to stats |
| required_stat | Map<Stat, Int> | Y | Stat gate for equipping (§4.1, `statReq = material_tier × 10`). Godly weapons ignore this gate. |
| attunement_element | Element/None | Armor only | Element attunement |

**The canonical name format** for a weapon is `<material> <weapon_type>` — "Steel Greatsword," "Oak Bow," "Iron Sword," "Mithril Staff." There is no creative name field, no designer-authored per-weapon name. The weapon is identified entirely by its material, type, quality, hardness, and applied composite.

`[DESIGN INTENT: The data model under v12.6 is simpler than v12.4. The `element_1`, `element_2`, `tier_multiplier`, `is_volatile`, and `creative_name` fields are all deleted. Element identity is computed from the applied composite at query time, so it is always consistent with what the player currently has applied to the weapon. There is no risk of the stored element_tier getting out of sync with the stored element_1 and element_2. The weapon is just: material, quality, hardness, enchanted bool, applied composite. The elements are properties of the composite, not of the weapon.]`

#### §4.10 Naming Convention (v12.6 Revised)

**Weapons have no creative names.** A weapon's display name is simply `<material> <weapon_type>`: Steel Greatsword, Oak Bow, Iron Sword, Mithril Staff, Origin Greatsword. The color of the nameplate indicates quality grade (Normal no color, Rare silver, Elite gold, Godly prismatic shimmer). Quality border shapes (line, notched, radiant) reinforce color for accessibility (§4.6b). Hardness level appears as a small numeric badge in the corner of the weapon icon (0 hidden; 1–5 displayed).

**Applied gem identity** is shown as small icon glyphs next to the weapon name or in the tooltip: a Flame gem icon and a Frost gem icon next to a Steel Greatsword indicates "Steel Greatsword with a Flame+Frost composite applied." The volatile/stable status of the composite is shown with a crackle indicator between the two element icons if volatile. If the weapon is not enchanted, no gem icons appear and no composite can be applied.

**Full identity at a glance.** Quality (nameplate color + border shape), material tier (weapon name prefix), weapon type (weapon name), hardness (corner badge), enchanted state (gem slot icons visible or not), and applied composite (gem icons + volatile indicator) are all visible without opening a tooltip. Tooltips show the canonical numbers: base damage, quality multiplier, hardness bonus, applied gem details. The player identifies their weapons by their attributes, not by a per-drop designer-authored flourish.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Removing creative weapon names is a design choice with real implications for the player-emergent mythology framework (Creative Reference §8.12a). Under v12.4, a player who hardened their "Ashenveil" to Hardness 5 became "the player with Ashenveil" — the weapon had a name and the player inherited it. Under v12.6, a player who hardens their Steel Greatsword to Hardness 5 becomes "the player with the Hardness 5 Steel Greatsword" — the weapon is identified by its stats and history, not by an authored name. This is *more* honest in a specific way: the weapon's identity comes from what the player did to it, not from a fixed designer label. A Steel Greatsword is generic; *this specific player's* Steel Greatsword is unique because of the hardness ladder they climbed and the composite they applied. The player's relationship with the weapon is built on shared history, not on a shared name. Community-emerged nicknames for famous weapons will still happen — players will call the server's one Hardness 5 Origin Bow by whatever name the community settles on — but the names will be player-emergent, not designer-authored. This is the correct direction: the game provides the raw material, the community writes the names.]`

#### §4.11 Loot Rules

**The two-roll structure (CANONICAL):**

When a monster dies, its loot table evaluates. The loot table contains many possible outcomes (gold, materials, consumables, **gems**, etc.). One of those outcomes is a **weapon drop event**, which has a per-monster rate. A reference baseline: a same-level squishy fodder monster has approximately a 1-in-200 chance of producing a weapon drop event. This rate scales with monster type — bosses guarantee weapon drops, brutes drop weapons more often than fodder, etc. The exact per-monster rate is tuned in the loot table data (`monsters.json`) and is independent of the quality system.

**All weapon drops are base weapons.** Weapon drops are `<material> <weapon_type>` with no element and no applied composite. A weapon drop is a Steel Greatsword, an Oak Bow — never a "Fusion Flame/Frost Greatsword" or an "Elemental Storm Staff." Elements come from gems (§4.13), not from drops. This is the most important v12.6 rule: **no weapon ever drops with elements.**

**When a weapon drop event fires, the quality is rolled** per the §4.6b distribution: Normal 90.0998% / Rare 9% / Elite 0.9% / Godly 0.00025% (1 in 400,000). Godly is *not* a separate roll — it is the asymptote of the same ladder as Rare and Elite. Every weapon drop carries a tiny chance of being Godly. This is what makes Godly the perpetual carrot for every player at every phase of the game.

**The compound per-kill Godly rate** (against a same-level squishy with a 1-in-200 weapon drop rate) is approximately 1 in 80,000,000. Across a 1,000-player server, this produces approximately 80 Godly weapons per year — about one every 4-5 days entering circulation. See §4.6b.i for the full Godly cosmology and protection rules.

**Gem drops are a separate loot category.** Monsters can drop gems independently of weapon drops. Gems are **zone-locked by type** — a fire-zone monster can only drop Flame gems, an ice-zone monster can only drop Frost gems, etc. Gem drops have their own per-monster rate (tuned in `monsters.json`) and their own level distribution (Level 1 gems are the vast majority of drops; higher-level gems come from higher-level monsters and from Gem Cutting combination). See §4.13 for the canonical gem specification.

**Other loot rules:**

- Weapons/armor scale with level permanently via material tier multiplier.
- Drop rates scale with monster level.
- **No valid-threat rule for loot.** Any player can receive the maximum loot offered by any monster they kill, regardless of level difference. A Level 100 player killing a Level 1 slime gets the same per-kill weapon drop chance and the same per-drop quality distribution as anyone else. The inefficiency of farming trivial enemies for loot is self-deterring through the §1.4 stat system (which *does* gate stat XP to ±5 levels) — players who farm trivial enemies receive zero stat training, which is a steeper penalty than any loot rule could impose. The systems make trivial farming irrational without forbidding it.
- **Hardness always drops at 0.** Hardness is exclusively earned through Blacksmith activity (§4.6c). No drop can pre-roll a hardness level.
- **Enchanted state always drops as false.** All weapon drops are unenchanted. Enchanting is exclusively performed at the Enchanter (§4.12).
- **Applied composite always drops as null.** Gems are never pre-applied to dropped weapons.
- Weapon drops match equipped weapon type.
- Per-player instanced drops in parties.
- **Mystery reveals on Rare and above.** Drops at Rare grade or higher appear in the world as question mark icons rather than their final visual identity. Reveal happens on pickup. See §4.6b.ii for the canonical mystery reveal mechanic, the tiered RNG animation, despawn timers, and the reveal animation stages.
- **Godly drops are sacred objects and tradeable (v12.7).** A Godly weapon cannot be lost on death (any zone), taken by the Ferryman, or destroyed by salvage. It **can** be traded, sold, gifted, and marketplace-listed — the v12.5 binding rule was removed in v12.7. The protections travel with the object regardless of who currently holds it. See §4.6b.i for the canonical sacred-properties specification and §17.4 for the cryptographic provenance and lineage system.
- **Mystery items are tradeable (v12.7).** Unrevealed mystery items (Rare-or-better question-mark drops) can be traded and marketplace-listed before reveal. The reveal animation plays only for whoever currently holds the item at the moment of pickup-to-reveal — the seller of a traded mystery item never learns what it was. Mystery items obey ordinary inventory rules including loss on death (see §5.5 and §26.4) — they are *not* preemptively protected even though they might be Godly. The protection only attaches once the reveal completes and the underlying grade is confirmed Godly.
- **Godly weapon gem interaction.** Godly weapons can be enchanted normally and can have gem composites applied normally. The gems remain separable — a player can decouple a composite from a Godly weapon and recover the gems unchanged. The Godly weapon's *sacred properties* (death protection, Ferryman exception, salvage rejection, stat-gate exemption, audit-gate exemption) attach to the weapon itself; applied gem composites are ordinary gems and follow ordinary rules. When a Godly weapon is traded, any applied composite transfers with it (the new owner inherits whatever gems were applied), but the gems themselves remain ordinary tradeable items if decoupled.

#### §4.11b Weapon History (v12.30 CANONICAL)

Every weapon accumulates a persistent **History** — a record of milestones achieved while the weapon was equipped. History persists through trades, is visible on the weapon's inspect card, and gives long-wielded weapons visible identity beyond their mechanical attributes. A Steel Greatsword that has killed 10,000 monsters and defeated three Apex bosses has a *story* legible to any player who inspects it, independent of its Hardness, Temper, or applied composite.

**History fields.** A weapon's History contains both counter fields (aggregated over the weapon's lifetime) and log fields (individual event entries). Counters store high-frequency events; logs store meaningful individual events.

**Counter fields (aggregated):**

| Field | Increments on |
|-------|---------------|
| `total_kills` | Every monster killed while the weapon is equipped (final blow only) |
| `total_damage_dealt` | Aggregate damage dealt through the weapon's attacks (rounded to the nearest 1,000 for compactness) |
| `arena_matches_won` | Every Arena match won while the weapon is equipped |
| `clan_wars_won` | Every Clan War won while the weapon is equipped (participating player's weapon) |

**Log fields (individual entries):**

| Field | Entry content |
|-------|---------------|
| `bosses_defeated` | `{boss_id, zone_id, timestamp}` — one entry per boss defeated while the weapon is equipped |
| `godly_reveals_witnessed` | `{zone_id, timestamp}` — one entry per Godly reveal witnessed while the weapon is equipped. **No player name recorded** — the fact of witnessing is logged without identifying whose Godly dropped, preserving the §8.12a anonymous-accomplishments rule. |
| `hardness_successes` | Cross-reference only. The §17.5 HardeningLedger already contains the full per-weapon hardening history; Weapon History's `hardness_successes` field is a projection of successful entries from that ledger, displayed alongside other History items on the inspect card. No duplicate storage. |
| `first_crafter` | `{crafter_name, crafter_id, crafted_at}` — populated from §18.3 crafter attribution. Single entry (crafter is fixed at craft time). Nullable for weapons without crafter attribution (NPC items, loot drops, pre-v12.25 items). |

**Inspect card display.** History appears on the weapon's inspect card below the Temper field. Format:

> **History**
> *10,427 kills · 4.2M damage dealt*
> *★ Boss: Imp King (first Apex defeat, 47 days ago)*
> *Boss: Abyss Warden (23 days ago)*
> *Godly reveal witnessed: Ember Fields, 12 days ago*
> *Arena: 8 wins*
> *First wielded by Bro Mason, 112 days ago*
> *[show full history]*

Counters render first in a compact line. Individual log entries render as short lines with the event type and timestamp. The full history (expandable via the link) shows every logged entry chronologically; the compact inspect card shows the top 10 items in star-priority-then-recency order.

**Player-curated starring.** Players can tap any individual history entry to **star** it. Starred entries appear first in the top-10 inspect card view, overriding recency. Stars are toggled — tapping a starred entry un-stars it. Unlimited stars allowed (no cap). The auto-logged History captures everything; starring is a lightweight curation layer players can use to surface the entries they feel define the weapon's story.

**Star persistence through trades.** Starred state travels with the weapon through trades. A weapon sold to a new owner arrives with the previous owner's curation intact — *"this is what the previous holder felt mattered."* The new owner can star additional entries or un-star existing ones; there is no distinction between "previous owner's stars" and "new owner's stars." Stars are always the current state, editable by the current owner.

**Weapons with no history display no History section.** A freshly-crafted weapon that has never been used shows no History block on its inspect card (not "History: (none)"). The section appears once the weapon has any counter ≥1 or any log entry.

**Salvage destroys History.** Consistent with §18.3 attribution and §4.6c.6 Temper, salvage destroys the weapon including its full History. A player who salvages a high-History weapon loses that History permanently. This creates an explicit player choice: preserve a story-rich weapon, or salvage it for materials. The trade-off is exactly the design intent.

**Godly weapon lineage vs. Weapon History.** Godly weapons (§4.6b.i, §17.4.1) carry a separate lineage system tracking ownership chain. Weapon History and Godly lineage are **orthogonal** — a Godly weapon can have both a lineage ("originally received by Bro Alice, traded to Bob, bought by Carol") and a history ("killed 10,000 monsters, defeated Imp King five times, witnessed 3 Godly reveals"). Both display on the inspect card. Non-Godly weapons have only History.

**Size management.** Counters are fixed-size (no growth). Log fields grow with events but at bounded rates:
- `bosses_defeated`: a weapon wielded through the full 120 bosses (§10 boss catalog) across multiple replays accumulates at most a few hundred entries
- `godly_reveals_witnessed`: a player who witnesses 20 Godly reveals across their character lifetime produces 20 entries
- `hardness_successes`: at most 5 entries per weapon (Hardness 0→5 ladder)

A typical active weapon accumulates <500 total log entries over its lifetime. At ~200 bytes per entry (see architecture.md §9.5.5 for schema), a heavily-used weapon carries ~100KB of History metadata. Acceptable overhead given the content it provides.

**Anti-cheat.** All History writes are server-authoritative. Counters increment only via the canonical combat-resolution code paths (monster kill registration in §8, damage aggregation in §2, Arena/Clan War result recording). Log entries are appended only via canonical event paths (§10 boss defeat, §4.6b.ii Godly reveal completion). Clients cannot fabricate History entries, modify counts, or forge logs. See §17.4.3 for the Weapon History Ledger specification.

**Creative direction:** see `creative-reference.md` §8 for the "weapons accumulate meaning" framing.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.30): Temper (v12.28) gave weapons a record of failed attempts — the weapon that remembers its own persistence. Weapon History gives weapons a record of achievements — the weapon that remembers its own victories. Together they form a parallel pair: persistence and accomplishment, both legible on the inspect card, both traveling through trades, both building the "weapons accumulate story" design thread. A player who has wielded a single weapon through their entire character lifetime sees that weapon acquire increasingly dense metadata — kills, damage, bosses defeated, Godly reveals witnessed, Hardness levels climbed, Arena wins, Clan Wars — all legible as the weapon's biography. The starring mechanism is deliberately minimal: most players will never use it, but players who care about their weapons' stories get a small curation layer. The auto-log captures everything so nothing is lost; the curation layer lets the player surface what they want prominently. Starred state persisting through trades is the subtle-but-important detail — it means a sold weapon arrives carrying the previous owner's sense of what mattered about it, which is content the buyer gets for free. Over years of server life, high-History weapons accumulate layered curation across multiple owners: each one starred what they felt defined the weapon during their time with it. The weapon becomes not just an object but a collaborative artifact between successive players. This is player-emergent mythology extended into the individual object layer — Temper gave us "the weapon that remembers its attempts," Weapon History gives us "the weapon that remembers its life."]`

#### §4.12 Enchanting (v12.6 CANONICAL)

**Enchanting is a one-time permanent transformation** that converts a base weapon into a gem-capable weapon. A weapon must be enchanted before any gem composite (§4.13) can be applied to it. Enchanting is performed at the Enchanter NPC in town or at a player-built Enchanting station (if unlocked via the Enchanting life skill).

**Enchanting cost.** Enchanting a weapon costs:
- **Gold:** scales with the weapon's material tier. Reference baseline: `ENCHANT_BASE_COST(50g) × material_tier`. A Tier 1 copper sword enchants for 50g; a Tier 20 origin sword enchants for 1000g. (**v12.9 update:** lowered from 100g to 50g per the v6.1 economy resolution — see §4.13.7 and §23.6a.2.)
- **Catalyst materials:** a small quantity of **elemental essence dust** (one dust per element the weapon will eventually carry, which in practice is 2 dust per enchanting since most weapons end up with two-element composites). Essence dust is a new material (added in v12.6) produced as a byproduct of gem operations — each gem combination at the Enchanter produces 1 essence dust, and decoupling a composite produces 1 essence dust. The dust is element-agnostic. The practical economic loop: players who combine and decouple gems frequently accumulate essence dust, which they then spend on enchanting weapons. Essence dust is tradeable on the marketplace.

**Enchanting is permanent.** Once a weapon is enchanted, it stays enchanted for the lifetime of the weapon. There is no "un-enchanting" path — the weapon does not revert to an un-enchanted state. Enchanting is a threshold moment: the player commits to making this weapon a gem vessel, and the commitment is one-way.

**Enchanting does not apply gems.** The enchanting step only makes the weapon *capable* of holding gem composites. It does not put any elements on the weapon. The player must then acquire gems and combine them into a composite (or acquire a Shift gem) and apply the composite to the enchanted weapon as a separate action.

**Unenchanted weapons are fully functional.** A player who never enchants a weapon can still use it — it just operates as a Common-tier weapon with no elemental behavior. Unenchanted weapons have the full baseline damage from §4.4 and can still be hardened, quality-graded, and leveled up. **The element system is additive, not required.** A player who prefers a pure melee experience without any elemental complexity is playing the game correctly.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Enchanting as a threshold moment creates a meaningful commitment point. The player's first enchant is a small ceremony — they're choosing to open their weapon to the gem system, and the weapon remembers that choice forever. This also gives the Enchanter NPC a clear value proposition as the gateway to the elemental system. The one-way permanence prevents the enchanting action from being reduced to a trivial toggle — a player who enchants their starter weapon and then outgrows it has committed that weapon to the gem system, and if they want to experiment with a fresh unenchanted weapon later, they need a new base weapon. This matches the life-skill progression curve: enchanting costs scale with material tier, so players naturally enchant weapons they intend to keep, not weapons they expect to replace immediately. The essence dust byproduct is the small economic thread that ties enchanting, gem combination, and gem decoupling into a single material economy — players who engage heavily with gems naturally produce the material they need to enchant more weapons, and players who only want to enchant occasionally can buy dust on the marketplace from the gem-heavy players.]`

#### §4.13 Gem System (v12.6 CANONICAL, v13 REPLACEMENT — Direct Slot Fusion)

**v13 REPLACEMENT — Composite intermediary object removed.** Canonical "composite object" intermediary (where two gems combine into a `composite` item in inventory before being applied to a weapon) is replaced by **direct slot fusion**: enchanted weapons have two element slots; players slot gems directly into weapon slots, and the two slotted gems produce their combination in-situ. No `composite` item in inventory; the weapon holds the gems themselves and produces the combination on demand.

**What this changes:**

- **Inventory simplification.** One less item type to manage. Players have gems + weapons, not gems + composites + weapons.
- **Slot-based UI.** Weapon inspect shows two element slots with currently-slotted gems. Slotting is drag-and-drop (desktop) or tap-to-slot (mobile). Right-click (desktop) or long-press (mobile) unslots a gem back to inventory.
- **Combination happens in-weapon.** When both slots are filled, the weapon produces the combined elemental behavior (same mechanics as canonical composite). When only one slot is filled, the weapon produces single-element behavior for that element. When both slots are empty, the weapon is unenchanted.
- **Unslotting is free.** Canonical "decouple composite" operation (costs gold) is replaced by free slot removal. Slot the gems you want; swap freely. The weapon is your canvas.
- **Essence dust economy preserved.** Dust is produced by gem *combinations* (per §4.13.2 canonical skill-gated leveling), which happens at Enchanter NPC or Gem Cutting station as canonical. Slotting itself produces no dust; only the gem leveling process does.

**Canonical §4.13 mechanics preserved:**

- Gem types (10 elements + Shift), zone-locked drops
- Gem levels (1-10) and combination into higher levels
- Gem Cutting skill thresholds for combination
- Combination cost (gold + produces essence dust)
- Level-matching requirement (two Level N gems combine to Level N+1)
- Zone-locked gem type sources
- Marketplace tradability of gems

**What's removed:**

- `composite` item type in inventory
- "Apply composite to weapon" as a separate action
- "Decouple composite" as a gold-cost action (now free unslot)

**Why the change.** The composite intermediary object was canonical-correct but added inventory friction. Players slotting gems in weapons is a cleaner mental model — the weapon *has* elements, not "the weapon has a composite object that has elements." Direct slot fusion matches conventional ARPG gem-slotting patterns while preserving Brotown's distinctive two-element combination system (which is the load-bearing design innovation; the composite-object intermediary was just implementation).

**Migration.** Characters with existing composite items in inventory have those composites automatically applied to their equipped weapon on v13 load (if the weapon is already enchanted and has empty slots). Composites in inventory that cannot be auto-applied (no enchanted weapon, all weapons slotted) remain in inventory as "legacy composites" that can be manually slotted via a one-time UI migration flow. Once all legacy composites are resolved (~2 weeks post-launch), the composite item type is fully removed.

**Enchanting remains the threshold moment.** Weapons still must be enchanted (§4.12 canonical) before they can hold gems. Enchanting now grants the weapon two slots; gems are slotted into the slots after enchanting. Canonical one-way enchanting commitment preserved.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): The canonical two-element composite system is Brotown's distinctive gem mechanic — two elements combine on a weapon to produce specific elemental pairings. v13 preserves that core mechanic while simplifying the inventory/UX layer. Players no longer manage three item categories (gems, composites, weapons) — they manage two (gems, weapons). The weapon is the canvas on which elements are composed directly. This matches the conventional gem-slot pattern in ARPGs (players slot gems into weapon sockets, not into "socket items") while keeping Brotown's distinctive two-element-combination-produces-specific-pairings mechanic intact. Slot operations becoming free (both slot and unslot) removes a gold sink but creates experimentation — players try different pairings without economic friction. The lost gold sink is compensated by the preserved combination cost (leveling gems from Level N to Level N+1 still costs gold per §4.13.2). Per §0.2 progressive UI revelation, the weapon slot view appears only after the player has enchanted their first weapon; new Bros without enchanted weapons don't see slot UI they cannot yet use.]`

#### §4.13.1 Gem Types (CANONICAL preserved)

A gem has a **type** (the element it represents) and a **level** (1–10, with higher levels indicating stronger effects and access to higher combinations). The 10 elemental types are the same as the combat system's ten elements: Flame, Frost, Storm, Stone, Wind, Venom, Flora, Water, Dark, Light. Plus the special Shift gem type (§4.8), which is not a standard element but a rare Ascendant-area drop.

**Gem types are zone-locked.** A gem's type is determined by its source zone. Monsters in a fire zone (Ember Fields, volcanic caves, lava monuments) can only drop Flame gems. Mining nodes in a fire zone can only produce Flame gems. Every zone has a canonical element, and gems of that element come only from that zone's monsters and mining nodes. The full zone→element mapping lives in the zone data (`zones.json`); the mapping is the same one used for armor attunement and elemental ambient effects.

Players who want elements from zones they don't frequently visit have two options: travel to those zones and farm them directly, or trade for the gems on the marketplace. The zone-lock creates a natural inter-zone economy — players who specialize in fire-zone farming become suppliers of Flame gems to players who prefer other zones, and the marketplace stratifies by element supply.

##### §4.13.2 Gem Levels and Combination

Gems have 10 levels. A Level 1 gem is the baseline drop output and the result of basic mining. Higher-level gems are produced exclusively by **combining lower-level gems** at the Enchanter (or at a player-built Gem Cutting station once unlocked via the Gem Cutting life skill).

**Combination rule.** Combining **two gems of the same type and level** produces one gem of that type at level N+1. This operation requires a sufficient **Gem Cutting skill level** (simple threshold, mirroring Blacksmith skill gating):

| Gem Cutting Skill | Max Combination Level Allowed |
|:-----------------:|:-----------------------------:|
| 5 | Level 1 → Level 2 |
| 20 | Level 2 → Level 3 |
| 35 | Level 3 → Level 4 |
| 50 | Level 4 → Level 5 |
| 65 | Level 5 → Level 6 |
| 80 | Level 6 → Level 7 |
| 90 | Level 7 → Level 8 |
| 95 | Level 8 → Level 9 |
| 100 | Level 9 → Level 10 |

A player with Gem Cutting skill 65 can combine any gems up to Level 5 → Level 6, but cannot yet combine Level 6 gems into Level 7. The skill gates access, not success rate — combination is deterministic and always succeeds (there is no failure roll like hardening), but the combination is simply locked until the skill level is reached.

**Cost of combination.** Each combination operation costs a small amount of gold (`GEM_COMBINE_BASE_COST(25g) × (gem_level + 1)`) and produces 1 essence dust as a byproduct. The dust feeds the enchanting economy (§4.12). Gold sinks from gem combination contribute to the §23 economy ledger. (**v12.9 update:** base cost lowered from 50g to 25g per the v6.1 economy resolution — see §4.13.7 and §23.6a.2.)

**What higher levels do.** Higher-level gems produce stronger elemental effects when applied to a weapon. A Level 5 Flame gem applies a stronger Burn (more status damage per tick, longer duration) than a Level 1 Flame gem. The exact per-level scaling is tuned in the gem data (`gems.json`); a reference baseline is a ~15% effect increase per level, compounding. A Level 10 gem is roughly 4× as effective as a Level 1 gem.

Higher-level gems also **gate higher-level composites.** A composite of two Level N gems can only contain gems of level N — you cannot mix a Level 5 Flame gem with a Level 3 Frost gem into a single composite. This keeps composites level-consistent and gives the player a reason to level up *both* of their preferred gems rather than just one.

##### §4.13.3 Composites

A **composite** is a bundled pair of gems (or a single gem) produced at the Enchanter, ready to be applied to an enchanted weapon. Composites are the player's elemental build expressed as an item: the player takes their gems, decides what combination they want, produces the composite, and applies it to the weapon they want to wield that build on.

**Composite creation rules:**

- A composite contains either **one gem** (single-element composite) or **two gems of the same level** (two-element composite). Two-element composites can be same-element (pure) or different-element (fusion).
- Composites are produced at the Enchanter for a small gold fee (`COMPOSITE_CREATE_COST(10g)`). (**v12.9 update:** lowered from 25g to 10g per the v6.1 economy resolution.)
- A composite is a concrete inventory item with its own icon. It can be traded on the marketplace, stored in the bank, or held in inventory.
- A composite can be applied to any enchanted weapon the player owns. Applying the composite stores the composite reference in the weapon's `applied_composite` field.
- A weapon can hold **at most one composite** at a time. Applying a new composite replaces the previous one (which returns to the player's inventory as a separate item — it is not destroyed).

**Composites are not slots.** The weapon does not have "slot 1" and "slot 2" that hold individual gems. It has a single applied composite. Under the hood, the composite is a bundle of up to two gems whose positions are interpreted as auto-attack and swipe elements in the combat system (§10), but the player never interacts with gem slots directly — they interact with composites as whole objects.

**Orientation.** Two-element composites have an orientation — which element is auto-attack and which is swipe. Orientation is set when the composite is created at the Enchanter and can be flipped at any time via a "flip orientation" action at the Enchanter for a minor gold fee (`ORIENTATION_FLIP_COST(5g)`). Orientation determines volatile direction (§4.7) — the player chooses consciously which element takes which role. (**v12.9 update:** flip cost lowered from 10g to 5g per the v6.1 economy resolution.)

##### §4.13.4 Decoupling

A composite can be **decoupled** at the Enchanter to recover its constituent gems. Decoupling produces the gems unchanged (same type, same level) and returns them to the player's inventory. The composite itself is consumed in the process.

**Decoupling cost:**
- **Gold:** `DECOUPLE_BASE_COST(50g)` (minor, to prevent free experimentation loops). (**v12.9 update:** lowered from 75g to 50g per the v6.1 economy resolution.)
- **Time:** a brief delay at the Enchanter (reference baseline: 20 seconds of held interaction). The time delay is small but non-trivial — it adds friction to rapid recombination loops and makes the player's composite choices feel like real commitments rather than temporary configurations.
- **Essence dust:** decoupling produces 1 essence dust as a byproduct, mirroring the combination side of the loop.

Decoupling does not change the gems' levels. A composite of two Level 5 gems decouples back into two Level 5 gems, exactly as they were before combination. The gem *levels* are permanent; only the composite *bundling* is reversible.

##### §4.13.5 Shift Gems (Cross-Reference)

Shift gems are a special gem type specified in §4.8. They drop only from Ascendant areas, cannot be combined with non-Shift gems, and can only be combined with other Shift gems of the same level for level-up progression. Shift gems are applied to weapons as single-gem composites (no second gem partner) and grant the weapon adaptive elemental behavior.

**Shift gem adaptive behavior.** When applied to an enchanted weapon, a Shift gem causes the weapon's elemental identity to cycle between multiple elements based on combat context. The exact cycling mechanic: the Shift gem grants the weapon access to **3 randomly-selected non-special elements** (from the 10 standard elements, excluding Dark and Light), and the active element rotates automatically based on which element is most effective against the nearest enemy. The player does not control the rotation; the Shift gem does. This makes Shift weapons mechanically unique: the player trades fixed-build identity for adaptive optimization, and the weapon "picks its own element" during combat.

The 3 elements a Shift gem grants are rolled at drop time and are fixed for that gem. Two different Shift gems might grant different element sets. This gives Shift gems their own mini-lottery dimension — a player who wants Shift+Flame coverage has to find a Shift gem that happens to include Flame in its rolled set, or combine multiple Shift gems to level them up (which rerolls the element set on combination).

`[DESIGN INTENT: Shift gems are the design's one concession to "elements chosen by the game, not the player." Every other part of the v12.6 gem system is player-driven — the player chooses their zones, their gems, their combinations, their composites, their orientations. Shift gems invert this: the gem chooses. This is the correct framing for the Ascendant-area reward because the Ascendant areas are where the game's metaphysics start to break down (§55–§57) and a gem that "picks its own element" fits that framing. A Shift gem is not a tool; it is a companion that decides what to do in combat based on its own logic. The player who equips a Shift weapon is accepting that their combat identity is partially delegated to the weapon. This is weird and interesting and appropriate to the endgame zones that produced it.]`

##### §4.13.6 Gem Data Model

| Field | Type | Req | Desc |
|-------|------|:---:|------|
| gem_id | String | Y | Unique ID |
| gem_type | Flame/Frost/Storm/Stone/Wind/Venom/Flora/Water/Dark/Light/Shift | Y | Element type |
| gem_level | 1–10 | Y | Gem level |
| source_zone | ZoneId | Y | Zone where the gem originated (for traceability) |
| is_shift | Bool | Y | True for Shift gems (restricts combination rules) |
| shift_elements | Array<Element> | Shift only | The 3 elements a Shift gem grants (rolled at drop, fixed thereafter) |

| Composite Field | Type | Req | Desc |
|----------------|------|:---:|------|
| composite_id | String | Y | Unique ID |
| gem_1 | GemId | Y | First (auto-attack) gem reference |
| gem_2 | GemId/null | N | Second (swipe) gem reference. Null for single-element composites. |
| orientation | Normal/Flipped | Y | Which gem is auto-attack vs swipe |
| is_volatile | Bool | Y (derived) | Derived from gem types and orientation per §4.7 |

##### §4.13.7 Gem System Tuning

| Variable | Default | Min | Max | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| GEM_COMBINE_BASE_COST | 25g | 25g | 100g | Gold cost per combination, multiplied by (gem_level + 1). **v12.9: lowered from 50g default to 25g** to address the v6.1 economy breach (§23.6a.1). |
| COMPOSITE_CREATE_COST | 10g | 10g | 50g | Gold cost to create a composite from gems. **v12.9: lowered from 25g default to 10g** to address the v6.1 economy breach. |
| DECOUPLE_BASE_COST | 50g | 50g | 150g | Gold cost to decouple a composite. **v12.9: lowered from 75g default to 50g** to address the v6.1 economy breach. |
| DECOUPLE_TIME | 20s | 10s | 45s | Held-interaction time at the Enchanter |
| ORIENTATION_FLIP_COST | 5g | 5g | 25g | Gold cost to flip a composite's orientation. **v12.9: lowered from 10g default to 5g** to address the v6.1 economy breach. |
| ENCHANT_BASE_COST | 50g | 50g | 200g | Base gold cost to enchant a weapon, multiplied by material_tier. **v12.9: lowered from 100g default to 50g** to address the v6.1 economy breach. |
| DUST_NPC_BUYBACK_PRICE | 30g | 20g | 50g | **NEW in v12.9.** Floor price the Enchanter NPC offers for essence dust. Provides a guaranteed faucet for dust producers when the player marketplace is illiquid. See §23.6d. |
| ESSENCE_DUST_PER_OP | 1 | 1 | 2 | Essence dust produced per gem combine or decouple |
| GEM_LEVEL_EFFECT_SCALING | 1.15 | 1.10 | 1.25 | Per-level effect multiplier (compounding) |
| SHIFT_GEM_DROP_RATE_ASCENDANT | ~1/50 | 1/100 | 1/25 | Shift gem rate within Ascendant-area weapon drops |

**v12.9 tuning rationale.** The v12.6 gem economy defaults were set without running them through the v6 economy validator. The v12.8 v6.1 validator pass found that the original defaults pushed the model out of band at Lv 50 and Lv 100. The v12.9 defaults move every gem operation cost to the lower end of its tunable range, which (combined with the new DUST_NPC_BUYBACK_PRICE faucet introduced in v12.9) brings the model back within the §23.6 bands. **All other constants in the table are unchanged.** The lower-end choice uses up the available downward tuning headroom, which means future versions cannot lower these costs further without changing the min values themselves. If Phase 1 testing shows the economy is too inflationary even at the v12.9 minimums, the next adjustment would have to come from elsewhere (additional sinks or a separate faucet adjustment), not from these costs. This is a deliberate one-way commitment: v12.9 trades future tuning flexibility for the ability to close the v6.1 breach without widening the §23.6 bands.

#### §4.14 Enchanting and Gem System — Interaction Summary

The following is the player's interaction flow with the v12.6 elemental system, from acquisition to application:

1. **Acquire a base weapon.** Any weapon drop from any monster. The weapon is unenchanted, has no gems, is just a material + type + quality + hardness 0 base weapon.
2. **Enchant the weapon.** Take the weapon to the Enchanter. Pay gold (scales with material tier) + essence dust. The weapon becomes enchanted permanently. It still has no elements — it is just *capable* of holding composites now.
3. **Acquire gems.** Farm zone-appropriate monsters and mine zone-appropriate ore nodes to collect gems. Gems come as Level 1 by default. Zone-locked: Flame gems from fire zones, etc. Alternatively, buy gems on the marketplace.
4. **Level up gems (optional).** If you want stronger effects or higher-level composites, take two gems of the same type and level to the Enchanter (or a Gem Cutting station) and combine them into one gem of the next level. This requires Gem Cutting skill to unlock the combination level (§4.13.2).
5. **Create a composite.** Take one or two gems (of the same level if two) to the Enchanter. Pay the composite creation fee. Choose the orientation (which element is auto-attack, which is swipe). The composite appears in your inventory as a distinct item.
6. **Apply the composite to your weapon.** At the Enchanter, apply the composite to an enchanted weapon. The composite is now the weapon's current elemental identity.
7. **Use the weapon in combat.** Collisions, volatile/stable direction, and element effects all flow from the applied composite.
8. **Swap composites freely.** A player can create multiple composites for different situations and swap which is applied to a weapon at any time (no cost beyond the one-time composite creation). They can also decouple a composite to recover the gems (at the cost of the composite object itself).

The flow is longer than "drop a Fusion weapon" was in v12.4, but the depth is meaningfully greater. A player's elemental build is now a conscious expression of their farming, gem-leveling, and composite-building decisions, not an accident of what the loot table happened to drop.

### §5. Actions, Timing, and State Machine

#### §5.1 Action Data Model

| Field | Type | Req | Desc |
|-------|------|:---:|------|
| action_id | String | Y | Unique ID |
| action_type | AutoAttack/SwipeTrigger/Dodge/Block/Movement/Item/Lunge/RetreatShot | Y | Resolution path |
| timing_profile | {cast_time, active_frames, recovery} | Y | Seconds |
| resource_costs | Map<Resource, Amount> | N | Checked before execution |
| cooldown | Float(s) | N | 0=none |
| interrupt_policy | Interruptible/HyperArmor/Unstoppable | Y | Stagger/CC interaction |
| damage_event | DamageEvent | N | Null for non-damaging |
| status_application | StatusApplication | N | Status to apply on hit |
| tags | Set<String> | N | MELEE, RANGED, AOE, etc. |
| poise_damage | Float | N | Default 0 |

#### §5.2 Action Resolution Pipeline

```
1. VALIDATION    — Valid state? Resources affordable? Off cooldown?
2. CONSUME       — Deduct resources. Start cooldown.
3. STATE         — Transition per action_type.
4. TIMING        — Cast → Active → Recovery (buffer active during Recovery).
5. EFFECTS       — Ordered sub-steps (each completes before next):
   5a. DAMAGE        → DamageInterceptorChain.process() → DamageResolver.resolve()
   5b. STATUS        → StatusManager.apply_status()
   5c. COLLISION     → CollisionResolver.check() [if 5b applied different element than existing]
   5d. MANA RESTORE  → §3.5 [if 5c consumed setup]
   5e. SIGNAL        → Fire OnActionResolved
6. STATE RETURN  — →Idle. Buffered input → back to Step 1.
```

**Swipe trigger** follows the same pipeline. At 5a it deals the swipe's element damage. At 5b it applies the swipe's element status. At 5c it checks for collision with any existing different-element status on the target. The auto-attack element is the setup. The swipe element is the trigger.

**Lunge and Retreat Shot** follow the same pipeline. At 5b, both apply element_1 status (the auto-attack element — setup, not detonation). At 5c, collision check fires only if the target has a pre-existing different-element status. Both are setup tools, not trigger tools. See §5.8.

> **v12.6 legacy field reference.** The §5.x prose and pseudocode below reference `element_1` and `element_2` as if they were stored fields on the weapon. **They are not.** Under v12.6 (§4.9), the weapon stores only its applied composite reference, and the auto-attack/swipe elements are derived from the composite at query time. The illustrative pseudocode treats `element_1` and `element_2` as accessor methods that resolve to the composite's first/second gem element, in the orientation set when the composite was applied. A reader of the §5.x pseudocode should mentally translate `player.weapon.element_1` as `player.weapon.applied_composite.gem_1.element` (and similarly for `element_2`). The §5.x logic itself is unchanged from prior versions — what changed is where the elements come from.

#### §5.3 Poise and Stagger

**Poise Threshold (CANONICAL):** `POISE_BASE(20) + Fortification × POISE_FRT_COEFF(0.04)`

At Fortification 400: poise = 20 + 16 = 36.

Regen: 40%/s after POISE_REGEN_DELAY(3.0s). Stagger duration: STAGGER_DUR(0.8s).

#### §5.3b Active Block (v12.27 CANONICAL)

Block is an **active tap-at-impact input**, not a held stance. Pre-v12.27, holding the block input put the player in a BLOCKING state with continuous partial damage reduction while held. Under Active Block, blocking is a timed reactive action — the player taps the block input *at the moment of incoming impact* and the system resolves the block based on how close the tap landed to the hit.

**The three timing outcomes:**

- **Perfect block** — tap within PERFECT_BLOCK_WINDOW(0.15s) before impact → full damage negation (damage = 0), stamina refund of PERFECT_BLOCK_STAM_REFUND(10), distinct audio-visual signature (metallic chime + gold flash on weapon/shield).
- **Standard block** — tap within BLOCK_COMMITMENT_WINDOW(0.4s) before impact but outside the perfect window → reduced damage (applies `block_reduction` from §2.3), standard block stamina cost BLOCK_STAM_COST(15), standard block impact sound.
- **No block** — no tap, or tap outside the 0.4s commitment window → full damage taken, no stamina effect.

**Mechanic shape.**

```
on block_tap:
    player.record_block_tap(server_tick_time)

# In damage pipeline P10 Block interceptor (§8.2):
on incoming_hit at impact_time:
    block_tap = player.get_block_tap_within(impact_time - BLOCK_COMMITMENT_WINDOW, impact_time)
    if block_tap is None:
        # Interceptor passes through; full damage applies
        return hit unchanged
    tap_delta = impact_time - block_tap.time
    if tap_delta <= PERFECT_BLOCK_WINDOW:
        # Perfect block
        hit.damage = 0
        player.refund_stamina(PERFECT_BLOCK_STAM_REFUND)
        emit_event('perfect_block', player, hit.attacker)
    else:
        # Standard block
        hit.damage = hit.damage × (1 - block_reduction)   # §2.3
        player.consume_stamina(BLOCK_STAM_COST)
        emit_event('standard_block', player, hit.attacker)
    consume_tap(block_tap)   # Each tap resolves at most one hit
    return hit
```

**One tap, one block.** Each block tap is consumed by the first hit it successfully intercepts. A player tapping block during a multi-enemy pile-on blocks one hit; to block subsequent hits requires subsequent taps. This preserves Active Block as a precision mechanic rather than an AoE immunity button.

**Buffer window.** Block taps register on any input during Idle or Recovering states (§5.4). A tap during Attacking or other committed states is a null input (the block cannot trigger because the player is committed elsewhere). Block tap input during Staggered or CC'd states is null (consistent with the §5.4 table).

**Damage eligibility.** Active Block works on:
- **Direct attacks** (weapon strikes, monster attacks, PvP attacks) — yes
- **Swipe triggers** (element swipe effects) — yes
- **Brute Collapse impact frames** — yes (Brute Collapse produces a direct-attack damage event per §13; Active Block can intercept)
- **Reflected damage** (e.g., Thorns, Counter Resonance generation=1) — yes, symmetric with direct attacks
- **DoT ticks** (Burn, Venom Root DoT) — **no**, block cannot intercept DoT
- **Collision damage** — **no**, consistent with dodge (collisions cannot be dodged either — §8.2)
- **Environmental damage** (zone hazards, fall damage) — **no**

**Threat generation.** Perfect blocks still generate block-threat at the *full* amount of damage that would have hit (per §11 Threat and §20.4a). This preserves Tank identity — perfect-blocking a hit does not reduce the threat generated by successfully defending against it. Standard blocks generate threat based on the reduced damage mitigated. The threat formula:

- Perfect block: `threat = pre_reduction_damage × BLOCK_THREAT_MULT(2.0)`
- Standard block: `threat = mitigated_damage × BLOCK_THREAT_MULT(2.0)`
- Unblocked hit: `threat = damage × DIRECT_THREAT_MULT(1.0)` (standard)

Tank play pattern is preserved: perfect-blocking a Brute Collapse generates high threat (keeping aggro) plus fully negates the damage (keeping the Tank alive). This is the design's cleanest heroic moment.

**No interrupt.** Perfect block does not interrupt the attacker's recovery animation — it is a defensive reward, not a parry. Attacker continues their planned action; the player simply took zero damage from this particular hit. A separate parry mechanic, if added later (Phase 2+), would be its own subsection.

**PvP behavior.** Active Block works identically in PvP. A player who perfect-blocks an opposing player's attack gets the damage negation, stamina refund, and threat generation just as in PvE. No PvP-specific modifiers.

**Stamina during block input.** A failed block tap (no hit within the commitment window) produces no stamina effect — the tap expires silently. This prevents a "tap-spam to refill stamina through failed blocks" exploit and matches the design intent that only successful blocks have mechanical effect.

**The pre-v12.27 held-block stance is removed.** There is no player state "Blocking" during active play. The `BLOCKING` value in the player state enum is retained as a deprecated placeholder for backwards compatibility with serialized saves (see Appendix A) but the game never enters this state. No systems key on it. All block logic flows through the active-tap mechanic.

**Tuning (§20.1):**

| Variable | Default | Min | Max |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|
| PERFECT_BLOCK_WINDOW | 0.15s | 0.10s | 0.20s |
| BLOCK_COMMITMENT_WINDOW | 0.4s | 0.3s | 0.5s |
| PERFECT_BLOCK_STAM_REFUND | 10 | 5 | 20 |

**Creative direction:** see `creative-reference.md` §8 for the Active Block feel (the perfect-block metallic chime, gold flash on weapon, and "committed choice" felt moment).

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.27): Pre-v12.27 block was a passive stance the player held. Players could set block and let it absorb damage continuously at the cost of stamina. The mechanic was defensive but passive — a stance, not a decision. Active Block reshapes block into a committed reactive action that rewards reading the attack, timing the tap, and accepting the cost of failure. The three-tier timing outcome (perfect / standard / miss) gives the mechanic the same skill-expressive shape that dodge already has.
