A player who taps early misses and takes full damage; a player who taps late misses the same way; a player who taps *right* gets the heroic outcome (full negation, stamina refund, the chime). Tank play pattern is preserved because Perfect Block still generates full threat — the Tank reading a Brute Collapse and perfect-blocking it produces the iconic moment (damage negated, threat held, party saved). Standard-block threat scales with mitigated damage, matching the intuition that a partial block is partial credit. The BLOCKING state removal is load-bearing — it ensures block cannot be ambiguously "held" and rules out exploits where players could hold block indefinitely at stamina cost for passive damage reduction. Every block in v12.27+ is a committed decision in the moment.]`

#### §5.4 Player States

| State | Available Actions | Damage Reception |
|-------|------------------|------------------|
| Idle | All | Normal (block tap may fire — see §5.3b) |
| Attacking | Input buffer | Per interrupt_policy |
| Recovering | Buffer + dodge cancel + block tap | Normal (block tap may fire — see §5.3b) |
| Dodging | None (committed) | Immune during i-frames, then normal |
| Lunging | None (committed) | Immune during i-frames (shorter than Dodge), then normal |
| Retreating | None (committed) | Normal (no i-frames) |
| Staggered | None | Normal (no dodge/block — block tap is null input) |
| CC'd | Root: no move, can attack. Freeze: no action, can aim. | Normal (block tap is null input while CC'd) |
| Dead | Respawn only | None |
| Disconnected | None | Immune |

**Blocking state removed (v12.27).** The pre-v12.27 "Blocking" player state represented a committed-to-block stance. Under Active Block (§5.3b), block is an event-triggered mitigation rather than a persistent state. The state enum retains the `Blocking` value as a deprecated placeholder (see Appendix A) for backwards compatibility with serialized saves, but the game never enters this state during play and no interceptors or systems key on it.

**Transition Priority** (lower wins): 0=Dead, 1=Staggered, 2=CC'd, 3=Dodging/Lunging/Retreating, 4=Attacking, 5=Recovering, 6=Idle.

#### §5.5 Death and Respawn


**Death:** 50% of carried inventory scatters on ground. Equipped gear is SAFE. Banked items are SAFE. DEATH_SCATTER_RECOVERY(30s) recovery window. Escalating respawn: RESPAWN_BASE(5s) + 2s per death within 120s window, max 15s.

**Godly weapon protection (v12.7 CANONICAL).** Godly weapons (§4.6b.1) are exempt from all loss-on-death mechanics. They cannot be scattered, dropped, lost, or stripped under any circumstances. This applies in every zone including Lawless Land. The protection is a property of the *object* and travels with the weapon regardless of who currently holds it — a player who *bought* a Godly weapon on the marketplace receives the same on-death protection as the original recipient. See §4.6b.1 for the full sacred-properties specification.

**Mystery item death behavior (v12.7 CANONICAL).** Mystery items (unrevealed Rare-or-better drops, see §4.6b.ii) **are not protected on death** even though they *might* be Godly. Mystery items obey ordinary inventory rules: they scatter in standard zones per the 50% rule, and they are stripped entirely in Lawless Land. A player who picks up a question mark and dies before revealing it loses the mystery item along with the rest of their inventory, and a killer who picks up the scattered or stripped mystery item can reveal it themselves. **The protection only attaches once the reveal completes and the underlying grade is confirmed Godly.** Until reveal, the item is just an unrevealed possibility, and possibilities are subject to mortal risk. This creates a real decision moment for any player carrying a mystery item: reveal at the drop site to know what you have and gain protection if it's Godly, or carry the unrevealed item to safety while accepting the risk that it might be lost in transit. The decision is intentionally sharp — it's the design's way of making mystery items *meaningful* objects to carry rather than free dopamine.

**Lawless Death:** In the wasteland (lawless:true zone), death strips ALL equipment, inventory, gold, and weapon stash, **except Godly weapons** (which remain in the holder's inventory per the sacred properties of §4.6b.1). Everything else, **including unrevealed mystery items**, is scattered as recoverable loot for any player to pick up. Respawn in town.

**Respawn:** RESPAWN_BASE(5s), escalation +2s per death within 120s window, max 15s.

| Context | Respawn Location |
|---------|-----------------|
| Open world | Nearest SafeZone |
| Dungeon (party) | Entrance or last checkpoint |
| Dungeon (solo) | Entrance only |
| Boss | Boss room entrance. Boss HP resets only if ALL dead simultaneously. |

On respawn: HP/stamina/mana = max. All cooldowns reset.
Respawn immunity: RESPAWN_IMMUNITY(3s). Can't attack/apply status. Can move.

**Death presentation:** Whimsical. Limbs detach in a comical pile. The visual language says "that was unfortunate" not "that was devastating."

**Killing blow display:** The death screen shows a single line identifying the source and element of the final damage event: `last_damage_event.source_name (last_damage_event.element)`. Examples: "Killed by Ember Brute (Flame)", "Killed by Burn DoT", "Killed by Magma collision splash", "Killed by Lava (Environmental)". No attribution chain, no multi-source breakdown — just the final hit. The player learns what killed them through patterns across multiple deaths, not through a single detailed analysis. Displayed below the respawn timer in the same font as damage numbers.

#### §5.6 Core Timing Values

| Concept | Value |
|---------|-------|
| Input buffer window | 0.15s |
| Dodge stamina cost | 25 |
| Dodge cooldown | 0.1s |
| Dodge total duration | 0.6s |
| Swipe cooldown | 2.0s |
| Block stamina per hit | 15 |
| Stagger duration | 0.8s |
| Lunge stamina cost | 30 |
| Lunge i-frames | 0.15s |
| Lunge active frames | 0.3s |
| Lunge recovery | 0.2s |
| Retreat shot stamina cost | 25 |
| Retreat shot active frames | 0.4s |
| Retreat shot recovery | 0.15s |

#### §5.7 Resonance Window

##### §5.7.1 Overview

When a status is active on a target and the player holds a weapon with a different element ready to trigger a collision, the status enters a **resonance window** during the final portion of its duration. Triggering a collision during the resonance window grants bonus damage and bonus mana restore. The resonance window is the skill-expression layer of the collision system — it rewards timing without punishing immediate triggers.

`[DESIGN INTENT: The collision system's core loop — apply status, trigger collision — currently has no timing pressure. The player applies a status and triggers as soon as swipe cooldown allows. Resonance adds a hold-or-fire decision to every collision: trigger now for guaranteed damage, or wait for the resonance window and risk the status expiring. Against Fodder you trigger immediately because they'll die anyway. Against Brutes you wait because you need the bonus. Against Swarms you can't afford to wait because more enemies are closing in. Same two inputs, different timing decisions per encounter.]`

##### §5.7.2 Resonance Mechanics (CANONICAL)

**Resonance activation condition:** A status on a target enters resonance when ALL of the following are true:
1. The target has an active status of element Y
2. The player's current weapon has a different element X capable of triggering a collision with status Y (valid pair exists in §10.5)
3. The status remaining duration ≤ `status.max_duration × RESONANCE_WINDOW_RATIO`

**Resonance window size:** The final 25% of the status duration.

```
resonance_start = status.max_duration × (1 - RESONANCE_WINDOW_RATIO(0.25))
is_resonating = status.remaining_duration <= (status.max_duration × RESONANCE_WINDOW_RATIO(0.25))
```

Example: Burn has a 4.0s base duration. Resonance begins at 3.0s elapsed (1.0s remaining). With Elemental Mastery extending duration to 4.64s, resonance begins at 3.48s elapsed (1.16s remaining). The window scales with duration — Influence builds that extend statuses get wider resonance windows.

**Resonance damage bonus (CANONICAL):**

```
func get_resonance_multiplier(status):
    if not is_resonating(status): return 1.0
    
    # How deep into the resonance window (0.0 = just entered, 1.0 = about to expire)
    window_total = status.max_duration × RESONANCE_WINDOW_RATIO(0.25)
    window_elapsed = window_total - status.remaining_duration
    resonance_depth = clamp(window_elapsed / window_total, 0.0, 1.0)
    
    # Linear scaling from base to peak
    bonus = RESONANCE_BONUS_BASE(0.10) + resonance_depth × (RESONANCE_BONUS_PEAK(0.30) - RESONANCE_BONUS_BASE(0.10))
    
    return 1.0 + bonus
```

At resonance window entry: +10% collision damage. At final frame before expiry: +30% collision damage. Linear interpolation between.

**Resonance mana restore bonus:** The existing timing_mult in §3.5 already scales mana restore based on remaining duration. Resonance unifies this — the §3.5 timing scaling IS the resonance mana bonus. No additional mana multiplier needed. The formula in §3.5 already rewards late triggers:

```
timing_mult = 1.0 + (1.0 - remaining / max_dur) × 0.5
```

At resonance entry (25% remaining): timing_mult = 1.375. At expiry: timing_mult = 1.5. This is now explicitly named as part of the resonance system rather than an unnamed scaling factor.

##### §5.7.3 Resonance Visual Feedback

**Status icon pulsing:** When a status enters the resonance window, the status icon above the target begins pulsing. Pulse rate accelerates linearly as the status approaches expiry:

```
pulse_frequency = RESONANCE_PULSE_BASE(1.5) + resonance_depth × RESONANCE_PULSE_ACCEL(3.5)
```

At resonance entry: 1.5 pulses/second (gentle). At final frame: 5.0 pulses/second (urgent).

**Pulse visual:** The status element's shape glyph (§10.1) scales between 80% and 120% of normal size on each pulse cycle. The glyph's color brightens toward white at higher resonance depth — at maximum depth the glyph is near-white with only a tint of its element color remaining. This is visible to all players, not just the status applicator.

**Resonance ring:** When a collision is triggered during the resonance window, the collision ground mark (§10.7) renders with an additional bright ring around its perimeter. Ring brightness proportional to resonance_depth at trigger time. A collision triggered at maximum resonance depth produces a visibly brighter, more dramatic ground mark than a standard collision. This is the spectator-visible skill signal.

**No UI indicator for the window itself.** No progress bar, no timer overlay, no popup. The pulse IS the indicator. The game teaches timing through feel, not through UI.

##### §5.7.4 Resonance and Build Interactions

**Influence builds benefit from wider windows.** Influence extends CC-type status durations (Freeze, Root, Slow) via §2.6. Longer durations mean the 25% resonance window is proportionally longer in absolute seconds. A high-Influence player applying a 5.0s Freeze (extended from 3.0s base) has a 1.25s resonance window instead of a 0.75s window.

**Elemental Mastery builds benefit from higher peak damage.** The resonance multiplier applies to collision damage, which is already multiplied by EM collision coefficient (§2.2). The multiplicative stacking rewards EM investment for timed collisions.

**Agility builds benefit from easier access.** Higher attack speed (§4.5) means more auto-attack hits to refresh/maintain the status while waiting for the resonance window.

**Fortification builds are neutral.** Resonance doesn't interact with defensive stats. Tank players trigger collisions at whatever timing is safe.

##### §5.7.5 Resonance and Collision Resolution Integration

**Modification to §10.6 collision damage multiplication chain:**

After collision damage is calculated via §10.6 base formula, before downstream multipliers:

```
# Resonance bonus (§5.7)
if is_resonating(consumed_status):
    event.base_damage ×= get_resonance_multiplier(consumed_status)
```

Updated full multiplication chain:
```
collision_damage = (COLLISION_BASE + scaling_stat × COLLISION_COEFF)
    × Resonance multiplier (§5.7, 1.0–1.30)
    × Elemental Mastery collision mult (§2.2)
    × Volatile bonus if directional match (§4.7, ×1.30)
    × Armor attunement offense if matching (×1.20)
    × Effectiveness vs target element (§10.2)
```

##### §5.7.6 Resonance Tuning

| Variable | Default | Min | Max | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| RESONANCE_WINDOW_RATIO | 0.25 | 0.15 | 0.40 | Fraction of status duration |
| RESONANCE_BONUS_BASE | 0.10 | 0.05 | 0.15 | Damage mult at window entry |
| RESONANCE_BONUS_PEAK | 0.30 | 0.20 | 0.50 | Damage mult at expiry |
| RESONANCE_PULSE_BASE | 1.5 Hz | 1.0 | 2.5 | Pulse rate at window entry |
| RESONANCE_PULSE_ACCEL | 3.5 Hz | 2.0 | 5.0 | Additional pulse rate at expiry |

##### §5.7.7 Resonance Streak (v12.38 CANONICAL)

Consecutive resonance-timed collisions within a rolling time window compound a **mana restoration bonus** — each streak step increases the mana restored by the next resonance-timed collision, producing a positive-sustain flow-state feedback loop for players who reliably land their collisions within the resonance window.

**Mechanic.**

- **Streak increment.** Each resonance-timed collision within `RESONANCE_STREAK_WINDOW`(10s) of the previous resonance-timed collision increments the player's streak counter by 1. A collision outside the window (or a non-resonance collision) silently resets the streak to zero.
- **Mana restore bonus per step.** The mana restored by each resonance-timed collision is multiplied by `(1 + streak_count × RESONANCE_STREAK_MANA_BONUS)`, capping at `1 + RESONANCE_STREAK_CAP`. With defaults (bonus 0.10/step, cap 0.50), streaks of 0/1/2/3/4/5+ produce mana-restore multipliers of 1.00×/1.10×/1.20×/1.30×/1.40×/1.50×.
- **Streak cap at 5 steps.** The cap corresponds to a 5-step streak (1.50×) — further streak increments do not increase the bonus. This bounds the feedback loop at a sustainable level rather than allowing arbitrary escalation.
- **Per-player, not per-party.** Each player builds their own streak independently in party play. A player whose partymate chains resonance collisions does not inherit any streak bonus.

**Baseline mana restore source.** The mana-restore bonus applies to the existing per-collision mana restoration (from §9.3 status consumption refund and §10.6 collision mana mechanics). Pre-v12.38 each collision restores a fixed amount of mana to the triggering player; v12.38 scales that amount by the streak multiplier without changing the baseline restoration rule.

**UI feedback.** The streak counter displays next to the player's combo count display (§5.9 Combo Chain UI area) when streak ≥ 1. Shown as a small number badge with an implicit countdown — the badge fades slightly as the streak's time-remaining approaches the 10s window boundary. Loss of streak (timeout or non-resonance collision) is **quiet** — no audio sting, no screen effect, no popup. The badge simply disappears. The design intent is that streak loss feels like gravity rather than punishment; players notice when they're in flow and feel flow's absence when it's gone, without the game narrating either state.

**No damage bonus.** The v12.38 streak mechanic does NOT amplify collision damage. This is the design's load-bearing choice: amplifying damage at the resonance-skill ceiling would push toward the INV-16 Damage Ceiling (§22) and require either a ceiling adjustment or a separate post-cap multiplier layer. More importantly, the damage-amplification framing fights the emotional register of flow state — ARPG flow feels *tireless*, not *devastating*. The mana-restore amplification captures the "tireless" register: a chained-resonance player refills mana faster, enabling more swipes, enabling more potential collisions — a positive sustain loop rather than a damage escalator.

**Interaction with §3.5 mana entropy grace period.** The mana-restore amplification stacks cleanly with the existing mana-entropy grace period mechanics. A player sustaining a high streak experiences both (1) reduced mana decay from the grace period (rewarding engagement rhythm) and (2) amplified mana restoration from the streak (rewarding precision). The two systems compound the flow-state feeling without double-counting — grace period reduces one side of the ledger (mana loss rate), streak increases the other (mana gain amount).

**INV-16 unaffected.** The streak mechanic is mana-side only. Damage-stack ceiling at 3.2× (combo × volatile × EM × attunement × resonance, excluding effectiveness multipliers) is unchanged. No Monte Carlo re-derivation required. The v12.38 ship confirms INV-16 holds exactly as specified in §22.

**Data model.** `rpg._combat.resonance_streak` stores `{ count: number, last_resonance_timestamp: number }`. Evaluated and updated server-authoritatively on every resonance-timed collision. Client displays the badge based on state pulled from the existing combat state payload — no new network message required.

**Tuning.**

| Variable | Default | Min | Max | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| RESONANCE_STREAK_WINDOW | 10s | 5s | 20s | Rolling window for consecutive resonance collisions |
| RESONANCE_STREAK_MANA_BONUS | 0.10 | 0.05 | 0.15 | Per-step mana-restore bonus |
| RESONANCE_STREAK_CAP | 0.50 | 0.25 | 0.75 | Max cumulative mana-restore bonus (5-step cap at defaults) |

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.38): The original Resonance Streak plan spec compounded damage at the resonance-skill ceiling — the very place the game's damage-stack ceiling already lives. This would have forced either an INV-16 adjustment or a post-cap layer, both of which undermine the ceiling's clarity. More importantly, it missed the actual emotional register the streak mechanic should capture. When an ARPG player is in flow state, they don't feel like they're hitting harder; they feel like they're *never running out*. Mana is the game's scarcity resource, and the streak's effect is to relieve that scarcity briefly in a way directly tied to skilled play. The positive-sustain loop (hit resonance → restore more mana → cast more swipes → hit more resonance) is the feel of flow state in ARPG terms, and it's structurally distinct from the damage-ceiling system so it adds a new expressive dimension without disturbing an existing one. The 10-second window is tight enough that the player must sustain engagement rather than chain collisions across exploration; the 5-step cap bounds the loop at a sustainable level. The quiet streak-loss (no audio, no popup) keeps the feature textural rather than punishing — players notice when they're in flow, and feel flow's absence without the game narrating either state. This is the v12.38 bundle's contribution to the combat-sensory compilation thread: recognition through mana-system flow, parallel to v12.36's recognition-moments thread but operating in the per-second feedback layer rather than the per-milestone layer.]`

#### §5.8 Contextual Dodge (Lunge & Retreat Shot)

##### §5.8.1 Overview

The dodge input (screen swipe on mobile, Spacebar on desktop) is context-sensitive based on lock-on state, swipe direction, and equipped weapon type. The same input produces three distinct actions:

| Context | Input Direction | Equipped Weapon | Result |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|--------|
| No lock-on | Any | Any | **Dodge** (standard §5.4) |
| Locked on | Away from target | Any melee | **Dodge** (standard) |
| Locked on | Toward target | Melee | **Lunge** |
| Locked on | Away from target | Ranged (bow/staff) | **Retreat Shot** |
| Locked on | Toward target | Ranged (bow/staff) | **Dodge** (standard — closing distance with ranged is not rewarded) |
| Locked on | Lateral (perpendicular) | Any | **Dodge** (standard) |

**Zero new inputs.** The player's existing muscle memory for dodge is preserved. Lunge and Retreat Shot are discovered naturally through context.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Combat currently lacks an engagement verb. The approach to every fight is "walk into range, start auto-attacking." Lunge gives melee an opener — a way to close distance with initiative and begin the collision setup in the same motion. Retreat Shot gives ranged a repositioning tool that doubles as setup — disengaging seeds the battlefield for the next collision. Both emerge from the existing dodge input with no new buttons, no new UI, no new tutorial screens. The context does the teaching.]`

##### §5.8.2 Lunge (CANONICAL)

**Trigger conditions:**
1. Player has an enemy locked on (§15.2 Tap Enemy)
2. Swipe direction is toward the locked target (within ±45° of the vector from player to target)
3. Active weapon is melee (Greatsword or Sword)
4. Dodge is off cooldown
5. Stamina ≥ LUNGE_STAMINA_COST

**Resolution:**

```
func resolve_lunge(player, target):
    # Resource check
    if player.stamina < LUNGE_STAMINA_COST: return resolve_dodge(player, direction)
    player.consume_stamina(LUNGE_STAMINA_COST)
    player.start_cooldown('dodge', DODGE_CD)
    
    # Movement — same distance as dodge, toward target
    lunge_direction = normalize(target.position - player.position)
    lunge_distance = DODGE_DIST_BASE + player.agility × DODGE_DIST_COEFF  # Same formula as dodge (§2.5)
    player.move(lunge_direction × lunge_distance)
    
    # I-frames — shorter than dodge (offensive choice = less safety)
    player.set_state(LUNGING)
    player.iframes_remaining = LUNGE_IFRAMES
    
    # Damage on arrival — reduced weapon damage
    if distance_after_lunge(player, target) <= melee_range(player):
        lunge_event = DamageEvent {
            base_damage: player.weapon_damage × LUNGE_DAMAGE_MULT,
            type: PHYSICAL,
            source: DIRECT_ATTACK,
            element: player.weapon.element_1,  # Auto-attack element — setup, not trigger
            generation: 0,
            poise_damage: LUNGE_POISE_DAMAGE
        }
        
        # Route through standard action resolution pipeline §5.2 steps 5a-5e
        # Step 5b applies element_1 status (setup, not detonation)
        # Step 5c checks for collision ONLY if target already has a different status
        DamageInterceptorChain.process(lunge_event)
        StatusManager.apply_status(target, player.weapon.element_1)
        CollisionResolver.check(target, player.weapon.element_1)
```

**Key properties:**
- **Same distance as dodge.** Agility investment benefits both equally.
- **Shorter i-frames than dodge.** LUNGE_IFRAMES < DODGE_IFRAMES_BASE. The player is choosing aggression — less invulnerability as a tradeoff.
- **Applies auto-attack element (setup).** The lunge hit applies element_1 status. The lunge opens the collision sentence — it does not resolve it.
- **Does NOT trigger collisions on its own** unless the target already has a pre-existing different-element status from another source.
- **Reduced damage.** LUNGE_DAMAGE_MULT is less than 1.0. The lunge is a positioning + status application tool.
- **Shares dodge cooldown.** You cannot lunge and then immediately dodge. The player commits their movement resource.
- **Moderate poise damage.** Contributes to stagger threshold without trivializing it.

##### §5.8.3 Retreat Shot (CANONICAL)

**Trigger conditions:**
1. Player has an enemy locked on (§15.2 Tap Enemy)
2. Swipe direction is away from the locked target (outside ±45° of the vector from player to target)
3. Active weapon is ranged (Bow or Staff)
4. Dodge is off cooldown
5. Stamina ≥ RETREAT_SHOT_STAMINA_COST

**Resolution:**

```
func resolve_retreat_shot(player, target, swipe_direction):
    # Resource check
    if player.stamina < RETREAT_SHOT_STAMINA_COST: return resolve_dodge(player, swipe_direction)
    player.consume_stamina(RETREAT_SHOT_STAMINA_COST)
    player.start_cooldown('dodge', DODGE_CD)
    
    # Movement — dodge away in swipe direction (identical to standard dodge)
    dodge_distance = DODGE_DIST_BASE + player.agility × DODGE_DIST_COEFF
    player.move(normalize(swipe_direction) × dodge_distance)
    
    # NO i-frames — the shot is the tradeoff for safety
    player.set_state(RETREATING)
    player.iframes_remaining = 0
    
    # Fire status-applying shot at locked target during movement
    retreat_event = DamageEvent {
        base_damage: player.weapon_damage × RETREAT_SHOT_DAMAGE_MULT,
        type: ELEMENTAL,
        source: DIRECT_ATTACK,
        element: player.weapon.element_1,  # Auto-attack element — setup, not trigger
        generation: 0,
        poise_damage: 0  # Parting shots don't stagger
    }
    
    # Projectile fires at locked target regardless of player facing
    fire_projectile(player, target, retreat_event)
    
    # Standard pipeline resolves on hit — status applied as setup
    # Step 5b applies element_1 status
    # Step 5c checks for collision (rare — retreat shot is a setup tool)
```

**Key properties:**
- **Zero i-frames.** The retreat shot trades invulnerability for offensive utility. The player is vulnerable during the movement.
- **Guaranteed status application on hit.** Seeds the battlefield during repositioning.
- **Reduced damage.** Setup tool, not damage tool.
- **No poise damage.** A parting shot while retreating shouldn't stagger.
- **Fires at locked target.** The projectile auto-targets the locked enemy regardless of the player's movement direction.
- **Shares dodge cooldown.** You commit your movement resource — retreat shot OR dodge, not both.
- **Staff cone exception.** When a Staff is equipped, the retreat shot fires a narrow cone (RETREAT_STAFF_CONE) in the target's direction, potentially hitting multiple enemies near the locked target.

##### §5.8.4 Direction Detection (CANONICAL)

```
func resolve_dodge_context(player, swipe_direction):
    # No lock-on: always standard dodge
    if not player.has_lock_on: return resolve_dodge(player, swipe_direction)
    
    target = player.lock_on_target
    to_target = normalize(target.position - player.position)
    dot = dot_product(normalize(swipe_direction), to_target)
    
    # Toward target: dot > cos(45°) ≈ 0.707
    is_toward = dot > LUNGE_DIRECTION_THRESHOLD(0.707)
    
    # Away from target: dot < -cos(45°) ≈ -0.707
    is_away = dot < -LUNGE_DIRECTION_THRESHOLD(0.707)
    
    # Lateral swipes (between ±45° and ±135°): always standard dodge
    if not is_toward and not is_away:
        return resolve_dodge(player, swipe_direction)
    
    # Toward + melee = Lunge
    if is_toward and player.weapon_is_melee():
        return resolve_lunge(player, target)
    
    # Away + ranged = Retreat Shot
    if is_away and player.weapon_is_ranged():
        return resolve_retreat_shot(player, target, swipe_direction)
    
    # Toward + ranged = standard dodge (don't lunge with a bow)
    # Away + melee = standard dodge (melee retreat is just escape)
    return resolve_dodge(player, swipe_direction)
```

**Lateral tolerance:** Swipes roughly perpendicular to the target vector (between 45° and 135° off-axis) always produce a standard dodge. This prevents accidental lunges/retreat shots when the player intends to sidestep.

##### §5.8.5 Action Data Models

**Lunge action data:**

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| action_id | `lunge` |
| action_type | Lunge |
| timing_profile | {cast: 0, active: LUNGE_ACTIVE(0.3s), recovery: LUNGE_RECOVERY(0.2s)} |
| resource_costs | {stamina: LUNGE_STAMINA_COST} |
| cooldown | Shared with Dodge (DODGE_CD) |
| interrupt_policy | HyperArmor |
| damage_event | weapon_damage × LUNGE_DAMAGE_MULT, element_1, physical |
| status_application | element_1 status |
| tags | MELEE, ENGAGE |
| poise_damage | LUNGE_POISE_DAMAGE |

**Retreat Shot action data:**

| Field | Value |
|-------|-------|
| action_id | `retreat_shot` |
| action_type | RetreatShot |
| timing_profile | {cast: 0, active: RETREAT_ACTIVE(0.4s), recovery: RETREAT_RECOVERY(0.15s)} |
| resource_costs | {stamina: RETREAT_SHOT_STAMINA_COST} |
| cooldown | Shared with Dodge (DODGE_CD) |
| interrupt_policy | Interruptible |
| damage_event | weapon_damage × RETREAT_SHOT_DAMAGE_MULT, element_1, elemental |
| status_application | element_1 status |
| tags | RANGED, DISENGAGE |
| poise_damage | 0 |

##### §5.8.6 Contextual Dodge Tuning

| Variable | Default | Min | Max | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| LUNGE_STAMINA_COST | 30 | 20 | 40 | Slightly more than dodge (25) — aggression costs more |
| LUNGE_IFRAMES | 0.15s | 0.10s | 0.20s | Shorter than DODGE_IFRAMES_BASE (0.25s) |
| LUNGE_DAMAGE_MULT | 0.6 | 0.4 | 0.8 | Fraction of weapon_damage — setup tool, not damage tool |
| LUNGE_POISE_DAMAGE | 8 | 4 | 15 | Moderate — contributes to stagger without trivializing |
| LUNGE_ACTIVE | 0.3s | 0.2s | 0.5s | Active frames (movement + hit) |
| LUNGE_RECOVERY | 0.2s | 0.1s | 0.3s | Recovery before next action |
| LUNGE_DIRECTION_THRESHOLD | 0.707 | 0.5 | 0.866 | cos(45°) — lower = wider detection cone |
| RETREAT_SHOT_STAMINA_COST | 25 | 20 | 35 | Same as dodge — traded safety for utility, not extra cost |
| RETREAT_SHOT_DAMAGE_MULT | 0.5 | 0.3 | 0.7 | Lower than lunge — the value is the status, not the damage |
| RETREAT_ACTIVE | 0.4s | 0.3s | 0.5s | Slightly longer than dodge — includes shot animation |
| RETREAT_RECOVERY | 0.15s | 0.1s | 0.25s | Quick recovery — you're already repositioned |
| RETREAT_STAFF_CONE | 25° | 15° | 35° | Narrower than standard staff AOE (45°) |

##### §5.8.7 Dynamic Reticle (v12.27 CANONICAL)

While a player holds the swipe input with lock-on active (finger down, not yet released), a small reticle overlay provides real-time feedback about the currently-detected swipe action. The reticle updates continuously as the drag direction changes, so the player can swing their drag from lateral to toward and watch the reticle shift in real time, then adjust before releasing.

**Three reticle states:**

- **Red reticle on locked target** — drag is heading into the toward-cone (±45° of the locked target), **Lunge detected.** Releasing will commit to the Lunge direction.
- **Blue arrow pointing away from target** — drag is heading into the away-cone (±45° inverted from the target), **Retreat Shot detected.** Releasing will commit to the Retreat Shot.
- **Grey tick along drag path** — drag is lateral (neither toward nor away), **standard dodge detected.** Releasing will commit to the standard dodge.

The reticle displays as a small UI overlay at the current drag end point, roughly 16×16 pixels. It is translucent, positioned on the HUD layer, and does not occlude combat elements beneath it.

**Continuous interpretation.** Unlike pre-v12.27's touch-down-only hint, the reticle updates every frame as the player's drag moves. A drag that starts lateral (grey tick), curls toward the target (shifts to red reticle), then veers away (shifts to blue arrow) produces visible reticle transitions. The player's finger is always showing them what would happen if they released right now.

**Release commits to current state.** Whatever reticle state is active at the moment of release is what commits. There is no "cancel" — release always commits to the detected action. Players who want to cancel a swipe entirely must hold the input beyond the maximum swipe duration (existing §5.1 behavior, unchanged).

**Auto-retirement threshold.** The Dynamic Reticle is a teaching layer. After the player has successfully performed CONTEXTUAL_DODGE_HINT_AUTO_OFF(100) successful contextual dodges (either lunges or retreat shots resolved with intent-matching release), the reticle automatically disables. A notification fires once: *"Contextual dodge hint auto-disabled. You've got this. Re-enable in Settings → Accessibility → Combat Hints if you want it back."* The threshold rewards skill development — the feature retires itself before it becomes visual noise for players who've internalized the system.

**Settings override.** The reticle is toggleable at any time under Accessibility → Combat Hints → Dynamic Reticle. Default: enabled for new players until auto-retirement fires; disabled for players who opted out manually. Players who disabled the reticle can re-enable at any time.

**Lock-on requirement.** The reticle only displays when lock-on is active. Without lock-on, there is no contextual decision to interpret — swipe always produces a standard dodge — so the reticle adds no information and would clutter the HUD.

**Accessibility.** The three states differ by shape and position in addition to color:
- Red reticle is a circular target-shape positioned *on the locked enemy*
- Blue arrow is a directional arrow positioned *away from the locked enemy*
- Grey tick is a small dash positioned *along the current drag path*

Shape + position differentiation allows colorblind players to distinguish the three states without relying on color alone. This satisfies the §58 Accessibility pattern of dual-channel state encoding.

**Tuning (§20.1):**

| Variable | Default | Min | Max |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|
| CONTEXTUAL_DODGE_HINT_AUTO_OFF | 100 | 50 | 200 |

**Creative direction:** see `creative-reference.md` §8 for the reticle visual feel (subtle, informative, never distracting from combat).

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.27): Pre-v12.27, the contextual dodge system relied on the player internalizing the toward/away/lateral cone rules by trial and error, with a post-hoc "Contextual Dodge unlocked" mastery notification at Mastery Level 1.5 as the only tutorial surface. This worked for committed players but produced a hidden-input-rules feeling for newer ones — "I swipe, sometimes I lunge, sometimes I retreat, I don't know why." The Dynamic Reticle makes the system legible by giving real-time visual feedback during the drag itself, the moment the player is actively deciding. The continuous-interpretation update is load-bearing — a reticle that only showed on touch-down (the pre-v12.27 plan draft) would flash briefly and then vanish, leaving the player to complete the drag blind. By keeping the reticle visible throughout the drag, the player's finger is always connected to a visible interpretation, which is the most natural intent-to-feedback loop input design can produce. The auto-retirement threshold is equally load-bearing — the feature is designed to disappear once it's taught its lesson, because a persistent overlay in veteran play would become clutter. 100 successful contextual dodges is a soft commitment to "you've internalized this; you don't need the training wheels anymore."]`

#### §5.9 Combo Chain

##### §5.9.1 Overview

Auto-attacks build a per-target **combo count** (0–3) that determines a bonus applied to the next swipe trigger. The combo count turns auto-attacks from dead time between swipes into a sequencing decision: the player chooses when to break the chain and swipe based on what the fight demands right now.

**Three words define the three combo positions:**
- **Now** (count 1) — burst damage bonus. Swipe fast, hit hard.
- **Wide** (count 2) — burst + spread. The collision propagates the consumed status to a nearby enemy.
- **Next** (count 3) — burst + spread + the next status application has extended duration, feeding a wider resonance window.

Each position includes all bonuses from lower positions. The cost of reaching higher counts is time and exposure — the player stays in auto-attack range longer, risking damage, to earn a stronger swipe.

`[DESIGN INTENT: The combo chain makes the game's most frequent action — auto-attacking — inhabited rather than vacant. The player is always aware of where they are in the chain and always making a contextual decision about when to swipe. Against Fodder, hit 0 or hit 1 swipes are fine — they die fast. Against Brutes, hit 2 or hit 3 swipes reward patience. Against packs, hit 2's spread seeds the next collision target. Against bosses, hit 3's duration investment compounds over multiple cycles. Same input, different timing decisions per encounter.]`

##### §5.9.2 Combo Chain Mechanics (CANONICAL)

**Combo count:** Per-player, per-target integer. Range: 0–3. Incremented by 1 on each auto-attack hit against the tracked target. Capped at 3 (does not increment further).

**Tracked target:** The enemy the combo count is building against. Set to the target of the first auto-attack hit. Changes if the player hits a different target (count resets to 1 on the new target).

```
func on_auto_attack_hit(player, target):
    if target != player.combo_target:
        # New target — reset chain
        player.combo_target = target
        player.combo_count = 1
    else:
        # Same target — advance chain
        player.combo_count = min(player.combo_count + 1, 3)
    
    # Update weapon glow visual
    update_combo_glow(player, player.combo_count)
```

**Swipe resolution with combo bonus (CANONICAL):**

```
func resolve_swipe_combo(player, collision_event):
    count = player.combo_count
    
    # Count 0 — no auto-attacks landed on this target before swipe
    # Base collision damage. No combo bonus.
    if count == 0: 
        return
    
    # Count 1+ — Burst: bonus collision damage
    collision_event.base_damage ×= (1 + COMBO_BURST_BONUS)
    
    # Count 2+ — Spread: consumed status jumps to nearby enemy
    if count >= 2:
        spread_target = find_nearest_enemy(
            collision_event.target, 
            COMBO_SPREAD_RADIUS, 
            exclude = [collision_event.target]
        )
        if spread_target:
            apply_status(
                spread_target, 
                collision_event.consumed_status.element,
                collision_event.consumed_status.remaining_duration × COMBO_SPREAD_DURATION_MULT
            )
    
    # Count 3 — Next: flag player for extended status on next application
    if count >= 3:
        player.combo_next_extended = true
        player.combo_next_extended_timer = COMBO_NEXT_WINDOW
    
    # Swipe always resets combo count
    player.combo_count = 0
    player.combo_target = null
```

**Extended status application from count 3 (CANONICAL):**

```
func on_status_applied(player, target, status):
    if player.combo_next_extended:
        status.duration ×= (1 + COMBO_NEXT_DURATION_BONUS)
        player.combo_next_extended = false
        player.combo_next_extended_timer = 0
```

The extended duration flag expires after COMBO_NEXT_WINDOW seconds if not consumed by a status application. This prevents the player from earning count 3, disengaging for 30 seconds, and applying the extended status on a completely different fight.

##### §5.9.3 Combo Count — State Rules

**What increments the count:**
- Auto-attack hit on the tracked target: +1 (to max 3)
- Lunge hit on the tracked target: +1 (lunge IS an auto-attack for combo purposes)

**What resets the count to 0:**
- Swipe trigger (always resets after resolving combo bonus)
- Death

**What resets the count to 1 on a new target:**
- Auto-attack or lunge hitting a different target than the tracked target

**What does NOT affect the count:**
- Dodge (count preserved if auto-attacks resume on same target within grace window)
- Block (same — count preserved within grace window)
- Weapon swap (count is per-target, not per-weapon — persists across swaps)
- Retreat shot (does not advance count, does not reset count — the retreat shot is outside the combo chain)
- Movement (count persists regardless of player position)

**Grace window:** If the player stops auto-attacking for longer than COMBO_GRACE_WINDOW, the count resets to 0. Grace window = weapon's attack interval × COMBO_GRACE_MULT. This prevents stale combo counts from persisting across long pauses.

##### §5.9.4 Combo Count — Spread Mechanics

The count 2 spread applies the consumed status (the status that was consumed by the collision) to one nearby enemy within COMBO_SPREAD_RADIUS of the collision target. The spread status has reduced duration (COMBO_SPREAD_DURATION_MULT of the consumed status's remaining duration at time of consumption).

**Spread target selection:** Nearest enemy within radius that does not already have the same status active. If no valid target exists, the spread bonus is wasted — no spread occurs.

**Spread status properties:** The spread status is a standard status application. It can be consumed by subsequent collisions. It enters resonance windows normally. It can be refreshed by auto-attacks. The spread status functions identically to a directly-applied status in all respects except its initial duration.

**Spread does NOT chain.** A collision that consumes a spread-applied status does not itself spread. Only collisions triggered from combo count 2+ spread. This prevents infinite chain reactions.

##### §5.9.5 Combo Count — Visual Feedback

**Weapon glow:** The equipped weapon's element glow cycles through three intensities corresponding to combo count:

| Count | Glow | Visual Signal |
|:-----:|------|---------------|
| 0 | None | Base weapon appearance. No combo active. |
| 1 | Faint | Subtle element-color glow on weapon edge. "Loaded." |
| 2 | Medium | Visible element-color glow extending along weapon. "Building." |
| 3 | Bright | Full element-color glow with subtle pulse. "Full." |

The glow resets to none on swipe (count reset). The glow transitions are smooth (0.1s interpolation), not instant. The glow is visible to all players — a spectator can see another player's combo count by their weapon brightness.

**Spread visual:** When a count 2+ collision spreads status to a nearby enemy, a brief arc of the element's color connects the collision target to the spread target for 0.3 seconds. The arc says "the collision reached further." Visible to all players.

**Extended status visual:** When the count 3 extended status is applied on the next auto-attack, the status icon above the target renders slightly larger than normal for 0.5 seconds before settling to standard size. The brief size pulse signals "this status is special" without adding a permanent visual distinction.

##### §5.9.6 Combo Chain — Interaction with Other Systems

**Resonance (§5.7):** The combo count and resonance window are independent systems that compound. A count 3 swipe produces a collision with burst bonus, spread, AND the next status has extended duration. If that extended status enters resonance, the resonance window is proportionally wider. A player who consistently hits count 3 and then resonance-times the next cycle's collision is operating at the game's highest sustained damage output.

**Collision damage formula (§10.6):** The combo burst bonus (COMBO_BURST_BONUS) applies as a multiplier in the collision damage chain, positioned after the base collision calculation and before the resonance multiplier:

```
collision_damage = (COLLISION_BASE + scaling_stat × COLLISION_COEFF)
    × Combo burst bonus (§5.9, ×1.0 at count 0, ×1.0+COMBO_BURST_BONUS at count 1+)
    × Resonance bonus (§5.7, ×1.10–1.30)
    × Elemental Mastery collision mult (§2.2)
    × Volatile bonus if directional match (§4.7, ×1.30)
    × Armor attunement offense if matching (×1.20)
    × Effectiveness vs target element (§10.2)
```

**Lunge (§5.8.2):** Lunge advances the combo count because it's a melee hit. A player on count 2 who lunges at their tracked target advances to count 3 on the lunge hit. The lunge becomes a chain-completer — the player auto-attacks twice, lunges forward for hit 3, and swipes immediately on arrival for a full-stack collision.

**Retreat Shot (§5.8.3):** The retreat shot does not advance or reset the combo count. It exists outside the chain. A player on count 2 who retreat-shots and then re-engages with auto-attacks on the same target is still on count 2 (if within the grace window). The retreat shot is a tactical interruption that doesn't disrupt the chain.

**Weapon swap:** The combo count persists across weapon swaps because the count tracks target commitment, not weapon commitment. The player auto-attacks twice with frost sword (count 2), swaps to flame sword, auto-attacks once (count 3), swipes for Steam collision with full stack. The swap is a natural part of the collision setup loop and the combo count accommodates it seamlessly.

**Staff AOE swipe:** When a Staff swipe triggers collisions on multiple targets, the combo count applies based on the player's tracked target only. The tracked target's collision gets the full combo bonus. Other targets hit by the cone get base collision damage (no combo bonus). The combo count is per-target commitment — the Staff Mage chose which target to build the chain against, and that target benefits.

##### §5.9.7 Combo Chain Tuning

| Variable | Default | Min | Max | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| COMBO_BURST_BONUS | 0.15 | 0.08 | 0.25 | Collision damage multiplier at count 1+ |
| COMBO_SPREAD_RADIUS | 20u | 10u | 35u | Max distance for count 2 status spread |
| COMBO_SPREAD_DURATION_MULT | 0.60 | 0.40 | 0.80 | Spread status duration as fraction of consumed status |
| COMBO_NEXT_DURATION_BONUS | 0.20 | 0.10 | 0.35 | Extended duration bonus for next status (count 3) |
| COMBO_NEXT_WINDOW | 4.0s | 2.0s | 6.0s | Time limit to use count 3 extended status |
| COMBO_GRACE_MULT | 1.5 | 1.2 | 2.0 | Grace window as multiple of attack interval |

##### §5.9.8 Visual Combo Posture (v12.42 CANONICAL)

The weapon-glow combo indicator (§5.9.5) extends at count 2 and count 3 into a **full-body character posture shift** that makes the combo state visible across the battlefield. Where §5.9.5 governs weapon-edge glow scaling with count, §5.9.8 governs the character's pose — at peak combo, the character physically *looks* ready to deliver a payoff swipe.

**Posture progression.**

| Count | Posture | Description |
|:-----:|---------|-------------|
| 0 | Neutral | Standard idle/combat stance. No posture modification. |
| 1 | Loaded glow only | §5.9.5 faint weapon glow. No posture change — this is the subtle "something is brewing" tier where glow alone carries the signal. |
| 2 | Ready lean | §5.9.5 medium glow + subtle ready-stance lean. Character's weight shifts slightly toward forward foot; off-hand position adjusts to a readiness pose (hand up and forward rather than at the side). The lean is small (~5° forward) and readable peripherally. |
| 3 | Full flourish | §5.9.5 bright glow + full weapon-specific flourish posture (see per-weapon table below). |

**Per-weapon count-3 flourish posture.**

- **Greatsword (Power-Bruiser):** Character plants the blade tip in the ground between their feet with both hands on the hilt, shoulders squared, weight shifted back slightly as though coiling for a heavy swing. The character is *physically braced*. Reads as "the next swing lands with everything."
- **Sword (Agility-Duelist):** Blade raised to ready position at shoulder height, off-hand extended behind the body for balance, torso angled slightly sideways to present a narrower silhouette. Fencer's ready pose. Reads as "precise strike incoming."
- **Bow (Agility-Archer):** Arrow already drawn to full tension at anchor point (not just nocked — fully drawn and holding). Off-hand holds the bow at full extension, drawing-hand fingers just behind the ear. The tension in the posture reads as *the shot is already aimed*.
- **Staff (Mind-Mage):** Gem or crystal suspended visibly above the staff tip, hovering ~30cm above the tip with visible energy buildup (faint particles orbiting the gem). Character's off-hand is raised with palm open toward the gem, channeling. Reads as "the spell is formed and waiting."

**Posture scope and constraints.**

- **Visible to all players.** Party members, opposing players, spectators all see the posture. This is consistent with §5.9.5's existing "weapon glow is visible to all" rule — the combo count is public information at all tiers.
- **Count-2 lean and count-3 flourish transitions are ~0.2s interpolated**, not instant. A player hitting count 2 sees their character smoothly adjust into the lean; a player hitting count 3 sees the flourish pose resolve over a brief window. Transitions back to neutral (on swipe-reset) are also ~0.2s, producing a gentle "releases the tension" read when the combo commits.
- **Purely visual.** Posture does not affect hitbox, collision geometry, movement speed, or any mechanical system. Character movement, turning, and attack inputs remain fully responsive during posture — the character can walk, run, rotate, or swipe at any time without animation commitment. The posture is a visual overlay on the existing animation state, not a replacement for it. In practice this means a character in count-3 flourish who starts running shifts smoothly between the flourish pose and the run animation without stutter; the flourish visually "resumes" when the character stops moving.
- **No hitbox or collision changes.** The braced Greatsword stance doesn't shrink or expand the character's hitbox. The drawn Bow stance doesn't affect projectile origin. Mechanical geometry per §5.4 hitbox rules is unchanged.

**Weapon-swap during combo.** Per §5.9.3, combo count persists through weapon swap. When a player swaps weapons mid-combo, the posture snaps to the new weapon's equivalent count-level **instantly** (no transition interpolation) — a player at count 3 with Greatsword who swaps to Bow immediately adopts the drawn-Bow posture. The snap-instead-of-interpolation rule is the exception to the general 0.2s transition discipline, and is deliberate: weapon swap is already an instant visual change (the sprite swaps), and a transition-animating posture during swap would produce confusing intermediate poses. Snap is the correct feel.

**Interaction with §49 Stat-Based Visuals.** The §49 Quiet Archetype Emergence system (v12.29) governs the player's visual archetype drift (Berserker/Mage/Rogue) based on sustained combat behavior. Archetype visuals are *ambient* — they affect idle/neutral pose, particle emissions around the character, and passive stance modifications during non-combat states. Combo posture is *active-combat* — it modifies the pose during combo-chain tiers specifically. The two systems compose without conflict: a Berserker-archetype character at count 3 with Greatsword shows both the Berserker's ambient aggressive stance drift AND the Greatsword count-3 braced-plant posture. The archetype affects baseline, posture affects combo-tier overlay on that baseline. No priority resolution needed; they animate different aspects of the character visual.

**Interaction with §9.2.1 Collision Opportunity Weapon Edge Glow (v12.38).** The v12.38 weapon edge glow signals compatible-status proximity. This operates on the weapon edge; combo posture operates on the character body. Both systems can fire simultaneously — a character at count 3 with Greatsword near a Burn-carrying enemy shows both the braced flourish posture AND the Flame-red weapon edge glow. The two signals compose: "my tool is ready to collide, and my character is at peak combo." Strong compound signal, no channel conflict.

**Accessibility — toggleable reduced motion.** Settings → Accessibility → Motion → Reduced Motion disables the count-2 lean and count-3 flourish posture animations. In reduced-motion mode, the character remains in neutral pose regardless of combo count; the §5.9.5 weapon-glow tiers remain visible as the sole count indicator. This preserves the mechanic for all players while giving motion-sensitive players a less visually-busy option. The weapon glow alone is sufficient signal; posture is the richer add-on.

**Data model — client-side animation state.** Combo count is already broadcast in the existing combat state payload (§16). Posture rendering is client-side animation derived from the broadcast count value — no new server-side state, no new network messages. The per-weapon flourish pose specifications live in the client's animation configuration file (`combo-posture.json`) with per-weapon entries. Content authoring is bounded: one flourish pose per weapon type × one lean pose shared across all weapons = five total pose variants (four weapon-specific flourishes + one shared lean).

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.42): The v12.38 combat-sensory thread opened three new channels (resonance streak via mana flow, collision opportunity via weapon edge glow, haptic via device tactile). v12.42 extends the visual-feedback channel with another register: the player's *own character body* as the combo signal. The weapon glow tiers (§5.9.5) already communicate combo count through the weapon; v12.42 adds the character's posture as a second visual channel that reinforces the glow at count 2 (subtle lean) and count 3 (full flourish). The character at peak combo looks ready to deliver a payoff swipe — which is exactly what the combo state means mechanically. This is recognition-through-body, parallel to the recognition-through-tool pattern of v12.38 weapon edge glow. Per-weapon flourish specificity is load-bearing: a Greatsword character braced with blade planted reads differently than a Bow character at full draw, and this specificity carries the weapon's personality into the combo signal. A player who plays multiple weapon types learns their own character's flourish vocabulary; spectators can read another player's build at a glance by their combo posture. Visible-to-all-players discipline (matching the existing §5.9.5 glow rule) means combo state is public information — spectators, party members, and opposing players all read the same signal, which keeps the social visibility of combat skill consistent across channels. The purely-visual constraint is critical: posture must not affect hitbox, movement, or any mechanical system. A character in count-3 flourish who starts running shifts smoothly between poses without stutter; nothing about the visual overlay degrades combat responsiveness. This respects the core rule that feel-state signals never compromise mechanical feel. Together with §5.9.5 glow, §9.2.1 weapon edge glow, §2.4 haptic feedback, and the §5.7.7 resonance streak badge, the combo-and-resonance combat state is now expressed through weapon-edge visual (glow), character-body visual (posture), peripheral weapon visual (compatibility glow), tactile (haptic), and resource-ledger (streak badge) channels simultaneously. The sensory layering continues to accumulate.]`

### §6. Monster Scaling

#### §6.1 Tri-Phase Scaling (CANONICAL)

Continuous. All ceil(). Base Goblin L1: 40HP, 8Dmg, 10XP, 5Gold.

| Stat | Base | R_RAMP (L1–30) | R_PLATEAU (L31–65) | R_ENDGAME (L66–100) |
|------|:----:|:---------------:|:-------------------:|:--------------------:|
| HP | 40 | 1.065 | 1.035 | 1.025 |
| Damage | 8 | 1.045 | 1.025 | 1.018 |
| XP | 10 | 1.045 | 1.025 | 1.018 |
| Gold | 5 | 1.035 | 1.020 | 1.015 |

Monsters are zone-leveled, not player-leveled.

#### §6.2 XP Requirement (CANONICAL)

Kill-target coupled formula. XP requirement scales with both a power curve and monster XP yield, ensuring consistent kill pacing regardless of monster reward scaling:

```
killTarget = ceil(100 × (Level - 1)^0.7)
monXp      = monsterStat(10, Level, 1.045, 1.025, 1.018)
XP_Req     = killTarget × monXp

Post-100:  XP_Req = ceil(base × (1 + (Level-100) × 0.1)^3.5)
```

**Tuning constant:** The power exponent (0.7) is a tuning parameter stored as `XP_POWER_EXPONENT`. Recommended range: 0.7–1.0. Lower values flatten the curve (more time in early gear tiers). Higher values steepen it (faster early game, longer endgame). Adjustable post-launch based on player behavior data.

**Design rationale:** The 0.7 exponent was chosen to flatten progression across 20 gear tiers. Each 5-level tier receives meaningful play time (1.5–17 hours), ensuring players engage with crafting and equipment at every tier rather than skipping early content. The formula couples kill targets to monster XP so that kill pacing holds regardless of monster reward tuning.

**Milestone pacing (at 3h/day):**

| Level | Kills/Level | Time/Level | Cumulative @3h/day |
|:-----:|:-----------:|:----------:|:------------------:|
| 2 | 100 | 8m 20s | instant |
| 10 | 466 | 39m | 1.2 days |
| 25 | 926 | 1h 17m | 6 days |
| 50 | 1,525 | 2h 7m | 21 days |
| 75 | 2,035 | 2h 49m | 42 days |
| 100 | 2,495 | 3h 27m | 68 days (2.3 months) |

**Total combat time to Level 100: ~204 hours.** Positioned between Diablo 4 (~150h) and OSRS (~300–500h).

**Gear tier pacing (5 levels per tier, 20 tiers total):**

| Tier | Levels | Time in Tier |
|:----:|:------:|:------------:|
| 1 | 1–5 | 1h 27m |
| 5 | 21–25 | 6h 14m |
| 10 | 46–50 | 10h 26m |
| 15 | 71–75 | 14h |
| 20 | 96–100 | 13h 42m |

Tier ratio (slowest ÷ fastest): 9.4×. All 20 equipment tiers receive meaningful play time.

#### §6.3 Enemy Archetypes

| Archetype | HP | Dmg | Speed | Role |
|-----------|:--:|:---:|:-----:|------|
| Fodder (40%) | 0.6 | 0.8 | 1.0 | Power fantasy. Grand Slam rhythm. 1-2 hit kills. |
| Brute (15%) | 1.5 | 1.3 | 0.7 | Patience teacher. Collision payoff. |
| Swarm (15%) | 0.4 | 0.6 | 1.2 | Groups of 3–5. Spatial awareness. AOE value. |
| Sentinel (10%) | 1.0 | 1.0 | 1.0 | Resistant to one element. Elemental flexibility. |
| Volatile (10%) | 0.8 | 1.0 | 1.0 | Explodes on death (30% maxHP elemental). Dodgeable. |
| Stalker (5%) | 0.7 | 1.2 | 1.3 | Invisible until attacking. Lock-on as tactical tool. |
| Hexer (5%) | 0.9 | 0.8 | 1.0 | Applies statuses to player. Armor attunement relevance. |

#### §6.3a Archetype Death Behaviors

Most archetypes use the standard death animation (pop + loot drop). Two archetypes have unique death behaviors that reinforce their combat identity:

**Brute — Collapse.** On death, the Brute does not pop. It staggers (0.2s), holds (0.15s), then falls forward (0.3s). The ground shakes on impact, scaled to sprite size — a 16×16 Brute at Depth 1 produces a barely visible tremor, a 176×176 Brute at Depth 8 shakes the nearby screen (v12.40 endgame-scale). A brief dust cloud spawns at the impact point, element-colored. Loot drops on impact, not on death trigger. The Brute's death has weight because the Brute had weight. The player who spent 5+ seconds fighting a Brute watches it fall and feels the relief.

**Swarm — Flinch Chain.** When a Swarm member dies, all other Swarm members within SWARM_FLINCH_RADIUS(15u) play a brief flinch animation — a 0.1s visual recoil toward the center of the group. **Purely cosmetic. No hitbox change, no interrupt, no gameplay stagger.** The flinch does not delay the Swarm member's next attack or movement. It is a visual reaction that communicates "they noticed." Clearing a Swarm pack produces a domino effect: kill one, the others flinch, kill the next during the visual flinch (which doesn't actually protect you — they can still attack), the last one flinches twice. The flinch makes the pack feel like a group rather than five independent entities.

| Variable | Default | Min | Max |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|
| BRUTE_DEATH_STAGGER | 0.2s | 0.1s | 0.4s |
| BRUTE_DEATH_HOLD | 0.15s | 0.05s | 0.3s |
| BRUTE_DEATH_FALL | 0.3s | 0.15s | 0.5s |
| BRUTE_DEATH_SHAKE_SCALE | 0.5 | 0.2 | 1.0 |
| SWARM_FLINCH_RADIUS | 15u | 8u | 25u |
| SWARM_FLINCH_DURATION | 0.1s | 0.05s | 0.2s |

#### §6.3b Archetype Introduction Schedule (v12.37 CANONICAL)

The seven archetypes defined in §6.3 are not all available to all zones at all depths. Instead, archetypes are **progressively introduced across depths 1-7**, with each new depth unlocking one additional archetype for the zone's spawn pool. By depth 8+, all seven archetypes are in rotation.

**Per-depth archetype roster.**

| Depth | Archetypes Available in Spawn Pool | New This Depth |
|:-----:|-----------------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Fodder | Fodder (baseline) |
| 2 | Fodder + Brute | Brute |
| 3 | Fodder + Brute + Swarm | Swarm |
| 4 | Fodder + Brute + Swarm + Sentinel | Sentinel |
| 5 | Fodder + Brute + Swarm + Sentinel + Volatile | Volatile |
| 6 | Fodder + Brute + Swarm + Sentinel + Volatile + Stalker | Stalker |
| 7 | Fodder + Brute + Swarm + Sentinel + Volatile + Stalker + Hexer | Hexer |
| 8+ | All seven | — |

**Spawn distribution at each depth** uses the §6.3 archetype percentages *renormalized to the available pool at that depth*. Archetypes not yet introduced at a depth do not have their population redistributed to specific other archetypes — instead, the full §6.3 distribution is scaled up proportionally. Example: at depth 2 (Fodder 40% + Brute 15% available, totaling 55% of the §6.3 distribution), the effective spawn rates become Fodder 73% (40/55) and Brute 27% (15/55). This preserves the relative weighting the §6.3 design intent captures while concentrating the depth-1 zone experience on the power-fantasy Fodder encounter the player is ready for.

**Why progressive, not front-loaded.** A new player arriving at depth 1 and immediately encountering Stalkers (invisible until attacking), Hexers (apply statuses to player), and Volatiles (explode on death) would face a barrage of mechanical complexity before they had internalized the basic combat loop against Fodder. The v12.37 schedule gives the player a clear teaching curve: *depth 1 teaches attack and movement, depth 2 teaches patience against big HP (Brute), depth 3 teaches AOE against groups (Swarm), depth 4 teaches element swapping against resistance (Sentinel), depth 5 teaches positioning against death-triggered AOE (Volatile), depth 6 teaches lock-on vs surprise attacks (Stalker), depth 7 teaches attunement matching (Hexer), depth 8+ combines everything.* Each new archetype fits into a curriculum slot that matches the player's progression stage.

**First-encounter Codex reveal.** The first time a player encounters each new archetype (kills one, or is hit by one, whichever comes first), a one-time Codex entry reveal fires in the non-blocking upper-left notification format established by v12.33 Surfaced Mastery Ladder. Example:

> *New archetype discovered: Stalker.*
> *Invisible until attacking. Lock-on is the counter — it reveals position early. The Codex has a new entry.*

Reveals are one-time per character, queued through combat (never interrupting active fighting), and dismissed by tap or 4-second timeout. The reveal names the archetype and provides a single-sentence tactical hint, matching the v12.33 opt-in-hint register. The Codex entry itself is the canonical content — the reveal is just the notification that new content is available.

**Boss and zone-unique monster exception.** The §6.3b schedule governs *standard archetype spawns* only. Dungeon bosses and zone-unique monsters (per §6.9 and §6.10) are hand-authored per zone regardless of archetype classification — they exist to provide fight-feel variety independent of the archetype system. A depth-2 dungeon's boss can be mechanically designed to use Hexer-like status application even though Hexers are not in the depth-2 archetype pool; the boss is a specific designed encounter, not a standard spawn.

**Late-depth hybrid zones.** §14.4 Late-Depth Hybrid Zones (v12.35) applies at depths 7-8 by adding secondary-element variants to the spawn pool. The v12.37 archetype schedule interacts cleanly: depth 7 unlocks Hexer for standard archetypes, and depth 7-8 also unlocks the hybrid-zone variants (16 per spec, art reskins of existing recurring forms). Both layers run in parallel — the archetype-schedule governs *what archetypes spawn*, and the §14.4 hybrid-zone system governs *what elemental variants exist*. A depth 7 Ember Fields dungeon can spawn a Stone-reskinned Hexer (Hexer archetype, Stone visual/status layering), which is the convergence point of both systems.

**§6.5 Kill-Time Audit Gates compatibility.** The §6.5 audit gates validate combat pacing against a Brute-archetype baseline at each depth. The v12.37 schedule maintains this validation — Brute is available from depth 2 onward, and depth 1 (Fodder-only) uses a depth-1-specific pacing target that already accounts for the fact that depth 1 has no Brute presence. No audit gate adjustments required.

**Quest system interaction (§25).** The capstone quest system (§25) includes quests that may involve specific archetype kills. No current capstone quest requires an archetype kill at a depth below where that archetype is introduced — spot-checked at v12.37 integration. Future capstone quests should validate their archetype-depth requirements against this schedule; flagged as a quest-authoring constraint.

**Data.** Spawn pool per depth lives in `zone-config.json` per zone, with the per-depth archetype availability list added as a field. The renormalized percentages are computed at server-side spawn resolution time rather than stored explicitly, to preserve the §6.3 distribution as the single source of truth for archetype weighting. First-encounter notification state lives in the player's existing character state (`archetype_encounters_seen` as a Set of archetype IDs). No schema migration required — the field is added as opt-in with empty-set default.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.37): The seven-archetype system has been in the design since early versions, but pre-v12.37 it specified that all archetypes were available at all depths (scaled by zone and level). This produced a legitimate tuning risk at low depths: a depth-1 Ember Fields could spawn a Hexer (status-applying archetype) before the new player had equipped armor with elemental attunement, producing a combat encounter the player couldn't defend against because they lacked the gear system the encounter required. The theoretical fix was for the zone designer to weight low-depth zones away from the problematic archetypes, but that produces per-zone manual tuning at every depth and no clear rule for where to weight. The v12.37 progressive schedule converts this into a *system-level pedagogical structure*: each depth has a canonical archetype roster, the roster expands monotonically, and the teaching curve matches the player's progression stage. The first-encounter Codex reveal is the pedagogical surface — when depth 5 introduces Volatiles (which explode on death), the player gets a one-sentence tactical hint via the v12.33 notification infrastructure so they know what they're about to encounter. This is the same pattern as v12.32 (zones teach collision curricula) and v12.33 (certifications acknowledge demonstrated skills) applied to the monster archetype system: make the teaching structure legible instead of asking the player to infer it. The renormalization rule (preserving §6.3 relative weights rather than reassigning populations) is load-bearing for preventing flavor homogeneity — a depth-2 zone where Brutes comprise 27% of spawns still feels like a zone with some Brute presence, which matches the §6.3 intent of Brutes as periodic patience-teaching encounters rather than as rare sightings. The boss exception preserves the zone-unique monster flexibility that makes each zone's dungeon feel hand-authored. Together with v12.32 (Codex curricula), v12.33 (Mastery Ladder), and v12.35 (late-depth hybrid zones), v12.37 completes the zone-as-pedagogy design: depths teach progressively, and what each depth teaches is visible through the Codex and the archetype-introduction notifications.]`

#### §6.4 Monster Defenses

Monsters have **zero** flat mitigation, zero block, zero dodge. Player damage formula output applies directly to monster HP. Monster challenge comes from HP scaling, archetypes, and player-side pressure.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Zero monster defenses keeps the feedback loop tight. "What you see in damage numbers is what you get." Kill-time tuning is predictable.]`

#### §6.5 Kill-Time Audit Gates (v12.8 Recalibrated)

The audit gates validate weapon damage scaling against monster HP across the level curve. The reference target for each gate is a **Brute archetype monster** (§6.3, 1.5× HP) of the same level as the player, killed by a **median-Power player at that level** wielding a **Normal-quality, Hardness 0 weapon** at the level-appropriate material tier. Boss kill times are validated separately (Brute × 5).

The audit gates are intentionally targeted at brutes rather than fodder because brutes are the §6.3 "patience teacher" archetype — the monster designed for collision payoff and the one against which weapon damage *should* feel meaningfully tuned. Fodder kills (1-2 hits) are validated by §6.3 as the power-fantasy baseline and don't need separate audit. Default-archetype monsters fall between fodder and brute (~1.5-2.5 hits at the audit reference levels) and are validated implicitly by the brute-archetype audit.

**Median-Power reference.** The audit uses a median Power value appropriate to the player's level, not a fixed L100 median. Power trains through use under §1.4, so a Level 15 player has accumulated only ~15 levels of stat training and carries roughly Power 21; a Level 100 player has reached the full median of Power 110 (computed across the §24 reference builds: Bruiser 170, Ranger 130, Mage 130, Tank 90, Healer 60, Control 100, arithmetic mean ≈ 113, rounded to 110 for the median reference). The level-to-Power scaling is approximately linear: `median_power(L) ≈ 6 + (L − 1) × 1.05`. This reflects the §1.4 stat training rate of ~5 T1 points per level distributed across the player's active stats, with Power receiving roughly a 20-35% share for melee-leaning builds.

| Level | Material Tier | Median Power | Weapon Damage | Brute HP | Hits to Kill | TTK (approx) |
|:-----:|:-------------:|:------------:|:-------------:|:--------:|:------------:|:------------:|
| 15 | T3 | 21 | ~80 | 146 | 1.83 | ~2s |
| 35 | T7 | 42 | ~181 | 444 | 2.46 | ~3s |
| 65 | T13 | 73 | ~401 | 1,244 | 3.10 | ~4s |
| 100 | T20 | 110 | ~763 | 2,949 | 3.87 | ~5s |

**v12.8 calibration methodology.** The kill counts above were computed from first principles using: the §4.4 weapon damage formula (`weapon_damage = (weapon_base + Power × 0.8) × material_tier_mult`, no element tier multiplier), weapon_base 35 for a reference Greatsword, Normal quality (1.00×), Hardness 0, material tier multiplier interpolated linearly from 1.0× at T1 to 6.2× at T20, the §6.1 monster HP scaling formula (base 40, ramp 1.065, plateau 1.035, endgame 1.025), and the §6.3 Brute archetype 1.5× HP modifier. The level-appropriate median Power values were computed from the linear formula above. Every number in the table is reproducible from these inputs. **A planned audit validator script (proposed name `audit-validator.py`, not yet authored) would perform this calculation whenever §4.4, §6.1, §6.3, or §24 is edited.** Until the script exists, the calculation is reproduced by hand during each audit pass.

**Audit gate target ranges (CANONICAL under v12.8):**

| Level | Target Hit Range | Rationale |
|:-----:|:----------------:|-----------|
| 15 | 1–3 | Power-fantasy early game. Fodder 1-hit, brutes 1-3 hit range covers the normal spread. |
| 35 | 2–3 | Mid-ramp. Brutes start requiring real commitment. Fodder still 1-2 hit. |
| 65 | 3–4 | Plateau. Brutes are extended encounters. Collision payoff is most valuable here. |
| 100 | 3–5 | Endgame. Brutes demand full combo sequences. Boss encounters scale from this baseline (Brute × 5). |

**All four reference rows pass the audit gates.** L15 at 1.83 hits falls within the 1-3 range. L35 at 2.46 hits falls within the 2-3 range. L65 at 3.10 hits falls within the 3-4 range. L100 at 3.87 hits falls within the 3-5 range. The v12.6 damage formula produces kill counts that match the audit intent without requiring any monster HP rebalancing.

**v12.8 correction to the L15 audit gate.** Earlier versions of this document asserted an L15 audit gate of "2-3 hits." The v12.8 recalibration widens this to "1-3 hits" after re-examining the design intent. At Level 15, players are still in the power-fantasy early game (§6.3 Fodder 1-2 hit kills as the design baseline), and demanding a minimum of 2 hits on brute archetypes would push the early game away from the intended feel. A brute that dies in 1.5-2 hits at L15 is exactly right for the early game's "you are becoming powerful" feel. The widening is structural, not a band-drift concession — it brings the audit gate into alignment with the explicit design intent in §6.3 rather than the opposite direction. **INV-30** (validator band drift detector, §22) permits widenings that correct documented misalignment; this is such a correction and is flagged here explicitly to satisfy that invariant.

**Why the earlier v12.8 pass produced wrong numbers.** A prior draft of this section asserted kill counts of 2.2 / 3.5 / 4.1 / 3.9 and claimed the audit passed without rebalancing. That draft made a specific technical error: it used Power 110 (the L100 median) as the reference Power at *every* audit level, rather than computing level-appropriate median Power. Using L100 Power at L15 produces weapon damage that is ~2.4× higher than a real L15 player would actually deliver, which made the kill counts look reasonable but described a scenario that doesn't exist in play. The corrected calculation above uses Power 21 at L15, which is what a real L15 median player actually has. The lesson: audit gates that mix a level-scaled quantity (monster HP) with an un-scaled quantity (player stats) will silently produce nonsense. Always scale all inputs to the audit level consistently.

**Why no rebalance was needed (with correct math).** The v12.6 changelog flagged the §6.5 rebalance as a follow-up requirement on the assumption that removing the element tier multiplier would significantly reduce baseline weapon damage and require monster HP reductions to compensate. The v12.8 audit pass tested this assumption and found it was the wrong sign: v12.5 with the Fusion 2.25× multiplier was producing kill counts of 0.81 / 1.10 / 1.38 / 1.72 at the four audit levels against level-appropriate Power values — **v12.5 was underkilling its own audit gates**, with endgame brutes dying in 1.7 hits against a 3-5 hit target. Removing the element tier multiplier in v12.6 *brought kill counts up* into the audit range rather than dropping them further below it. The audit passes under v12.6 because v12.5 was killing brutes too fast and v12.6's formula is slower, which is what the audit always wanted.

**The lesson.** When a doc asserts an audit gate without making the audit's assumptions explicit, the gate can drift quietly between versions because every reader (including future passes of the same doc) interprets the missing assumptions slightly differently. The v12.5 audit gate "L100: 3-5 hits" was never wrong as a *target*, but it was wrong as a *current state* — the v12.5 formula was producing 1.7 hits at L100 and the doc didn't notice because nobody computed the actual numbers. The v12.8 recalibration makes the audit's assumptions fully explicit (brute archetype, level-appropriate median Power, Normal quality, Hardness 0, reference Greatsword weapon_base 35) so future versions can reproduce the calculation and catch drift.

**Audit gate failure modes (what would cause future passes to fail).** The audit passes if level-appropriate median-Power players kill brutes within the gate hit ranges. It fails if any of the following changes happen without compensating updates: (a) the §4.4 weapon damage formula changes its multiplier structure or coefficients, (b) the §6.1 monster HP scaling rates change, (c) the §6.3 brute HP modifier changes, (d) the §24 reference build Power distributions shift significantly (changing the median), (e) the §4.2 weapon base damage values change, (f) the §4.1 material tier multiplier interpolation changes from the current linear 1.0-6.2 ramp, or (g) the §1.4 stat training rate changes (changing the level-to-median-Power relationship). Any of these triggers a re-run of the audit calculation.

**Note on §24 reference builds.** §24 defines stat distributions for play patterns, not damage projections. The audit uses the *median* Power across the six reference builds (Bruiser 170, Ranger 130, Mage 130, Tank 90, Healer 60, Control 100; mean ≈ 113, rounded to 110) as the reference baseline because no single build represents the "average" player, and the audit is meant to validate that the *typical* player across builds has appropriate kill times against typical monsters. Specialized builds (Bruiser at the high end, Healer at the low end) will see kill times shifted from the median by a factor of roughly `Power_specialist / 110` — Bruiser kills brutes ~1.5× faster than the audit predicts, Healer ~1.8× slower. Both are intentional. The audit defends the median, not the extremes.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.8): The §6.5 audit pass surfaces an embarrassing pattern that's worth documenting honestly. The audit table existed in v12.4 and v12.5 with the same numbers (L15 2-3, L35 2-3, L65 3-4, L100 3-5), and through both versions nobody actually computed whether the table's targets matched the formula's outputs. The v12.6 changelog flagged a "rebalance is required" follow-up based on the intuition that "removing a 2.25× multiplier should require monster HP reductions." The intuition was wrong because v12.5's monster HP was already too low for the v12.5 formula — the 2.25× was being absorbed into kill times that were already under-target. Removing the 2.25× brought kill times back up, accidentally fixing the latent v12.5 mistuning.

This is the kind of doc-quality issue that only surfaces when someone runs the actual numbers. The audit table looked sensible because the *targets* were sensible (2-5 hits is a reasonable ARPG kill time), but the *math wasn't validated* against the targets. v12.8 fixes this by making the audit's assumptions explicit, computing the actual numbers, and locking in a procedure for future re-validation. Nothing about the gameplay changes — it was probably going to feel right under v12.6 anyway because the v12.6 formula happens to produce the right kill counts. What changes is the doc's *honesty* about how it knows that.]`

#### §6.6 Enemy Elemental Identity

Zone-aligned. Their attacks deal that element's damage. Player armor attunement reduces matching incoming damage. Enemy element doesn't prevent player-applied statuses.

#### §6.7 Monster Forms

Each zone contains 5 monster types: 3 recurring evolutionary forms and 2 zone-unique monsters.

**Recurring Forms (appear in every zone, evolve per depth tier):**

| Form | Archetype | Combat Role | Silhouette Identity |
|------|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Imp** | Evader | Agile, magic-based, unpredictable movement. Forces precision and timing. | Wings + pointed ears at every stage |
| **Skeleton** | Aggressor | Humanoid, charges player, readable attack patterns, hits hard. Forces telegraph reading. | Exposed bones + skull visible at every stage |
| **Eye** | Space Controller | Squishiest. Controls space through beams and area denial. Forces repositioning. | Central iris + floating form at every stage |

**Imp evolution path:** Tiny mischievous creature → winged fiend → horned demon → armored demon → demon prince → archdemon → demon lord → elder demon lord → shadow/light ascended. Elemental theming through wing material, skin color, magic effects.

**Skeleton evolution path:** Bare skull → skull with ribcage → skeleton with arms and weapon → full skeleton warrior → armored skeleton → skeleton champion → death knight → lich lord → shadow/light ascended. Each depth adds meaningful body parts. Bones remain prominent — never hidden behind full plate armor. Elemental theming through bone color, aura, weapon type.

**Eye evolution path:** Simple floating eyeball → veined eye with lashes → eye with tentacle stalks → multi-eyed cluster → fleshy mass with eyestalks → beholder entity → elder beholder → cosmic eye horror → shadow/light ascended.
