The iris matches the elemental zone and serves as a combat telegraph — it shifts, pulses, or changes pattern before firing. Fire eye: burning orange iris. Frost eye: pale crystalline blue. Poison eye: toxic green slit pupil.

**Zone-unique monsters (2 per zone):** Found only in that specific element × depth combination. Never seen outside their home zone. Designed to complement the three recurring forms.

**Monster spatial distribution within zones:** All 5 monster types are visible in the zone regardless of player level. Monsters are separated by distance from the zone entrance — monsters appropriate for the zone's lowest levels are near the entrance, with progressively higher-level monsters further out. A new monster type appears approximately every 2 levels of distance.

#### §6.8 Monster Sizes (v12.40 CANONICAL)

Monster sprite size is fixed per depth tier. Progression is **smooth through depths 1-7** and contains **one dramatic jump at depth 8** that marks the endgame-entry transition visually as well as mechanically. Depths 8, 9, and 10 all share the endgame sprite size (176×176), turning the Darkness and Light zones into a visually-unified endgame register rather than continuing the linear growth curve.

| Depth | Levels | Monster Size | Boss Size (1.5×) | Note |
|:-----:|:------:|:------------:|:----------------:|------|
| 1 | 1–10 | 16×16 | 24×24 | Introductory scale |
| 2 | 11–20 | 32×32 | 48×48 | |
| 3 | 21–30 | 48×48 | 72×72 | |
| 4 | 31–40 | 64×64 | 96×96 | Player-width match (player ~64×32) |
| 5 | 41–50 | 80×80 | 120×120 | |
| 6 | 51–60 | 96×96 | 144×144 | |
| 7 | 61–70 | 112×112 | 168×168 | Last depth before endgame-scale transition |
| 8 | 71–80 | **176×176** | **264×264** | **Dramatic jump (~57%) — the endgame-entry moment** |
| 9 (Darkness) | 81–90 | 176×176 | 264×264 | Plateau — endgame scale |
| 10 (Light) | 91–100 | 176×176 | 264×264 | Plateau — endgame scale |

Player character is ~64×32 pixels. Monsters match player width at depth 4 (first "we're fighting peers" moment) and dwarf the player dramatically from depth 8 onward (the "we're fighting giants" moment).

**Why one dramatic jump instead of two.** An earlier design draft proposed two size cliffs (at depth 4 and depth 8). The v12.40 ship consolidates this into a single dramatic jump at depth 8 because two jumps dilute each other — a player who has already experienced one "monsters got huge" moment at depth 4 finds the depth 8 version less striking. A single emphatic jump reserved for the endgame-entry moment produces one genuinely memorable visual transition instead of two medium ones. Depths 1-7 scale smoothly in 16-pixel increments per depth (16→32→48→64→80→96→112), producing a natural growth curve through the first 70 levels. The depth 7→8 jump from 112 to 176 is a ~57% increase — meaningfully larger than any other depth transition — creating *the* endgame-entry visual moment: clearing the depth 7 dungeon and walking into depth 8 to find monsters that are visibly, emphatically different from everything the player has fought before.

**Boss size scales proportionally.** Boss sprites remain 1.5× the standard monster size at each depth. At depth 8, boss sprites reach 264×264 pixels — the largest boss sprites in the game. Guardian zone monsters (§55) inherit the depth-8 standard-size of 176×176, matching their level-100 tier. The largest entity in the game is no longer a combat entity — the v14.0 ritual chamber (§57.2b) contains ten standing elemental presences at rest, arrayed in a ring around the player; the individual elements are comparable in sprite scale to apex bosses but they are not combat entities and there is no single largest "boss" in the v14.0 endgame. The canonical Primordial-at-320×320 spec is superseded and preserved only in §57 historical record.

**INV-03 Kill Time Range unchanged.** Size is purely presentation — kill-time audit gates (§6.5) are based on HP scaling and damage formulas, not sprite size. Larger sprites do not increase HP, do not change damage output, and do not affect any combat math. The §6.5 audit thresholds for each depth remain exactly as specified; the v12.40 ship changes the *visual* progression curve without touching the mechanical progression curve.

**Hitbox continuity.** Monster hitboxes remain tied to sprite bounds as before. The depth 8 jump means hitboxes at depth 8+ are larger than depth 7 hitboxes, which means a player's swipe and auto-attack arcs have more generous target-hit geometry against depth 8+ monsters than against depth 7 monsters. This is acceptable and intentional — the endgame-scale transition includes the gameplay side-effect of "bigger targets are easier to connect to," which pairs appropriately with the harder-to-kill HP scaling at those depths.

**Content authoring scope.** The v12.40 change is a tuning/content update to existing `zones.json` or per-depth sprite-size fields in the existing monster art pipeline. No new art required — depth 8 monster sprites are re-rendered at 176×176 instead of 128×128, depth 9 at 176×176 instead of 144×144, depth 10 at 176×176 instead of 160×160. For an existing sprite rendered at 128×128, upscaling to 176×176 is handled by the art pipeline's existing per-depth size variants; the same sprite design scales to the new size. No new sprite designs required, no new animation frames, no new rigging work. The art work is a re-export pass at the updated sizes.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.40): The linear size progression from depth 1 to depth 10 was mechanically sound but emotionally flat — each depth introduced slightly bigger monsters than the last, producing a slow-ramp growth curve without any single visually-memorable transition. The v12.40 non-linear jump concentrates emotional weight at the endgame-entry moment (depth 7→8). A player who has just cleared the depth 7 dungeon boss (already a difficult encounter given the v12.37 archetype schedule introducing Hexers at depth 7) walks into depth 8 and finds the monsters are not just more dangerous but *visibly larger than anything they've fought*. The ~57% size jump is noticeable peripherally — a player doesn't have to be looking at the UI to register that the enemies are different. Depths 8, 9, and 10 all sharing the 176×176 size creates a visually-unified endgame register: once the player crosses into endgame scale, they stay there through Darkness and Light. *(v14.0 note: the canonical Primordial-at-320×320 reference previously here is superseded by the v14.0 ritual framing per §57; the v12.40 depth-scale argument is preserved unchanged but no longer terminates in a Primordial sprite-size statement.)* The visual progression curve now matches the emotional progression curve: smooth growth through the first 70 levels, a transition moment, then endgame plateau. This is the kind of change that costs almost nothing to implement (one art re-export pass at updated sizes) but produces a meaningfully different experiential feel across the full depth progression. It's also an example of how the zone-as-pedagogy thread (v12.32/v12.33/v12.35/v12.37) extends into presentation: depth 8 isn't just mechanically harder, it *looks* harder at a glance.]`

#### §6.9 Dungeon Bosses

Every zone has a dungeon boss. The boss is one of the zone's 2 unique monsters scaled to 1.5× the zone's standard monster size with unique pattern-based attacks (charge patterns, beam sweeps, phase transitions, summon mechanics). Boss variants are art modifications of existing unique monster sprites, not entirely new designs.

#### §6.10 Content Totals

| Category | Count |
|----------|------:|
| Standard elemental zones (8 elements × 8 depths) | 64 |
| Endgame zones (Darkness + Light) | 2 |
| Imp evolution sprites (10 stages × 8 reskins + 2 endgame) | 82 |
| Skeleton evolution sprites | 82 |
| Eye evolution sprites | 82 |
| Zone-unique monsters (2 × 66 zones) | 132 |
| Dungeon boss variants | 66 |
| **Total monster sprites** | **378 + 66 boss** |

### §7. Multiplayer Contribution XP

XP divided by contribution fractions:

| Role | Formula |
|------|---------|
| DPS | Damage / Monster Max HP |
| Tank | Damage Mitigated / Monster Total Damage Output |
| Evasion | Attacks Dodged / Total Attacks |
| Healer | Effective Healing / Total Party Damage Taken |
| Aggro | time_as_primary_target / total_fight_time |

GOLD_CONTRIBUTION_MINIMUM(0.05): below → no gold. Per-role clamp 0–2.

`player_xp = monster_xp_yield × (player_contribution_total / sum_of_all_player_totals)`

Recipients must be alive and connected.

#### §7.1 Per-Fight Role Titles (v12.25 CANONICAL)

At the conclusion of any multiplayer fight where a monster is killed with two or more contributing players, each party member is assigned a single **role title** for that fight, drawn from a curated pool of ~30 evocative descriptors matched to the player's contribution shape during that fight.

**Examples (illustrative, not exhaustive):**

- **The Spearhead** — majority DPS contribution
- **The Closer** — delivered the final killing blow after sustained team engagement
- **The Bulwark** — majority damage mitigated, primary tank
- **The Ghost** — majority Evasion fraction, dodge-heavy
- **The Scalpel** — highest crit-rate contribution
- **The Anchor** — most time as primary aggro target
- **The Healer** — any role with >30% healing share
- **The Opportunist** — most collisions triggered
- **The Tactician** — highest status-application count
- **The Lifeline** — single decisive healing action in a critical moment

The full pool of approximately 30 titles, along with the specific contribution-shape-to-title matching logic, lives in a content file (`role-titles.json`, authored as a v12.25.1 content ship). Each title has a defined contribution-shape trigger: "The Spearhead" fires when a player's DPS fraction exceeds the party average by >15% AND no other fraction is higher; "The Closer" fires when a player delivered the final blow after 80%+ of the fight was already in play; and so on. The matching logic selects exactly one title per player per fight — in cases where multiple titles would match, the rarest-triggered title wins (tiebreak: alphabetical).

**Display.** Post-fight, each party member sees their own title as a brief popup (~3 seconds) with the title name, a one-line flavor text, and the contribution data that earned it ("The Spearhead · 48% DPS share · +1,240g"). The same popup is visible on the party panel to other party members so they can see each other's titles.

**Solo play.** In solo combat, no role title fires. Titles emerge from contribution comparison and are meaningless when only one player contributed. This is intentional — the system produces characterization within parties, not individual-achievement titles.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Contribution XP was already tracked per-role; v12.25 adds characterization on top. A percentage breakdown ("42% DPS, 28% Tank") is bureaucratic; a title ("The Spearhead") is a story. The pool of 30 titles is calibrated to produce rotational characterization — same player, different fights, different titles — so the system never produces a fixed "you are this type of player" label. Parties develop memorable per-fight moments: "that was the fight where Bro Alice was the Closer." The contribution data is unchanged; the presentation is new.]`

#### §7.2 Long-Term Contribution Signature (v12.25 CANONICAL)

A player's role titles from their most recent 50 multiplayer fights are aggregated into a **contribution signature**, displayed on the inspect card. The signature shows the player's title frequency distribution — "primary" (most-frequent title), "secondary" (second-most-frequent, if distinct), and an optional "tertiary" for players with broadly varied play patterns.

**Examples of rendered signatures:**

- Fighting style: **The Spearhead** (primary), The Closer (secondary)
- Fighting style: **The Bulwark** (primary), The Anchor (secondary), The Opportunist (tertiary)
- Fighting style: **The Healer** (primary) — single-role specialist

**Derivation.** The signature is computed from the rolling 50-fight history. Fights older than the 50th most recent roll off and no longer contribute. This means a player's signature evolves with their recent play — a DPS who switches to tanking will see their signature shift within 20-30 fights. Players who play widely varied roles may see their signature show three titles with closely-matched frequencies; specialists see a single dominant title.

**Display.** The signature lives on the inspect card in a dedicated "Fighting style" line positioned above Recent roles. Tapping the line opens an expanded view showing the full 50-fight role distribution (title names with frequency counts). The inspection is passive — any player can read another's signature without interaction.

**Minimum fight count.** Players with fewer than 10 multiplayer fights show a placeholder ("Fighting style: yet to emerge"). This avoids premature or unstable signatures for new characters.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Per-fight titles are ephemeral felt moments; the long-term signature is the emergent identity those moments produce. Two time scales interact naturally — a party seeing Bro Alice's inspect card before a fight knows her style; after the fight, she earned a specific title for that fight. Parties develop characterization both in the moment and across the long game. The 50-fight window is a balance: long enough that occasional off-style fights don't skew the signature, short enough that real play-style changes are reflected within a reasonable number of sessions.]`

---

## PART II — COMBAT FRAMEWORK (§8–§13)

### §8. Damage Pipeline (Interceptor Chain)

#### §8.1 DamageEvent Data Model

| Field | Type | Desc |
|-------|------|------|
| base_damage | Float | Pre-multiplied by crit_mult if crit |
| damage_type | Physical/Magic/Elemental/True | Which mitigation applies |
| damage_source | DirectAttack/SwipeTrigger/DoT/Collision/Reflected/Environmental | Origin for routing |
| element | Element/None | For effectiveness and collision logic |
| source_actor | ActorRef | Who dealt it |
| poise_damage | Float | 0 for DoT/Collision/Environmental |
| is_crit | Bool | Rolled before pipeline, mult pre-applied |
| generation | Int | 0=player-initiated. 1=reactive(Thorns). P90 only fires on gen 0. |

#### §8.2 Interceptor Chain

| Pri | Interceptor | Logic |
|:---:|------------|-------|
| 0 | Immunity | True → skip to P80. If immune to element: ×0.20 |
| 10 | Block | If active block tap registered within commitment window of this hit: check perfect window → if within PERFECT_BLOCK_WINDOW, damage = 0 + stamina refund; else apply block_reduction (§2.3), deduct BLOCK_STAM_COST. No tap registered → interceptor passes through, damage unchanged. See §5.3b. |
| 20 | Dodge | If Dodging in i-frames: dmg=0. DoT/Collision sources CANNOT be dodged. Returns early (no Thorns). |
| 30 | Armor Attunement | If incoming element matches armor attunement: ×(1 - ATTUNEMENT_DEFENSE(0.20)) |
| 40 | Effectiveness | If strong element vs target: ×0.75. If weak: ×1.25. Physical unaffected. |
| 50 | Damage Modifiers | Vulnerability debuffs (Soak, Curse), damage-taken mults. |
| 80 | Final Application | Deduct HP. Fire OnDamageTaken/OnKill. Synchronous kill resolution (loot/gold/XP inline). |
| 90 | Post-Damage (gen 0 only) | Thorns, Counter Resonance, Poise damage. |

```
func resolve_damage(event, target):
    # P0 — Immunity
    if event.damage_type == TRUE: goto P80
    if event.element != NONE and target.is_immune(event.element):
        event.base_damage ×= 0.20

    original_damage = event.base_damage

    # P10 — Block (Active Block, v12.27 — §5.3b)
    if event.damage_source in [DIRECT_ATTACK, SWIPE_TRIGGER]:
        block_tap = target.get_active_block_tap(event.impact_time)  # returns tap if within BLOCK_COMMITMENT_WINDOW
        if block_tap is not None:
            tap_delta = event.impact_time - block_tap.timestamp
            if tap_delta <= PERFECT_BLOCK_WINDOW:
                event.base_damage = 0  # perfect block: full negation
                target.refund_stamina(PERFECT_BLOCK_STAM_REFUND)
                target.fire_perfect_block_fx()  # chime + gold flash
            elif tap_delta <= BLOCK_COMMITMENT_WINDOW:
                reduction = target.get_block_reduction()  # §2.3
                event.base_damage ×= (1 - reduction)
                target.consume_stamina(BLOCK_STAM_COST)
            target.consume_block_tap(block_tap)  # tap is single-use against nearest hit

    # P20 — Dodge (returns early — no Thorns/CR)
    if target.state == DODGING and target.in_iframes:
        if event.damage_source not in [COLLISION, DOT]: return

    # P30 — Armor Attunement Defense
    if event.element != NONE and event.element == target.armor_attunement:
        event.base_damage ×= (1 - ATTUNEMENT_DEFENSE)

    # P40 — Elemental Effectiveness
    if event.element != NONE and event.damage_type == ELEMENTAL:
        effectiveness = get_effectiveness(event.element, target.element)
        event.base_damage ×= effectiveness  # 0.75 (strong), 1.0 (neutral), 1.25 (weak)

    # P50 — Damage Modifiers (Soak, Curse, etc.)
    event.base_damage ×= target.get_damage_taken_multiplier(event)

    # P80 — Final Application
    label P80:
    target.hp -= event.base_damage
    fire OnDamageTaken(target, event)
    if target.hp <= 0:
        fire OnKill(event.source_actor, target)
        resolve_kill_loot_synchronous(target, event.source_actor)  # §23.2

    # P90 — Post-Damage (gen 0 only)
    if event.generation > 0: return
    mitigated_amount = original_damage - event.base_damage

    # Thorns
    if mitigated_amount > 0 and event.damage_source in [DIRECT_ATTACK, SWIPE_TRIGGER]:
        resolve_thorns(target, event.source_actor, mitigated_amount)

    # Counter Resonance
    if target.is_player and event.damage_type != TRUE:
        apply_counter_resonance(event.source_actor, target)

    # Poise
    if event.poise_damage > 0:
        target.poise -= event.poise_damage
        if target.poise <= 0: force_stagger(target)
```

#### §8.3 Thorns (CANONICAL)

```
func resolve_thorns(target, attacker, mitigated_amount):
    if not target.is_player: return
    if target.thorns_procs_this_second >= THORNS_PROCS_PER_SEC(4): return
    if target.last_active_damage_tick < current_tick - THORNS_ACTIVITY_WINDOW(5.0): return

    eff = clamp(attacker.level / target.level, 0.35, 1.0)
    pack = clamp(1 + 0.12 × enemies_targeting_player, 1.0, 3.4)
    fort = target.get_fortification()
    thorns_dmg = ((THORNS_MIT_COEFF(0.08) × mitigated_amount) + (THORNS_FORT_FLAT(0.4) × fort)) × min(eff × pack, 3.4)

    deal_damage(attacker, thorns_dmg, type=PHYSICAL, source=REFLECTED, generation=1)
    target.thorns_procs_this_second += 1
```

**Thorns Pack-Flash (v12.26).** When the pack multiplier threshold is crossed (3+ enemies simultaneously targeting the same Thorns-active target), a brief green aura appears around the target (~1 second) and all affected attackers flash with thorn-spike particles for ~0.3 seconds. This fires on both PvE Fortification Thorns procs and PvP Tank-hold moments. Visual and audio spec in `creative-reference.md` §8.

### §9. Status Effect System

#### §9.1 Ten Statuses

| Element | Status | Effect | Type |
|---------|--------|--------|------|
| Flame | Burn | DoT, 0.5s ticks | Damage |
| Frost | Freeze | Hard CC — no action, can aim | Control |
| Water | Soak | Vulnerability — amplified elemental damage taken | Debuff |
| Venom | Root | Soft CC — no movement, can attack. DoT 1.0s ticks | Control + Damage |
| Storm | Shock | Next hit amplified, then discharges | Amplifier |
| Stone | Fracture | Stacking poise reduction, up to 5 stacks | Stacking debuff |
| Wind | Slow | Reduced movement and attack speed | Soft control |
| Flora | Thorn | Retaliatory DoT — enemies that hit the afflicted target take damage. 1.0s ticks. | Reactive Damage |
| Dark | Curse | Enemy damage reduced, damage taken increased | Force multiplier |
| Light | Reveal | Strips resistances. Swipe also heals self and allies (Bless). | Debuff + Heal |

**Eight palette** (Flame, Frost, Water, Venom, Storm, Stone, Wind, Flora) available through world exploration. **Two endgame** (Dark, Light) gated behind deepest zones.

#### §9.2 Status Specifications

| Status | Base Duration | Refresh | Max Duration | Stacks | ICD |
|--------|:------------:|:-------:|:------------:|:------:|:---:|
| Burn | 4.0s | +1.0s extend | 6.0s | 1 | 0.5s |
| Freeze | 3.0s | +0.5s extend | 5.0s | 1 | 0.5s |
| Soak | 5.0s | +1.0s extend | 7.0s | 1 | 0.5s |
| Root | 5.0s | +0.5s extend | 7.0s | 1 | 0.5s |
| Shock | 4.0s | Reset timer | 4.0s | 1 | 0.5s |
| Fracture | 6.0s | Reset+stack | 6.0s | 5 | 0.5s |
| Slow | 5.0s | +1.0s extend | 7.0s | 1 | 0.5s |
| Thorn | 5.0s | +1.0s extend | 7.0s | 1 | 0.5s |
| Curse | 6.0s | +1.0s extend | 8.0s | 1 | 0.5s |
| Reveal | 5.0s | +1.0s extend | 7.0s | 1 | 0.5s |

All durations multiplied by Elemental Mastery duration derivative (§2.2) on application, and by Influence derivative (§2.6) for CC-type statuses (Freeze, Root, Slow).

##### §9.2.1 Collision-Opportunity Weapon Edge Glow (v12.38 CANONICAL)

When a nearby enemy carries a status that the player's currently-equipped weapon would *trigger a collision against* via its swipe attack, the weapon sprite's edge displays a subtle peripheral glow in the setup element's color. The glow communicates *"this target is ready for a collision"* without adding markers to the enemy sprite.

**Trigger rule.**

- **Setup status + trigger element = collision compatibility.** The game consults the existing §10.5 Collision Table to determine whether the player's swipe element can trigger a collision against an enemy's active status. When a match exists, the weapon glows.
- **Proximity.** The glow activates when at least one enemy within 20u of the player carries a compatible status. Enemies outside 20u do not contribute to the glow. This bounds the signal to combat-relevant range rather than every statused creature on screen.
- **Status-timer intensity coupling.** The glow's intensity scales with the compatible status's remaining duration — stronger glow as status timer approaches expiry. This conveys urgency in peripheral vision: the player learns to read the glow's brightness as "hurry" without a numeric timer.
- **Multiple compatible enemies.** When multiple enemies carry compatible statuses, the glow intensity tracks the *most-urgent* (lowest-remaining-duration) compatible status. The player is cued to act before the nearest expiry, regardless of how many compatible setups are present.

**Visual treatment.** The glow renders as a 2-pixel outer edge ring on the weapon sprite, in the setup element's canonical color (per §10.1 element palette — Flame red, Frost blue, Venom green, etc.). Intensity ranges from ~15% alpha (status just applied, plenty of time remaining) to ~60% alpha (status within 1 second of expiry). The glow never exceeds 60% alpha — it is peripheral vision signal, not attention-grabbing banner. See `creative-reference.md` §1.9.8 for the full visual direction.

**Why the weapon, not the enemy.** The original plan draft placed a small arrow on the enemy's status icon indicating collision compatibility. The revised v12.38 spec moves the signal onto the player's own weapon because:

1. **Peripheral vision discipline.** The player already watches their own weapon — it's the hand in the foreground. A glow there is caught peripherally without the player looking for it. An arrow on an enemy status icon requires the player to track the icon, which competes with tracking enemy position and the fight's tactical state.
2. **Clutter scaling.** In crowded fights with many statused enemies, the arrow approach adds one marker per compatible enemy, producing visual noise that scales with combat density. The weapon-glow approach adds zero battlefield markers; information density stays constant regardless of how many enemies match.
3. **Information richness.** The weapon glow encodes two signals in one channel: element (color) and urgency (intensity). The arrow approach would require a separate channel for urgency. Condensing both into the weapon's edge keeps the signal dense but compact.
4. **Interpretability.** A glowing weapon reads as "my tool is ready" — an empowering register. A marker on an enemy reads as "the enemy has been identified for you" — a slight training-wheels register. The weapon-glow framing respects the player's role as the combat protagonist.

**Accessibility.**

- **Toggleable.** Players can disable the glow via Settings → Accessibility → Combat Hints → Collision Opportunity Glow. Default on for new players; remains on for experienced players unless explicitly toggled off. There is no auto-retirement — unlike the v12.27 Dynamic Reticle, this hint does not become clutter for veterans because its signal bandwidth is low and intermittent.
- **Colorblind-safe.** Element color distinction is supported by the §10.1 shape-first palette; the weapon-glow inherits the setup element's shape-coded color from the existing palette. Players using the colorblind-safe palette option see the same glow mechanic with the adjusted palette.
- **Shape-first supplement.** For players on the colorblind-safe palette, the glow's shape-edge rendering uses the setup element's shape glyph (§10.1) along the weapon edge — Flame diamond-cornered, Frost hex-cornered, etc. The glow's shape texture is present for all players but becomes the primary signal channel when color distinction is degraded.

**No damage effect, no mechanical impact.** The weapon-glow is a purely informational signal. It does not amplify collision damage, does not change collision timing windows, does not grant any bonus. Collision mechanics per §10.3 are unchanged. The glow is a UI/visual layer on top of the existing collision system.

**Data.** Rendering uses client-side state — the client already tracks active enemy statuses (for the §9 status UI) and knows the player's equipped weapon. The glow is computed client-side from these existing data flows; no new server message required. A settings flag `collision_glow_enabled` (default true) gates rendering.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.38): The original plan spec placed arrows on enemy status icons to indicate collision compatibility, which creates a marker-per-enemy visual pattern that scales poorly with combat density. The revised spec moves the signal onto the player's own weapon, which is the most peripherally-visible object in the combat scene (the hand in the foreground). A glowing weapon reads as "my tool is coming alive" — an empowering register that respects the player's agency rather than framing compatibility as a system-provided hint. The status-timer-coupled intensity encodes urgency into the same channel as element identity, giving the signal two bits of information per glow without requiring a numeric timer anywhere on screen. In crowded fights the glow tracks the most-urgent compatible status, which naturally prioritizes the player's decision-making toward the shortest-remaining-duration opportunity. The glow never exceeds ~60% alpha — it's a peripheral signal, not a banner. The feature is permanently toggleable rather than auto-retiring because its signal bandwidth is low and its interpretation cost is near-zero for experienced players (a faint Flame-colored edge on my weapon means there's a nearby Burn to trigger); veterans who've internalized the collision table still benefit from the at-a-glance awareness without the signal demanding attention. This is the v12.38 bundle's contribution to peripheral-signal discipline — turning the player's own tool into the information display instead of cluttering the battlefield with markers.]`

#### §9.3 Status Scaling

**DoT damage:** Burn base + Power × BURN_POWER_COEFF(0.3), then multiplied by EM status damage mult (§2.2).
**Root DoT:** base + Power × ROOT_POWER_COEFF(0.15), then × EM mult.
**Soak vulnerability:** SOAK_BASE(0.15) + Influence × SOAK_INF_COEFF(0.0003). Multiplies post-mitigation elemental damage at P50.
**Shock amplification:** SHOCK_BASE(0.40) + Influence × SHOCK_INF_COEFF(0.0005). Next hit's damage multiplied.
**Fracture poise reduction:** FRACTURE_BASE(3) + Influence × FRACTURE_INF_COEFF(0.005) per stack.
**Slow reduction:** SLOW_BASE(0.20) + Influence × SLOW_INF_COEFF(0.0004). Reduces move speed and attack speed.
**Thorn retaliatory damage:** THORN_BASE(5) + Power × THORN_POWER_COEFF(0.20) per tick, then multiplied by EM status damage mult (§2.2). Triggered when an enemy attacks the afflicted target — each incoming hit starts one tick cycle. Does not stack with multiple attackers (single source, refreshes on each incoming hit).
**Curse damage reduction:** CURSE_DMG_REDUCE(0.15) + Influence × CURSE_INF_COEFF(0.0003). Also increases damage taken by same amount.
**Reveal resistance strip:** Removes REVEAL_STRIP(1) resistance tier. With high Influence, may strip additional resistances.

#### §9.4 Light Element — Bless Mechanic (v13 REPLACEMENT — Eclipse-Only Healing)

**Canonical Light-heals mechanic is replaced.** Light alone no longer heals. The Light swipe trigger retains its Reveal status application but no longer produces heal. Healing now occurs only when Light collides with Dark (Eclipse per §33.5).

**Revised Light swipe behavior:**

When using a Light-element weapon, the **swipe trigger** applies Reveal status to enemies hit. No healing effect. Power and Restoration no longer feed into a Light-specific heal formula — restoration stats now primarily scale Eclipse healing (see §33.5 Eclipse specification) and regeneration rates (§2.4).

**Why the change.** Canonical Light-heals positioned Light as a support/healer element independent of Dark. This dilutes the §33.5 Eclipse-as-thematic-capstone framing — if Light alone heals, Eclipse's unique restorative role is undermined. Making healing require Eclipse makes the game's central duality (Dark + Light) the only source of restoration in combat. Party composition becomes thematically meaningful: a party seeking healing must include both Dark and Light element players, and healing becomes a moment of thematic reconciliation rather than a one-element support role.

**Eclipse healing behavior (cross-reference to §33.5).** When a Dark-element status on a target is triggered by a Light-element swipe (or vice versa), the Eclipse collision produces:
- Massive damage to the target (per §10.6 collision damage formula with Eclipse-specific multiplier)
- AOE heal to the triggering player and nearby allies (heal amount = BLESS_BASE(30) + Power × BLESS_POWER_COEFF(0.3), multiplied by Restoration heal mult per §2.4) — same formula canonical Light-heals used, but now gated behind Eclipse-collision requirement
- Cleanse of all statuses on triggering player and nearby allies

The heal formula is preserved from canonical Light-heals; only the trigger condition changes.

**Visual and audio (v12.26 PRESERVED for Eclipse).** The Eclipse heal fires a soft gold wave radiating outward from the caster in a ~20-unit radius over 0.8 seconds. Affected allies briefly glow gold. Heal damage numbers render in a warmer gold tint than standard heal numbers. A specific heal chime distinct from the standard healing sound plays. Visible to all players (including opposing PvP players). Full creative direction in `creative-reference.md` §8.

**Migration.** Characters with high Restoration stats built around canonical Light-heals retain stat values; the healing effect now requires Eclipse collisions instead of Light swipes alone. Players with primarily-Light-element builds may need to add Dark-element weapons or allies to their party composition to preserve support capability.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): Canonical Light-heals mechanic produced a healer role but diluted the game's central Eclipse-as-capstone framing. v13 preserves Light as a tactical element (Reveal application is unchanged) while making healing require the Eclipse collision. The duality of Dark+Light becomes the unique source of restoration in the game — party composition now thematically requires both halves of the fracture to produce healing. This is the game's Eclipse-as-thesis extended into party play: the fracture's healing is experienced through the fracture's reunification. Restoration stats remain valuable — they scale the Eclipse heal — but the trigger condition now carries thematic weight rather than being a free consequence of one element choice.]`

#### §9.5 CC Diminishing Returns (v13 REPLACEMENT — Visible Will Meter)

**Canonical hidden-cooldown DR is replaced by visible Will meter.** Monsters have a visible Will meter that displays above their health bar. CC application depletes Will. At zero Will, the monster cannot be CC'd and becomes erratic (see below). Will refills when CC pressure stops.

**Will meter mechanics:**

- Each monster has a `will_max` value scaling with archetype and level (see tuning below). Will_max displays as a small secondary bar above the health bar.
- Each successful CC application on a monster depletes `will_max × CC_WILL_DEPLETE(0.25)` from the Will bar. The depletion scales with CC severity — hard CC (Stun, Freeze) depletes more; soft CC (Slow, Blind) depletes less per application (see severity table below).
- When Will reaches 0, the monster enters **erratic state**: 
  - CC applications have no effect (replaces the canonical "30% duration at 3rd+ application")
  - The monster's behavior becomes more unpredictable — attack patterns deviate from baseline, movement speed varies, target selection is less consistent
  - Visual indicator: the monster's Will bar shows empty (or pulses faintly), and the monster has a subtle visual effect (shaken outline or erratic motion)
  - The erratic state persists until Will refills
- **Will refill:** When no CC has been applied to the monster for CC_WILL_REFILL_GRACE(3.0s), the Will bar begins refilling at `will_max × CC_WILL_REFILL_RATE(0.20 per second)`. Full refill takes ~5 seconds from empty. During refill, CC effects resume normal application once Will is above 0.

**CC severity table (v13 — replaces canonical three-tier diminishing returns):**

| CC Severity | Example Statuses | Will Depletion per Application |
|-------------|------------------|-------------------------------|
| Hard | Stun, Freeze, Root | will_max × 0.35 |
| Medium | Blind, Slow, Shock | will_max × 0.20 |
| Soft | Reveal, Curse, Bless (defensive) | will_max × 0.10 |

Approximately 3 hard CCs, 5 medium CCs, or 10 soft CCs deplete a full Will bar from max to 0.

**Per-monster, per-target.** Will tracking is per-monster instance, not per-party. Each monster tracks its own Will bar. A party focus-firing CC on one monster depletes that specific monster's Will; other monsters' Will bars are unaffected.

**Boss Will:** Bosses have higher `will_max` values and faster refill rates (see tuning below). Bosses reach erratic state later than regular monsters but refill faster once engagement pauses.

**CC Refresh Cap preserved:** CC_REFRESH_CAP_PER_SEC(2 open world, 4 dungeon). Prevents perma-lock in 50-player rooms. The Will meter operates alongside this cap.

**Tuning:**

| Variable | Default | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|-------|
| CC_WILL_MAX_FODDER | 50 | Fodder/Swarm baseline Will |
| CC_WILL_MAX_BRUTE | 150 | Brute/Sentinel baseline Will |
| CC_WILL_MAX_HEXER | 200 | Hexer/Volatile baseline Will |
| CC_WILL_MAX_BOSS | 500 | Dungeon boss baseline Will |
| CC_WILL_MAX_APEX | 1000 | Apex boss baseline Will |
| CC_WILL_DEPLETE_HARD | 0.35 | Fraction of will_max per hard CC |
| CC_WILL_DEPLETE_MEDIUM | 0.20 | Fraction of will_max per medium CC |
| CC_WILL_DEPLETE_SOFT | 0.10 | Fraction of will_max per soft CC |
| CC_WILL_REFILL_GRACE | 3.0s | Time without CC before refill begins |
| CC_WILL_REFILL_RATE | 0.20 | Fraction of will_max refilled per second |
| CC_REFRESH_CAP_PER_SEC | 2 open world, 4 dungeon | Preserved from canonical |

**Visual design.** The Will bar renders as a thin slate-colored bar above the health bar, tighter in visual weight so it doesn't compete with health for attention. Will depletion animates smoothly. At 0 Will, the bar flashes briefly then dims to show the erratic state is active. Refilling bar uses a soft gradient to signal ongoing recovery.

**Player-facing legibility.** CC becomes a readable resource interaction. Players can see *why* their CC stopped working (Will depleted) and plan around it (wait for refill, rotate targets, focus non-CC damage during erratic windows). The hidden-timer frustration of canonical DR is replaced by visible resource management.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): Canonical DR used a hidden internal cooldown (12s memory, 60%/30% scaling) that prevented chain-locking but was invisible to players. When CC stopped working, it felt like a bug or RNG. v13 Will meter makes the mechanic *readable combat state* — players see when a monster is approaching erratic state, plan their rotations around it, and feel like they're managing a resource rather than fighting against opaque rules. The erratic state is a tactical opportunity rather than a penalty: the monster's unpredictability can be advantageous for burst damage, while CC-focused builds wait for refill and target other monsters in the meantime. The visual weight of the Will bar is deliberately smaller than health to preserve combat focus — Will is secondary information, present when needed, not intrusive when ignored.]`

#### §9.6 ICD (CANONICAL, v13 ADDITIVE — Brief Visibility)

Per source (weapon+actor pair), per status, per target.
`STATUS_ICD = max(STATUS_ICD_FLOOR(0.5s), status.tick_interval × STATUS_ICD_RATIO(0.8))`

ICD escalation on consecutive refreshes:
`effective_ICD = STATUS_ICD × (1 + consecutive_refreshes × ICD_ESCALATION(0.50))`
Counter resets on expiry or collision consumption.

**v13 ADDITIVE — Brief visibility on block.** When an ICD blocks an expected status application (e.g., the player expects Burn to apply but ICD prevents it), a small indicator briefly fades in at the attack's resolution point — a faint clock icon with a ~0.5s visible duration. Players learn what ICD is by seeing it prevent an expected effect, rather than being confused by missing status applications. The indicator does not broadcast to other players; it's a personal legibility layer.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13 additive): ICD as a mechanic prevents trivial permalock exploits, but its invisibility creates confusion — players who expect Burn but see no Burn appear think they've found a bug. A brief indicator when ICD blocks an expected effect gives players transparency exactly where confusion would otherwise occur. Non-intrusive: only displays when the player's action would have applied a status but ICD prevented it; invisible in all other cases.]`

### §10. Elemental Collisions

`[DESIGN INTENT: Collisions are the skill ceiling and build diversity engine. Applying element A (via auto-attack) then triggering with element B (via swipe) creates combos that reward gear planning and timing. Without collisions, combat is "hit until dead." With them, combat is "build a setup, trigger a payoff, sustain your resources."]`

#### §10.1 Ten Elements

| Element | Shape | Color | Anim |
|---------|-------|-------|------|
| Flame | Diamond | #C0392B | Flicker |
| Frost | Hexagon | #2980B9 | Ripple |
| Water | Circle | #3498DB | Wave |
| Venom | Teardrop | #27AE60 | Breathe |
| Storm | Star | #8E44AD | Flash |
| Stone | Square | #795548 | Static |
| Wind | Triangle | #7F8C8D | Spin |
| Flora | Leaf | #2ECC71 | Grow |
| Dark | Trefoil | #2C3E50 | Pulse |
| Light | Starburst | #F1C40F | Steady glow |

Shapes are the PRIMARY identification channel (not color). Colorblind-safe palette option in settings.

#### §10.2 Effectiveness Circle (CANONICAL)

```
Flame → melts → Frost → freezes → Storm → shatters → Stone → blocks → Wind → scatters → Venom → corrupts → Flora → absorbs → Water → extinguishes → Flame
```

**+25% elemental damage** when strong against target's element. **-25%** when weak. Physical auto-attack base unaffected.

**Dark ↔ Light:** Mutual +25% both directions.

**9 Volatile relationships** (circle-adjacent): Flame-Frost, Frost-Storm, Storm-Stone, Stone-Wind, Wind-Venom, Venom-Flora, Flora-Water, Water-Flame. Plus Dark-Light (mutual). **Volatile collision bonus is directional** — only the strong→weak direction gets +30%. See §4.7 for the full directional map. **10 volatile collisions total** (8 directional + 2 mutual Dark↔Light). 80 stable collisions.

Every link passes the "would a ten-year-old understand this" test.

#### §10.3 Collision Resolution Logic (CANONICAL)

```
Given: target has STATUS of element Y, incoming swipe/attack has element X

1. X == Y (same element)?
   → REFRESH existing status. No collision. No mana restore. Done.

2. X != Y (different element)?
   → Lookup §10.5 collision table.
   a. Match found?
      → Create collision DamageEvent: {type: Elemental, source: Collision,
         element: X, gen: 0, poise: 0}
      → Damage = §10.6 formula
      → If Volatile direction (trigger X is strong vs setup Y on effectiveness circle,
         OR Dark↔Light mutual): damage × (1 + VOLATILE_COLLISION_BONUS(0.30))
      → Armor attunement offense bonus if applicable (§4.3)
      → Elemental Mastery collision multiplier (§2.2)
      → Route through full interceptor chain independently
      → Consume setup status (remove from target)
      → Check mana restore (§3.5)
      → Fire OnCollisionTriggered → Codex update if new
      → Spawn cosmetic ground mark (client-side only, §10.7)
   b. No match?
      → No collision. Normal damage. Incoming may apply its own status.
```

#### §10.4 Swipe Rhythm

The swipe is NOT a second auto-attack. It costs mana. It has a fixed cooldown (SWIPE_COOLDOWN). The player cannot auto-attack and swipe simultaneously.

**Combat flow:** Auto-attack (count 1), auto-attack (count 2), auto-attack (count 3) — status builds, weapon glow intensifies. Watch the icon appear. Choose the moment. SWIPE. Collision detonates with combo bonus based on count. Mana chime. Count resets. Cooldown. Return to auto-attacks. Rebuild.

**The combo decision (§5.9):** The player can swipe at any combo count. Count 0 gives base collision. Count 1 gives burst bonus. Count 2 adds spread. Count 3 adds next-cycle investment. The player decides when to break the chain based on context — Now, Wide, or Next.

**Staff rhythm is different:** Tag enemy 1, tag enemy 2, tag enemy 3. One swipe detonates all three. Longer buildup, bigger payoff. The combo count tracks only the primary target — the target the Staff Mage built the chain against gets the combo bonus.

#### §10.5 Collision Table (AUTHORITATIVE — 90 Pairs)

##### §10.5a Quick Lookup Matrix

Rows = setup status active on target. Columns = trigger element incoming. Read as: "Target has [row] status, hit by [column] element → [collision name]." **—** = same element (refresh, no collision — pure weapons trade collision burst for sustained status pressure, §4.2).

| Setup ↓ Trigger → | Flm | Frs | Wtr | Vnm | Stm | Stn | Wnd | Flr | Drk | Lgt |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Flame** | — | Steam | Quench | ToxFumes | Overchg | Magma | Firestm | Wildfire | Shadowfr | SacFlame |
| **Frost** | Meltwtr | — | FlshFrz | Shatter | Hailstm | Permfrst | Blizzard | ThwBloom | DrdChill | DiamDust |
| **Water** | Scald | IceShell | — | Dilute | Conduit | Mudslide | Monsoon | Overgrth | Abyss | HolyWtr |
| **Venom** | AshCloud | FrzVein | Dissolve | — | Blight | Petrify | Miasma | Decomp | Nightshd | Antidote |
| **Storm** | Ignition | StatFrz | ShrtCirc | ToxShock | — | SeismPls | Tempest | ChrgCanp | Blackout | Judgment |
| **Stone** | Forge | Shale | Washout | Corrode | Tremor | — | Erosion | Reclaim | Crumble | Mend |
| **Wind** | Updraft | Frstbite | Downpour | PlgueWnd | Cyclone | Sandstrm | — | Overgrow | DrdWind | Zephyr |
| **Flora** | ScchErth | BrtlThrn | Bloom | BltGardn | LgtnRod | PtrfWood | SctrSeed | — | Witherng | PurfBlm |
| **Dark** | Hellfire | Dread | Drown | Wither | Hex | Shackle | Haunt | Strngld | — | Eclipse |
| **Light** | RadFire | Purify | Baptism | ClnsBlm | DvnStrk | Conscrte | Salvatn | Sanctry | Umbra | — |

90 collision pairs. 10 volatile collisions (8 directional strong→weak + 2 mutual Dark↔Light, each +30%). 80 stable collisions. **—** = same element (refresh, no collision).

Eclipse is the endgame capstone. Players who have gone pure Good or pure Evil in alignment must make one choice against their alignment in the endgame zones before Eclipse becomes narratively "complete" (unlocks the Eclipse cosmetic ground mark variant). `[DESIGN INTENT: Thorn is the "fight back" status — the only status that punishes enemies for attacking the afflicted target. This makes Flora the defensive-reactive element. A Flora weapon on a Tank build creates a layered Thorns fantasy: the Fortification stat Thorns (§8.3) trigger on blocked hits, while the Thorn status triggers on all incoming hits to anyone with the status active. Flora's collisions lean toward sustain (heals, roots, regen zones) rather than burst damage — Flora is the element that keeps you alive, not the one that kills fastest. The Verdant Wilds zone is where the Tank build finds its identity.]`

**Reverse Palette Pairs (21):**

The reverse direction of existing palette collisions. Where the original expresses the *trigger element's nature acting on the setup*, the reverse expresses *what the setup status does when destabilized*. Every element pair now has TWO collisions with different identities — weapon orientation (which element is auto-attack vs swipe) always matters.

`[DESIGN INTENT: The 21 reverse pairs mean every two-element weapon now has a split personality based on orientation. Steam (Flame→Frost) is burst. Meltwater (Frost→Flame) is control. Same weapon, different element orientation, different combat identity. The player choosing which element is auto-attack vs swipe is choosing between two playstyles — not just two damage numbers.]`

Dark as trigger corrupts and twists. Each collision takes the setup status and makes it more sinister — hidden, lingering, feeding on weakness.

**Palette Setup → Light Trigger (7):**

Light as trigger purifies and transforms. Each collision redeems the setup status — converting damage into healing, debuffs into buffs, punishment into protection.

| # | Setup Status | Trigger Element | Name | Effect | Scaling Stat |
|:-:|-------------|----------------|------|--------|:------------:|
| C82 | Burn (Flame) | Light | Sacred Flame | Burn consumed, attacker and nearby allies gain damage buff for 4s | Mind |
| C83 | Freeze (Frost) | Light | Diamond Dust | Burst + leaves prismatic field — allies in field gain crit chance bonus | Agility |

`[DESIGN INTENT: Palette→Dark collisions make Dark-trigger weapons feel evil — the status becomes twisted, hidden, fear-amplified. Palette→Light collisions make Light-trigger weapons feel compassionate — the status becomes purified, redirected into healing and protection. Exception: Judgment (Storm→Light) is pure burst — Light isn't only merciful. Exception: Mend (Stone→Light) gives the attacker damage resistance — the light that fills the cracks protects the one who brought it. A Stone/Light weapon on a Tank is a self-buff tool: Fracture the enemy, trigger Light, gain resistance.]`

**Wind Setup → Flora Trigger (1):**

| # | Setup Status | Trigger Element | Name | Effect | Scaling Stat |
|:-:|-------------|----------------|------|--------|:------------:|
| C89 | Slow (Wind) | Flora | Overgrow | Slow consumed, Root applied with extended duration (slowed target can't escape the vines) | Vitality |

**Light Setup → Dark Trigger (1):**

| # | Setup Status | Trigger Element | Name | Effect | Scaling Stat |
|:-:|-------------|----------------|------|--------|:------------:|
| C90 | Reveal (Light) | Dark | Umbra | All resistances stripped by Reveal permanently consumed — target cannot regain those resistances for the rest of the fight. Moderate burst. No heal. | Mind |

Eclipse heals the world. Umbra marks it. Eclipse says "we can be whole." Umbra says "you were seen, and now you can never hide again." Both consume Reveal. Eclipse adds; Umbra subtracts. Neither is wrong.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Umbra is Eclipse's shadow — the Sovereign's capstone. A Sovereign who triggers Umbra on a boss permanently strips its resistances, making the fight easier for everyone in the room. The most selfish alignment produces the most generous result. The Sovereign must equip a Light weapon slot to access Umbra — the game's most selfish path requires its opposite element. This mirrors the Eclipse alignment gate in §33.5.]`

##### §10.5c Zone-Paced Collision Discovery (v12.32 CANONICAL)

All 90 collision pairs remain available as before. The v12.32 addition is **pedagogical structure**: each zone becomes the canonical teacher of a specific cluster of collision pairs, and the Codex (§35.2) reflects this zone-based curriculum openly. A player visiting Frozen Shore knows they are in "Frost territory" and can expect to encounter Frost-involved collisions during play; the Codex shows *which* Frost collisions are the zone's signature curriculum so players can track their discovery progress per zone.

**This is content-design structure, not a runtime mechanic.** No gating, no hidden conditions, no locked entries. Every collision still fires whenever its setup+trigger combination is produced. The v12.32 change is how the Codex *presents* the 90 pairs — zone-clustered rather than flat — and how players understand where each pair is most naturally discovered.

**Why zones rather than tiers.** The original plan framing grouped collisions into hidden-difficulty "discovery tiers" (Tier 1: obvious, Tier 2: cryptic conditions, Tier 3: unknown conditions). That framing would have required players to guess or stumble into Tier 2/Tier 3 pairs, creating invisible progression. Zone-paced discovery aligns pedagogy with the game's existing spatial structure — zones are already meaningful, players already understand them, and tying collisions to zones makes the Codex a *map* rather than a checklist.

**Signature zone assignment.** Each collision is tagged with one or more `signature_zones` in `collision-config.json`. A collision's signature zones are the zones where it naturally arises because the zone's primary or secondary element appears in the collision. Zone mapping (§14.2):

| Zone | Primary Element | Secondary Element |
|------|:---------------:|:-----------------:|
| Ember Fields | Flame | Stone |
| Mistwood | Venom | Wind |
| Frozen Shore | Frost | Storm |
| Thunder Peaks | Storm | Flame |
| Deep Hollows | Stone | Venom |
| Sky Reaches | Wind | Frost |
| Tidal Caves | Water | Venom |
| Verdant Wilds | Flora | Water |
| Shadow Sanctum | Dark | Various |
| Radiant Heights | Light | Various |

**Signature rule:** A collision belongs to a zone's signature curriculum if *either* the setup element or the trigger element matches the zone's primary element (or the collision involves both of the zone's elements). Collisions with both elements in the pair mapped to different zones appear in both zones' curricula — this is expected and correct. Steam (Flame setup + Frost trigger) is a signature collision of both Ember Fields (Flame zone) and Frozen Shore (Frost zone): a player discovering Steam in Ember Fields is learning part of Ember Fields' curriculum; a player discovering it in Frozen Shore is learning part of Frozen Shore's curriculum. The Codex shows this overlap honestly.

**Example cluster — Frozen Shore.** Collisions with Frost as setup or Frost as trigger:

- *Frost setup:* Steam (→Flame), Flash Freeze (→Water), Shatter (→Venom), Hailstorm (→Storm), Permafrost (→Stone), Blizzard (→Wind), Thawed Bloom (→Flora), Dread Chill (→Dark), Diamond Dust (→Light) — 9 pairs.
- *Frost trigger:* Steam (Flame setup), Meltwater (Water setup), Frost Vein (Venom setup), Static Freeze (Storm setup), Shale (Stone setup), Frostbite (Wind setup), Brittle Thorn (Flora setup), Dread (Dark setup), Purify (Light setup) — 9 pairs (Steam appears in both lists — counted once as 9 + 8 unique = 17 unique Frost-involved pairs, but the player-facing cluster shows all 17).

Frozen Shore's signature curriculum is therefore 17 collisions. A Codex entry for Frozen Shore shows all 17 with their current discovery state (discovered ✓ / undiscovered ○), letting the player track their progress through the zone's pedagogical content.

**Capstone collisions (Eclipse, Umbra).** Eclipse and Umbra are the endgame capstones (see §10.5b detail entries). They are classified as signature collisions of **The Convergence** zone (§56) — the dual-element zone where both Dark and Light exist simultaneously. Players cannot complete Eclipse/Umbra discovery without accessing the Convergence, which is thematically appropriate — the endgame capstones live at the game's endgame zone.

**Codex display structure.**

The Codex (§35.2) presents collision entries grouped by signature zone rather than as a flat list. For each zone the player has visited, the Codex shows:

```
ZONE NAME (e.g., Frozen Shore)
  Discovered: 6 of 17
  
  ✓ Steam (Flame setup + Frost trigger)
  ✓ Meltwater (Water setup + Frost trigger)
  ✓ Flash Freeze (Frost setup + Water trigger)
  ✓ Shatter (Frost setup + Venom trigger)
  ✓ Hailstorm (Frost setup + Storm trigger)
  ✓ Permafrost (Frost setup + Stone trigger)
  ○ Blizzard (Frost setup + Wind trigger)
  ○ Thawed Bloom (Frost setup + Flora trigger)
  ○ Dread Chill (Frost setup + Dark trigger)
  ○ Diamond Dust (Frost setup + Light trigger)
  ○ Frost Vein (Venom setup + Frost trigger)
  ○ Static Freeze (Storm setup + Frost trigger)
  ○ Shale (Stone setup + Frost trigger)
  ○ Frostbite (Wind setup + Frost trigger)
  ○ Brittle Thorn (Flora setup + Frost trigger)
  ○ Dread (Dark setup + Frost trigger)
  ○ Purify (Light setup + Frost trigger)
```

The zone headings show progress fraction (6 of 17). Discovered entries expand on tap to show the full collision detail (mechanical effect, scaling stat, Codex hint); undiscovered entries show only the pair name and elements (no effect description). This preserves discovery surprise for undiscovered pairs while giving players a clear *curriculum roadmap* — "I haven't found Blizzard yet; it's a Frost+Wind collision that Frozen Shore or Sky Reaches can produce."

**Cross-zone discovery counts.** A collision listed in multiple zone curricula (e.g., Steam in both Ember Fields and Frozen Shore) counts toward discovery progress in *both* zones once discovered. A player who finds Steam in Ember Fields sees their Ember Fields discovery count increase AND their Frozen Shore discovery count increase — both zones taught this collision, both get credit. This is the same mechanical discovery (the player's `discoveredCollisions` Set gains `'steam'`), just with multi-zone credit in the display.

**Discovered-elsewhere visibility.** When a player opens Frozen Shore's Codex tab and sees Steam marked as ✓, the display notes the zone of first discovery if it wasn't Frozen Shore: *"Steam ✓ (first discovered in Ember Fields)"*. This gives players narrative context for their own discovery journey and lets Codex browsing serve as a record of where they've been.

**Flat-list toggle (Accessibility).** The zone-clustered view is the default. An Accessibility toggle under Settings → Encyclopedia lets players switch to a flat list sorted by collision ID (C01-C90) for completionist browsing, reference lookup, or players who prefer the pre-v12.32 presentation. Both views access the same underlying discovery data; only the presentation layer differs.

**Content authoring work.** Classifying all 90 collision pairs into signature zones is a one-time content pass. The classification lives in `collision-config.json` as a new `signature_zones` field per collision entry. No new tuning constants, no balance changes, no runtime behavior changes. The v12.32.1 content ship will deliver the full classification table; the v12.32 ship specifies the infrastructure and the classification rule.

**Not tracking Codex completion.** There is no "Master of Collisions" achievement for discovering all 90 pairs, and the zone discovery counts do not feed any cosmetic reward or title. Discovery is its own reward — the map unlocks as the player plays, the curriculum reveals itself, and the satisfaction is in the learning. Gamifying the completion count with an achievement would convert pedagogy into a checklist, which is exactly what zone-paced discovery is designed to avoid.

**Creative direction:** see `creative-reference.md` §8 for the "Codex as map, not checklist" framing, the undiscovered-entry teaser register, and the zone heading typography.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.32): The pre-v12.32 Codex was a flat list of 90 discoverable entries — a checklist that revealed itself gradually through play but offered no pedagogical structure. Players who stumbled into Steam learned Steam; players who didn't stumble into it never found out that Steam even existed. This is discovery as accident, not discovery as curriculum. Zone-paced discovery changes the Codex from "list of things you've found" to "map of what this zone teaches." A player visiting Frozen Shore sees the zone's 17-collision curriculum laid out openly — including the ones they haven't discovered yet — and can now play with intention. "I want to find Blizzard; I need to hit a Frozen target with Wind; let me try that on Frost monsters next session." The Codex becomes a *tool for directed learning* rather than a passive receipt of accidents. This matters because the collision system is the game's signature combat depth mechanic, and players who don't understand the depth don't engage with it. Zone-paced discovery doesn't add mechanics; it makes the mechanics that already exist legible. The 90 pairs haven't changed. What's changed is that the game now tells you what it's trying to teach, zone by zone. The pedagogy lives in the structure. Content authoring becomes classification rather than hidden-condition writing — the original plan proposed 40 hidden trigger conditions and the revision eliminates that work entirely. The 10 zones each have 8-17 signature collisions. Over the course of normal play through the game's content, a player naturally discovers their zone curriculum without guessing. The capstone collisions (Eclipse, Umbra) remain gated at the Convergence because they should be — endgame capstones belong at endgame zones. Everything else is discoverable through play, with the Codex serving as the map that makes the play legible.]`

#### §10.6 Collision Damage Formula (CANONICAL)

`collision_damage = COLLISION_BASE[collision_id] + scaling_stat × COLLISION_COEFF[collision_id]`

Then multiplied by:
- Combo burst bonus if combo count ≥ 1 (§5.9, ×1.15 at defaults)
- Resonance bonus if triggered during resonance window (§5.7, ×1.10–1.30)
- Elemental Mastery collision mult (§2.2)
- Volatile bonus if directional match — strong triggers weak (§4.7, ×1.30)
- Armor attunement offense if matching (×1.20)
- Effectiveness vs target element (§10.2)

Each collision's BASE and COEFF stored in collision-config.json.

**Damage tier guidelines:**

| Collision Category | Base Range | Coeff Range | Examples |
|-------------------|:----------:|:-----------:|---------|
| Pure burst (single target) | 60–100 | 1.0–1.6 | Shatter, Divine Strike, Overcharge |
| AOE burst | 30–50 | 0.5–0.8 | Hailstorm, Blizzard, Seismic Pulse |
| DoT/cloud | 20–35 | 0.3–0.6 | Toxic Fumes, Magma, Drown |
| Utility (heal, CC, debuff) | 20–40 | 0.3–0.8 | Dilute, Flash Freeze, Petrify |
| Spread/application | 15–30 | 0.2–0.5 | Firestorm, Monsoon, Miasma, Scatter Seed |
| Sustain/heal (Flora) | 25–45 | 0.4–0.7 | Bloom, Sanctuary, Purifying Bloom, Thaw Bloom |
| Reactive DoT (Flora) | 20–35 | 0.3–0.6 | Scorched Earth, Blight Garden, Decompose |
| Flora CC (root/petrify) | 20–40 | 0.3–0.8 | Overgrowth, Petrified Wood |
| Reverse burst (setup reacts) | 40–70 | 0.6–1.0 | Ignition, Scald, Toxic Shock, Tremor |
| Reverse control (setup shapes) | 20–40 | 0.3–0.6 | Meltwater, Updraft, Cyclone, Frostbite |
| Reverse utility (setup transforms) | 15–35 | 0.2–0.5 | Forge, Dissolve, Downpour, Overgrow |
| Dark corruption (palette→Dark) | 30–60 | 0.5–0.9 | Shadowfire, Nightshade, Blackout, Crumble |
| Light redemption (palette→Light) | 20–45 | 0.3–0.7 | Sacred Flame, Holy Water, Antidote, Zephyr |
| Light judgment (burst variant) | 70–100 | 1.0–1.4 | Judgment |
| Capstone reverse (Umbra) | 80 | 1.2 | Umbra |
| Capstone (Eclipse) | 120 | 1.8 | Eclipse (VIT 60%/Mind 40% split) |

Collision damage is further multiplied by Combo burst bonus (§5.9), Resonance bonus (§5.7), Elemental Mastery (§2.2), Volatile bonus if directional match (§4.7), armor attunement offense (§4.3), and effectiveness (§10.2). These multipliers stack multiplicatively — a count 1+ swipe with peak resonance timing in the volatile direction on a Fusion weapon with matching armor attunement and EM 400 can produce collision damage at roughly 3.7× the base formula output.

#### §10.7 Cosmetic Ground Marks (v12.26 CANONICAL)

Collisions leave cosmetic ground marks. **Client-side only. No gameplay effect.** Intensity scales with collision damage. Lifetime is tiered by collision significance — casual collisions produce marks that fade quickly to keep zones breathing; volatile collisions persist as medium-term memory; capstone collisions persist as server-scale history.

| Element | Ground Mark |
|---------|-------------|
| Flame | Dark char with ember glow |
| Frost | Blue-white crystalline patches |
| Water | Wet sheen that evaporates |
| Venom | Green-black marks with bubble |
| Storm | Branching lightning scars |
| Stone | Radiating fracture lines |
| Wind | Circular dust disturbance |
| Dark | Shadow pools shrinking inward |
| Light | Golden glow dimming softly |

**Tier A — Casual marks (standard-palette collisions and most stable collisions).** Fade over GROUND_MARK_LIFETIME_CASUAL (10 minutes). Full opacity for the first 5 minutes, gradual fade over the next 5, invisible afterward. A fresh-window pulse (subtle glow oscillation at ~1Hz) is visible in the first GROUND_MARK_FRESH_WINDOW (2 minutes) signaling "recent presence." Covers the vast majority of collisions produced in normal play.

**Tier B — Volatile marks (all volatile collision pairs, including the 8 directional volatile collisions and the Dark/Light mutual volatiles except Eclipse and Umbra).** Persist GROUND_MARK_LIFETIME_VOLATILE (60 minutes) with slower decay. The crackle visual signature that marks volatile collisions (faint electrical arcing at the mark's edges) is preserved throughout the fade, so a volatile mark remains visually distinct from a casual mark throughout its lifetime. Volatile marks represent medium-term zone memory — a player entering a zone 30 minutes after a significant combat event still sees residue of it.

**Tier C — Capstone marks (Eclipse and Umbra only).** Persist GROUND_MARK_LIFETIME_CAPSTONE (6 hours) with extremely slow decay. Eclipse and Umbra are the capstone cross-alignment collisions — rare enough that a capstone mark on the ground is genuinely a monument to a memorable moment. A player stumbling on an Eclipse mark hours after the fact sees the residue of something significant that happened in that location. Capstone marks have their own distinct visual (Eclipse: dimmed golden ring with fading shadow interior; Umbra: dark pool with faint golden rim) that persists at reduced but visible intensity throughout the lifetime.

**Tuning (§20.X additions):**

| Variable | Default | Min | Max |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|
| GROUND_MARK_LIFETIME_CASUAL | 10 min | 5 min | 30 min |
| GROUND_MARK_LIFETIME_VOLATILE | 60 min | 30 min | 120 min |
| GROUND_MARK_LIFETIME_CAPSTONE | 6 hr | 3 hr | 12 hr |
| GROUND_MARK_FRESH_WINDOW | 2 min | 1 min | 5 min |

**Deprecated constants (no longer used):** GROUND_MARK_LIFETIME (uniform lifetime superseded by tiered), GROUND_MARK_ECLIPSE_LIFETIME (subsumed into GROUND_MARK_LIFETIME_CAPSTONE).

**Creative direction:** see `creative-reference.md` §8 for the fresh/aged mark feel and the decay register per tier.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.26): Uniform ground mark lifetime treats all collisions as equally memorable, diluting the "notable history" signal. Tiered lifetimes stratify marks by rarity — casual marks are the breathing pulse of active zones, volatile marks are medium-term memory, capstone marks are server-scale history. A player walking into a zone reads recent activity (fresh casual marks), sees notable recent combat (volatile marks still visible), and occasionally encounters the residue of something rare (capstone marks that have been there for hours). The system produces layered environmental storytelling without any additional server state — all marks remain client-side rendering from collision event data.]`

#### §10.8 Multi-Actor Collision Resolution (CANONICAL)

In a 50-player room, multiple players interact with the same enemies simultaneously. The following rules resolve collision ownership, credit, and race conditions.

**Rule 1 — Status ownership transfers on refresh.** When multiple players apply statuses to the same target, the most recent applicator owns the status for collision credit purposes. If Player A applies Burn and Player C later refreshes that Burn (extending it), Player C is the new setup owner.

**Rule 2 — Collision credit goes to the trigger player.** The player whose action triggers a collision (the swipe/attack that causes two different statuses to resolve) receives: collision mana restore (§3.5), combo chain interaction (§5.9), collision XP contribution (§7), and Codex discovery credit. The setup owner receives setup XP contribution per §7's contribution model.

**Rule 3 — First-processed wins on simultaneous triggers.** If two players swipe the same enemy on the same server tick, both attempting to consume the same setup status, the server processes swipes in arrival order (ts_offset from §16.14). If genuinely simultaneous (identical ts_offset), lower player_id wins. The second swipe deals its damage and applies its element status normally, but finds no setup to collide with — no collision fires for the second player.

```
func resolve_collision_race(target, swipe_events):
    # Sort by arrival: ts_offset ascending, then player_id ascending as tiebreaker
    sorted_events = sort(swipe_events, key=(e.ts_offset, e.source_actor.id))
    
    for event in sorted_events:
        existing_status = target.get_status_different_from(event.element)
        if existing_status != null:
            # This swipe wins the collision
            resolve_collision(event.source_actor, target, existing_status, event.element)
            # Status consumed — subsequent swipes in this tick find nothing
        # Swipe damage and status application proceed normally regardless
        apply_swipe_damage(event)
        apply_swipe_status(target, event.element)
```

**Rule 4 — Cross-player collisions are valid.** Player A's Burn setup + Player B's Frost swipe = Steam. Player B triggered it. This is the standard case in party play — setup and trigger come from different players. All collision multipliers use the trigger player's stats (EM, combo count, resonance timing).

**Rule 5 — AoE collisions resolve sequentially.** Staff swipe hitting 3 targets (§4.2a) resolves collisions one target at a time, in order of distance from the caster (nearest first). Each resolution completes (including combo spread from §5.9) before the next begins. This prevents infinite cascade loops where spread from collision 1 sets up collision 2 which spreads to set up collision 3.

```
func resolve_staff_aoe_collisions(caster, targets):
    sorted_targets = sort(targets, key=distance_from(caster))
    for target in sorted_targets:
        # Full resolution per target: damage → status → collision → mana → spread
        resolve_swipe_on_target(caster, target)  # §5.2 pipeline steps 5a-5e
        # Spread from §5.9 may seed status on a NEW enemy not in the original target list
        # That new enemy is NOT added to this resolution pass — it's setup for the NEXT swipe cycle
```

**Rule 6 — Spread targets are setup-only.** Combo spread (§5.9 count 2+) seeds a status on a nearby enemy. That status is a setup — it cannot immediately trigger a collision in the same resolution pass. The spread target must be hit by a separate action (auto-attack or swipe) to trigger its collision. This prevents one swipe from cascading into unlimited collisions across a pack.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Multi-actor collision rules exist to make party play feel fair and predictable. The trigger player gets credit because they made the timing decision. The setup player gets contribution XP because they enabled it. First-processed-wins prevents double-dipping on the same setup. Sequential AoE resolution prevents cascade exploits. These rules are invisible in solo play — they only matter when 2+ players are fighting the same target, which is the majority of gameplay in a 50-player room.]`

### §11. Threat and Aggro

Monsters maintain a per-target threat table that determines who they attack in party play. Threat accumulates from a small set of weighted actions, decays with distance, and drives target switching above a threshold. The Tank's role identity (§24) is built on this table — block-threat is what converts defensive play into aggro generation. See also §20.4a for canonical tuning values.

**Threat sources:**

| Action | Threat |
|--------|:------:|
| Damage dealt | 1.0× |
| Healing | 0.5× |
| Status applied | 15 flat |
| Damage blocked | BLOCK_THREAT_MULT(2.0)× blocked amount |
| Proximity (melee) | 2/sec |
| Taunt (active ability) | current_highest × 1.3 for 4.0s |

**Taunt:** An active ability available to all players (not weapon-gated). Cooldown: TAUNT_CD(12s). Range: TAUNT_RANGE(15u). Sets the user's threat to 1.3× the current highest on the target mob for TAUNT_DURATION(4.0s). During Taunt duration, all threat generated by the taunter is multiplied by TAUNT_THREAT_MULT(1.5). Taunt is available immediately on respawn (cooldown resets on death, per §5.5).

Decay: 5%/s outside THREAT_RANGE(30u). Switch threshold: 1.1× (boss: 1.2×). Death/disconnect: threat zeroed. Max entries per table: 16.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Block-threat converts the Tank's defensive identity into aggro. The more damage you absorb, the better you hold aggro. Being hit is the Tank's job, not a failure state.]`

#### §11.1 Threat Direction Arrows (v12.42 CANONICAL)

Each monster with active aggro displays a small directional arrow above its sprite, pointing toward the specific player it is currently targeting. The arrow updates in real-time as aggro shifts, giving all players a continuous peripheral read on threat direction across crowded combat scenes.

**Visual spec.**

- **Size.** `AGGRO_ARROW_SIZE`(8px) default, rendered at pixel-perfect scale regardless of camera zoom. Range 4px-16px tunable for accessibility (larger arrows for users who need stronger peripheral signal).
- **Position.** Above the monster sprite, centered on the sprite's horizontal midpoint, at a fixed vertical offset (~8px above the sprite's top edge). The arrow tracks the monster's world position — it moves with the monster.
- **Direction.** Points from the monster toward the currently-targeted player's world position. If the target moves, the arrow re-aims in real-time (updates every client tick; no interpolation delay). If the target is off-screen, the arrow still points correctly (off-screen targets produce an arrow indicating the direction the threat is aimed, which matters more than whether the target is rendered).
- **Color.** Neutral muted orange (`#D68A3C`, between Flame red and Flora green to read as threat-indicator rather than element). Semi-transparent (~70% alpha) to keep the arrow peripheral rather than attention-grabbing.
- **Shape.** Simple triangular arrow, wider at the base (monster end) and pointed at the tip (target direction). Flat shape, no shadow, no outline glow. Peripheral readability over ornamentation.

**Aggro-transition behavior.** When a monster's aggro shifts to a new target, the arrow **fades briefly during the transition** — `AGGRO_ARROW_FADE_ON_TRANSITION`(0.3s) default — then re-resolves pointing at the new target. The fade-and-re-resolve is visible to the previous-target player (they see the arrow fade as the threat leaves them) and to the new-target player (they see the arrow solidify pointing at them). This makes aggro transitions legible as *events* rather than as instant snaps.

**No aggro — no arrow.** Monsters not currently aggroed on any player show no arrow. When a monster's threat table empties (all players out of range, or all players dead), the arrow fades over 0.3s and disappears. When the monster next gains aggro, the arrow re-appears pointing at the new target.

**Scope — works in both party and overworld contexts.**

- **4-player party play.** Each player sees arrows above enemies in their party's engagement. A Tank watching three enemies sees three arrows pointing at them (aggro held); if one arrow flips toward the DPS, the Tank knows to re-taunt before damage lands. The DPS watching their friend's enemies sees arrows pointing away from themselves — until one flips toward them, at which point they see the threat coming before taking damage.
- **50-player overworld combat.** Arrows work identically in overworld contexts. A player in a busy overworld engagement sees arrows on enemies currently aggroed on any player (self or others); arrows pointing at the self-player are the critical signal. The directional information scales cleanly with combat density — more monsters = more arrows, but each arrow is directional and independent rather than requiring color-decoding across a large palette.
- **Solo play.** A solo player sees arrows pointing at themselves on every aggroed enemy. This reinforces "you are the target" in a way that scales with enemy count. A solo player fighting 5 aggroed enemies sees 5 arrows converging on their position — a visceral peripheral signal of threat concentration.

**Why directional arrows instead of color-coded outlines.** An earlier design candidate proposed per-party-member colored outlines on monster sprites (blue outline = aggroed on party member 1, red = party member 2, etc.). The v12.42 ship integrates the revised directional-arrow spec instead. The arrow approach is strictly better on three axes:

1. **Answers the question players actually ask.** Mid-combat, players ask "is that enemy coming at me?" not "which party member is that enemy assigned to?" Directional arrows answer the question directly — arrow points at you = enemy coming at you. Colored outlines require mapping "this outline color means player X, and player X is me, therefore this enemy is aggroed on me" — a multi-step decoding that adds cognitive load.
2. **Scales to overworld contexts.** A color palette with 4 party colors breaks in 50-player overworld combat where no fixed "which player is this" mapping exists. Directional arrows work identically regardless of player count — the arrow points where it points.
3. **Works for solo players.** A solo player is always the target of their enemies' aggro, so color-coded outlines would be redundant (always "my color"). Directional arrows reinforce the "you are the target" signal visibly at each enemy, which matches how solo combat actually feels.

**Accessibility.**

- **Toggleable.** Settings → Accessibility → Combat Hints → Threat Direction Arrows. Default on. Players who find the arrows visually noisy can disable them without losing any mechanical capability — aggro still functions server-side; only the arrow-rendering is toggled.
- **Colorblind-safe.** The muted orange color is chosen to be distinguishable in all major colorblind palettes (protanopia, deuteranopia, tritanopia). The arrow's shape-based directional information is independent of color — a colorblind player reads direction from the pointed triangle shape regardless of color perception.
- **Reduced motion.** Settings → Accessibility → Motion → Reduced Motion disables the 0.3s fade-on-transition animation. In reduced-motion mode, aggro transitions produce instant arrow-snap instead of fade-and-resolve. Monster death still fades the arrow (monster sprite fades with it), but the mid-combat transitions snap.

**Server-authoritative, client-rendered.** The aggro state broadcast already exists per §11's server-side threat table. The arrow is purely client-side rendering from existing broadcast data — no new server-side state, no new network messages. The client interpolates arrow direction from tick to tick based on monster and target positions, which come from the standard position-update payload. Zero architecture impact.

**Tuning.**

| Variable | Default | Min | Max | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| AGGRO_ARROW_SIZE | 8px | 4px | 16px | Arrow size at standard camera zoom |
| AGGRO_ARROW_FADE_ON_TRANSITION | 0.3s | 0.1s | 0.6s | Fade duration on aggro-transition event |

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.42): Party and overworld combat both suffer from the same legibility gap — players can't tell at a glance which enemies are about to attack them versus which are attacking someone else. The information exists server-side (the threat table knows exactly who each monster is targeting) but isn't surfaced to the client in a readable way. The original v12.25 plan draft proposed per-party-member colored outlines, which works in 4-player parties but breaks in 50-player overworld and adds cognitive load (players have to remember their own color and decode outlines against that). The directional-arrow revision answers the question players actually ask mid-combat: "is that enemy coming at me?" Arrow points at you = yes. This works identically in every context (solo, party, overworld), scales with enemy count without visual confusion, and requires zero decoding — the arrow's direction is its meaning. The fade-on-transition behavior makes aggro shifts visible as *events*, which is important for Tank-style play where re-taunting in response to aggro drift is a core mechanic. A Tank who sees an arrow flip away from them during a fight knows to re-taunt before the DPS takes damage, which turns a previously-invisible server state into actionable combat information. The muted-orange color and semi-transparent rendering keep the arrows peripheral rather than attention-grabbing — they register at a glance without competing with enemy sprites, telegraphs, or the player's own character. This is the v12.38 weapon-edge-glow discipline applied to the threat-direction channel: information placed where the player can catch it peripherally, scaling the signal by density rather than by palette. Combined with v12.38's collision-opportunity glow and v12.42's combo posture, v12.42's threat arrows complete a full peripheral-vision combat-awareness vocabulary — what's ready to attack (threat arrows), what's ready to collide (weapon edge glow), what's ready to deliver (combo posture). All three channels operate in the peripheral zone without demanding focal attention, which is how expert ARPG players actually process combat: the foveal view is on the enemy they're actively fighting, and peripheral awareness tracks the rest.]`

### §12. Learning Curve

| Tier | Level | Concept | Gear |
|:----:|:-----:|---------|------|
| 0 | 1–10 | Move, dodge, attack. Grand Slam feel. | Common weapon, no element |
| 0.5 | 5–15 | First element. Status effects appear. Effectiveness arrows. Weapon glow cycles noticed. | Elemental weapon (pure) |
