| 1 | 10–25 | First collision. Codex unlocked. "Wait, what just happened?" | Fusion weapon (two elements) |
| 1.5 | 15–30 | Lunge discovered (swipe toward locked enemy). Retreat Shot discovered (ranged disengage). Combo burst noticed — "swipe hit harder after attacking." | Fusion weapon — collisions motivate lunge-to-trigger sequences |
| 2 | 25–50 | Collision mastery. Swipe rhythm. Mana loop conscious. Combo spread discovered — "the status jumped to another enemy." | Volatile vs Stable choices |
| 2.5 | 30–50 | Resonance window visible. First timed collision. Combo investment discovered — "the next status lasted longer." Now-Wide-Next becomes conscious vocabulary. | Volatile fusions — resonance bonus stacks with Volatile and combo bonuses |
| 3 | 50–75 | Build optimization. Armor attunement. Party synergy. | Full build identity |
| 4 | 75–100 | Dark and Light. Eclipse. Endgame capstone. | Endgame elements |

**Codex:** Tracks per-player collision discovery. First collision triggers one-time tutorial tooltip. Subsequent discoveries show standard entry.

#### §12.1 Surfaced Mastery Level (v12.33 CANONICAL)

The learning curve tiers above are surfaced to the player as a **Mastery Level** indicator, matching the tier numbers (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4) verbatim. The Mastery Level is the player-facing representation of the learning curve — a visible progression indicator that updates as the player demonstrates the mechanics each tier introduces. It is pedagogical infrastructure, not a reward system: there is no gold, gear, stat, or unlock tied to Mastery Level. The Level is information, not currency.

**This IS the onboarding.** There is no forced tutorial, no mandatory tooltip walkthrough, no "press X to learn about collisions" dialog. A new player starts at Mastery Level 0 and advances as they naturally encounter and demonstrate each tier's mechanics. The game does not explain concepts before the player experiences them; it acknowledges the experience when it happens and offers deeper learning on opt-in.

**Tier advancement triggers.** Mastery Level advances when the player demonstrates the *defining action* of the next tier. The specific trigger per tier:

| Tier | Advancement Trigger |
|:----:|--------------------|
| 0 | Starting level — no trigger |
| 0.5 | First elemental status applied to any enemy (Burn, Freeze, Shock, etc.) |
| 1 | First collision triggered (any of the 90 pairs) |
| 1.5 | First Lunge OR first Retreat Shot executed |
| 2 | First Combo Spread OR first Volatile collision triggered |
| 2.5 | First Resonance-timed collision hit within the §6.4 resonance window |
| 3 | First damage taken while wearing armor with matching elemental attunement to the incoming damage's element (demonstrates the player has a build that uses attunement) |
| 4 | First Eclipse triggered (Hero/Arbiter) OR first Umbra triggered (Sovereign) OR first Apex boss defeated |

Tier advancement is one-directional — once a Mastery Level is reached, it does not regress. Advancement is per-character, not per-account; a new character starts at Mastery Level 0 regardless of the account's other characters' progress.

**Tier-crossing notification.** When the player advances to a new Mastery Level, a small non-blocking notification appears in the upper-left of the screen for 4 seconds. The notification shows the tier reached and an opt-in hint offering a specific next thing to try. Example wording:

> *Mastery Level 1 reached.*
> *You triggered your first collision. The Codex now shows what each zone teaches. Try: open the Codex from the Inventory menu.*

The notification is dismissible by tap. The hint is skippable — the notification disappears after 4 seconds regardless of whether the player reads it. **The notification never interrupts combat.** If the tier-crossing trigger fires during active combat (combat flag set), the notification queues and displays when combat ends.

**Notification register.** See `creative-reference.md` §8.49 for the creative direction — respectful, brief, informational rather than celebratory; the tone is an acknowledgment of what the player just did, not a reward ceremony around it. No fanfare audio, no screen-dim, no "achievement unlocked" presentation.

**Hint opt-in, never mandatory.** The notification text ends with a specific suggested next action ("try: swipe toward a locked target with a melee weapon"). The suggestion is one sentence, phrased as a concrete try — not a paragraph of instruction. Players who want to follow it have a clear next step; players who don't can ignore it and continue playing as they were. No "tutorial mode" is entered, no game state changes, no attention-grabbing visual pulls the player toward the suggested action.

**Mastery Level display.** The player's current Mastery Level is visible in two places: (1) the Inspect Card (shown when other players tap the player's character in town or the Friends list), rendered as *"Adept · Mastery 2.5"* or similar (see §12.2 for certification count); (2) the Inventory menu's character-info panel, shown alongside level, class, and guild affiliation. The Mastery Level is not shown during active combat — the HUD stays clean.

**Tier name mapping.** Each Mastery Level has a canonical name used in the Inspect Card display. The names are descriptive of the pedagogical stage rather than comparative to other players.

| Tier | Name |
|:----:|------|
| 0 | Newcomer |
| 0.5 | Initiate |
| 1 | Apprentice |
| 1.5 | Student |
| 2 | Skilled |
| 2.5 | Adept |
| 3 | Expert |
| 4 | Master |

These names are intentionally different from the guild rank names (Novice/Apprentice/Journeyman/Adept/Expert/Master/Grandmaster/Legendary/Transcendent, per §38). Where guild ranks are earned through skill progression and gate skill-specific content, Mastery tier names describe combat-system pedagogical stages and gate nothing. The overlap of "Apprentice," "Adept," "Expert," and "Master" as words between the two systems is unavoidable for common English vocabulary but does not imply equivalence — a player could be Mastery Adept without being any guild's Adept rank, or vice versa. The Inspect Card shows both when relevant (guild rank + Mastery tier are separate lines).

**Accessibility — notification off-switch.** Players can disable Mastery Level notifications entirely via Settings → Accessibility → Notifications → Mastery Level. When disabled, tier crossings still register internally (the Level advances, certifications earn as normal), but no notification appears. Players who find the notifications intrusive, or who prefer to experience the game without structured pedagogical framing, can remove the notification layer without losing any gameplay content. The Mastery Level itself remains visible on the Inspect Card and Inventory panel for players who toggle this off and later want to check their progression.

**Data model.** The player's current Mastery Level and per-tier advancement timestamps are stored in the player's character state, server-authoritative (to prevent client-side faking of Level for social signaling on the Inspect Card). The `mastery_level` field is a decimal (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4); the `mastery_level_timestamps` field is a map of `{tier: server_timestamp}` recording when each tier was first reached. See architecture §9.5.10 for the character-state schema extension.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.33): The Mastery Level surfaces what the learning curve tier system already described internally. The tier table in §12 has existed as a design reference since early versions, describing the conceptual progression a player moves through as they engage with the game's systems. v12.33 makes that progression visible to the player — not as a reward or progression mechanic, but as informational acknowledgment. "You just triggered your first collision. Here's what that means and where to explore further if you want." This is experience-first pedagogy in its clearest form: the game notices what the player has done and offers context for it. The alternative — a traditional tutorial or tutorial-laden onboarding — would force concepts on players before the concepts became meaningful. The Mastery Level ties learning to demonstrated experience instead. A player who has triggered their first collision has already *had* the experience of a collision firing; the notification is telling them "that thing you just did has a name and there's more of it to explore." The opt-in hint respects player agency — it's a suggestion, not a directive. Players who want the scaffolding get it; players who prefer pure discovery don't have it imposed. The notification-off Accessibility toggle ensures even the light presentation isn't mandatory. This is the structural complement to v12.32 Zone-Paced Collision Discovery: v12.32 made the collision system's depth legible in space (zones are curricula); v12.33 makes the game's mechanical progression legible in time (Mastery Level acknowledges what the player has just demonstrated). Together, they form a pedagogical infrastructure that teaches through structure rather than through tutorials.]`

#### §12.2 Skill Certifications (v12.33 CANONICAL)

Layered on the Mastery Level ladder are **Skill Certifications** — discrete badges marking specific demonstrated actions. Where the Mastery Level shows the *continuous progression* through the learning curve, certifications mark *specific earned skills* within that progression. Together they produce the Duolingo/RPG hybrid that matches how players actually experience progression: continuous ladder climb + discrete accomplishments within the climb.

**Certification list.** There are 14 certifications in v12.33, distributed across the Mastery tiers. Each certification is tied to a single demonstrated action (one trigger event, unambiguous). Earning a certification is automatic on the trigger; there is no application, no claim button, no prerequisites beyond performing the demonstrated action.

| Cert ID | Name | Tier | Trigger |
|---------|------|:----:|---------|
| `first-grand-slam` | First Grand Slam | 0 | Defeat a monster via swipe (not ranged shot) while the monster has ≤ GRAND_SLAM_HP_THRESHOLD HP remaining, per §2.5 |
| `first-status` | First Status Applied | 0.5 | Apply any elemental status (Burn, Freeze, Shock, Wet, Poison, etc.) to any enemy for the first time |
| `first-collision` | First Collision | 1 | Trigger any of the 90 collision pairs for the first time (same trigger as Mastery Level 1 advancement) |
| `first-lunge` | First Lunge | 1.5 | Execute a Lunge (swipe toward a locked-on enemy with a melee weapon) per §5.3b |
| `first-retreat-shot` | First Retreat Shot | 1.5 | Execute a Retreat Shot (ranged disengage) per §5.3b |
| `first-combo-burst` | First Combo Burst | 1.5 | Trigger a combo burst — chain damage multiplier kicks in when combo count ≥ 1, per §5.9 |
| `first-perfect-block` | First Perfect Block | 1.5 | Execute a Perfect Block (tap-to-block within the PERFECT_BLOCK_WINDOW) per §5.8.7 |
| `first-combo-spread` | First Combo Spread | 2 | Chain-spread a status to a nearby enemy via combo count, per §5.9.3 |
| `first-volatile` | First Volatile Collision | 2 | Trigger a volatile collision (one of the 10 directional-strong volatile pairs with +30% bonus) per §10.3 |
| `first-resonance-hit` | First Resonance-Timed Hit | 2.5 | Trigger a collision whose timing falls within the §6.4 resonance window, earning the resonance bonus |
| `first-combo-chain-3` | First Combo Chain to 3 | 2.5 | Reach combo count 3 within a single status duration, per §5.9 |
| `first-attuned-defense` | First Attuned Defense | 3 | Take damage while wearing armor with elemental attunement matching the incoming damage's element (demonstrates resistance from attunement) |
| `first-apex-defeat` | First Apex Defeat | 4 | Defeat any Apex boss for the first time (same trigger as one of three Mastery Level 4 advancement paths) |
| `first-capstone` | First Endgame Capstone | 4 | Trigger Eclipse (Hero/Arbiter) or Umbra (Sovereign) for the first time, per §56 |

**Earning a certification.** When the player first performs the certified action, a brief notification fires similar to the Mastery Level notification: upper-left, 4 seconds, dismissible. Certification notifications are smaller and quieter than tier-crossing notifications — the tier crossing is the bigger event; the certification is a smaller mark within it. Example:

> *Skill Certification: First Volatile Collision.*
> *You triggered a collision with a volatile bonus.*

Certification notifications do not include opt-in hints (the player just demonstrated the skill; they don't need a hint for what they already did). They are acknowledgment only — a small salute to the accomplishment.

**Certification list display.** Tapping the Mastery Level on the Inspect Card or Inventory panel opens the certification list for the player's current tier and earlier tiers. Earned certifications show with a filled badge, the earning timestamp, and a short "context line" describing the circumstance (e.g., *"Earned 47 days ago — triggered Steam in Ember Fields"*). Unearned certifications within the current tier show with an empty badge, the certification name, and a brief opt-in hint describing what earns it. Unearned certifications in *later* tiers are not shown (preserves tier-advancement surprise).

**The opt-in hint register.** The unearned-certification hint is the same "respectful, brief, informational" register as the Mastery Level notification hints. Example:

> *First Resonance-Timed Hit (not yet earned).*
> *Trigger a collision within the resonance window. The window briefly opens after a status is applied.*

Hints are one short sentence, never instructional paragraphs. Players who want the specifics can tap through to §6.4 Resonance in the Encyclopedia (if available); players who want only the hint get it in-place.

**Certification persistence.** Certifications are permanent on the character. Unlike Mastery Level which is single-valued, certifications are an append-only set — once earned, they are never lost. Certifications do not transfer between characters; each character earns their own.

**Certification count display.** The Inspect Card summarizes certifications compactly: *"Adept · Mastery 2.5 · 9 certifications"*. The certifications are counted across all earned tiers. This is the identity-level summary other players see; tapping the summary opens the full list per above.

**What certifications are NOT.**
- Not unlocks — no gear, no areas, no abilities are gated by certifications.
- Not achievements with rewards — no gold, no cosmetics, no titles.
- Not progression — the player's character level, stats, and build are unchanged by certification earning.
- Not competitive — there is no "fastest to all 14" leaderboard, and the earning order does not signal player skill relative to other players.
- Not visible during combat — the HUD stays clean.
- Not reversible — once earned, certifications are permanent.

**Data model.** The player's earned certifications are stored as a `certifications_earned` set of `{cert_id, timestamp, context_string}` entries on character state. Set membership is the earning indicator; the `timestamp` and `context_string` support the Inspect Card display. See architecture §9.5.10 for the schema extension.

**Content authoring scope.** Each of the 14 certifications is defined by a single trigger event. The trigger logic is evaluated server-side on the canonical event resolution pipeline (combat resolution for collision triggers, gear-state resolution for attuned-defense, etc.) — no new anti-cheat surface, no new validation pipeline. The `context_string` generation at earn-time is similarly automatic (derived from the trigger event's existing metadata: zone, collision name, monster defeated, etc.). The v12.33 ship specifies the infrastructure and the 14 certification definitions; no content authoring follow-up required.

**Future certifications.** The v12.33 ship hard-codes 14 certifications corresponding to the existing learning curve mechanics. Future ships may add certifications for new mechanics introduced post-v12.33 (e.g., if a future ship introduces a new combat system, a certification for "first use of the new system" would join the list). The certification list is therefore not a v12.33-final artifact — it's the v12.33 starting set that grows as the game grows. A "certifications-added-in-vNN" section in the v12.33 changelog entry documents the additive growth pattern.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.33): The mastery ladder shows the journey; certifications mark specific earned skills within that journey. This is the Duolingo/RPG hybrid — continuous progression plus discrete accomplishments. The 14 certifications correspond to single demonstrated actions (one trigger event each), which keeps the design lightweight and content-authoring work minimal. Each certification is an unambiguous hook on a canonical event path; no new anti-cheat surface, no new gameplay logic, just a small note when the hook fires. The "what certifications are NOT" list is load-bearing: certifications deliberately do not unlock, reward, rank, or gate anything. They are pedagogical acknowledgment, not progression mechanic. If certifications unlocked content, players would optimize the earning order, which would distort the pedagogy the ladder is trying to enable. By keeping them purely informational, the ladder's "demonstration earns acknowledgment" philosophy is preserved. The Inspect Card summary ("Adept · Mastery 2.5 · 9 certifications") gives other players a quick read on a character's mechanical familiarity without making it a ranking surface — a new player sees "Adept" and understands this character has gotten past tier 2, not "this player is ranked higher than me." The design goal is recognition, not comparison.]`

### §13. Resistance and Immunity

| Tier | Status Application | Collision Damage |
|------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Neutral | Normal | Normal |
| Resistant | Duration −40% | Damage −30% |
| Immune | Blocked (no application) | Element-specific −80% |

Immunity blocks APPLICATION, not TRIGGERING. Pre-existing statuses can still be consumed by collision even if target later gained immunity. Max 1 immune element per enemy.

**Resistance feedback (v12.26).** Damage numbers communicate resistance tier visually and audibly. Neutral hits render standard damage numbers with the standard impact sound. Resistant hits render ~15% smaller damage numbers with a subtle grey tint, accompanied by a muffled (low-pass filtered, quieter) version of the standard impact sound. Immune hits render damage numbers with a diagonal strikethrough shape and produce a distinctly flat, low-frequency "dull thunk" sound. The feedback is symmetric — the player's own damage-taken numbers show resistance tier when monsters hit them, so the player learns enemy resistance patterns through their own damage too. Visual and audio specs in `creative-reference.md` §1.4 UI and §2 Audio respectively. Shape-based visual + audio gives resistance tier information through two accessibility-independent channels.

---

## PART III — WORLD AND MULTIPLAYER (§14–§19)

### §14. World Design

#### §14.1 Hub and Spoke

Town center. Themed spokes radiate outward. Any spoke accessible from town. Internal progression per spoke follows eight standard depth tiers (each covering a 10-level range), plus two endgame tiers:

```
TOWN ──→ [Depth 1] ──dungeon──→ [Depth 2] ──dungeon──→ ... ──dungeon──→ [Depth 8] ──→ [Darkness] ──→ [Light]
```

Each depth tier covers 10 combat levels. Monsters are zone-leveled (§6.7, §6.8). Depth transitions gated by dungeon clears.

| Depth | Levels | Monster Size | Zone Type |
|:-----:|:------:|:------------:|-----------|
| 1 | 1–10 | 16×16 | Standard — tutorial difficulty, Tier 1 nodes |
| 2 | 11–20 | 32×32 | Standard — harder monsters, Tier 2 nodes, first farming plots |
| 3 | 21–30 | 48×48 | Standard — difficulty spike, Tier 3 nodes, monsters apply statuses |
| 4 | 31–40 | 64×64 | Standard — monsters match player size |
| 5 | 41–50 | 80×80 | Standard — monsters surpass player size |
| 6 | 51–60 | 96×96 | Standard — enhanced monster AI |
| 7 | 61–70 | 112×112 | Standard — Tier 4 resources |
| 8 | 71–80 | 176×176 | Standard — Tier 5 legendary resources, Shift weapon blueprint (v12.40 endgame-scale) |
| 9 | 81–90 | 176×176 | Darkness — endgame, dark element |
| 10 | 91–100 | 176×176 | Light — endgame, light element |

Total: 64 standard elemental zones (8 elements × 8 depths) + 2 endgame zones = 66 zones.

#### §14.2 Zones

| Spoke | Theme | Primary Element | Secondary |
|-------|-------|:---------------:|:---------:|
| Starting Meadow | Safe | None | None |
| Ember Fields | Volcanic | Flame | Stone |
| Mistwood | Swamp forest | Venom | Wind |
| Frozen Shore | Coastal ice | Frost | Storm |
| Thunder Peaks | Mountain storms | Storm | Flame |
| Deep Hollows | Underground | Stone | Venom |
| Sky Reaches | High altitude | Wind | Frost |
| Tidal Caves | Coastal depths | Water | Venom |
| Verdant Wilds | Overgrown jungle | Flora | Water |
| Shadow Sanctum | Dark | Dark | Various |
| Radiant Heights | Luminous | Light | Various |
| The Convergence | Duality | Dark + Light | Both coexist |

50 players per overworld section. 4-player dungeon instances.

**Terrain affects feel (cosmetic):** Sand slows slightly. Ice adds slide to deceleration. Swamp adds weight. Stone paths feel crisp. Subtle, not punishing.

#### §14.3 Town

The town is the game's social hub. All core services are within 10 seconds of town center. Services include: Marketplace (global async), Vendor, Enchanting Station, Cooking Station, Farm access (which now includes home storage per §19.2 v13 REPLACEMENT), Party Formation point, and the town-specific locations described below.

**v13 changes to town services:**
- Bank removed as a separate building (§19.2 v13 REPLACEMENT) — storage is integrated into personal farm/home.
- Vows Shrine removed as a separate building (§14.3.1 v13 REPLACEMENT) — stat locking distributed across NPCs per §1.5.1.

##### §14.3.1 Distributed Locking Geography (v13 REPLACEMENT of Vows Shrine)

The Vows Shrine is removed from town. Its function (stat locking and the Recent Vows social surface) is distributed across the stat-associated NPCs per §1.5.1. Each locking NPC maintains their own small Recent Vows display at their location — the Blacksmith's forge shows recent Power/Ferocity vows, the Ferryman's post shows recent Mind vows, and so on.

**Town reference.** Players seeking to lock a stat navigate to the appropriate NPC rather than a single building. The town map surfaces this via NPC iconography (each locking NPC is marked with a small vow-glyph when the player hovers on desktop per §34.1, or via a tap on mobile). No central Shrine building exists.

**Social surface preservation.** The ambient-lore function of the former Recent Vows display is preserved through the per-NPC displays — players walking past the forge, the Ferryman, the Tavern Keeper, etc., can read each location's Recent Vows in passing. The aggregate server-wide lock activity is visible through distributed exposure across the town's NPCs rather than centralized at one building.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): See §1.5.1's design intent. The town becomes a geography of commitment decisions rather than a menu of them. The single Shrine building is removed; its function distributes across the town's existing NPC locations, each of which already has thematic connection to the stat they now also witness.]`

##### §14.3.2 Clan Halls (v12.25 CANONICAL, v13 ADDITIVE)

Clan Halls are shared interior spaces accessible from a portal in town (§29.1). Each clan's hall is a separate instance — members of the clan enter their own hall; non-members see the portal but cannot enter another clan's hall. Visitors to a town can see the Clan Hall portal but their access is determined by their clan membership. Hall content (displayed accumulated achievements from members, donated crafted items, the clan message board) is persistent per clan and grows over time. Full Clan Hall specification: §29.1.

**v13 ADDITIVE — posted traditions calendar.** Clan Halls gain a calendar widget where clan members can post recurring traditions (weekly fishing tournaments, monthly honor ceremonies, specific named events). Traditions display as a clan-authored schedule visible to all hall members. Per §0.1 tonal rule, tradition names are unconstrained — "The Tuesday Fishing Incident (Never Again)" is as valid as "Monthly Honor Tournament." Inside-joke culture is supported explicitly.

**v13 ADDITIVE — dissolved clan memorials.** Clans dissolved for 1+ year gain an optional player-authored epitaph (under 200 chars) submitted by former founding members. Epitaphs become permanent server lore. NPCs may reference dissolved clans in gossip (§23.9). Old hall locations (preserved via canonical archive per §29.1) become memorial sites if not re-founded within the one-year window. The canonical archive-for-revival mechanic is preserved for clans dissolved less than 1 year.

##### §14.3.3 Time-of-Day Variation (v12.26 CANONICAL, v13 ADDITIVE)

The town visually and audibly shifts based on server time. Four tonal phases cycle across a 24-hour server day. **No gameplay effects.** All mechanics (NPC availability, services, trade, combat) are identical at all times. Only ambient atmosphere changes.

**Phases:**

- **Morning (server 06:00–11:00):** Warm golden light cast at a shallow angle. Birdsong ambient audio, light rustling. Fewer ambient NPC chatter lines. The mood is quiet, just-waking.
- **Afternoon (server 11:00–17:00):** Full daylight, neutral color temperature. Standard town ambient at full density — vendor calls, cart rattles, marketplace bustle. The mood is busy, active.
- **Evening (server 17:00–21:00):** Warm amber light, golden-hour quality. Expanded ambient NPC chatter density. Gentle breeze audio layer. The mood is convivial, social.
- **Late Night (server 21:00–06:00):** Dim cool lighting. Flickering torches replace some of the daylight lighting. Ambient density thins significantly — fewer NPC voices, more wind, occasional distant owl. The mood is hushed, contemplative.

Transitions between phases are gradual (~20-minute crossfade) so the change is felt rather than announced.

**Server time, not local device time.** All players on the server experience the same time-of-day simultaneously — "late night" is the same late night for everyone. This creates shared temporal memory: *"I was there at 3 AM when..."* becomes a meaningful social reference.

**NPC line variation.** Each NPC has ~2-3 phase-exclusive lines per tonal phase. Core dialogue is identical at all times; only the phase-flavored lines shift.

**v13 ADDITIVE — NPC daily schedules.** Key NPCs (Blacksmith, Enchanter, Tavern Keeper, Mayor, Ferryman, Herbalist) gain actual daily routines — the Blacksmith opens the forge in morning and closes at late night, the Tavern fills most heavily in evening, the Ferryman's walks happen at dawn and dusk. NPCs are interactable at all hours but their visible activity state varies. Time-specific events (Ferryman's dawn walk with a signature line, Tavern Keeper's late-night story) rotate across server days so every timezone can encounter them over time.

**v13 ADDITIVE — NPC recognition across playtime.** Town NPCs recognize players more specifically over accumulated playtime:
- 50+ hours: polite recognition by name. *"Alice, hello."*
- 200+ hours: familiar/exasperated. *"Alice. Again. What now."*
- 500+ hours: warm mockery. *"There she is. Town's favorite disaster. How many did you kill today?"*

Per §0.1 tonal rule, each tier's dialogue includes both grave and wry variants authored per NPC. A Blacksmith at 500h may be grumpily warm ("You again. Good, I need someone to blame when this fails"); the Enchanter at 200h may be dryly observational ("I was starting to wonder if you'd stopped enchanting. Or died").

**Creative direction:** see `creative-reference.md` §1.2a Zone Mood Guides for the per-phase lighting and audio specification.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.26): A town that looks identical at all times is a game environment; a town that shifts with the server day is a place. Players playing at different times experience different tonal atmospheres organically.]`

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13 additions): Daily schedules make time functional rather than purely atmospheric — the Blacksmith is more visibly active in morning, the Tavern in evening. Timezone rotation of specific events prevents time-of-day features from rewarding one timezone over another. NPC recognition across playtime creates relational depth — the town grows into knowing you. Per §0.1, each recognition tier carries tonal range so the NPC's voice stays specific rather than generic across the long arc of the player's residence in the town.]`

#### §14.4 Late-Depth Hybrid Zones (v12.35 CANONICAL)

At depths 7-8, each elemental zone's content begins incorporating its §14.2 secondary element. The hybridization manifests in three layers — visual palette shift, two hybrid monster variants, and collision opportunities involving the secondary element — while preserving the zone's primary identity. A player entering late-depth Ember Fields is still in Ember Fields (Flame zone) but now encounters Stone elements that didn't appear at depths 1-6. The zone extends itself rather than becoming something else.

**Hybrid applies to the 8 elemental spokes only.** Shadow Sanctum and Radiant Heights (Dark and Light primary zones, "Various" secondary per §14.2) do not receive hybrid treatment. These zones remain single-primary through all depths because their identity is tied to alignment endgame rather than elemental progression; hybrid variants would dilute the Dark/Light clarity the endgame depends on.

**§14.4.1 Hybrid zone element mapping.**

The hybrid secondary element per zone follows the existing §14.2 primary/secondary pairings:

| Zone | Primary | Depth 7-8 Hybrid | Thematic Framing |
|------|:-------:|:----------------:|-------------------|
| Ember Fields | Flame | Flame + Stone | Volcanic-fissure geology; lava-rock composite terrain |
| Mistwood | Venom | Venom + Wind | Miasma carried on mountain gusts; spore-drift |
| Frozen Shore | Frost | Frost + Storm | Ice-storm squalls; hail-driven cliffs |
| Thunder Peaks | Storm | Storm + Flame | Magmatic storm cells; lightning over volcanic vents |
| Deep Hollows | Stone | Stone + Venom | Toxic mineral seeps; contaminated rock formations |
| Sky Reaches | Wind | Wind + Frost | Glacial wind channels; freezing altitude jets |
| Tidal Caves | Water | Water + Venom | Brackish toxic pools; poisoned underwater currents |
| Verdant Wilds | Flora | Flora + Water | Wetland swamp thickening; flooded undergrowth |
| Shadow Sanctum | Dark | (no hybrid — identity preserved) | — |
| Radiant Heights | Light | (no hybrid — identity preserved) | — |

**§14.4.2 Visual palette shift.**

Depth 1-6 of each zone uses the zone's standard visual palette (per CR §1.2a Zone Mood Guides). Depth 7-8 applies a palette blend that introduces the secondary element's color language without replacing the primary. Transition from depth 6 to depth 7 is visible as the player traverses the zone — not a hard boundary but a gradient shift where the secondary element's tint increasingly appears in ambient textures, lighting temperature, particle effects, and environmental details.

**Ember Fields example.** Depth 1-6: dominant orange-red lava tones, volcanic haze, heat-shimmer. Depth 7-8: orange-red lava tones joined by charcoal grey and ashen browns from the Stone hybrid — solidified lava rivers, basalt rock formations, scorched stone outcrops. The primary lava palette is unchanged; the Stone palette is added underneath and alongside. A player standing at a depth 7 boundary sees both: bright lava flows still dominate the foreground, but the background has gained a rocky solidity the depth 1-6 areas lacked.

**§14.4.3 Hybrid monster variants.**

Each hybrid zone introduces **two hybrid monster variants** at depths 7-8. The variants are *art reskins* of two of the zone's three recurring forms (§6.7 Imp / Skeleton / Eye), applying the secondary element's visual language to the existing sprite evolution. No new sprite silhouettes, no new animation sets — the hybrid variant shares the recurring form's core animations and uses the secondary element's particles, colors, and combat effects.

**Per-zone hybrid variants (canonical).**

| Zone | Hybrid Variant 1 | Hybrid Variant 2 |
|------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Ember Fields | Basalt Imp (Stone reskin of Imp) | Forgestone Skeleton (Stone reskin of Skeleton) |
| Mistwood | Gust Imp (Wind reskin of Imp) | Storm-Breath Eye (Wind reskin of Eye) |
| Frozen Shore | Hailstorm Imp (Storm reskin of Imp) | Tempest Skeleton (Storm reskin of Skeleton) |
| Thunder Peaks | Magma Skeleton (Flame reskin of Skeleton) | Pyre Eye (Flame reskin of Eye) |
| Deep Hollows | Venomspore Imp (Venom reskin of Imp) | Toxmold Skeleton (Venom reskin of Skeleton) |
| Sky Reaches | Glacial Imp (Frost reskin of Imp) | Rime Eye (Frost reskin of Eye) |
| Tidal Caves | Brackish Skeleton (Venom reskin of Skeleton) | Venomtide Eye (Venom reskin of Eye) |
| Verdant Wilds | Marsh Imp (Water reskin of Imp) | Floodbloom Eye (Water reskin of Eye) |

**Hybrid variants apply the secondary element's combat properties.** A Basalt Imp in late-depth Ember Fields applies Stone-related statuses (Fracture, per §10), resists Stone-based incoming attacks at the same rates other Stone-aligned monsters do, and has the Stone element's telegraph color coding. The variant is mechanically equivalent to a Stone-element monster of the same depth — the "hybrid" is aesthetic layering on top of a mechanically-clean Stone monster.

**Collision opportunities.** Because the hybrid variants apply the secondary element's statuses, players encountering them can trigger collisions involving both the primary and secondary elements. A player fighting a Basalt Imp (Stone statuses) with a Flame weapon (their Ember Fields loadout) naturally triggers the Flame+Stone collision (Magma, C05). This is the in-world mechanism by which the v12.32 zone-paced collision curriculum becomes discoverable — depth 7-8 is where the zone's full signature collision list becomes experientially accessible.

**Monster count in hybrid zones.** Depths 1-6 of each zone follow the standard §6.7 structure: 3 recurring forms (Imp/Skeleton/Eye at the appropriate evolution stage) + 2 zone-unique monsters = 5 monster types encountered. Depths 7-8 of hybrid zones have: 3 recurring forms + 2 zone-unique monsters + 2 hybrid variants = **7 monster types encountered.** The 2 extra variants are added content at late depth, giving depth 7-8 a content-density increase that reflects the zone's deepening progression.

**§14.4.4 Shared dungeon, hybrid content within.**

Each depth has a single dungeon per spoke — the hybrid content manifests in monsters, palette, and collisions *within* the existing depth's dungeon rather than as a separate instance. A depth 7-8 Ember Fields dungeon is still *the* Ember Fields depth 7-8 dungeon; the hybrid monsters appear alongside the recurring and unique monsters in the same rooms. This preserves the existing dungeon economy (clear counts, party matchmaking, progression gating) without fragmenting the content pool.

**§14.4.5 Ambient audio layering.**

Each hybrid zone's depth 7-8 ambient audio adds a thin secondary layer to the primary zone's soundscape. Ember Fields depth 1-6 soundscape: lava bubbling, ember-crackle, distant volcanic rumbles. Ember Fields depth 7-8 soundscape: adds stone-scrape, distant rock-fall impacts, the creak of cooling basalt. The primary layer remains dominant; the secondary layer is audible as texture rather than as theme. A player with headphones can notice the layering; a player with speakers feels it as depth.

See `creative-reference.md` §1.2a for the full per-zone ambient audio specifications.

**§14.4.6 Gem drops unaffected.**

Gem drops in hybrid zones remain **primary-element only**. A depth 7-8 Ember Fields monster (including hybrid Basalt Imps and Forgestone Skeletons) drops Flame gems, never Stone gems. The secondary element adds combat variety and collision opportunities; it does not extend the zone's gem economy. This preserves §4.13 Gem Cutting's zone-specialization design — Stone gems remain the specialty of Deep Hollows (the Stone-primary zone), not a secondary-drop from any zone that happens to be Flame+Stone hybrid.

Players wanting Stone gems still must farm Deep Hollows. The Ember Fields hybrid gives Stone-involved collisions and combat encounters but does not give Stone materials. This maintains the inter-zone economy (§4.13 supplier-specialization dynamics) that the gem market depends on.

**§14.4.7 Level progression unchanged.**

Depths 7-8 cover levels 61-80 per §6.8 (standard 10-level-per-depth progression). The hybrid content does not extend the level range or create new depth tiers; it enriches the existing depth 7-8 range. A player at level 65 in Ember Fields is in depth 7, encountering the standard progression plus hybrid elements; no new progression gate applies.

**§14.4.8 Transition to depth 9 (Darkness).**

Depth 9 is Shadow Sanctum / Darkness — the Dark endgame zone. The transition from depth 8 (hybrid Ember Fields, Mistwood, etc.) to depth 9 (Darkness) is a *reset* — the elemental hybrid treatment ends at depth 8 because depth 9 belongs to a different zone entirely. A player leaving Ember Fields depth 8 and entering Shadow Sanctum sees no Stone elements continuing; Shadow Sanctum is Dark, full stop. This is the correct transition semantics: hybrid is a late-depth extension *of each elemental spoke*, not a cross-zone blending.

**§14.4.9 Content authoring.**

The v12.35 ship specifies the infrastructure, thematic mapping, and authoritative hybrid variant list (16 variants across 8 zones). The actual sprite art for the 16 hybrid variants is a v12.35.1 content-authoring follow-up. Each variant is a recolor of an existing recurring-form sprite with the secondary element's palette and particle effects — the art work is **reskin rather than new creation**, keeping the art budget bounded. The zone palette shift art is a shader/tint layer applied to existing zone textures; no new environment art required.

**Creative direction:** see `creative-reference.md` §1.2a for the per-zone palette-blend specifications, the ambient audio layering direction, and the thematic framing for each hybrid combination.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.35): The late-depth hybrid zones complete the zone-identity system that v12.32 Zone-Paced Collision Discovery established as the game's pedagogical infrastructure. v12.32 classified every collision pair to signature zones and displayed the full zone curriculum in the Codex, but depth 1-6 of each zone provided natural encounters only for collisions involving the zone's primary element. A player's signature zone might list 17 collisions in the Codex, but their routine play encountered only 9-10 of them; the remaining ones involving the secondary element required finding secondary-element applications elsewhere (usually in neighboring zones). v12.35 closes this gap: depth 7-8 of each hybrid zone naturally produces secondary-element encounters, making the full zone curriculum discoverable through in-zone play. A player who wants to complete their Ember Fields curriculum can now do it entirely *in* Ember Fields by going deep enough. The hybrid variants are art reskins rather than new monsters to keep the content-authoring scope bounded — 16 hybrid variants is substantial but not art-budget-breaking since each one reuses the existing recurring-form sprite and animation set with the secondary element's visual layer. The gem-drop-primary-only rule is load-bearing: the gem economy's zone specialization (Deep Hollows supplies Stone gems, Verdant Wilds supplies Flora gems, etc.) is protected by keeping gem drops tied to zone primary element rather than to monster element. A player in late-depth Ember Fields encounters Stone statuses for combat variety; they must still visit Deep Hollows to buy Stone gems for their weapons. The inter-zone economy the gem market depends on is untouched. The Shadow Sanctum / Radiant Heights exclusion is deliberate: those zones' alignment endgame depends on clarity of Dark/Light primary, and hybrid treatment would dilute the endgame's thematic weight. The two depth 9/10 zones stand apart from the elemental-spoke system because they belong to a different design layer. Together, v12.32 and v12.35 produce a complete zone-as-pedagogy design: the Codex maps the curriculum, the zones teach it through depth-graded encounters, and late-depth hybrid content ensures the full curriculum is encounterable without cross-zone grinding.]`

### §15. Control Scheme

**Landscape only.** The game enforces landscape orientation on mobile. Portrait displays a rotate prompt.

#### §15.1 Dual Virtual Joystick Layout

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                                                          │
│  [EXIT]                               [PLAYER LIST]      │
│                                                          │
│                     GAME WORLD                           │
│                   (Canvas area)                          │
│                                                          │
│                                                          │
│   ┌──────────┐                          ┌──────────┐     │
│   │  LEFT    │                          │  RIGHT   │     │
│   │ JOYSTICK │                          │ JOYSTICK │     │
│   │ (Move)   │                          │(Rot+Atk) │     │
│   └──────────┘                          └──────────┘     │
│                                          [SHIELD 🛡️]     │
│                                                          │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

#### §15.2 Input Mapping

**Left Joystick — Movement**

Analog stick. Direction = move direction. Magnitude = speed (walk to run). Character moves in the stick direction regardless of facing.

**Screen Swipe — Dodge / Lunge / Retreat Shot (Contextual)**

A swipe anywhere on the screen (outside of joystick zones) triggers a contextual movement action. The action depends on lock-on state, swipe direction relative to the locked target, and equipped weapon type:

- **No lock-on:** Standard dodge roll in swipe direction.
- **Locked on, swipe toward target, melee weapon equipped:** **Lunge** — dash toward the locked target, dealing reduced damage and applying auto-attack element status on arrival (§5.8.2).
- **Locked on, swipe away from target, ranged weapon equipped:** **Retreat Shot** — dodge away in swipe direction while automatically firing a status-applying shot at the locked target (§5.8.3).
- **Locked on, lateral swipe (perpendicular to target):** Standard dodge roll in swipe direction.
- **Locked on, swipe toward target, ranged weapon equipped:** Standard dodge roll (closing distance with ranged is not incentivized).
- **Locked on, swipe away from target, melee weapon equipped:** Standard dodge roll (melee retreat is just escape).

Direction detection uses a ±45° cone from the player-to-target vector. See §5.8.4 for canonical resolution logic.

**Tap Enemy — Lock On**

Tapping an enemy places a lock-on indicator (red rectangle target box) on that enemy. While locked on, auto-attacks track the locked target. Tap the same enemy to unlock. Tap a different enemy to switch lock. Lock persists through joystick release.

**Right Joystick — Rotation and Auto-Attack**

Holding the right joystick activates auto-attack. Dragging the stick rotates the character's facing direction and aim. Auto-attacks fire in the aim direction (or toward the locked target if one exists). Releasing the stick stops attacking but maintains the last facing direction.

When a ranged weapon is equipped, a line-of-sight indicator extends from the character in the aim direction. In-flight projectiles follow the right joystick's current rotation — wherever the stick is pointing, that's where all active projectiles are heading. This gives the player direct, continuous control over projectile direction until impact or off-screen.

**Double Tap Right Joystick — Weapon Swap**

Double-tapping the right joystick area toggles between melee and ranged weapon. Only one ranged weapon active at a time. The swap is instant. The joystick icon changes to reflect the active weapon.

**Swipe on Right Joystick — Special Attack (Elemental Trigger)**

A swipe gesture performed within the right joystick zone fires the special attack / elemental trigger in the direction of the swipe. The entire joystick area is the swipe canvas — the swipe direction is determined by the vector from swipe start to swipe end, not by which quadrant the touch began in. So a swipe that starts in the southwest of the joystick and ends in the northeast fires the special attack northeast.

If no element is on the weapon: the special attack deals higher base damage than a normal auto-attack (SPECIAL_ATK_MULT(1.8)× weapon damage). No elemental effect.

If the weapon has an element: the special attack applies the weapon's second element and its status effect in the swipe direction. This is the collision trigger — the swipe that detonates the setup. Costs mana (§4.5). Has cooldown (SWIPE_COOLDOWN).

For Staff: the special attack swipe fires in a cone (STAFF_AOE_CONE) in the swipe direction, hitting up to STAFF_AOE_CAP targets.

**Shield Button — Block**

Positioned to the right of the right joystick. A large circular button with a ring indicator showing remaining shield energy. Must be held down to keep the shield active — releasing drops the shield. Shield energy depletes while absorbing hits and recharges when not held. The shield faces the same direction as the right joystick's current rotation — the player aims the shield with the same stick they aim attacks with.

Ring indicator: Green > Yellow > Red as energy depletes. If fully drained, enters a cooldown period (ring turns red, button unresponsive until recharged).

##### §15.2a Haptic Feedback Layer (v12.38 CANONICAL)

Mobile devices with haptic motors (iOS Taptic Engine, Android haptic actuators) receive tactile feedback for key combat events. The haptic layer is **additive sensory-channel recognition** — every haptic event corresponds to an existing visual/audio event; no event is haptic-only. Players with haptics disabled or on devices without haptic hardware lose nothing mechanically and nothing informationally; the game is fully playable visually and aurally. Players with haptics enabled gain a third sensory channel that particularly benefits hearing-impaired players and players in environments where audio is muted.

**Haptic event registry.**

| Event | Pattern | Duration | Intensity |
|-------|---------|:--------:|:---------:|
| Auto-attack hit | Single short pulse | 10ms | Light |
| Critical hit | Double pulse (10ms gap) | 30ms total | Medium |
| Collision triggered | Triple pulse (15ms spacing) | 60ms total | Medium-strong |
| Status applied by player | Single short pulse | 10ms | Light |
| Status applied to player | Single longer pulse | 25ms | Medium |
| Grand Slam (player-dealt) | Sustained rolling pulse | 120ms | Strong, escalating |
| Perfect Block success (v12.27) | Double-pulse "click" | 20ms total | Light-medium |
| Godly reveal — Stage 3 | Sustained vibration matching audio motif | 3.5s | Medium, wave-modulated |
| Hardness 5 success | Distinctive 4-pulse celebratory pattern | 80ms total | Medium-strong |
| Death | Single heavy pulse, then silence | 50ms | Strong |
| Mastery Level crossing (v12.33) | Subtle single pulse | 15ms | Light |
| Act-transition acknowledgment (v12.36) | Subtle single pulse | 15ms | Light |

**Device detection.** The game queries the device's haptic capabilities on startup via the Web Vibration API (Android) and WebKit haptic APIs (iOS). Devices without haptic support silently skip all haptic output; gameplay is unaffected. Devices with haptic support default haptics on; players can disable via Settings → Controls → Haptics.

**Per-event granularity in settings.** The Settings → Controls → Haptics panel offers a single master toggle (on/off) for launch-simplicity. Future enhancement may add per-category toggles (Combat Haptics / Celebration Haptics / Ambient Haptics separately) based on player feedback, but the v12.38 launch ships with a single binary toggle to keep the surface minimal.

**Duration budget.** No single haptic event exceeds 3.5 seconds (the Godly reveal Stage 3 peak). Combat haptics are all under 120ms. This avoids haptic fatigue during sustained combat while reserving longer-duration haptics for rare cinematic moments (Godly reveal, Hardness 5 success) where the sensory emphasis is appropriate.

**Accessibility register.** The haptic layer is explicitly an accessibility feature for hearing-impaired players. Audio-critical signals (Perfect Block timing, resonance pulse, Godly reveal motif) all have haptic counterparts so the game remains fully playable without audio. See `creative-reference.md` §2.4 Haptic Feedback Design for the full per-event design rationale and pattern specifications.

**Desktop fallback.** Desktop browsers typically lack haptic hardware; the haptic layer is silently inert on desktop. Future enhancement may support gamepad rumble on desktop (if gamepad input is added in Phase 2+), using the same haptic event registry with gamepad-appropriate pattern adaptations. Flagged as a non-v12.38 future work item.

**Battery impact.** Haptic motors draw meaningful current. Long sustained haptics (Godly reveal) are budgeted to the rare-event tier. Per-frame or per-second haptic events are explicitly avoided — auto-attack haptic fires once per hit, not once per attack-animation-frame. The haptic layer adds negligible battery drain in typical combat sessions.

**No new tuning constants.** Haptic patterns are content-authored in `haptic-patterns.json` with per-event pattern specifications (pulse sequences, durations, intensities). The patterns are not tunable via §20 since they don't affect gameplay balance — they are sensory output matching existing gameplay events. Pattern refinement happens through content iteration rather than tuning adjustment.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.38): The haptic layer exists for two reasons. First, it's an accessibility feature — hearing-impaired players gain a third sensory channel that makes audio-critical gameplay signals (Perfect Block timing, resonance pulse, Godly reveal motif) felt rather than heard. The game's combat depth relies on catching timing-sensitive events, and a hearing-impaired player without a tactile alternative is missing a necessary input channel. Second, it's a *recognition moment* sensory layer that parallels v12.36's narrative/social recognition threads — when the player hits a Perfect Block, the haptic click confirms the success in the fingers; when a Godly reveals, the hand holds the weight of the moment. This is the same "the game notices what the player did" discipline applied to the tactile channel. The duration budget (nothing over 3.5s, combat haptics under 120ms) prevents haptic fatigue during sustained play. The accessibility-first framing ensures the feature is genuinely useful for hearing-impaired players rather than being a cosmetic enhancement. The desktop silent-fallback preserves cross-platform playability without pretending haptics exist where they don't. Battery awareness is explicit — haptics aren't free, and the registry is calibrated to significant events rather than per-frame events. This is the v12.38 bundle's contribution to the combat-sensory compilation thread: the game's feedback expressed through the third sensory channel most games neglect.]`

#### §15.3 Resource Rings on Buttons

Combat resources display as **circular SVG rings around their respective buttons**:

| Button | Ring Resource | Visual |
|--------|-------------|--------|
| Right joystick | Attack energy | Green > Yellow > Red. Tick mark at special attack threshold. |
| Shield button | Shield energy | Green > Yellow > Red. Depletes while held. Recharges when released. Red during cooldown. |

Mana is displayed as a small bar or ring on the HUD near the right joystick area — the player needs to know their mana to decide when to swipe trigger.

#### §15.4 Weapon-Specific Aim Indicators

**Bow:** When equipped and right joystick is active, a dashed LOS line extends from the character across the visible area in the aim direction. Animated dash offset scrolls along the line. Chevron arrowhead at the tip. All in-flight arrows follow the joystick's current rotation — the LOS line shows both where the next arrow fires and where active arrows are currently heading.

**Staff:** When equipped and right joystick is active, a translucent cone indicator shows the AOE zone for both auto-attacks and the swipe trigger. Cone angle: STAFF_AOE_CONE(45°). Element-colored edge.

**Greatsword / Sword:** No persistent aim indicator. The right joystick controls facing direction. The swing arc is visible during the attack animation only.

#### §15.5 Desktop Controls

Detected automatically via `(pointer:fine)` media query. Any keyboard input also confirms desktop mode. Touch joysticks and shield button hidden on desktop. Crosshair cursor replaces default.

| Input | Action |
|-------|--------|
| WASD | Move |
| Mouse position | Aim direction (replaces right joystick rotation) |
| Left click (hold) | Auto-attack toward cursor |
| Right click | Special attack / elemental swipe trigger toward cursor |
| Spacebar | Contextual: Dodge roll (no lock-on or lateral), Lunge (locked on + toward + melee), Retreat Shot (locked on + away + ranged). See §5.8. |
| Q (hold) | Block — shield faces cursor direction. Release to drop shield. |
| E | Interact (buildings, NPCs, gather nodes, sleep, workshop, pet house, minigame arena) |
| Tab | Cycle weapon slot (melee → ranged → staff) |
| 1 / 2 / 3 | Direct weapon slot select: 1=melee, 2=ranged, 3=staff |
| F | Special attack / elemental swipe toward mouse aim |
| Escape | Close all open panels |

**Desktop Stat HUD:** Replaces mobile joystick resource donuts. Fixed bottom-left display showing HP, Mana, and Stamina as horizontal progress bars with numeric values. Uses `Space Mono` monospace font. Bars color-coded: HP red, Mana purple, Stamina green.

**Desktop Aim Reticle:** Subtle crosshair drawn at mouse world position on canvas. Only visible in desktop mode.

**Desktop Keybind Hints:** Semi-transparent key labels shown at bottom-left, below stat HUD. Auto-fade to 60% opacity, full opacity on hover. Shows WASD, Space, E, Q, Tab, F, Esc bindings. Building interact buttons also show `[E]` key badges when in desktop mode.

#### §15.6 Tuning

| Variable | Default | Min | Max |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|
| SPECIAL_ATK_MULT | 1.8 | 1.3 | 2.5 |
| SWIPE_GESTURE_THRESHOLD | 40px | 25px | 60px |
| SWIPE_GESTURE_TIME_LIMIT | 300ms | 200ms | 500ms |
| DOUBLE_TAP_WINDOW | 300ms | 200ms | 500ms |

#### §15.7 Collision Preview (Loadout Menu)

When the player opens the element orientation menu (§4.2 — choosing which element is auto-attack vs swipe), the two collisions produced by the current orientation display inline:

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ELEMENT ORIENTATION                         │
│                                              │
│  Auto-attack: ◆ Flame (Burn)                │
│  Swipe:       ❄ Frost (Freeze)              │
│                                              │
│  → Setup Burn + Frost trigger = STEAM        │
│    Burst damage + brief miss chance area     │
│                                              │
│  → Setup Freeze + Flame trigger = MELTWATER  │
│    AOE slow field (volatile +30%)            │
│                                              │
│  [SWAP ORIENTATION]                          │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

The preview shows both collisions because the player will trigger both in combat — the auto-attack element applies the setup status, and the swipe triggers the collision. But in party play or multi-weapon scenarios, the reverse collision (enemy has the swipe element's status from another source, player's auto-attack triggers it) also fires. Showing both collisions keeps the player informed without requiring them to memorize the full matrix.

**Volatile indicator:** If either collision is volatile (strong element is the trigger), the word "volatile" and the +30% bonus display next to that collision name. The other direction shows no indicator — the absence is the signal.

**Codex link:** Each collision name in the preview is tappable → opens the Codex entry for that collision. The preview is a gateway to discovery, not a replacement for the Codex.

`[DESIGN INTENT: 90 collisions is too many to memorize. The player doesn't need to. They need to see the 2 collisions their current weapon orientation produces, right now, in the loadout menu. The preview turns the full matrix from "overwhelming reference" into "here's what your weapon does." The Codex remains the discovery and reference tool. The preview is the at-a-glance tool.]`

### §16. Networking

#### §16.1 Architecture

Server: Cloudflare Workers (Durable Objects), 20Hz authoritative tick. Client: 60Hz render with interpolation.
Transport: WebSocket. Protocol: JSON initially, binary optimization later.

**Server-authoritative:** HP, statuses, damage, loot, gold, collision resolution, threat tables.
**Client-authoritative:** VFX, animation, camera.
**Client-predicted:** Dodge i-frames, block reduction, immunity checks.

#### §16.2 Tick Execution Order (20Hz)

1. Snapshot player states to history buffer (§16.12 — PvP lag compensation)
2. Process player inputs/actions (§5.2 per action, atomically; PvP damage uses §16.12 rollback)
3. Tick status durations / DoT
4. Tick resource regen
5. Process event bus queues
6. Compute + broadcast room-wide state delta (§16.8)

#### §16.3 Interest Management (Client-Side)

All interest filtering is performed client-side. The server broadcasts full room state; the client decides what to render. Inner 25u = full rendering at 60fps. Outer to 40u = minimap blips, no VFX, no damage numbers. Beyond 40u = discarded (not rendered, not processed). Disabled in dungeons (all entities visible, max 4 players).

#### §16.4 Reconciliation

| Category | Tolerance | Fallback |
|----------|:---------:|---------|
| HP | ±5% | Red pulse |
| Resources | ±10% | Flash |
| Damage Numbers | ±20% | Silent |
| Status Durations | ±25% | Shimmer |

Cumulative drift guard: rolling 10-tick window. Sum exceeds 10% max_hp → forced snap.

#### §16.5 Status Reconciliation (Two-Phase)

**Phase 1 (client, immediate):** 50% opacity icon. DoT predicted. VFX reduced.
**Phase 2 (server confirms, 50–150ms):** 100% opacity. DoT reconciled. Full VFX.
**Rejection:** Icon fades 0.2s. DoT discarded.

#### §16.6 Rooms

50-player open world rooms, 4-player dungeon instances. Room creation on-demand. Marketplace is global (cross-room). Zone access gating: `player.level >= zone.min_level` or party leader meets requirement (underleveled members get 50% damage penalty).

#### §16.7 Reconnection

RECONNECT_GRACE(30s). Status durations paused. Immune. Threat zeroed (no recovery bonus). Spawn at disconnect position with 1.5s immunity.

#### §16.8 Tick Delta Batching (Room-Level Broadcast)

All state changes within a single server tick are collected into **one batched delta message per room**. This single message is broadcast to all connected WebSocket clients in the room simultaneously. No per-observer filtering is performed server-side — the client applies interest management locally (§16.3). The tick execution order (§16.2) runs atomically — step 5 assembles the aggregate delta after all mutations are complete.

**Cost model:** One outbound WebSocket message per room per tick. At 20Hz with 20 active rooms: 400 messages/second, ~1.04 billion messages/month at 40% average utilization. Cloudflare DO request cost: ~$150/month at 1,000 concurrent players.

**Delta message schema:**

```
TickDelta {
  tick: UInt32,                    // Server tick sequence number
  ts: UInt32,                      // Server timestamp (ms, truncated to 32-bit offset)
  players: [{                      // ALL players in room with changed state
    id: UInt16,                    // Session-local player ID (not global UUID)
    x: Int16,                      // Quantized position (×10, 0.1u precision)
    y: Int16,
    facing: UInt8,                 // 0-255 (256 angle steps, ~1.4° precision)
    state: UInt4,                  // PlayerState enum (11 values, see Appendix A)
    hp_pct: UInt8,                 // 0-255 (0.4% precision, sufficient for health bars)
    flags: UInt8,                  // Bitfield: [blocking, dodging, attacking, staggered, cc, sprinting, shielding, dead]
  }],
  events: [{                       // ALL combat events in room this tick
    type: UInt4,                   // EventType enum: damage/status/collision/kill/heal/loot
    source: UInt16,                // Actor ID
    target: UInt16,                // Actor ID
    value: UInt16,                 // Damage amount / heal amount / status ID
    flags: UInt8,                  // Bitfield: [crit, element(4 bits), collision, kill, dodge]
  }],
  statuses: [{                     // ALL status changes in room this tick
    target: UInt16,
    status: UInt4,                 // Status enum (9 values)
    action: UInt2,                 // 0=apply, 1=refresh, 2=remove, 3=consume
    remaining: UInt8,              // Remaining duration (×10, 0.1s precision)
  }],
  monsters: [{                     // Monster state (only changed monsters)
    id: UInt16,
    x: Int16,
    y: Int16,
    hp_pct: UInt8,
    state: UInt4,
  }],
  resources: [{                    // Per-player resource updates (only players whose resources changed)
    id: UInt16,                    // Player ID
    hp: UInt16,
    max_hp: UInt16,
    stamina: UInt16,
    mana: UInt16,
    gold: UInt32,
    xp: UInt32,
  }],
}
```

**Omission rules:** Arrays omitted entirely if empty. Resource entries only included for players whose resources changed this tick. Player entries only included for players whose position OR state changed since last tick. Monster entries only included for monsters whose state changed.

**Client-side interest filtering:** Each client receives the full room delta but only processes and renders entities within its interest radius (§16.3). Entities outside the inner radius (25u) are ignored for rendering and combat feedback. Entities between inner (25u) and outer (40u) are processed at reduced fidelity (minimap blips, no VFX). Beyond outer: discarded entirely. This filtering is cosmetic — the server has already resolved all game state authoritatively.

**Security posture:** Room-level broadcast means all clients receive all player positions. This enables potential client-side radar/minimap cheats. This is an accepted tradeoff because: (a) all game-critical state (HP, damage, gold, XP, loot, statuses) is server-authoritative and cannot be manipulated regardless of what data the client sees; (b) positional awareness provides minimal advantage in a PvE-focused game; (c) the cost savings (~97% reduction in DO request billing) justify the tradeoff.

**Encoding:** JSON initially with field-name shortening (`p` for players, `e` for events, `r` for resources, `x`/`y`/`f`/`s`/`h` for position/facing/state/hp). Binary protocol upgrade path uses the UInt types above for wire format — estimated 8 bytes per player entry, 7 bytes per event, 5 bytes per status change, 18 bytes per resource entry.

#### §16.9 Movement Batching

Player positions are **not broadcast individually**. All players in the room whose position or state changed are packed into the `players` array of the single room-wide TickDelta. The client applies interest filtering locally (§16.3) to determine which players to render.

**Position quantization:** Positions quantized to 0.1 game-unit precision (×10 stored as Int16). Range: ±3,276.7 units — sufficient for any zone map. Facing angle quantized to 256 steps (UInt8).

**Dead reckoning (client-side):** Between server ticks (50ms gap at 20Hz), the client extrapolates player positions using last known velocity vector. On receiving the next TickDelta, client interpolates from predicted position to authoritative position over INTERP_DURATION(100ms). Correction threshold: if prediction error > DEAD_RECKONING_SNAP(2.0u), snap instantly instead of interpolating (prevents rubber-banding on large desync).

**Stationary culling:** Players who haven't moved since the last tick are excluded from the `players` array. The client retains their last known position. A player reappears in the array on their next movement or state change.

**Bandwidth per tick (50-player room, worst case — all moving):**
- JSON (shorthand): ~50 × 40 bytes = ~2,000 bytes per room per tick
- JSON at 20Hz: ~40 KB/s per room (shared across all observers)
- Binary (upgrade): ~50 × 8 bytes = ~400 bytes → ~8 KB/s per room
- Typical (20 players moving): ~16 KB/s JSON, ~3.2 KB/s binary per room

#### §16.10 Combat Event Batching

All damage events, status applications, collision triggers, kills, heals, and loot events from a single tick are collected into the `events` and `statuses` arrays of the room-wide TickDelta. No individual combat messages are sent. The client filters which events to render based on proximity (§16.3) — distant combat events are silently discarded on the client.

**Aggregation rules:**
- Multiple auto-attack hits from the same source to the same target within one tick: summed into one event entry with combined value.
- DoT ticks on the same target from the same status: summed into one event entry.
- Multiple status refreshes on the same target: only the final state sent (last-refresh-wins).
- Kill events include the final damage event that caused the kill — no separate message needed.

**PvP batching:** PvP damage events follow the same batching path as PvE. In a Clan War (§42) with 20+ players fighting in the same zone, all PvP kills, damage, and status effects are part of the same room-wide TickDelta. Since there is only one message per room per tick regardless of event count, PvP does not increase message volume — only message size.

**Event budget per tick:** EVENTS_PER_TICK_WARN(200), EVENTS_PER_TICK_CAP(500). If a single tick generates more than CAP events, lower-priority events are dropped from the broadcast (priority: kills > collision > status > damage > heal > loot). Dropped events are still resolved server-side — only the client notification is suppressed. This caps message size during extreme scenarios (50-player open-world brawls).

**Clan War broadcast optimization:** During an active Clan War, war-specific events (kill scoring, MVP updates, war timer) are piggybacked onto the TickDelta as an optional `war` block rather than sent on a separate channel. This avoids doubling the message count during the highest-load scenario.

```
war: {                             // Only present during active Clan War
  scores: [UInt16, UInt16],        // [clan_a_kills, clan_b_kills]
  latest_kill: {                   // Most recent war kill (if any this tick)
    killer: UInt16,
    victim: UInt16,
  } | null,
  remaining: UInt16,               // Seconds remaining
} | null
```

#### §16.11 Bandwidth Budget

Room-level broadcast means every client in a room receives the same message. Message size scales with room activity, not observer count. Each client's inbound bandwidth equals the room's single outbound message size.

| Scenario | Message Size (JSON) | Per-Client Inbound | Per-Room Egress (×50 clients) |
|----------|:-------------------:|:------------------:|:-----------------------------:|
| Idle town (50 players, minimal movement) | ~1.5 KB/tick | ~30 KB/s | ~1.5 MB/s |
| Active PvE (20 moving, moderate combat) | ~3 KB/tick | ~60 KB/s | ~3.0 MB/s |
| Clan War (40 moving, heavy combat) | ~6 KB/tick | ~120 KB/s | ~6.0 MB/s |
| Dungeon (4 players, all active) | ~0.5 KB/tick | ~10 KB/s | ~40 KB/s |

**Server-side total egress (20 rooms, mixed activity):** Average ~2 MB/s per room × 20 rooms = ~40 MB/s peak. Cloudflare Durable Object WebSocket egress supports this within standard limits.

**Per-client inbound is higher than per-observer filtering** because clients receive data for all 50 players, not just nearby ones. The worst case (120 KB/s during clan war) is acceptable for broadband connections. Mobile clients on slower connections benefit from the backpressure system below.

**Backpressure:** If a client's WebSocket send buffer exceeds BACKPRESSURE_THRESHOLD(64 KB), subsequent TickDeltas for that client are downsampled to 10Hz (skip every other tick). If buffer exceeds BACKPRESSURE_CRITICAL(256 KB), downsampled to 5Hz. Client interpolation covers the gaps. Buffer recovery resets to 20Hz. Note: backpressure is per-client even though broadcast is per-room — the server tracks each WebSocket's buffer independently and skips sends for backed-up clients.

**DO request cost model (1,000 concurrent players, 40% avg utilization):**
- 20 rooms × 20 ticks/sec × 1 broadcast/tick = 400 outbound messages/sec
- 1,000 players × 12 inputs/sec avg = 12,000 inbound messages/sec
- Total: ~12,400 messages/sec × 0.4 utilization × 2.59M sec/month = ~12.9B messages/month
- Inbound player inputs dominate. At $0.15/million: ~$1,930/month
- With input batching at 10Hz + priority flushing (§16.13): ~12 avg inbound/sec during combat, ~10/sec otherwise → ~2,800 total/sec → ~2.9B/month → **~$435/month**

`[DESIGN INTENT: Room-level broadcast trades per-client privacy (all positions visible) for massive cost reduction. The server builds one delta, sends it once, Cloudflare fans it out to all WebSockets. Server compute scales with room activity, not player count. Client inbound bandwidth is higher but well within modern connection limits. Input throttling is the remaining cost lever — clients don't need to send position updates faster than 10Hz when the server only ticks at 20Hz.]`

#### §16.12 PvP Lag Compensation (Attacker-Favored Rollback)

PvP damage events use **attacker-favored rollback** to resolve hits against the target's historical state rather than their current server state. This ensures that if an attack visually connected on the attacker's screen, the server validates it. PvE damage is unaffected — monsters run server-side with zero latency and use current-state resolution.

**State history buffer:** The server maintains a rolling buffer of player combat states per room. Each entry stores the minimum data needed for damage pipeline checks (§8.2 P10–P20):

```
StateSnapshot {
  player_id: UInt16,
  tick: UInt32,
  state: UInt4,          // PlayerState enum (Idle/Dodging/Lunging/Retreating/Staggered/CCd/etc.; Blocking retained as deprecated value — see §5.4, Appendix A)
  in_iframes: Bool,      // True if mid-dodge i-frame window
  position: {x: Int16, y: Int16},
  facing: UInt8,
  recent_block_tap_tick: UInt32 | null,   // v12.27+: last tick at which target registered a block tap (for PvP active-block lag comp; null if no recent tap)
}
```

**Buffer depth:** LAGCOMP_BUFFER_TICKS(6) snapshots per player — 300ms of history at 20Hz. This covers up to 150ms one-way network latency (round-trip 300ms), which handles the vast majority of player connections. States older than the buffer are discarded.

**Memory cost:** 50 players × 6 snapshots × ~12 bytes = ~3.6 KB per room. Negligible.

**Resolution flow (PvP damage events only):**

```
func resolve_pvp_damage(event, target, attacker):
    # Calculate attacker's perception time
    attacker_rtt = attacker.websocket.estimated_rtt
    rewind_ticks = min(ceil(attacker_rtt / 2 / TICK_DURATION), LAGCOMP_BUFFER_TICKS)

    # Look up target's historical state
    historical = state_buffer.get(target.id, current_tick - rewind_ticks)

    if historical is null:
        # Buffer miss — fall back to current state (no compensation)
        resolve_damage(event, target)  # Standard §8.2 pipeline
        return

    # Resolve P10 (Block) and P20 (Dodge) against historical state
    # All other interceptors (P0, P30–P90) use current state
    if historical.state == DODGING and historical.in_iframes:
        if event.damage_source not in [COLLISION, DOT]:
            # Target WAS in i-frames from attacker's perspective — miss
            return

    # Active Block lag compensation (v12.27): check whether the target registered a block tap
    # in the historical window, rather than whether they were in a persistent BLOCKING state.
    if historical.recent_block_tap_tick is not null:
        tap_delta_seconds = (current_tick - historical.recent_block_tap_tick) × TICK_DURATION
        if event.damage_source in [DIRECT_ATTACK, SWIPE_TRIGGER] and tap_delta_seconds <= BLOCK_COMMITMENT_WINDOW:
            # Target HAD a block tap within the commitment window from attacker's perspective —
            # resolve via §5.3b three-tier outcome against the historical tap time
            if tap_delta_seconds <= PERFECT_BLOCK_WINDOW:
                # Perfect block
                event.base_damage = 0
                target.refund_stamina(PERFECT_BLOCK_STAM_REFUND)
            else:
                # Standard block
                event.base_damage ×= (1 - target.get_block_reduction())
                target.consume_stamina(BLOCK_STAM_COST)
            consume_tap(historical.recent_block_tap_tick)

    # Continue with standard pipeline from P30 onward using current state
    resolve_damage_from_p30(event, target)
```

**What this means in practice:**

For the **attacker:** If you saw your hit land on your screen, it lands on the server. Your swipe that detonated a collision doesn't get retroactively dodged by latency. Combat feels responsive and honest.

For the **defender:** Occasionally you dodge on your screen but still take the hit, because the attacker's perception (100-150ms earlier) saw you before the dodge. This reads as "I should have dodged earlier" rather than "the game cheated me." The defender always knows the Lawless Land and arena are latency-sensitive environments — the stakes being high reinforces the need for decisive action.

**Scope — PvP only:** Lag compensation applies exclusively when `event.source_actor.is_player AND target.is_player`. PvE damage (player→monster and monster→player) uses pure current-state resolution. Monster AI runs server-side with zero latency, so rollback would add complexity with no benefit.

**RTT estimation:** The server estimates each player's round-trip time from WebSocket ping/pong frames. Updated every LAGCOMP_RTT_SAMPLE(5s). Smoothed with exponential moving average (α = 0.3) to prevent jitter from causing erratic rollback depths. Capped at LAGCOMP_RTT_CAP(300ms) — players with worse latency than this get partial compensation but not full.

**Edge cases:**

**Target died between perception and current state:** If the target was alive in the attacker's perception window but died from another source before the attacker's hit resolves, the hit is discarded. Dead targets cannot be hit regardless of historical state. This prevents overkill exploits.

**Target disconnected:** Disconnected players are immune (§16.7). Lag compensation does not override disconnect immunity.

**Multiple attackers rewinding to different ticks:** Each PvP damage event independently rewinds to its attacker's perception. Two attackers with different latencies may see different historical states of the same target. This is correct — each attacker's experience is validated independently.

**Arena matches (§43):** Lag compensation is active during arena combat. Both players' RTTs are displayed on the pre-match screen (not exact values — a three-tier indicator: green < 80ms, yellow 80-150ms, red > 150ms). This sets expectations without revealing exploitable information.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Attacker-favored rollback is the standard for competitive multiplayer because it makes the game feel responsive for the person acting. The alternative — pure server-state resolution — punishes attackers for latency they can't control and creates phantom hits that erode trust. In the Lawless Land where death means losing everything, "I hit you and it counted" is more important than "I dodged and it always works." The 300ms buffer cap means extremely high-latency players get partial compensation, not a time-travel advantage. PvE exclusion keeps the system simple — only ~5-15% of damage events in a typical room are PvP, so the rollback logic rarely fires.]`

#### §16.13 Networking Tuning Table

| Variable | Default | Min | Max |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|
| TICK_RATE | 20 Hz | 10 | 30 |
| CLIENT_INPUT_RATE | 10 Hz | 5 | 20 |
| INPUT_BATCH_WINDOW | 100ms | 50ms | 200ms |
| INTEREST_INNER | 25u | 15u | 40u |
| INTEREST_OUTER | 40u | 25u | 60u |
| INTEREST_OUTER_RATE | 2 Hz | 1 | 5 |
| INTERP_DURATION | 100ms | 50ms | 200ms |
| DEAD_RECKONING_SNAP | 2.0u | 1.0u | 4.0u |
| POSITION_QUANTIZE | 0.1u | 0.05u | 0.5u |
| FACING_STEPS | 256 | 64 | 256 |
| EVENTS_PER_TICK_WARN | 200 | 100 | 400 |
| EVENTS_PER_TICK_CAP | 500 | 200 | 1000 |
| BACKPRESSURE_THRESHOLD | 64 KB | 32 KB | 128 KB |
| BACKPRESSURE_CRITICAL | 256 KB | 128 KB | 512 KB |
| RECONNECT_GRACE | 30s | 15s | 60s |
| LAGCOMP_BUFFER_TICKS | 6 | 3 | 10 |
| LAGCOMP_RTT_SAMPLE | 5s | 2s | 10s |
| LAGCOMP_RTT_CAP | 300ms | 200ms | 500ms |

`[DESIGN INTENT: Room-level broadcast is the single biggest cost control lever. Client input throttling (10Hz) with priority flushing is the second. PvP lag compensation is the feel lever — attacker-favored rollback makes high-stakes PvP honest at the cost of ~4 KB of state history per room. Together these three systems keep infrastructure cost low, combat responsive, and PvP fair.]`

#### §16.14 Client Input Protocol (Priority Flushing)

The client collects all player inputs into a buffer and sends them to the server at CLIENT_INPUT_RATE(10Hz) — one message per INPUT_BATCH_WINDOW(100ms). This reduces inbound WebSocket messages from 60/sec (per-frame) to 10/sec per player.

**Input buffer schema:**

```
InputBatch {
  tick: UInt32,                    // Client's estimated server tick
  move: {                          // Movement state (if changed)
    dx: Int8,                      // Joystick/WASD direction X (-127 to 127)
    dy: Int8,                      // Joystick/WASD direction Y (-127 to 127)
  } | null,
  aim: {                           // Aim direction (if changed)
    angle: UInt8,                  // 256-step facing angle
  } | null,
  actions: [{                      // Queued actions in order
    type: UInt4,                   // ActionType enum
    ts_offset: UInt8,              // ms offset from batch start (0-255)
  }],
}
```

The `ts_offset` field preserves sub-batch timing for combat actions. If a player dodges 30ms into a 100ms batch window, the server knows it happened at batch_start + 30ms, not batch_start + 0ms. This preserves timing fidelity for i-frame resolution.

**Priority flushing:** Certain actions bypass the batch timer and flush the buffer immediately on input. This ensures combat-critical actions are never delayed by up to 100ms of batch buffering.

**Priority flush triggers:**
- Dodge
- Block (start and release)
- Swipe trigger
- Item use (potion, consumable)
- Taunt

**Non-priority (batched normally):**
