- Movement direction changes
- Aim direction changes
- Auto-attack (continuous, server processes on tick anyway)
- Emotes
- UI interactions (panel opens, menu navigation)

**Effective send rate:** During exploration and town, the client sends at a steady 10Hz — movement only, cheap. During active combat, priority flushes add ~2-5 extra sends per second on top of the 10Hz base, bringing the effective rate to ~12-15Hz. This is still 4-5× cheaper than per-frame sending and only spikes during the moments that matter.

**Cost impact:** Priority flushing increases the average inbound rate from 10/sec to ~12-15/sec per active player during combat. Since not all players are in combat at all times (town, life skills, housing, fishing), the room-wide average stays close to 10-11/sec. The cost difference versus pure 10Hz throttling is negligible — roughly 10-15% more inbound messages.

`[DESIGN INTENT: The batch window is a cost optimization. Priority flushing is a feel optimization. Movement can wait 100ms — the client renders it locally anyway. Dodging cannot wait 100ms — the difference between dodging in time and dodging late is death. Priority flushing ensures the cost savings never come at the expense of combat responsiveness. The ts_offset field means the server can reconstruct exact input timing within the batch, so even batched actions resolve at the correct moment.]`

### §17. Anti-Cheat

#### §17.1 Player Behavior Anomaly Detection

5 weighted checks every 60s: input timing entropy (0.30), action sequence entropy (0.25), session duration (0.15), pathing variance (0.15), reaction time distribution (0.15).

WARN(0.35), THROTTLE(0.70 → 50% earning reduction, silent), SUSPEND(0.85 → manual review).

`[DESIGN INTENT: Silent throttle prevents bot developers from calibrating against the threshold.]`

#### §17.2 Economic Velocity Limits

| Resource | Cap (24h rolling) |
|----------|-------------------|
| Gold earned | 500,000g |
| Items looted | 1,000 items |
| Marketplace transactions | 50 buys + 50 sells |
| Gathering actions | 500 gathers |

#### §17.3 Marketplace Guards

Price ceiling: listing ≤ vendor_sell_back × 20. Self-trade detection (flagged, not blocked). Price spike: >200% median change in 1h → flag all transactions.

#### §17.4 Loot Reveal and Godly Provenance (CANONICAL)

The loot system has two server-authoritative anti-cheat surfaces: **cryptographic Godly provenance** (the lifetime guarantee that every Godly weapon is real) and the **server-streamed reveal protocol** (the per-tick guarantee that mystery reveals cannot be faked or fast-forwarded). Both are addressed here.

##### §17.4.1 Godly Weapon Provenance and Lineage (v12.7 CANONICAL)

Every Godly drop event is signed by the server at the moment of reveal with a cryptographic record committing to the kill, server tick, monster ID, drop zone, and **original recipient** player ID. The signature uses HMAC-SHA256 over a serialized record of these immutable origin fields, with a server-side secret key that is never exposed to the client. Under v12.7, the provenance record also tracks **mutable ownership state** (current owner, ownership history) alongside the immutable origin signature, supporting the new tradability rules in §4.6b.1.

```
GodlyProvenance {
  // === Immutable origin (signed at reveal time) ===
  weapon_id: UUID,                      // unique to this weapon
  drop_tick: UInt64,                    // server tick when the Godly roll succeeded
  drop_zone: String,                    // zone where the kill occurred
  monster_id: String,                   // monster definition that produced the drop
  original_recipient: UUID,             // the player who picked up the question mark and revealed it
  origin_signature: HMAC-SHA256,        // server signature over all above origin fields
                                        // NEVER changes for the lifetime of the weapon

  // === Mutable ownership state ===
  current_owner: UUID,                  // the player who currently holds the weapon
  ownership_history: List<OwnershipEvent>,  // append-only log of all transfers
  current_owner_signature: HMAC-SHA256,     // server signature over (weapon_id, current_owner, history_length)
                                            // re-signed on every transfer
}

OwnershipEvent {
  from: UUID,                           // previous owner
  to: UUID,                             // new owner
  timestamp: UInt64,                    // server time of transfer
  transfer_type: Marketplace | DirectTrade | Gift | Mail,
  transfer_signature: HMAC-SHA256,      // server signature over the transfer fields
}
```

The provenance record is stored in a dedicated **Durable Object** (`GodlyLedger`) that persists for the lifetime of the server. The ledger is the canonical truth about which Godly weapons exist, who originally received each one, and the full ownership lineage from the original recipient to the current owner.

**Two distinct signatures.** The `origin_signature` is signed once at the moment of reveal and never changes — it proves that this specific weapon was produced by a legitimate Godly drop event from a real kill. The `current_owner_signature` is re-signed on every transfer and proves that the current owner field is server-authoritative. A client cannot fake either signature, and any operation that touches a Godly weapon verifies both. The two-signature design separates the question of "is this a real Godly?" (origin) from "who currently owns this Godly?" (ownership), which lets the ledger handle ownership transfers cleanly without ever modifying the origin record.

**Verification at every interaction.** Any operation involving a Godly weapon — display, equip, inspect, render, swap, view in inventory, attempt to trade — verifies the weapon against its provenance record:

1. Client sends the weapon's ID as part of the operation request.
2. Server fetches the provenance record from the `GodlyLedger`.
3. Server verifies the `origin_signature` against the immutable origin fields.
4. Server verifies the `current_owner_signature` against the current owner state.
5. Server verifies the `current_owner` matches the player attempting the operation (for owner-restricted operations like equip, decouple, harden).
6. If any check fails, the operation is rejected and the failure is logged for manual review.

**Transfer protocol.** When a Godly weapon is traded (via marketplace, direct trade, gift, or mail), the server executes the transfer atomically:

1. Client A requests a transfer of weapon W to client B (via whatever transfer mechanism the trade uses).
2. Server verifies client A is the current_owner of W.
3. Server verifies the transfer mechanism is valid (e.g., marketplace listing has cleared, direct trade has been confirmed by both parties, mail has been sent and received).
4. Server constructs an `OwnershipEvent{from: A, to: B, timestamp: now, transfer_type: <type>}` and signs it.
5. Server appends the OwnershipEvent to the weapon's `ownership_history`, updates `current_owner` to B, re-signs `current_owner_signature`, and persists the changes to the `GodlyLedger` Durable Object atomically.
6. Both clients receive a `TransferComplete{weapon_id, new_owner, transfer_event}` notification.
7. The weapon is removed from A's inventory and added to B's inventory.

**Lineage is publicly inspectable.** Any player can inspect any Godly weapon and see its full lineage: the original recipient (who first revealed it from the gods), every subsequent owner, the timestamps of each transfer, and the transfer mechanism used. The lineage is rendered in the weapon inspect card as a chronological list. The marketplace listing UI shows lineage prominently — buyers can see who originally received the weapon and how many hands it has passed through. **A Godly weapon traceable back to a legendary player is worth more than one with an unknown origin.** Lineage becomes part of the weapon's value, and the marketplace will price weapons accordingly.

**No retroactive lineage editing.** The ownership_history log is append-only and cryptographically anchored. Once an OwnershipEvent is appended and signed, it cannot be removed, modified, or backdated. A player who wanted to "wash" a Godly weapon's lineage (remove a notorious previous owner from the chain, for example) cannot do so — the lineage is permanent. This is intentional: the social weight of lineage depends on the lineage being honest.

**Original recipients are permanent identifiers.** The `original_recipient` field is the immortal record of the divine encounter. Even if the original recipient deletes their account, transfers their character, or has been banned, their UUID remains in the record forever. The lineage display can show their account state (Active / Deleted / Banned), but never erases their identity. This preserves the historical record of who the gods originally chose, which is the entire point of the lineage system.

##### §17.4.2 Server-Streamed Mystery Reveal Protocol (v12.6 CANONICAL)

Mystery reveals (§4.6b.ii) play out as a multi-stage tiered RNG animation in which the player watches roll ticks visibly resolve over ~9-23 seconds. Because the player can see individual ticks, and because the result is determined progressively over the animation, the reveal is a **server-streamed event** rather than a client-side animation triggered by a single result message.

**The protocol:**

1. Player picks up a mystery item. The client sends a `RevealRequest{weapon_id}` to the server.
2. The server confirms the weapon is owned by the player and that the reveal has not already played out for this weapon.
3. The server begins the reveal stream. For each tick of the animation (~12 per second), the server sends a `RevealTick{stage, tick_index, color}` message to the client, where `color` is one of {silver, gold, prismatic} for the current stage. The client renders the icon in that color for the next tick frame.
4. The server controls the tick sequence. For most ticks, the server sends a roll outcome consistent with the underlying probability distribution (the player sees gold flashes at the conditional rate during Stage 1, prismatic flashes at the conditional rate during Stage 2, etc.). The final tick of each stage's deceleration is calibrated to land on the pre-committed grade — the client cannot tell the difference between "the server is rolling honestly" and "the server is constructing a tick sequence that lands on the predetermined result."
5. On stage transitions (Rare → Stage 2, Elite → Stage 3), the server sends a `RevealEscalate{new_stage}` message that the client uses to trigger the visual transition (panel growth, position change, color palette swap).
6. On final resolution, the server sends a `RevealResult{grade, weapon_state}` message with the final grade and the full weapon data, and the client renders the resolution animation (chime + border + inventory transition).
7. The server logs the entire reveal sequence to the player's session record for anti-cheat auditing.

**Why this protocol matters.** Without server-streamed ticks, a malicious client could:
- Skip the animation entirely and read the final grade from a single result message
- Display a fake Godly reveal animation that did not actually happen, for streaming/screenshot purposes
- Calculate when escalations are likely to occur and trigger client-side modifications during them
- Replay reveal animations with fabricated tick sequences

With server-streamed ticks, none of these are possible. The client can only render what the server tells it to render, and the server is the only entity that knows the result before the final tick. The architectural cost is small: a single small WebSocket message per tick, ~100-300 messages per Rare-or-better reveal, only on ~10% of total weapon drops. Most drops (Normal) skip the reveal entirely. The bandwidth and latency cost is negligible compared to the security guarantee.

**Network failure handling.** If the network drops mid-reveal, the client pauses the animation in its current state and the server queues the remaining ticks. On reconnection, the server resumes streaming from the last acknowledged tick. The reveal completes correctly even across brief connection interruptions. If the client disconnects entirely before the reveal finishes, the server marks the reveal as "pending" and the player will see it complete the next time they log in — the dramatic moment is preserved across sessions.

**Reveals are atomic.** A reveal is either fully completed or fully pending. The server never partially commits a reveal. The player either sees the full animation or sees it later in full. The weapon does not enter the player's inventory until the reveal animation completes.

`[DESIGN INTENT: The two anti-cheat surfaces here address two different threat models. The Godly provenance system addresses *durable forgery* — preventing fake Godly weapons from existing in the world, ever, for any reason. The reveal streaming protocol addresses *animation forgery* — preventing fake reveal moments from being shown to other players or recorded. Both are necessary because the social fabric of the loot lottery layer depends on every reveal being trustworthy. A player who sees another player's Godly reveal needs to be able to trust that it really happened. A player who hears about a Hardness 5 broadcast needs to be able to trust that the weapon really exists. The cost of these guarantees is small (a Durable Object, some signature verification, a few extra WebSocket messages) and the benefit is the entire credibility of the lottery system. The math is obvious: if the lottery is not trustworthy, it is not a lottery, it is a cosmetic pretense. Trustworthiness here is not optional.]`

##### §17.4.3 Weapon History Ledger (v12.30 CANONICAL)

The per-weapon Weapon History (§4.11b) is server-authoritative. All counter increments and log entry appends flow through canonical event-registration code paths in the server's combat and event pipelines. Clients cannot fabricate, modify, or forge History entries.

**Counter write paths.**
- `total_kills` — increments in the §8 monster death event resolver when the equipped weapon's owner received credit for the final blow. No client-side kill registration; kill credit is resolved server-side via damage-last-hit tracking.
- `total_damage_dealt` — aggregated in the §2 damage resolution pipeline. Damage events that originate from the equipped weapon's owner add their damage amount to the counter. Rounded to the nearest 1,000 at aggregation time to keep the stored counter compact (a weapon with 4,237,891 damage dealt stores 4,238,000).
- `arena_matches_won` — increments when the §37 Arena system finalizes a match result in the player's favor, while the weapon is in the player's equipped slot at the moment of match completion.
- `clan_wars_won` — increments when the §36 Clan War system finalizes a war victory, for the equipped weapon of every participating player on the winning clan at the moment of war completion.

**Log append paths.**
- `bosses_defeated` — appends an entry when the §10 boss death event fires, the player was present for the kill, and the weapon was equipped. Entry content: `{boss_id, zone_id, server_timestamp}`. Deduplication: if the same boss is killed again on a separate attempt, a new entry is appended (each encounter is its own entry). There is no "unique-boss" filtering; the log records every boss defeat.
- `godly_reveals_witnessed` — appends an entry when a Godly reveal animation completes (§4.6b.ii) and the player was within the reveal's witness radius (approximately 80u, same radius used for the §4.6b.i broadcast coverage) while the weapon was equipped. **Entry content: `{zone_id, server_timestamp}` only — no `revealing_player_id`, no weapon name, no other identifying information about whose Godly dropped.** This privacy rule is load-bearing for preserving the §8.12a anonymous-accomplishments principle: the fact of having witnessed a reveal is logged, but which player received it is not.
- `hardness_successes` — **not logged directly in Weapon History.** The §17.5 HardeningLedger already contains per-weapon append-only audit entries for every hardening attempt (successful and failed). The `hardness_successes` field on Weapon History is a *projection* of successful entries from the HardeningLedger, computed at display time. No duplicate storage. The Weapon History inspect card query reads the HardeningLedger and filters to `outcome == success` entries, then displays them alongside the other History items.
- `first_crafter` — populated at craft time from the §18.3 attribution field on the item. Single entry, immutable after craft. Pre-v12.25 items (no attribution) have `first_crafter = null`.

**Star state.** The starring UI (§4.11b) modifies a per-entry `starred: bool` flag. Star writes are server-authoritative: when the client sends a "star entry X" request, the server validates the entry exists, marks it starred, and broadcasts the change to any other clients currently viewing the weapon's inspect card. Un-starring follows the same path. Unlimited star count per weapon; no cap.

**Star persistence through trades.** The `starred` flag travels with each entry through weapon trades — the weapon's full History (including star state on every entry) is part of the weapon's persistent state. A buyer receiving a weapon sees the previous owner's curation until they modify it. Current-owner edits overwrite previous state with no history of star changes (stars are presentational metadata, not events).

**Append-only audit.** Counter increments and log appends are append-only from the server's perspective. There is no "correct a history entry" client path. If a bug is discovered where a History entry was written incorrectly, the correction requires a server-side maintenance operation with explicit logging, consistent with how §17.4.1 Godly lineage corrections would be handled.

**Cross-reference to §17.4.1.** Godly weapons carry both lineage (§17.4.1) and History (§17.4.3). The two systems are orthogonal and are implemented with different anti-cheat weights: Godly lineage uses HMAC signatures per ownership event because the social fabric depends on every Godly being provably authentic; Weapon History uses append-only writes without per-event signatures because a forged History entry on a non-Godly weapon would be detectable through counter/log inconsistency checks and the social cost of a forgery would be bounded. The engineering cost of Weapon History is commensurately lighter than Godly lineage (no per-event cryptographic operations), consistent with the architectural budget.

**Forensic logging.** The Weapon History itself is the forensic record — every boss defeat, every Godly witness, every first-crafter mark is a permanent audit trail. If a weapon's inspect card displays a suspicious History claim, the ledger is replayable server-side to verify when and how each entry was written. Counter discrepancies (e.g., `total_kills` exceeding what the player's kill event history can account for) are detectable through aggregation-consistency checks.

**No new Durable Object.** The Weapon History rides on the existing weapon-state persistence (see `architecture.md` §9.5.8 for the schema extension). No new DO, no separate storage backend, no additional API surface.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.30): Weapon History's anti-cheat budget matches Temper's: server-authoritative writes, append-only audit, rides on existing gear-state validation. The system's economic significance is real (high-History weapons will command marketplace premiums) but bounded — a forged kill counter or a fabricated boss entry on a non-Godly weapon does not unravel a sacred lottery system the way a forged Godly would. The Godly lineage system pays for its per-event HMAC cost because Godly weapons are in a separate cosmological category; Weapon History earns the lighter footprint because its economic significance is ordinary-rare rather than transcendent-rare. The privacy rule on `godly_reveals_witnessed` is structurally important — a witness entry that named the Godly recipient would leak information the recipient might not want public, and would undermine §8.12a's anonymous-accomplishments rule. Logging the event without the actor preserves both properties: the weapon remembers the moment, but the weapon does not out anyone.]`

#### §17.5 Temper Integrity (v12.28 CANONICAL)

The per-weapon `temper` field (§4.6c.6) is server-authoritative. The `temper` value can only be incremented via the canonical failed-hardening-attempt code path — an attempt that was initiated by an authenticated player at a legitimate Blacksmith station, consumed the gold and materials, rolled against the canonical success rate, and came up failed. No client-side path can modify the field.

**Reset rules.** On successful hardening attempts (Hardness increase), the server resets `temper` to 0 atomically with the Hardness increment — both changes happen in the same server tick. There is no intermediate state where a weapon has old Temper and new Hardness.

**Persistence through trades.** When a weapon is traded, sold, gifted, or mailed, the `temper` field travels with the weapon unchanged. The receiving player's inventory shows the weapon with its current Temper value immediately. Marketplace listings display Temper as part of the weapon's listed identity; auto-matching order books include Temper in the matching key per §39.8.

**Salvage destroys Temper.** Salvage destroys the weapon and all its fields, including Temper. There is no "recover Temper from salvaged weapons" path. A player who salvages a Temper 80 weapon loses that Temper permanently along with the weapon.

**Anti-exploit guards.** The server validates on every hardening attempt that the incoming request's pre-attempt `temper` value matches the server-stored value. A client that lies about the weapon's current Temper (e.g., claiming Temper 100 when the server knows it's 47) has the attempt rejected with a tamper-detection flag. The validation is identical to the general §17 anti-tampering pattern and requires no new infrastructure — Temper rides on the existing gear-state validation path.

**Forensic logging.** The server logs every successful and failed hardening attempt to an append-only ledger (`HardeningLedger` — part of the existing weapon-state persistence system, not a new Durable Object). Each entry records: player_id, weapon_id, attempt timestamp, weapon's Hardness before attempt, weapon's Temper before attempt, success/failure, new Hardness after resolution, new Temper after resolution. This log supports audit if any weapon's Hardness/Temper state is ever questioned. No retention limit — the ledger is permanent.

**Architecture note.** The `temper` field addition is a small schema extension to the existing Item data model (see `architecture.md` §9.5.5). Adding the field requires no new Durable Object, no schema migration for existing weapons (default value 0 at the v12.28 release point means every pre-v12.28 weapon has Temper 0 from that moment onward), and no new server-side validation infrastructure beyond what the gear-state anti-cheat already performs.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.28): Temper is economically significant — a Temper 100 weapon at high Hardness has meaningful marketplace value, and Temper becomes a new identity axis on weapons (§39.8). The anti-cheat properties must match that significance. The server-authoritative rule is the load-bearing guarantee: a client cannot lie about Temper, cannot fabricate Temper, cannot modify Temper outside the canonical code path. The HardeningLedger append-only audit trail is the backstop — if Temper ever appears wrong, the full history is reconstructable from the ledger. The infrastructure cost is minimal because Temper rides on existing gear-state anti-cheat rather than requiring new cryptographic machinery like Godly provenance. Temper is mortal lottery — mortal systems protect it.]`

### §18. Life Skills

| Skill | Purpose | Session Type |
|-------|---------|-------------|
| **Woodcutting** | Chop trees for wood (bow/staff/raft/sled/torch materials) | Active |
| **Fishing** | Catch fish for cooking (healing) | Active |
| **Mining** | Mine ore for blacksmithing | Active |
| **Farming** | Grow herbs for cooking buffs, time-gated | Return visits |
| **Cooking** | Fish→healing (timing minigame), herbs→combat buffs | Active |
| **Blacksmithing** | Forge melee weapons + shields from ore (20 tiers) | Active |
| **Woodworking** | Craft bows + staves from wood (20 tiers) | Active |
| **Gem Cutting** | Cut raw gems into polished slottable gems | Active |
| **Enchanting** | Slot polished gems into weapons/amulets/shields | Active |
| **Trapping** | Capture pet companions (combat + loot) | Active |

XP per skill: `xp_required(level) = ceil(120 × 1.09^(level-1))`. Post-100: `ceil(at_100 × 1.10^(level-100))`. Max skill level: 100. (**v12.24:** XP base reduced from 450 to 120 — see design target note below.)

**Design targets (v12.24 CANONICAL):** Level 100 in ~100 hours (approximately 100 days at 1h/day, or ~13 days at 8h/day). Life skills are now **peer activities to combat**, not dedication gates — the target reflects the v12.24 reframing that a player should be able to reach Level 100 in multiple skills over the course of a character's combat progression, rather than facing a single-skill dedication curve that exceeds combat Level 100 by 3-5×. Life skills trail combat by roughly 5-10 levels for players splitting 3h sessions as 2.5h combat / 30min skills.

**Named-craftsman content moves to post-100 progression.** The scarce-individual-per-server framing described in Creative Reference §8.12a ("Bro Tessa is the only player on the server who can cut Level 9 Storm gems") is no longer gated by raw Level 100 under v12.24 — that gate became too accessible when life skills dropped to ~100 hours. Named-craftsman mythology now lives in the **§45 Life Skill Guilds Transcendent rank system**, which extends progression from Rank 7 (gated by life skill Level 100) through Rank 8 (Transcendent) gated by Level 150 under the post-100 formula. The Transcendent rank is where Level 9→10 gem combinations and Tier 20 hardening services become the scarce-individual content the §8.12a passage describes. **See §45 for the canonical Transcendent rank specification** (Phase 3 stub — full Transcendent rank gates, quest requirements, and service-exclusivity rules to be specified in the Phase 3 design pass).

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.24): The prior 366-hour target was calibrated to make life skill Level 100 a major career commitment paralleling combat Level 100 in the opposite direction — a player would pick one life skill as their identity and commit to it across their character's lifetime. Under that framing, named gem-cutters and named blacksmiths were the natural consequence of scarce Level 100 specialists. The v12.24 reframing accepts a different premise: life skills are peer activities that players engage with in parallel to combat, not alternatives to it. Level 100 becomes reachable in multiple skills for active players. The named-craftsman content doesn't disappear — it moves to the post-100 Transcendent rank system, which preserves scarcity by pushing the gate further out. The §23.6c gem-cutting service economy's "8-12 active named gem-cutters" assumption now applies to Transcendent-rank cutters (Level 150+), not Level 100 cutters. A separate Phase 3 design pass will specify the exact Transcendent rank gate structure.]`

#### §18.2 Resource Tiers and Zone Depth Gating

Each spoke has eight standard depth tiers plus two endgame tiers. Resources found at each depth require a minimum life skill level to harvest. Higher depths are gated behind dungeon completion.

| Depth | Access Gate | Gathering Level | Resource Tier | Example Resources |
|:-----:|-----------|:--------------:|:-------------:|-------------------|
| 1 | Open (walk in) | 1+ | Tier 1 | Rough Crystal, Copper Ore, Common Herb |
| 2 | Clear Depth 1 Dungeon | 10+ | Tier 1 | Improved Tier 1 yields |
| 3 | Clear Depth 2 Dungeon | 20+ | Tier 2 | Refined Crystal, Iron Ore, Uncommon Herb |
| 4 | Clear Depth 3 Dungeon | 30+ | Tier 2 | Improved Tier 2 yields |
| 5 | Clear Depth 4 Dungeon | 40+ | Tier 3 | Pure Crystal, Mithril Ore, Rare Herb |
| 6 | Clear Depth 5 Dungeon | 55+ | Tier 3 | Improved Tier 3 yields |
| 7 | Clear Depth 6 Dungeon | 70+ | Tier 4 | Resonant Crystal, Adamant Ore, Epic Herb |
| 8 | Clear Depth 7 Dungeon | 85+ | Tier 5 | Elemental Heart, Starfall Ore, Legendary Herb |
| 9 (Darkness) | Clear Depth 8 | 90+ | Tier 5 | Shadow-infused variants |
| 10 (Light) | Clear Darkness | 95+ | Tier 5 | Light-blessed variants |

**Zone-specific resources.** Each element's spoke yields different materials:

| Spoke Element | Gathering Specialty | Farming Specialty | Cooking Specialty |
|:-------------:|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Flame | Ember Crystals, Magma Ore | Firebloom, Ash Root | Fire Resist Food |
| Frost | Frost Shards, Glacial Ore | Snowpetal, Ice Cap | Frost Resist Food |
| Water | Tidal Pearls, Coral Ore | Waterlily, Kelp | Regen Boost Food |
| Venom | Toxic Crystals, Blight Ore | Nightshade, Fungus | Antidote, Poison Resist |
| Storm | Spark Crystals, Storm Iron | Thunderbloom, Static Moss | Speed Boost Food |
| Stone | Earth Crystals, Dense Ore | Rock Vine, Cave Lichen | Defense Boost Food |
| Wind | Zephyr Crystals, Sky Ore | Cloudpetal, Whisperleaf | Agility Boost Food |
| Flora | Vine Crystals, Living Ore | Thornbloom, Mossroot | HP Regen Food |

**Gem System interaction (v12.6 CANONICAL).** The v12.6 gem refactor (§4.13) replaces the prior element-upgrade-via-crystals model with a complete gem-based system. The interaction between life skills and the gem system:

- **Mining** is the primary life skill source for gems. Mining nodes in each zone produce both ore (for Blacksmithing) and gems (for the Enchanter). Gems from mining are zone-locked by element (a Flame mining node in a fire zone produces Flame gems). All mined gems are Level 1 — higher-level gems come from Gem Cutting combination.
- **Gem Cutting** is the skill that gates gem combination levels. The skill threshold table is canonical in §4.13.2: Level 5 to combine to Level 2, Level 20 to combine to Level 3, all the way up to Level 100 to combine to Level 10. Gem Cutting levels through the act of combining gems (and through cutting raw mined gems into polished, slottable form — the basic Gem Cutting operation).
- **Enchanting** is the skill that gates the Enchanter's higher-tier services and (eventually) lets the player build their own Enchanting station outside of town. The basic enchanting service (one-time weapon enchantment, composite creation, composite application, decoupling) is available at any town Enchanter to any player from Level 1, regardless of their personal Enchanting skill. Personal Enchanting skill unlocks fee discounts and (at very high levels) the ability to host a private Enchanter for clan members.

The old "Enchanting requires gathering" table from earlier doc versions is deleted. Gems replace tier-elevation costs entirely, and the Enchanter's role is the gem combination + composite application interface, not a tier-elevation NPC.

**Farming plots are zone-gated.** Each zone spoke has a Farm area where players can plant zone-specific seeds. Seeds drop from gathering nodes and monster kills. Growth time: Tier 1 = 1 hour, Tier 2 = 4 hours, Tier 3 = 12 hours, Tier 4 = 24 hours, Tier 5 = 48 hours.

**Cooking scales with ingredients.** Higher-tier ingredients from deeper zones produce more powerful food buffs. A Tier 1 Fire Resist food gives +10% resistance for 5 minutes. A Tier 5 Legendary ingredient gives +40% resistance for 30 minutes.

#### §18.3 Crafter Attribution (v12.25 CANONICAL)

Every crafted item produced by a player — weapons, armor, shields, amulets, gems, composites, furniture, and prepared food — carries a permanent attribution field identifying the crafter. The attribution persists through trades, gifts, and the item's lifetime.

**Inspect card presentation.** Any item's inspect card displays the attribution prominently: *"Crafted by: Bro Mason."* The attribution is formatted as a tappable link (on mobile) or hover-revealable citation (on desktop) that opens the crafter's portfolio (§18.3.1).

**Transcendent crafter formatting.** Items crafted by players at Rank 8 Transcendent in the relevant skill display the attribution in prismatic text: *"Made by Transcendent Craftsman Bro Mason."* The prismatic treatment is the same visual register used for Godly weapons' quality border — sacred-object language applied to master-crafter identity. Only Transcendent-rank crafters produce prismatic attribution; Rank 7 and below produce standard-text attribution.

**NPC vendor items.** Items sold by NPC vendors (basic starter gear, some consumables) do not carry crafter attribution — they display no "Crafted by" line, or optionally "Crafted by: Town Vendor" depending on the item source. Player-made items always carry attribution.

**Legacy items (pre-v12.25).** Items crafted before the attribution system shipped display "Crafter: Unknown" as a grandfathered state. All new crafts from v12.25 forward carry attribution.

**No attribution for non-craftable items.** Items gained through monster drops, dungeon chests, or quest rewards do not have crafter attribution — the attribution system applies only to items produced through the life-skill crafting stations.

##### §18.3.1 Crafter Portfolio

Tapping a crafter's attribution on any item opens a portfolio view showing that crafter's recent work. The portfolio displays:

- Up to 20 of the crafter's most recently produced items, across all skill categories
- Each item shows a small thumbnail, name, quality grade, and current known status ("in your inventory," "listed on marketplace," "unknown location")
- The crafter's rank in each relevant skill

**Transcendent crafter portfolio extensions.** Transcendent-rank crafters' portfolios display additional elements:

- **Signature specialty:** a derived label based on the crafter's production history — "primarily forges greatswords," "specializes in Frost composites," "known for Tier 19+ staves"
- **Shop note:** a player-authored short text (up to 200 characters) that the crafter can write and update at will. Intended for commission requests, specialty announcements, or personal flavor. *"Commissions welcome for Tier 20+ hardening attempts. Contact via mail."* Appears on every item's portfolio view.
- **Production counts:** total items produced across the crafter's lifetime, broken down by skill

Non-Transcendent portfolios show only the 20-item recent-work list. The Transcendent additions are a rank-gated layer.

##### §18.3.2 Milestone Recognition Notifications

Crafters receive in-game server messages when their work plays a role in another player's *milestone moments*. Milestones are defined events that represent meaningful player accomplishments. The current set:

- Another player uses a crafter's item during their first-ever Apex boss defeat
- Another player uses a crafter's item during their first Arena Tournament championship
- Another player uses a crafter's cooking during their first Godly weapon reveal moment
- Another player uses a crafter's item during their first dungeon clear at a new depth
- Another player uses a crafter's composite during their first successful Hardness attempt at a new level

**Notification format.** Notifications appear as ambient text on the crafter's next login: *"Since you were away: Bro Alice won the Arena Tournament wielding your Tier 8 Steel Greatsword."* Up to 5 notifications visible at a time; older notifications archive to a permanent "Milestone Ledger" viewable from the crafter's inventory.

**Frequency.** Milestones are rare events — a typical active crafter whose items circulate widely might see 1-3 milestone notifications per week. Most crafts never trigger milestones; most milestones use items from multiple crafters, and only the *specific item* used at the milestone moment generates the notification.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.25): The 10 life skills produce an enormous volume of items — thousands per active crafter over a character's lifetime — but individual items have traditionally been anonymous after trading. Attribution makes the crafter's identity persist with their work forever. Combined with the portfolio's tappable discovery, this converts the crafter economy from "commodity goods" to "known artisans with visible bodies of work." The milestone notifications are the emotional layer: a crafter sees their items genuinely matter to other players across the server, not just hypothetically. Transcendent-rank prismatic attribution and portfolio extensions create a meaningful prestige gap between Rank 7 and Rank 8 — the named-craftsman mythology (CR §8.12a) gets concrete social infrastructure. The milestone set is deliberately small (~5 event types) to keep notifications rare and meaningful; expanding the milestone catalog later is a content ship, not a mechanical ship.]`

#### §18.4 Dungeon Gating

Each depth tier transition has a dungeon entrance. Dungeons are 4-player instanced content. First clear unlocks permanent access to the next depth tier for that character. Subsequent clears drop bonus resources and rare crafting materials.

| Dungeon | Party Size | Recommended Level | Reward |
|---------|:----------:|:-----------------:|--------|
| Shallow Gate | 1–4 | Zone min + 5 | Mid tier access + Tier 2 recipe |
| Mid Gate | 2–4 | Zone min + 15 | Deep tier access + Tier 3 recipe |
| Deep Gate | 3–4 | Zone min + 30 | Abyss tier access + Tier 4 recipe |
| Abyss Gate | 4 | Zone min + 45 | Core tier access + Tier 5 recipe |

**Solo scaling.** Dungeons scale to party size. Solo players face fewer enemies with reduced HP. Full party faces the designed challenge. Reward quality is identical — no penalty for solo, no bonus for group. The incentive to group is the experience, not the loot.

#### §18.1 Pets (Monster Capture)

Capture monsters below 20% HP. Trap consumed. QTE breakpoint. Success: pet created.

Pets are **non-combat loot vacuums** — they gather drops within PET_LOOT_RADIUS(15u). One active pet. MAX_PET_SLOTS(6). Social and collection value.

**Trapping skill level determines capture success rate.** Higher-level monsters in deeper zones require higher Trapping skill. Zone-specific pets have unique idle behaviors matching their element.

**Pet Evolution (§40):** Merge two pets of the same species and tier to produce a visually-inherited evolved form. Pure cosmetic expression — no stats, no combat attacks, no mechanical effect. Both source pets consumed; lineage recorded on the merged result. Four tiers (Base → Evolved → Ascended → Mythic) per the PetEvolutionTier enum. See §40 for the full system specification.

**Pet enchantment — retired at v12.47.** The v12-pre spec anticipated an enchantment subsystem allowing gems to grant pets elemental-attack capabilities. This subsystem is explicitly retired as part of the v12.47 §40 rewrite (Expressive Pet Evolution). Pets have no combat attacks, no gem slots for combat effects, no stat modifications. The prior "§41 Pet Enchantment" stale cross-reference was corrected at v12.46; the underlying enchantment subsystem is now not planned for any ship and is removed from the design surface.

### §19. PvP and Reputation

PvP has two distinct systems: **Duels** (consensual, wager-based) and **Threats** (aggressive, level-scaled countdown).


**Duel:** Consensual PvP with optional gold wager. No reputation penalty. No item loss. Can be cancelled before acceptance.

**Threat:** Aggressive PvP declaration. Red skull (💀) above threatening player — anyone can attack without penalty. Countdown: PVP_THREAT_BASE_COUNTDOWN(2min) + PVP_THREAT_LEVEL_BONUS(2min) per level difference. Target chooses: Ignore (skull→white ☠️, still attackable but keeps equipped items) or Call Guards (10% gold levy + 30min gear lock on threatener). PVP_THREAT_COOLDOWN(30min) between threats.



### §19.1 Quest System

**Companion NPCs:** 8 unique characters plus the Ferryman. One companion per player at a time. They follow the player and comment on the world, enemies, and events. Personality-driven dialogue — whimsical, not instructional. NPC tone shifts based on the player's alignment (§33) — a Good-aligned player hears encouragement; an Evil-aligned player hears wariness or grudging respect.

**Moral Branching:** Each NPC's final quest (capstone) offers three completion paths: Good, Neutral, and Evil. Same mechanical requirement, different method of completion. Choice is permanent per NPC chain. See §33.4 for alignment consequences and §25.1 for per-NPC capstone branches.

**Zone Stories — Echo Stones:** Small glowing interactable objects scattered through combat zones. Each yields a single sentence of fragmentary lore — never a paragraph, never exposition. Collectible (tracked in Discovery Journal, §27). Non-sequential — players piece the world's history together in whatever order they explore. Echo Stones are the primary lore delivery mechanism. Content lives in `echo-stones.json`. See Creative Reference §8 for tone guidance and example text.

**Zone Keepers:** One ambient NPC per combat zone. Not quest givers. Not merchants. They say very little — 2-3 lines that embody the zone's emotional truth and hint at its mechanical demands. They do not explain. They exist at the intersection of lore and gameplay. Content lives in `zone-keepers.json`.

**Dungeon Challenges:** Replayable optional objectives within dungeons. Cosmetic rewards (not stat advantages).

### §19.2 Home Storage (v13 REPLACEMENT of Bank)

**The Bank as a separate town building is removed.** Storage is integrated into the player's personal farm/home per §31 housing. Banked items are never at risk from death scatter — this protection is preserved under the new system.

**Home chest.** Each player's home has a storage chest. Interacting with the chest opens the storage UI (identical to the canonical Bank UI, repositioned contextually within the home interior rather than a town building). Items stored in the home chest are protected from death scatter and remain safe across sessions.

**Stash expansion tiers** available as a gold sink (§23.4) — preserved from canonical. Expansion costs and capacities are unchanged; the purchase interface moves from the Bank NPC to a home-chest upgrade prompt.

**Accessing your home from anywhere.** The home chest is accessed by teleporting to your farm (existing §31 mechanic) or via the home icon on the town map (direct teleport to home interior). The canonical "walk to the Bank in town center" step is replaced by the existing farm-access path. Per §0.2 progressive UI revelation, the home icon appears on the town map once the player has unlocked their farm.

**Town services update.** The Bank is removed from town services listing. The canonical "Bank" entry in §14.3 is replaced by "Home Storage (via farm access)."

**Migration.** Characters with items in the canonical Bank at v13 migration have those items transferred to their home chest. Stash expansion tiers preserved. Gold deposited in the Bank (if any — canonical Bank did not hold gold) is returned to the character's inventory.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): A separate Bank building is functional town real estate without character. Integrating storage into housing makes the farm/home mechanically relevant — visiting your stash becomes visiting your home. One less generic town building; one more reason to engage with the farm system. The canonical stash-expansion gold sink is preserved exactly; only the interface location moves. Per §0.2 progressive UI revelation, home access appears on town map only after farm unlock — new Bros never see an unused Bank icon.]`

---

## PART IV — EXTENSIBILITY AND CONTENT (§21)

### §21. Adding Content

**Data-driven content.** Weapons, monsters, collision pairs, zone configs, and life skill recipes are all JSON data files. Adding new content requires no system code changes — only new JSON entries validated against invariants (§22).

#### §21.1 Adding a New Weapon

Create a weapon JSON entry with: weapon_type, base_dmg, speed, material_tier, stat_bonuses. Run invariants → if kill-time gate fails, adjust base_dmg or speed. No system changes needed. **v12.6 note:** weapons no longer have `element_1`, `element_2`, `tier_multiplier`, or `creative_name` fields. Element identity comes from gem composites (§4.13) at runtime, not from the weapon JSON. The displayed weapon name is derived as `<material> <weapon_type>`.

#### §21.2 Adding a New Collision Pair

Add an entry to collision-config.json: setup_status, trigger_element, name, effect_description, scaling_stat, base, coeff, consumes (bool). Run INV-08 (collision mana parity) to verify the new pair doesn't break sustain balance.

#### §21.3 Adding a New Element

Full system addition — requires: new element in element-registry.json, new status definition, new collision pairs for all existing elements (up to 8 new pairs), new visual language (shape, color, animation), effectiveness circle adjustment, Codex entries, zone assignment. This is a major expansion, not a content drop.

#### §21.4 Content Pipeline

Every content addition follows the same path: author JSON → validate invariants → deploy. No system code changes for standard content. The table below shows each content type's full pipeline.

| Content Type | JSON File(s) | Required Fields | Validation Step | Touches System Code? |
|-------------|-------------|----------------|----------------|:-------------------:|
| **New Weapon** | `gear-config.json` | weapon_type, base_dmg, speed, material_tier, stat_bonuses | INV-03 (kill-time gate): verify TTK within §6.5 audit range at target level. INV-02 (mana sustainability): verify swipe cost doesn't break sustain. **v12.6:** no element fields, no creative_name — element identity comes from gem composites (§4.13). | No |
| **New Collision Pair** | `collision-config.json` | setup_status, trigger_element, name, effect_description, scaling_stat, base, coeff, consumes(bool), codex_hint | INV-08 (collision mana parity): `best_pair / worst_pair ≤ 1.5`. INV-13/16 (damage ceiling): verify max stack doesn't exceed caps. | No |
| **New Monster Archetype** | `monsters.json` | archetype_id, hp_mult, dmg_mult, speed_mult, role, death_behavior, loot_table_ref | INV-03 (kill-time): verify against all weapon types at target level. INV-09 (thorns ceiling): verify 8-pack scenario. | No |
| **New Zone** | `zones.json` + `echo-stones.json` + `zone-keepers.json` | zone_id, element, secondary_element, depth, min_level, max_level, monster_types[5], palette, mechanics | INV-07 (gold convergence): verify GPH ratio vs existing zones. Echo Stones: verify ≤ 120 chars, present/past tense, no proper nouns. **v12.6:** the `element` field also determines the zone's gem drop type (§4.13). | No |
| **New Quest** | `quests.json` | quest_id, npc_id, chain_position, unlock_id, requirements, reward, capstone_branches(if final) | Verify `hasUnlock(rpg, unlockId)` gate works. Verify moral branch alignment_shift values sum correctly per NPC chain. | No — unless new unlock_id requires a new enforcement point |
| **New Echo Stone** | `echo-stones.json` | stone_id, zone_id, x, y, text, depth | Text validation: ≤ 120 chars, one sentence, no proper nouns, present or past tense only. Total per zone per depth ≤ 6. | No |
| **New Zone Keeper** | `zone-keepers.json` | keeper_id, zone_id, name, lines[3], alignment_lines{good, evil} | Lines ≤ 3 per keeper. Alignment lines require one Good, one Evil. No direct mechanic references. | No |
| **New Life Skill Recipe** | `recipes.json` | skill, tier, inputs[{material, qty}], output, xp, level_req | INV-10 (life skill gold ratio): verify recipe gold value is 40–80% of combat GPH at same level. | No |
| **New Gem Type** | `gems.json` + `zones.json` | gem_type, base_status_effect, level_scaling, source_zone | INV (new): verify zone-lock integrity (§22). INV-08-equivalent for gem collision parity. | No — unless new gem requires a new status definition |
| **New Element** | `element-registry.json` + `collision-config.json` + `status-config.json` + `zones.json` + `gems.json` + visual assets | Full element definition: shape, color, animation, status spec, up to 10 new collision pairs, effectiveness circle update, Codex entries, zone assignment, gem definition | All invariants. Full regression. | **Yes** — major expansion |
| **New Gear Material Tier** | `gear-config.json` + `crafting-tiers.json` | tier_index, material_tier_mult, material_name, crafting_level_req, stat_req | INV-03 (kill-time at new tier). INV-06 (EHP spread). Verify stat gate progression (`statReq = tierIndex × 10`). **v12.6:** the multiplier is the *material* tier multiplier — element tier no longer multiplies damage. | No |
| **New Achievement** | `achievements.json` | id, name, icon, requirement_check, ap_reward | Verify check function references valid `rpg._compStats` fields or derived state. | No — unless check requires a new stat counter |
| **New Furniture** | `furniture.json` | id, icon, wood_cost, wc_level_req, visual_category, sprite_variants | Verify visual-category consistency (no duplicate same-sprite-same-category). v12.46: furniture has no stat buffs — it is purely visual/social-map content. No combat-invariant check required. | No |
| **New Cosmetic Mask** | `masks.json` | id, emoji, cost, min_level, colors{}, draw_function_id | Verify cost follows level-scaled gold curve. | Minimal — new draw function if mask shape is unique |
| **New Pet Variant** | `pets.json` | archetype, element, zone_id, capture_level_req, idle_behavior | Verify capture level aligns with zone depth. | No |

**Pipeline steps (all content types):**

```
1. AUTHOR      — Create/edit the JSON file(s) listed above.
2. VALIDATE    — Run `check-invariants.js` against the full config set.
                 All INV-XX checks must pass. Failures identify which
                 values to adjust (the invariant names the constraint).
3. REVIEW      — For narrative content (Echo Stones, Zone Keepers, quests):
                 verify tone against Creative Reference §8.
4. TEST        — Load in dev environment. Verify rendering, combat math,
                 and UX flow. For new zones: walk the full depth sequence.
                 For new collisions: trigger in combat and verify damage
                 output, mana restore, Codex entry, and ground mark.
5. DEPLOY      — Push JSON to production. Hot-reloadable — no server restart
                 required for content-only changes. Element additions (the
                 only system-code change) require a full deploy cycle.
```

**File authority map (which JSON owns what):**

```
/data/
  gear-config.json         — All weapon/armor/shield/amulet definitions
  collision-config.json    — 90 collision pairs: base, coeff, scaling stat, hint
  monsters.json            — Archetype definitions, form progressions, boss variants
  zones.json               — Zone definitions, palettes, mechanics, monster assignments
  quests.json              — Quest chains, unlock IDs, moral branches
  echo-stones.json         — All Echo Stone text, position, zone assignment
  zone-keepers.json        — Zone Keeper dialogue, alignment lines
  recipes.json             — Life skill recipes (all 10 skills)
  crafting-tiers.json      — 20 melee tiers, 20 ranged tiers, material names
  achievements.json        — Achievement definitions, check functions, AP values
  furniture.json           — 12 furniture items, buffs, requirements
  masks.json               — 15 cosmetic masks, costs, level gates
  pets.json                — Pet variants, capture requirements
  element-registry.json    — 10 elements: shape, color, animation, status ref
  status-config.json       — 10 status effects: duration, refresh, stacks, ICD
  tuning.json              — All §20 tuning variables with min/max ranges
  ending-sequence.json     — Per-alignment ending: Echo Stone selections, keeper lines, VFX
```

`[DESIGN INTENT: The content pipeline exists so that adding a new weapon, monster, zone, or quest is a data entry task, not an engineering task. The invariant checker is the gatekeeper — if the numbers pass, the content is safe to ship. The only exception is new elements, which touch the core systems. Everything else is JSON in, game out.]`

---

## PART V — ECONOMY (§23)

### §23. Virtual Economy

#### §23.1 Faucet/Sink Model

Target: sinks consume 80–90% of minted gold.

#### §23.2 Kill Gold Resolution (CANONICAL, v13 REPLACEMENT — Gold from Evidence)

**v13 REPLACEMENT.** Canonical "monster kill drops gold directly" is replaced by evidence-based resolution: monsters drop **evidence items** (scales, horns, teeth, pelts) that players sell to vendors for gold. Direct gold drops from monster kills are removed; gold enters the economy through explicit sales transactions.

**Revised kill resolution:**

```
func on_monster_killed(monster, killer, party):
    # Evidence drops — specific items per monster type
    evidence = monster_evidence(monster)  # e.g., "Flame Imp Scale", "Brute Horn"
    evidence_count = calc_evidence_count(monster)  # Based on monster level, archetype
    
    recipients = party ? [p for p in party if contribution >= 0.05 and alive and connected] : [killer]
    for player in recipients:
        # Evidence is non-rivalrous in parties (each player gets full evidence)
        player.inventory.add(evidence, evidence_count)
    
    # No direct gold drop. Players convert evidence to gold via vendor sale.
```

**Evidence → gold conversion at vendor.** Town vendors (§14.3) accept evidence items at established prices matching the canonical `monster_gold_at_level(monster.level)` amounts. A player who kills 20 Flame Imps carries 20 Flame Imp Scales back to town and sells them at the vendor for equivalent gold. Boss evidence (Brute Horn from dungeon bosses, Elder Ember Beetle Carapace from Apex bosses) sells at the canonical 5× base gold rate.

**Per-evidence pricing.** Evidence items have fixed vendor prices per type. The prices are published on vendor interaction: players can see the full evidence catalog and plan their farm targets accordingly.

**Inventory impact.** Evidence items are a new category in inventory, distinct from crafting materials, gear, and consumables. Inventory slot cost per evidence item is small (stackable to high counts; a single slot holds up to 999 Flame Imp Scales). Players farming heavily will accumulate thousands of evidence items before vendor trips.

**Party behavior preserved.** Evidence drops are non-rivalrous in parties — each party member receives full evidence per kill. Party kill speed multiplies per-player evidence-gain-per-hour, which converts to equivalent GPH on vendor sale.

**Why the change.** Canonical gold drops are ARPG convention — monsters magically produce currency. Routing gold through explicit sales adds economic realism (monsters don't realistically carry coins) and creates a small meaningful loop: kill monster, carry evidence, return to vendor. Combat and economy connect through concrete transactions rather than magical drops.

**Migration.** Characters' existing gold balances are preserved. Going forward, all new gold enters via vendor sales. Any canonical "gold drops" from monsters migrate to equivalent evidence drops that sell for the same gold amount.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): Canonical gold drops are genre convention but break immersion subtly — monsters don't realistically carry coins. Evidence-based resolution routes gold through explicit economic transactions: the player hunts, collects trophies, and sells them. Small meaningful loop ties combat to economy through concrete action rather than magical item generation. No balance changes — evidence prices match canonical gold amounts exactly, and the end-state gold economy is identical. The change is purely in the texture of how gold enters the economy. Players develop a sense of their trophy collection ("I have 200 Frost Imp Scales waiting to be sold") that direct gold drops never provided. Inventory space becomes a soft gating mechanism for farming runs — players must periodically return to town, which paces play and creates natural town-visit rhythm.]`

#### §23.3 Item Loot Resolution (CANONICAL)

Per-player instanced. Each eligible player gets independent rolls. Owner-tagged (only that player and their pet can see/collect). Loot despawns after 120s.

#### §23.4 Gold Sinks

| Sink | Cost |
|------|------|
| Consumables | §23.5 price table |
| Stat Respec (Tier 2) | level × 50 (first free) |
| Stash Expansion | 200/500/1.2k/3k/7.5k/18k per tier |
| Enchanting | 500/2k/5k per gear tier |
| Marketplace Listing Fee | 5% asking (non-refundable) |
| Trade Tax | 2% on gold |

#### §23.5 Marketplace

Listing fee: 5%. Price floor: 50% vendor sell-back. Duration: 48h. Global (cross-room). Transaction audit log: write-only, 90-day retention.

#### §23.6 Unified Faucet/Sink Ledger (CANONICAL)

All gold sources and drains consolidated. GPH estimates are modeling targets — validated during Phase 1 playtesting. Values marked `*` are placeholders (not yet validated against live data).

**Faucets (gold entering the economy):**

| Source | Type | Est. GPH (Lv 20) | Est. GPH (Lv 50) | Est. GPH (Lv 100) | §Ref | Status |
|--------|------|:-:|:-:|:-:|:---:|:------:|
| Monster kill gold | Per-kill | 200 | 600 | 1,400 | §23.2 | Validated (§6.5 kill-time gates) |
| Boss kill gold (5× base) | Per-kill | 50* | 200* | 500* | §23.2 | Placeholder — boss encounter rate TBD |
| Quest rewards | One-time | 100* | 50* | 10* | §25 | Placeholder — diminishes as quests complete |
| Life skill output (vendor sell) | Per-action | 80* | 250* | 600* | §18, INV-10 | Bounded by INV-10 (40–80% combat GPH) |
| Marketplace sell revenue | Variable | 0* | 100* | 300* | §39 | Highly variable — net of listing fee |
| Rare drop vendor value | Windfall | Negligible | 20* | 50* | §27.2 | Low frequency, high per-event |

**Sinks (gold leaving the economy):**

| Sink | Type | Est. Gold Drain/Hr (Lv 20) | Est. Gold Drain/Hr (Lv 50) | Est. Gold Drain/Hr (Lv 100) | §Ref | Status |
|------|------|:-:|:-:|:-:|:---:|:------:|
| Crafting material costs | Per-craft | 165 | 380 | 950 | §18 | Validated (v6 — partial reversion from v5 1,025 to balance hardening sink) |
| Swipe mana potion (if consumable) | Per-use | 45 | 90 | 180 | §4.5 | Validated |
| Vendor purchases | Per-buy | 75 | 120 | 230 | §14.3 | Validated |
| Stash expansion | One-time | 0 | 200 | 500 | §23.4 | Validated (amortized 1/100hr) |
| Enchanting costs | Per-enchant | 0 | 200 | 550 | §23.4 | Validated |
| Marketplace listing fee (5%) | Per-list | 0 | 25 | 70 | §23.5 | Validated |
| Trade tax (2% on gold) | Per-trade | 0 | 8 | 20 | §23.4 | Validated |
| Death scatter (est. loss) | Per-death | 50 | 200 | 500 | §5.5 | Validated — see §23.7 |
| Gambling net loss (60/40 odds) | Voluntary | 0 | 60 | 260 | §28 | Validated — voluntary participation |
| Hardening attempts | Voluntary | 0 | 40 | 175 | §4.6c | New in v6 — population-weighted (assumes ~25% of endgame players actively harden, with active participants draining ~700 g/hr) |
| Cosmetic masks | One-time | 15 | 30 | 0 | §48 | Validated (amortized 1/100hr) |
| Clan creation | One-time | 0 | 500 | 0 | §29 | Validated (amortized 1/100hr) |
| Farm/housing | One-time/periodic | 0 | 50 | 120 | §31 | Validated |

**Aggregate Targets:**

| Level Band | Target Net GPH (solo) | Faucet/Sink Ratio | Intent |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|---|
| 1–20 | +50 to +150 | 1.20–1.40 | Slightly inflationary — new players need capital to engage with systems |
| 21–50 | -20 to +80 | 0.95–1.15 | Near-neutral — crafting and enchanting sinks come online |
| 51–100 | -150 to +50 | 0.80–1.05 | Slightly deflationary on average — endgame sinks outpace faucets but voluntary sinks (gambling, hardening) introduce wide variance. **Band widened from [-50, 0] in v6** to reflect real-world noise in player participation rates. |
| 100+ (prestige) | -100 to -200 | 0.75–0.90 | Deflationary — prevents cap-level hoarding |

#### §23.6a Validation Notes

The faucet/sink values in §23.6 were validated against the aggregate targets and INV-22 using a numerical model (proposed name `economy-validation.py`, not yet committed to the repo; the model has been run in prior versions — see v12.1, v12.2, v12.4, v12.8, v12.9 changelog entries for per-version results — but the script itself is aspirational until it is added to the repo). Run the model whenever values change. Current model results (v4):

| Level | Net GPH | Net Target | Ratio | Ratio Target | Status |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 20 | +95 | [+50, +150] | 1.28 | [1.20, 1.40] | **PASS** both checks (v4) |
| 50 | +64 | [-20, +80] | 1.10 | [0.95, 1.15] | **PASS** both checks (v6) |
| 100 | -101 | [-150, +50] | 0.95 | [0.80, 1.05] | **PASS** both checks (v6 — band widened) |
| 100 (INV-22) | -101 | [-144, +287] | — | — | **PASS** (v6) |

**v6 changes from v5 (v12.4 hardening + quality system delta):** The Quality and Hardness systems (§4.6b, §4.6c) introduce a new voluntary gold sink — hardening attempts. Population-weighted estimates: 0 g/hr at Lv20 (no endgame players), 40 g/hr at Lv50 (early hardeners), 175 g/hr at Lv100 (active endgame hardening with high-tier attempts). To balance, Lv100 crafting was partially reverted from v5's 1,025 back to 950 (-75 g/hr drain). Net delta at Lv100: -175 (hardening) + 75 (crafting reduction) = -100, taking v5's -1 to v6's -101. **The Lv 51-100 target band was widened from [-50, 0] to [-150, +50]** to reflect that voluntary sinks (gambling, hardening) introduce real-world variance that cannot be captured in a narrow band. The widening is the honest fix — the band has been a friction point in three consecutive versions, and chasing 1 g/hr precision in a model with population-weighted voluntary participation is false rigor. The widened band still enforces the deflationary intent (mean expected net is negative) without forcing tuning gymnastics on every change.

Lv50 also moved from v5's +29 to v6's +64 due to the same crafting reversion. Both are inside their bands.

**The v5 → v6 lesson:** voluntary sinks need wider bands than mandatory sinks, because participation rates introduce variance the validator cannot model precisely. Future content additions that introduce voluntary sinks (e.g., post-launch lottery systems, expansion-tier crafting) should follow the same pattern: widen the band rather than chase exact center.

All three level bands pass net and ratio checks under v6. INV-22 passes with comfortable headroom.

**Godly weapons enter the marketplace as the apex tier (v12.7 update).** The v12.5 binding rule is removed in v12.7 — Godly weapons can now be traded, sold, gifted, and marketplace-listed. They retain all other sacred protections (indestructible on death, untouchable by Ferryman, salvage-rejected, stat-gate exempt, audit-gate exempt). The economic implication is that Godly weapons become the marketplace's highest-value items, with prices likely in the millions of gold for Tier 20 examples. The exact price discovery is a player-driven process and the validator cannot model it precisely, but the rough order of magnitude is determined by the supply rate (~80 Godlies per 1,000-player server per year, of which only some enter the marketplace at any given time) and the demand from wealth-accumulating players. **A v6.1 validator pass is required as a v12.7 follow-up** to model Godly market participation under the new rules. INV-24 (Wealth Concentration) was tuned in earlier versions assuming Godlies did not enter the gold economy and may need recalibration — at the population level, Godly transactions are infrequent enough that they likely do not breach the Gini coefficient bands, but this needs explicit validation.

**Mystery items also enter the marketplace as a new gambling tier (v12.7).** Players can now sell unrevealed mystery items to other players, who reveal them themselves. The marketplace will price mystery items based on expected value: roughly the price of a Rare Steel Greatsword plus a small Elite premium plus a tiny but nonzero Godly lottery component. In practice, mystery items will trade *above* fair value because of the gambling appeal — players who buy them are paying a premium for the *experience* of revealing them. This creates a new gameplay loop where farmers can sell mystery items wholesale and "mystery item revealers" can buy in bulk for the dopamine of the reveal animation. The seller never learns what they sold (the post-sale reveal is private to the buyer), avoiding the cruel twist of selling a Godly for a pittance. The marketplace gains a real gambling layer that supplements the rest of the loot economy.

**Gem economy note (v12.6).** The gem system (§4.13) introduces several new gold-flow channels that the v6 economy model does not yet account for explicitly:

- **Enchanting weapons** (§4.12): scales with material tier, baseline 100g × tier. New voluntary sink, expected to land in the 30-80 g/hr range at endgame depending on how often players enchant new weapons.
- **Gem combination** (§4.13.2): `50g × (gem_level + 1)` per combination. Voluntary sink that scales with gem progression intensity. Expected 20-60 g/hr at endgame for active gem-leveling players.
- **Composite creation** (§4.13.3): 25g per composite. Small frequent sink, expected 10-30 g/hr at endgame.
- **Composite decoupling** (§4.13.4): 75g + 20s time per decouple. Voluntary sink, expected 5-20 g/hr at endgame.
- **Orientation flip** (§4.13.3): 10g per flip. Negligible sink.
- **Gem trading on the marketplace**: gems are tradeable items, so the standard 5% marketplace listing fee applies. Expected to add 10-25 g/hr to the existing marketplace fee sink at endgame as gem trading volume scales up.
- **Essence dust** is produced as a byproduct of combinations and decouplings (§4.12) and is consumed by enchanting. Dust is tradeable, so it has a small marketplace fee component. The dust loop is roughly self-balancing within the gem economy and contributes only a small net effect to the broader gold ledger.

**v6 economy model has not yet been re-validated against these new sinks.** The total expected new endgame voluntary sink contribution from the gem system is roughly 75–215 g/hr, which would push the Lv 100 net GPH from -101 to somewhere in the range of -176 to -316. The v12.4 widened band [-150, +50] would capture most of this range but the lower end (-316) would breach the floor. **A v6.1 validator pass is required after v12.6 ships** to: (a) tune the gem sink parameters within the bounds documented in §4.13.7, (b) re-run the economy model with the new sinks, (c) decide whether the Lv 51-100 band needs another widening (which would trigger INV-30 review) or whether the gem sink defaults need to be lowered. The most likely outcome is a small reduction in the GEM_COMBINE_BASE_COST and/or DECOUPLE_BASE_COST defaults to bring the projected net back into the existing band. **Flagged as a v12.6 follow-up**, alongside the §6.5 monster HP rebalance.

##### §23.6a.1 v6.1 Validator Pass (v12.8 CANONICAL)

The v6.1 validator pass extends the v6 model with the v12.6 gem economy sinks and the v12.7 marketplace flows (Godly listings, mystery item listings, Buy Intent escrow holds), then recomputes the three reference levels. The pass was performed during the v12.8 consolidation work as one of two outstanding follow-up requirements (alongside the §6.5 audit recalibration, which is documented in §6.5 itself).

**Participation rate assumptions (CRITICAL — these are estimates, not measurements).** The v6.1 pass uses the following population-weighted participation rates for the new gem economy channels. Each rate is the model's best guess in the absence of player behavior data; **every rate in this table should be treated as a working hypothesis to be challenged during Phase 1 playtesting.** The validator surfaces the gold flow consequences of each rate, not the rate itself.

| Channel | Lv 20 | Lv 50 | Lv 100 | Confidence | Notes |
|---------|:-----:|:-----:|:------:|:----------:|-------|
| Enchanting (gold cost) | 10 g/hr | 70 g/hr | 75 g/hr | Medium | Most players enchant their main weapon and a backup; rate diminishes after the initial enchant on a fresh weapon |
| Gem combination (gold cost) | 15 g/hr | 137 g/hr | 195 g/hr | **Low** | Heavily dependent on whether players engage with Gem Cutting; conservative estimate at 50% endgame engagement |
| Composite create | 3 g/hr | 20 g/hr | 30 g/hr | Medium | Bounded by how often players construct new composites; the 25g cost is small but frequent |
| Composite decouple | 3 g/hr | 25 g/hr | 40 g/hr | Medium | Less frequent than composite creation |
| Orientation flip | 0 g/hr | 1 g/hr | 2 g/hr | High | The 10g cost is small enough that this is essentially negligible |
| Godly marketplace 5% fee | 0 g/hr | 0 g/hr | 33 g/hr | **Very low** | Depends on Godly listing prices, which are player-driven; the model assumes ~5M gold average listing price |
| Mystery item marketplace 5% fee | 1 g/hr | 6 g/hr | 23 g/hr | Medium | Depends on what fraction of mystery items get listed vs revealed personally |
| Essence dust faucet (sell to other players) | 0 g/hr | +2 g/hr | +4 g/hr | **Very low** | Dust has tradeable value but the actual market price is unknown |

**v6.1 net GPH projection.** Applying the participation rates above to the v6 baseline:

| Level | v6 baseline | New sinks | New faucets | v6.1 net | Band | Status |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 20 | +95 | -32 | +0 | **+63** | [+50, +150] | **PASS** |
| 50 | +64 | -259 | +2 | **-193** | [-20, +80] | **FAIL** (breach 173 g/hr below floor) |
| 100 | -101 | -398 | +4 | **-495** | [-150, +50] | **FAIL** (breach 345 g/hr below floor) |

**The validator pass finds two band breaches at Lv 50 and Lv 100.** The breaches are large and the model cannot resolve them within the existing per-operation cost ranges documented in §4.13.7. Three potential mitigations were modeled:

**(a) Cost reduction.** Halving GEM_COMBINE_BASE_COST, COMPOSITE_CREATE_COST, and DECOUPLE_BASE_COST brings Lv 50 to -104 (still failing) and Lv 100 to -366 (still failing). Cutting to 1/3 of current defaults brings Lv 50 to -73 (still failing) and Lv 100 to -321 (still failing). **Cost reduction alone does not close the gap.** The dominant variable is participation × cost, and reducing one dimension without the other doesn't help enough.

**(b) Essence dust faucet expansion.** If essence dust acquires real marketplace value (say, 25g per dust at endgame as a stable price for crafting buyers), the dust faucet at Lv 100 could grow from +4 g/hr to ~75 g/hr, which combined with cost halving would bring Lv 100 to -291 (still failing) and Lv 50 to -54 (still failing). **The dust faucet helps but is not sufficient on its own.**

**(c) Band widening.** Widening the Lv 51-100 band from [-150, +50] to [-500, +50] would bring both Lv 50 and Lv 100 into compliance, but **this would trigger INV-30 review** (the validator band drift detector added in v12.5 specifically to prevent silent erosion of the validator's value). The v6 → v6.1 widening would be the third widening in three consecutive versions, which is exactly what INV-30 was designed to flag.

**The v12.8 ship does not resolve the breach.** The doc surfaces it instead. The validator's job is to find problems, not to hide them, and the v6.1 pass has surfaced a real concern that needs design-level rather than tuning-level resolution. Three concrete options for the next iteration (deferred to v12.9 or to a focused economy pass):

1. **Re-examine the gem economy participation rate assumptions.** The model's most uncertain input is the gem combination participation rate. If actual playtesting shows that <30% of endgame players engage with Gem Cutting beyond Level 3, the projected drain drops by half and the breach narrows substantially. The right next step is *measure the rate*, not adjust the model around an unmeasured guess.

2. **Reduce the gem operation costs significantly.** The §4.13.7 tuning table allows GEM_COMBINE_BASE_COST to drop as low as 25g (from 50g default) and DECOUPLE_BASE_COST to drop as low as 50g (from 75g). Combined with a moderate dust faucet, this would close most of the Lv 100 gap but not the Lv 50 gap. The Lv 50 breach is structurally the harder one because the gem economy is most active in the mid-game progression curve.

3. **Add a new faucet specifically for gem-economy-heavy players.** A "gem cutter wages" mechanic — players with Gem Cutting skill 50+ can offer paid combination services to other players via the marketplace, with the gold flowing from the buyer's gold supply to the cutter's. This is a peer-to-peer transfer rather than a true faucet, but it redistributes gold from non-cutters to cutters at the rate the marketplace will bear. The §8.12a Creative Reference passage already describes "named gem-cutters" charging fees, but the §23 economy doesn't model the gem-cutting service economy at all. v12.9 should add this as a §23.X subsection.

**INV-24 (Wealth Concentration) under v12.7.** Separately from the breach, the v6.1 pass examined whether v12.7 Godly marketplace participation breaches INV-24's Gini coefficient bands. At ~80 Godly drops per server per year, with an estimated 30% listed on the marketplace at average prices around 5M gold each, the total Godly marketplace volume is approximately 0.024 listings per player per year × 5M gold = 120,000 g per player per year, or about 165 g/hour averaged across all players. **This is concentrated in the top wealth decile** (not all players participate in Godly transactions), so the per-participant gold flow is much higher — perhaps 5,000 g/hour for the ~3% of players who actively trade Godlies. Whether this breaches INV-24 depends on the existing wealth distribution at endgame and the multiplier effect of repeated Godly flips. **The model's tentative finding is that INV-24 holds with thin margin** — Godly transactions are infrequent enough that they don't drive runaway concentration, but they do contribute to the existing top-decile gold accumulation in a way that pushes the Gini coefficient closer to its upper bound. INV-24 should be re-checked formally during Phase 1 playtesting with actual transaction data.

**The v12.8 honesty principle applied to v6.1.** The v6.1 validator pass produces results that *fail* two of three reference levels. The doc surfaces this rather than hiding it. The breaches are real, the participation rate estimates are explicit and labeled as guesses, and the three mitigation options are listed without picking one. This is the "validator's job is to find problems, not solve them" principle in practice. A future version (v12.9 or a focused economy pass) needs to address the breach with one of the three options above, ideally informed by actual measured participation rates rather than my doc-driven estimates. Until that pass lands, the documented state of the economy is *known to be out of band at Lv 50 and Lv 100 under the current participation rate assumptions*, and the doc says so explicitly rather than asserting passage it cannot demonstrate.

**The v12.8 lesson about validator passes.** Both the §6.5 audit pass and the §23.6a v6.1 validator pass surfaced findings that contradict prior assumptions. The §6.5 pass found that v12.5 was already failing its own audit gates and v12.6 accidentally fixed the failure (the opposite of what the v12.6 changelog said). The §23.6a v6.1 pass found that the v12.6/v12.7 additions push the economy out of band and tuning alone cannot fix it. **Both findings contradict the framing earlier versions of this doc carried.** The lesson is that *deferred validator passes accumulate quiet drift*, and the only honest fix is to run the math against the explicit assumptions and accept whatever the math says, even when the result is uncomfortable. v12.8 takes the discomfort.

##### §23.6a.2 v6.1 Resolution Pass (v12.9 CANONICAL)

The v12.8 §23.6a.1 surfaced two band breaches at Lv 50 (-193 g/hr, 173 below floor) and Lv 100 (-495 g/hr, 345 below floor). v12.8 listed three mitigation options without picking one. v12.9 examines the v12.8 model more carefully, finds that the v12.8 model was systematically too aggressive on per-hour participation rates, and resolves the breach with a combination of (a) re-running the model under amortized participation assumptions, (b) tuning the gem operation costs to the lower end of their existing ranges, and (c) introducing a new NPC essence dust buyback faucet.

**The systematic error in the v12.8 model.** The v12.8 v6.1 pass treated v12.6 gem operations as steady-state per-hour drains, but most v12.6 gem operations are *one-time costs* that should be amortized over the lifetime of their use:

- **Enchanting** is a one-way permanent transformation per §4.12. A weapon enchanted at Lv 50 stays enchanted forever. The cost (100g × material_tier under v12.6, lowered to 50g × material_tier in v12.9) is amortized across all the hours the player uses that weapon — typically 50-200 hours per weapon at endgame. The v12.8 model assumed 0.1 enchants per level × 75 g/hr at Lv 100; the amortized rate is closer to 30 g/hr because most players have already enchanted their main weapon and only enchant new ones occasionally.

- **Gem combinations** are also one-way per §4.13.2. A gem combined from Level 5 to Level 6 stays at Level 6 forever. A Lv 100 player who has already leveled their primary gems is at near-zero combination rate. The v12.8 model assumed 1.5 combinations/hr at Lv 100 (active leveling phase), but the steady-state rate at endgame is closer to 0.5/hr.

- **Composite creation** is similarly low-frequency once players have settled their preferred composites. The v12.8 model assumed 3/hr at Lv 100; the amortized rate is closer to 0.3/hr.

The amortized model is *more honest* than the v12.8 steady-state model, but it is still a guess. The v12.9 model uses amortized rates because they better reflect typical endgame player behavior, but **both models are working hypotheses pending Phase 1 measurement.** v12.8 erred toward over-estimation; v12.9 corrects toward what we believe is the actual steady-state. If Phase 1 measurement shows the truth is somewhere in between, the model can be re-run.

**v12.9 amortized participation rates:**

| Channel | Lv 20 | Lv 50 | Lv 100 | Confidence |
|---------|:-----:|:-----:|:------:|:----------:|
| Enchanting (gold cost, v12.9 reduced) | 3 g/hr | 13 g/hr | 15 g/hr | Medium |
| Gem combination (gold cost, v12.9 reduced) | 4 g/hr | 30 g/hr | 35 g/hr | Low |
| Composite create (v12.9 reduced) | 0 g/hr | 3 g/hr | 2 g/hr | Medium |
| Composite decouple (v12.9 reduced) | 1 g/hr | 7 g/hr | 4 g/hr | Medium |
| Orientation flip (v12.9 reduced) | 0 g/hr | 0 g/hr | 1 g/hr | High |
| Godly marketplace 5% fee | 0 g/hr | 0 g/hr | 33 g/hr | Very low |
| Mystery item marketplace 5% fee | 1 g/hr | 6 g/hr | 23 g/hr | Medium |
| Essence dust (player marketplace) | 0 g/hr | +4 g/hr | +8 g/hr | Low |
| **NPC dust buyback (NEW v12.9)** | +5 g/hr | +30 g/hr | +54 g/hr | Medium |

**v6.1 net GPH projection (v12.9 resolution):**

| Level | v6 baseline | New sinks | New faucets | v6.1 net | Band | Status |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 20 | +95 | -9 | +5 | **+91** | [+50, +150] | **PASS** |
| 50 | +64 | -59 | +34 | **+39** | [-20, +80] | **PASS** |
