They show collected title count (e.g., "47 titles earned") and highlight the player's active + showcase selections. The §28 Gambling system's AP-related features (canonical: some gambling modes use AP as entry requirements) are migrated to title-count requirements (e.g., "requires 10+ titles collected" instead of "requires 50+ AP").

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): AP as a scored numerical reward is conventional ARPG accounting. Titles as identity layers fit Brotown's Unlikely Hero frame (§33.3, §53.3) better — Bros don't have "scores," they have *identities*. Converting AP to titles moves the acknowledgment surface from a number to a list of specific things the player did. Title authoring per §0.1 tonal rule ensures titles aren't all solemn — "The Hopeful Chef" earned after burning 50 dinners is a title the game awards with warm mockery, not somber commendation. The migration path preserves existing achievement history as Journey Log lore without breaking characters' accumulated play.]`

#### §27.4 Journey Log (CANONICAL, v13 ADDITIVE — Prose Format + Tonal Range + Inspect Excerpts)

Auto-generated chronological timeline of milestone events. The Journey Log is the Unlikely Hero narrative made legible — the player can scroll back through their entire arc from first kill to ritual completion (§57).

**Milestone events logged (with timestamp):**

| Category | Events |
|----------|--------|
| Combat | First kill, first death, first Grand Slam, first boss kill |
| Zones | First visit to each zone (66 entries over full progression — 64 standard + 2 endgame, see §6.10) |
| Collisions | First discovery of each collision pair (36 entries) |
| Quests | Each NPC quest completion, with register path noted (Responsible/Mischievous/Cool/Ruthless per §25.1 v13) |
| Dungeons | First clear of each dungeon |
| Progression | Every 10 levels (L10, L20... L100), Ascendant title, post-100 milestones |
| Alignment | Each register increment event, dominant-register changes |
| Narrative | Ferryman first visit, Ferryman quest unlock (if applicable), ending trigger, title bestowed |
| Endgame | Guardian zone entries, Convergence entry, first Convergence gem, ritual-chamber entry (§57), Soul Fragment creation (§57.5), Fragment visits (§57.7), Unfinished Fragment produced if walkout (§57.2c), Hall of Fragments first visit (§57.8) |
| Stat Training | Per-session recap on logout (now delivered as session letter per §1.6 v13 — short letter from character to themselves) |
| Stat Locks | Each lock toggle event, with NPC witness and stat name at time of lock |
| Farm/Minigames (v13) | Per minigame session with narrative flavor entry |
| First-Use Techniques (v13) | First use of each dodge variant (per §5.8), first-species pet captures, etc. |

**v13 ADDITIVE — Prose narrative format.** Entries are written in narrative prose rather than event-log format. The `display_text` field is generated with tonal range per §0.1:

- Grave: *"The Tidal Caves gave you their greatest gift. You stood in the deepest pool and the water did not move for you."*
- Wry: *"Tried to harden. Failed. The Blacksmith looked sad for me."*
- Self-deprecating: *"Died in Lawless. Everyone watched. I'd like to pretend that didn't happen."*
- Warm-observational: *"First capture of a Venom Imp. It looked surprised, then accepting. You named it something ridiculous."*

The prose format is the default. A toggle in settings allows reverting to event-log format for players who prefer terse entries.

**v14.0.5 REMOVAL — Evolving entries cut.** The prior v13 spec included an evolving-entries feature: entries older than 200 in-game hours would self-rewrite during quiet server-time moments into a more reflective retrospective voice, with the original event data preserved but the narrative prose updated. A `display_text_evolved` field in the data model stored the rewritten prose; a per-entry UI action "read evolved version" surfaced it. The v14.0.5 ship removes this entire feature. The rationale: the Journey Log's emotional premise is that the game *remembers what actually happened to you*, and generated prose evolution replaces the player's memory of a moment with a machine's reflection on the moment. The evolved-form example the prior spec used — *"you don't remember its face anymore — imps look the same after a while"* — is a factual claim about the player's inner state that the game cannot actually know. If the player does remember, the generated reflection is a lie the game told them about their own history. If the player doesn't remember, the generated reflection is the game replacing their memory with fiction. Rereading an entry and having it gain new meaning as the player changes around it is what rereading *is*; auto-evolution performs the rereading *for* the player and undermines the memoir-authorship relationship the Log is structured to give the player. Cut entirely. The `display_text_evolved` field is removed from the data model (see revised data model below). The per-entry UI action "read evolved version (if available)" is removed. The periodic background rewriting process is not implemented. All other Journey Log features are preserved: prose format at entry-creation, tonal variants selected per player context, inspect card excerpts, pin-as-highlight, milestone non-pruning.

**v13 ADDITIVE — Inspect card excerpts.** Partial excerpts from the player's Journey Log are visible on their inspect card — the most recent 3 entries plus 3 highlighted milestones (player's choice of which to pin). Viewers who inspect the player read these snippets as a micro-biography. Privacy preserved: canonical "Show Journey Log" toggle remains for full-log visibility; excerpt visibility is default-on (opt-out available).

**Data model (v13 REVISED, v14.0.5 UPDATED):**

```
JourneyEntry {
    timestamp: ISO datetime,
    category: String,       // combat/zone/collision/quest/dungeon/progression/alignment/narrative/endgame/farm/technique
    event_id: String,       // unique event key
    display_text: String,   // human-readable narrative prose (v13)
    display_text_variants: Array<String>, // v13: multiple tonal variants selected per context
    metadata: Object|null,  // optional metadata
    pinned_excerpt: Boolean // v13: true if player pinned this as an inspect-card highlight
}
```

The `display_text_evolved` field is removed in v14.0.5. Entries are immutable after creation — no field in a JourneyEntry is rewritten after the entry is first written.

**Storage:** `localStorage.bt_journey` — JSON array of JourneyEntry objects, append-only. Capped at JOURNEY_MAX_ENTRIES(500). At cap, oldest non-milestone entries are pruned (milestone entries — first kill, endings, Soul Fragment creation per §57.5 — are never pruned).

**UI:** Accessed via 📖 button (shared with Encyclopedia). Per §0.2 progressive UI revelation, the Journey Log icon appears at character creation with a single initial "You washed up" entry. Scrollable chronological list. Entries color-coded by category. Per-entry actions: pin as inspect excerpt, share to friend.

**Implementation:** Event listeners on existing handlers. Prose generation happens at entry-creation time using templates per category + tonal variant selection. No post-creation entry rewriting; entries are immutable after write.

**Social.** Canonical "Show Journey Log" toggle preserved for full-log privacy. v13 adds inspect-excerpt default visibility (overridable per setting).

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): Canonical event-log format is informational — "First visited Frozen Shore" conveys the fact. Prose format conveys the *experience* — "The Frozen Shore gave you a memory of cold that has never left." The Journey Log becomes literally the player's memoir of their character's life. Per §0.1 tonal rule, prose variants mix grave and wry registers so the memoir reflects Brotown's native voice rather than uniform solemnity. Evolving entries add temporal depth — long-lived characters' oldest memories are written in the voice of a character looking back, which itself is a progression reward. Inspect excerpts make the Journey Log socially legible at a glance — you can tell who someone is not by their stats but by what the game remembers about them.]`

### §28. Gambling System

The Gambling Den is a town building (§14.3) that offers the game's canonical gold-sink mechanics. Under v12.34, the den rotates across four time-of-day event modes, each using the same tonal-phase boundaries established by §14.3.3 Town Time-of-Day Variation. The underlying economic property is preserved across all four modes: the house captures 40% gold equivalent on average (per the §28.1 DESIGN INTENT), ensuring gambling remains a net gold sink regardless of which mode is active.

#### §28.1 Double or Nothing

GAMBLE_WIN_CHANCE(0.40). Wager selector (10-10,000g). Instant resolution. Full stat tracking.

`[DESIGN INTENT: 60/40 house odds = net gold sink. Players who are gold-rich have a place to burn it that feels like a choice.]`

#### §28.2 Weekly Jackpot

Server-side pool (Supabase). Players deposit (JACKPOT_MIN_DEPOSIT = 50g). JACKPOT_HOUSE_CUT(0.10). Random winner weekly. Creates community event + additional gold sink.

#### §28.3 Time-of-Day Event Modes (v12.34 CANONICAL)

The Gambling Den rotates across four event modes keyed to server-time tonal phases (§14.3.3). Each mode has a distinct format, atmosphere, and participating-player density; the 40% house edge is preserved across all four via different distribution structures.

**Mode phase boundaries.** The event modes use the same hour boundaries as the §14.3.3 Town Time-of-Day phases, ensuring the Den's mode transition coincides with the town's tonal transition. A player who enters the Den at 16:55 sees the afternoon mode; at 17:00, the evening mode begins alongside the town's golden-hour ambient shift.

| Server Time | Town Phase | Gambling Mode |
|-------------|:-----------|:---------------|
| 06:00-11:00 | Morning | Quiet Individual (base mode) |
| 11:00-17:00 | Afternoon | Dealer's Hour |
| 17:00-21:00 | Evening | House Tournament |
| 21:00-06:00 | Late Night | The Quiet Room |

**§28.3.1 Morning — Quiet Individual Gambling.**

The base Double or Nothing mechanic (§28.1) is the exclusive format. No named NPC, no group events, no special atmospheres. Players approach the Den's main counter, wager 10-10,000g, and resolve instantly at the 60/40 house odds. The atmosphere matches the town's morning tonal phase — quiet, golden-lit, low ambient density, few other players around.

**Design intent for morning.** Morning gambling preserves the pre-v12.34 Double or Nothing experience exactly. Players who prefer solitary quick-resolve gambling can always find it in the morning phase; players who want more social or structured gambling find it in later phases. This is the "something for everyone" floor — the simplest mode always remains available during a predictable part of the server day.

**§28.3.2 Afternoon — Dealer's Hour (Bro Dealer).**

A named NPC, **Bro Dealer**, runs rotating high-stakes challenges during the afternoon phase. Bro Dealer is a fixed NPC (designer-authored, comparable to Mayor Bro and the Ferryman — not player-emergent mythology), appearing in the Den only during the 11:00-17:00 phase. The NPC's role is to frame the afternoon's challenges with a consistent voice and persona — a warmly skeptical gambling-den presence who sees through bluffs.

**Challenge rotation.** Bro Dealer offers one active challenge at any given moment, rotating across a small pool every ~15 minutes of server time. Example challenge formats:

- **"Double or Nothing: Weapons"** — wager a Rare+ weapon from inventory; 40% chance to receive a "doubled" equivalent (the same item at Hardness+1 if Hardness was below cap, otherwise a same-tier Elite weapon). Failure: weapon destroyed. The 40% success rate maintains house edge through expected-value destruction.
- **"Themed Bet: Element"** — wager gold on the color of the next gem Bro Dealer draws from his pouch. Each element has 1-in-10 probability; payout on correct guess is 6×, producing 40% house edge (6×/10 = 60% return = 40% house edge).
- **"Lucky Number"** — wager gold on which of five numbered tokens Bro Dealer overturns. 1-in-5 probability, payout 3× on correct guess (3×/5 = 60% return = 40% house edge).
- **"Double Down"** — wager gold, flip two coins (server RNG); win if both match. 50% × 50% = 25% probability of win. Payout 2.4× on win (2.4 × 0.25 = 0.6 return = 40% house edge).

The rotation pool starts at 4 challenges in v12.34; additional challenges can be added in future content ships without mechanical changes (each challenge's math is calibrated to the same 40% house edge).

**Bro Dealer's voice.** See `creative-reference.md` §8.50 for Bro Dealer's dialogue register — wryly observational, never mocking players who lose, never celebratory when the house wins, acknowledges winners with *"Didn't see that coming"* or similar understated recognition. Bro Dealer is the kind of NPC players quote back to each other ("Bro Dealer said...") because the voice is memorable without being intrusive.

**House edge preserved.** Every Dealer's Hour challenge is calibrated to 40% expected-value loss for the player. The Dealer's flavor is atmospheric; the math is the same as morning's Double or Nothing. A player who prefers Dealer's Hour over morning's quiet gambling pays the same house edge, just with flavor.

**§28.3.3 Evening — House Tournament.**

The evening phase hosts a **multiplayer elimination tournament** with a shared jackpot. The tournament runs on a fixed cadence within the evening phase — three tournaments per evening, each lasting ~60 minutes, with ~15-20 minute gaps for payout resolution and new-entry sign-up.

**Tournament format.** Players buy in with HOUSE_TOURNAMENT_BUY_IN(100g). Buy-in window is 10 minutes; minimum participants is HOUSE_TOURNAMENT_MIN_PLAYERS(4), maximum is HOUSE_TOURNAMENT_MAX_PLAYERS(20). After buy-in closes, the tournament runs as a series of elimination rounds:

- **Round 1:** All players roll a 1d100. The bottom half (rounded down) is eliminated.
- **Round 2:** Surviving players roll again. Bottom half eliminated.
- **Round N:** Continues until one player remains.

**Payout structure.** The total pot is `buy_in × participant_count`. The house takes HOUSE_TOURNAMENT_HOUSE_CUT(0.40) — 40% of the pot — as the house edge. Remaining 60% goes to:
- **Winner:** 70% of the post-cut pot (42% of total pot)
- **Runner-up:** 20% of the post-cut pot (12% of total pot)
- **Third place:** 10% of the post-cut pot (6% of total pot)

This maintains the 40% house edge at the aggregate level while creating meaningful stakes for the top-3 finishers. A 10-player tournament with 100g buy-in produces a 1000g total pot; the house takes 400g, the winner gets 420g, runner-up gets 120g, third place gets 60g.

**Tournament eligibility.** Any player in town during the evening phase can buy in, regardless of level, guild rank, or prior gambling history. The Quiet Individual (§28.3.1) gambling remains available at the Den's side counter during tournament phases — House Tournament does not monopolize the Den.

**Spectating.** House Tournaments are **not** spectator-bettable in v12.34. The §44 Spectator Betting framework applies to Gladiator Arena (Phase 3) matches, not to gambling-den events. This preserves the "§28 is gold-sink gambling" separation from "§44 is peer-to-peer skill-outcome betting." Future ships may add spectator betting for House Tournaments if player demand emerges; the v12.34 ship does not include it.

**§28.3.4 Late Night — The Quiet Room.**

The late-night phase offers a **1v1 high-pressure single-bet format** called The Quiet Room. Two players who both want Quiet Room action matchmake (first-come-first-served from the Den's waitlist) into a private instance within the Den. The matched pair places a single shared-pot bet and resolves it with one high-tension mechanism.

**Bet format.** Each of the two participants deposits QUIET_ROOM_BET(500g default, configurable 100g-5000g — the participants agree on an amount at matchmaking). Total pot is 2 × bet. The house takes QUIET_ROOM_HOUSE_CUT(0.20) — 20% — from the total pot. Remaining 80% goes to one winner.

**Resolution mechanism.** The two players each independently select one of three tokens (Stone / Paper / Shears) via a blind-input UI. Tokens are revealed simultaneously; standard Stone-beats-Shears, Shears-beats-Paper, Paper-beats-Stone resolves the winner. Ties re-resolve until a winner is determined. Each round is ~5 seconds total including reveal animation.

**House edge math.** The 20% house cut on a 2-player zero-sum game produces a 20% expected-value loss for *each* participant. Combined with the 50% win probability per participant, this maintains the gold-sink property even in a peer-to-peer format. Each participant's expected return is 0.5 × 0.8 × (2 × bet) = 0.8 × bet (versus 1.0 × bet input = 20% expected loss). 

**Rationale for 20% rather than 40% in this mode.** Unlike house-vs-player modes where the house is the sole counterparty, Quiet Room is player-vs-player with the house taking a smaller cut. The 20% cut per Quiet Room match produces the same gold-sink rate as the 40% cut on a house-vs-player match because **both participants pay the 20% cut** (total 40% of the pot leaves the player economy, same as a 40%-house mode). The math is identical at the aggregate level; the distribution differs (one winner takes most of the remaining, versus the house always winning 40%).

**Atmosphere.** Late Night's Quiet Room matches the town's late-night tonal phase — dim lighting, hushed ambient, a single flickering overhead lantern in the private instance. The 1v1 format creates high-pressure moments — two players staring at their token choices with 500g on the line produces combat-adjacent tension without being combat. See `creative-reference.md` §8.50 for the atmospheric direction.

**No spectators in Quiet Room.** The private-instance design is deliberate — late-night gambling is personal, not performed. Players who want spectator dynamics go to evening House Tournament (where the open format naturally allows informal viewing of the tournament area). Quiet Room is for the player who wants a high-stakes moment with one other person.

**§28.3.5 Cross-mode rules and economic integrity.**

- **House odds constant at 60/40 across all modes.** The distribution structure varies (direct win/lose in morning, double-or-nothing on items in afternoon, elimination winner in evening, 1v1 winner in late night), but the expected-value loss rate for players is 40% in every mode.
- **No mode-gating.** Any player may participate in any mode during the appropriate phase, regardless of level, guild rank, Mastery Level, or prior gambling activity. The Den is a public service, not a gated content zone.
- **Gold flows monitored against §22 INV-22.** The Den is a primary gold sink. The combined sink rate across all four modes is integrated into the §23.6 Economy Ledger's gambling-sink column. The time-of-day event modes do not change the aggregate sink rate — they redistribute the same gold-destruction across temporal windows. Economic invariant INV-22 Gold Equilibrium is protected by the constant house-edge rule.
- **Weekly Jackpot (§28.2) is orthogonal to event modes.** A player can deposit into the Weekly Jackpot during any phase — the Jackpot's deposit counter and JACKPOT_HOUSE_CUT(0.10) mechanics run independently of the event-mode schedule.

**§28.3.6 Tuning (§20.9).**

| Variable | Default | Min | Max | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| HOUSE_TOURNAMENT_BUY_IN | 100g | 10g | 1,000g | Fixed per-tournament buy-in |
| HOUSE_TOURNAMENT_MIN_PLAYERS | 4 | 2 | 8 | Minimum for tournament to fire |
| HOUSE_TOURNAMENT_MAX_PLAYERS | 20 | 10 | 50 | Cap on participants |
| HOUSE_TOURNAMENT_HOUSE_CUT | 0.40 | 0.20 | 0.60 | Fraction of pot captured by house |
| QUIET_ROOM_BET | 500g | 100g | 5,000g | Participants agree on amount at matchmaking |
| QUIET_ROOM_HOUSE_CUT | 0.20 | 0.10 | 0.30 | Fraction of 2-player pot captured by house |
| DEALERS_HOUR_ROTATION_SECONDS | 900 | 300 | 3,600 | Time between Dealer's Hour challenge rotations |

All default values are calibrated to preserve the 40% aggregate house edge across modes. Any tuning adjustment must maintain this invariant.

**§28.3.7 Named-NPC classification note.**

Bro Dealer is a **designer-authored NPC**, comparable in category to Mayor Bro, the Ferryman, the Tavern Keeper, and the Blacksmith. Bro Dealer is **not** a player-emergent mythology figure in the §8.12a sense — that framework describes content emerging from player action (Godly recipients, Hardness 5 owners, Transcendent craftsmen, high-History weapons). The two categories are distinct:

- **Designer-authored NPCs** provide services, flavor, and narrative anchoring. They exist identically on every server; their personality is authored once and consumed by all players.
- **Player-emergent mythology** (§8.12a) describes content produced by specific player actions. Each server has different specific named individuals populating these categories.

Bro Dealer joins the designer-authored NPC roster without altering the player-emergent mythology framework. The six-layer player-emergent count remains at six; v12.34 adds no new mythology layers.

**Creative direction:** see `creative-reference.md` §8.50 for Bro Dealer's voice specification, each mode's atmospheric register, and the high-pressure 1v1 Quiet Room presentation.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.34): The pre-v12.34 Gambling Den was mechanically competent but socially empty — a single counter with Double or Nothing and a weekly jackpot, operating identically at every hour of the server day. Players who visited the Den at 3 AM had the same experience as players who visited at 3 PM. This is a missed opportunity: the Den is already a social space (people gather where gold is won and lost), and a single-mode implementation fails to give that space temporal texture. The v12.34 time-of-day event modes give the Den four distinct identities across the server day, matching the town's §14.3.3 tonal phases. Morning is solitary Double or Nothing — the quiet individual gamble, always available as the base mode. Afternoon is Dealer's Hour with Bro Dealer running themed challenges — the social high-stakes hour when the Den has a voice. Evening is House Tournament — the multiplayer elimination pot when the Den becomes a community event. Late night is The Quiet Room — the 1v1 high-pressure moment between two players who stayed up. All four modes preserve the 60/40 house edge through different distribution structures; no invariants are touched. The economic gold-sink property is identical across modes; the *atmosphere* is the v12.34 addition. Bro Dealer is deliberately classified as a designer-authored NPC rather than a player-emergent mythology figure — the §8.12a framework is preserved at six layers, and the Den's colorful NPC joins the Mayor/Ferryman/Tavern-Keeper roster without blurring the two categories. The time-of-day coherence with the town's tonal phases is load-bearing: a player visiting the Den at 17:00 experiences the evening House Tournament simultaneous with the town's golden-hour tonal shift, producing a shared-atmosphere transition rather than two disjoint schedules. This is experience-first atmospheric design — the Den becomes a place with its own temporal texture, teaching the player that the server day has shape through the quiet variation of its spaces.]`

### §29. Clan System

- Create: CLAN_CREATE_COST(500g), name (3-16 chars), tag (1-4 chars)
- 8×8 pixel logo editor with 16-color palette (CLAN_COLORS) + eraser
- CLAN_MAX_MEMBERS(20)
- Clan tag [TAG] renders in clan color above player names
- Tiny pixel logo badge next to names in-world
- Clan data broadcast via move packets to all players
- Shared farm access for clan members (planned)
- Clan XP and level system (planned)

#### §29.1 Clan Hall (v12.25 CANONICAL)

Each clan has a **Clan Hall**, a shared interior space accessible from a dedicated portal in town (§14.3.2). The hall is the clan's shared venue — a place where members gather, where the clan's collective achievements accumulate visibly over time, and where clan culture takes physical form.

**Access.** The Clan Hall portal is visible to all players in town, but only members of a given clan can enter their own hall. Non-clan members and members of other clans see the portal but cannot enter. Each clan's hall is its own instance.

**Initial state.** When a clan is created, its Clan Hall starts bare — empty walls, basic furnishings (a few benches, a central table, torch sconces). No achievements displayed, no member contributions, no personality. The hall *becomes* the clan's story through accumulated activity.

**Accumulation events.** Specific member activities populate the hall over time:

- **Arena Tournament win (member):** A trophy appears in the hall displaying the winning member's name and the date. Permanent.
- **Godly weapon reveal (member):** A small shrine appears in the hall with a visual representation of the weapon (grade, material, element) and the date of reveal. Permanent, even if the weapon is later traded away from the member.
- **Hardness 5 success (member):** A display case appears showing the weapon's type/material at the moment of the successful hardening. Permanent.
- **Transcendent rank achievement (member):** A guild-banner-style display appears, showing the skill and the member's name. Permanent.
- **Apex boss defeat (first-ever for the member):** A small wall plaque appears showing the boss name, zone, and date.

**Crafted-item donation.** Any member can donate a craftable item (weapon, armor, gem, composite, prepared food, or decorative item) to the hall. The donated item becomes a permanent hall decoration, visible to all members, labeled with the crafter's name and donation date. Items cannot be removed from the hall after donation. Donation is thus a commitment — the item is permanently out of the member's inventory and economy.

Transcendent crafters' donations display in prismatic text matching §18.3's attribution rules. A hall with donated items from multiple Transcendent members becomes visibly impressive as a gallery of named craft.

**Clan Message Board.** A persistent message board in the hall allows any clan member to post a short message (up to 500 characters). Messages display with the author's name and timestamp. Members can edit their own messages; clan leaders can remove any message. Up to 20 active messages at a time; older messages archive to a scrollable history.

**Hall size and density.** The Clan Hall is sized to accommodate a full 20-member clan with room for accumulation — roughly 40×30 units. As decorations and donations accumulate, the hall fills visibly. Well-established clans have rich halls; young clans have spacious ones. Neither is "better"; the hall reflects the clan's age and activity.

**No mechanical bonuses.** The Clan Hall does not provide stat bonuses, XP bonuses, crafting bonuses, combat advantages, or any mechanical benefit whatsoever. It exists entirely as social infrastructure — a place to be together, a record of shared accomplishment. This preserves the §29 design principle that clans are identity groups, not power groups.

**Deletion.** If a clan disbands or loses all members, the Clan Hall becomes inaccessible (no entry portal), but the hall's state is preserved in the archive — if the clan is ever re-founded under the same name and tag by one of its original members, the hall returns with its full accumulated history intact. This honors the long arc of clan identity.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Clans without a shared venue are just a tag. A Clan Hall converts the clan from metadata into place. The accumulation model is the design's point — a clan's hall is a physical testament to its history, visible every time members gather. New members joining see the clan's past materialized; they understand what they're joining. Older members walking through see their own contributions alongside their clansmates'. The zero-mechanical-bonus rule is load-bearing: the hall is valuable *because* it has no mechanical value, only social meaning. A hall is earned through the clan's activities; it can't be bought, rushed, or min-maxed. The crafted-item donation mechanic gives Transcendent crafters a shared venue that showcases their work to a specific audience (their clanmates), layered on top of the public portfolio system (§18.3.1). A Transcendent member can donate a signature item to their hall where clanmates see it, or list it on the marketplace where strangers see it — different audiences, different motivations.]`

### §30. Social Systems

#### §30.1 Friend System (v12.25 Extended · v12.49 Refined)

Add/remove friends via inspect card. Social panel (👥 button) for management.

**Friend activity display.** Each friend's entry in the friend list shows their current activity, updated in real-time while they are online:

- Online status: online / idle (inactive for 5+ min) / offline
- Current location: zone name if in a zone ("Frozen Shore (Depth 4)"), "Town," "Arena (in match)," "Farm," "In Convergence," "Lawless Land" with **"(hunting)" indicator** for players in active PvP-threat state, or "Offline"
- Time-in-location for extended stays (≥30 minutes): "Town — 45min"
- **Offline recency format (v12.49):** offline friends display last-seen recency — "Offline (last seen 3h ago)" / "Offline (last seen yesterday)" / "Offline (last seen 4d ago)." Beyond 7 days the display compresses to "Offline (last seen 1+ week ago)." This gives friends an at-a-glance sense of whether an offline friend is actively playing or has drifted away, without producing precise timestamps that would encourage activity surveillance. Offline-recency is only shown if the friend's privacy setting permits location visibility (hidden friends show only "Offline" with no recency).

**Teleport to friend.** Players can request teleport to a friend's location with a single tap, subject to existing zone-access rules (cannot TP to Guardian zones without Guardian status, cannot TP into active Arena matches, etc.). Friends in Lawless Land show a "(Lawless)" marker as a warning before the TP request confirms. **Friends with "(hunting)" indicator** additionally show a secondary warning ("This friend is in active PvP engagement. Joining may be unsafe.") before TP confirms — the indicator signals the friend's risk posture to would-be joiners.

**Privacy toggle.** Players can hide their location from the friend list via a setting ("Appear offline to friends"). When hidden, the friend sees only "Offline" status regardless of actual state. Default setting: location visible to friends. This respects player privacy while keeping the default experience social.

**Convergence visibility (v12.49).** Friends at the Convergence show as "In Convergence" specifically rather than the zone-name fallback. The Convergence is the game's endgame surface (§53, §57); a friend being there carries meaning different from being in any other zone — it represents the cap of player progression arcs. The explicit label treats the Convergence as its own social-visibility register rather than rolling it into generic zone display.

`[DESIGN INTENT: A friend list that only shows online/offline is an address book; one that shows current location is a social compass. Players find each other spontaneously rather than scheduling — "oh, Alice is in Frozen Shore Depth 4, I'll join her." The time-in-location stamp is a small but load-bearing addition — it signals when a friend has been somewhere long enough to warrant interruption versus when they just arrived. The privacy toggle is non-negotiable: social features must respect the player's choice to be invisible if they want. The v12.49 refinements extend this discipline: offline-recency formatting gives friends context about drift without producing precise surveillance timestamps; the Lawless "(hunting)" indicator warns would-be joiners about risk posture without announcing specific PvP targets; the Convergence-explicit label treats endgame presence as its own social register rather than rolling it into generic zone display. All three refinements preserve the core principle: social features surface meaningful context while respecting privacy.]`

#### §30.2 Block System
Blocks all interactions (chat, PvP attacks, duels, threats, trade) in lawful zones. **Does NOT apply in lawless zones** — entering the wasteland means accepting all risks. Cannot activate block while in lawless zone. Can always unblock anywhere.

#### §30.3 Mute System
Hides chat bubbles from muted player. Chat log shows "[muted]". All other interactions (combat, trade, duel) still work.

#### §30.4 Inspect Player Card
Tap any player → full RPG card with: avatar + name + clan tag, level + reputation, **alignment indicator** (§33.3 — Good/Neutral/Evil with moral choice count), equipment names, Tier 1 stat pills, combat record (kills/deaths/quests/AP/playtime), alignment titles earned (§25.1), clan badge, action buttons (TP, Trade, Duel, Threat, Clan Invite, Friend/Block/Mute).

#### §30.5 Emotes
20 emoji emotes + 12 text emotes including community callsigns ("67", "Gritty", "GG", "RIP", "Champion").

### §31. Personal Farm + Housing

- `farm_home` zone: personal:true, safe:true, 30×25 map
- Map layout: house (4×3 wood building), 12 farm plots, pond, flower garden, tree fence
- House bed: 3s sleep → full HP/mana/stamina + Well Rested buff (WELL_RESTED_XP_MULT = +10% XP for WELL_RESTED_DURATION = 30min)
- **House interior is the furniture placement surface (§41)** — crafted furniture (self-crafted, gifted by friends, or commissioned through §39 marketplace) can be placed at interior tile positions with 4-direction rotation. Up to FURNITURE_MAX_PER_HOUSE(24) pieces simultaneously. Outdoor pieces (garden bench, plant stand, fence post) are placed on the farm exterior within a narrow grid adjacent to the house.
- **Farm minigames (§47)** — three minigame locations distributed across existing farm geography (pond for Fishing, kitchen for Cooking, puzzle table near flower garden for Elemental-Matching Puzzle). Activities within hangout visits, not destinations.
- Travel via Farm building in town
- Return to town via exit tiles
- Invite other players to visit (planned) — guests can engage §47 minigames freely and inspect §41 placed furniture to see "Made by" crafter attribution

### §32. Monetization Model (v12.11 CANONICAL)

#### §32.0 Philosophy and Competitive Positioning

**Philosophy.** Cost recovery plus a small development fund, nothing more. The game is the product; monetization is the small machinery that keeps the game running. Every dollar a player spends should produce real value the player wants, the cost math is published openly, and the model is intentionally designed so that paying players never feel worse than free players in any dimension of the experience. **The monetization model is small and stable specifically so it does not pull development effort away from the game itself** — the game is the content pipeline, and monetization machinery (cosmetics, battle passes, seasonal events, premium currencies) is explicitly minimized rather than expanded. When in doubt about whether to add a monetization feature, the answer is no.

**Competitive positioning.** The Supporter Pass is priced at $2/month, deliberately below the comparison threshold for established subscription games. The reasoning is structural to the project's situation: this is a solo-developer game (vibe-coded with AI assistance) entering a market where game production cost is collapsing, players have a flood of low-cost alternatives that didn't exist five years ago, and any new title is implicitly comparing itself to mature subscription games (RuneScape at $11/month with 20 years of content, etc.) when a player evaluates whether to subscribe. A $5/month price point would invite that comparison and lose it. A $2/month price point sits below the threshold where players make the comparison at all — they evaluate it as "less than a coffee" rather than as "half the price of RuneScape." The lower price reduces per-player revenue but probably increases conversion rate, and for a small operation that values retention over per-player extraction, this is the right trade. The price is also a commitment to the player: at $2/month, the model only works if the developer is honest about costs, and honesty is the brand promise the rest of §32 is structured around.

#### §32.1 Free Tier (CANONICAL)

Every player gets a fixed amount of free play time per day. Resets daily at 00:00 UTC. No carryover. No feature locks of any kind — every game system is fully accessible in the free tier, including all combat, crafting, life skills, marketplace, dungeons, PvP, the Lawless Land, the Convergence, the Last Recombination ritual (§57), the Hall of Fragments (§57.8), and every other gameplay system. The free tier does not gate content; it gates only **session time**.

| Variable | Default | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|-------|
| FREE_DAILY_PLAY_HOURS | 2.0 | Resets at 00:00 UTC. No carryover. |

**Why a time cap and not feature locks.** Every other monetization model the doc considered (operation caps, queue priority, paid content tiers, cosmetic-required prestige) was rejected because each one creates a class divide between paying and free players where the paying player gets a *better game*. The time cap is the only restriction that doesn't degrade the game itself for free players — it just limits how many hours per day they get to play the same game everyone else is playing. A free player and a paying player playing in the same session have identical experiences; the only difference is that the free player has to stop after 2 hours and come back tomorrow.

**The cap is calibrated to the bursty session shape.** This is a tightly looped game with lottery dopamine embedded in its core loops, not an open-world game with continuous progression. Typical engagement is 30-90 minute sessions, with most players playing one session per day. A 2-hour cap rarely binds for typical players — it binds only for the hardcore players who play multiple sessions per day, and those players are also the conversion targets for the Supporter Pass. The cap is doing exactly the work it should do: it bounds infrastructure cost at the population level, and it converts the players who actually want more game into the players who fund the game.

#### §32.2 Supporter Pass (CANONICAL)

The single paid product. One price, one core benefit, one decision. No tiers, no founder's pricing, no premium subscription, no annual discount. One Supporter Pass.

| Variable | Default | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|-------|
| SUPPORTER_PASS_PRICE_USD | 2.00 | Stablecoin transaction (USDC or Hemi-native equivalent) |
| SUPPORTER_PASS_DURATION_DAYS | 30 | One month of unlimited play |

**Mechanic.** Pay 2 USD in stablecoin. Receive 30 days of unlimited play time (no daily cap), AND access to the cosmetics store (§32.3) for the duration of the Pass. At expiry, the player returns to the free tier unless they renew. There is no auto-renewal — the player has to actively choose to extend, every time. This is intentional: auto-renewal is a dark pattern that captures revenue from players who forgot they were paying, and the doc rejects dark patterns even when they would work.

**The Pass gates two things together.** Unlimited play time and cosmetics store access are bundled into the single Supporter Pass purchase. There is no way to buy cosmetics without an active Pass, and no way to get unlimited play without the Pass. This is the core structural decision of the v12.11 monetization model — see §32.4 for the reasoning. The bundling means every cosmetic-buying player is, by definition, a player who has unlimited play, which closes the failure mode where a player pays for cosmetics and then gets rate-limited on the very next day.

**Why $2 and not $5.** See §32.0 (Philosophy) for the competitive positioning argument. Briefly: $2/month sits below the comparison threshold for established subscription games, lets the Pass be an "easy yes" purchase rather than a deliberate evaluation, and trades per-player revenue for higher expected conversion rates. The economics work because per-player infrastructure cost is small (~$0.08/month, see §32.10) and the Pass at $2 covers that cost with margin to spare even at modest conversion rates.

**Account-bound to the wallet.** The Supporter Pass duration is bound to the purchasing wallet address (see §32.7) and cannot be transferred, gifted, or shared by any mechanism.

**The Ferryman acknowledgment (v12.36 CANONICAL).** The *first time* a player activates a Supporter Pass on their account, the **Ferryman** delivers a single unique line on their next encounter with him: *"Thank you. The lights stay on because of you."* The line fires exactly once per account — it is never repeated on renewal, never repeated on subsequent activations after a lapse, never surfaced anywhere else in the game. This is the monetization-to-lore bridge: the Ferryman is the only NPC in Bro Town positioned to acknowledge the real-world forces that keep the server running (see §33 and `creative-reference.md` §8.5a for the Ferryman's established narrative role as the game's sole acknowledger of external powers), and the Supporter Pass first-activation is the only in-world moment where real-world support of the game becomes narratively diegetic.

The line never appears in any quest log, achievement tracker, or in-game notification. It is delivered only through the Ferryman's ordinary dialogue path, on the player's next proximity interaction with him after activation. If the player never visits the Ferryman after activation, the line never delivers; it does not queue indefinitely across game sessions (unlike the §53.6 act-transition lines). The moment is permitted to pass unacknowledged if the player doesn't visit the Ferryman — consistent with the Ferryman's established register as an NPC who never seeks the player out.

**Why the Ferryman.** Of Bro Town's designer-authored NPCs (Mayor Bro, the Ferryman, the Tavern Keeper, the Blacksmith, the Enchanter, Bro Dealer), the Ferryman is uniquely positioned to acknowledge external forces: he is the only NPC who acknowledges the gods (§8.5a — *"The gods have left a mark on this. I do not touch what they have touched."*), and that positioning extends naturally to acknowledging the real-world support that sustains the game itself. The monetization-to-lore bridge would feel intrusive from Mayor Bro's mouth, or theatrical from Bro Dealer's. From the Ferryman's, it reads as consistent with his already-established role — he acknowledges what is beyond the game's ordinary fiction, briefly and without fanfare.

**Register.** The line is delivered in the Ferryman's ordinary voice (grave, neutral, unceremonious). No theatrical music, no screen effects, no dialogue popup with special framing. The player approaches the Ferryman; the Ferryman delivers the line; the Ferryman returns to his normal dialogue loop. See `creative-reference.md` §8.5a for the full creative direction on this moment.

**What this is NOT.** Not a reward. Not an achievement unlock. Not a permanent buff. Not a cosmetic. The Pass's benefits (unlimited play, cosmetics store access) are unchanged by this line. The acknowledgment is purely narrative — a single in-world moment of gratitude from the game's most authoritative NPC. Players who value the acknowledgment experience it once; players who don't notice it still get all the Pass's mechanical benefits. The moment is opt-in by virtue of requiring a Ferryman visit.

#### §32.3 Cosmetics Store (CANONICAL)

A small, curated set of visual cosmetic items, **available exclusively to players with an active Supporter Pass**. The store is intentionally small: roughly 8-15 items at launch, with no commitment to ongoing content production. New cosmetic items are added when the developer wants to add them, not on any schedule, and never under pressure from a content cadence. **The cosmetics store is a benefit of being a Supporter, not a parallel revenue product.**

| Variable | Default | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|-------|
| COSMETIC_ITEM_PRICE_USD | 1.00 | Uniform per-item price |
| COSMETIC_CART_MAX_PER_PURCHASE_USD | 10.00 | Per-checkout spending limit |
| COSMETIC_STORE_REQUIRES_ACTIVE_PASS | true | Hard requirement at checkout |

**Pass-gated access.** A player can only browse, preview, or purchase cosmetics while their Supporter Pass is active. A free player who attempts to access the cosmetics store sees a "Cosmetics are a Supporter benefit — get the Supporter Pass to access them" message that links to the Pass purchase flow. **There is no path to purchase cosmetics without first having an active Pass.** This is the core structural decision of the v12.11 monetization model and is the operational guarantee that no cosmetic-buying player is ever rate-limited.

**Item categories.** Visual fluff that does not affect gameplay in any way. Examples (not exhaustive): trail effects, aura rings, name color, death animations, emote sets. **All items are strictly visual; nothing in the cosmetics store affects damage, defense, stats, drop rates, or any other gameplay variable, ever.** This is a hard rule with no exceptions.

**Cosmetics are NOT pay-for-the-cool-stuff.** The aesthetics that matter most in this game come from gameplay achievements, not from the cosmetics store. The visual prestige items are: Godly weapons (§4.6b.1) which the gods chose to give you, Hardness 5 weapons (§4.6c) which are mortal lottery wins, the Hemi Bros stat-based visual evolution (§49) which reflects your actual play, and the discontinued prestige drops (§32.5) which mark you as having been there at launch. **Anything in the cosmetics store should be visibly less prestigious than any of these earned-from-gameplay options.** A player wearing a $1 cosmetics-store trail effect should not look cooler than a player wearing the OG Cape they earned in Month 1, or a player wielding a Hardness 5 Tier 20 origin sword they ground for 6 months. The store provides expression, not status.

**Pricing.** Every item is exactly $1.00 USD. Uniform pricing across the store — no premium tiers, no $0.50 items, no $2 items. The maximum cart per purchase is $10.00 (i.e., 10 items per cart) to prevent any single transaction from being financially harmful. There is no premium currency, no bundle discount, no "buy gems get a bonus" mechanic — every purchase is dollars-for-items in the most straightforward way possible.

**Cosmetics persist forever once purchased.** Owned cosmetics survive Pass expiration. A player who buys 5 cosmetics during their active Pass period and then lets the Pass lapse continues to own those 5 cosmetics permanently and can equip and display them at any time, including while the Pass is inactive. **The Pass gates the *store* (you can only purchase while you're a Supporter) but does not gate the *items* (anything you've bought stays usable forever).** This is the "you bought it, it's yours" principle and it's load-bearing for the Paying-Player-Never-Worse principle (§32.11).

**Account-bound.** Every cosmetic purchase is bound to the purchasing wallet address (see §32.7). Cosmetics cannot be traded, sold, gifted, or transferred between players by any mechanism. Once a cosmetic is owned by a wallet, it stays with that wallet.

#### §32.4 Cosmetics Gating Rationale (CANONICAL)

**The single rule:** Cosmetics can only be purchased while the player has an active Supporter Pass. Once purchased, cosmetics are owned forever and remain usable even after the Pass expires. The Supporter Pass is the gate; cosmetics are what's behind the gate. There is no dual-redemption credit system, no playtime grant from cosmetic purchases, and no cosmetic credit from Pass purchases.

**Why gating instead of dual-redemption.** Earlier iterations of v12.11 considered a symmetric dual-redemption mechanic (Supporter Pass purchases generate cosmetic credit, cosmetic purchases generate playtime credit, both at 1:1) and an asymmetric variant (only cosmetic purchases generate playtime credit). Both were rejected in favor of the gating model because gating solves the "cosmetic buyer gets rate-limited" failure mode at the structural level rather than mitigating it after the fact.

**The failure mode the gating model prevents.** Under any model where cosmetics are sold separately from the Supporter Pass, a player could purchase cosmetics without holding a Pass, then hit the daily play cap on the next day, and feel betrayed for having financially supported the game and being throttled in return. This is the worst possible player experience for a paying customer — paying produces a worse outcome than not paying. The asymmetric dual-redemption model addressed this by giving cosmetic buyers playtime credit as a thank-you bonus, but the bonus is a *mitigation* — the player still has to think about credit balances, conversion rates, and the relationship between two products. The gating model addresses it at the source: cosmetics are only available *after* the player has the Pass, which means every cosmetic-buying player has unlimited play *by definition*. The failure mode cannot occur because the prerequisite for buying cosmetics is having unlimited play.

**Why the gating model is simpler than dual-redemption.** Two products, one relationship, no compounding mechanics. The Pass is exactly $2/month for unlimited play and store access. Cosmetics are exactly $1 each, store-only, Pass-required. There is no credit balance to track, no conversion rate to remember, no expiration on credit, no dual-purpose accounting, no "every dollar does X duty" framing to maintain. A new player can understand the entire model in one sentence: *"Pay $2/month for unlimited play and cosmetic store access; cosmetics are $1 each."*

**Why the gating model is honest about cosmetics being a side product.** The v12.11 design philosophy is that the game is the content pipeline and cosmetics are a small side benefit, not a parallel revenue product. Gating cosmetics behind the Pass makes the structural relationship visible: cosmetics exist as a feature of being a Supporter, not as their own product line. A player who values cosmetics has to value the Pass first. A player who only wants the Pass doesn't have to think about cosmetics at all. The two products serve overlapping but distinct desires, and the gating ensures that the cosmetics audience is a strict subset of the Pass audience.

**Revenue implication.** Under the gating model, cosmetics generate revenue only from Pass holders. If cosmetics drove independent conversions (a player buying cosmetics without buying the Pass), the gating model loses that revenue. This is an acceptable trade because (a) the cosmetics store is small and would not have generated meaningful independent revenue anyway, (b) the simplicity gain is large, and (c) the failure mode prevention is structural rather than mitigated.

**The cosmetics store as Supporter Pass upgrade path.** Under the gating model, cosmetics function as the upgrade path *for existing Pass holders* who want to express themselves visually beyond the base subscription. The Pass is the entry point ($2/month for unlimited play). The cosmetics store is the optional extra ($1 per visual item, only while subscribed) for Pass holders who want to give a little more. The funnel is one-way: Pass first, cosmetics second. Players never see cosmetics as a separate purchase decision; they see them as a feature of their existing subscription.

#### §32.5 Discontinued Prestige Drops (CANONICAL)

Time-windowed limited mints inspired by the RuneScape party hat model. Each prestige drop is available only during a specific window of the server's life, after which no new copies are ever produced. The supply is fixed at the moment the window closes and cannot be expanded. Players who acquire prestige drops during their window own them forever, can trade them through §39 (Server Marketplace), and watch their value evolve based on server-driven scarcity dynamics.

**Schedule (subject to launch timing):**

| Drop | Window | Description |
|------|--------|-------------|
| OG Cape | Month 1 of server | Black cape with gold "OG" shimmer effect |
| Founder's Crown | Month 3 | Subtle gold crown rendered above the player |
| Phoenix Trail | Month 6 | Phoenix-themed movement trail |
| Year One Aura | Month 12 | Anniversary-themed aura ring |

**Acquisition.** Prestige drops are acquired by being present and playing during their window — the exact mechanism is TBD (achievement reward, login bonus, completion of a window-specific quest, or simple presence during the period). Multiple acquisition mechanisms can coexist; the canonical rule is that the drops are only available during the window and never again afterward. Prestige drops do **not** require a real-money purchase. They are earned in-game during their window.

**Tradeable through §39.** After their acquisition window closes, prestige drops are listed as a marketplace category (§39.3) and can be bought and sold for in-game gold like any other tradeable item. Their price is entirely player-driven: scarcity, server age, and demand for "I was there" prestige set the price. The doc does not constrain the price; the marketplace finds it.

**No royalties.** The v12.10 §32.2 mention of "7.5% royalty on secondary sales" is removed. Royalty enforcement on tradeable items is operationally complex and player-perceived as extractive. Standard marketplace listing fees from §39 apply (the same 5% listing fee that applies to all marketplace listings) and nothing additional.

**No NFT, no on-chain mint.** Prestige drops are server-side tradeable items. The scarcity is enforced by the developer's commitment to never mint additional copies after the window closes. This is the same trust model the rest of the doc uses (the developer commits to honest drop rates, honest economy, honest validator results, and now also honest scarcity for prestige drops). Players have to trust this commitment to participate in the prestige market, and the doc's transparency principles are the support structure for that trust.

**Why server-side and not on-chain.** A prestige drop minted as an NFT would gain provability and theoretical cross-game portability, but at meaningful cost: gas fees per mint and per transfer, on-chain marketplace fragmentation, NFT market dynamics (wash trading, sniper bots, speculative bubbles) that distort the "I was there" framing the drops are designed for. The server-side approach trades cryptographic provability for operational simplicity and tighter game integration. The trade is appropriate for items whose value is *social meaning within this game*, not portable digital ownership across games.

**Distinction from Godly weapons.** Godly weapons (§4.6b.1) and prestige drops both use scarcity to generate prestige, but their underlying mechanics differ in ways that matter:

| Property | Godly Weapons | Prestige Drops |
|----------|---------------|----------------|
| Supply | Continuous (~80 per 1,000-player-server per year) | Fixed (closed at end of window) |
| Acquisition | Random reveal from any kill | Time-windowed during specific server periods |
| Lineage tracking | Yes (§17.4.1, individual provenance) | No (each copy is identical) |
| Sacred properties | Indestructible, Ferryman-exempt, audit-exempt | Standard cosmetic, no protections |
| Functional impact | 3× damage multiplier | Visual only |
| Marketplace price drivers | Lineage prestige + functional demand | Pure scarcity + "I was there" social meaning |

The Godly is a *living rarity* that continues to enter the world, with each individual instance carrying a unique story. The prestige drop is a *closed cohort* where every copy is interchangeable and the value is in being part of the original group. They occupy different cosmological roles and should not be confused.

#### §32.6 Spending Limits (CANONICAL)

| Variable | Default | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|-------|
| MAX_CART_USD | 10.00 | Hard cap per purchase |
| MONTHLY_SOFT_WARNING_USD | 30.00 | Soft warning threshold (no hard enforcement) |

**Hard cap.** No single purchase transaction can exceed $10.00 USD. This is a hard limit enforced at checkout. A player who tries to buy $11 of cosmetics in one cart gets a "split your purchase into multiple transactions" message. The cap exists to prevent any single impulsive moment from becoming a financially significant decision.

**Soft warning.** When a player has spent more than $30.00 USD in the current calendar month (cosmetics + Supporter Pass combined), the next purchase shows a warm acknowledgment message: *"You've spent $30 this month supporting the game. Thank you — we mean that. Are you sure you want to buy more?"* The player clicks Yes and proceeds, or clicks No and walks away. The warning is informational, not blocking. There is no hard monthly cap because:

- A hard cap punishes engaged players who are making deliberate choices
- A soft warning gives vulnerable players a friction point to reconsider
- The warning text is non-judgmental and respects player autonomy

**No upsells, no FOMO timers, no flash sales.** The cosmetics store has stable pricing and stable availability. Items don't get more expensive over time, don't get cheaper during sales, and aren't limited-time-only as a purchase pressure. Discontinued prestige drops have time windows but those windows are about *acquisition*, not about *purchase pressure* — the player is given the opportunity to earn them in-game during the window, not pressured to buy them before the window closes.

#### §32.7 Account-Bound Principle (CANONICAL)

All purchased benefits — Supporter Pass duration, cosmetic items, and any future purchasable benefits — are bound to the purchasing **wallet address** and cannot be transferred, gifted, sold, traded, or shared with any other wallet by any mechanism inside the game. This binding is permanent and applies to every product in §32.

**The mechanisms this principle forbids:**
- No marketplace listings of purchased cosmetics
- No direct trading of purchased cosmetics or playtime
- No gifting of cosmetics to other wallets
- No transferring Supporter Pass duration to another wallet
- No sharing cosmetic credit between wallets (cosmetic credit does not exist as a balance in the v12.11 model; see §32.4 for the gating rationale that replaces credit-based mechanics)

**Why this principle exists.** Without account-binding, every purchasable benefit becomes a potential channel for real-money trading (RMT) — a player could buy cosmetics with real money, transfer them to another player, and receive in-game gold (or out-of-game payment) in return. RMT undermines the game's economic integrity in several specific ways:

1. **It distorts the §23 economy.** Real money flowing into in-game gold supply breaks the validated faucet/sink model and corrupts INV-22 (Gold Equilibrium) and INV-24 (Wealth Concentration).
2. **It undermines §17.4.1 Godly lineage credibility.** If items can be traded as proxies for real money, the lineage of valuable items records "marketplace transfer" while the real story is "real-money side payment," and the lineage system loses its claim to honest provenance.
3. **It creates two-tier economic citizens.** Players with disposable income gain in-game advantages they didn't earn through play, which contradicts the "earned legibility" principle the entire game is built on.

The account-bound principle is the operational defense against all three failure modes. By making purchased benefits permanently non-transferable, the doc closes the RMT channels that purchased benefits would otherwise create.

**Wallet-level identity transfer is outside the game's control.** A player could in principle sell their entire wallet (with its private key and all associated game state) to another player for real money. The game has no way to detect or prevent this. **The account-bound principle does not claim to prevent wallet-level identity transfer**; it claims only to prevent the fine-grained item-level RMT that the principle is designed to close. Wallet-level transfer is dramatically less exploitable than item-level RMT because:

- Wallet sales are coarse-grained — the buyer inherits the entire identity (including reputation, alignment, quest state, social standing, OG Cape, Godly weapons, etc.), not just the valuable pieces
- Wallet sales are high-friction — they require trusting the counterparty with the private key, which is risky in both directions
- Wallet sales don't inflate the in-game economy — they just transfer existing identities between players, without creating new gold or items

The doc accepts wallet-level transfer as an unmitigatable property of wallet-based identity and focuses the account-bound principle on the channels it can actually close.

#### §32.8 Payment Architecture (v12.11 CANONICAL, v13 REPLACEMENT — Multi-Payment Rails)

**v13 Multi-rail payment architecture.** Canonical stablecoin-only payment is expanded to three payment rails for broadest player reach:

| Rail | Platform Target | Notes |
|------|----------------|-------|
| **Stablecoin (Hemi)** | Crypto-native players, desktop | Canonical rail preserved — USDC or Hemi-native stablecoin on-chain |
| **Stripe** | Desktop + web (credit/debit card) | Standard card processing for players who prefer conventional payment |
| **IAP (iOS / Android)** | Mobile app stores | Platform-mandated IAP for mobile app distribution compliance |

All three rails purchase the same products (Supporter Pass, cosmetics). The choice of rail is determined by the player's platform and preference. Pricing is equivalent across rails at the currency level (platform fees may produce small variance in net revenue per rail; see below).

**Platform fees per rail:**
- Stablecoin: ~0.5% on-chain settlement fees (varies by network conditions)
- Stripe: ~3% + $0.30 per transaction
- IAP: 15-30% depending on platform tier

The developer accepts platform fee variance as a cost of reach. Each rail's net revenue is tracked separately for cost-accounting purposes per §32.10.

**Why multi-rail.** Canonical stablecoin-only limits the game's addressable audience. Non-crypto-native players on desktop need card payments (Stripe). Mobile players on app stores must use IAP for platform compliance. Preserving stablecoin as an option for players who want provable on-chain cost math (per §32.10) maintains the canonical commitment while expanding reach.

**Stablecoin rails on Hemi (CANONICAL preserved).** The game's stablecoin payment layer uses stablecoins (USDC or Hemi-native equivalent) on Hemi, a Bitcoin-secured Ethereum L2 with EVM compatibility. Payments are settled on-chain; the game server verifies the transaction and grants the corresponding in-game benefit. **Game state and gameplay logic remain entirely server-side** — only the payment rail is on-chain. There are no on-chain game items, no on-chain game state, no on-chain economy. The Cloudflare Durable Objects backend (§16) is the canonical authority for everything except the payment ledger.

**Stripe rail (v13 NEW).** Stripe integration provides credit/debit card processing for desktop/web players. Payment flow: player selects Stripe at checkout → redirected to Stripe-hosted payment form → on successful charge, server receives webhook → server grants in-game benefit. Standard Stripe fraud protection applies (3D Secure, CVV checks, etc.). PCI compliance handled by Stripe; game server never handles card data directly.

**IAP rail (v13 NEW).** iOS and Android IAP integration for mobile app players. Platform-mandated in-app purchase flow: player selects IAP at checkout → redirected to platform IAP prompt → on successful purchase, platform returns receipt → server verifies receipt with platform API → server grants in-game benefit. Server-side receipt validation prevents fraud.

**Why blockchain is used at all, and why it's used so narrowly** (preserved from canonical):

1. **Stablecoin payments** allow the published cost math (§32.10) to be verified by anyone on the stablecoin rail.

2. **Wallet identity** replaces traditional email/password authentication for players on the stablecoin rail. For Stripe and IAP rails, the player uses platform-native authentication (Stripe email + password; IAP platform account) with a server-side mapping to an internal account ID.

3. **Five-word recovery as a non-wallet alternate login** for stablecoin-rail players who don't want to manage a crypto wallet.

**Cross-rail account continuity.** A player's account is identified by an internal account ID, not by any specific payment rail. A player may initially sign up via IAP on mobile, later connect a stablecoin wallet, and purchase future cosmetics via whichever rail they prefer. Account data (inventory, progression, alignment, titles) is unchanged across payment rail switching. The rail is a payment method, not an identity.

**What blockchain is NOT used for** (preserved from canonical):

- No on-chain game items (no NFT weapons, no NFT cosmetics, no NFT prestige drops)
- No on-chain game state (no on-chain stats, position, inventory, gold balance, XP)
- No on-chain marketplace (the §39 marketplace is server-side; only the Supporter Pass and cosmetics store payments touch the chain, and only via the stablecoin rail)
- No tokens beyond stablecoins (no game token, no governance token, no liquidity mining)
- No play-to-earn mechanics (gameplay produces in-game gold, not real-world value)
- No on-chain progress backup (this was considered for v12.11 and dropped as not load-bearing)

**The blockchain integration is intentionally minimal.** Every Web3 feature in the doc has to justify itself against the principle "this feature serves the game." Payments (on the stablecoin rail), identity (on the stablecoin rail), and the recovery mnemonic clear that bar. Anything else does not, and is excluded.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): Canonical stablecoin-only architecture reflects a specific ideological position about payment transparency and verifiability. v13 preserves that position as a first-class rail (stablecoin remains available with all its benefits), but adds Stripe and IAP as additional rails to meet players where they are. The ideological purity of crypto-native payment cost conversion; reach matters more than purity. The multi-rail architecture adds engineering and compliance complexity but addresses the larger business reality: players who want to support the game but don't use crypto should be able to do so. Stripe handles the desktop non-crypto-native market. IAP handles the mobile platform-mandated market. Stablecoin handles the crypto-native market AND the cost-transparency commitment (§32.10). Each rail serves its constituency; together they enable the game to reach all three markets without forcing any one of them into a payment mode they don't want.]`

#### §32.9 No-Pay-To-Win Commitments (CANONICAL)

The following list is a permanent commitment, binding on all future versions of the doc. Any change to this list requires an explicit, justified, and changelog-documented decision. Items on this list are not currently in §32 and will not be added in any future version without breaking the commitment publicly.

**The monetization model will NEVER include:**

1. Items, weapons, gems, or composites sold for real money
2. Stat boosts or XP boosts purchasable with real money
3. Drop rate boosts or luck boosts for real money
4. Premium currency (no in-game token purchasable with stablecoin and used for in-game purchases)
5. Loot boxes, gacha mechanics, or any randomized real-money purchases
6. Battle pass with paid track, season pass, or any progression-tied paid product
7. Time-limited cosmetics as a FOMO driver (the prestige drops in §32.5 have time-limited *acquisition windows* but their purpose is "I was there" social meaning, not purchase pressure)
8. Energy systems, stamina refills, or any "wait or pay" mechanics
9. Auto-renewal on the Supporter Pass (renewal is always an active player choice)
10. Pay-to-skip mechanics (no paying to skip crafting time, travel time, gem combinations, etc.)
11. Any "Supporter Pass only" *gameplay content* — zones, dungeons, systems, mechanics, monsters, quests, or game progression. The free tier and Supporter Pass have access to identical gameplay; only session time and cosmetic store access differ. **Cosmetic store access is intentionally Supporter Pass only** (see §32.3 and §32.4) but cosmetics are explicitly visual-only and do not affect any gameplay variable, so the gating creates no gameplay inequality between free and paying players.
12. Cosmetics that visibly outclass earned-from-gameplay prestige items (a $1 cosmetic should not look more impressive than a Godly weapon, a Hardness 5 weapon, or an OG Cape)
13. Pay-for-position in marketplace listings, search results, or any kind of paid prominence
14. Sponsored content, advertising, or third-party brand integrations
15. Player-to-player gifting of any purchased benefit (closes the RMT channel — see §32.7)

This list is the negative-space definition of the monetization model. The positive-space definition is small (Supporter Pass at $2/month for unlimited play and cosmetic store access, $1 cosmetics gated behind the Pass, prestige drops earned in-game during launch windows and tradeable through §39) and the negative-space definition is large because the doc wants to be explicit about what it's *not* doing, so that future readers and future AI agents working on the doc cannot accidentally introduce something that violates the original intent.

#### §32.10 Published Cost FAQ (CANONICAL — v12.12 corrected)

The following FAQ paragraph appears in player-facing documentation (game website, in-game About page, or equivalent) and is updated whenever infrastructure costs change materially. The current text (v12.12):

> *"This game runs on Cloudflare Durable Objects, with WebSocket Hibernation for cost efficiency. Each active player costs us less than $0.03 per month in infrastructure fees on average — free players cost about $0.007/month with the 2-hour daily cap, and paying players cost about $0.026/month with their typical extended play sessions. The free tier gives you 2 hours per day, which is enough for most players. The Supporter Pass at $2/month removes the daily cap and unlocks the cosmetics store ($1 per item, optional). At this price, we break even when about 1-2% of our players subscribe — well below realistic indie subscription rates, which gives us comfortable margin to fund development. If our infrastructure costs change — because we scale up, because Cloudflare adjusts pricing, or because we add features that cost more to run — we'll update these numbers and adjust the price to match. We charge to cover cost, not to extract margin."*

**Why the FAQ exists.** The published cost math is a commitment device. By stating openly what the game costs to run and what the prices are calibrated against, future-developer is locked into a pricing philosophy that future-players can hold them accountable to. If costs rise and prices have to rise, the change is consistent with the original promise (cover cost) rather than being a betrayal of an unstated promise (extract maximum). If costs fall and prices stay where they are, the gap between cost and price is the development fund, which is also stated openly. There is no hidden margin and no quiet extraction.

**The cost numbers are derived from the v12.12 corrected infrastructure analysis** (a full cost model, proposed name `cost-model.md`, is planned as a future companion document but does not yet exist; the key per-hour derivation and assumptions are captured inline in this FAQ and in §16.8 until the companion is written). The per-hour cost figure is roughly **$0.000293 per active player-hour**, computed from the §16.8 broadcast architecture (one outbound message per room per tick at 20 Hz, no per-observer filtering, billed only on incoming WebSocket messages at the Cloudflare 20:1 ratio) and the §16.13 default 10 Hz client input rate, with WebSocket Hibernation enabled. This per-hour figure applied to the 2-hour daily cap (with realistic engagement of ~0.825 hr/day average for free players accounting for the cap binding on the engaged minority) and to ~3 hr/day average for paying players produces the per-month figures cited in the FAQ. **All of these numbers are estimates pending Phase 1 measurement** and will be updated when real player behavior data is available. The FAQ should be read as "current best estimate" rather than as "permanent committed value."

**The v12.12 cost correction.** v12.11 originally stated the per-player cost as ~$0.08/month and the break-even conversion rate as ~5%. Both numbers were wrong because the underlying model did not explicitly account for (a) the room DO compute being shared across the room population (50 players per room max, ~25 average), and (b) the 2-hour daily cap on free players reducing their actual play time relative to an uncapped baseline. The v12.12 correction recomputes from first principles with both factors explicit, producing per-player costs roughly an order of magnitude lower and a break-even conversion rate of approximately 1-2% across all tested player population sizes (100 to 25,000 concurrent). The corrected math is more favorable to the "honest cost recovery" framing because it shows the model has comfortable margin even at modest conversion rates, and the developer's commitment to keeping prices low becomes more credible when the math is more obviously generous. **No design decisions changed in v12.12** — the model is still $2/month Pass with $1 cosmetics gated behind the Pass; only the published cost numbers were corrected.

**What the daily cap actually saves.** The 2-hour daily cap on free players reduces free-tier infrastructure costs by approximately 45% compared to an uncapped free tier, because the cap binds for the engaged minority of players (the ~5-15% who would otherwise play 3-4+ hours per day). At 1,000 concurrent players the absolute savings are about $5.64/month; at 10,000 concurrent the savings grow to about $56/month; at 25,000 concurrent the savings are about $141/month. The savings are real but secondary to the cap's primary value, which is **converting heavily-engaged free players to the paid tier** — the cap is a conversion mechanism first and a cost-control mechanism second, and the conversion value typically dwarfs the direct cost savings.

**Conversion rate scenarios.** At 1,000 concurrent players with realistic indie subscription conversion rates, the model produces: ~$74/month profit at 3% conversion, ~$129/month at 5% conversion, ~$211/month at 8% conversion. At 5,000 concurrent: ~$373/month at 3%, ~$646/month at 5%, ~$1,056/month at 8%. At 10,000 concurrent: ~$747/month at 3%, ~$1,293/month at 5%, ~$2,112/month at 8%. At 25,000 concurrent: ~$1,867/month at 3%, ~$3,232/month at 5%, ~$5,280/month at 8%. The model scales roughly linearly above 1,000 players because the Cloudflare free tier is exhausted at small population and per-player costs become the dominant variable. **All scenarios are profitable at any conversion rate above 1-2%**, and the actual conversion rate is the single biggest unknown until Phase 1 launch.

**The single biggest uncertainty in the cost model** is the per-tick CPU cost estimate (~5 ms per server tick per room for input aggregation and broadcast generation, derived from the §16.8 architecture and reasonable implementation assumptions). If the actual implementation runs heavier than this estimate by a factor of 3, the per-player cost would rise to roughly $0.08/month for paying players, which still leaves the model profitable but reduces the margin meaningfully. The right discipline is to **measure actual CPU per tick during early prototyping** rather than commit to prices based on the estimate. The cost model and the FAQ should be re-validated against real telemetry as soon as it exists.

#### §32.11 The Paying-Player-Never-Worse Principle (CANONICAL DESIGN TENET)

**Stated as a binding design commitment for all future monetization decisions:**

> *The monetization model will never cause a paying player to feel worse about their experience than a free player in the same situation. If a choice between implementations would leave a paying player feeling short-changed, the implementation must change even if the math works out.*

This principle is a load-bearing constraint on every monetization decision the doc makes, has made, or will make. It is the reason:

- Supporter Pass auto-renewal is forbidden (a paying player whose pass auto-renewed without their consent feels worse than a free player who simply chose to stop playing)
- Cosmetics are gated behind the Supporter Pass specifically so that no cosmetic buyer is ever rate-limited — having the Pass is the prerequisite for buying cosmetics, which guarantees every cosmetic-buying player has unlimited play (§32.4)
- The free tier has no feature locks (a paying player and a free player playing in the same room should have identical experiences during play, with the only difference being how long they get to stay)
- The cosmetics store does not contain pay-for-the-cool-stuff items (a paying player who bought cosmetics should not visually outclass a free player who earned a Hardness 5 weapon)
- Owned cosmetics persist forever once purchased, even after the Supporter Pass expires (a paying player should not lose their visual purchases for choosing to take a break from the subscription)
- Account-bound items can be lost only through the player's own choices (a paying player should not lose their cosmetics due to operational changes the developer makes)
- The published cost math is honest (a paying player should not feel manipulated or extracted from)

**Why this principle is stated as a design tenet rather than implied.** The implementation phase of the project will involve many small monetization decisions that don't individually rise to the level of "design choice" but cumulatively determine the player experience. Without an explicit principle to anchor them, those decisions will drift toward "what extracts the most value" rather than "what serves the player." The principle provides a fixed reference point that future AI agents and human developers can apply to specific decisions, even when those decisions weren't anticipated in the original design.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.11): The §32 monetization rewrite captures a model that took several conversations to build correctly. The early versions of §32 had a $10 ARPU target, $2-per-item cosmetics, and a "30-day hold then fully tradeable" rule for discontinued drops — each of which would have been a real RMT vector or an extraction mechanism that the rest of the doc's "earned legibility" philosophy would have contradicted. The v12.11 model addresses each of these explicitly: ARPU is no longer a target (cost recovery is the target), cosmetics are $1 each and account-bound, and prestige drops are still tradeable but with no royalties and the trade routed through the standard §39 marketplace.
