The cosmetics gating mechanism is the load-bearing v12.11 design decision — cosmetics are sold only to active Supporter Pass holders, which structurally guarantees that no cosmetic-buying player is ever rate-limited (because having the Pass is the prerequisite for buying cosmetics in the first place). Earlier iterations considered symmetric and asymmetric dual-redemption credit mechanics; both were rejected because the gating model solves the same problem more cleanly with no credit accounting at all. The price point is $2/month — deliberately below the comparison threshold for established subscription games — chosen because this is a solo-developer game in a saturated market and the competitive positioning matters more than per-player extraction. The published cost FAQ is the commitment device that protects future-developer from accusations of greed if costs rise and prices have to rise. The no-pay-to-win commitment list in §32.9 is intentionally exhaustive because the negative-space definition of the model is more important than the positive-space definition — it's easier to enforce "we will never add X" than to enforce "the model is exactly Y." The Paying-Player-Never-Worse principle in §32.11 is the design tenet that anchors every future monetization decision, and it exists because the implementation phase will involve many small decisions that need a fixed reference point to drift against. The whole §32 ship is small and stable on purpose: the game is the content pipeline, and monetization is the small machinery that keeps the game running, not a parallel product that pulls effort away from the core work.]`

---


### §33. Narrative, Alignment, and Lore Delivery

> **Core Premise — The Unlikely Hero:** Hemi Bros are blank slates. They arrive with no memory, no destiny, no prophecy. The world has no reason to believe in them — and says so. Bros are common. They wash up. They leave. Most don't survive past the Meadow. The ones who do become something. Every player's progression is a personal origin story, not a predetermined hero's journey.

`[DESIGN INTENT: The "unlikely hero" frame transforms tutorial quests into acts of defiance. The same "visit 3 buildings" quest feels different when the NPC giving it doesn't expect you to finish. Identity is built through systems — stat training, alignment choices, clan, gear, reputation — not through scripted narrative. The lore exists to give those systems emotional weight.]`

#### §33.1 World Premise — The Fracture

The world exists in a state of tonal fracture. It was once whole. Something split it. The zones are not biomes — they are emotional residue. The elements are not natural phenomena — they are aspects of a whole that was shattered.

This premise is **never stated explicitly in-game.** It is conveyed entirely through Echo Stones (fragmentary single-sentence lore objects), Zone Keeper dialogue, environmental design, and the elemental collision system itself. Players piece together the world's history through exploration and community discussion.

**The ten elements as emotional aspects:**

| Element | Aspect | Zone Expression |
|---------|--------|-----------------|
| Flame | Rage | Ember Fields burn because something raged here |
| Frost | Grief | Frozen Shore is cold because something grieved here |
| Water | Memory | Tidal Caves rise and fall like remembering and forgetting |
| Venom | Betrayal | Mistwood poisons because trust was broken here |
| Storm | Chaos | Thunder Peaks crack because order shattered here |
| Stone | Defiance | Deep Hollows are dark because something refused to move |
| Wind | Loss | Sky Reaches are empty because something left |
| Flora | Yearning | Verdant Wilds overgrow because life reached for more with nothing to shape it |
| Dark | Fear | Shadow Sanctum hides because something cannot face itself |
| Light | Truth | Radiant Heights blind because some truths are too much at once |
| Eclipse | Wholeness | Dark + Light = the fracture healed (§33.5) |

**Implementation note:** These metaphors are for creative direction only. They inform Echo Stone writing, Zone Keeper dialogue, and visual design. They are never communicated as game mechanics or explicit lore text.

**Zone Emotional Audio Layer (v12.26):** Each zone's ambient soundscape subtly shifts toward its emotional aspect after extended play — a thin emotional tone layers underneath the standard ambient after ~3 minutes in the zone. The shift is below conscious notice for most players; dedicated players feel the zones differently on long sessions. Full specification (per-zone instrument, register, dynamic level, fade timing) in `creative-reference.md` §2 Audio.

#### §33.2 Lore Delivery Systems

**Echo Stones** — Environmental collectibles.

| Field | Type | Desc |
|-------|------|------|
| stone_id | String | Unique ID |
| zone_id | String | Zone where stone appears |
| x, y | Int | Tile coordinates |
| text | String | Single sentence. Max 120 characters. |
| depth | 1-10 | Which depth tier |
| discovered | Bool | Per-player tracking |

Interaction: Tap stone → single sentence fades in over the game world (no panel, no popup). 2-second display. Text logged to Discovery Journal. Stone dims after reading (still visible, not re-readable until zone reset).

Spawn density: 4 per zone per depth tier (standard/endgame), 6 per Guardian zone. Total target: ~320 Echo Stones across all zones.

**Zone Keepers** — Ambient NPCs. One per combat zone.

| Field | Type | Desc |
|-------|------|------|
| keeper_id | String | Unique ID |
| zone_id | String | Zone placement |
| name | String | e.g., "The Still One" (Frozen Shore) |
| lines | String[3] | Exactly 3 dialogue lines, cycled |
| alignment_lines | Map<Good/Evil, String> | Bonus line if player has alignment |

Zone Keepers cannot be attacked, traded with, or given quests. They exist only to speak. They are drawn with the same pixel-art composite system as town NPCs but with muted, zone-matched palettes.

#### §33.3 Alignment System (v13 REPLACEMENT — Four Registers)

**Canonical Good/Neutral/Evil axis is replaced by four independent action registers.** See §0.3 for the framing principle. Alignment is character expression, not moral evaluation.

**The four registers:**

| Register | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Responsible** | Takes care, protects, follows through on commitments |
| **Mischievous** | Teases, pranks, bends rules playfully without harm |
| **Cool** | Detached, reserved, doesn't get involved |
| **Ruthless** | Chooses hard efficiency, willing to harm |

None is "good" or "bad." Each is an identity players can inhabit.

**Alignment tracking:** Four separate counters accumulate as the player makes capstone choices per §25.1.

**Sources of register shift:**
- Capstone quest Responsible path: +1 responsibleCount
- Capstone quest Mischievous path: +1 mischievousCount
- Capstone quest Cool path: +1 coolCount
- Capstone quest Ruthless path: +1 ruthlessCount
- Ferryman quest completion: all four counts are preserved (the Ferryman doesn't shift alignment but gates on spread; see §33.6)

**Maximum range:** 0-8 per register, up to 8 total choices distributed across 8 NPC chains.

**Display color per dominant register:**

| Dominant Register | Label | Inspect Color |
|-------------------|-------|:-------------:|
| Responsible (pure, 8 of 8) | The Steadfast | Gold (#F1C40F) |
| Mischievous (pure, 8 of 8) | The Mischief-Maker | Rose (#E91E63) |
| Cool (pure, 8 of 8) | The Unmoved | Slate (#546E7A) |
| Ruthless (pure, 8 of 8) | The Ruthless | Dark red (#7F1D1D) |
| Dominant (majority but not pure) | [Dominant title] | Muted version of register color |
| Mixed (2-register split) | Compound (see §54.1) | Blended color |
| Balanced (3+ registers) | "The Complete Bro" / "The Unlikely One" | Prismatic |

**Alignment is visible:** Inspect card shows per-register dot counts (e.g., "Responsible ●●● Mischievous ●● Cool ● Ruthless ●○"), dominant label, and the aggregate title earned. This is social information — other players see your register composition, not a moral score.

**Alignment does NOT affect:**
- Base stats, damage, HP, or any combat number
- Gear equip requirements
- Zone access
- Drop rates

**Alignment DOES affect:**
- NPC dialogue tone (§19.1, §33.7) — companion NPCs react differently per dominant register
- Zone Keeper bonus lines (§33.2) — Keepers have register-specific lines
- Titles earned (§25.1, §54.1) — each capstone path grants a register-specific title; compound and dominant titles emerge from accumulated register choices
- Inspect card social identity — other players read you by your register mix
- Ferryman hidden quest eligibility (§33.6) — requires at least 3 of 4 registers represented
- Eclipse cosmetic variant (§33.5) — expanded to register-specific variants
- Endgame ending (§53.4) — five endings: four dominant-register endings (Hero / Trickster / Arbiter / Sovereign) plus Weaver for balanced registers (v14.0.5)
- Ritual gesture mapping (§57.3) — each register performs a register-specific gesture (Opening / Consuming / Dancing / Watching) that contains all four registers at cosmological scale
- Cosmological pole unification (§57.5, §57.12) — dominant register maps to Light / Dark / Eclipse-warm / Eclipse-reserved; balanced registers map to the Convergence pole (v14.0.5)
- Convergence perception (§56) — each register sees the Convergence differently

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): Canonical Good/Evil binary with Neutral fallback implicitly ranked morality. Four independent action registers, none of which maps to "correct," turn alignment into character expression rather than moral evaluation. The Mischievous register specifically gives Brotown's humor a place to live as moral identity — the mischievous-warmth Bro previously had no path that fits them. Each register produces distinct titles, NPC reactions, endgames, and — under v14.0 — distinct ritual gestures that unify with distinct cosmological poles per §57. The "no mechanical effect" rule canonical to alignment is preserved — registers influence social surfaces and narrative gates only, never combat or progression math. Per §0.3 framing.]`

#### §33.4 Alignment Data Model (v13 REPLACEMENT — Four Registers)

```
rpg._alignment = {
    responsibleCount: 0,
    mischievousCount: 0,
    coolCount: 0,
    ruthlessCount: 0,
    choices: {},              // { quest_id: 'responsible'|'mischievous'|'cool'|'ruthless' }
    titlesEarned: [],         // title strings from capstone paths
    echoStonesFound: [],      // stone_id array
    ferrymanUnlocked: false,  // true when at least 3 of 4 registers have count >= 1
    dominantRegister: null,   // computed on display: highest count, or 'balanced' if tie or 3+ equal
    endingAlignment: null,    // 'hero'|'trickster'|'arbiter'|'sovereign' — set at ending
};
```

**Dominant register computation.** The display register is computed on demand:
- If one register's count strictly exceeds all others: that register is dominant.
- If two registers tie for highest and others are lower: "mixed" display (compound title eligible).
- If three or four registers tie for highest: "balanced" display (Complete Bro framing).
- If no choices have been made: "untested" display (new character).

**Ending alignment computation at True Guardian.** At the moment the True Guardian achievement fires, the four counts are snapshotted and locked into `endingAlignment`:
- **Hero:** responsibleCount strictly greater than every other count
- **Trickster:** mischievousCount strictly greater than every other count
- **Arbiter:** coolCount strictly greater than every other count
- **Sovereign:** ruthlessCount strictly greater than every other count
- **Weaver (v14.0.5 NEW):** no single register strictly exceeds all others — any tie at the top (two, three, or four registers sharing the highest count, including the balanced 1/1/1/1 case) resolves to the **Weaver** ending, pole **Convergence**. See §53.4 for the Weaver ending's creative content and §57.7a for the Weaver's canonical Naming sentence.

`endingAlignment` is now a five-value enum: `'hero' | 'trickster' | 'arbiter' | 'sovereign' | 'weaver'`.

**Why no tiebreaker (v14.0.5 correction).** The prior v13 spec used the ordering `Responsible > Mischievous > Cool > Ruthless` to break ties into the Hero ending, on the reasoning that "the more socially-collaborative register wins a tie" and pure balanced characters should receive "the classical Unlikely Hero thesis." This encoded exactly the moral hierarchy §0.3 and §0.4 spent their length rejecting — Responsible beat all others; Ruthless lost to all others; a balanced 1/1/1/1 Bro was classified as *most truly* the Hero. That misreads the four-register system. A balanced character is the most thesis-aligned character of all: they hold four oppositions simultaneously rather than resolving them into a dominant. The Weaver ending (§53.4) and its Convergence pole (§57.7a) give them an ending that reflects that structure. Per §0.4, balanced registers are not a tiebreaker problem — they are the deepest expression of the thesis and deserve their own ending, not a defaulted one.

**Persistence:** Saved to localStorage as part of RPG state. Broadcast via presence for inspect card visibility.

**Migration note (for v12.x → v13):** Canonical characters have single `score` and `goodCount/evilCount/neutralCount`. Migration maps: `goodCount` → `responsibleCount`, `evilCount` → `ruthlessCount`, `neutralCount` → `coolCount`. No characters receive Mischievous choices through migration (that register must be earned through new v13 capstone choices). Existing `titlesEarned` entries are preserved verbatim.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Four independent counters enable compound titles (The Steadfast Rascal = Responsible + Mischievous, The Unmoved Hand = Cool + Ruthless) that capture genuine identity mixes. The three-of-four Ferryman gate is harder and more meaningful than the canonical mixed-alignment gate — it requires genuine exploration across registers rather than a single contrarian choice. Migration preserves canonical character integrity while introducing the new register space as earnable through continued play.]`

#### §33.5 The Duality Payoff — Eclipse and Alignment (v13 REVISED for Four Registers)

The entire game teaches that elemental combination is destruction. Flame meets Frost → Steam (damage). Every collision is violent. Players learn this grammar over 100 levels.

Eclipse (Dark + Light) is mechanically different: it deals massive damage AND heals allies AND cleanses all statuses. It is the only collision that is simultaneously destructive and restorative. This is the tonal duality — the game's thematic capstone expressed through its core combat system. **v13 reinforcement:** per §9.4 v13 REPLACEMENT, Light alone no longer heals; healing occurs only through Eclipse. This makes the Dark+Light duality the unique source of restoration in the game.

**Eclipse Cosmetic Variant (v13 REVISED, v14.0.5 CORRECTED).** The canonical True Eclipse variant required mixed Good+Evil alignment. v13 replaces this with **register-specific Eclipse variants**:

| Player's Dominant Register | True Eclipse Variant |
|---------------------------|----------------------|
| Responsible | **Anchored Eclipse** — steady, symmetrical, holds longest |
| Mischievous | **Playful Eclipse** — shimmer animates, tricks light into motion |
| Cool | **Still Eclipse** — minimal animation, dignified clean lines |
| Ruthless | **Severe Eclipse** — sharp edges, dramatic dark-gold contrast |
| Balanced (3+ registers) | **Complete Eclipse** — sequential cycle through all four pure variants (v14.0.5) |

All variants persist for 5s instead of the standard 2s. Purely cosmetic. They signal to other players: *this person has held this particular register.*

**Complete Eclipse — sequential cycle structure (v14.0.5).** The Complete Eclipse holds for the standard 5-second duration but is not a single layered visual. It *cycles sequentially* through the four pure variants at 1.25 seconds per variant, in this canonical order: Anchored → Playful → Still → Severe. Each variant renders fully as itself during its 1.25s window — the Anchored phase is the Anchored Eclipse exactly, sharp-edged and symmetrical; the Playful phase is the Playful Eclipse exactly, with full shimmer animation; and so on. Transitions between phases are crisp cuts, not crossfades — each register is held, released, and replaced by the next register held.

**Why the sequential cycle (v14.0.5 correction).** The prior v13 spec for the Complete Eclipse was "all four variants layered together, prismatic." That is *synthesis*, not unification — four aesthetics dissolved into a composite that subsumes them. Per §0.4, Brotown's thesis is explicitly the opposite: unification is the permanent holding of opposites, not their merger. A layered-composite Complete Eclipse would have done at the visual layer exactly what the thesis rules out at every other layer. The sequential cycle preserves each register's full identity — the Anchored phase is not diluted by the Playful phase; they are held successively rather than blended. The balanced player's Eclipse is visibly *the one that refuses to collapse the registers*. This is the thesis expressed at cosmetic-variant scale.

**Unlock condition (v13):** Players who have triggered Eclipse AND have made at least one capstone choice in their dominant register unlock that register's Eclipse variant. The Complete Eclipse unlock requires triggering Eclipse AND having at least 3 of 4 registers represented.

**No alignment gate for unlock.** The canonical Shadow Sanctum / Radiant Heights alignment-shift Zone Keeper mechanic is preserved but serves a different role — the Keeper offers a cross-register dialogue moment that grants a choice in a register the player has not yet chosen, unlocking that register's Eclipse variant if eventually chosen. No forced alignment change; voluntary register exploration.

**Creative direction companion.** Full visual/audio creative direction for the five Eclipse variants — per-register shader and audio treatment, canonical cycle order for the Complete Eclipse, observer register, authoring-scope estimate — lives in `creative-reference.md` §8.61 (v5.42 CANONICAL). The GDD owns the mechanical spec (what the variant is, how it's unlocked, its 5s duration); the CR owns the experiential spec (how each variant feels, what register it lands in, how the cycle reads).

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13, v14.0.5 correction): Register-specific Eclipse variants make the cosmetic reward reflect *who you are*, not just *that you had a mix*. The Complete Eclipse becomes the rare prestige variant — earnable only by players who genuinely explored three or more of the four registers. Per §9.4 v13 REPLACEMENT, healing-requires-Eclipse makes this cosmetic the visible signal of the one who carries the game's sole restorative power. The v14.0.5 correction rewrites the Complete Eclipse's visual from a layered blend (which would have been synthesis) to a sequential cycle (which is unification). The difference is load-bearing — a blend would have taught the player that holding multiple registers means merging them into a composite, which is the exact misreading of the thesis that §0.4 rules out. The sequential cycle teaches the correct reading: hold the registers as themselves, move through them, refuse to let any subsume the others. This also produces a more visually striking cosmetic — four crisp variants rendered in succession are more legible and more beautiful than one muddled blend. Thesis and aesthetics align.]`

#### §33.6 The Ferryman's Hidden Quest (v13 REVISED GATE)

**v13 Unlock condition:** All 8 NPC chains completed AND at least 3 of 4 registers have count ≥ 1 (register spread ≥ 3). Characters dominant in a single register or split purely two ways cannot access this quest. Canonical gate `goodCount >= 1 AND evilCount >= 1` is replaced by the broader spread requirement.

**Quest: "The Crossing"** (unchanged mechanics)
- **NPC:** The Ferryman
- **Desc:** Cross the Lawless Land from spawn fence to the far wall and return. Do not kill anyone.
- **Check:** `(rpg._questFlags?.lawlessCrossing && rpg._questFlags?.lawlessCrossingKills === 0)`
- **Reward:** "The Unlikely One" title (prismatic color, shifts through all element colors). All four register counts are preserved — the Ferryman doesn't shift alignment, he gates on spread. FERRYMAN_QUEST_AP(25) achievement points.
- **Ferryman dialogue (v13 REVISED tonal range per §0.1):**
  - Start (grave): *"You've done responsible things. Mischievous things. Cool things. Ruthless things. I don't judge. But I have one question: can you cross my land without becoming what it wants you to be?"*
  - Start (dry): *"You've picked all four sides at some point. Impressive or indecisive, hard to tell. We'll find out."*
  - Progress (observational): *"Still alive? Don't kill anyone. That's the only rule I've ever had."*
  - Progress (wry): *"You're making good time. And not killing anyone. I'd congratulate you, but you're not done yet."*
  - Complete (grave): *"They all end up here eventually. The good ones just take longer. You? You're something else."*
  - Complete (dry): *"Didn't kill anyone. Full stop. I'm keeping that in mind next time someone tells me Bros can't be trusted."*

**v13 ADDITIVE — Silent companion.** The Ferryman gives you a silent companion NPC to escort across Lawless. If you don't attack anyone, the companion is safe and arrives at the far wall with you. If you attack anyone, the companion leaves (quest fails regardless of kill-count state). At the end, the companion speaks one line and disappears. Companion names include tonal flavor in their display — *"Bro Thomas (Nervous)"* gets a grave final line *"Thanks. I thought I was going to die out there."*; *"Bro Carson (Trembling)"* gets a dry final line *"That was... something. Thanks for not murdering me."* Companion identity and final line are randomized per attempt to preserve the ritual quality.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): The three-of-four Ferryman gate is harder and more meaningful — it requires the player to have genuinely explored across alignment registers, not just taken a contrarian choice once. The silent companion raises stakes: restraint protects *someone else*, not just your own quest state. The companion's brief final line gives the quest's end a felt moment beyond the canonical reward. Per §0.1, Ferryman dialogue carries tonal range — he's the anchor character, but even anchors can be dry sometimes. The "Unlikely One" title becomes rarer and more meaningful because it represents someone who genuinely explored across the moral space.]`

#### §33.7 NPC Tone — The Unlikely Hero Frame (v13 ADDITIVE — Four-Register Variants)

All NPC opening dialogue is written with the assumption that the player will fail. They have seen Bros come and go. The town is full of optimists who have been disappointed.

**Mayor Bro** canonical tone shift (preserved): *"Another one washed up. They never last."* → (after quest 1) *"Huh. You're still here."* → (after quest 3) *"I stopped betting against you."*

**The Ferryman** is the exception. He doesn't judge. He doesn't hope or despair. He takes everyone's gold and asks no questions. His neutrality is the anchor.

**NPC dialogue tables (canonical)** include three variants per line: `unlikely_early` (dismissive/skeptical), `unlikely_mid` (surprised/warming), `unlikely_late` (respectful/invested). The phase transition is gated by quest completion count, not level.

**v13 ADDITIVE — Four-register tone variants.** Each phase's dialogue gains four-register variants based on the player's dominant alignment. Each major NPC has a dialogue table of form `{phase, register}` → line pool. Per §0.1, each variant includes both grave and wry register-appropriate options.

Register variants per phase (example: Mayor Bro late phase):
- **Responsible** (Steadfast) dominant: *"I stopped betting against you."* (grave) / *"You showed up. Every time. I respect that."* (warm)
- **Mischievous** (Mischief-Maker) dominant: *"You're still here? I'd say you're taking this seriously, but you're not, are you."* (wry) / *"Every time you come back, something breaks. I've stopped trying to figure out if that's good or bad."* (warm)
- **Cool** (Unmoved) dominant: *"You crossed over. I noticed."* (observational) / *"You don't say much. Neither do I. I think we understand each other."* (dry)
- **Ruthless** dominant: *"You made it this far. That means something. I'm not sure what, yet."* (wary) / *"Most people who chose your path aren't still here. You are. That's worth a conversation."* (grave)

Critically, **no register is treated as villainy.** The Ruthless Bro is respected for their choices even when feared. Brotown doesn't moralize against its players.

**Content authoring scope.** Per-NPC register variants expand the canonical content pool. Initial v13 ship authors variants for the 6 most-visible town NPCs (Mayor, Blacksmith, Ferryman, Enchanter, Tavern Keeper, Herbalist) across all three phases and all four registers — 6 × 3 × 4 = 72 line pools with tonal range per §0.1. Remaining NPCs receive register variants as ongoing content updates.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): Canonical NPC tone progresses through quest count. Adding alignment-register framing makes each NPC respond to who you are, not just what you've done. Tonal range per §0.1 ensures the four registers don't collapse into uniform voices — the Mayor's late-phase Mischievous variant is dry-mocking while his Responsible variant is sincere-warm, even though both reach the player at the same quest count. No register is framed as villain; this is critical — the game honors its Ruthless players as specific identities, not as morally failed Bros.]`

#### §33.8 Narrative Tuning

| Variable | Default | Min | Max |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|
| ECHO_STONE_DISPLAY_TIME | 2.0s | 1.5s | 4.0s |
| ECHO_STONE_FADE_IN | 0.5s | 0.3s | 1.0s |
| ECHO_STONE_PER_ZONE_DEPTH | 4 | 2 | 6 |
| ALIGNMENT_REP_SHIFT | 50 | 25 | 100 |
| FERRYMAN_QUEST_AP | 25 | 15 | 50 |
| TRUE_ECLIPSE_MARK_DURATION | 5.0s | 3.0s | 8.0s |
| NPC_TONE_PHASE_2_QUESTS | 5 | 3 | 8 |
| NPC_TONE_PHASE_3_QUESTS | 15 | 10 | 20 |
| FERRYMAN_REGISTER_SPREAD_REQUIRED (v13) | 3 | 2 | 4 |


---


## PART X — BRO TOWN EXPANSION SYSTEMS (§34–§52)

### §34. Desktop Controls

Full keyboard+mouse control scheme, auto-detected via `(pointer:fine)` media query. See §15.5 for complete keybind table.

**Detection:** `window.matchMedia('(pointer:fine)').matches` on init. Any keyboard input also sets `S._isDesktop = true`.

**UI Changes on Desktop:**
- Touch joystick zones hidden via CSS `@media (pointer:fine)` rule
- Crosshair cursor on game canvas
- Stat HUD (HP/Mana/Stamina bars) replaces mobile joystick resource donuts at bottom-left
- Keybind hint overlay at bottom-left with semi-transparent key labels
- Building interact buttons show `[E]` keyboard badge
- Aim reticle drawn at mouse world position on canvas

**Dodge Direction Priority:** WASD keys → mouse aim angle → current facing direction.

**Shield Toggle:** Q press activates shield, Q release drops shield. Shield faces cursor direction.

#### §34.1 Desktop Power-User Controls (v12.51 CANONICAL)

Desktop play surfaces several power-user affordances that are natural on keyboard+mouse but awkward or impossible on touch. v12.51 formalizes these as first-class desktop features — not plugin-like additions, but integral to the desktop play experience. All are read-only convenience layers on existing systems; none alter mechanics.

**Weapon stash keybinds 1-8.** Desktop keys `1` through `8` map to the player's 8 weapon stash quick-slots. Pressing a key instantly swaps the currently equipped weapon with the weapon in that stash slot. The swap is a standard inventory operation (identical to the drag-swap path in the inventory UI) — no stat changes, no cooldown, no combat restriction beyond the standard weapon-swap mechanics. Empty slots are no-ops (key press does nothing).

Quick-swap enables desktop combat fluidity: a player can cycle between Greatsword (slot 1), Bow (slot 2), and Staff (slot 3) mid-combat by tapping keys rather than opening the inventory menu. This closes a meaningful desktop-vs-mobile gap — mobile players can only swap by opening the stash menu, which breaks flow; desktop keybinds restore flow without giving a stat advantage.

**Weapon stash slot count — WEAPON_STASH_QUICK_SLOTS(8).** The 8-slot quick-access count matches keybind `1` through `8`. Players can store additional weapons in the bank (unlimited), but only the first 8 slots are keybind-accessible. Slots 9+ require inventory-menu access for swap. This is a design-level cap, not a bank storage cap — bank capacity is separate and governed by §23.4 stash expansion tiers.

**Tab target cycling.** Pressing `Tab` cycles the lock-on target through enemies within the camera frustum (~90° forward arc from player-to-camera direction). Cycling order: closest to farthest among visible enemies. `Shift+Tab` reverses direction (farthest to closest). Unlocks if no enemy is within the frustum. Matches the existing §5 combat lock-on system — Tab is an input alternative to aim-based auto-lock.

**Hover-tooltips on inventory items.** Hovering the mouse over any inventory item for HOVER_TOOLTIP_DELAY(0.6s) shows a full item-detail tooltip: stats, crafter attribution, weapon history (if §4.11 applies), quality grade, hardness level, gem slots, and — for crafted items — the full §18.3 attribution chain. Tooltip appears adjacent to cursor, positioned to remain within viewport. Fades out on mouse-leave.

**Open questions — customizable keybinds.** Phase 2 scope per Entry 51 recommendation. Launch with fixed binds; player feedback will inform which binds warrant customization. v12.51 explicitly does NOT introduce a keybind-remapping UI; adding one should be a later ship with proper scope.

#### §34.2 Screenshot Mode (v12.51 CANONICAL)

`F12` toggles Screenshot Mode — a dedicated capture interface for desktop players to produce shareable still images of their gameplay.

**Behavior on F12 press:**

1. **UI hides.** All HUD elements (stat bars, keybind overlays, chat, minimap, floating damage numbers, status icons) hide. Only the gameplay world renders.
2. **Cursor switches to framing reticle.** Small reticle overlays show available framing positions.
3. **F12 toggles back to normal UI.** No auto-exit on inactivity — players can compose shots at leisure.
4. **Game continues running.** Screenshot Mode does not pause gameplay. This is a deliberate choice — players capturing combat moments need the combat to continue. A player who wants to capture a still frame of a specific pose should time their shot.

**Framing options.** Four modes selected via number keys in Screenshot Mode:

- **Centered** (`1`) — camera centered on player character, standard perspective
- **Left-third** (`2`) — character positioned at left-third of frame (composition rule-of-thirds)
- **Top-third** (`3`) — character positioned at top-third of frame (landscape-emphasis composition)
- **Free-roam** (`4`) — camera detaches from character, free-move via WASD (bounded to 40 units from character to prevent map scouting exploits)

**Optional filters.** Four filters selected via `F1`-`F4` in Screenshot Mode:

- **None** (`F1`, default) — unfiltered render
- **Vintage** (`F2`) — desaturated with warm tint
- **High-contrast** (`F3`) — pushed blacks, pushed whites, vivid midtones
- **Dreamy** (`F4`) — soft bloom, slight gaussian blur on non-foreground, saturation boost

**Capture trigger.** `Enter` captures the current frame with selected framing + filter. Image saved to user's configured screenshot folder (OS-dependent default) and simultaneously copied to clipboard.

**Clipboard share.** A "Copy to clipboard" indicator briefly shows in the corner on capture — players can immediately paste into Discord, social media, messaging apps. No game-specific sharing infrastructure; standard OS clipboard.

**Mobile fallback — tap-and-hold UI hide.** Mobile devices receive a limited screenshot experience: tap-and-hold on any UI element (3-second hold) temporarily hides the HUD for 5 seconds, allowing the player to use their device's native screenshot function to capture a clean gameplay image. No filters, no framing options, no in-game capture — mobile players use the phone OS's native screenshot capability with UI hidden. This pattern respects mobile UX conventions (phone screenshots are OS-level, not app-level) while providing equivalent capability.

**Screenshot Mode tuning.**

| Variable | Default | Min | Max | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| SCREENSHOT_FREE_ROAM_MAX_DIST | 40 units | 20 | 80 | Bounds on free-roam camera distance from character |
| MOBILE_UI_HIDE_DURATION | 5s | 3 | 10 | Mobile tap-and-hold UI-hide window |
| MOBILE_UI_HIDE_HOLD_THRESHOLD | 3s | 2 | 5 | Hold duration to trigger UI hide |

#### §34.3 Replay Clip Library (v12.51 CANONICAL, v13 DELETED — Replaced by §34.2 Auto-Capture + OS-Native Recording)

**v13 DELETION.** The canonical Replay Clip Library is removed. The use cases it served are covered by:

1. **§34.2 Screenshot Mode (canonical preserved, v13 ADDITIVE auto-capture)** — big-moment screenshots auto-capture on threshold events (Godly reveals, first Apex kill, Hardness 5 success, alignment capstones, etc.). The auto-capture threshold list is taken from the canonical Replay Clip Library trigger list but produces still images instead of video clips. Screenshots save to the player's annotatable personal gallery.

2. **OS-native video recording.** Players who want motion captures use their operating system's native screen recording capability (Windows Game Bar, macOS screen recording, Android/iOS screen recording). The game does not provide in-engine video recording infrastructure.

**Why the deletion.** Video infrastructure (H.264 encoding, Cloudflare R2 storage, share-link hosting, moderation pipeline) is engineering-heavy for a solo-dev team. The canonical 15-second buffer, per-clip encoding, 200MB client storage, watermark system, share-rate-limiting, and OS-specific codec paths represent significant ongoing engineering and cost. Deletion is scope honesty — cut what's expensive to maintain when cheaper alternatives exist.

**Migration.** Players with saved clips from canonical Replay Library at v13 migration retain their existing clips (existing storage preserved). New clips cannot be created via the removed system. The canonical Replay Library UI is removed from the inspect panel; migration-era clips become accessible via a read-only "Legacy Clips" view for 90 days after v13 ship, then expire.

**Auto-capture screenshot integration (per §34.2 v13).** The canonical Replay Library's trigger list (Godly reveals, Apex falls, Arena match wins, Hardness 5 success, etc.) becomes the auto-screenshot trigger list. Each trigger captures a still image at the triggering moment (or a burst of 3-5 stills across the moment, per trigger configuration) to the player's screenshot gallery. Gallery is annotatable per §34.2 v13.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13 deletion): The Replay Library was ambitious and well-designed, but its engineering cost (H.264 encoding pipeline, clip hosting infrastructure, share-link system, mobile parity across iOS/Android codecs) is disproportionate to player value at the current scope. Screenshots cover ~80% of the "I want to capture this moment" use case at ~10% of the engineering cost. OS-native video recording covers the remaining 20% without requiring the game to maintain video infrastructure. The canonical auto-capture trigger list is preserved — players still get automatic captures of their Godly reveals, Apex kills, etc. — just as still images rather than video clips. This is scope honesty: cut what's expensive when cheaper alternatives cover the use case. Players wanting video can still capture it; they just use the OS's native capability.]`

### §35. Encyclopedia

Four-tab knowledge compendium. Persisted to localStorage per discovery set. Accessed via 📖 button in action bar.

#### §35.1 Bestiary Tab

Tracks discovered monsters as `archetype:zoneId` keys. New entry triggers floating "📖 New Bestiary Entry!" notification. Discovery fires on first kill of each archetype in each zone.

**Data model:** `discoveredMonsters` Set of strings (`'fodder:meadow'`, `'brute:ember'`, etc.). Stored in `localStorage.bt_bestiary`.

#### §35.2 Codex Tab

Tracks discovered elemental collisions. First collision trigger fires one-time tutorial tooltip. Subsequent discoveries show standard Codex entry with collision name, setup element, trigger element, and effect description.

**Tactical hint:** Each Codex entry includes a single-sentence question that hints at the collision's strategic application without prescribing an answer. Questions are interpretive — the player thinks through the answer themselves. Hints stored in `collision-config.json` per collision entry.

**Hint examples (full set lives in collision-config.json):**

| Collision | Hint |
|-----------|------|
| Steam (Flame+Frost) | *"What happens to a Brute that can't see?"* |
| Quench (Flame+Water) | *"What if the fire was the problem all along?"* |
| Toxic Fumes (Flame+Venom) | *"What stays behind after you leave?"* |
| Overcharge (Flame+Storm) | *"What if the spark had fuel?"* |
| Magma (Flame+Stone) | *"What burns long after the fire goes out?"* |
| Firestorm (Flame+Wind) | *"What if the fire could reach the ones behind?"* |
| Flash Freeze (Frost+Water) | *"What's worse than being frozen?"* |
| Shatter (Frost+Venom) | *"What breaks when it's frozen?"* |
| Hailstorm (Frost+Storm) | *"What if the cold could chase?"* |
| Conduit (Water+Storm) | *"What if they were all wet?"* |
| Petrify (Venom+Stone) | *"What happens to something that can't move and can't heal?"* |
| Seismic Pulse (Storm+Stone) | *"What if everyone staggered at once?"* |
| Dilute (Water+Venom) | *"What washes poison away?"* |
| Baptism (Light+Water) | *"Who else is hurting right now?"* |
| Divine Strike (Light+Storm) | *"What if judgment only needed to land once?"* |
| Eclipse (Dark+Light) | *"What if destroying and healing were the same thing?"* |

Remaining collision hints follow the same pattern — one question per collision that points toward the collision's tactical niche without stating it directly. Questions use the same tone as Echo Stones: short, present-tense, emotional rather than informational.

**Data model:** `discoveredCollisions` Set of collision IDs (`'steam'`, `'quench'`, etc.). Stored in `localStorage.bt_codex`.

**Zone-clustered presentation (v12.32).** The Codex's default view groups collision entries by signature zone per §10.5c — each zone's section lists the collisions in its signature curriculum with discovery state (✓/○), letting the player see what the zone teaches and track progress against each zone's curriculum. Discovered entries show full detail; undiscovered entries show element-pair names only (preserving discovery surprise for the effect). Discovered collisions annotate the zone of first discovery ("first discovered in Ember Fields") when viewed in a different zone's tab. A flat-list view toggle (Accessibility → Encyclopedia) switches to the pre-v12.32 sorted-by-ID presentation for completionist browsing. Both views use the same underlying `discoveredCollisions` data.

#### §35.3 Materials Tab

Tracks discovered life skill materials as `skill:tierName` keys (e.g., `'mining:Copper Ore'`). Populated on first gather of each material type.

**Data model:** `discoveredMaterials` Set of strings. Stored in `localStorage.bt_materials`.

#### §35.4 Zones Tab

Tracks visited zones. Discovery fires on zone transition. Displays zone name, element, level range, and atmospheric info.

#### §35.5 Flavor Text (v12.29 CANONICAL)

Every encyclopedia entry — bestiary, codex, materials, zones — carries a **flavor text** field in addition to its mechanical information. Flavor text is narrative rather than tactical: a single sentence or short fragment that gives the entry's subject a suggestive identity rather than a strategic utility. Tactical hints (e.g., Codex collision questions in §35.2) are a separate field and live alongside flavor text, not replacing it.

**Discoverability gate.** Flavor text does not appear on first encyclopedia-entry unlock. It is revealed only after the player has made *meaningful* engagement with the subject:

- **Bestiary:** ~10 kills of that monster archetype in the relevant zone (KILLS_FOR_FLAVOR threshold, stored in `encyclopedia-flavor-config.json` per the "content-threshold constants live in the config file rather than in §20" rule described below).
- **Zones:** 3+ visits to that zone, each at least 5 minutes in duration.
- **Materials:** 20+ harvests of that material tier.
- **Codex (collisions):** 5+ successful triggers of that specific collision.

The specific thresholds live in `encyclopedia-flavor-config.json` (v12.29.1 content ship) and can be tuned without code changes. Thresholds should err toward slightly-more-than-incidental engagement — flavor text should feel *earned* rather than automatic.

**Narrative register.** Flavor text is fragmentary, suggestive, interpretive. It never explains a mechanic, never names gameplay numbers, never provides tactical advice. It gestures at the subject's nature without resolving it. Examples drawn from the authoring pool:

- **Ember Brute (Depth 2):** *"The Ember Fields remember something that burned for a long time. These brutes are what remained when the burning was done."*
- **Venom Serpent (Mistwood):** *"The serpents here know something about secrets. They were not always like this."*
- **Thunder Peaks (zone):** *"The ground vibrates once per hour. Some players claim to hear a syllable when it happens."*
- **Steel (material tier):** *"The first metal the Bros learned to trust. It is heavier than wood and more patient than bronze."*
- **Petrify (Venom+Stone collision):** *"Something that cannot move cannot heal. That is almost all the mythology the Bros have about this."*

The tone register is calibrated to the game's broader Fracture-lore style (§33) — voice-less, compressed, oblique. Flavor text reinforces Fracture lore without ever stating it directly. This is intentional: Fracture lore is the game's mythological spine, and flavor text is its capillaries reaching every discoverable surface.

**Authoring pool estimates.**

| Category | Approximate Count |
|:---------|:-----------------:|
| Monsters | ~1,500 (378 archetypes × ~4 per-zone variations) |
| Zones | ~220 (66 zones × ~3.3 per-zone descriptions reflecting depth/season) |
| Material tiers | ~80 (20 tiers × ~4 material families) |
| Collisions | ~90 (one per collision) |
| **Total** | **~1,900 flavor entries** |

**Authoring phasing.** The v12.29 ship specifies the system; content authoring is staged:

- **v12.29.1 content ship (Phase 1 launch):** Monster flavor (~1,500 entries). This is the largest pool and the most frequently consumed by players — bestiary engagement is the highest-volume encyclopedia interaction.
- **Phase 2 content ship:** Zone flavor (~220) + collision flavor (~90).
- **Phase 3 content ship:** Material tier flavor (~80).

Each content ship is additive — earlier entries continue working while later pools are authored. Players never see "[flavor unauthored]" placeholder text; entries without flavor simply don't show the flavor field until their pool ships.

**Data model.** Extends the existing encyclopedia JSON schema with a new field:

```json
{
  "entry_id": "ember_brute:ember_fields",
  "name": "Ember Brute",
  // ... existing mechanical fields ...
  "flavor_text": "The Ember Fields remember something that burned for a long time. These brutes are what remained when the burning was done.",
  "flavor_threshold": 10
}
```

The `flavor_threshold` is the category-specific engagement count (kills, visits, harvests, triggers). The client tracks per-entry engagement in `localStorage.bt_encyclopedia_engagement` and reveals the flavor field once the threshold is crossed. No server round-trip required — flavor reveal is purely client-side state.

**Creative direction:** see `creative-reference.md` §8 for the narrative register.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.29): The encyclopedia, as of v12.28, is a mechanical reference — it tells players what monsters exist, what collisions trigger, what materials drop. Flavor text converts it into a *discoverable atmosphere layer*. A player kills their tenth Ember Brute and suddenly the bestiary entry grows a sentence that gestures at the monster's nature. The player who stops to read it gets something meaningful; the player who doesn't stop doesn't miss anything mechanical. This is the shape of good ambient content — valuable to engaged players, invisible to disengaged ones. The engagement gate is load-bearing: flavor text revealed on first encounter becomes decorative noise; flavor text revealed only after the player has spent real time with the subject becomes an acknowledgment. The narrative register is calibrated to Fracture lore's voice-less, compressed, oblique register — the entire game's mythology already operates in this mode, and flavor text extends that voice into every discoverable corner of the encyclopedia. A player who has been through 30 hours of play and has unlocked ~200 flavor entries has absorbed ~200 gestures toward the game's underlying mythology, none of them requiring a cutscene or a dialogue to deliver.]`

### §36. Dungeon Workshop (Phase 3 — Stub, v13 REPLACEMENT — Scope Down to Friend-Validated Authorship)

**v13 REPLACEMENT.** Canonical "open UGC dungeon creation" is replaced by **one-per-month friend-validated dungeon authorship**. A player authors at most one dungeon per server-month. The dungeon must be validated by at least 3 friends (per §30 friends system) before it becomes publicly available. Scope reduction reflects solo-dev/AI-assisted maintenance realism — an open UGC pipeline has moderation, abuse, and content-quality liabilities that exceed the team's capacity.

**Authorship flow (v13):**

1. Player at level 50+ accesses the Dungeon Workshop building on their personal farm.
2. Workshop UI allows authoring one dungeon per month (server-time month). Previous authored dungeons remain published; only the authorship slot is cooldown-gated.
3. Dungeon design uses canonical terrain packs (11 types, boss-gated per canonical), monster packs (12 types, element-gated), and wave-based format (1-10 waves). Canonical §36 dungeon authoring mechanics preserved for the authoring UI itself.
4. On author submit, the dungeon enters a **Friend Validation** state. It is NOT yet publicly playable.
5. The author invites 3+ friends to play-test the dungeon. Each friend plays through and submits either approve or reject (with optional comment).
6. If 3+ friend validations are APPROVE, the dungeon becomes publicly available.
7. If any friend rejects, the author sees the comment and can revise the dungeon (no cooldown penalty for revision within the same authorship slot).
8. Public dungeons are visible to all players via the dungeon selection UI (canonical search/browse preserved).

**Why friend-validated.** Quality control without central moderation. Trolling and broken dungeons get filtered by the author's own social network. Quality dungeons get polished before release. The friend-validation gate also makes the authorship feel like a social project rather than a private submission — authoring is collaborative with trusted playtesters, not an individual publication event.

**Why one per month.** Rate-limiting authorship prevents catalog pollution. A server with 1,000 players and unlimited authorship could produce thousands of dungeons per week; most would be unplayed. One-per-month caps catalog growth to a sustainable ~12 dungeons per active author per year, producing a quality-weighted catalog where each dungeon represents meaningful investment.

**Level-cap discipline preserved.** Canonical "monster level cannot exceed creator's level" rule preserved. Canonical validation engine checks still apply before a dungeon enters Friend Validation state.

**Gold-purchased packs preserved.** Canonical terrain/monster pack pricing (300-800g terrain, 200-1,000g monsters) preserved as gold sinks. Packs are one-time unlocks per player.

**Canonical §36 specifications (boss-gated unlocks, terrain types, monster types, wave mechanics) all preserved.** The replacement is scope — frequency cap plus friend validation — not system redesign.

**Dependencies:** Dungeon system (§18.4), personal farm (§31), friend system (§30), all zone boss clears for pack unlocks.

**Migration.** Existing published dungeons at v13 remain public. The friend-validation gate applies only to new authorship going forward. Canonical high-volume-author characters (who authored many dungeons pre-v13) retain their published catalog but are subject to the one-per-month rate limit going forward.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): Open UGC is tempting design territory but carries heavy maintenance liabilities — moderation, spam, quality control, content abuse, and community escalation patterns. Solo-dev + AI-assisted development cannot sustain open UGC at reasonable quality. Friend-validated + rate-limited authorship preserves the core creative-expression value (players author dungeons and get them into the game) while offloading moderation to the author's own social network (friends don't sign off on broken dungeons). One-per-month caps catalog growth to manageable levels. The system produces ~12 authored dungeons per active author per year — quality-weighted, socially-validated — which is exactly the catalog size and quality profile a solo-dev team can sustain. Canonical creation mechanics (packs, waves, terrain types) preserved verbatim; only the publishing gate is added. The reduction is scope honesty: the system's core value (player-authored dungeon content) is preserved while its operational risks (open moderation, spam) are eliminated.]`

**Full specification deferred to Phase 3 design pass.** Planned location: `phase3-specs/dungeon-workshop.md`.

### §37. Leaderboards

Seven-category leaderboard system. Data fetched from server Durable Object. Accessed via 🏆 button.

#### §37.1 Categories

| Tab | Metric | Sort |
|-----|--------|------|
| Level | Combat level | Descending |
| Life Skills | Total life skill levels | Descending |
| Achievement Points | Total AP | Descending |
| Kills | Monsters killed | Descending |
| Dungeons | Dungeons cleared | Descending |
| Gold | Total gold earned (lifetime) | Descending |
| Playtime | Total play time (seconds) | Descending |

**Server authority:** Leaderboard data stored in a Cloudflare Durable Object. Client polls periodically. Anti-cheat velocity limits (§17) protect leaderboard integrity.

#### §37.2 Personal-Scale Scoping (v12.25 CANONICAL · v12.49 Refined)

Each leaderboard tab supports three scope filters:

- **Friends** — shows only the player's friends (§30.1). **Default when player has ≥3 friends (v12.49 refined from ≥1).**
- **Clan** — shows only members of the player's clan. Default when player is clanned AND has <3 friends.
- **Global** — all players on the server. Default when player has <3 friends AND no clan.

**Threshold discipline (v12.49).** The Friends-default threshold is raised from ≥1 to ≥3 because a leaderboard with only 1 or 2 entries reads as lonely rather than social. A player with 2 friends opening the Friends tab sees a 3-entry leaderboard (self + 2 friends) and the comparison produces no meaningful "rank" sensation. The ≥3-friends threshold ensures the Friends tab contains at least 4 entries (self + 3 friends) — enough for the player's position within the group to be legible as social context rather than a miniature list. Below 3 friends, the Clan-or-Global fallback produces a more substantive comparison surface. The threshold is tunable via LEADERBOARD_FRIENDS_DEFAULT_THRESHOLD.

The leaderboard UI opens to the appropriate default scope automatically. The player can switch scopes via tabs at the top of each leaderboard. The scope choice persists per-tab — a player who prefers Global for Level but Friends for Achievement Points retains those preferences per-session.

**Top-of-scope visible as secondary reference (v12.49).** In each scope, the leaderboard shows:

- The player's current position and stat
- Up to 2 positions above the player (next to beat)
- Up to 2 positions below the player (who's chasing)
- **The top 3 of the scope as a secondary reference strip at the top of the panel** (v12.49 refined from "top 10"). The top-3 reference strip is visually distinct from the nearby-neighbors display — smaller text, subtler treatment — and provides aspirational reference without dominating the view.

This "zoomed-in" view respects that most players care about their *immediate* competitive peers, not the top of the global chart. A Level 47 player in a Level-40-average clan sees themselves at Clan Rank 3 of 8, with who's above and below — meaningful stakes. The top-3 reference lets players see the scope's ceiling without the nearby-neighbors display competing with a long top-10 list for attention.

**Scope performance.** Friend and Clan scopes are computed client-side from the cached leaderboard DO data (filtered to the player's friend/clan list); Global is served directly from the DO. No additional server infrastructure needed.

**Tuning constant (v12.49).**

| Variable | Default | Min | Max | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| LEADERBOARD_FRIENDS_DEFAULT_THRESHOLD | 3 | 1 | 10 | Minimum friend count for Friends tab to be default scope |
| LEADERBOARD_TOP_REFERENCE_COUNT | 3 | 2 | 10 | Top-of-scope entries shown as secondary reference strip |
| LEADERBOARD_NEIGHBOR_RADIUS | 2 | 1 | 5 | Positions above/below player shown in nearby-neighbors display |

`[DESIGN INTENT: Global leaderboards engage 1% of the population — the players competing for server-top positions. The other 99% glance at global rankings once and never return. Friend and Clan scopes are where the actual social competition happens: I want to beat my friends; I want to be the clan's top kill-count player. These are personal-scale stakes that motivate every player. The default-to-personal-scope rule is the load-bearing design choice — most players never configure settings, so defaults matter. The v12.49 refinement raises the Friends-default threshold from ≥1 to ≥3 to address an edge case: a player with 1 or 2 friends opening the Friends tab sees a 3-entry leaderboard that reads as lonely rather than social. ≥3 friends guarantees enough entries that rank position is legible as social context. The top-3-reference refinement (down from top-10) lets the nearby-neighbors display dominate the view without a long top-10 list competing for attention — players see their immediate peers clearly and the scope's ceiling as quiet reference. Both refinements preserve the v12.25 thesis (personal-scale stakes motivate engagement) with tighter tuning. A player opening the leaderboard sees themselves in their 8-person friend group at rank 3 rather than in a 10,000-player global at rank 4,273. The first view motivates continued engagement; the second produces checkout.]`

### §38. Level Uncap (Infinite Progression)

#### §38.1 Design

Level cap removed. Players can progress beyond Level 100 indefinitely. However, **stat training stops contributing past Level 100** — the per-level Tier 1 and Tier 2 budgets (§1.4) are zero for levels 101+, and the per-stat ceiling of 99 is reached well before Level 100 for any focused training pattern. Post-100 levels are prestige markers and grant no additional stat budget.

#### §38.2 Post-100 XP Scaling (CANONICAL)

Post-100 uses the same coupled formula as pre-100 (§6.2) with an additional polynomial soft cap multiplier:

```
killTarget = ceil(100 × (Level - 1)^0.7)
monXp      = monsterStat(10, Level, 1.045, 1.025, 1.018)
base       = killTarget × monXp
XP_Req     = ceil(base × (1 + (Level-100) × 0.1)^3.5)
```

The polynomial soft cap creates asymptotic flattening — no hard ceiling, but effectively unreachable beyond ~120:

| Level | Kills Required | At 3h/day (cumulative from L1) |
|:-----:|:--------------:|:------------------------------:|
| 100 | 2,495 | 68 days (2.3 months) |
| 105 | 10,673 | 74 days (2.5 months) |
| 110 | 30,197 | 88 days (2.9 months) |
| 115 | 68,039 | 120 days (4.0 months) |
| 117 | — | ~365 days (~1 year) |
| 120 | 132,721 | 181 days (6.0 months) |

#### §38.3 Post-100 Rewards

- Leaderboard ranking (§37)
- Achievement milestones: "Half Century" (L50), "Centurion" (L100), "Prestige" (L200)
- Life skill guild ranks extend to L150 ("Transcendent" rank — §45)
- Social prestige on inspect card

`[DESIGN INTENT: Removing the level cap gives dedicated players infinite runway without creating power disparity. Since stats stop at 100, a Level 200 player and a Level 100 player are mechanically identical in combat. The difference is time invested — a social signal, not a power advantage.]`

### §39. Server Marketplace (Order Book)

Cross-room marketplace using a Cloudflare Durable Object for persistent order storage and auto-matching.

#### §39.1 Order Types

| Type | Description | Funds |
|------|------------|-------|
| **Buy Order** | "I want to buy X at price Y" | Gold deducted on placement (escrow) |
| **Sell Order** | "I'm selling item X at price Y" | Item removed from inventory on placement |

#### §39.2 Matching Logic

Orders auto-match when: same category + same subtype + same tier + same elements + buy price ≥ sell price. Match executes at sell price (buyer gets refund of difference).

#### §39.3 Categories

| Category | Subtypes | Notes |
|----------|----------|-------|
| Weapons | greatsword, sword, bow, staff | Base material+type only — weapons no longer carry elements (v12.6). Quality grade is part of the matching key. |
| Armor | armor | Includes attunement element (which IS a property of armor, not weapons) |
| Shields | shield | |
| Amulets | amulet | |
| **Gems** (v12.6) | flame, frost, storm, stone, wind, venom, flora, water, dark, light, shift | Each gem type is its own subtype; gem level is part of the matching key. The marketplace is the cross-zone equalizer for the zone-locked gem economy. |
| **Composites** (v12.6) | single-element / two-element | Pre-built gem composites can be listed for sale. The buyer gets the composite as a single object ready to apply to an enchanted weapon. |
| **Mystery items** (v12.7) | greatsword, sword, bow, staff | Unrevealed Rare-or-better drops can be listed for sale before reveal. The buyer reveals after purchase. The seller never learns the result. See §39.5. |
| **Godly weapons** (v12.7) | greatsword, sword, bow, staff | The apex marketplace tier. Godly weapons retain all sacred properties on transfer (indestructible on death, untouchable by Ferryman, salvage-rejected, stat-gate exempt, audit-gate exempt) — only the binding rule was removed. Listings display the full lineage. See §39.6. |
| **Prestige drops** (v12.11) | og_cape, founders_crown, phoenix_trail, year_one_aura, ... | Discontinued cosmetic items from past server windows. Fixed supply (closed at end of acquisition window). Server-side scarcity, no per-item lineage tracking. Each prestige drop subtype is its own marketplace bucket. See §32.5. |

**Tiers:** All 20 blacksmith tiers and 20 woodworking tiers serve as weapon material grades. Quality grade (Normal/Rare/Elite/Godly) is a separate matching dimension.

**Matching key under v12.6/v12.7:** weapons match by `category:subtype:material_tier:quality_grade` only — element fields are removed because weapons no longer carry elements. Players who want a specific elemental setup buy a base weapon plus the gems they need separately. Gems match by `gems:gem_type:gem_level`. Mystery items match by `mystery:weapon_type:material_tier` (the quality grade is hidden from the listing — buyers see what *kind* of weapon and *what material*, but not whether it's Rare, Elite, or Godly). Godly weapons match by `godly:weapon_type:material_tier` and are always individually listed (no fungibility — each Godly is a unique object identified by its lineage).

**Order expiry:** 1 hour (3,600,000ms) for fungible items. Godly listings expire after 24 hours due to their unique nature and the time required for buyers to consider lineage and price. Expired orders return gold/items to owner.

**Price estimation:** `estimatePrice = weaponBase × material_tier_mult × quality_mult × 2` — displayed as suggestion in UI for fungible items. Mystery items show an EV-based suggestion: `mystery_estimate = (rare_price × 0.91) + (elite_price × 0.091) + (godly_floor × 0.00028)`. Godly listings have no estimate — the seller sets the price based on lineage prestige and the buyer evaluates it manually.

#### §39.4 Order Book Indexing

The Marketplace Durable Object maintains **composite indexes** to avoid linear scans over the full order book. Orders are indexed by a compound key for O(1) bucket lookup with O(n) scan only within the matching bucket.

**Primary index (buy/sell matching) under v12.6/v12.7:**
```
weapons:    `weapons:${subtype}:${material_tier}:${quality_grade}`
gems:       `gems:${gem_type}:${gem_level}`
composites: `composites:${gem1_type}:${gem2_type || 'none'}:${gem_level}:${orientation}`
mystery:    `mystery:${weapon_type}:${material_tier}`  (quality hidden — all mystery items at this material tier match together)
godly:      `godly:${weapon_type}:${material_tier}:${weapon_id}`  (per-weapon, never aggregated — each Godly is its own listing)
```

Example: `weapons:greatsword:12:elite` indexes all Tier 12 Elite Steel Greatsword orders. `gems:flame:5` indexes all Level 5 Flame gem orders. `mystery:greatsword:12` indexes all Tier 12 Greatsword mystery items regardless of underlying grade. `godly:greatsword:20:abc-123-def` indexes that one specific Godly Tier 20 Greatsword.

#### §39.5 Mystery Item Listings (v12.7 CANONICAL)

A mystery item — an unrevealed Rare-or-better drop, see §4.6b.ii — can be listed for sale before the player reveals it. The player who picked up the question mark from the world chooses to either reveal it themselves or sell it as-is. Sellers will choose to list mystery items rather than reveal them when they want gold faster than dopamine, or when they don't want to commit time to the reveal animation, or when they're farming at high volume and prefer to wholesale their loot stream.

**The reveal happens when the buyer picks up the item from their inventory.** The mystery item enters the buyer's inventory in the same unrevealed state, and the next time the buyer interacts with it, the reveal animation plays for them — in the same hybrid input model as a personal pickup (Stage 1 in a non-blocking corner, Stage 2 escalation, Stage 3 hijack if Godly). The buyer experiences the reveal as if they had picked up the question mark themselves. The dramatic moment belongs to the holder at the time of reveal.

**The seller is never notified of the reveal result.** This is a hard rule. The seller's relationship with the mystery item ends at the sale. They get the gold, the item leaves their inventory, and they never find out what the buyer's reveal produced. There is no post-sale notification, no stat tracking of "how many of your sold mysteries were Godlies," no leaderboard. The seller chose to convert dopamine into gold; the buyer received the dopamine. The two transactions are sealed.

**Why this rule matters.** Without it, the natural failure mode is the cruel twist: a player sells a mystery item for 20,000 gold, learns later that the buyer revealed it as a Godly worth millions, and feels they were robbed even though the trade was voluntary. The seller's regret would be psychologically devastating and would deter mystery item trading entirely. The "you never find out" rule preserves the marketplace by removing the post-sale comparison. Sellers price based on EV and accept the variance. Buyers gamble on individual reveals. Neither party can later second-guess the trade.

**Mystery item listings show:** weapon type silhouette (so the buyer knows what kind of weapon they're potentially getting), material tier (so they know the floor of the weapon's value), and the seller's asking price. They do NOT show the underlying quality grade — that is the entire point of the listing. The marketplace UI labels these listings clearly as "MYSTERY" with a question-mark icon and an EV-based price suggestion.

**Mystery items obey ordinary inventory rules in transit.** If a buyer purchases a mystery item and dies before revealing it, the mystery item scatters or strips per the §5.5 rules. A mystery item carried into Lawless Land can be lost on death. The buyer's gamble is therefore two-layered: the gamble that the underlying grade is good, and the gamble that they survive long enough to reveal it.

**Buy Intents apply to mystery items.** A buyer who wants a mystery item with specific criteria the order book can't express ("Tier 18+ Greatsword mystery only") can create a Buy Intent in either escrow or negotiated mode. See §39.7 for the canonical Buy Intent system.

#### §39.6 Godly Weapon Listings (v12.7 CANONICAL)

Godly weapons can be listed on the marketplace under v12.7. Each listing is for a specific Godly weapon identified by its unique weapon_id, and the listing displays the full §17.4.1 lineage: original recipient (the player who first revealed the weapon from the gods), every subsequent owner, transfer timestamps, and transfer mechanisms. Buyers can inspect the lineage before purchasing and use it as part of their valuation.

**The listing shows:** weapon type, material tier, hardness level, applied composite (if any), the full lineage tree from the original recipient through every owner, and the seller's asking price. Sacred properties are listed explicitly so the buyer understands what they're buying — indestructible on death, untouchable by Ferryman, salvage-rejected, stat-gate exempt, audit-gate exempt. The listing also shows the original recipient's account state (Active / Deleted / Banned) but never erases their identity from the lineage.

**Lineage prestige is real and priced into the market.** A Godly weapon traceable back to a famous early-server player commands a higher price than a Godly weapon whose original recipient was never well-known. Lineage with a small number of transfers is generally worth more than lineage with many transfers — fewer hands suggests the weapon hasn't been "flipped" repeatedly for short-term profit. A Godly that has stayed with the original recipient for years and is now being sold for the first time is a different category of object than one that has changed hands a dozen times. Players will form opinions about lineage value over time and the marketplace will reflect those opinions.

**Transfer protocol on purchase.** When a Godly listing is matched, the §17.4.1 transfer protocol executes atomically: the seller's current_owner_signature is verified, an OwnershipEvent is constructed and signed, the event is appended to the ownership_history log, current_owner is updated to the buyer, current_owner_signature is re-signed, and the GodlyLedger Durable Object is updated atomically. Both clients receive confirmation. The weapon transfers from seller to buyer with all sacred properties intact and the lineage extended by one event.

**No price ceiling.** The §17.3 marketplace guards include a price ceiling rule (`listing ≤ vendor_sell_back × 20`) for fungible items. Godly weapons are exempt from this ceiling because they have no vendor sell-back value (vendors refuse them) and because their fair market price is determined by player-driven valuation rather than by a baseline. A Godly Tier 20 weapon might list for 5 million gold, 50 million gold, or any other figure the seller chooses; the market determines whether it sells.

**No price floor either.** A player can list a Godly for 1 gold if they want. There is no minimum. A player who wants to gift a Godly to a friend without going through the direct trade window can list it at a token price; the friend buys it and the lineage records the transfer. This is the only "gifting" path that updates the lineage ledger atomically.

**Buy Intents apply to Godly weapons.** Buyers who want to express demand for Godly weapons with specific criteria the order book can't express (lineage prestige, specific hardness levels, applied composite preferences) can create a Buy Intent in either escrow mode (commit gold up front, accept the first matching seller's response) or negotiated mode (signal interest, negotiate via direct trade when a seller responds). See §39.7 for the canonical Buy Intent system. Negotiated mode is the natural fit for high-value Godly transactions where lineage and other unique attributes drive price discovery.

`[DESIGN INTENT: Godly listings are the marketplace's apex tier and the design's biggest single shift in v12.7. The v12.5 doc treated Godlies as objects outside the economy entirely; v12.7 treats them as the economy's top tier. The lineage system is what makes this work cosmologically — every Godly weapon carries the memory of its original divine encounter forever, regardless of how many hands it has passed through, and the marketplace can display that memory as part of the weapon's value. A buyer who pays 10 million gold for a Godly is paying for the object AND the lineage AND the social weight of owning a piece of server history. The original recipient's encounter with the gods is permanent and recorded forever. Subsequent owners are mortals who acquired a divine artifact through normal economies. Both kinds of ownership are real, both are recorded in the lineage, and both contribute to the weapon's identity over its lifetime.]`

**Index structure:** `Map<index_key, { buys: SortedArray<Order>, sells: SortedArray<Order> }>`. Buy orders sorted descending by price (highest-bid-first). Sell orders sorted ascending by price (lowest-ask-first). Matching checks only the head of each sorted array — O(1) per match attempt.

**On order placement:** Insert into the correct bucket's sorted array. Check the opposite array's head for an immediate match. If match found, execute trade and remove both entries. If no match, order sits in the bucket until matched or expired.

**Expiry sweep:** Lazy expiry — orders checked on access and on a periodic sweep (MARKETPLACE_SWEEP_INTERVAL = 60s). Expired orders flagged and bulk-removed. No per-order timer.

**Index cardinality estimate:** 4 categories × ~8 subtypes × 20 tiers × ~12 element combos = ~7,680 possible buckets. In practice, most buckets are empty. Active buckets typically contain 1–20 orders. Total order book size at scale: ~500–2,000 active orders, distributed across ~100–300 non-empty buckets.

**Memory budget:** Each order is ~200 bytes (item data + price + timestamps + owner). 2,000 orders = ~400 KB. Index overhead ~50 KB. Total Durable Object memory for marketplace: < 1 MB. Well within Cloudflare Durable Object memory limits.

`[DESIGN INTENT: The compound index eliminates the O(n) full-scan problem. Matching is always O(1) against the bucket head. The 1-hour expiry + lazy sweep prevents stale order accumulation. The index cardinality is bounded by game design (finite categories, tiers, elements) — this cannot grow unbounded.]`

#### §39.7 Buy Intent System (v12.7 CANONICAL — Universal)

The order book (§39.1–§39.4) handles auto-matchable demand: buyers and sellers agree on a category, subtype, tier, and grade, and the matching engine pairs them automatically. But the order book has limits. It cannot express criteria the matching key doesn't carry — *"I want a Tier 18 Greatsword with Hardness 3+"*, *"I'm looking for a Volatile Flame+Frost composite oriented strong-side-trigger"*, *"I want a Godly Tier 20 Greatsword with a famous lineage"*. It also cannot signal demand for items that aren't currently listed — a buyer who wants a Level 6 Flame gem can't place a regular buy order if no seller has listed one, and there's no path for that demand to flow to players who *might* level a gem to 6 if they knew demand existed.

**The Buy Intent system is the marketplace's secondary mechanism for non-auto-matchable demand.** It is universal — Buy Intents work for *every* item category, not just Godly weapons. Where the order book handles the high-volume, fungible-item, auto-matched flow, Buy Intents handle the long-tail demand: specific criteria the matching key doesn't express, items that aren't currently listed, unique objects whose value depends on attributes the buyer wants to filter on, and high-value items where negotiation is part of the transaction. The two systems coexist for every category, and the buyer chooses which path fits their need.

**Buy Intent fields.** A player creates a Buy Intent with the following fields:

- **Category:** weapon / gem / composite / mystery / godly / armor / shield / amulet
- **Subtype:** weapon type (greatsword/sword/bow/staff), gem element type, composite element pair, etc.
- **Minimum tier or level:** the floor — material tier for weapons/armor/shields, gem level for gems, composite level for composites
- **Maximum price:** the ceiling the buyer is willing to pay (advisory in negotiated mode, enforcing in escrow mode)
- **Optional notes:** free-text up to 200 characters where the buyer expresses criteria the system can't natively express ("Hardness 3+", "Volatile Meltwater orientation", "lineage from a known player preferred")
- **Mode:** **escrow** or **negotiated** (see below)

**Two flavors: escrow and negotiated.**

**Escrow mode.** The buyer commits gold up front equal to their max price. The gold leaves their inventory at intent-creation time and is held in marketplace escrow. When a seller responds to the intent by tapping "Fill Intent" with a specific item from their inventory, the marketplace creates an immediate sell listing for that item at the intent's max price, auto-matches it against the escrowed gold, and executes the trade atomically. The buyer receives the specific item the seller chose; the seller receives the full intent price; the intent is consumed. **The buyer cannot back out.** Once the seller fills the intent, the trade settles. This flavor is best when the buyer wants certainty: *"I will pay 500g for any Tier 12 Frost gem, please someone sell me one — I'll take whichever one shows up first."* The buyer is committing to the price up front and accepting whatever specific item matches.

**Negotiated mode.** No gold is escrowed. When a seller responds to the intent by tapping "Open Trade", the marketplace opens a direct trade window between buyer and seller for them to negotiate the actual price and confirm the transaction. The buyer can decline the trade, counter-offer, or accept. The seller can also withdraw at any point. If the trade settles, the gold and item transfer atomically through the direct trade path. If either party walks away, no transaction happens and the intent remains active. This flavor is best when the buyer wants flexibility on high-value or unique items: *"I'm interested in a Hardness 4 Greatsword, willing to pay around 50,000g but want to discuss based on the specific weapon's hardness and material tier."* The buyer is signaling interest with a price hint, not committing to a fixed transaction.

**Both flavors work for every category.** A player can create an escrow intent for a Godly weapon if they want certainty (*"I will pay exactly 10 million gold for the next Tier 20 Godly Greatsword anyone will sell me, no negotiation, the lineage doesn't matter"*). A player can create a negotiated intent for a Tier 5 Frost gem if they want to ask for a fair price before committing. The choice is about *how the buyer wants to commit*, not about *what they're buying*.

**How sellers see intents.** When a player opens their inventory, the marketplace UI scans active Buy Intents against the items the player holds. For any item that matches an active intent, the inventory icon shows a small "Matching Buy Intents" badge. Tapping the badge opens the list of intents that match that item, sorted by maximum price (highest first). Each entry shows:

- The buyer's display name and intent reputation (see anti-abuse below)
- The intent's max price
- The flavor (escrow or negotiated)
- The optional notes
- A button: **"Fill Intent"** for escrow intents (instantly settles the trade), **"Open Trade"** for negotiated intents (opens a direct trade window with the buyer)

The seller is in control. They can ignore intents and continue holding the item, choose to fill an intent, or post a public order book listing if they prefer. The intent is information, not pressure. There is no obligation on the seller — intents are visible but never auto-applied to the seller's inventory.

**Quotas (anti-abuse).** Each player can have at most **10 active escrow intents and 10 active negotiated intents** at any time, separately tracked. Total quota: 20 active intents per player. Escrow intents consume gold (which is its own throttle); negotiated intents don't, which is why the quota matters as a separate cap. Quotas reset as intents are consumed, expire, or canceled.

**Reputation tracking (anti-abuse, negotiated intents only).** When a seller responds to a negotiated intent and opens a trade window, the buyer either follows through (accepts the trade or makes a reasonable counter-offer that eventually settles) or walks away (declines without counter-offering, fails to respond, closes the window). The buyer's **Buy Intent reputation** is a server-tracked stat: percentage of negotiated intents that resulted in completed trades versus walked-away interactions. Reputation is shown to sellers when they view the intent — *"Notice: this buyer's intent reputation is 23% — they have walked away from 77% of trades initiated through their intents."* Sellers can choose to ignore low-reputation intents. Escrow intents do not need reputation tracking — the gold is already committed, so there's no walk-away path.

**Minimum account age.** Only player accounts older than 30 days can create Buy Intents. This prevents fresh alt accounts from being used as throwaway demand-signaling tools to manipulate market prices. The age requirement applies to intent creation only — older accounts can respond to (fill) intents created by anyone, including newer accounts that don't qualify to create their own intents.

**Intent expiry.** Buy Intents persist longer than regular order book entries because they are slow-moving signals rather than active orders. Default expiry: **30 days** for both flavors. The buyer can edit or cancel an intent at any time; expired intents auto-cancel and (for escrow intents) refund the escrowed gold to the buyer.

**Buyer notifications.** The buyer is notified when activity happens on their intents — when a seller views the intent (batched, one notification per session per intent), when a seller opens a negotiated trade window (immediate), when an escrow intent is filled and settles (immediate), or when an intent expires (immediate, with a refund confirmation if escrow). Notifications are batched per session to avoid spam.

**Intent indexing.** The Marketplace Durable Object maintains a separate intent index alongside the order book index:

```
intents:weapons:${weapon_type}:${minimum_material_tier}
intents:gems:${gem_type}:${minimum_gem_level}
intents:composites:${gem1_type}:${gem2_type || 'none'}:${minimum_level}
intents:mystery:${weapon_type}:${minimum_material_tier}
```
