**How it works.** As a player satisfies an achievement's condition, the achievement fires and appears in the player's Journey Log (per §27.4) with its specific trigger context. Before firing, the achievement is invisible to the player — they did not see it on a checklist to chase.

**"Someone has seen this" markers.** Each achievement entry in the Journey Log includes a small marker showing how many other server members have also earned it. If the marker shows "2/500 players on server" next to a newly-fired achievement, the player knows they're among the rare few who have earned it. Markers create gentle FOMO without spoiling individual discovery — a player sees that a rare achievement exists (because it has *some* server members who earned it) without seeing *what condition* to trigger.

**Why remove the visible list.** Canonical achievement lists turn play into checklist completion. Players pursue items on the list because they're visible, not because the underlying action is rewarding. Hidden achievements preserve the *mystery* of play — you do things because you want to, and the game notices. Server-awareness markers replace the checklist's "to-do" pressure with a gentler "what might be possible" curiosity.

**Per §27.3 v13**, each achievement grants a named title rather than AP. The v13 title conversion is applied across the canonical achievement list below — each achievement now awards a specific title on trigger.

**The 24 canonical achievements (v13 REVISED — titles awarded per §27.3):**

| ID | Name | Icon | Requirement | v13 Title Awarded |
|----|------|:----:|-------------|-------------------|
| guild_first | Guild Member | 🏛️ | Join your first life skill guild | "The Inductee" |
| guild_master | Guild Master | 👑 | Reach Master rank in any guild | "The Master-Hand" |
| guild_all | Renaissance | 🎭 | Reach Journeyman in all 10 guilds | "The Complete Craftsman" |
| arena_enter | Gladiator Hopeful | 🏟️ | Enter the arena | "The Challenger" |
| arena_win3 | Arena Victor | ⚔️ | Win 3 arena matches | "The Contender" |
| arena_champion | Champion | 🏆 | Win a gladiator tournament | "Gladiator" |
| sponsor_first (v13 REVISED from bet_first) | First Sponsor | 🤝 | Sponsor your first competitor per §44.1 v13 | "The Backer" |
| sponsor_win5 (v13 REVISED from bet_win5) | Good Eye | 👁️ | 5 sponsored competitors win their matches | "The Insightful" |
| dive_first | Deep Breath | 🫧 | Dive underwater for the first time | "The Diver" |
| dive_treasure | Sunken Treasure | 🏴‍☠️ | Find underwater treasure | "The Discoverer" |
| pet_evolve | Metamorphosis | 🧬 | Evolve a pet | "The Shapewright" |
| pet_mythic | Mythic Tamer | 🌟 | Create a Mythic pet | "The Mythmaker" |
| furniture_first | Interior Designer | 🪑 | Craft your first furniture | "The Homemaker" |
| furniture_all | Dream Home | 🏠 | Craft all 12 furniture pieces | "The Dreamweaver" |
| mkt_first | Trader | 🏪 | Complete a marketplace trade | "The Merchant" |
| dungeon_create | Architect | 🏗️ | Author a custom dungeon per §36 v13 | "The Dungeon Architect" |
| dungeon_play | Dungeon Master | 🐉 | Clear 5 custom dungeons | "The Crawler" |
| war_first | Warmonger | ⚔️ | Participate in a clan war | "The Soldier" |
| war_mvp | War Hero | 🌟 | Earn MVP in a clan war | "The Banner-Holder" |
| lv50 | Half Century | ⭐ | Reach combat level 50 | "The Persistent" |
| lv100 | Centurion | 💯 | Reach combat level 100 | "Ascendant" (preserves canonical §54.1 title) |
| lv200 | Prestige | ✦ | Reach combat level 200 | "The Long Player" |
| mask_first | Behind the Mask | 🎭 | Buy your first cosmetic mask | "The Masked" |
| minigame_win | Party Animal | 🎮 | Win a farm minigame | "The Convivial" |

**v13 additional hidden achievements** (not on any visible list; discovered through play):

| Hidden ID | Trigger | Title Awarded |
|-----------|---------|---------------|
| burn_50 | Burn dinner 50 times | "The Hopeful Chef" (wry, per §0.1) |
| die_100 | Die 100 times | "The Persistent" (register: wry) |
| echo_author | Have a player-generated Echo Stone read by 10+ other players | "The Scribe" |
| emote_mimic | Trigger 20 Trickster Mimicry moments in Convergence | "The Conductor" |
| dust_hoard | Hold 10,000+ dust for combat bonus per §23.6d v13 | "The Hoarder" |
| fragment_pilgrim | Visit 100 Soul Fragments across the server (v14.0) | "The Pilgrim" |

Hidden achievements expand the "discovered not listed" space — players find them by doing things, not by chasing list items.

**Dependencies on v13 replacements:** §27.3 (AP → titles), §36 (Dungeon Workshop), §44.1 (sponsorship not betting), §33.6 (Ferryman register gate). Achievements referencing deprecated canonical systems are migrated to the v13 equivalent.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): Canonical achievement lists turn play into checklist completion. Players pursue items on the list because they're visible, and "complete the list" becomes an optimization goal separate from enjoying the game. Hidden achievements preserve play's native motivation — you do things because the doing is rewarding, and the game acknowledges what you did. Server-awareness markers ("someone has seen this") create community-scale mystery: rare achievements exist but their conditions are not spoiled. Players talk about achievements they earned in terms of the moments that triggered them ("I earned The Hopeful Chef by actually burning 50 dinners — didn't mean to, but there it was"). Per §27.3 v13, every achievement grants a specific title rather than AP; titles carry identity, AP carries accounting. The hidden achievement pool expands the space without expanding the checklist — new achievements can be added over time without disturbing any visible UI.]`

### §53. Narrative Story Arc

#### §53.1 Seven-Act Structure (v13 REVISED, v14.0.5 UPDATED — Five Endings)

The narrative is never told through cutscenes or exposition. It emerges through Echo Stones, Zone Keeper dialogue, NPC tone progression, alignment choices, and the elemental systems themselves. The player pieces together the world's story through play.

| Act | Levels | Depths | Narrative Beat |
|-----|--------|--------|----------------|
| I — The Arrival | 1–10 | 1 | Player is nobody. World expects failure. First Echo Stones hint at emotional fracture. NPCs dismissive. |
| II — The Pattern | 11–40 | 2–4 | Eight elements explored. Echo Stones reveal zones as emotional residue. Moral choices begin (capstone quests, per §25.1 v13 four-register paths). Town warms to player. |
| III — The Deepening | 41–70 | 5–7 | Monsters outgrow player. Crafting essential. Ferryman quest available for register-diverse players (per §33.6 three-of-four gate). Life skills become critical. |
| IV — The Duality | 71–90 | 8–9 | Depth 8 = elemental limit. Darkness zone: shadow clone (fight yourself). First Eclipse collision: destruction + healing unified (per §9.4 v13, Eclipse-only healing). |
| V — The Truth | 91–100 | 10 | Light zone: blinding truth. Level 100 = Ascendant title. NPCs fully invested. Alignment register composition finalized. |
| VI — The Guardian | 100+ | Guardian | 8 Guardian zones unlock. Apex bosses (Imp King, Skeleton Lord, Elder Eye). Elemental mastery tested. |
| VII — The Ending | Completion | — | Register-determined ending sequence (v13, v14.0.5 expanded). Title: Hero / Trickster / Arbiter / Sovereign / Weaver. |

#### §53.2 The Fracture — Core Theme

The world was once whole. Something split it. The zones are emotional residue — places where aspects of the whole pooled after the fracture. The elements are not natural phenomena; they are aspects of a shattered whole.

This is **never stated explicitly**. Players piece it together through Echo Stones, Zone Keepers, and the collision system itself (every collision recombines two separated forces — Eclipse is the only one that heals, per §9.4 v13).

#### §53.3 The Unlikely Hero Frame

Bros are blank slates. They wash up with no memory, no destiny. The world actively doubts them. NPCs progress through three tone phases gated by quest completion count. Per §33.7 v13, each phase's dialogue further varies by dominant alignment register (Responsible / Mischievous / Cool / Ruthless).

- **Phase 1 (0–4 quests):** Dismissive. "Another one washed up. They never last."
- **Phase 2 (5–14 quests):** Surprised. "Huh. You're still here."
- **Phase 3 (15+ quests):** Invested. "I stopped betting against you."

The player's identity is built through systems (stats, alignment registers, clan, gear), not scripted narrative.

#### §53.4 Five Endings (v13 REPLACEMENT — Register-Dominant, v14.0.5 EXPANDED — Fifth Ending for Balanced)

Endings are triggered by True Guardian achievement + `endingAlignment` computation per §33.4. Under v14.0.5, the ending count expands from four to five: four dominant-register endings plus one balanced-register ending.

**Hero (Responsible/Steadfast dominant):** The 8 elemental spokes connect — lines of energy form a ring around town. Echo Stones replay as a coherent story: the fracture was caused by isolation, not violence. The player became the connection the world was missing. A final Zone Keeper appears and bows without speaking. Title: **Hero.**

**Trickster (Mischievous/Mischief-Maker dominant) — NEW v13:** The 8 elemental spokes transform playfully — each zone's element visibly *tricks* the others. Flame gently warms Frost instead of destroying it. Storm plays tag with Stone. The elements rearrange like a good-natured puzzle rather than a cosmic resolution. Echo Stones replay as a comedy of misunderstandings revealing the Fracture was caused by *seriousness itself* — the elements forgot they could play together. The player remembered how. A final Zone Keeper appears, makes eye contact, and winks. No words. Title: **Trickster.**

The Trickster ending's tonal register (per §0.1) is specifically *warm and playful* — not gag-comedy, not ironic, but the kind of humor that arises from affection. The implication: seriousness can be its own form of destruction; play can be its own form of healing.

**Arbiter (Cool/Unmoved dominant):** The 8 spokes stabilize independently — ordered but not connected. Echo Stones reveal this is a cycle: the world fractures, someone walks the pieces, the pieces stabilize, time passes, the world fractures again. There was a Guardian before. There will be one after. Final Zone Keeper speaks one word: *"Again."* Title: **Arbiter.**

**Sovereign (Ruthless dominant):** The 8 spokes converge inward — energy flows into the player. Echo Stones reveal that previous Guardians who absorbed the world's power caused the fracture to repeat. The player is the pattern repeating, not the solution. Final Zone Keeper: *"Everything is yours now."* Title: **Sovereign.** (v12.24 line preserved — purely observational, matching the other endings in refusing to moralize.)

**Weaver (balanced — no register strictly dominant) — NEW v14.0.5:** The 8 elemental spokes neither connect (Hero) nor stabilize-apart (Arbiter) nor converge-inward (Sovereign) nor play-together (Trickster). They **hold at their full separation**, each spoke at full intensity, each keeping its own character, and from the town's center a fifth pattern emerges — a weaving motion that passes *through* each spoke in turn without merging them. The spokes remain eight distinct elements; the weaving is what connects them without collapsing them. Echo Stones replay as fragments from *all four* register-curations interleaved in chronological rather than register order — the player's arc read as it actually happened, register by register, without being sorted into a single story. The Fracture, in this reading, was not caused by isolation (Hero) or seriousness (Trickster) or absorption (Sovereign) or inevitability (Arbiter). It was caused by *the demand that the world be one thing.* The player held four things without insisting they become one. A final Zone Keeper appears and holds up one open hand — neither bow, nor wink, nor speech. Held. Title: **Weaver.**

The Weaver ending's tonal register (per §0.1 and §0.4) is simultaneously all four — grave AND playful AND reserved AND decided, each present in the sequence without any subordinating the others. Per §0.4, the ending is the thesis at its clearest expression: four registers held permanently as themselves, the fifth state (the Weaver) being the act of holding rather than any synthesis of the four.

**No ending is mechanically punished, and no ending is ranked.** The Weaver is not the "best" ending for holding four; the Hero is not the "best" for being socially collaborative; the Sovereign is not the "worst" for being ruthless. Each of the five is a valid resolution of a specific arc the player walked. The debate about which ending is "best" is the game's final narrative act, happening in the community. Per §0.3, none of the five is a correctness test; each is an identity.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13, v14.0.5 expanded): Five endings map to four registers plus the balanced-hold state. The Trickster ending specifically is the Mischievous endgame — the Fracture resolved through play rather than solved through seriousness. Per §0.1 tonal rule, the Trickster ending carries the game's humor register into its final moment, answering a question that canonical three-ending design couldn't: "what does the Mischievous Bro get for their dominant playstyle?" They get an ending that validates play as a form of meaning-making.

The v14.0.5 Weaver ending answers the parallel question the prior v13 spec could not: "what does the balanced Bro — the player who genuinely held all four registers rather than committing to one — receive?" The v13 spec gave them the Hero ending via tiebreaker, which was the thesis error §33.4 v14.0.5 corrects. A balanced Bro is not a failed-to-commit Bro; they are the most thesis-aligned Bro in the game, the one who lived §0.4's unification through the permanent holding of opposites as lived play rather than endgame ritual. The Weaver ending reflects that structure: the spokes hold at their full separation, the weaving passes through without merging, the final Keeper holds up an open hand rather than performing a register-specific gesture. The Weaver is not more than the other four endings — it is the specific resolution for a specific arc the other four don't describe. The five endings together demonstrate the thesis has five equally-valid outcomes: four where the player crystallized into one register's shape, one where the player refused to crystallize and held all four.

The Convergence pole (§57.7a) is the natural cosmological pole for the Weaver — the zone's established role as "the geography where Dark and Light coexist" extends at the personal layer to "the self where all four registers coexist." This completes the pole taxonomy at five values (Light / Dark / Eclipse-warm / Eclipse-reserved / Convergence) and matches the endpoint count. The pole enumeration and the ending enumeration are now in structural correspondence.]`

#### §53.5 Ending Sequence — Technical (v13 REVISED, v14.0.5 EXPANDED — Five Paths)

60–90 second non-interactive sequence using existing rendering systems:
1. Camera slowly zooms out from player in town center
2. Elemental spoke effects play (connect / transform playfully / stabilize / converge / **hold-and-weave**, based on `endingAlignment`)
3. Echo Stone text replays in sequence (register-filtered selection for Hero/Trickster/Arbiter/Sovereign; chronological interleave of all four register selections for Weaver)
4. Final Zone Keeper appears (unique sprite — fully saturated, unlike other Keepers)
5. Zone Keeper action (bow / wink / speak / speak / **hold up open hand**)
6. Title change animation
7. Return to gameplay with permanent title

Data: `ending-sequence.json` contains per-register Echo Stone selections, Zone Keeper lines, and visual effect parameters. v13 expansion: add `trickster` register-key with new Echo Stone curation list, new visual-effect parameters for "playful transformation" spoke animation, and the winking Final Zone Keeper sprite variant. v14.0.5 expansion: add `weaver` register-key with chronological-interleave Echo Stone selection logic (draws from all four register-curations in timestamp order rather than using a dedicated curation list), new visual-effect parameters for "hold-and-weave" spoke animation, and the open-hand Final Zone Keeper sprite variant.

**Content authoring scope.** The Trickster ending requires:
- New Echo Stone curation list (~15-20 entries drawn from existing stones plus 3-5 new Trickster-coded stones authored fresh)
- New spoke transformation VFX (one-off implementation)
- Winking Zone Keeper sprite frame (extends existing Final Zone Keeper sprite)

The v14.0.5 Weaver ending requires:
- No new Echo Stone curation list — the Weaver selection reuses the four existing register-curations via chronological interleave (selection algorithm specified in `ending-sequence.json`)
- New hold-and-weave spoke VFX — spokes hold at full intensity; a fifth weaving pattern passes through them in sequence without merging (one-off implementation)
- Open-hand Zone Keeper sprite frame (extends existing Final Zone Keeper sprite with a single new pose)

All other ending assets are reused from canonical three-ending implementation.

**Creative direction companion.** Full visual/audio creative direction for the Weaver ending — hold-and-weave spoke VFX shading and cycle timing, chronological-interleave reading rationale, open-hand Keeper pose register, Convergence-pole Naming audio treatment, 2.5-3.5 weeks authoring-scope estimate — lives in `creative-reference.md` §8.62 (v5.42 CANONICAL). The GDD owns the mechanical spec (what triggers, what sequences, what reuses); the CR owns the experiential spec (how the weave reads, why the Keeper's hand is open, how the cycle feels).

#### §53.6 Act-Transition Acknowledgments (v12.36 CANONICAL, v13 ADDITIVE)

When a player crosses an act threshold, **Mayor Bro** delivers one brief ambient line in town referencing the *specific* accomplishment that crossed the threshold. The acknowledgment is informational, not ceremonial — no quest popup, no cutscene, no "Act II Begins!" banner. The player simply notices that next time they pass Mayor Bro in town, he says something that references what they just did.

**Why Mayor Bro is the canonical speaker.** The town's central NPC is the player's first and most recurring point of contact. Mayor Bro's tone progression (§33.7, Phase 1/2/3 dismissive→surprised→invested) already tracks player progress through accumulated quest completions. Act-transition acknowledgments layer on this: Mayor Bro notices specific big-event accomplishments alongside the general quest-count progression.

**Trigger model.** Each act transition has a canonical trigger event that fires the acknowledgment:

| Transition | Act Names | Canonical Trigger |
|------------|-----------|-------------------|
| I → II | The Arrival → The Pattern | First time player reaches Level 10 **AND** has visited at least 3 different elemental zones |
| II → III | The Pattern → The Deepening | First time player clears a depth-4 dungeon (any spoke) |
| III → IV | The Deepening → The Duality | First time player completes any capstone moral choice (any Zone Keeper quest chain's alignment branch) |
| IV → V | The Duality → The Truth | First time player triggers an Eclipse or Umbra collision |
| V → VI | The Truth → The Guardian | First time player earns the Ascendant title (Level 100 + Light zone cleared) |
| VI → VII | The Guardian → The Ending | First time player defeats an Apex boss in a Guardian zone |

Each trigger fires exactly once per character. A player crossing an act threshold in a session may not reach town until later — the acknowledgment queues and delivers on the player's next town visit (see delivery semantics below).

**Line pool per transition.** Each transition has 5-7 possible lines; the server selects one based on trigger-context variants (e.g., which zones were visited for I→II, which dungeon was cleared for II→III, which capstone was chosen for III→IV). The variant selection produces some unpredictability while keeping each line's specificity intact — a player who cleared Frozen Shore's depth-4 dungeon gets a different II→III line than a player who cleared Ember Fields's.

**Act I → II pool (~5 lines, variants keyed to zones visited before Level 10).** The player has just become not-entirely-new. Mayor Bro's register shifts from dismissive toward watchful.
- *"You've been to Ember Fields. Twice. That's more than most first-week arrivals."* — fired when Ember Fields was among visited zones
- *"Frost in your cloak. You went to the Shore. Good."* — fired when Frozen Shore was among visited zones
- *"I hear you've been wandering. Mistwood is not an easy start. Respect."* — fired when Mistwood was among visited zones
- *"Three zones in a week. You're a wanderer. I'll say that much."* — fired for any player who visited 3+ zones including a non-featured one
- *"You've been places. The dismissive tone I had earlier — set aside. Provisionally."* — default fallback

**Act II → III pool (~6 lines, variants keyed to which depth-4 dungeon was cleared).** The player has survived a real dungeon. Mayor Bro's register shifts from watchful toward respectful.
- *"You cleared the frost caves. That's real. The Shore doesn't give those up easily."* — fired for Frozen Shore depth-4 clear
- *"Ember Fields at depth four. The volcanics take their due. You paid it and walked out."* — fired for Ember Fields depth-4 clear
- *"The Hollows. At depth four. I know people who gave up at depth two down there."* — fired for Deep Hollows depth-4 clear
- *"Verdant Wilds depth four. You cut through what most of us wouldn't step into."* — fired for Verdant Wilds depth-4 clear
- *"Sky Reaches depth four. Thin air, thin patience. You had both."* — fired for Sky Reaches depth-4 clear
- *"Depth four. You're not a beginner anymore. Doesn't mean you're safe — means you're serious."* — default fallback for any other spoke

**Act III → IV pool (~6 lines, variants keyed to capstone choice made).** The player has made a real moral choice. Mayor Bro's register acknowledges the weight without judgment.
- *"I hear you made a choice at the Zone Keeper's. Everyone's talking. Won't say which way I'd have gone."* — default fallback, works for any capstone
- *"You chose mercy. That one took courage. I've seen it go the other way."* — fired when alignment shifted Benevolent
- *"You chose the hard line. Some will respect that. Some won't. Your choice was yours."* — fired when alignment shifted Evil
- *"You took the middle path. Hardest choice, sometimes. The ones who commit are easy to read."* — fired when alignment stayed Neutral across capstone
- *"Word reached me before you did. The town remembers choices like that."* — fired for any capstone that publicly shifts a Zone Keeper's disposition
- *"You crossed into the Deepening. Not by level — by decision. That's different."* — secondary fallback

**Act IV → V pool (~6 lines, variants keyed to Eclipse vs Umbra, first-ever-server vs not).** The player has triggered the game's capstone collision. Mayor Bro acknowledges the mythology.
- *"Eclipse. I don't get to say that word to many people. Most Bros never see it."* — fired for Eclipse trigger
- *"Umbra. The shadow's shadow. You chose to see it."* — fired for Umbra trigger (Sovereign path)
- *"Someone triggered Eclipse on the server last night. I hear it was you."* — fired for first-ever-server Eclipse (before §23.9 gossip propagates)
- *"You hit duality. That's the Fracture itself, for one moment. Most of us never will."* — secondary fallback
- *"The Truth act opens now. Whatever Eclipse showed you stays with you."* — fired specifically post-Eclipse with transition framing
- *"Umbra says something to the one who triggers it. I don't ask what. Not my place."* — secondary fallback for Umbra

**Act V → VI pool (~6 lines, always keyed to Ascendant achievement).** The player has crossed the Light-zone threshold and earned the Ascendant title. Mayor Bro acknowledges the entry to Guardian content.
- *"Ascendant. You've heard the word. Now people will use it when they talk about you."* — default fallback
- *"Level 100 and Radiance. The ones who finish both don't come back to town the same. You might."* — fired if player returns to town within 10 minutes of Ascendant
- *"The Light zone. I've never been. I hear it hurts. You went through it."* — fired if player's first town visit post-Ascendant
- *"Guardian zones open to you now. Eight of them. Take your time — they don't go anywhere."* — fired specifically as Guardian-content pointer
- *"Some of us noticed you were missing from the marketplace for a few days. Now we know where you were."* — variant for social flavor
- *"You're a Guardian candidate. Not yet. Candidate. The final tests are the Apex."* — secondary fallback with VI→VII foreshadowing

**Act VI → VII pool (~6 lines, always keyed to first Apex boss defeat).** The player is moving toward True Guardian. Mayor Bro acknowledges the Apex defeat and points at the ending arc.
- *"Imp King. Down. I've waited a long time to hear that sentence."* — fired for Imp King defeat
- *"Skeleton Lord. You put him back where he came from. Good work."* — fired for Skeleton Lord defeat
- *"Elder Eye. The one that saw everything. You closed it."* — fired for Elder Eye defeat (and Apex variants across zones)
- *"One Apex down. Seven to go. The True Guardian path is long but there's a path."* — fired when first Apex is defeated
- *"You'll remember the Apex for the rest of your time here. I see it in how you carry yourself."* — default fallback
- *"The Guardian path doesn't have endings. It has transitions. This was one. Others follow."* — secondary fallback pointing at VII

**Delivery semantics.** Act-transition lines queue at trigger time and deliver on the player's next town visit where Mayor Bro is present (which is almost always — Mayor Bro is a fixed town NPC per §14.3). Delivery:
- If the player is in town at trigger time, the line fires on next proximity-interaction with Mayor Bro (tapping him OR walking within 3u of him for 2+ seconds)
- If the player is in a zone at trigger time, the line queues; on next town entry, Mayor Bro's dialogue opens with the queued line (either on first tap or on proximity)
- If multiple transitions are queued (rare — e.g., player crosses Level 100 and then first Apex within one session before returning to town), lines deliver in reverse-chronological order (most recent first, then earlier). No batching, no stacking — each line plays in full and the next follows after a tap to dismiss.

**One line per transition, permanent.** Once a transition's line has delivered (played fully, player dismissed or walked away), the line does not repeat. Mayor Bro's dialogue returns to his §33.7 tone-phase defaults. The act-transition acknowledgment is a one-time moment tied to the first crossing — subsequent Apex defeats, subsequent capstone choices, subsequent Eclipse triggers do not re-fire the VII or IV line.

**Journey Log integration.** Each act-transition acknowledgment is recorded in the player's Journey Log (§27.4) with Mayor Bro's specific delivered line preserved verbatim. Players who want to review "what Mayor Bro said when I hit Ascendant" can find it in their log. This makes the ambient moment permanent as personal history even though it played only once in-world.

**Register.** See `creative-reference.md` §8.51 for the tonal specification — specific, observational, never congratulatory, never ceremonial. Mayor Bro is noticing what the player did, not celebrating it. The difference matters: celebration is about the game congratulating the player; noticing is about the town's central NPC registering what happened in the town's presence.

**Data.** Line pool stored in `act-transition-dialogue.json` with per-transition variant arrays and trigger-context selection rules. Total pool size: 35 lines across 6 transitions (5+6+6+6+6+6). Content-authoring scope is bounded; all lines are specified in this spec and can ship as initial content without a follow-up content pass.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.36): The seven-act structure from §53.1 has existed since early versions as a design framework — the game tracks player progress against 7 narrative acts defined by level + depth + choice milestones — but the acts have been entirely invisible to the player up to now. No popup, no narrator, no indicator announces "you have entered Act III." The player just plays, and occasionally realizes in retrospect that their relationship to the world has shifted. This invisibility is a deliberate choice: the game's narrative arc is emergent from systems (Echo Stones, NPC tone progression, zone atmosphere), not announced by a narrator. But the acts *do* happen — they represent real thresholds in the player's experience — and the invisibility means those thresholds pass without acknowledgment. The v12.36 change gives each transition one brief moment of in-world acknowledgment through Mayor Bro. Not ceremony. Not tutorial. Not cutscene. Just Mayor Bro noticing what the player did, once, in his observational register that already exists (§33.7 tone phases). The spec is specificity — Mayor Bro doesn't say "Congratulations on entering Act IV"; he says "Eclipse. I don't get to say that word to many people." The specificity is what makes the acknowledgment feel like recognition rather than announcement. Players who never notice the feature still progress through the game exactly as before; players who notice the feature feel the game noticing them back. This matches the v12.27 Combat Feedback Visibility, v12.29 Quiet Surfaces, and v12.32-v12.33 pedagogical-infrastructure threads: the game teaches its depth through structure, acknowledges what the player has done without celebration, and respects the player's capacity to feel progression without having it narrated at them. Mayor Bro as the canonical speaker ties act-transitions to the existing NPC-tone-progression system rather than introducing a new narrative surface — players are already learning to listen to Mayor Bro across the quest-count tone phases; act-transition acknowledgments extend that listening relationship. The one-line-per-transition permanence is load-bearing: the line happens once, it's preserved in the Journey Log, and it becomes personal history for this specific character. The feature is small but adds a particular texture to the seven-act arc that has been invisible until now.]`

### §54. Title Progression

#### §54.1 Title Hierarchy (v13 REPLACEMENT — Four-Register System, v14.0.5 EXPANDED — Fifth Dominant Title)

Canonical three-alignment title structure is replaced by four-register structure per §0.3. The v14.0.5 expansion adds a fifth primary endgame title (Weaver) for the balanced-register case per §33.4 and §53.4.

**Primary milestone titles:**

| Milestone | Title | Requirements | Permanent |
|-----------|-------|-------------|:---------:|
| Level 100 + beat Light zone | **Ascendant** | Clear depth 10 final boss | Yes |
| Register-diverse + Ferryman quest | **The Unlikely One** | All 8 NPC chains + at least 3 of 4 registers represented + cross Lawless Land without killing | Yes |
| True Guardian (Responsible dominant) | **Hero** | All 8 Guardian gauntlets + responsibleCount strictly greater than all other counts | Yes |
| True Guardian (Mischievous dominant) | **Trickster** | All 8 Guardian gauntlets + mischievousCount strictly greater than all other counts | Yes |
| True Guardian (Cool dominant) | **Arbiter** | All 8 Guardian gauntlets + coolCount strictly greater than all other counts | Yes |
| True Guardian (Ruthless dominant) | **Sovereign** | All 8 Guardian gauntlets + ruthlessCount strictly greater than all other counts | Yes |
| True Guardian (balanced — no register dominant) | **Weaver** (v14.0.5) | All 8 Guardian gauntlets + no single register strictly exceeds all others (any tie at the top qualifies, including 1/1/1/1 balanced) | Yes |
| Community feedback (v12.29) | **Voice** | Author a non-anonymous feedback item that accumulates 100+ upvotes (§51.4) | Yes |

**Pure-register titles (earned before True Guardian):**

| Register | Pure Title | Requirement |
|----------|------------|-------------|
| Responsible | **The Steadfast** | All 8 capstone choices are Responsible path |
| Mischievous | **The Mischief-Maker** | All 8 capstone choices are Mischievous path |
| Cool | **The Unmoved** | All 8 capstone choices are Cool path |
| Ruthless | **The Ruthless** | All 8 capstone choices are Ruthless path |

**Compound titles (earned from register mixes, 2-register dominant pairs):**

| Register Mix | Compound Title |
|--------------|----------------|
| Responsible + Mischievous | **The Steadfast Rascal** |
| Responsible + Cool | **The Solitary Shepherd** |
| Responsible + Ruthless | **The Iron Hand** |
| Mischievous + Cool | **The Quiet Prankster** |
| Mischievous + Ruthless | **The Trickster's Edge** |
| Cool + Ruthless | **The Unmoved Hand** |
| Three registers (pure mix of 3) | **The Three-Faced Bro** |
| All four (balanced 2/2/2/2) | **The Complete Bro** |

Compound titles are awarded automatically at True Guardian completion based on the player's accumulated register counts. They stack with the endgame title (Hero/Trickster/Arbiter/Sovereign/Weaver) — a player with 5 Responsible + 3 Mischievous choices earns both "Hero" (endgame) and "The Steadfast Rascal" (compound). A player with 2 Responsible + 2 Mischievous + 2 Cool + 2 Ruthless earns both "Weaver" (endgame) and "The Complete Bro" (compound) — these are two titles describing the same identity from different angles (the Weaver is the endgame resolution; the Complete Bro is the compound register identity) and naturally pair in the Title Showcase slot (e.g., **Weaver · The Complete Bro**).

All titles are permanent once earned. Players can display any earned title. The highest-tier earned title is shown by default. Titles are visible on nameplate and inspect card.

**Title Showcase slot (v12.36 CANONICAL, preserved).** In addition to the active title, players can pin one additional earned title as a **showcase title** that displays alongside the active title. The active title is the primary identity line; the showcase title is a secondary line displayed in a slightly smaller weight. Both are visible on the nameplate and inspect card.

Example showcase displays (active · showcase) with v13 register-aware compositions:

- **Hero · The Unlikely One** — True Guardian Responsible-dominant who also walked the register-diverse path
- **Trickster · The Steadfast Rascal** — Mischievous-dominant True Guardian whose mix earned the compound title
- **Sovereign · The Unmoved Hand** — Ruthless-dominant True Guardian with substantial Cool register
- **Arbiter · Gladiator** — Cool-dominant True Guardian who also mastered the Arena (§43)
- **Ascendant · Voice** — Level 100 Light-zone completer who has also earned the community-feedback Voice title

**Rules for the showcase slot** (preserved from v12.36):
- **One additional title only.** The showcase is a single slot.
- **Same title cannot be both active and showcase.** 
- **Either slot can be unset.** 
- **Showcase selection is changeable.** 

**Visual treatment** (preserved).

**v13 ADDITIVE — Constellation view on full inspect card.** In addition to the nameplate two-slot display, the full inspect card renders earned titles as a small constellation diagram — titles as connected nodes showing register relationships. Dominant titles sit centrally; pure-register titles sit at cardinal points; compound titles bridge between them; achievement-specific titles (Voice, Ascendant, Gladiator) form an outer ring. Dense identities produce richer constellations; minimal identities show sparse clean structures. The constellation is a single static image rendered from title data; it updates as new titles are earned.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): Four registers enable four dominant endgame titles and many compound titles that capture genuine identity variety. Canonical three-alignment system produced 3 dominant titles; v13 produces 4 dominant + 8 compound + 4 pure-register = 16 primary titles, not counting achievement titles. The constellation view on full inspect card shows structure rather than a flat list — players with deep histories produce visibly distinct constellations, making identity-at-a-glance richer than a scrolling list of title strings.]`

#### §54.2 Title Data Model (v13 REVISED for Four Registers)

```
rpg._titles = {
    earned: [],             // ['ascendant', 'the_unlikely_one', 'hero', 'the_steadfast_rascal']
    active: 'hero',         // currently displayed active title
    showcase: 'the_unlikely_one',  // currently displayed showcase title (v12.36), nullable
    endingPlayed: false,    // true after ending sequence
    endingAlignment: null,  // 'hero'|'trickster'|'arbiter'|'sovereign' — v13 register-keyed
    compoundTitle: null,    // v13: computed compound title from register mix, e.g. 'the_steadfast_rascal'
    pureRegisterTitle: null,// v13: 'the_steadfast'|'the_mischief_maker'|'the_unmoved'|'the_ruthless' if pure
};
```

**Compound title computation.** At True Guardian, the server computes `compoundTitle` from the four register counts. If any single register accounts for 8/8 choices, `pureRegisterTitle` is set instead (e.g., "the_steadfast" for 8 Responsible choices). If two registers dominate with comparable counts and two others are low, a compound title is awarded (e.g., "the_steadfast_rascal" for 5 Responsible + 3 Mischievous). If three registers are represented, "the_three_faced_bro" is awarded. If all four registers have counts (balanced 2/2/2/2 or similar), "the_complete_bro" is awarded.

**Showcase validation** (preserved from v12.36 canonical).

**Migration.** Canonical pre-v13 `endingAlignment` values ('benevolent', 'neutral', 'evil') map to ('hero', 'arbiter', 'sovereign') respectively. No existing characters have earned the Trickster ending (it is v13-new), so no migration path awards Trickster retroactively. Existing `earned` title arrays are preserved verbatim.

### §55. Guardian Zone System

#### §55.1 Guardian Status

Guardian status is granted upon clearing the Light zone (depth 10) final boss. Account-wide, permanent unlock. Visible on inspect card and nameplate as "Ascendant" title.

Guardian status unlocks access to 8 Guardian zones (one per standard element). It does NOT grant additional stat points, combat advantages, or access to non-Guardian content.

#### §55.2 Guardian Zone Structure

| Zone | Element | Monster Level | Monster Size |
|------|---------|:---:|:---:|
| Guardian Ember Fields | Flame | 100 | 176×176 |
| Guardian Frozen Shore | Frost | 100 | 176×176 |
| Guardian Tidal Caves | Water | 100 | 176×176 |
| Guardian Mistwood | Venom | 100 | 176×176 |
| Guardian Thunder Peaks | Storm | 100 | 176×176 |
| Guardian Deep Hollows | Stone | 100 | 176×176 |
| Guardian Sky Reaches | Wind | 100 | 176×176 |
| Guardian Verdant Wilds | Flora | 100 | 176×176 |

Accessed from town via the same spoke as their standard element — new path opens beyond depth 8. Guardian monsters inherit the v12.40 endgame-scale sprite size matching depths 8-10.

#### §55.3 Guardian Zone Monsters

5 monster types per zone: 3 recurring forms (Imp, Skeleton, Eye at level 100, 176×176 with enhanced visuals — battle scars, glowing eyes, elemental coronas) + 2 Guardian-exclusive unique monsters (never seen outside Guardian zones).

#### §55.4 Environmental Hazards

Guardian zones feature active environmental mechanics:

| Element | Hazard | Effect |
|---------|--------|--------|
| Flame | Rising lava | Floor sections flood periodically, forcing repositioning |
| Frost | Creeping ice | Standing still 3+ seconds applies Slow |
| Water | Tidal surges | Waves push player toward hazards |
| Venom | Toxic fog | Visibility reduces over time, clears near vents |
| Storm | Lightning strikes | Random targeted strikes with brief telegraph |
| Stone | Cave-ins | Ceiling collapses create obstacles and damage zones |
| Wind | Gust walls | Wind pushes player in shifting directions |
| Flora | Encroaching vines | Paths close off over time, arena shrinks |

Hazards add pressure, not primary lethality. They force movement and adaptation.

#### §55.5 Apex Bosses

Each Guardian zone has a 3-boss gauntlet: Imp King, Skeleton Lord, Elder Eye. Players can challenge individually or attempt the full gauntlet for bonus rewards.

**Boss size:** 240×240 (1.5× Guardian zone monster size).

**Imp King** — Apex Evader. Phase 1 (100–60%): fast evasive patterns, multi-directional magic, short teleports. Phase 2 (60–30%): summons 2–3 lesser Guardian imps, mixes melee dashes with ranged. Phase 3 (30–0%): wings shift to zone element color, attack speed doubles, teleport frequency increases, pure 1v1 execution. Elemental variation: lava trails (Flame), ice patches (Frost), thorn patches (Flora).

**Skeleton Lord** — Apex Aggressor. Phase 1 (100–60%): shield raised, player breaks through with sustained damage or collisions, slow devastating hits. Phase 2 (60–30%): shield breaks, 3–5 hit combo chains with tight dodge windows, each hit applies zone status. Phase 3 (30–0%): summons skeleton warriors, alternates attacking and commanding adds, periodic room-wide attack with safe zone.

**Elder Eye** — Apex Space Controller. Phase 1 (100–60%): central beam sweep, eyestalk tracking beams with iris pulse telegraph. Phase 2 (60–30%): spawns 2–3 detached smaller eyes, bullet-hell beam intersections. Phase 3 (30–0%): massive continuous rotating beam, iris cycles through 2–3 elements mid-phase.

#### §55.6 Guardian Loot

| Material | Source | Use |
|----------|--------|-----|
| Guardian Crystals | Gathering nodes | Guardian-tier enchanting |
| Guardian Ore | Gathering nodes | Guardian-tier blacksmithing |
| Apex Essence (Imp) | Imp King drop | Guardian-tier weapons |
| Apex Essence (Skeleton) | Skeleton Lord drop | Guardian-tier armor |
| Apex Essence (Eye) | Elder Eye drop | Guardian-tier amulets |
| Apex Heart | Full gauntlet bonus | Guardian-tier shields |

Guardian-tier gear: statistically equivalent to max Tier 5 but with unique visual effects (elemental auras, particle trails, glow). Prestige, not power disparity.

#### §55.7 Party Scaling

| Party Size | Boss HP | Boss Damage |
|:----------:|:-------:|:-----------:|
| 1 (solo) | 1.0× | 1.0× |
| 2 | 1.6× | 1.1× |
| 3 | 2.2× | 1.15× |
| 4 | 3.0× | 1.2× |

#### §55.8 Guardian Achievements

| ID | Name | Icon | Requirement |
|----|------|:----:|-------------|
| guardian_unlock | Ascendant | 🛡️ | Beat Light zone, achieve Guardian status |
| guardian_imp | Imp Slayer | 👿 | Defeat the Imp King in any Guardian zone |
| guardian_skel | Death's End | 💀 | Defeat the Skeleton Lord in any Guardian zone |
| guardian_eye | Eye Closer | 👁️ | Defeat the Elder Eye in any Guardian zone |
| guardian_gauntlet | Gauntlet Runner | ⚔️ | Complete a full 3-boss gauntlet |
| guardian_all | True Guardian | 👑 | Complete the gauntlet in all 8 Guardian zones |
| guardian_solo | Lone Guardian | 🌟 | Complete a gauntlet solo (no party) |

### §56. The Convergence

#### §56.1 Overview (v13 REVISED — Four Registers)

The Convergence is the game's ultimate endgame zone. It unlocks after a player achieves their final title (Hero / Trickster / Arbiter / Sovereign) via the ending sequence (§54). It is a single zone where Darkness and Light exist side by side — Dark and Light monsters occupy the same physical space. How the player experiences this zone is fundamentally different depending on their alignment register.

The Convergence is where the game's duality theme becomes a gameplay mechanic. Heroes lead Light. Sovereigns command Dark. Arbiters stand alone and weaponize Eclipse. **Tricksters (v13 new) play with both factions** — they don't command either, but Dark and Light beings both tolerate their presence and occasionally produce unexpected cooperative moments in response to Trickster play.

**v13 ADDITIVE — Perceptual layer per register.** Each alignment register perceives the Convergence zone differently. The underlying geometry, monster placement, and gameplay mechanics are identical; the visual and audio rendering varies. Four different worlds depending on who you are:

- **Steadfast Bro** (Responsible dominant) sees **radiant sanctuary** — warm gold-and-white palette, soft choral ambient, architectural clarity. The canonical Hero perception.
- **Mischief-Maker** (Mischievous dominant) sees **carnival** — kinetic shadow-and-light playing tricks on each other, colors shifting unexpectedly, playful motes and shimmer. Ambient audio is lighter, occasionally includes distant laughter-like tones. The Convergence reads as a place of *play* rather than cosmic weight.
- **Unmoved Bro** (Cool dominant) sees **architectural clarity** — monochrome palette, pure form without decoration, ambient reduced to single sustained tone. The Convergence reads as structure observed from distance.
- **Ruthless Bro** sees **dark citadel** — cold palette, sharp-edged architecture, ambient includes deep percussive pulses. The canonical Sovereign perception.

**Balanced characters** (all four registers represented) see a **flickering overlay** — the four perceptions cycle subtly over time, with the player's current dominant register determining the primary layer. The Convergence shows its full self only to those who held all four registers.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): Canonical three alignments have three perceptions; four registers means four perceptions. Mischievous perception specifically is new and distinctive — the Convergence as a place of playful delight rather than moral weight. Per §0.1 tonal rule, the Mischievous perception's audio and visual design carries the game's humor register into its highest-stakes endgame zone. The visual-only layer preserves all §56 canonical mechanics (combat, allies, drops) — nothing changes mechanically across registers; what changes is what the world *looks and feels like*.]`

#### §56.2 Access

| Requirement | Details |
|-------------|---------|
| Final title achieved | Hero / Trickster / Arbiter / Sovereign (§54) — v13 four-register |
| Zone location | Accessible from town center — a new path opens after the ending sequence |
| Monster level | 100 |
| Monster size | 176×176 (Dark and Light beings coexist — v12.40 endgame-scale) |

#### §56.3 Alignment-Specific Gameplay (v13 REVISED — Four Registers)

**Hero (Responsible/Steadfast dominant):**

Light beings in the Convergence are allied to the Hero. They cannot be attacked and do not attack. Instead, they actively fight alongside the Hero against Dark beings — healing, buffing, and dealing damage as AI companions.

- Light allies heal the Hero periodically
- Light allies apply Reveal status to nearby Dark enemies (stripping resistance)
- Light allies deal moderate damage to Dark monsters, speeding up kills
- The Hero's role shifts from solo fighter to **commander** — positioning to maximize ally effectiveness
- Only **Dark gems (Tier 7)** drop from kills. Light gems do not drop for Heroes in this zone.
- Heroes must trade with Sovereigns to obtain Tier 7 Light gems

**Sovereign (Ruthless dominant):**

Dark beings in the Convergence are allied to the Sovereign. They cannot be attacked and do not attack the Sovereign. Instead, they fight alongside the Sovereign against Light beings — dealing massive damage but with **erratic, unpredictable target selection**.

- Dark allies deal high burst damage to Light enemies
- Dark allies exhibit **targeting chaos**: on each attack cycle, there is a CONVERGENCE_DARK_ALLY_TARGET_CHAOS_CHANCE(0.25) chance the ally targets a non-focus Light enemy instead of the Sovereign's currently-focused target.
- Dark allies apply Curse status to nearby Light enemies every 12s
- The Sovereign's experience is **volatile and chaotic**
- Only **Light gems (Tier 7)** drop from kills. Dark gems do not drop for Sovereigns in this zone.
- Sovereigns must trade with Heroes to obtain Tier 7 Dark gems

**Arbiter (Cool/Unmoved dominant):**

Neither Dark nor Light beings are allied to the Arbiter. Both are hostile. The Arbiter fights alone against both factions simultaneously — the hardest combat experience in the game.

However, the Arbiter has a **unique mechanic** available to no other alignment: they can trigger **Eclipse collisions** in the Convergence by manipulating Dark and Light monsters into proximity. When a Dark monster with Curse status and a Light monster with Reveal status are within CONVERGENCE_ECLIPSE_RADIUS(30u) of each other, the Arbiter can trigger an Eclipse collision that deals massive AOE damage to all nearby enemies of both factions.

- No allies, no healing, no buffs — pure solo combat
- Both Dark and Light monsters are hostile
- Eclipse collisions are devastating AOE events that can clear clusters of both factions simultaneously
- **Both Dark and Light gems drop** for Arbiters, but at moderate rates (not as fast as a specialist)
- The Arbiter is self-sufficient but must master the unique Eclipse manipulation mechanic to be efficient
- The Arbiter is the **only player who can weaponize Eclipse as a zone mechanic**

**Trickster (Mischievous dominant) — v13 NEW:**

Neither Dark nor Light beings are outright hostile to the Trickster; neither are they outright allied. Both factions treat the Trickster with *bewildered tolerance* — monsters pause before engaging, sometimes looking at each other as if confused why this Bro is here.

- Dark and Light monsters will attack the Trickster, but with reduced aggression (longer aggro windups, occasional target-loss pauses)
- Trickster has a unique mechanic: **Mimicry.** By using any emote near a group of monsters (Dark or Light), the Trickster can cause a CONVERGENCE_MIMIC_CHANCE(0.30) chance per monster that the monster briefly mimics the emote — a 1-second confusion during which the monster does not attack and takes +20% damage. This turns the emote system (§30.5) into a tactical tool unique to the Trickster alignment.
- **Both Dark and Light gems drop** for Tricksters, at moderate rates comparable to Arbiter.
- The Trickster mechanic produces *opportunities for play* — the Trickster can chain emote-triggered mimicries across groups of monsters, producing combat sequences that feel more like performance than battle.
- Trickster combat has distinctive visual language: monsters mid-mimicry have a brief cartoon-style "stunned" visual (swirling motes above head, animation pause) that is visible to other players.

The Trickster gem drops are moderate (Tier 6 for both Dark and Light, like Arbiter), reflecting the same design principle: *commitment (Hero/Sovereign) is more powerful than flexibility (Arbiter/Trickster), but flexibility is more independent than commitment.* Tricksters who want peak Tier 7 dual-gem builds must still trade with Heroes and Sovereigns, participating in the cross-register economy.

**Why the Trickster mechanic is Mimicry.** The Mischievous register's signature action is *warm trickery* — teasing, pranking, bending rules with affection rather than malice. Mimicry is the game's expression of that register in combat: the Trickster doesn't command monsters (like Hero/Sovereign) and doesn't dominate them (like Arbiter via Eclipse manipulation); they *confuse them into brief cooperation*. The mechanic rewards engagement with the game's emote system, turning social expression into combat capability.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): Trickster gameplay gives the Mischievous register its own mechanical identity in the endgame. Canonical three-alignment Convergence design had no home for the Mischievous Bro; they would have been forced into Arbiter (a mismatch — Arbiter is detached where Mischievous is engaged). The Mimicry mechanic captures the register's essential character: creative engagement that doesn't fit the rules everyone else is playing by. The Trickster is neither commanding (Hero/Sovereign's role) nor confronting (Arbiter's role) — they're *playing* with the encounter, and the game rewards them for finding humor in endgame combat. The moderate gem tier matches Arbiter's balance principle: flexibility trades peak power for independence.]`

#### §56.4 The Narrative Payoff (v13 REVISED — Four-Register Economy)

The Convergence creates an organic social ecosystem:

- Heroes and Sovereigns farm the same zone simultaneously but fight different enemies. They see each other's enemies as allies. A Hero watching a Sovereign fight Light beings — creatures the Hero considers friends — creates emergent narrative tension without any scripted dialogue.

- The marketplace becomes register-driven. Heroes produce surplus Dark gems. Sovereigns produce surplus Light gems. Arbiters and Tricksters produce surplus of both types at moderate rates. Peak power requires Tier 7 gems, which only Heroes and Sovereigns drop. **Register opposites must trade with each other for peak builds;** Arbiters and Tricksters can self-sustain at Tier 6 or also trade up.

- The Arbiter stands between both factions, needing nothing from anyone. Their independence is real but earned through harder combat. When other players see an Arbiter triggering Eclipse collisions in the Convergence, that is the most visually spectacular moment in the game.

- **The Trickster (v13) stands *among* both factions**, causing them to pause and mimic rather than commanding them. When other players see a Trickster dancing in a Convergence corridor with Dark and Light monsters briefly pausing to dance along, that is the game's most unexpectedly *joyful* endgame moment — and one of its most visually distinctive.

#### §56.5 Convergence Gem System (v13 REVISED — Four Registers)

| Gem Type | Tier | Source | Who can farm |
|----------|:----:|--------|:------------:|
| Standard Dark | 5+ | Depth 9 (Darkness) | All players (pre-ending) |
| Standard Light | 5+ | Depth 10 (Light) | All players (pre-ending) |
| Convergence Dark | 7 | Convergence Dark monsters | Hero only |
| Convergence Light | 7 | Convergence Light monsters | Sovereign only |
| Convergence Dark (Arbiter) | 6 | Convergence Dark monsters | Arbiter only |
| Convergence Light (Arbiter) | 6 | Convergence Light monsters | Arbiter only |
| Convergence Dark (Trickster) | 6 | Convergence Dark monsters | Trickster only (v13 NEW) |
| Convergence Light (Trickster) | 6 | Convergence Light monsters | Trickster only (v13 NEW) |

Tier 7 gems are the strongest in the game. A fully optimized build requires both Tier 7 Dark AND Tier 7 Light gems, which means a Hero or Sovereign who has traded for their missing type.

Arbiter and Trickster gems are Tier 6 — stronger than any pre-Convergence gem but below the specialist ceiling. Players of these registers can equip a full set without trading but will never match the peak power of a committed Hero or Sovereign who trades.

The absolute strongest players in the game are a Hero and a Sovereign who traded with each other. The game's ultimate power literally requires opposites cooperating.

#### §56.5a Communal Gem Pool — Convergence Shrine (v12.41 CANONICAL, v13 ADDITIVE — Growing Crystal Aesthetic)

The cross-alignment gem trade economy established in §56.5 requires direct Hero↔Sovereign cooperation to reach the absolute-peak Tier 7 dual-gem build. At low server populations, this direct cooperation may be thin — a Hero online without any Sovereigns online cannot complete their trade loop, and vice versa. v12.41 adds a fallback economy mechanic that is **always present and always available** regardless of population: a **Communal Gem Pool** at a neutral shrine in the Convergence, accumulating from Convergence deaths and available for purchase by any alignment at a gold premium.

**v13 AESTHETIC REPLACEMENT.** The canonical "shrine structure" (stone altar with NPC-trader framing) is replaced by a **Growing Crystal** at the physical boundary between the Shadow and Radiant halves of the Convergence. All mechanical rules are preserved — pool accumulation, 30% drop rate, 2× purchase premium, contribution credits, etc. — but the visual and audio aesthetic becomes the Growing Crystal rather than a trading altar.

**The crystal grows and shrinks visibly.** The crystal's size is proportional to the pool's current gem count. When gems are contributed (via Convergence death), the crystal visibly grows by a small increment with a faint chime. When gems are purchased, the crystal shrinks by a small increment with a softer tone. A crystal on a Year 3 server is visibly massive compared to a crystal on a Day 3 server — the pool's accumulated history is visible geometry.

**Interaction.** Players approach the crystal and interact with it. The crystal's surface becomes a scrollable display of pool inventory (same grid as canonical NPC-trader display). Purchases and contribution-credits function identically to canonical. No NPC trader present — the crystal itself is the interface.

**Aesthetic details.** The crystal refracts the Convergence's zone lighting. In the Hero perception, it shines white-gold. In the Sovereign perception, it glows dark-red. In the Arbiter perception, it pulses steady pale light. In the Trickster perception (v13), it flickers playfully through all three states plus occasional unexpected colors. The crystal is the only Convergence object whose visual changes per player register per §56.1.

**Server history through the crystal.** Year 5 players stand in the Convergence and see a crystal built from thousands of prior Hero and Sovereign deaths — each contribution literally visible as part of the crystal's mass. The crystal is a monument to accumulated Hero-Sovereign cooperation across the server's history. Tapping the crystal's base surfaces a rolling "who contributed recently" view showing the most recent 20 contributors (like the canonical Recent Vows display at §1.5.1 but for Convergence).

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13 aesthetic): Canonical NPC-trader framing is bureaucratic. A growing crystal that accumulates physical presence over server history turns the Communal Pool into a *monument* — players see the aggregate result of years of Hero-Sovereign cooperation as visible geometry. Year 3 crystal is visibly larger than Year 1 crystal; players feel the server's history when they approach the Convergence. All canonical mechanical rules are preserved — only the visual treatment changes, and the change adds a new expressive layer (crystal grows, crystal shrinks, crystal's color shifts per register perception). The interface remains unchanged for mechanical interaction; the aesthetic layer adds presence without adding bureaucracy.]`

**The shrine structure (CANONICAL, v13 AESTHETIC REVISED).** The crystal sits at the physical boundary between the Shadow and Radiant halves of the Convergence map — the midpoint between the zone's two engagement regions. The crystal is non-combat infrastructure (cannot be damaged, cannot be fought) and is accessible to Heroes, Sovereigns, Arbiters, and Tricksters alike. It is always present in the Convergence regardless of server population, time of day, or any other external factor. Players in the Convergence walk past the crystal during normal movement between the zone's halves and can interact with it at will.

**Pool accumulation — gem transfer on Convergence death.** When any player dies in the Convergence (which happens in the alignment-split engagement zones, when monsters defeat the player), each equipped element gem has a `CONVERGENCE_POOL_DROP_RATE(0.30)` chance to transfer to the Communal Pool instead of dropping to the ground per normal death-scatter mechanics (§23.7). The transferred gem is added to the pool's inventory at the crystal; the player sees a "Your [Element] gem is now in the Crystal" notification at their death screen acknowledging the contribution.

The remaining 70% of gems drop normally to the ground per existing death-scatter rules and can be recovered by the player's subsequent return to the death location (or looted by other players in Lawless-Land-equivalent scenarios — though the Convergence is not Lawless). The 30% pool-transfer rate is calibrated to produce a steady pool inflow without depleting individual players' death recovery; most gems still return to their owner through the normal mechanic, but a meaningful minority feed the communal economy.

**Pool inventory display.** Players interacting with the crystal see the current pool inventory as a list of gems grouped by element (Flame, Frost, Water, Venom, Storm, Stone, Wind, Flora, Dark, Light), with per-element counts and tier distributions. The display is a simple sortable grid: element glyph (per §10.1 shape palette), gem count at each tier, and buy-price column. Players scroll through the inventory and tap to purchase any gem currently available in the pool.

**Purchase pricing.** Gems are purchased from the pool at `CONVERGENCE_POOL_PRICE_MULTIPLIER(2.0)` times the §23 base marketplace price for equivalent gems — a 2× gold premium reflecting the pool's role as fallback rather than primary trade channel. This premium applies to all purchases regardless of the purchasing player's alignment.

**Contribution credit — not ownership.** When a player's gem transfers to the pool via their death, the pool records a contribution-credit note attached to that gem. The contribution note displays when any other player later purchases that gem: "Contributed by [Player] on [Day N]." This provides the contributing player with a soft social acknowledgment of their pool contribution without granting them economic ownership — purchasers pay the 2× premium to the pool (gold is burned, not transferred to the contributor), and the original contributor gets no gold when their gem is purchased. The credit-note is identity-acknowledgment, not economic claim.

**Pool inflow rate vs draw rate — population-adaptive economy.**

- **High population:** Many Convergence deaths per day → steady pool inflow. Many players drawing from the pool → steady pool outflow. Pool churns rapidly as new gems arrive and are purchased within hours; inventory stays varied but thin at any moment. The pool operates as a just-in-time supplementary economy alongside direct player-to-player trades.
- **Low population:** Few Convergence deaths per day → slower pool inflow. Few players drawing → slower outflow. Pool accumulates slowly but doesn't empty; inventory grows over days into a larger reserve. A single Hero logging in on a sparse-population day finds the pool has accumulated contributions from the past week's sporadic deaths, including Sovereign-dropped Dark gems they would not be able to obtain through direct trade. The pool functions as a population-independent supply when direct cooperation is thin.

The economic shape self-adjusts to population density — no server-side "low population detected" threshold, no NPC appearance/disappearance logic, no mode-switching. The pool is always there; its behavior at any moment reflects the current server's activity level.

**No alignment restriction on purchases.** Heroes can purchase Dark gems from the pool (completing their cross-alignment build without needing a live Sovereign trader). Sovereigns can purchase Light gems. Arbiters can purchase either. The pool is alignment-neutral by design — it replaces the direct-trade channel's population dependency without introducing new alignment friction.

**Pool inventory cap — none.** The pool has no size limit. Accumulated gems persist until purchased. A pool that grows to thousands of gems over months of sparse activity remains valid; purchases deplete it over time. Storage is server-side persistent data (see architecture §9.5.13).

**Gold sink via the 2× premium.** Purchases pay gold into the pool (burned, not transferred), producing a steady gold sink proportional to pool engagement. This integrates with the §23 economic model as an endgame-tier gold sink that specifically absorbs the currency wealth of Tier 7 build-completing players — the players most likely to have accumulated surplus gold through endgame activity. The gold sink scales with how many Convergence players engage with the fallback economy; high-population servers with active direct trade produce smaller pool-related gold sinks, low-population servers produce larger ones (because direct trade is thinner, more players route through the pool). This is the correct incidence — the players most affected by low-population trade friction pay the gold premium that compensates for the friction.

**INV-22 Gold Equilibrium preserved.** The pool's 2× purchase premium is a new gold sink proportional to pool engagement. Pool engagement is bounded by pool inflow (deaths × 0.30 transfer rate), which is bounded by Convergence death frequency, which is bounded by player population and combat outcomes. The new sink's gold-flow rate is strictly smaller than existing §23 sinks (Gem Cutting Service, NPC Enchanter Dust Buyback) at all reasonable population scales. No INV-22 recalibration required. At server scales where the pool's gold sink is large enough to be noticeable, it is functioning correctly — absorbing surplus gold from endgame players via a fallback economy they voluntarily participate in.

**INV-24 Wealth Concentration note.** The pool's contribution-credit mechanic is *not* an economic transfer — contributors get identity credit, not gold, when their gem is purchased. No wealth-concentration vector is introduced. Purchasers' gold is burned; contributors receive no gold. This preserves INV-24's "no new peer-to-peer concentration vectors" discipline.

**Anti-cheat.** Pool operations (contribution on death, purchase on interaction) are server-authoritative via existing death-resolution and shrine-interaction pipelines. No client-side pool manipulation possible. The contribution credit note is written at the transfer-time event and cannot be retroactively modified. Purchasers' gold deduction is atomic with pool-inventory decrement. Standard anti-cheat protections per §17.

**Why a pool instead of an NPC.** An alternative design (the plan's original v12.25 draft) proposed an NPC called "The Trader" who appears in the Convergence during low-population windows and vanishes when population normalizes. The revision to a community gem pool converts a "failure indicator NPC" (an NPC whose visible presence signals the server's population is low) into an always-present emergent economy mechanic. The pool is atmospherically better — the Convergence itself becomes the economy, with gem contributions accumulating at the shrine as a visible trace of the zone's two alignments meeting there. The Convergence's existing lore ("two alignments meet here and both leave traces") is extended mechanically: deaths leave gem traces, and those traces become shared resources. No appearance/disappearance logic, no population-threshold metric, no NPC speech triggered by server health. The mechanic is self-regulating and population-adaptive through its own economic behavior rather than through external triggers.

**Tuning.**

| Variable | Default | Min | Max | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| CONVERGENCE_POOL_DROP_RATE | 0.30 | 0.10 | 0.50 | Gem transfer chance per equipped gem on Convergence death |
| CONVERGENCE_POOL_PRICE_MULTIPLIER | 2.0 | 1.5 | 3.0 | Multiplier applied to base §23 marketplace price for pool purchases |

The original v12.25 draft's `CONVERGENCE_TRADE_MINIMUM` constant (population-threshold trigger for NPC Trader appearance) is **not shipped** — the pool has no threshold because it is always present.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.41): The Convergence's cross-alignment trade economy is load-bearing for the game's endgame power curve — a Hero and a Sovereign who trade with each other reach the peak Tier 7 dual-gem build (§56.5). At high server populations this works naturally; at low populations, a Hero logging in to an empty server cannot complete their trade loop. The plan's original draft solved this with an NPC Trader who appeared when population fell below threshold and vanished when it recovered. That works mechanically but introduces atmospheric friction — an NPC whose visible presence signals "the server's population is low" is essentially a failure indicator in character form. The community gem pool revision converts the failure indicator into an always-present emergent economy. Deaths in the Convergence leave gem traces that feed a communal pool; any alignment can draw from the pool at a 2× gold premium. The Convergence itself becomes the fallback economy rather than an NPC deployed to manage the failure mode. This is atmospherically consistent with the zone's existing lore (two alignments meet here and both leave traces) and mechanically self-regulating (high population → high pool churn; low population → larger pool reserve; no threshold or mode-switching needed). The 2× gold premium ensures the pool is genuinely fallback-tier rather than primary — direct Hero↔Sovereign trades remain the better-value path when available. The 30% death-transfer rate is calibrated to produce steady inflow without meaningfully depleting individual players' death recovery (70% of gems still drop normally for the dying player to recover). The contribution-credit mechanic gives contributors soft social acknowledgment without economic transfer, preserving INV-24's "no new wealth-concentration vectors" discipline. Gold burned on purchase integrates cleanly with the §23 gold-sink model — the pool produces an endgame-tier gold sink proportional to engagement, scaling naturally with population density in exactly the direction the incidence should fall (low-population servers with thinner direct trade produce larger per-transaction sinks because more players route through the pool). The design is better than the original NPC-Trader concept on every axis: atmospheric consistency, mechanical elegance, population adaptiveness, and alignment with the game's existing economic and lore systems. This is why the revised spec supersedes the original — the revision isn't a refinement but a genuinely different approach that produces a better outcome across the board.]`

#### §56.6 Convergence Ally AI

**Light allies (Hero path):**

- Behavior: supportive, protective, predictable
- Healing: LIGHT_ALLY_HEAL_RATE(5% of Hero's max HP every 10s)
- Damage: moderate, consistent, single-target focused
- Buff: Reveal applied to nearest Dark enemy every 15s
- Count: 2-3 Light allies accompany the Hero at any time
- Light allies do not follow into dungeon boss rooms — the Apex bosses (if replayed) are always solo/party

**Dark allies (Sovereign path):**

- Behavior: aggressive, chaotic, unpredictable
- Damage: high burst, multi-target, sometimes overkill
- Targeting chaos: CONVERGENCE_DARK_ALLY_TARGET_CHAOS_CHANCE(0.25) — 25% chance per Dark ally attack cycle to target a non-focus Light enemy instead of the Sovereign's currently-focused target. The ally still only damages Light enemies (never the Sovereign), but its damage is often wasted on wrong targets. **v12.24: replaces prior friendly-fire mechanic** (Dark allies occasionally damaging the Sovereign directly for 10% of attack damage) to restore mechanical parity with the Hero path. The chaos identity is preserved through inefficient targeting rather than through damage tax on the player.
- Debuff: Curse applied to nearest Light enemy every 12s
- Count: 2-3 Dark allies accompany the Sovereign at any time
- Dark allies are visually wilder, more erratic in movement than Light allies — the aesthetic matches the chaos

#### §56.7 Convergence Eclipse Mechanic (Arbiter Only)

**Trigger conditions:**
1. A Dark monster must have Curse status active
2. A Light monster must have Reveal status active
3. Both must be within CONVERGENCE_ECLIPSE_RADIUS(30u) of each other
4. The Arbiter must deal damage to either monster while both conditions are met

**Effect:**
- Massive AOE burst (Eclipse collision formula from §10.6, with CONVERGENCE_ECLIPSE_BONUS(1.5×) multiplier)
- Damages ALL monsters (Dark and Light) within the radius
- Consumes both statuses
- Drops either a Dark or Light gem (50/50) on each kill from the Eclipse
- Visual: full Eclipse ground mark (dark-and-gold) at 2× normal size, persists 8s
- Sound: True Eclipse collision sound with extended harmonic tail

**Skill expression:** The Arbiter must apply Curse to a Dark monster (via Dark element weapon) and Reveal to a Light monster (via Light element weapon) or lure pre-afflicted monsters into proximity. This requires dual-element weapon mastery, spatial awareness, and timing — the highest skill ceiling mechanic in the game.

#### §56.8 Convergence Achievements

| ID | Name | Icon | Requirement |
|----|------|:----:|-------------|
| conv_enter | The Convergence | ⚡ | Enter the Convergence for the first time |
| conv_hero_100 | Light Commander | ✨ | Kill 100 Dark monsters with Light allies |
| conv_sov_100 | Dark Commander | 🌑 | Kill 100 Light monsters with Dark allies |
| conv_eclipse | Eclipse Wielder | 🌓 | Trigger 10 Eclipse collisions as Arbiter |
| conv_eclipse_50 | Walking Eclipse | 💫 | Trigger 50 Eclipse collisions as Arbiter |
| conv_trade | Duality Complete | 🤝 | Equip both Tier 7 Dark and Light gems (requires trading) |
| conv_full_gear | Convergence Master | 👑 | Fully equip Convergence-tier gear in all slots |

`[DESIGN INTENT: The Convergence is the game's thesis statement expressed as a zone. Connection requires vulnerability (Heroes allied with Light are exposed to Dark). Power requires chaos (Sovereigns wielding Dark allies accept unpredictable targeting and must compensate through tactical positioning). Independence requires mastery (Arbiters must weaponize Eclipse alone). And the ultimate conclusion: the strongest version of yourself requires cooperation with your opposite. Hero needs Sovereign. Sovereign needs Hero. The Arbiter needs no one — and pays for that freedom with peak power. Every philosophical position the game explored through alignment choices over hundreds of hours culminates here as lived gameplay.]`

### §57. The Ending (v14.0.4 CANONICAL — The Ritual)

*Supersedes v14.0, v14.0.1, v14.0.2, v14.0.3. The ritual's mechanical structure is preserved unchanged from v14.0.3; v14.0.4 is a vocabulary reframe only. §57.1 now names collision grammar as the engine that produces the ritual's shape, with unification thesis (§0.4) reframed as collision grammar at the self-scale. No mechanical changes, no execution-quality gate changes, no asset changes. Historical record of superseded iterations preserved in §57.HIST.1 through §57.HIST.8.*

#### §57.1 Overview

The ending is a private ritual performed in a chamber at the Convergence's center, accessible only to players who have earned a final register title (Hero / Trickster / Arbiter / Sovereign per §54) and accumulated RITUAL_GEM_THRESHOLD Convergence gems (50 per §57.2).

The ritual is the first collision in the game whose both elements are the player. Per §0.4, collision is the game's core verb — two elements meet, a third state emerges. For a hundred hours the grammar has been external: the player's element collides with something else. At the ritual, the grammar turns inward. The pole the player has become (Light / Dark / Eclipse-warm / Eclipse-reserved, derived from their register arc) and the seed that pole grew from (their first-weapon grip, silently remembered since first equip) are the two elements. The twenty-second chest-cradle hold is the collision window stretched to ritual duration — selves take longer to meet than elements do. The Fragment is the third state: the player themselves, rendered permanent. The sky flash is the world's atmospheric layer registering one more collision event, in the same register it registers any other.

This reframe is the unification thesis (§0.4) stated in the game's native vocabulary. Unification through the permanent holding of opposites is what collision grammar looks like at the self-scale. The ritual is possible only at the end because the player needed a hundred hours to become a second element — early-game Bros have not yet accumulated enough self for an internal collision to have two poles.

A Bro, whose identity has been accumulating across a hundred hours of play, performs the act of holding their origin and their present simultaneously for twenty seconds. This holding is the ritual. The Fragment is what the holding looks like when it crystallizes permanently. The sky flash is what the world sees when one more address becomes one more specific identity.

**Five elements carry the ritual:** the **grip** (the seed the pole grew from), the **eight lights** (the pole itself, rendered as the accumulated signature of every prior collision the player has caused), the **chest-cradle hold** (the collision window, sustained), the **Fragment construction** (the third state, made permanent), and the **sky flash** (the world's atmospheric register of the collision). Each does distinct work. None is scaffolding.

**Three whimsy anchors frame the ritual's gravity:** the **Ferryman's dry threshold line** (before the Rise), the **grip's gentle descent** (mid-Rise), and **Mayor Bro's return acknowledgment** (post-walkout). Per §0.1 tonal rule and §0.4 unification thesis, the ritual holds grave and absurd simultaneously — neither register is suspended, even at the ritual's most private beats.

**Two honest outcomes:** completion (grip and current weapon dissolve into a permanent Soul Fragment; sky flashes server-wide) and walkout (grip retained in inventory as resume token; player walks out with both objects). Walkout is the collision deferred rather than refused — the two poles are present, the ring has risen, the grip has descended, but the window closed before the third state emerged. The grip in inventory is the unresolved collision in portable form. The v14.0.1 Overwhelmed Fragment is explicitly deprecated (§57.HIST.8) — input loss during the hold resolves to walkout.

**Design thesis, stated plainly.** The game has silently remembered the first weapon the player ever equipped. It returns that memory at the end because the ritual is a collision and both elements must be physically present for the collision to read. The player holds the pole they became in one hand (as the ring around them, as the weapon they wield) and the seed that pole grew from in the other (the rusted grip against the chest), for twenty seconds, and either lets the collision resolve into a permanent Fragment or carries the unresolved collision back out. The most powerful object in the world is the rusted trash the Bro picked up in their first minute, held close to the chest alongside whatever they became, because that trash is what the whole pole grew out of. Unification is the refusal to release either side — which is what the collision window requires to produce a third state at the self-scale.

#### §57.2 Access

Access requirements preserved from v14.0.1:

| Requirement | Details |
|-------------|---------|
| Final title earned | Hero / Trickster / Arbiter / Sovereign (§54) |
| Convergence gems accumulated | RITUAL_GEM_THRESHOLD(50) |
| Entry location | Ritual chamber at Convergence center (§57.3) |

The Ferryman stands at the chamber threshold per §33.6. His role is unchanged: between-figure, psychopomp, unexplaining. He does not comment on the ritual's cosmological weight. He does not greet the player with reverence.

##### §57.2a Ferryman threshold lines (v14.0.3 NEW)

As the player crosses the chamber threshold, the Ferryman delivers one canonical line matched to the player's dominant register. The line is dry, brief, and refuses to make the moment precious.
