Per §0.1 and §0.4, this is the ritual's whimsy anchor at its opening beat — the absurd register establishing itself before the Rise's grave beats begin.

| Dominant Register | Ferryman Threshold Line |
|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Responsible (Steadfast) | *"You've been ready for this for a while. It was polite of you to wait."* |
| Ruthless (Sovereign) | *"Most people hesitate. You didn't. I appreciate the efficiency."* |
| Mischievous (Trickster) | *"I wondered if you'd actually show up. You did. Here we are."* |
| Cool (Unmoved) | *"You're here. You know what this is. Let's not pretend."* |

The line is delivered at conversational volume, not ceremonial. Audio mix treats it as an ordinary NPC line, not a ritual cue. The player may acknowledge it or not; the ritual proceeds regardless. After the line, the Ferryman returns to his canonical unexplaining register for the rest of the ritual.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v14.0.3): The Ferryman's threshold line is the ritual's tonal anchor. Without it, the ritual opens grave, sustains grave through every interior beat, and only admits whimsy at the post-walkout Mayor Bro line or the Hall's architectural details. Per §0.1 and §0.4, grave-only surfaces violate the thesis. The threshold line is brief, dry, and register-matched — it carries the Ferryman's canonical §33.6 role (between-figure who names acts without narrating them) and the game's tonal rule (every grave moment admits its release valve). The four lines are authored to refuse preciousness without undermining weight. A Responsible player is acknowledged as punctual; a Ruthless player as efficient; a Mischievous player as slightly surprising; a Cool player as too honest to dissemble. Each reading is true to the player's arc; none is heraldic. The ritual's interior beats can now carry their full gravity because the absurd register has already spoken once at the threshold and will speak again at the Hall and at Mayor Bro's return. The whimsy frames the gravity without interrupting it.]`

#### §57.3 The Chamber

Preserved from v14.0.1/v14.0.2 with the authorship lore at §57.13. The chamber is circular, built over generations by past Guardians through the Keeper-authoring mechanic. Ten elemental shapes are inlaid in the floor in the canonical arrangement. Gold tracery crosses the bedrock stonework in patterns that reflect the server's accumulated authorship history. The ceiling carries a constellation arrangement that predates any living player.

The player enters carrying whatever weapon they have equipped, as normal. No inventory manipulation, no cutscene, no loading screen between the Convergence and the chamber. After the Ferryman's threshold line, the camera tightens toward the center of the chamber and the Rise begins.

#### §57.4 The Rise

Eight lights rise around the player in **canonical elemental-wheel order** — the same order for every player, across the entire server's history. This is the cosmological structure: the eight that fractured from the primordial pair, returning to witness their source being held again.

1. Flame
2. Frost
3. Water
4. Venom
5. Storm
6. Stone
7. Wind
8. Flora

**Intensity is personal, order is cosmological.** Each light's brightness is fixed at **30–100% linear scaling by lifetime usage share**. The element the player used most renders at 100%. The element they used least (or never) renders at 30% — never lower, to preserve the cosmological ring's visible continuity. Elements in between scale linearly. A specialist's ring has one 100% light and seven ~30% lights. A generalist's ring has eight lights clustered between 40-70%. Every distribution produces a legible pattern without tuning.

**Why this split.** Order encodes the world's fracture (the same eight for every player, rising in the same sequence). Intensity encodes the individual's relationship to that fracture (how much each player engaged with each elemental identity). The Rise teaches cosmology and reads personal history in a single image. Neither competes with the grip (origin-memory) or the Fragment's upper silhouette (build-identity), because history-as-atmosphere is doing work neither of the other two does.

**Constraints on the lights.** Rise in order. Settle into position. No further movement. No pulsing. No color shift during the Rise. No response to player input. Each light holds its rest state for approximately 0.8 seconds before the next rises. Total Rise duration for the eight: ~6.5 seconds.

#### §57.5 The Grip's Return

**Between the fifth and sixth light's rise,** the player's first-weapon grip appears in their empty off-hand.

**Source data.** The `first_weapon_memory` field is written silently at the moment of first weapon equip, per character. The field records: material, weapon type, acquisition zone, and repair count. It is never exposed in any UI, never inspectable, never referenced by any other system. It persists through:

- Salvage of the physical weapon
- Trade, gift, mail, or marketplace sale
- Death-scatter loss
- Hardening-failure destruction

The memory is the game's quiet record of where the player started. The player does not know the record exists until this moment.

**The grip's appearance — gentle descent (v14.0.3 specification).** The grip is not summoned. It is not materialized in place. The grip descends from just above the player's view into the waiting off-hand over approximately half a second. The movement is slow, smooth, and *domestic* — like someone handing back an object the player had forgotten, not like a sacred restoration. The off-hand is already cupped in a receptive position before the grip arrives.

Per §0.1 and §0.4, this is the ritual's whimsy at the animation layer. The cosmological ritual is returning the memory of the trash the player started with, with the care of returning someone's keys. The gentleness is the absurd register held alongside the moment's gravity. The game's willingness to render this specific animation — not heraldic, not sacred, but *domestic* — is the tonal rule operating at the pose layer.

**The grip itself.** Rusted, exactly as it read in the player's hand at first equip. Bronze for early beta starters, iron for launch, copper for later-cohort characters. Material is from `first_weapon_memory.material`. If the player repaired the first weapon before losing it, the grip shows notches — one per repair, physically modeled on the grip's surface. If they never repaired it, the grip is pristine, undecorated, and unmistakably trash-that-was-never-cared-for.

**Chest-cradle pose.** Once the grip arrives, the player's off-hand cradles it against the chest. The off-hand is *protecting* a small personal object close to the body — not gripping it, not displaying it, not wielding it. The dominant hand continues to hold the current weapon in its normal ready stance. This pose is biomechanically unambiguous: carrying something close, while holding a tool ready. The pose resolves any "juggling" reading at the design layer — the player is not holding two objects; they are holding a tool and carrying a memory.

After the grip arrives and the chest-cradle pose settles, the Rise continues — the sixth light rises, then seventh, then eighth. The ring holds. Then Darkness rises on one side, Light on the other, per §57.3 canonical. The ten elements hold at rest. The Ferryman is no longer visible in the player's view; the camera has tightened to the ring.

#### §57.6 The Hold

The ritual's central act. **Twenty seconds. Uniform resistance. Universal across all players** regardless of register, skill, Guardian Zone history, or any other metric.

**The chest-cradle hold.** The off-hand holds the grip against the player's chest. The dominant hand maintains weapon-ready pose. Both hands must sustain steady input pressure against a physical resistance that tries to pull both apart. The resistance is constant across all twenty seconds — no phases of escalation, no variable tuning. The cost is egalitarian.

**Input specification.**
- **Mobile:** both thumbs hold steady pressure on their joystick zones
- **Desktop:** movement keys and primary action key must be maintained continuously

Any release of either input immediately ends the hold. No forgiveness window.

**Why universal and flat.** A variable-difficulty hold would encode skill-prestige at the ritual's most private moment. A graduated-difficulty hold would bias toward dramatic escalation at the climax. Both are wrong for a commitment register whose meaning is *everyone pays the same cost for crossing*. The twenty seconds are the same twenty seconds for every Crossed player on the server, across its entire history. This is load-bearing for §0.4's unification thesis: the ritual must hold opposites equally, and equality of cost is its mechanical guarantee.

**The voices.** During the hold, one primary voice line plays, drawn from the player's Journey Log at ritual time using register-specific templates per §0.1 tonal rule. The line is specific to the player's play history — referencing a specific NPC, specific choice, or specific moment. Delivery is quiet, central to the sound mix but not loud. The line arrives approximately 8-12 seconds into the hold.

A second optional line may play approximately 16-18 seconds in if the player is still holding — a brief final argument near completion. This second line is not guaranteed; it arrives in roughly 40% of rituals based on Journey Log richness (rich Journey Logs are more likely to produce a compelling second line; sparse Journey Logs default to single-line delivery).

Voice authoring scope: approximately 20-30 template lines across four registers, filled at ritual time with player-specific data. Templates live in `ritual-voice-templates.json`. Each template must be authored to §0.1's tonal rule — grave and wry variants where appropriate, register-specific voice throughout.

The rest of the hold is silent except for the chamber's ambient (a low sustained tone, barely audible) and the player's own breathing in the mix. The silence is load-bearing. The ritual's weight is carried by *not filling the space* — the voices speak once, maybe twice, and the remaining seventeen-plus seconds are the player alone with their hands, their grip, their weapon, and the argument that was made.

#### §57.7 The Two Outcomes

##### §57.7a Completion

The player sustains both inputs for the full twenty seconds.

1. **White flash.** The chamber renders white. One second of sustained white. Visual only — no screen shake, no cue beyond the ambient continuing underneath.

2. **Fragment construction (4 seconds total, locked).** Both objects — grip and current weapon — dissolve into particles at the player's hands. The particles drift downward to the chamber floor in front of the player. Over three seconds, the particles reassemble: the grip at floor level, embedded in the chamber stone; crystalline mineral-growth in the player's pole color begins at the grip's base and grows upward through the corroded rust, taking the silhouette of the player's lifetime-dominant weapon type. At the three-second mark, the Fragment is complete. The Fragment holds for one full second, the player viewing it from eye-level.

   **The continuity is visible.** The grip and weapon do not disappear and reappear as something new. They dissolve *together* into the same particle cloud, which reassembles as a single object: the rusted base with the crystalline form growing through it. The Fragment is not *adjacent* to the grip — the crystal visibly emerges *through* the corroded metal, pitting the surface, with mineral creep visible across the first two centimeters above the base. The player watches their origin and their present become permanent as one object. Per §0.4: both poles dissolve together because they were always two poles of a single act of holding, and the Fragment is that act made permanent.

3. **Direct cut to the Naming.** The chamber fades to pure black. Full sequence: 1s white + 4s Fragment construction + 1s black = 6 seconds total from hold-release to Naming.

4. **The Naming (§57.4a canonical, v14.0.5 EXPANDED to five sentences).** One of five canonical pole-specific sentences appears in the black, selected by the player's `endingAlignment` per §33.4:

   | Dominant Register | Pole | Canonical Sentence |
   |-------------------|------|---------------------|
   | Responsible (Steadfast / Hero) | Light | *She held the dark until it remembered it was part of something.* |
   | Ruthless (Sovereign) | Dark | *He stared into the light until it blinked.* |
   | Cool (Unmoved / Arbiter) | Eclipse (reserved) | *They held both hands until the hands remembered they were the same body.* |
   | Mischievous (Trickster) | Eclipse (warm) | *They laughed until the dark forgot why it was serious.* |
   | Balanced (Weaver) — **v14.0.5 NEW** | Convergence | *They held all four until the four remembered they had never been separate.* |

   The five sentences share a grammatical spine — a pronoun, a verb of holding or perceiving, an object, a *until* clause naming the resulting recognition — so the Weaver's Naming lands in the same register as the prior four. Held for six seconds with the perfect-fifth sustained-note audio per §57.4a preserved. Fades to black over two seconds. One second of additional pure black. The Convergence pole's audio treatment is unchanged from the other four — the same sustained perfect fifth, no new audio asset required.

5. **Beach.** Per §57.3b canonical. The player wakes on the beach — the new life begins. Current weapon is a fresh spawn (the new life starts with nothing); grip is absent from inventory (it became the Fragment).

##### §57.7b Walkout

The player releases either input before twenty seconds elapse, or input is lost.

1. **The resistance releases.** Both hands are free. The ten elements in the ring slowly fade.

2. **The side door.** A door becomes visible in the chamber wall to the player's left. The player walks out.

3. **Beach.** The player wakes on the beach. Current weapon is still in their dominant hand (the new life has not yet started — the ritual was not completed, so the reset does not fire). The grip is now cradled in the off-hand and persists in inventory as a grip item.

4. **Resume semantics (v14.0.3).** The grip in inventory is the resume token. Activating it from inventory re-opens the chamber portal and resumes the ritual at §57.4 (the Rise) on next chamber entry. The grip is consumed only on ritual completion (becoming the Fragment base); it persists indefinitely across any number of walkouts.

   **Why the grip is the resume token.** The Unfinished Fragment (v14.0.1) was a separate inventory item with metadata tracking walkout count and phase-at-failure. v14.0.3 collapses this into the grip itself — the grip *is* the unfinished ritual, the grip *is* the walkout record, the grip *is* the resume mechanism. One object does the work of three. Semantically: the grip in inventory means *you held your origin; you have not yet made the holding permanent; you may return to do so whenever you choose*. Per §0.4, the walkout is not a failure — it is the unified state in portable form, waiting for the player to decide whether to make it permanent.

5. **Mayor Bro canonical line (preserved, v14.0.3 tonal anchor).** On next town visit after walkout: *"Huh. You're still here."* Per §33.6 and §0.1, this is the ritual's whimsy anchor at its aftermath. Mayor Bro acknowledges the player's return in the same register he'd use for a return from any dungeon run. The cosmological weight of walking out of the ritual holding the memory of your own origin is answered with dry disinterest. This is the game holding grave and absurd simultaneously — the player just refused to let their identity crystallize permanently, and Mayor Bro greets them like any other Tuesday.

**The v14.0.1 Overwhelmed Fragment is deprecated.** Input loss during the hold — whether through connection drop, accidental release, device failure, or intentional release — resolves to walkout. The distinction between "chose to release" and "couldn't sustain" collapses. One grip item represents both. The player's inventory record is honest: you hold your origin; you have not yet chosen to let it transform. See §57.HIST.8 for the preserved historical record of the Overwhelmed concept and the migration path for pre-v14.0.3 characters.

#### §57.8 The Sky

At the instant of the white flash (§57.7a completion step 1), every outdoor player server-wide experiences a brief sky event.

**Visual.** A three-second rim-light appears on the horizon in the completing player's pole color, faintly tinted by their starter-weapon material (bronze warmer, iron cooler, copper neutral). The rim-light is subtle — not a flare, not an announcement. A player looking up notices it; a player focused on combat may not. No nameplate, no popup, no text.

**Audio.** A soft perfect-fifth plays in the listening player's current zone key, harmonized with their local ambient. The audio cue is keyed to the *listener's* zone, not the completer's zone — a Frost-zone listener hears the fifth in a cooler register than a Flame-zone listener. Volume: low, approximately 20% of standard combat audio. Duration: two seconds, fading over the final half-second.

**Scope rules.**
- Players indoors (dungeons, buildings, any interior) do not see the flash and do not hear the fifth
- Players outdoors but looking away from the horizon see peripheral color shift only
- The flash does not interrupt any other visual system — combat effects, weather, day/night cycle all continue unchanged
- The flash fires unconditionally at ritual completion regardless of whether any players are online or outdoors

**Why brevity is whimsy (v14.0.3 explicit naming).** The sky flash is three seconds. Most completions happen when few players are online and looking up. The world registers one more Bro crossing over with a color shift most observers miss. Per §0.1 and §0.4, this is the ritual's whimsy at the social layer — the world's refusal to announce itself is the absurd register holding the grave register in balance. A Bro just unified their identity after a hundred hours of play. The world's response: a brief color shift on the horizon, mostly unseen. That's funny. That's also true. The world does not stop to honor each unification because the world has its own business. The flash exists for anyone who happens to be looking; for everyone else, it didn't happen, except it did.

**The aggregate reading across server years.** The sky's color distribution over time becomes a legible record of which registers dominated each server's culture. A server whose sky trends Light has a culture of Bros who unified around responsibility. A server whose sky trends Eclipse-warm has a culture of Bros who unified around play. Year 5 players standing outside can feel the server's accumulated temperament in the sky's aggregate palette — warmer than Day 1 if the server leaned Light, cooler if Dark, shifting and unpredictable if Eclipse-dominant. This is server memory at the atmospheric layer, produced for free by the sky flash's unconditional firing across the server's lifetime.

#### §57.9 The Hall of Fragments

Preserved from v14.0.1 with architectural properties consolidated in v14.0.3. The Hall is a memorial zone accessible to all players (pre-ritual and post-ritual alike) per §57.8 historical. The Keeper's role at the Hall entrance is preserved from §57.13 (v14.0.2).

**Every Fragment follows the same visual structure:** a rusted grip at the base, embedded in the Hall floor, with a crystalline form growing upward through the corroded metal in the Fragment-owner's pole color, taking the silhouette of that player's lifetime-dominant weapon type.

**Two-axis legibility:**

- **From five meters:** Unity. Every Fragment reads as the same silhouette — rust at the bottom, pole color at the top. The Hall is a forest of crystalline forms sharing one design language. New players walking the Hall understand immediately that *every completer started the same way.* This is the thesis at Hall scale: we all arrived with nothing; we all held our origin alongside what we became; we all crystallized the holding into this form.

- **From one meter:** History and identity. The grip material reveals cohort (bronze = early beta, iron = launch, copper = later). Notch count on the grip reveals repair history. The crystalline upper form reveals build — greatsword-broad, staff-narrow, bow-arced. The pole color reveals register. The inscription (optional, authored by the player at completion per §57.5 canonical) reveals voice. Five distinct readings at close range, each adding specificity without resolving the unity read from five meters.

**The untouched-grip whimsy (v14.0.3 explicit naming).** Some Fragments — a minority — have pristine grips with no notches. These are the Fragments of players who never repaired their first weapon before losing it: players who salvaged their starter for materials in the first hour, or had it destroyed in early hardening failure, or simply never thought about it. The pristine grip appears in the Hall as a quiet architectural joke. The game, the Hall, and the grip itself refuse to comment. The grip is simply pristine, smooth, untouched, sitting at the base of a crystalline form that represents a hundred-plus hours of identity accumulation and a twenty-second ritual of holding origin and present simultaneously. The Bro who produced this Fragment unified their identity in the most serious ritual the game performs, and the origin they held was trash they never bothered to take care of.

Per §0.1 and §0.4, this is the tonal rule at architectural scale. The Hall's memorial gravity is held in balance by the pristine grip's quiet absurdity. The memorial is not interrupted — it is *completed* by admitting its absurd register. A Hall full of only-notched grips would be grave-only and would fail the thesis. A Hall full of only-pristine grips would be absurd-only and would fail equally. The real Hall, with both types coexisting, holds both registers permanently.

`[DESIGN INTENT: The pristine-grip detail is the Hall's primary whimsy vector and is load-bearing for the thesis at the architectural layer. Future design passes must preserve it. A well-intentioned "fix" that adds notches retroactively to pristine grips (to "honor all completers equally") would destroy the Hall's tonal balance and violate §0.4. The Bro who never repaired their first weapon earned their pristine grip through the same hundred hours and same twenty seconds as every other completer. The Hall honors them by showing their actual history — including the part where they didn't maintain their origin weapon. That honesty is the memorial's integrity.]`

**Grip material as server timestamp — permanent constraint.** Grip material is the Hall's legible record of when each completer started playing. No future character-creation promotion, seasonal event, or cosmetic variation may alter starter weapon material. This constraint is load-bearing for the Hall's demographic readability across server years and is **non-negotiable**. Future designers considering starter-weapon variation should recognize that any such variation corrupts the Hall's server-history record. The trade is named and the design commits: the Hall's timestamp is the game's permanent cost of preserving starter-material consistency.

#### §57.10 Living Fragment Properties

Preserved from v14.0.1:

- Fragments rotate at full brightness when the owner is online and active
- Fragments dim to 85% brightness when the owner is offline
- Fragments pulse softly once when the owner dies in combat (non-ritual death)
- Visitors to a Fragment are logged; the owner receives a quiet notification on next login ("Three people visited your Fragment while you were away")

**No mechanical effects.** Inspecting a Fragment, visiting the Hall, or proximity to another player's Fragment produces no buffs, previews, resources, or any gameplay advantage. INV-34 Fragment Integrity is preserved absolutely — Fragments are memorial only.

#### §57.11 The Crossed Property

Preserved from v14.0.1. Completing the ritual permanently sets the `crossed` property on the player's account. Effects:

- Ambient visual/audio shift within CROSSED_AMBIENT_RADIUS (25u) around the Crossed player
- NPC recognition from the Ferryman ("Oh. You're back.") and the Mayor's half-remember around level 30 of the new life
- Occasional voice echoes near the Crossed player — fragments of the arguments the ritual made during their specific recombination, audible at low volume to nearby players

Non-stacking, purely atmospheric, no mechanical effect. These effects reflect the ritual's permanent world-alteration at the ambient layer — the Crossed Bro carries a trace of having-held-both into their new life's zones.

#### §57.12 Data Model (v14.0.3, v14.0.5 EXPANDED — Convergence pole added)

```
rpg._firstWeaponMemory = {
    material: string,         // material of first equipped weapon (bronze | iron | copper | ...)
    weapon_type: WeaponType,  // weapon type of first equipped weapon
    acquisition_zone: ZoneId, // zone where first weapon was acquired
    equipped_at: UInt64,      // timestamp of first equip
    repair_count: UInt8,      // count of repairs before weapon was lost (for grip notches)
};
// Written once at first weapon equip. Immutable. Never modified after first write.
// repair_count is updated each time the specific first-weapon instance is repaired,
// and frozen at the count when the weapon is lost (salvage, death-scatter, trade, destruction).

rpg._weaponTypeHours = {
    greatsword: Float,
    sword: Float,
    bow: Float,
    staff: Float,
    // ... per weapon type
};
// Lifetime accumulator. Updated on combat-time ticks while a weapon is equipped.
// Used at ritual completion to determine Fragment upper silhouette.
// Window is lifetime (v14.0.3 resolution of prior 80-100 window debate).

rpg._ritualState = {
    walkouts_so_far: UInt8,
    grip_in_inventory: Boolean,  // true after first walkout, false after completion or before first walkout
    crossed: Boolean,            // true after completion
    completed_at: UInt64 | null, // timestamp of completion, if crossed
    pole: Pole | null,           // Light | Dark | Eclipse-warm | Eclipse-reserved | Convergence (v14.0.5), set at completion
};

// Pole enum (v14.0.5) — five values corresponding to the five endings per §33.4 and §53.4:
//   Light              → Hero ending (Responsible dominant)
//   Dark               → Sovereign ending (Ruthless dominant)
//   Eclipse-reserved   → Arbiter ending (Cool dominant)
//   Eclipse-warm       → Trickster ending (Mischievous dominant)
//   Convergence        → Weaver ending (no register strictly dominant; v14.0.5 NEW)
```

**Fragment data** per §57.5 canonical, with v14.0.3 additions and v14.0.5 pole expansion:

```
Fragment = {
    // ... existing fields from §57.5 canonical ...
    grip_material: string,       // from first_weapon_memory.material
    grip_notches: UInt8,         // from first_weapon_memory.repair_count
    upper_silhouette: WeaponType, // from lifetime weapon-type-hours dominant
    pole: Pole,                  // one of the five pole values above (v14.0.5: Convergence is now a valid value)
    // ... existing fields continue ...
};
```

**v14.0.5 additive rendering note.** The Convergence pole color is not a fifth hue on a four-color wheel — it is the five-frame sequential rendering described for the Complete Eclipse at §33.5 (Anchored → Playful → Still → Severe, 1.25s per frame across a 5s Fragment-rotation cycle). Weaver Fragments in the Hall rotate through the four pure pole colors sequentially at the same cadence as the Complete Eclipse variant, producing a visibly distinct Fragment that reads as "holding all four" at both near and far viewing distances per §57.9's two-axis legibility. No new pole color asset is required; the existing four pole-color assets are composed into the sequential cycle at render time.

Architecture spec for data additions: see architecture.md §9.5.X (deferred to follow-up spec pass). v14.0.5 architecture impact: `Pole` enum gains one value (`Convergence`); `FragmentRenderer` gains a branching case for `pole === 'convergence'` that drives the sequential-cycle animation. Both changes are additive; no migration required because no pre-v14.0.5 Fragment has `pole === 'convergence'` (the enum value did not exist, so the case cannot arise in historical data).

#### §57.13 Execution Quality Gates

The design is complete at the specification layer. A production team shipping this design reaches a 10-rated ending through quality investment in four specific bounded items. The four gates are independently assessable. The ending's final quality rating depends on how many of them land at high quality.

##### Gate 1: Chest-Cradle Hold Animation and Haptics

The pose is biomechanically specified (grip against chest, weapon in ready hand, off-hand protecting grip). The remaining work is animation quality and haptic feel. The pose must read as *carrying a personal object close to the body while holding a tool ready* rather than any alternative interpretation.

**Prototype deliverable:** 20-second chest-cradle hold animation on both mobile and desktop, with differentiated haptic feedback (lighter pulse in off-hand corresponding to the cradled grip, steady resistance in dominant hand corresponding to weapon ready).

**Playtest target:** testers describe the moment as *"holding something close to me"* or equivalent unprompted. The two-object reading must not occur.

**Fallback if animation fails playtest:** grip-only hold with current weapon absent from the chamber. This is the simplified version without the fifth-element addition. The ending is still strong but loses the present-self-in-hand thesis. Not preferred.

##### Gate 2: Voice Line Content and Journey Log Integration

Authoring scope: 20-30 template lines across four registers, filled at ritual time with player-specific Journey Log data (specific NPC names, specific choices, specific moments). Template lines must be authored to §0.1 tonal rule and must produce specific-feeling lines when filled.

**Deliverable:** authored template set plus Journey Log integration logic.

**Playtest target:** testers describe the voice as *"the game knows me"* rather than *"generic ritual text"*.

**Fallback if voices land generically:** reduce to zero voice lines during the hold. The twenty seconds become silent except for the low ambient. This is a quieter ending but doesn't damage the ritual's register — silence is honest. The voices are an enhancement, not a load-bearing requirement.

##### Gate 3: Grip-to-Fragment Particle Construction VFX

The four-second Fragment construction sequence is fully specified: particles dissolve from both objects at the player's hands, drift to the floor, reassemble as grip-at-floor, crystal growth upward through corroded metal over three seconds in pole color, taking lifetime-dominant weapon silhouette, held for one second, cut to black.

**Deliverable:** shot-listed VFX pass on the four-second sequence. The visual has to read as *transformation* — a single object (grip plus weapon) becoming a single new object (Fragment) — not as *particle effect followed by object appearance*.

**Playtest target:** testers describe the moment as *"my things became the crystal"* or equivalent. The continuity between grip-as-source and Fragment-as-result must be visually legible.

**Fallback if VFX lands as generic:** extend the sequence to 6 seconds and add a brief narrative beat ("The chamber remembers what you carried.") in the voice system. This weakens the pure-visual discipline but ensures the thematic beat lands.

##### Gate 4: Naming Sentence Timing Within the Black

The Naming's four canonical sentences are preserved from v13 (per §57.4a preserved in §57.7a). The remaining work is the delivery's exact timing within the post-Fragment black. Current spec: 1s fade-in, 6s hold, 2s fade-out, 1s additional black. Total: 10 seconds of Naming.

**Deliverable:** exact timing implementation plus the perfect-fifth sustained-note audio layered per §57.4a canonical.

**Playtest target:** testers do not describe the Naming as *"too long"* or *"too short"* — the sentence should feel *held*, not *rushed* or *dwelt upon*. The six-second hold is the primary variable; 5.5 to 6.5 seconds is likely acceptable, outside that range feels wrong.

**Fallback:** tune the hold duration within the 5-7 second range based on playtest response. No design rework required.

#### §57.14 Design Intent

The v14.0.3 ending is the honest settlement of concerns developed across extended design iteration.

First, the ritual needed a commitment register that felt like physical work without encoding skill-prestige. The uniform twenty-second chest-cradle hold delivers this. Every Crossed player pays the same twenty seconds. Specialists and generalists, elite players and casual players, pay the same cost at the same time. Per §0.4, the equality of cost is load-bearing — the ritual's thesis requires that the act of unification be equally accessible to every player regardless of skill.

Second, the four-register alignment thesis required each register's ending to feel specific without mechanically advantaging any register. The ending delivers this through the Ferryman's register-matched threshold line, the Naming's pole-specific sentence, the Fragment's pole color, and the sky flash's pole tint. Mechanical outcomes are identical across registers; expressive outcomes are distinct. §0.3 non-hierarchical four-register system preserved.

Third, the tonal register needed to hold grave and absurd simultaneously per §0.1 and §0.4. Earlier iterations (v14.0, v14.0.1, v14.0.2) had correctly gravitated toward ceremonial weight but under-served the absurd register. v14.0.3 corrects this by naming three whimsy anchors explicitly — the Ferryman's threshold line (ritual opening), Mayor Bro's return acknowledgment (post-walkout), and the Hall's pristine-grip detail (architectural) — with one animation-layer whimsy (the grip's gentle descent). The ritual's interior beats retain full gravity; the whimsy anchors frame the interior without interrupting it. The ritual now holds both registers at every layer it occupies.

Fourth, the social layer needed to exist without attaching mechanics to Fragments and without creating authoring-heavy systems. The sky flash delivers this at zero marginal cost and zero authoring cost. Earlier proposals (traces, Fragment-as-buff-dispenser, co-authored walkouts, Hall settling shimmers, ambient effect radii) were rejected because they either violated INV-34, created consolation-mechanics that undermined walkout integrity, or introduced authoring/tuning surfaces that the ending's scale didn't warrant. The sky generates social narrative ("did you see the flash at dusk") server-wide. Its brevity is whimsy; its unconditional firing is gravity; both together embody the thesis at the social layer.

Fifth, and most structurally: the ending is the unification thesis (§0.4) made physical. The grip is origin. The current weapon is present. The twenty-second chest-cradle hold is the act of holding both permanently. The Fragment is what the holding looks like when it crystallizes. The sky is the world's acknowledgment. The Hall is the accumulated record. Every element of the ritual embodies the thesis directly; nothing is scaffolding; no element can be removed without breaking the thesis's embodiment.

The ritual is the game's single most important piece of cosmological design, and v14.0.3 is the version where its structure fully aligns with its thesis. Earlier versions had the mechanics roughly right but had not yet stated what the mechanics were doing. The thesis statement (§0.4) and this revision (§57) together complete the work: the ritual is now self-describing at the specification layer. A designer reading §57 can derive why every beat matters from §0.4 alone. A player experiencing §57 can feel the thesis without reading it.

The grip is the thesis in one image. *Identity emerges from what you did with the trash, not from whether the trash changed.* Five elements embody this. Three whimsy anchors balance it. Four execution gates determine whether it lands at 10. The design is done.

#### §57.15 Tuning Constants

| Variable | Default | Min | Max | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| RITUAL_GEM_THRESHOLD | 50 | 20 | 100 | Convergence gems required for access |
| HOLD_DURATION_SECONDS | 20 | 15 | 30 | Universal hold duration |
| FRAGMENT_CONSTRUCTION_DURATION | 4.0 | 3.0 | 5.0 | Total construction sequence seconds |
| CRYSTAL_GROWTH_DURATION | 3.0 | 2.5 | 3.5 | Upward growth portion of construction |
| CRYSTAL_HOLD_DURATION | 1.0 | 0.5 | 1.5 | Held view before cut to black |
| NAMING_HOLD_SECONDS | 6.0 | 5.0 | 7.0 | Naming sentence held duration |
| SKY_FLASH_DURATION | 3.0 | 2.0 | 4.0 | Server-wide rim-light duration |
| SKY_AUDIO_VOLUME | 0.2 | 0.1 | 0.3 | Perfect-fifth volume relative to ambient |
| LIGHT_INTENSITY_FLOOR | 0.30 | 0.20 | 0.40 | Minimum brightness for unused elements |
| LIGHT_INTENSITY_CEILING | 1.00 | 0.90 | 1.00 | Maximum brightness for most-used element |
| VOICE_PRIMARY_OFFSET_SECONDS | 10.0 | 8.0 | 12.0 | When primary voice line arrives in hold |
| VOICE_SECONDARY_OFFSET_SECONDS | 17.0 | 16.0 | 18.0 | When optional secondary line arrives |
| VOICE_SECONDARY_PROBABILITY | 0.40 | 0.20 | 0.60 | Chance of secondary line firing |
| GRIP_DESCENT_DURATION | 0.5 | 0.3 | 0.8 | Gentle-descent animation duration |

#### §57.16 Chamber Authorship Lore (preserved from v14.0.2 §57.13)

The chamber's authorship lore introduced in v14.0.2 — past Guardians as the chamber's collective builders, the Keeper of the Hall as a former Guardian with per-server pole variation, the new-life chamber-authoring mechanic — is preserved unchanged in v14.0.3. See v14.0.2 canonical §57.13 content reproduced below:

**The chamber was begun by the first Guardians of this server and is collectively maintained by each subsequent Crossed player who chooses to add to it.** The chamber's "hand of a maker" texture (specified in creative-reference.md §8.15) is literal: every imperfection, asymmetry, and gold tracery is the work of a specific past Crossed Bro. This matches the doc's server-memory thread (Growing Crystal §56.5a, Godly lineage §17.4.1, player-generated Echo Stones, Fragment persistence) — the chamber is the oldest and deepest memorial surface.

**Access lore — earned, not chosen.** The gate conditions (final register title earned + RITUAL_GEM_THRESHOLD Convergence gems collected) are both about *having become* rather than being selected. No prophecy, no oracle, no chosen-one framing anywhere in the cosmology. The player is one Bro among many who have done enough work to be prepared. Year 5 servers have hundreds of Crossed Bros; the player is not the first, not the last, not distinguished. Per §0.4, §32 anti-pay-to-win, §0.3 four-register equality, §8.12a player-emergent mythology thread.

**New-life chamber-authoring mechanic.** A Crossed player in their new life may return to the chamber via the Convergence portal and make up to 3 authored additions (placed stone, gold tracery strand, inlay piece). Materials are basic; the action is meaningful because it is chosen, not because it is costly. Additions are append-only, optional, opt-in, no mechanical reward, quietly attributed via a small engraved marker (~1u size) with the authoring Bro's name and date. Most players will never do this; some will; the chamber fills over the server's lifetime with the hands of those who chose to return.

**The Keeper of the Hall is a former Guardian with per-server pole variation.** Each server's Keeper has a randomly-assigned pole at server instantiation: Dark, Light, Eclipse-warm, or Eclipse-reserved. The Keeper's visual palette, voice register per §0.1, stance, and ambient signature reflect the pole. Same Hall feels different across servers because of which register tends it. A Crossed visitor returning to the Hall receives, on their second or later post-ritual visit, one additional Keeper line: *"I walked the line between once too. Different pole. Same line."* — delivered once per visitor, ever, acknowledging the Keeper's own crossing without narrowing their pole.

See v14.0.2 changelog entry for the full lore grounding and creative-reference.md §8.15b for Keeper visual/audio direction.

---

#### §57 Historical Record — Preserved per Editing Protocol Step 6

The v12.44 Stability Fragments content and v13 Primordial / Acknowledgment Dialogue / Post-completion Witnessing content are superseded by v14.0 but preserved below as historical record per Editing Protocol step 6. These sections are NOT canonical; they document the design arc that reached the v14.0 ritual framing.

##### §57.HIST.1 — v13 Primordial Overview (SUPERSEDED)

v13 replaced the canonical "external primordial force" framing with a personal one: the Primordial you fight is a version of yourself — the alignment register you least embodied during your journey. Solo-only encounter against the player's own unchosen self. Per §0.3 four-register system, the Primordial took the form of the unchosen-register self per the opposition pairing:

| Dominant Register | Primordial Fought |
|-------------------|-------------------|
| Responsible | Inner Ruthless self |
| Ruthless | Inner Responsible self |
| Mischievous | Inner Unmoved self |
| Cool | Inner Mischievous self |

The Primordial addressed the player's specific history during combat via register-specific dialogue drawn from Journey Log.

v14.0 supersedes this framing on the basis that performing an act in the rejected register produces *fracture*, not unity — the opposition-pairing framing inherits boss-fight structure from the canonical cosmic-duality era without cohering with the four-register thesis's anti-opposition principle. v14.0 resolves by having each register perform its own register's gesture at cosmological scale, with the other three registers revealed inside it (§57.3).

##### §57.HIST.2 — v13 Acknowledgment Dialogue (SUPERSEDED)

v13 replaced v12.44 Stability Fragments with acknowledgment-through-dialogue — the Primordial referenced the player's prior attempts in its opening line, without granting mechanical prefill. Persistence recognized through voice, not through UI meter.

v14.0 supersedes this on the basis that the ritual has no retention cliff to solve — there is no fight to wipe out of. Walkout paths via Unfinished Fragment (§57.2c) replace both mechanics.

##### §57.HIST.3 — v13 The Two Primordials (SUPERSEDED)

Canonical (pre-v13 and preserved through v13) Primordial of Darkness (Fear Incarnate) and Primordial of Light (Truth Incarnate) — abstract emotional forces, 320×320 sprite, non-humanoid. Creative direction in creative-reference.md §8.15.

v14.0 supersedes this with the ritual chamber framing (§57.2b) and the ten-element rise (§57.3). No Primordial entities exist in v14.0.

##### §57.HIST.4 — v13 Alignment-Specific Combat Encounters (SUPERSEDED)

v13 had stability-bar combat per register: Hero illuminates arena with Light attacks; Sovereign shadows arena with Dark attacks; Arbiter triggers Eclipse collisions at the center boundary; Trickster performs emote-mimicry moments. Three phases per encounter (0-30%, 30-60%, 60-100% stability) with escalating Primordial resistance.

v14.0 supersedes combat mechanics entirely. The ritual is a non-combat ceremonial act.

##### §57.HIST.5 — v13 Final Echo Stone (SUPERSEDED)

v13 Final Echo Stone was read once at encounter completion — one sentence per register (four canonical sentences preserved). Attempt-count variation added addendums for persistence.

v14.0 supersedes with the Soul Fragment (§57.5). The four canonical v13 sentences are preserved as options in the Soul Fragment auto-generation pool and are available for player selection.

##### §57.HIST.6 — v13 Post-Completion Witnessing (SUPERSEDED)

v13 added a witness gallery allowing completed players to observe other players' active Primordial encounters from a distance.

v14.0 supersedes by removing the witnessing gallery. The solo discipline is preserved via the ritual's design (no witnesses can enter the chamber); the social memorial function is moved to the Hall of Fragments (§57.8), which is asynchronous (completed Fragments are visited, not active rituals observed).

##### §57.HIST.7 — v12.44 Stability Fragments (SUPERSEDED)

v12.44 introduced Stability Fragments — encounter-local consumable earned exclusively through failed Primordial attempts, spent to prefill 10% of stability bar per fragment, capped at 5 fragments (50% prefill ceiling). Fragments were per-character, non-purchasable, non-tradeable, consumed on arena entry.

The full v12.44 specification is preserved in git history at tag `gdd-v12.44`; git show `gdd-v12.44:gdd.md` retrieves the canonical specification. The v14.0 doc does not reproduce the full specification inline because v13 already superseded v12.44 via acknowledgment dialogue, and v14.0 supersedes both.

`[HISTORICAL DESIGN INTENT PRESERVATION: The arc from canonical cosmic Primordials → v13 unchosen-self reframing → v14.0 recombination ritual represents three design generations of the ending. Each generation caught something the previous generation missed. The canonical version had clean external-antagonist mythology but felt generic at the climax. The v13 version made the encounter personal but imported the boss-fight shape from the canonical version without re-examining whether the shape fit the personal framing. The v14.0 version discards the boss-fight shape entirely in favor of ritual-plus-artifact, producing an ending that rhymes with the opening (wash up → wash up again), matches the game's core verb (collision → final recombination), honors the four-register thesis (each register performs its own gesture at cosmological scale), and integrates with continuous-play multiplayer (player continues; world changes permanently around them via Crossed property + Soul Fragment). The v14.0 ending is the longest design-effort single section in the entire GDD history, developed across extended collaboration, and represents the doc's most mature expression of its design thesis. The preserved historical record documents the learning path without which the v14.0 version could not have been reached — each prior generation was a step toward the current spec, and the preservation of their reasoning is part of the doc's integrity.]`

---

##### §57.HIST.8 — v14.0.1 Overwhelmed Fragment (SUPERSEDED in v14.0.3, TRIMMED in v14.0.5)

v14.0.1 specified three ritual outcomes: completion, walkout-through-refusal, and Overwhelmed (input-loss-during-sustained-hold). v14.0.3 collapsed this to two outcomes (completion + walkout); input loss during the hold resolves to walkout. The deprecation is final. See §0.4 for the thesis reason why consolation outcomes that soften walkout violate the design.

**Migration (load-bearing for pre-v14.0.3 save data).** Pre-v14.0.3 characters holding Overwhelmed Fragments convert to grip-retained walkout state on first post-v14.0.3 load. The Overwhelmed Fragment item is removed from their inventory; the grip appears in its place with the player's `first_weapon_memory` material and notch count. Any Overwhelmed Fragment metadata (phase-at-failure, specific input lost) is discarded — the grip carries no record of which prior walkout or input-loss produced it.

**v14.0.5 trim note.** The extended design-rationale prose that argued the deprecation's logic in detail has been moved out of the living GDD to a separate design-archive reference. The rationale was an attractive nuisance — the Overwhelmed concept is the specific kind of thesis-violating "consolation kindness" that reads well in isolation and is one changelog rotation from re-emergence every time a designer re-reads the historical record. Future designers evaluating whether to reintroduce Overwhelmed as a distinct outcome should refer to §0.4 directly rather than to this section. The deprecation is canonical; the archival reasoning is not part of the working design doc.

## PART XI — ACCESSIBILITY (§58)

### §58. Accessibility Considerations

#### §58.1 Shape-First Design Principle (v12.43 CANONICAL)

Shape-first design is **the baseline UI discipline for Bro Town**, not an opt-in accessibility mode. Every UI element that conveys information is designed such that its *shape* carries the primary signal, and color is a secondary *enhancement* that reinforces but never uniquely identifies the information. This is a design constraint on all UI authoring — existing and future — rather than a toggle in settings.

**The rule stated precisely.** If a player with complete color-perception loss (full monochromatic vision) cannot distinguish two UI elements that need to be distinguished, the UI design has failed. The test is not "would colorblind modes help?" — the test is "does the shape alone carry the information?" If yes, the color adds polish and strengthens the read for players with color vision; if no, the UI must be redesigned until yes.

**Element palette as the archetypal example (§10.1).** The ten elements each have a unique geometric shape glyph that appears on every surface where the element is displayed — weapon nameplates, status effect icons, ground marks, Codex entries, marketplace listings, collision matrix, resonance pulse indicators. A colorblind player reads element identity from the diamond/hexagon/circle/teardrop/star/square/triangle/leaf/trefoil/starburst vocabulary. Color reinforces the identification (Flame is red, Frost is blue, Venom is green) but the shape is the answer.

**High-risk color conflicts and their shape resolution.**

| Colors | Elements | Shape Disambiguation |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| Red / Green (deuteranopia) | Flame / Venom | Diamond vs Teardrop |
| Red / Green (deuteranopia) | Flame / Flora | Diamond vs Leaf |
| Green / Green-yellow | Venom / Flora | Teardrop vs Leaf |
| Blue / Purple | Frost / Storm | Hexagon vs Star |
| Brown / Grey | Stone / Wind | Square vs Triangle |

Every pair that presents a color-vision conflict is resolved through shape difference at the palette level. No pair of elements shares both a color conflict and a shape conflict.

**Quality borders (§4.6b) use border *accent treatment* rather than color alone.**

| Quality | Treatment |
|---------|-----------|
| Common | No border accent |
| Uncommon | Thin silver outer border |
| Rare | Thick gold outer border |
| Legendary | Thick prismatic outer border with animated shimmer |
| Godly | Halo + divine glow + distinctive sacred geometry framing |

A player with no color perception reads quality from border thickness + presence of animation + halo/geometry, independent of color. Color reinforces the tier (silver is cooler than gold; prismatic has rainbow hue cycling) but shape and treatment carry the primary signal.

**Element tier indicators (§4.6) use distinct saturation and hue separation wide enough to survive most colorblind palettes.** Tier 1-4 (Grey → Blue → Purple → Prismatic) are also shape-distinguishable at the tier-glyph level — each tier has a distinct geometric framing pattern around the element's shape glyph. A colorblind player reads tier from the framing shape, not from the hue.

**Status effect icons (§9.1) use distinctive silhouettes.** Each of the ten statuses has a unique silhouette shape that reads at icon-size distances (typically 24-32px on the HUD). Burn is a flame silhouette; Freeze is an ice-shard silhouette; Root is a vine silhouette; etc. A colorblind player identifies the status by the silhouette in the icon even if the color-coded border around the icon is unreadable.

**Damage-number resistance tier (v12.26 §58.1a) uses typography treatment, not color.** The size scaling and strikethrough-on-Immune rules established in v12.26 are shape-based by design — a player reads resistance tier from number size (large = super-effective) and strikethrough presence (immune), independent of the subtle color-tint reinforcement that accompanies each tier.

**Threat direction arrows (v12.42 §11.1) use triangular shape.** The arrow's directional meaning is carried by the pointed triangle shape; the muted-orange color reinforces the "threat attention" register but the direction itself is shape-based. Colorblind players read direction from the shape alone.

**Combo posture (v12.42 §5.9.8) is fully shape-based.** Per-weapon count-3 flourish poses are distinguished by the character's body configuration (planted stance vs raised blade vs drawn bow vs suspended gem), not by any color coding.

**What this principle means for future UI authoring.** Every new UI element in future ships must pass the shape-first test before approval. A new status type must have a distinctive silhouette. A new quality tier must have a distinctive border treatment. A new notification type must be distinguishable from existing types by shape, position, or typography — not by color alone. Designers authoring new elements should verify shape-distinguishability before specifying color; if the shape cannot be distinguished at a glance, the design must be revised before color is added.

**Not a toggle — a baseline discipline.** v12.43 removes the "Phase 2 target: high-contrast mode in settings" language from the previous §58.1 spec because the design principle makes a separate high-contrast mode redundant — the game is *already* shape-first for all players. A high-contrast colorblind-safe palette option (mentioned in §10.1) remains in settings for players who want enhanced color-differentiation support, but the baseline experience does not rely on color perception. Players who use the colorblind-safe palette get additional color-distinction reinforcement on top of the shape-first foundation, not instead of it.

**The difference between Bro Town's approach and conventional colorblind-mode design.** Most games treat colorblind accessibility as a mode the player enables to transform the UI. Bro Town's approach is that the UI is *always* colorblind-accessible through shape — the colorblind-safe palette option is an optional enhancement, not a necessary mode. A colorblind player can play Bro Town at any zoom level, any lighting condition, any screen quality without enabling any accessibility settings. This is the correct direction for accessibility: the game works for all players by default, and settings add polish rather than correction.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.43): Colorblind accessibility as a toggle in settings is a sign that the base UI failed at accessibility and needs a special mode to become usable. The shape-first design principle is the inverse: the base UI works for everyone, and any enhancement settings are additive rather than corrective. This is not just philosophically better; it produces better UI for all players. A UI that reads on shape alone is legible at lower resolutions, smaller zoom levels, in glare/sunlight conditions, through motion, through transparency effects, and across the full range of display hardware. Color as reinforcement is a polish layer — it adds readability when display conditions are good — but never the primary channel. The v12.43 change makes this discipline explicit in the canonical doc so that all future UI authoring is constrained by it. Every new element needs a shape-distinguishable signature before any color is assigned. Every new quality tier needs a treatment that reads without hue. Every new status needs a silhouette that's identifiable at icon size. This constraint doesn't limit design — it focuses design. The best UIs are the ones that work peripherally, under stress, across conditions. Shape-first design is the structural property that produces those UIs. The game's existing UI vocabulary (element shapes, quality borders, status silhouettes, damage typography, threat arrows, combo posture) already follows this principle; v12.43 makes it explicit so that future work stays within the same discipline.]`

#### §58.2 Audio

Core gameplay audio cues (resonance pulse, collision sounds, Grand Slam) all have visual equivalents: resonance has a pulsing border indicator and weapon glow intensification (§5.7.3), collisions have ground marks (§10.7), Grand Slam has screen shake and slowmo (Creative Reference §1.3). No gameplay-critical information is conveyed through audio alone. The v12.38 Haptic Feedback Layer (§15.2a) adds tactile counterparts for 12 canonical combat events, giving hearing-impaired mobile players a third sensory channel for timing-critical signals.

**Highest-risk system:** The resonance pulse tick (§5.7.3) is a key timing cue. Players who cannot hear the accelerating tick rely on the visual pulse indicator and (on mobile) the v12.38 haptic pulse. The visual indicator alone has been verified sufficient during Phase 1 playtesting with audio muted.

##### §58.2a Visual-Enhanced Mode (v12.43 CANONICAL)

**Visual-Enhanced Mode** is an opt-in toggle in Settings → Accessibility → Visual Enhancement that amplifies the visibility of all audio-critical combat cues. The mode is designed primarily for hearing-impaired players who want stronger visual feedback in place of audio, but is equally useful for players who prefer enhanced visual feedback (bright-environment play, muted-audio contexts, or general visual preference).

**Enhancements applied when mode is enabled.**

| Cue | Default Intensity | Enhanced Intensity | Scaling |
|-----|:------------------:|:-------------------:|:-------:|
| Resonance pulse indicator (§5.7.3) | Standard alpha/glow | 2× alpha, 1.5× glow radius | +50-100% visibility |
| Collision timing flash (§10.7) | Standard flash | 2× flash intensity + 1.5× duration | +50-100% prominence |
| Grand Slam screen shake (§1.3 CR) | Standard shake | 2× amplitude, 1.5× duration | +50-100% magnitude |
| Status effect icons (§9.1) | Standard 24px | 36px at same screen position | +50% size |
| Collision ground marks (§10.7) | Standard spec | 1.3× size, 1.5× border thickness | +30-50% visibility |
| Damage-number flash (§58.1a) | Standard size | 1.3× size, 1.5× duration | +30-50% size/time |

**All enhancements are visual only.** Mechanical timing windows are unchanged — a resonance window is still 25% of status duration, collision timing is still the same, Grand Slam still triggers at the same threshold. Only the *visibility* of these events is amplified. This preserves all game balance and competitive fairness while giving players who need stronger visual feedback a path to access the same mechanics.

**No gameplay impact.** The mode affects rendering and visual effect layers only. Server state, combat resolution, damage formulas, and any multiplayer fairness properties are identical between Standard and Visual-Enhanced modes. A Visual-Enhanced player fighting a Standard player in Arena has no mechanical advantage or disadvantage — both see the same events, but one sees them louder visually.

**Default: off.** The mode is opt-in because the default visual feedback levels are already calibrated for hearing players who have audio available. Players who need stronger visual feedback discover the setting and enable it. The conservative default prevents the mode from affecting players who don't need it.

**Player-configurable intensity.** The Settings toggle offers three levels: Off, On (default enhancements per table above), and Stronger (3× intensity, 2× size — for players who want maximum visual emphasis, e.g., playing without audio in bright outdoor conditions). The Stronger level is more aggressive than typical visual design would recommend but matches the accessibility-first principle that players who need the feature should have it at the intensity that serves them.

**Co-composes with the v12.38 Haptic Feedback Layer.** A hearing-impaired mobile player can enable both Visual-Enhanced Mode AND haptics to receive amplified visuals plus tactile confirmation of combat events. The two accessibility layers do not conflict — they operate in orthogonal sensory channels (visual vs tactile) and reinforce each other. The combined experience: bright visible combat events with synchronized tactile pulses gives hearing-impaired players roughly the same level of per-event feedback that hearing players receive through audio + visuals.

**Performance impact — minimal.** The enhancements are shader multipliers and size scaling applied to existing render passes. No new render passes, no new assets, no additional GPU cost beyond the ~10-15% increase in overdraw from larger effects. Low-end mobile devices running Visual-Enhanced Mode see no meaningful frame-rate impact from the mode itself (though the larger effects may amplify existing overdraw costs in dense combat — acceptable trade for the accessibility benefit).

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.43): Visual-Enhanced Mode is the mirror of the v12.38 Haptic Feedback Layer — where haptics give hearing-impaired mobile players tactile counterparts to audio cues, Visual-Enhanced Mode gives hearing-impaired desktop players (and mobile players who prefer visuals) amplified visual counterparts. The mode's discipline is strict amplification — every enhancement scales visibility while leaving mechanics untouched. This preserves competitive fairness in Arena and party play while ensuring the mode genuinely serves its accessibility function rather than being a cosmetic setting. The three-level toggle (Off/On/Stronger) lets players calibrate to their specific needs rather than accepting a single designer-determined intensity. Co-composition with haptics means hearing-impaired mobile players get both amplified visuals AND tactile confirmation, producing roughly equivalent per-event feedback to hearing players. The mode is defined as a specific setting rather than as a design principle because enhancement is genuinely optional — hearing players don't benefit from amplified visuals (they get the audio channel unamplified), and enabling Visual-Enhanced Mode for them would cause visual fatigue during extended play. The design distinction is load-bearing: §58.1 Shape-First is the baseline for everyone; §58.2a Visual-Enhanced is a per-player preference for those who need stronger visual feedback. Together with the shape-first baseline and the haptic layer, the game's accessibility vocabulary now covers color-blind, hearing-impaired, and motor-constrained players with three complementary mechanisms operating on orthogonal sensory channels.]`

#### §58.3 Motor → Playstyle Preferences (v13 REFRAMED — Accessibility as Preference)

**v13 REFRAME.** Canonical "Motor Accessibility" framing is renamed to **Playstyle Preferences**. The features are unchanged; the social register shifts. Canonical timing-assist modes (Standard / Assisted / Relaxed) become **Deliberate / Standard / Quick** — presented as play-feel preferences rather than motor-accessibility modes.

**Why the reframe.** "Accessibility" framing creates stigma — "assistance mode" implies the player needs help. "Preferences" framing removes stigma while preserving the same features. Everyone uses preferences; no one is singled out. Same functionality, better social register.

**Mapping of canonical modes to v13 preference names:**

| v13 Preference | Canonical Mode | Effect |
|----------------|----------------|--------|
| **Quick** | Standard (canonical) | Default — tight timing, high reward (same as canonical Standard) |
| **Standard** | Assisted (canonical) | Moderate timing windows (same as canonical Assisted) |
| **Deliberate** | Relaxed (canonical) | Wide timing windows (same as canonical Relaxed) |

(Note: the v13 "Standard" preference corresponds to the canonical Assisted mode, not canonical Standard. The default for new players is "Quick" which matches canonical Standard behavior. The naming shift gives all three modes neutral labels — "Quick" suggests speed preference, "Deliberate" suggests pacing preference, neither suggests assistance. New players default to Quick; players who find tight timing uncomfortable can switch to Standard or Deliberate without any social stigma.)

The resonance window (last 25% of status duration, §5.7) rewards precise timing but does not require it. Collision damage at 1.0× (outside window) is the baseline. Resonance bonus (1.10–1.30×) is additive reward for skilled timing. Players who cannot hit tight timing windows are not locked out of collision gameplay — they deal full base collision damage regardless of preference setting.

**Preference tuning (v13):**

| Preference | RESONANCE_WINDOW_RATIO | RESONANCE_BONUS_PEAK | Feel |
|------------|:---:|:---:|---|
| Quick | 0.25 | 0.30 | Tight window, high reward — default |
| Standard | 0.375 | 0.20 | Moderate window, moderate reward |
| Deliberate | 0.50 | 0.15 | Wide window, steady reward |

Playstyle preference does NOT affect combo chain (§5.9), which is count-based (not timing-based). Auto-attack count builds at the player's own pace.

##### §58.3a Reaction-Assist Dodge Timing (v12.43 CANONICAL, v13 REFRAMED as Dodge Preference)

**v13 REFRAME.** Canonical Reaction-Assist Dodge is renamed to **Dodge Preference**. Modes renamed: Standard / Assisted / Relaxed → **Quick / Standard / Deliberate** matching the playstyle preferences framing from §58.3.

All canonical mechanics preserved exactly — only the naming shifts. Dodge iframe offsets remain identical:

| v13 Dodge Preference | Canonical Mode | iFrame Start | Forgiveness Window |
|----------------------|----------------|:------------:|:-------------------:|
| **Quick** | Standard (canonical) | DODGE_IFRAMES_BASE start on dodge command | 0s |
| **Standard** | Assisted (canonical) | 0.1s earlier | +100ms |
| **Deliberate** | Relaxed (canonical) | 0.2s earlier | +200ms |

All other canonical properties (iframe duration unchanged, dodge distance/stamina/cooldown unchanged, interaction with Contextual Dodge, multiplayer fairness analysis, privacy) preserved exactly.

The tuning constants (DODGE_IFRAMES_ASSIST_OFFSET_ASSISTED → DODGE_PREF_OFFSET_STANDARD, DODGE_IFRAMES_ASSIST_OFFSET_RELAXED → DODGE_PREF_OFFSET_DELIBERATE) are renamed to match but functionally identical.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13 reframe): Canonical "Accessibility" framing serves players with motor impairments but implicitly signals "this is for people who need help." The v13 "Playstyle Preferences" framing is a renaming only — same features, same tuning — but removes the stigma from the feature's *name*. A player who simply prefers slower-paced combat isn't "using an accessibility mode"; they're on the "Deliberate" preference. A player with motor impairments isn't "using assistance"; they're on the "Standard" or "Deliberate" preference. Everyone is on some preference; no mode is marked as compensatory. This matches §0.2 progressive UI revelation's principle that features should present themselves as earned or chosen, not as compensations for deficits. The mechanical behavior is unchanged from canonical; only the naming and presentation shifts.]`

#### §58.4 One-Handed Control Preset (v12.43 CANONICAL, v13 REFRAMED as Layout Preference)

**v13 REFRAME.** Canonical One-Handed Control Preset is renamed to **Layout Preference: Single-Thumb** alongside existing multi-thumb layout. Presented as a layout choice (like keyboard-vs-controller), not as an accessibility preset.

All canonical mechanics preserved exactly. Only the naming and menu placement shifts — the option moves from "Accessibility" submenu to "Controls > Layout" submenu.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13 reframe): Same as §58.3 — preference framing removes stigma while preserving features. "One-Handed Control" was originally framed as accommodating a motor condition; v13 presents it as a layout preference any player might choose (bus commute, one-handed screen mirroring context, wrist injury, or simply preference). Same feature; better social register.]`

#### §58.5 Listening Mode (v13 NEW — Audio-First Play)

**Listening Mode** is an optional play mode that shifts the game toward spoken descriptions and spatial audio. Native support for audio-first play. Anyone can enable it — blind players, players with tired eyes, players who want to listen while doing something else, or players who simply prefer audio navigation.

**Features of Listening Mode:**

- **Spoken descriptions of game state.** Text-to-speech narration of:
  - Quest text, NPC dialogue, Echo Stones, flavor text
  - Inventory items on pickup
  - Status effects on application
  - Journey Log entries on creation
  - Capstone choice confirmations
- **Spatial audio for combat and navigation.** Enhanced 3D audio positioning lets players locate enemies, items, and NPCs by sound. Combat is navigable with eyes closed through:
  - Directional audio cues for incoming attacks (per §0.1 and §58.2 redundant signal pairing)
  - Distance-based audio attenuation scaled for audio-first navigation
  - Monster-type audio signatures (each archetype has distinctive audio fingerprint)
- **Enhanced collision audio.** Each of the 90 collision pairs has a distinctive audio signature (preserved from canonical but reinforced for Listening Mode — audio cues are the primary combat feedback channel).
- **Keyboard-reachable UI.** Full keyboard navigation of all menus, with screen-reader-compatible ARIA labels on every interactive element.
- **Audio-described UI transitions.** When UI panels open/close, a brief spoken description fires ("Inventory opened. Weapon slot highlighted.").

**Opt-in enablement.** Listening Mode is found in Settings > Preferences > Accessibility (kept in Accessibility menu specifically because it is a substantive mode shift, not a minor preference). Enablement is a toggle; when on, the described features activate.

**Combat legibility in Listening Mode.** Combat remains viable via audio cues alone. Directional attack telegraphs produce positional audio. Collision resolution produces distinctive audio per collision type. Health/mana/stamina states announce at thresholds ("Health low." "Mana full.").

**Content authoring scope.** Listening Mode requires substantial voice-over content:
- NPC dialogue voice-over (using text-to-speech with per-NPC voice profiles; manual recording optional)
- Echo Stone narration
- Combat state announcements
- Quest and inventory narration

Initial ship delivers text-to-speech narration for all existing text content. Later iterations may replace TTS with recorded voice-over for key narrative moments (Final Echo Stones, Ferryman dialogue, ending sequences) if community interest warrants the investment.

**Interaction with other preferences.** Listening Mode stacks with all playstyle/dodge preferences. A player on Deliberate playstyle with Listening Mode enabled has both wide timing windows AND audio-first navigation.

**Privacy.** Listening Mode is invisible to other players. No UI indicator shows whether a player is using Listening Mode. The feature is personal; other players interact with the Listening Mode player exactly as they would any other player.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v13): Audio-first play is essential accessibility for blind players and useful as aesthetic choice for anyone (tired eyes, ambient play, listening while cooking). Framing as "Listening Mode" rather than "Screen Reader" or "Blind Mode" makes it a positive feature any player might enable rather than an accommodation for a specific disability. The mode's usefulness extends beyond accessibility — a player on a bus with their phone in their pocket can listen to their character's quest narration via headphones; a player cooking dinner can keep Brotown running in the background as an audio companion. The audio-first design respects §58.2's redundant signal pairing (every visual critical signal has audio counterpart) — this mode emphasizes that audio layer and adds the narrative/UI narration to complete the audio-only play surface. Content authoring scope is substantial but bounded (TTS covers initial ship; recorded voice-over is an optional future investment). The privacy rule ensures Listening Mode players interact invisibly with other players — the feature shapes their experience of the game, not their social presentation to others.]`

##### §58.3a Reaction-Assist Dodge Timing (v12.43 CANONICAL)

**Reaction-Assist Dodge Timing** is a motor-accessibility toggle that widens the invincibility-frame (iframe) window on dodge by starting the iframe *earlier* in the dodge animation. Motor-impaired players and players who find tight dodge timing difficult can select Assisted or Relaxed mode to gain a 0.1-0.2 second forgiveness window without sacrificing any other dodge property.

**The three modes.**

| Setting | iFrame Start | Forgiveness Window | Effect |
|---------|:------------:|:-------------------:|---|
| **Standard** | DODGE_IFRAMES_BASE start on dodge command | 0s | Default — canonical dodge timing |
| **Assisted** | DODGE_IFRAMES_BASE starts 0.1s earlier | +100ms | Small forgiveness — still requires reasonable timing |
| **Relaxed** | DODGE_IFRAMES_BASE starts 0.2s earlier | +200ms | Substantial forgiveness — significantly easier to survive incoming attacks |

**iframe duration unchanged.** The dodge's total iframe duration (DODGE_IFRAMES_BASE 0.25s, scaled by AGI per §5.8) is identical across all three modes — only the *start time* of the iframe window moves earlier. An Assisted-mode dodge has iframes from -100ms (before dodge command) to +150ms after; a Standard-mode dodge has iframes from 0ms to +250ms after. Both modes grant the same total 0.25s of invincibility; Assisted starts it earlier relative to the dodge input.

**Dodge distance, stamina cost, and cooldown unchanged.** All dodge properties other than iframe-start-time are identical across modes. DODGE_DIST_BASE, DODGE_STAM, DODGE_CD, and all other §5.8 dodge tuning values apply identically to Standard, Assisted, and Relaxed. This means Assisted-mode dodges do not travel farther, do not cost less stamina, and do not have shorter cooldowns — they only grant earlier iframe coverage.

**Interaction with Contextual Dodge (§5.8).** Lunge and Retreat Shot inherit the player's chosen dodge mode. An Assisted-mode lunge starts its iframe coverage 0.1s earlier than a Standard-mode lunge; same total iframe duration. This means the reaction-assist benefit applies uniformly to standard dodge, lunge, and retreat-shot actions — the setting governs dodge-family iframe timing, not a specific action subtype.

**Multiplayer fairness analysis.** A Reaction-Assist (Assisted or Relaxed) player has an iframe timing advantage of ~100-200ms over a Standard-mode player. In the worst-case Arena matchup, this is the same order of magnitude as network latency variation (typical latency 40-120ms, with spike variation up to 200ms). The advantage is also bounded — the Assisted player still needs to dodge in the correct direction and within reasonable timing; they cannot ignore timing entirely. **Accepted as a fairness trade-off**: players who need Reaction-Assist for accessibility reasons gain a real advantage, but the game prioritizes access over competitive-balance symmetry. This matches the discipline established by resonance timing assist (§58.3) — accessibility accommodations may produce small mechanical variations, and that trade is acceptable.

**Visibility to other players.** A player's Reaction-Assist mode is NOT publicly visible. Other players cannot see whether a given player is using Standard, Assisted, or Relaxed mode from any UI surface. This matches the privacy register established by the existing resonance-timing-assist setting (§58.3) — accessibility accommodations are personal and do not need to be displayed. A player choosing a mode does so based on their own need, not based on social signaling.

**Tuning constants.**

| Variable | Default | Min | Max | Notes |
|----------|:-------:|:---:|:---:|-------|
| DODGE_IFRAMES_ASSIST_OFFSET_ASSISTED | 0.1s | 0.05s | 0.15s | iframe start time offset in Assisted mode |
| DODGE_IFRAMES_ASSIST_OFFSET_RELAXED | 0.2s | 0.15s | 0.30s | iframe start time offset in Relaxed mode |

**Default: Standard.** New players start in Standard mode. Players who find dodge timing difficult discover the setting through the Accessibility menu. No forced tutorial or prompt; the setting is available if needed but does not announce itself to players who don't need it.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.43): The existing §58.3 resonance timing-assist establishes the design pattern — a timing system that rewards precision but does not require it, with an accessibility mode that widens the precision window at some cost to peak reward. Reaction-Assist Dodge applies the same pattern to dodge iframes: players who cannot hit tight timing windows get a forgiveness window, and the only cost is the small competitive-fairness variation accepted in Arena play. The iframe-start-offset approach is deliberately chosen over iframe-duration-extension because extending duration would give Assisted-mode players longer total invincibility per dodge, which would be a strict mechanical advantage. Starting the iframe earlier preserves total duration; the mode shifts *when* the invincibility covers without adding *how much* invincibility is granted. This keeps the accessibility mode a *timing forgiveness* rather than a *survivability buff*. The three-tier structure (Standard/Assisted/Relaxed) gives motor-impaired players calibration flexibility — Assisted at 100ms is a small forgiveness that most players find sufficient; Relaxed at 200ms is substantial forgiveness for players with more significant motor constraints. The accepted fairness trade-off matches the resonance-timing-assist precedent — the game chooses access over symmetry when those values conflict, and the trade is bounded (an Assisted player still has to dodge; they just have more time to initiate the input). The v12.38 haptic feedback layer composes naturally — a hearing-impaired mobile player using Reaction-Assist sees the amplified visual attack-telegraph AND feels the haptic collision confirmation AND has 100-200ms extra iframe coverage. The three accessibility mechanisms operate in independent channels and reinforce each other when combined.]`

#### §58.4 One-Handed Control Preset (v12.43 CANONICAL)

**One-Handed Control Preset** is a mobile-only layout configuration that routes all critical actions through inputs reachable by a single thumb. Players using the preset can play one-handed (temporary constraint, permanent motor condition, screen-mirroring context, or preference) with full access to the game's combat loop, accepting some constraints on tactical depth for accessibility.

**Layout changes.**

- **Single-thumb input zone.** All primary controls (movement, swipe-direction, auto-attack, block) route through the left thumb. The right side of the screen is passive — no buttons, no input zones except a minimal "menu" affordance.
- **Auto-attack becomes automatic.** Per-input auto-attack tapping is replaced by continuous auto-attack on the nearest locked-on enemy. The game attacks automatically at the standard attack rate when lock-on is active and the target is in range.
- **Auto-lock on nearest enemy.** Target selection uses automatic nearest-enemy lock-on rather than manual tap-to-lock. The lock automatically transfers to the next-nearest enemy when the current target dies or leaves range.
