Dive points as small whirlpools on water surface with soft ambient glow; 1-3 per Tidal Caves depth. Session structure: interaction begins dive, resurface (voluntary or forced) ends it; no hard cap on session count subject to decompression pause.

(2) **§46.2 Air Management and Resurface.** Air meter at top-center (diving only), 90-second baseline capacity, linear depletion, color-shift visual treatment. Voluntary resurface over 2.5s with audio crossfade. Forced resurface on depletion over 3.0s, no HP damage, no drowning state, no resource penalty. 1.5-second decompression pause on surface prevents frantic dive-cycle optimization while providing natural moment for inventory check or departure decision.

(3) **§46.3 Underwater Echo Stones — The Primary Reward.** Underwater variants of §33 Echo Stones. Content focus: fragments about things forgotten, sunk, drowned, lost to water — Tidal Caves' "memory" identity deepens. 3-5 per underwater zone-instance depth. Authoring discipline: validation enforces `placement: underwater` entries cannot duplicate at `placement: surface` in same zone. Collection tracking via existing §27 Discovery Journal with distinct underwater sub-category. Standard §33 Echo Stone rules apply (≤120 chars, one sentence, no proper nouns, present/past tense).

(4) **§46.4 Incidental Treasure.** Small gold drops + occasional low-tier gems. DIVE_GOLD_PER_HOUR_MULTIPLIER: 0.5 (underwater underperforms surface farming by half). No rare-tier treasure underwater — high-tier loot remains combat-surface-only per §4 discipline. Four treasure-tuning constants: DIVE_TREASURE_ENCOUNTER_PER_DIVE (0.5), DIVE_TREASURE_GOLD_MEAN (15), DIVE_TREASURE_GEM_CHANCE (0.05), DIVE_GOLD_PER_HOUR_MULTIPLIER (0.5).

(5) **§46.5 Visual and Audio Identity.** Four parallax depth layers (surface / mid-water / deep / bottom) as rendering gradients, not discrete zones. Audio direction: low-pass filtered surface audio, single swelling ambient tone per Tidal Caves variant, register closer to Final Echo Stone (§57, §8.16) and Convergence-shrine (§8.52) sacred-object audio than standard exploration music. Non-interactable creature silhouettes (whale-sized shapes drifting, long swimmers) communicate underwater aliveness without combat. Light behavior: god-rays through particles, Echo Stone glows as primary deep-layer illumination.

(6) **§46.6 Tuning Constants.** Six new constants: AIR_CAPACITY_BASE (90s baseline session length — up from stub's 20s to support contemplative pacing), DIVE_FORCED_RESURFACE_DURATION (3.0s), DIVE_VOLUNTARY_RESURFACE_DURATION (2.5s), DIVE_DECOMPRESSION_PAUSE (1.5s), DIVE_UNDERWATER_ECHO_STONES_PER_DEPTH (4), DIVE_UNLOCK_TIDAL_DEPTH (4).

(7) **§46.7 What This Is NOT.** Eight explicit rejections: not a combat system (no underwater monsters, creatures are decorative silhouettes only), not a life skill (no Diving skill progression), not a primary income source (DIVE_GOLD_PER_HOUR_MULTIPLIER enforces underperformance), not gated behind Supporter Pass, not a competitive surface (no leaderboards, rankings, or timed challenges), not connected to crafting (no underwater-exclusive materials), not randomized narrative (Echo Stones authored per-zone at content pipeline time), not a time-gated event (available any time player meets depth unlock).

**§-Reference Map updated.** Phase 3 stubs row reduced (Diving removed). §46 row rewritten to "Contemplative underwater diving, underwater Echo Stones, Tidal Caves narrative layer."

**Zero invariant surface touched.** Diving is not a combat system and does not interact with combat tuning. Underwater gold-rate cap (DIVE_GOLD_PER_HOUR_MULTIPLIER: 0.5) trivially satisfies INV-22 Gold Equilibrium — the system is a net gold *source* at below-surface rates, ensuring it cannot inflate the economy. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged:
- **INV-22 Gold Equilibrium:** Underwater gold rate is 50% of surface — net minor gold source well within equilibrium margins. Treasure caps at Normal-tier, preventing any high-tier economic leak.
- **INV-16 Damage Ceiling:** No combat interaction.
- **INV-04 Life Skill Distribution:** Diving is not a life skill — no XP, no progression curves, no life-skill budget impact.
- **All combat invariants:** Trivially unaffected since diving has zero combat surface.

**Identity-acknowledgment thread — twelfth surface.** v12.25 crafter attribution → v12.30 weapon history → v12.33 Mastery certifications → v12.36 title showcase → v12.41 pool contribution credit → v12.42 derivatives revealed → v12.44 Stability Fragments → v12.45 minigame badges → v12.46 furniture gift history → v12.47 Mythic pet lineage → v12.48 stat training milestones → **v12.50 underwater Echo Stone discoveries**. v12.50's distinctive contribution: the first acknowledgment surface that is *environmental* rather than *personal* — the Tidal Caves zone itself carries memory that the player can discover, and the Discovery Journal's underwater-stone count becomes a quiet record of the player's narrative engagement with the zone. Prior acknowledgment surfaces recorded player actions (crafts, certifications, pet merges) or player relationships (gift history, pool contributions); v12.50 acknowledges player *attention* — what the player chose to look at, to read, to spend time with.

**Block 6/7 Phase 3 elevation pattern — fourth ship.** v12.45 §47 Minigames, v12.46 §41 Furniture, v12.47 §40 Pet Evolution, and now v12.50 §46 Diving. Consistent design-language across all four: social/identity/cosmetic/contemplative framing with explicit rejection of combat-utility, stat-buff, competitive, and farming-optimization registers. Each ship reframes a Phase 3 stub away from its v12-pre mechanical framing toward experience-first register. v12.50 extends the pattern into environmental narrative (Tidal Caves-as-memory) — a new register variant complementing social (v12.45/46), personal-identity (v12.47), and foundational-legibility (v12.48).

**Light architecture touches — Architecture bumps v2.21 → v2.22.** Minimal additions: (1) Echo Stone schema extension — new `placement: 'surface' | 'underwater'` field on Echo Stone entries in `echo-stones.json`, with validation ensuring no Echo Stone entry has both values in the same zone. (2) Client-side diving-mode state machine tracking dive session start/end/air-depletion events for rendering transitions and audio register switches; no server-side state required beyond existing zone-presence tracking. (3) Discovery Journal sub-category addition for underwater stones (client-side filtering on the existing Journal DO data — no new storage). See architecture §9.5.18 for full schema detail.

**Companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.32 → v5.33 with new §8.57 Contemplative Underwater Diving — Creative Direction covering the meditative audio register (swelling tone, low-pass filter, Final Echo Stone / Convergence-shrine register), visual depth-layer approach (parallax-as-gradient-not-zones), creature silhouette discipline (aliveness-without-combat), air-rhythm narrative framing (breathing-not-failure), underwater Echo Stone voice (things forgotten, sunk, drowned — the zone's memory), and explicit creative rejection list.

**Why ship this now.** Third ship in Block 7 marathon (v12.48 / v12.49 / v12.50). Plan-entry integration count advances to 52 of 58 (6 remaining). Medium-heavy authoring weight (full §46 rewrite, new CR subsection, small Architecture schema extension) — comparable to v12.46 and v12.47 rather than v12.48/v12.49's refinement-weight ships. Completes the contemplative-register arc for Block 6/7 (four Phase 3 stub elevations into social/personal/environmental/foundational registers). Remaining marathon ships (v12.51-v12.54) pivot back to combat-adjacent surfaces (Arena transparency, Clan War spectator, desktop power-user features) before the v12.55 final hygiene pass closes the integration sequence.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.50): The §46 rewrite is a repositioning from "farming loop with drowning-damage tension" to "contemplative exploration with narrative-fragment reward and rhythmic air management." The two load-bearing design moves are (1) underwater Echo Stones as primary reward with cross-placement validation ensuring exclusive-to-underwater content, and (2) air-management-as-rhythm via forced-resurface-without-damage. Both moves are substantial departures from the pre-v12.50 stub. The first move gives diving a purpose that farming cannot replicate — a player diving for gold is choosing inefficiency, which means the players who dive are self-selecting for narrative engagement. The second move removes punitive tension that would otherwise conflict with contemplative register — air becomes pacing, not threat. The contemplative-register treatment (swelling audio, parallax depth layers, creature silhouettes, light behavior, Final-Echo-Stone audio vocabulary) positions diving as a mode change for the player rather than a combat-adjacent activity with different scenery; this is a register shift the prior stub did not support. The creature silhouettes are a deliberate narrative-over-combat choice: they communicate aliveness and scale without introducing combat, and they also suggest the zone has more depth than the player can access, reinforcing the Tidal-Caves-as-memory thesis. The v12.50 ship is the fourth Phase 3 stub elevation in the Block 6/7 arc (v12.45 Minigames, v12.46 Furniture, v12.47 Pet Evolution, v12.50 Diving) and extends the elevation pattern into a new register variant — environmental-narrative complementing social/personal/foundational. This variant is generalizable: future Phase 3 zone-specific content systems (underwater Ember Fields lava explorations? deep-forest canopy traversal? high-altitude glider gliding?) could adopt the same "narrative primary, resource secondary, rhythm-not-failure, contemplative register" pattern with lighter design work because v12.50 establishes the pattern and its invariants (content-exclusivity enforcement, non-optimal resource rates, no combat surface, no life-skill progression, no competitive leaderboards). The identity-acknowledgment thread extends to its twelfth surface in a new direction — environmental acknowledgment (what the player has attended to in the world) complementing the prior eleven surfaces' personal and relational acknowledgment. Architecture impact is minimal — Echo Stone schema gains a placement field, client-side diving state machine for rendering transitions, no server-side state beyond existing zone presence. The Block 7 marathon continues: v12.51 next with Desktop Power-User + Screenshot/Replay bundle, which pivots back to combat-adjacent UX surfaces before the final four ships close out the remaining Phase 3 elevations and the integration sequence.]`

### GDD v12.49

Design-ship — Social UI Refinements (friend activity + leaderboard defaults). Two entries from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 26 §30.1 Friend List Activity refinements + Entry 27 §37.2 Leaderboard Default Scope refinements), both landing as **refinements of existing v12.25-CANONICAL content** rather than new section authoring. E26's core spec (friend zone visibility, time-in-location, privacy toggle, teleport) was already integrated in v12.25; E27's core spec (three scope filters, default-to-personal-scope, nearby-neighbors display) was already integrated in v12.25. The v12.49 ship addresses the remaining fragment-level details: offline-recency formatting, Lawless hunting indicator, Convergence-explicit label, Friends-default threshold tune, top-of-scope reference refinement.

**The design principle — refinement over rewrite.** Both E26 and E27 arrived in v12.25 as substantive implementations that covered 85%+ of the plan spec. The remaining gap was details: the exact threshold for Friends-as-default, the visual treatment for top-of-scope reference, specific edge-case zones (Convergence, Lawless Land with PvP-threat state), offline-recency display format. A full rewrite of either subsection would have been wasteful; instead, v12.49 lands as targeted refinements that bring both subsections to plan-spec fidelity without disrupting their established canonical structure. This demonstrates the integration plan's design — many entries land cleanly in early ships, and late ships address the residual details rather than re-authoring whole subsections.

**New GDD canonical content — §30.1 v12.49 refinements:**

(1) **Offline recency formatting.** Friends show last-seen recency — "Offline (last seen 3h ago)" / "Offline (last seen yesterday)" / "Offline (last seen 4d ago)." Beyond 7 days compresses to "Offline (last seen 1+ week ago)" — gives friends context about drift without precise surveillance timestamps. Only shown if friend's privacy setting permits location visibility; hidden friends show only "Offline" with no recency.

(2) **Lawless "(hunting)" indicator.** Friends in Lawless Land with active PvP-threat state display "(hunting)" marker. Would-be joiners see the indicator on friend list entry AND secondary warning on teleport confirm: "This friend is in active PvP engagement. Joining may be unsafe." Signals risk posture without announcing specific PvP targets.

(3) **Convergence-explicit label.** Friends at the Convergence show as "In Convergence" specifically rather than generic zone-name fallback. The Convergence is the game's endgame surface (§53, §57); a friend being there carries distinct social-visibility meaning different from any other zone. Explicit label treats endgame presence as its own register.

**New GDD canonical content — §37.2 v12.49 refinements:**

(4) **Friends-default threshold ≥1 → ≥3.** The Friends tab becomes the default scope when a player has **≥3 friends** rather than ≥1. A leaderboard with only 1-2 friend-entries reads as lonely rather than social — the comparison produces no meaningful "rank" sensation. ≥3 friends guarantees at least 4-entry leaderboard (self + 3 friends), enough for rank position to be legible as social context. Below 3 friends, Clan-or-Global fallback produces more substantive comparison surface.

(5) **Top-of-scope reference refinement: top-3 instead of top-10.** The aspirational-reference strip at the top of each leaderboard panel shows the **top 3** of the scope rather than the previous top-10, with smaller text and subtler treatment than the nearby-neighbors display. Lets the player's immediate peer-view dominate while keeping scope-ceiling visible as quiet reference — a long top-10 list would compete for attention with the nearby-neighbors display that is the actual target surface.

**Three new tuning constants (v12.49):** LEADERBOARD_FRIENDS_DEFAULT_THRESHOLD (3), LEADERBOARD_TOP_REFERENCE_COUNT (3), LEADERBOARD_NEIGHBOR_RADIUS (2). All scoped to §37.2. The third constant (neighbor radius) codifies the existing "2 above, 2 below" behavior as a tunable per the feature-local constants pattern.

**Zero invariant surface touched.** v12.49 is pure UI/UX refinement with zero mechanical effect. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged:
- **INV-22 Gold Equilibrium:** No gold flow.
- **INV-16 Damage Ceiling:** No combat modification.
- **INV-04 Life Skill Distribution:** No combat/life-skill scaling change.
- **All combat invariants:** Unaffected.

**Zero architecture impact.** No schema change, no new RPCs, no anti-cheat updates, Architecture stays at v2.21. Offline-recency is computed client-side from the existing last_seen_at timestamp (already present in friend-list DO data). "(hunting)" indicator is computed client-side from the existing pvp_threat_active flag. Convergence-explicit label is a client-side rendering refinement. Leaderboard threshold changes are client-side default-scope computation — no server changes needed.

**No companion CR bump.** The v12.49 refinements do not introduce new creative direction — they refine existing established direction. §30.1 v12.25 Extended and §37.2 v12.25 CANONICAL creative direction (friend-list as social-compass, leaderboards-as-personal-stakes) remains authoritative unchanged. Creative Reference stays at v5.32.

**Why ship this now.** Second ship in Block 7 marathon. Plan-entry integration count advances to 51 of 58 (7 remaining). Lowest authoring weight of the marathon (two targeted subsection refinements, zero new content, zero invariant risk, zero architecture touch). Composes cleanly with v12.48 (both extend v12.25-era canonical content with small refinements to bring plan-spec fidelity) before moving to heavier Phase 3 stub elevations (v12.50 Contemplative Diving, v12.52 Clan War Spectator, v12.53 Arena Training + Public Bet Outcomes, v12.54 Mask Emotes).

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.49): v12.49 is a refinement ship, not a new-systems ship. Both E26 and E27 arrived in v12.25 with strong implementations that left only fragment-level gaps against their plan specs. The ship lands those fragments — offline-recency format, Lawless hunting indicator, Convergence label, Friends-threshold tune, top-of-scope visual refinement — without disrupting the canonical structure. The Friends-default threshold ≥1→≥3 is the most substantive change, addressing a real edge case: a player with 1-2 friends sees a lonely 2-3-entry leaderboard when opening Friends-as-default. ≥3 creates minimum comparison substance (4+ entries, self included). The top-3-reference refinement (from top-10) acknowledges that the nearby-neighbors display is the target surface and the scope-ceiling should be quiet reference, not a competing list. Offline-recency format resolves a surveillance-vs-context tradeoff by giving buckets ("3h ago," "yesterday," "4d ago") rather than precise timestamps — friends know whether a friend has drifted without being able to compute exact activity patterns. Lawless hunting indicator extends the v12.25-era discipline (friends see risky-zone markers on teleport) with finer detail (active PvP state warrants stronger warning than zone type alone). Convergence-explicit label treats endgame presence as its own register rather than rolling it into zone-name fallback — a friend being in the Convergence is narratively distinct from being in any other zone. Zero invariant or architecture impact — the ship is pure client-side UI/UX tuning. Block 7 marathon continues: v12.50 next with Contemplative Diving (Phase 3 stub elevation continuing the v12.45/v12.46/v12.47 pattern).]`

### GDD v12.48

Design-ship — Training Moment Feedback. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 1), extending §1.6 Training Visibility with a new §1.6.1 subsection covering two complementary legibility layers: **continuous-fill bars** (moment-to-moment sub-integer training visibility on the inspect card) and **threshold celebration** (first-productive-use character glow + floating label). Closes the legibility coverage gap between the §1.6 per-action micro-feedback and the Journey Log session recap — the intermediate time scales (sub-integer progression and milestone-crossing moments) are now explicitly articulated.

**The design principle — legibility across all five time scales.** The §1.6 infrastructure (training pane, combat micro-feedback, Journey Log recap) covered moment-to-moment, per-action, and session scales. The intermediate scale — sub-integer progression between integer ticks, and milestone-crossing moments — was the remaining gap. v12.48 fills it. The stat system now has complete legibility across all time scales: moment-to-moment (combat micro-feedback), integer-to-integer (continuous-fill bars), threshold-to-threshold (celebration), session (Journey Log), and lifetime (archetype emergence). Future work on §1 is expected to be tuning rather than legibility.

**New GDD canonical content — §1.6.1 Training Moment Feedback.** New subsection after §1.6 Training Visibility, structured in six content areas:

(1) **Continuous-fill bars.** Each stat on the inspect card displays a small fill bar between integer values. Power 149.7 shows ~70% filled bar under the integer "149." During active combat, the bar visibly fills as use-training accrues — smoothed render of the underlying continuous XP counter. Bar-completion ticks over to next integer and resets.

(2) **Threshold celebration trigger rules.** When a stat crosses a round-number threshold AND the player subsequently takes an action that benefits from the new tier, character glows in stat's accent color ~0.5s with a small floating label ("FEROCITY 150"). Other nearby players see the effect. **First productive use, not threshold crossing itself** — Ferocity 150 during idle fires nothing; next crit fires the celebration.

(3) **Tier-differentiated frequency.** T1 stats (Power/Ferocity/Fortification/Mind/Spirit/Nerve) celebrate at every +10 threshold. T2 derivative stats celebrate at every +25 threshold. Keeps T2 celebrations rare enough to stay meaningful given more-frequent sub-unit changes.

(4) **Stat-name pedagogy phase-out.** Label shows stat name alongside value for the first 10 celebration triggers per stat on a character ("FEROCITY 150"); after 10th trigger, drops to value-only ("150"). Teaches new players; respects veterans.

(5) **Five tuning constants.** TRAINING_T1_CELEBRATION_INTERVAL (10 points between T1 celebrations), TRAINING_T2_CELEBRATION_INTERVAL (25 points between T2 celebrations), TRAINING_CELEBRATION_DURATION (0.5s glow), TRAINING_NAME_PEDAGOGY_COUNT (10 triggers before label simplifies), TRAINING_FILL_BAR_SMOOTHING (0.3s animation window). All feature-local per established pattern.

(6) **What This Is NOT.** Five explicit rejections — not a popup, not stat-buff (crossing does nothing mechanically new), not broadcast beyond local view, not a completion track, not overlapping with existing micro-feedback (§1.6 continues to fire; v12.48 celebrations fire only at thresholds).

**Zero invariant surface touched.** v12.48 is pure UI/UX legibility work with zero mechanical effect. Threshold crossings do nothing mechanically that wasn't already happening — celebration is acknowledgment of existing stat progression, not a reward for it. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged:
- **INV-22 Gold Equilibrium:** No gold flow.
- **INV-16 Damage Ceiling:** No stat modification.
- **INV-04 Life Skill Distribution:** No combat or life-skill scaling change.
- **All combat invariants:** No combat mechanical change; celebration visual is non-interactive.

**Identity-acknowledgment thread — eleventh surface.** v12.25 crafter attribution → v12.30 weapon history → v12.33 Mastery certifications → v12.36 title showcase → v12.41 pool contribution credit → v12.42 derivatives revealed → v12.44 Stability Fragments → v12.45 minigame badges → v12.46 furniture gift history → v12.47 Mythic pet lineage → **v12.48 stat training milestones**. v12.48's distinctive contribution: the first surface to acknowledge the *foundational* identity surface (stats themselves) that all other identity-surfaces refer back to. Prior surfaces acknowledged *what the player did* (captures, certifications, crafted items, pet lineages); v12.48 acknowledges *what the player became* (the trained stat values that are the underlying substrate of identity). The acknowledgment thread now reaches from the deepest layer (trained stats) to the most visible (Mythic pets, furniture).

**Zero architecture impact.** No schema change, no new RPCs, no Architecture bump — Architecture stays at v2.21. Continuous-fill bars read from the existing stat-XP pipeline; threshold celebration uses client-side detection (player's stat crossed threshold N turns ago; their next productive-action event fires the celebration). Productive-action detection is client-side event-driven; the server validates celebration broadcasts for the "other nearby players see the effect" case using the existing nearby-player event-broadcast pipeline. No new server-side logic needed.

**Companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.31 → v5.32 with new §1.7 Training Moment Feedback — Visual Identity (inserted between existing §1.7 Stat-Based Visuals archetype section and §1.7a Weapon Quality visuals). Covers acknowledgment-not-reward register, character-glow presence-not-spectacle, floating label quiet typography, first-productive-use trigger discipline, stat-name pedagogy teach-then-trust, continuous-fill animation restraint, and explicit creative rejection list.

**Why ship this now.** First design ship in Block 7 (marathon close). Plan-entry integration count advances to 49 of 58 (9 remaining). Low authoring weight (one subsection extension in GDD, one subsection addition in CR, zero architecture), zero invariant risk, zero ripple. Opens the marathon sequence with a ship that closes an outstanding legibility gap in the §1 stat system — the foundational surface that other identity-acknowledgment surfaces build upon — before moving into the remaining social-UI, Phase 3 elevation, and accessibility-extension ships.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.48): The v12.48 §1.6.1 addition is a legibility completion. The §1.6 infrastructure covered moment-to-moment, per-action, and session scales; the intermediate scale — sub-integer progression and milestone-crossing — was the remaining gap. Continuous-fill bars address the first; threshold celebration addresses the second. The "first productive use" trigger discipline is the load-bearing choice: celebrating on threshold crossing itself would produce feedback disconnected from the player's actual experience. Tying celebration to the first productive use means the feedback fires when the improvement is actually experienced — the first crit after Ferocity 150, the first successful block after Fortification 100. This matches the shape-first / observational-warm register: the game acknowledges what the player did through their own action, rather than interrupting to announce a state change. The two-layer tier-differentiated frequency preserves the celebratory register by keeping triggers rare enough to stay meaningful — a player sees roughly 50 T1 celebrations over a character's lifetime, reading as punctuation rather than spam. The stat-name pedagogy phase-out respects both new and experienced players without requiring a settings toggle. No invariants touched — pure UI/UX legibility with zero mechanical effect. The v12.48 ship opens Block 7's marathon close — the remaining 9 plan entries will ship across v12.49-v12.54 with a final v12.55 hygiene pass. The stat system now has complete legibility coverage across all five time scales; future §1 work is expected to be tuning rather than legibility. The identity-acknowledgment thread extends to its eleventh surface and reaches its deepest layer — the trained stats themselves, the substrate all other identity surfaces refer back to.]`

### GDD v12.47

Design-ship — Expressive Pet Evolution. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 43), rewriting §40 from Phase 3 stub to canonical specification. Replaces the v12-pre stat-progression framing (4-tier evolution with stat increases and elemental-attack gem enchantment) with a pure-cosmetic visual-inheritance system: pets remain **non-combat loot vacuums** through all tiers, evolution is expressive rather than mechanical, and Mythic-tier pets are personal-identity artifacts unique to each player's specific capture arc.

**The design principle — your pet is yours.** Conventional pet-evolution systems treat the evolved form as an *upgrade* (higher stats, more slots, better combat utility). The v12.47 rewrite rejects this framing entirely — evolved pets are not upgrades, they are *personal artifacts*. A Mythic pet is meaningful because it carries the visual lineage of the specific Base pets the player captured over weeks or months of play. No two players produce identical Mythics because the source-pet combinations vary across zones, times, and species variants. The removal of combat relevance is the critical design move: any stat benefit at any tier would bias merge decisions toward optimization ("which pets produce the best Mythic for fighting Apex bosses?"), and the personal-artifact framing would fracture. With combat irrelevance absolute, every merge decision is a pure aesthetic choice.

**New GDD canonical content — §40 Expressive Pet Evolution.** Full rewrite from Phase 3 stub to substantive v12.47 canonical specification, structured in seven subsections:

(1) **§40.1 Pet Capture to Merge — The Long Arc.** Base pets enter inventory via existing §18.1 capture mechanics. Capture-to-Mythic arc consumes 8 Base pets across 7 merges (2^3 tier progression: 8 Base → 4 Evolved → 2 Ascended → 1 Mythic). Days to weeks of engagement depending on play frequency. Non-Mythic tiers tradeable via §39 marketplace; Mythic is non-tradeable by design.

(2) **§40.2 Merge Mechanics.** Same species required (Imp+Imp, Skeleton+Skeleton, Eye+Eye). Same tier required (Base+Base→Evolved, etc.). Both pets owned by same player. Both source pets consumed on merge — irreversible. Mythic is terminal tier; no further merges.

(3) **§40.3 Visual Trait Inheritance.** Three channels inherited per merge: **color palette** (deterministic blend or crossover per element-compatibility table; Flame+Frost → Purple-magenta or bi-tonal per designer-authored pair cells), **texture pattern** (deterministic inheritance from more-distinctive parent + stochastic placement of accent patterns), **shape elements** (deterministic inheritance from more-pronounced-feature parent; higher-tier wins ties). Traits never regress — Mythic cannot look simpler than ancestors. Lineage record persists all 8 Base source pets and 7 merge events.

(4) **§40.4 Mythic as Personal Artifact.** Unique emergent from vast combinatorial space (3 species × 10 elements × ~5 sub-variants × 8 source pets). **Non-tradeable across all paths** — marketplace rejects, mail-gift rejects, all transfer RPCs reject at server level. Prismatic nameplate treatment matching Godly-weapon / Transcendent-crafter visual register. Full merge-tree visualization on inspect card. Auto-generated lineage sentence at Mythic creation: *"This Mythic Imp was born from your first Flame Imp captured at Ember Fields, merged across 7 evolutions..."*

(5) **§40.5 Trade-Tier Boundaries.** Base/Evolved/Ascended tradeable on §39 marketplace, giftable via §41 pattern, mergeable. Mythic non-tradeable / non-giftable / non-mergeable (terminal). Tiered policy produces an economy around source-pet trade while preserving Mythic personal-identity — mirrors v12.41 Convergence pool discipline where most gems flow freely but the "personal-artifact" moment has constrained handling.

(6) **§40.6 Tuning Constants.** Six new constants: PET_MERGE_SAME_SPECIES_REQUIRED (true), PET_MERGE_SAME_TIER_REQUIRED (true), PET_MYTHIC_TRADEABLE (false), PET_VISUAL_TRAITS_PER_MERGE (3 = color + pattern + shape), PET_LINEAGE_MAX_DEPTH (8 Base pets in complete Mythic lineage), PET_PATTERN_PLACEMENT_STOCHASTIC (true — only the pattern-placement channel is random; color and shape are deterministic).

(7) **§40.7 What This Is NOT.** Seven explicit rejections including combat system retirement (no attacks, no stat bonuses, no gem-slot enchantment), no Supporter Pass purchase path, no randomization of main inheritance channels (deterministic merges let players predict outcomes before committing), not a "collect all pets" completionist system (depth > breadth), enchantment subsystem explicitly retired, pet inventory private by default.

**§18.1 Pets subsection updated.** Pet Enchantment paragraph retired — the pre-v12.47 gem-slot elemental-attack subsystem is explicitly removed from the design surface and replaced with a note documenting the retirement. Pet Evolution reference updated to point to the v12.47 canonical §40 spec. Combat-irrelevance reinforced at the §18.1 level.

**Six new tuning constants** — all listed in §40.6. Scoped to §40 per feature-local placement pattern.

**Zero invariant surface touched.** Pets produce no gold flow, no XP, no combat stats, no damage/defense/utility in any context. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged:
- **INV-22 Gold Equilibrium:** Merges are free (no gold cost beyond source-pet capture); §39 marketplace existing gold-flow unchanged for non-Mythic tiers.
- **INV-16 Damage Ceiling:** Pets have zero combat interaction by construction.
- **INV-04 Life Skill Distribution:** Trapping gains meaningful long-form engagement (8-pet capture arc toward Mythic) without producing combat-relevant scaling.
- **All combat invariants:** Trivially unaffected since pets have zero combat surface.

**Identity-acknowledgment thread — tenth surface.** v12.25 crafter attribution → v12.30 weapon history → v12.33 Mastery certifications → v12.36 title showcase → v12.41 pool contribution credit → v12.42 derivatives revealed → v12.44 Stability Fragments → v12.45 minigame badges → v12.46 furniture gift history → **v12.47 Mythic pet lineage**. v12.47's distinctive contribution: the Mythic pet is the first identity-acknowledgment surface where the *artifact itself is unique to the player*. Prior surfaces recorded what the player did (certifications, histories) or displayed relationships (gift history, pool contributions); the Mythic pet *is* the personal record made visible. Each Mythic is a specific snapshot of a specific player's capture arc; inspecting one tells a story no other player could tell.

**Enchantment subsystem retirement.** The v12-pre §40 stub anticipated an enchantment subsystem (slot elemental gems into pets for elemental attacks, slots scaling with evolution tier 1-4). v12.47 explicitly retires this subsystem. Pets have no combat attacks, no gem slots for combat effects, no stat modifications. The simplification is load-bearing for the "your pet is yours" framing — enchantment would have reintroduced combat-relevance and defeated the personal-identity intent.

**Opens §40 as canonical surface.** Third Phase 3 stub elevated to canonical in Block 6 (v12.45 §47 Farm Minigames, v12.46 §41 Gift-Economy Furniture, v12.47 §40 Expressive Pet Evolution). Consistent design-language across all three — social/identity/cosmetic framing with explicit rejection of stat-buff / combat-utility / competitive registers. Block 6's social-layer thesis holds: the farm is becoming a fully-realized social space with v12.45 activities + v12.46 decoration + v12.47 personal companions all composing cleanly.

**Light architecture touches — Architecture bumps v2.20 → v2.21.** Pet schema extensions: `lineage: Array<PetLineageEntry>` (tracking merge history with all source pet records), `visual_traits: PetVisualTraits` (computed at merge time and persisted). One new RPC: `merge_pets(pet_a_id, pet_b_id, new_name)`. Mythic non-tradeable enforcement at marketplace and mail-transfer RPC boundaries. Follows the established item-schema-extension pattern (v12.25 attribution fields, v12.46 gift metadata). See architecture §9.5.17 for full schema detail.

**Companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.30 → v5.31 with new §8.56 Expressive Pet Evolution — Creative Direction covering: "your pet is yours" framing, visual inheritance as identity-through-lineage, Mythic's uniqueness as personal artifact (matching the §57 Final Echo Stone's register of player-specific meaning), the bittersweet feel of merges (both parents consumed — something lost to gain something), the "no two Mythics are alike" emergent variety, capture-to-Mythic as long-form engagement arc.

**Why ship this now.** Third design ship in Block 6, continuing the identity-acknowledgment social-layer register. Plan-entry integration count advances to 48 of 58 (10 remaining). Medium authoring weight (one substantive rewrite of an existing stub, §18.1 reference updates, one new CR subsection, one Architecture subsection), zero invariant risk (pure cosmetic layer with no combat or economic surface). Clean composition with v12.45/v12.46 — all three ships open Phase 3 stubs as canonical social-layer surfaces. The enchantment retirement simplifies the design surface for future work: pets are now cleanly scoped to identity-expression without navigating the combat-gems complexity inherited from the original stub.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.47): The §40 rewrite is substantially a repositioning of the system's purpose. The original Phase 3 stub treated pet evolution as a stat-progression system with enchantment-based combat utility — a completionist collectible loop where merged pets became increasingly effective combat allies. The v12.47 reframing rejects this entirely in favor of pure cosmetic expression. Pets remain non-combat loot vacuums throughout all tiers; evolution is visual inheritance only; Mythic is a personal-identity artifact rather than a combat upgrade. The removal of combat relevance is the critical design move — any combat benefit at any tier would bias merge decisions toward stat optimization, and the "your pet is yours" framing would fracture. With combat irrelevance absolute, every merge decision is a pure aesthetic choice: "what do I want this pet to look like?" The visual-inheritance system (color + pattern + shape) produces enough variety that players' Mythics are visually distinct even when following similar capture paths, and the 8-source-pet lineage ensures substantial time investment per Mythic so each one carries real personal weight. The non-tradeable discipline for Mythic tier is load-bearing — it prevents a secondary market from emerging and preserves the personal-artifact framing. The prismatic nameplate treatment for Mythic pets matches the Godly-weapon and Transcendent-crafter visual vocabulary established for other sacred-object surfaces, positioning the player's Mythic pet in the same prestige register without making it mechanically prestigious. This continues Block 6's social-layer / identity-acknowledgment thesis into a new surface — v12.45 farm minigames as hangout texture, v12.46 furniture as social-map record, v12.47 pets as personal visual lineage. Each ship extends the "identity earned through behavior, not declared through paperwork" thesis into a different game surface. The capture-to-Mythic arc takes real time (8 Base pets captured across many zones over weeks of play), which gives the system substantial long-form engagement without requiring players to grind toward an endpoint — the Mythic naturally happens when the player has played long enough, and the specific visual outcome reflects the specific path taken. The retirement of the enchantment subsystem simplifies the design surface: pets are now a cleanly-scoped identity-expression system with no overlap into combat tuning. Block 6 now has three Phase 3 stubs elevated to canonical (§47, §41, §40) with consistent design-language across all three; future ships can continue this pattern or pivot depending on priorities.]`

### GDD v12.46

Design-ship — Gift-Economy Furniture. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 44), rewriting §41 from Phase 3 stub to canonical specification. The rewrite replaces the v12-pre stat-buff-aggregator framing (12 pieces with passive stat buffs aggregating multiplicatively, 1-25 woodworking skill gate, "dream home" completionist loop) with a pure-visual gift-economy system: furniture has **zero stat buffs, zero combat effect, zero progression mechanic**. Every piece carries permanent crafter attribution (leveraging the existing §18.3 infrastructure from v12.25) that displays visibly on the placed piece in the house, making a player's house a physical record of the woodworker friendships they have accumulated.

**The design principle — house as social map, not stat-collection.** Conventional housing systems either treat furniture as a pure cosmetic sink (visual-only with no social meaning) or as a combat-adjacent power system (stat buffs that incentivize collecting). The v12.46 rewrite rejects both framings in favor of the *social-map* framing: furniture is a carrier of social history. A veteran player's house becomes a record of their friendships; a new player's house is mostly empty with the occasional piece from a kind woodworker they met early; a dedicated woodworker's house displays primarily their own work alongside a few received gifts. The meaning is in the attribution, not the furniture itself. Stat buffs are explicitly removed because any mechanical effect would reframe the system as optimization rather than social memory — **mechanical pressure to display a gift you don't aesthetically want corrupts the gift's meaning**, and min-max pressure to cultivate woodworker friends for buff optimization would turn social connections into instrumental relationships.

**New GDD canonical content — §41 Gift-Economy Furniture.** Full rewrite from Phase 3 stub to substantive v12.46 canonical specification, structured in seven subsections:

(1) **§41.1 Furniture Catalog.** Approximately 12 piece types at launch across four categories (Bedroom, Living, Dining, Outdoor). Multiple aesthetic variants per type determined by crafter's material choice at craft time (oak, cherry, walnut, painted, weathered). Woodworking skill-level gated (1-25 per §18 progression). Material-primary cost (wood cost at crafting station; no per-piece gold beyond material). Transcendent-rank (Rank 8) woodworkers produce prismatic-attribution furniture matching §18.3 Transcendent-crafter discipline. Catalog is extensible — adding new pieces is content-data operation per the pipeline table (v12.46 furniture-entry specifically notes no combat-invariant check required since furniture has no stat buffs).

(2) **§41.2 Gift Flow.** Crafting for self (piece produced, owned, placeable). Crafting for a friend (at-craft-time "Gift to Friend" option; post-craft inventory "Give to Friend" action). Gifting is free — no gold flow for crafter or recipient. Marketplace commissioning (§39) remains available as the commercial path for players who want payment — marketplace-purchased furniture displays "Purchased from:" attribution while gifted furniture displays "Gift from:" attribution; both retain underlying §18.3 "Made by:" crafter attribution. Receiving a gift produces a small notification; recipient has three options (place in house / keep in inventory / return to crafter). Recipient is NOT obligated to display a gift — placement is discretionary, and the absence of display is not broadcast to the crafter as rejection.

(3) **§41.3 Placement and Display.** Up to FURNITURE_MAX_PER_HOUSE(24) placed pieces simultaneously in the §31 4×3 wood building interior. Free placement (most pieces, tile-grid aligned). Fixed slots (bed, wardrobe — structural pieces that cannot overlap with other fixed pieces). 4-direction rotation at placement. "Made by" label visibility via inspection — the label is NOT floating text above every piece (would produce visual clutter and read as achievement-display) but is revealed on inspect, matching §18.3's inspect-card discipline. Multi-piece visitor-read enables emergent social-network visibility: a visitor touring many friends' houses builds an implicit map of who-makes-what-for-whom across the social network.

(4) **§41.4 Self-Crafted Furniture.** Self-crafted pieces display "Made by: [self]" matching §18.3's standard crafter-attribution treatment — a Transcendent-rank woodworker crafting for themselves shows prismatic attribution in their own house. Mixed houses (self-crafted + gifts + marketplace-purchased) display each piece with its correct attribution type. A woodworker whose house is mostly self-crafted shows one identity; a non-woodworker whose house is decorated by friends shows a different identity; a mid-skill woodworker whose house mixes their own work with gifts from more-skilled friends shows the pattern of woodworking apprenticeship and gift exchange that typical play produces.

(5) **§41.5 Tuning Constants.** Five new tuning constants — FURNITURE_COUNT_AT_LAUNCH (12, content-data expandable), FURNITURE_MAX_PER_HOUSE (24 default, 12-48 range), FURNITURE_RETURN_REQUIRES_PERMISSION (true — discard allowed only after crafter declines return), FURNITURE_PLACEMENT_GRID (1-tile alignment), FURNITURE_ROTATION_COUNT (4 = N/E/S/W).

(6) **§41.6 What This Is NOT.** Seven explicit rejections: not a stat-buff system (v12-pre stat-buff aggregation explicitly superseded), not a gold sink (material-primary costs; no per-piece gold), not a combat surface (no furniture outside farm house), not tradeable through marketplace like weapons (commissioning is supported; re-selling received gifts is not — would break gift-economy framing), not a sortable/filterable content category (the house is a physical space to walk through, not a spreadsheet), not a Phase 3 stub anymore (elevated to canonical), not tradeable/gift-transferable/account-transferable (received gifts stay with the receiving character).

(7) **§41.7 §31 Cross-Reference.** Farm house (§31) gains furniture placement as a mode. The 4×3 wood building interior is the placement surface. Farm exterior (pond, garden, plots, fence) supports only outdoor-specific furniture (garden bench, plant stand) within a narrower grid adjacent to the house. Farm visitors via §31's existing invite system can enter the house and inspect placed furniture.

**Leverages existing v12.25 crafter attribution infrastructure.** §18.3 Crafter Attribution (v12.25 CANONICAL) already specifies that every crafted item — including furniture — carries permanent crafter identification. The v12.46 ship does NOT introduce new crafter-attribution mechanics; it activates the existing attribution system in the house-display context and articulates the gift-vs-commission-vs-self distinction. Items produced through any crafting station already carry "Made by:" metadata; v12.46 makes this metadata visible on placed furniture in the house. The v12.46 architectural footprint is correspondingly small — no new attribution schema, no new portfolio infrastructure, no new prismatic-attribution mechanics. The ship is primarily a specification of behavior that was already infrastructurally enabled.

**Zero invariant surface touched.** Furniture produces no gold flow, no XP, no combat stats, no life-skill bonuses, no XP multipliers, no regeneration bonuses, no any-kind-of-modifier. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged:
- **INV-22 Gold Equilibrium:** Material-primary crafting cost is an existing §18 woodworking cost; no new gold flow introduced. Marketplace commissioning of furniture uses existing §39 gold-flow machinery. Direct gifts carry no gold.
- **INV-16 Damage Ceiling:** Furniture has zero combat interaction by construction.
- **INV-04 Life Skill Distribution:** Woodworking gains a meaningful social-identity endgame (gift economy produces visible social reach for skilled crafters) without producing combat-relevant scaling. The skill distribution thesis (life skills are a parallel economy to combat, not a gate) is preserved.
- **All other invariants:** Furniture's zero mechanical footprint makes all combat-formula, economic-band, and drop-distribution invariants trivially unaffected.

**Identity-acknowledgment thread — ninth surface.** v12.25 crafter attribution (weapons), v12.30 weapon history, v12.33 Mastery certifications, v12.36 title showcase, v12.41 pool contribution credit, v12.42 derivatives revealed, v12.44 Stability Fragments, v12.45 minigame badges, **v12.46 furniture gift history**. Each surface answers "what has this character done?" or "who has this character been in relationships with?" with small persistent acknowledgments that are not mechanical rewards. v12.46's distinctive contribution extends the thread from "what this character has done alone" to "who this character has been in relationships with" — the house as social-map makes relationships visible as persistent game-state for the first time. Prior identity-acknowledgment surfaces surfaced solo accomplishments (certifications, badges, weapons); v12.46's furniture surfaces social history (gifts from friends).

**Opens §41 as canonical surface.** Prior to v12.46, §41 was a Phase 3 stub with a stat-buff framing that would have had to be rebalanced against existing combat invariants. The rewrite sidesteps this by removing mechanical effects entirely. The Phase 3 region (§36-§48) continues opening as canonical surfaces — v12.45 opened §47 Farm Minigames, v12.46 opens §41 Gift-Economy Furniture; future ships can continue this pattern for §40 Pet Evolution, §45 Personal Housing decorations, §42 Clan Wars, §43 Arena, §44 Betting, §46 Diving, with the social-layer surfaces particularly available for the "low-stakes, identity-first" framing thesis that v12.45-v12.46 have established.

**Woodworking gains a social-recognition endgame.** Prior to v12.46, woodworking's endgame was weapon-crafting (shared with metalworking and leatherworking) plus the §41 stub (stat-buff furniture). The rewrite gives woodworking a distinctive endgame character — woodworkers produce the gift-economy furniture that decorates the game's social spaces. A Transcendent-rank woodworker's portfolio (§18.3.1) shows not only their production history but also (through the game's broader data) the distribution of their work across many players' houses. This gives woodworkers a meaningful social position: they are the craftsmen of the game's domestic spaces.

**Light architecture touches — Architecture bumps v2.19 → v2.20.** The existing §18.3 crafter attribution infrastructure (v12.25, §9.5.5 item schema) covers furniture attribution identically to weapons/armor/composites — no new attribution-schema work needed. New architectural work covers house-placement state: one new field on PlayerState (`house_furniture_layout: Array<PlacedFurniture>`) tracking which pieces are currently placed, where, and in what orientation. Each `PlacedFurniture` record includes item reference, tile position, rotation, and a gift-metadata pointer (which, for gifted pieces, references the underlying §18.3 attribution plus gift-sender-at-transfer-time). Follows the established PlayerState-extension pattern. No new Durable Object, no schema migration. See architecture §9.5.16 for full schema detail.

**Companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.29 → v5.30 with new §8.55 Gift-Economy Furniture — Creative Direction covering: the "house as social map" tonal register, crafter-attribution visual treatment on placed pieces (peripheral-readable labels matching the v12.42 threat-arrow and v12.38 weapon-edge-glow peripheral discipline), the gift-reception and gift-giving feel registers (warm-without-ceremony), the privacy discipline around non-placed gifts (recipient's aesthetic agency protected without broadcasting declined placements to the crafter).

**Why ship this now.** Second design ship in Block 6, continuing the social-layer register opened by v12.45 Farm Minigames. Plan-entry integration count advances to 47 of 58 (11 remaining). The ship is a natural continuation of v12.45's hangout-framing thesis — v12.45 established farm activities as hangout texture; v12.46 establishes farm furniture as social-history record. Both ships extend the "identity earned through behavior, not declared through paperwork" thesis into the farm social surface. Clean composition with §18.3 Crafter Attribution (leveraging existing infrastructure), §31 Personal Farm (extended with placement UI), §39 Marketplace (commissioning path preserved), §47 Farm Minigames (activity-hangout + decoration-hangout together make the farm a fully-realized social space).

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.46): The §41 rewrite is substantially a repositioning of what the system is *for*. The original stub positioned furniture as a woodworking endgame with stat-buff aggregation — a completionist collectible system where players' goals were to craft all 12 pieces and maximize their combined buffs. This framing produces the wrong social outcomes. A stat-buff furniture system produces hoarding (you want every piece), optimization pressure (which pieces stack best?), and trading friction (sellers ask premium prices, buyers shop around). The v12.46 reframing replaces all of this with pure social exchange. Gift-economy means pieces flow between friends, not between strangers-with-gold. Attribution means every piece carries a face. Visible plaques (via inspect) mean every visit to a friend's house can become a tour of their social history when the visitor chooses to look closely. Over time, a player's house accumulates furniture not from a completionist drive but from actual relationships — the chair your friend made you when you hit Level 50, the bookshelf from the woodworker you met randomly in a dungeon, the dining table your guild leader made for every member. A veteran player's house is a record of their time in the world. A Transcendent woodworker's portfolio showing many houses displaying their work is a record of their reach — how many players they've touched through their craft. This is the correct framing for a social-layer hobby system. Combat-adjacent power is handled by weapons, armor, gear tempering, and stat investment. Social-identity is handled by titles, showcases, certifications, and now furniture. The systems don't overlap; each does its own work. The explicit removal of stat buffs is load-bearing — any combat-relevance would reframe the system toward optimization, defeating the social-map intent. The attribution-through-inspection discipline (rather than always-visible floating text) is load-bearing — the house is a decorated space first, a social record second, and a visitor sees the room they enter before they see the attributions. The gift-mechanic emphasis over trading is load-bearing — trading produces anonymous commercial exchange; gifting produces named social memory. The discretionary-placement rule is load-bearing — recipients aren't pressured to display gifts they don't like, and crafters don't see "your gift was rejected" signals because declined placements are private. The design matches the game's broader "identity earned through behavior" thesis — furniture identity is earned through being in relationships with woodworkers, not declared through stat-optimization. The v12.46 ship continues Block 6's social-layer register (v12.45 farm minigames → v12.46 furniture gift economy) and opens the §41 surface for future ships to extend (seasonal decorative pieces, farm exterior placements, multi-player crafting sessions, etc.) within the same social-first discipline.]`

### GDD v12.45

Design-ship — Farm Minigames. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 46), rewriting §47 from Phase 3 stub to canonical specification. The rewrite replaces the v12-pre seven-element-minigame spec (Lava Dodge / Ice Deflect / Lightning Strike / Poison Pulse / Rock Crusher / Gust Catcher / Pearl Diver, each with 25g entry fee and competitive scoring) with a three-minigame hangout-oriented framing: Fishing (timing-based, pond-located), Cooking (fast-paced sequence challenge, house kitchen), and Elemental-Matching Puzzle (tile-based using the §10.1 element glyphs). Minigames are farm-local, low-stakes, and produce zero gold/XP/items — the only persistent output is a small cosmetic badge on the inspect card earned once per minigame, the first time the player completes each minigame's daily round.

**The design principle — time spent, not score chased.** Most ARPG minigame systems layer competitive scoring onto secondary content to produce leaderboard engagement. The v12.45 rewrite rejects this framing explicitly. Minigames in Bro Town are hangout texture, not content surface — the farm is where social time happens, and minigames are activities that exist within social time rather than destinations players travel to for content. Every design decision reinforces this frame: no entry fee (money barriers break the hangout register), no leaderboard (competition breaks social ease), short rounds (60-180s, brief activities interspersed with chat), zero mechanical rewards (rewards imply the activity is instrumental), trivial earn-once badge (enough persistence for "I played this" identity, not enough for "I'm good at this" status).

**New GDD canonical content — §47 Farm Minigames.** Full rewrite from Phase 3 stub to substantive v12.45 canonical specification, structured in four subsections:

(1) **§47.1 The Three Minigames.**
- **§47.1.1 Fishing.** Located at the farm pond (existing §31 geography). Timing-based — cast line, wait for ripple-and-bob cue, tap within reel-in window. Tempo is slow and meditative; conducive to simultaneous chat. Missed reels don't punish. Full session is 3-5 casts (~180s default duration). Co-play mode: multiple players fish simultaneously at different pond spots independently. Watching without participating is also valid — the slow rhythm accommodates observer participation.
- **§47.1.2 Cooking.** Located at the farm house's kitchen area (new small indoor space within existing §31 4×3 house footprint). Fast-paced — recipe sequence of 8-15 ingredient/action prompts displayed in rhythm, player must tap corresponding on-screen targets in correct order within the 60-90s time limit. Misses produce a small "splat" effect but don't end the round. Two co-play modes: **side-by-side cooking** (each player has their own recipe and cooking station) and **collaborative cooking** (two+ players share a single recipe with prompts distributed across stations, recipe completes only when all players hit their assigned prompts in sequence).
- **§47.1.3 Elemental-Matching Puzzle.** Located at a small puzzle table near the farm's flower garden (existing §31 geography). Tile-based pattern game using the ten §10.1 element glyphs (Flame diamond, Frost hexagon, Storm star, Venom teardrop, Flora leaf, Stone square, Wind triangle, Water circle, Dark trefoil, Light starburst). Tap matching tile pairs; cleared matches cascade downward producing potential chain matches. **Leverages §58.1 Shape-First discipline** — the element-glyph shapes are the primary identification channel, so the puzzle is fully playable by colorblind players without any accessibility toggle. Color distinction supplements for players who have it rather than being required. This is a direct application of the v12.43 shape-first baseline at a new game-surface. Medium tempo — pattern recognition plus strategic sequencing. Two co-play modes: side-by-side (separate grids) and shared (both players tap on the same grid collaboratively).

(2) **§47.2 Hangout Framing.** The farm is the social hosting surface (§31); minigames are activities within hangout sessions. Guest access flows through the existing farm-invite system — being invited to a farm is the full access grant for all three minigames. No gold, no XP, no item drops, no stat buffs. The sole persistent output is the badge system: **Fisher's Badge** (first successful fish reel-in), **Cook's Badge** (first completed recipe), **Matcher's Badge** (first cleared puzzle grid). Badges display on inspect card alongside Mastery certifications (§12.2) and archetype-encounter entries (§49.3), matching the v12.33/v12.36/v12.42/v12.44 identity-acknowledgment pattern — the badge says "this player has played this minigame at least once," with no tiers, proficiency levels, or upgrade paths. Earn-once, display-forever. Daily first-completion detection resets at 00:00 UTC (matches §32 free-tier reset schedule). No leaderboard, no cross-farm comparison, no score visibility to other players — personal-best tracking is permitted locally but no public ranking. Privacy-as-default matches v12.43/v12.44 discipline.

(3) **§47.3 Round Duration and Concurrent Players.** Five new tuning constants: MINIGAME_FISHING_SESSION_DURATION (180s default, 60-300s range), MINIGAME_COOKING_ROUND_DURATION (75s default, 60-90s range), MINIGAME_PUZZLE_ROUND_DURATION (90s default, 60-120s range), MINIGAME_MAX_CONCURRENT_PLAYERS (4 default, 2-4 range — matches existing §31 farm-visit cap), MINIGAME_DAILY_RESET_UTC (00:00 — when daily first-completion window resets). All three minigames scoped to brief interactions — activity breaks measured in minutes, not session content measured in hours.

(4) **§47.4 What This Is NOT.** Eight explicit rejections: not a PvP surface, not a gold sink, not an XP source, not an item source, not element-themed beyond the Matching Puzzle, **the seven-element-minigame spec is explicitly superseded** (matches v12.42 Entry 20 REVISED precedent where the original color-outline spec was explicitly rejected), not a Phase 3 stub anymore (elevation to canonical), not tradeable/gift-able/account-transferable.

**Enum registry updates.** Two existing enums rewritten to match the v12.45 system:
- `MinigameType: fishing / cooking / elemental_matching` (renamed from prior `MinigameMechanic: dodge/block/attack/rhythm/sequence/catch/resource` which reflected the seven-element spec)
- `MinigameBadge: fisher / cook / matcher` (renamed from prior `MinigameScore: survival/blocks/hits/streak/correct/caught/pearls` which tracked per-game scoring categories that no longer exist)

The v12-pre enums are explicitly removed — the seven-element spec they served is explicitly superseded. Enum registry notes added documenting the v12.45 rewrite.

**Five new tuning constants** — all listed above in §47.3 summary. All are scoped to §47 (feature-local placement pattern matching v12.41 CONVERGENCE_POOL, v12.43 DODGE_IFRAMES_ASSIST, v12.44 PRIMORDIAL_STABILITY_FRAGMENT) rather than landing in the §20 global constants appendix.

**Zero invariant surface touched.** Minigames produce no gold, no XP, no items, no stat effects, no combat interaction. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged:
- **INV-22 Gold Equilibrium:** Zero economic surface — no entry fees, no rewards. The pre-v12.45 25g entry fee is removed explicitly, but this was never a significant gold sink in live play (the minigames were Phase 3 stubs and never shipped at the original spec), so its removal has no equilibrium impact.
- **INV-01, INV-02 (Mana/Stamina):** Minigames exist outside combat resource systems entirely.
- **INV-03 Kill Time Range:** Not a combat surface.
- **INV-16 Damage Ceiling:** Not a combat surface.
- **INV-28 Quality Drop Distribution:** No drops, no loot.
- **All economic invariants:** No gold flow, no trade flow, no item flow.

**Opens §47 as a canonical surface.** Prior to v12.45, §47 was a Phase 3 stub with no focused design-ship iteration. The rewrite elevates it to canonical specification while keeping implementation scheduling a separate decision — Phase 3 timing remains unchanged, but the spec is now substantive enough to inform downstream work (art, audio, UI) without requiring another design pass.

**Identity-acknowledgment thread extended.** The earn-once badge mechanism joins the established pattern — v12.25 crafter attribution, v12.30 weapon history, v12.33 Mastery certifications, v12.36 title showcase, v12.41 pool contribution credit, v12.42 derivatives revealed, v12.44 Stability Fragments. Each of these surfaces answers "what has this character done?" with a small persistent acknowledgment that is not a mechanical reward. The minigame badges extend this into the hangout-activity domain — a player's inspect card now can reflect their engagement with social farm activities in addition to their combat demonstrations.

**Shape-first discipline gets a game-surface application.** The v12.43 §58.1 Shape-First Design Principle articulates shape-first as a baseline UI discipline. v12.45's Elemental-Matching Puzzle is the first shipped game surface that leverages this discipline at the gameplay level — the puzzle is fully playable through shape recognition alone, with color as supplement. This demonstrates the discipline's downstream value: shape-first authoring makes new content automatically accessible without bolt-on accessibility work.

**Light architecture touches — Architecture bumps v2.18 → v2.19.** One new Set field added to PlayerState: `minigame_badges_earned: Set<MinigameBadge>` (default empty). Server-authoritative increment on first-completion detection in the farm-minigame-resolution pipeline (new code path for this subsystem); no decrement path — badges are earn-once permanent. Ephemeral per-player daily-completion tracker (reset at 00:00 UTC) lives in combat-state-adjacent transient storage and is not persisted across days, matching the v12.38 `resonance_streak` and v12.39 `grace_window_start` ephemeral-field pattern. Follows v12.33/v12.37/v12.42 Set-field persistent-state pattern. No new Durable Object, no schema migration (existing characters load with empty Set on first post-v12.45 login). See architecture §9.5.15 for the full schema extension detail.

**Companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.28 → v5.29 with new §8.54 Farm Minigames — Creative Direction covering: the "hangout material" tonal register across all three minigames, per-minigame feel (fishing as meditative-adjacent-to-chat, cooking as fast-paced-with-cooperative-option, matching as medium-cerebral-pattern-recognition), the farm-as-social-space framing that positions minigames as activities within hangouts rather than destinations for content, explicit rejection of victory/failure ceremony (no fanfare on completion, no punishment on timer expiry), badge display tonal discipline matching the identity-acknowledgment thread established across v12.33/v12.36/v12.42/v12.44.

**Why ship this now.** First design ship past the v12.44.1 hygiene pass, opening Block 6. Plan-entry integration count advances to 46 of 58 (12 remaining). The ship is a deliberate tonal contrast to the v12.41-v12.44 block's heavy focus on combat/encounter/accessibility surfaces — v12.45 is a social-layer ship touching surfaces that haven't received design focus since v12.34 Gambling Den. Medium authoring weight (one substantive rewrite of an existing stub, one new CR subsection, one Architecture subsection), zero invariant risk (additive social-layer with no economic/combat interaction), clean composition with §31 Personal Farm and §41 Furniture Crafting as natural adjacencies.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.45): The §47 rewrite is as much a repositioning as a specification. The original seven-element spec treated minigames as a parallel content surface — players would travel to the farm arcade, pay entry fees, play competitive rounds, compare scores, accumulate bragging rights. This model assumes minigames are *content* that players engage for its own sake. The rewrite rejects this assumption. Minigames in Bro Town are *texture* — background atmosphere that makes the farm feel lived-in as social space. A player visiting a friend's farm for conversation needs something to do together beyond standing around typing; that's what minigames provide. A player playing solo during a combat break needs something gentle to decompress with; that's also what minigames provide. Neither use case requires competitive scoring, leaderboards, rewards, or progression — in fact, all of those would work against the use case by making the activity instrumental rather than intrinsic. The three-count (down from seven) reflects the hangout-variety principle rather than the element-coverage principle — Fishing/Cooking/Matching provide three tonal registers (meditative/fast-paced/medium-cerebral) that together cover the mood-range of a typical hangout session. More than three would produce choice paralysis during short social visits; fewer than three would feel under-varied. The Elemental-Matching retention leverages the v12.43 shape-first design discipline — the element palette already supports colorblind play through shape glyphs, and this puzzle inherits that accessibility for free. The shape-first principle thereby gets a natural game-surface application, demonstrating the discipline's forward-reaching value (v12.43 articulation produces accessibility-ready game content at v12.45 without bolt-on accommodation work). The daily-first-completion badge mechanism extends the "identity acknowledgment, not mechanical reward" thread established across seven prior ships (v12.25 crafter attribution → v12.44 Stability Fragments) — earn-once permanent recognition that says "this character has engaged with this activity" without any power, progression, or prestige implication. The privacy-as-default (no leaderboard, no cross-farm comparison, no public score visibility) matches the §58 accessibility modes and §57.2b Stability Fragments disciplines — hangout time is personal, not competitive surface. The minigame system as shipped is deliberately small: three games, three badges, one farm location, zero economic surface. Small is correct for what this system is — it is not a pillar of the game's content, it is a background texture that makes the farm feel lived-in as social space. The explicit supersession of the seven-element spec matches the v12.42 Entry 20 REVISED pattern (original color-outline arrow spec explicitly rejected in favor of directional arrow); the original spec produced the wrong framing for this content type, and the rewrite articulates the correct framing rather than trying to retrofit the original to work. Opening §47 as canonical (rather than keeping it as Phase 3 stub) also opens the §36-§48 Phase 3 region for future design-ship iteration — a player-farm / social-layer thread that can extend to §41 Furniture (currently stub, touched adjacently here), §40 Pet Evolution, §45 Personal Housing, and other social-surface elements. Block 6 opens with a clear tonal beat (social hangout) that differs from Block 5's combat/accessibility heaviness; future v12.46+ ships can continue this tonal register or pivot back to combat surfaces depending on plan-entry priorities.]`

### GDD v12.44

Design-ship — Stability Fragments for the Primordial Encounter. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 41), opening §57 Primordial as a design surface. Failed Primordial attempts now yield a capped, non-tradeable, non-purchasable consumable — a Stability Fragment — that the player can spend on future attempts to prefill PRIMORDIAL_STABILITY_FRAGMENT_PREFILL(10%) of the encounter's stability bar. Fragments cap at PRIMORDIAL_STABILITY_FRAGMENT_CAP(5) per character, yielding a 50% prefill ceiling that preserves the encounter's difficulty floor (Phases 2 and 3 of each alignment encounter, where the Primordial actively resists, are always experienced in the present attempt).

**The specific failure mode addressed — endgame-boss attrition through demoralizing partial progress.** The Primordial is the game's ultimate challenge and narrative conclusion (§57). It is solo-only, 24h-cooldown-gated, and produces no loot by design (§57.8). A player who clears 70-80% of the stability bar, dies, and respawns at 0% experiences the maximum demoralizing-retry pattern: significant emotional investment, meaningful progress, no tangible acknowledgment of the learning. The cooldown ensures a full day between attempts, which compounds the sense of lost progress. Without an intermediate mechanic, the encounter's no-loot / no-retry-on-success discipline — load-bearing for the narrative register — risks becoming a retention cliff where players who fail multiple times simply stop trying. Stability Fragments are the smallest possible response: failures accumulate a bounded resource that meaningfully shortens future attempts without trivializing the encounter or introducing monetization pressure.

**New GDD canonical content — §57.2b Stability Fragments.** Placed between §57.2a Prelude Corridor and §57.3 The Two Primordials, matching the pre-arena preparation phase where fragments are chosen. The subsection covers:

- **Earning mechanics.** One fragment per failed attempt (any attempt where the player enters the arena and doesn't reach 100% stability before death or quit). Cap of 5 per character; failures past cap produce no fragment but are not punished. Successful attempts produce no fragments (encounter is concluded per §57.6 PRIMORDIAL_RETRY_ON_SUCCESS(false)). Per-character, not account-level, not alignment-level — each character earns fragments through their own failures.
- **Application mechanics.** Active choice before arena entry — after the prelude corridor (or skip), before arena opens, a prompt appears if fragment count > 0. Player selects 0 to min(count, 5) fragments to apply, each prefilling 10% stability. **Consumed on arena entry**, not on failure or success — prevents the "spend, see how it goes, quit to preserve" gaming pattern. **Default selection is all available fragments** — ergonomic default matches the "compound effort" framing (apply what you've worked for).
- **Cap mechanics — 50% prefill ceiling.** 5 fragments × 10% = 50% stability at arena entry. Phases 2-3 (30-60%, 60-100%) where Primordial actively resists are always experienced in the present attempt. Final Echo Stone earned through present action, not accumulated prior failure. Minimum time from first attempt to maximum fragments is 5 days (24h cooldown × 5 attempts), each day including a genuine attempt — farming-at-scale unavailable.
- **Per-alignment behavior.** Fragments are alignment-agnostic at the inventory level — a character has one fragment count regardless of current alignment. Fragments earned on one alignment's encounter apply identically to any alignment encounter. Matters rarely (alignment switching is meaningful and infrequent per §56) but matters when it does.
- **Privacy discipline.** Fragment count is not publicly visible — no leaderboard, no profile stat, no broadcast when spent. Matches §58 accessibility-mode privacy register and §57.8 solo-only design constraint. Visible only on the portal-entry prompt to the owning character.
- **First-attempt state.** Fragment count = 0 on first attempt; prompt is suppressed entirely. Preserves the weight of the initial encounter and matches §57.2a "first experience is mandatory" discipline.
- **Post-success state.** Unspent fragments remain in the player's count but become unusable — arena inaccessible per PRIMORDIAL_RETRY_ON_SUCCESS(false). A silent record of the path taken. Do not convert to any other currency, cannot be traded, not displayed publicly. Visible only in the Discovery Journal's Primordial encounter log (§45).
- **What the mechanic is NOT** — not purchasable, not tradeable, not permanent, not account-wide, not visible to others, not a combat buff. All enumerated explicitly in §57.2b for cross-system clarity.

**Two new tuning constants.** `PRIMORDIAL_STABILITY_FRAGMENT_PREFILL` (0.10 default, 0.05-0.15 range) and `PRIMORDIAL_STABILITY_FRAGMENT_CAP` (5 default, 3-7 range). Added to §57.6 Combat Tuning table between PRIMORDIAL_RESPAWN_COOLDOWN and PRIMORDIAL_RETRY_ON_SUCCESS.

**Zero invariant surface touched.** The Primordial encounter sits outside the game's economic and combat-formula invariants — no loot, no XP, no gold drops, no gem drops (§57.8). Stability Fragments as an encounter-local consumable that only affects encounter-start stability state do not touch any of the 33 invariants. Specific verification:

- **INV-16 Damage Ceiling:** The Primordial is not a standard monster and has no damage formula that fragments could influence. Fragments prefill stability, which is the encounter's progress metric, not a combat property.
- **INV-22 Gold Equilibrium:** No gold interaction. Fragments are earned through failure and spent on attempts — no gold-to-fragment or fragment-to-gold conversion exists.
- **INV-03 Kill Time Range:** Does not apply to the Primordial (solo endgame encounter with unique stability-bar progression rather than HP-based kill resolution). The encounter's total duration is shorter with fragments applied, but the per-phase engagement mechanics and their difficulty are unchanged.
- **INV-28 Quality Drop Distribution:** No loot from the Primordial; no quality-drop surface exists.
- **All economic invariants:** Fragments are explicitly non-purchasable, non-tradeable, non-gift-able. Zero economic surface area.

**Anti-pay-to-win discipline (§32) preserved.** Stability Fragments are explicitly not purchasable through the Supporter Pass, direct purchase, gem exchange, or any other monetization path. This is load-bearing — any monetization path would transform the encounter from "personal narrative conclusion" into "optimized grind with pay-to-skip." The "earned through failure only" discipline is absolute and matches the game's broader anti-pay-to-win thesis (§32). A Supporter Pass holder and a non-holder have identical access to fragments through identical mechanics.

**Opens §57 Primordial as a design surface.** Prior to v12.44, §57 had one substantive change (v12.41's Communal Gem Pool at Convergence Shrine touched §56 Convergence but not §57 Primordial directly). The v12.44 Stability Fragments ship is the first focused design-ship touching the Primordial encounter itself. This establishes §57 as a surface available for future ships — accessibility modes for the encounter (Primordial-specific timing assist, difficulty settings for players with motor constraints), Arbiter-specific tactical layers, victory-state afterimages in the Convergence post-encounter. The ship does not preview any of these; it only establishes the surface is live.

**Pedagogical pattern — failure as material.** The Primordial encounter teaches through attempts rather than tutorials. A first-time player attempting the encounter learns the alignment-specific mechanic (Hero illumination / Sovereign shadow / Arbiter Eclipse-at-boundary) through engagement with the Primordial's escalating resistance. Each failed attempt produces specific learning — "the Phase 2 shadow waves move faster than I expected" or "I need to preserve light circles on the western edge before moving east." Stability Fragments acknowledge this learning with a small mechanical register: the player who failed has learned, and that learning compounds. The fragment is not a participation trophy; it is the game's acknowledgment that present effort learns from past effort. This matches the §33 Mastery Ladder pedagogy (v12.33) — surface what the player has already demonstrated, don't teach through tutorial — and extends it into the endgame-encounter context.

**Light architecture touches — Architecture bumps v2.17 → v2.18.** One new scalar field added to PlayerState: `stability_fragments: number` (0-5, default 0). Server-authoritative increment on Primordial-encounter failure detection, server-authoritative decrement on arena entry (by selected spend amount). No new Durable Object. No schema migration (existing characters load with `stability_fragments = 0` on first post-v12.44 login). Primordial-encounter resolution pipeline (existing code path that computes encounter outcome) gains the increment hook; arena-entry flow gains the decrement hook and a prompt-display step. See architecture §9.5.14 for the full schema extension detail.

**Companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.27 → v5.28 with new §8 extension covering Stability Fragments creative direction — the "compound effort" tonal register for the fragment-use prompt, the silent inventory treatment (no UI chrome celebrating accumulation), the "earned through failure" language discipline that rejects participation-trophy framing.

**Why ship this now.** Fourth design ship past v12.40.1 hygiene (v12.41 + v12.42 + v12.43 + v12.44 in the cadence block). The 4-ship cadence triggers a hygiene pass next (v12.44.1), matching the empirically-validated pattern from the prior four cadence blocks. v12.44 closes the block with a single substantive ship touching a surface that hadn't received focused design attention (§57 Primordial), setting up future ships to extend the endgame-encounter design surface without requiring the Primordial infrastructure to be bootstrapped first.
