The ship is thematically coherent with the v12.43 accessibility bundle — both address retention/access patterns for players who encounter difficulty — without being an accessibility ship itself (Stability Fragments are earned through engagement, not enabled as a preference).

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.44): Stability Fragments address a specific retention failure mode that conventional endgame-boss design is vulnerable to — the demoralizing-retry pattern where partial progress produces no mechanical acknowledgment, and the player walks away without anything to show for significant emotional investment. The Primordial encounter is particularly vulnerable because it is solo (no social cushioning), 24h-cooldown-gated (failures compound across days), and intentionally loot-free (the narrative register requires the absence of tangible rewards). A player failing five attempts without any compounding progress would experience the encounter as punishing rather than challenging, undermining the narrative goal of the encounter as "the game's epilogue" (§57 DESIGN INTENT). Fragments are the smallest possible mechanical response: each failure produces a bounded, non-tradeable, non-purchasable credit that shortens the next attempt's pre-escalation phase by 10%, capped at 50% total. The encounter's difficulty floor is preserved (Phases 2 and 3 always experienced directly); the narrative arc is preserved (present action still required to reach 100% stability and earn the Final Echo Stone); the monetization discipline is preserved (fragments are strictly in-game, absolutely not purchasable). The "active choice" framing for fragment application treats the player as capable of deciding their own difficulty — a player who wants the full 100%-from-zero experience on a successful attempt can simply not spend fragments. The default-to-full-application ergonomic matches the compound-effort frame (apply what you've worked for). The privacy-as-default matches the §58 accessibility modes and §57.8 solo-only discipline — the Primordial is the game's most personal moment, and fragment use belongs to that personal arc, not to any public surface. Opening §57 as a design surface is a secondary but real benefit — prior to v12.44, the Primordial encounter was largely authored once in the pre-v12.25 canonical state and had not received focused design-ship iteration. Stability Fragments establish that the surface is live for future design work (Arbiter-specific tactical layers, Primordial-specific accessibility modes, post-encounter Convergence afterimages) without committing to any particular direction. The fourth-in-cadence timing matches the empirically-validated 4-ship block pattern — v12.44.1 hygiene pass is now due and will capture any drift from this cadence block before v12.45 opens the next.]`

### GDD v12.43

Design-ship — Accessibility Compilation Bundle. Four entries from the experience-first integration plan, bundled as a coherent "accessibility" theme: Entry 47 (§58.1 Always-On Shape-First Design), Entry 48 (§58.2a Visual-Enhanced Mode), Entry 49 (§58.3a Reaction-Assist Dodge Timing), Entry 50 (§58.4 One-Handed Control Preset). All four entries strengthen §58 Accessibility explicitly rather than treating accessibility as incidental. The ship establishes Bro Town's full accessibility vocabulary across six complementary mechanisms operating on orthogonal sensory and input channels.

**The unifying design principle — accessibility as baseline, not special mode.** Conventional colorblind/hearing/motor accessibility in games is typically implemented as toggleable modes that transform the UI for players who need them. v12.43 inverts this: the baseline UI works for all players, and accessibility settings are *additive enhancements* rather than *corrective modes*. §58.1 Shape-First is the clearest example — every UI element is designed shape-first by default, so the colorblind-safe palette option is a polish layer rather than a necessary mode. §58.2a Visual-Enhanced amplifies existing visual cues for hearing-impaired players. §58.3a Reaction-Assist and §58.4 One-Handed address motor constraints with timing forgiveness and layout reconfiguration respectively. Each mechanism is a specific preference addition, not a game-transformation mode.

**New GDD canonical content.**

(1) **§58.1 Shape-First Design Principle (rewritten from prior §58.1 Color).** Converts colorblind accessibility from a toggle to a baseline design discipline. Every UI element that conveys information is designed such that shape carries the primary signal and color is a secondary enhancement. Full high-risk color-conflict table (Red/Green deuteranopia, Red/Green-yellow, Blue/Purple, Brown/Grey — each pair resolved through shape difference). Quality borders (§4.6b) use border treatment (none/thin silver/thick gold/prismatic/halo-geometry) independent of color. Element tier indicators use distinct saturation + shape framing. Status silhouettes distinguishable at icon size. Damage-number resistance tier uses typography. Threat arrows use triangular shape. Combo posture uses body configuration. **Explicit constraint on future UI authoring:** every new UI element must pass the shape-first test before approval. The prior "Phase 2 target: high-contrast mode" language is removed — a high-contrast mode becomes redundant when the base UI is already shape-first.

(2) **§58.2a Visual-Enhanced Mode.** Opt-in toggle in Settings → Accessibility → Visual Enhancement that amplifies visibility of audio-critical combat cues. Enhancement table covering 6 cue types: resonance pulse (2× alpha, 1.5× glow radius), collision timing flash (2× intensity + 1.5× duration), Grand Slam screen shake (2× amplitude + 1.5× duration), status effect icons (24px → 36px, +50% size), collision ground marks (1.3× size + 1.5× border thickness), damage-number flash (1.3× size + 1.5× duration). **All enhancements visual-only — zero gameplay impact.** Mechanical timing windows unchanged; only visibility amplified. No mechanical advantage or disadvantage in multiplayer. Three levels: Off/On/Stronger (3× intensity, 2× size for maximum emphasis). Default off — conservative default. Co-composes cleanly with v12.38 Haptic Feedback Layer; hearing-impaired mobile players can enable both for amplified visuals + tactile confirmation, producing roughly equivalent per-event feedback to hearing players. Performance impact minimal (~10-15% overdraw increase from larger effects, no new render passes).

(3) **§58.3a Reaction-Assist Dodge Timing.** Motor-accessibility mode that widens the dodge iframe window by starting iframes earlier in the dodge animation. Three modes: Standard (0s offset, canonical timing), Assisted (iframes start 0.1s earlier, +100ms forgiveness), Relaxed (iframes start 0.2s earlier, +200ms forgiveness). **iframe duration unchanged across all modes** — only the start time moves earlier. Total invincibility per dodge is identical; Assisted-mode simply shifts when the invincibility covers. Dodge distance, stamina cost, cooldown all unchanged — mode only affects iframe-start timing. §5.8 Contextual Dodge (lunge, retreat shot) inherits the player's chosen mode. **Multiplayer fairness trade-off documented explicitly** — Reaction-Assist player has ~100-200ms iframe advantage, which is the same order as network latency variation; accepted as accessibility-over-symmetry trade-off matching the §58.3 resonance-timing-assist precedent. Visibility to other players: NOT publicly visible (privacy-as-default discipline). **Two new tuning constants** — DODGE_IFRAMES_ASSIST_OFFSET_ASSISTED (0.1s default, 0.05-0.15s range), DODGE_IFRAMES_ASSIST_OFFSET_RELAXED (0.2s default, 0.15-0.30s range).

(4) **§58.4 One-Handed Control Preset.** Mobile-only layout preset routing all critical actions through single-thumb input. All primary controls (movement, swipe-direction, auto-attack, block) on left side; right side passive. Auto-attack continuous at standard rate on locked-on enemy; auto-lock on nearest enemy with auto-transfer when target dies/leaves range. Full-screen swipe zone. Block via long-hold (0.3s) on combat area rather than separate button. **Accepted constraints documented** — target selection loses tactical granularity (no manual enemy priority), attack timing rhythm loses manual control (auto-fire rate), multi-target swipe positioning less precise, some builds less effective (Staff-mage reduced; Greatsword Bruiser fine). **Scope: per-session preset, not full game mode** — players toggle between modes across sessions without character-data effects. Multiplayer compatible in all contexts (party, overworld, Arena, Clan Wars). Four documented use cases (temporary constraint / permanent motor condition / screen-mirroring / preference). Desktop not applicable — desktop input patterns don't map to the spec; desktop players use §34 controls + §58.3/§58.3a/§58.2a for accessibility.

**Two new tuning constants.** `DODGE_IFRAMES_ASSIST_OFFSET_ASSISTED` (0.1s default, 0.05-0.15s range) and `DODGE_IFRAMES_ASSIST_OFFSET_RELAXED` (0.2s default, 0.15-0.30s range) — both live in §58.3a per the accessibility-local placement pattern. §58.1 shape-first is a design discipline not a tunable system; §58.2a intensity multipliers are content data in the settings config file; §58.4 layout spec is a preset not a tunable system.

**Zero invariant surface touched directly.** All four entries are pure accessibility layers over existing systems. No combat formulas, damage math, stat formulas, economic bands, or mechanical tuning changes. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged. Specific verification:
- **INV-03 Kill Time Range:** Reaction-Assist extends iframe coverage but doesn't change damage output or kill times. The mode affects survival timing, not damage timing.
- **INV-16 Damage Ceiling:** Visual-Enhanced Mode is visual-only; Reaction-Assist affects iframe timing only, not damage. No damage multipliers touched.
- **INV-22 Gold Equilibrium:** All four entries are preference/rendering systems with no economic vectors.
- **All combat-system invariants:** One-Handed mode accepts constraints on tactical depth (auto-attack rhythm, target selection) but doesn't modify underlying combat formulas — a One-Handed player uses the same damage, status, combo, and collision math as a two-handed player.

**Multiplayer fairness trade-off for Reaction-Assist — documented and accepted.** The 100-200ms iframe advantage granted by Assisted/Relaxed mode is on the order of network latency variation in multiplayer. This is a real advantage, not a cosmetic difference. The trade-off is accepted explicitly: the game prioritizes access over competitive-balance symmetry, matching the precedent established by §58.3 resonance timing assist (which also produces a small mechanical variation that other players cannot match without enabling the same mode). Players who need the mode get it; competitive play absorbs the variance the same way it absorbs network-latency variance.

**Accessibility mechanism vocabulary complete after v12.43.** Six complementary mechanisms across orthogonal channels:

| Mechanism | Target | Channel | Ship |
|-----------|--------|---------|------|
| §58.1 Shape-First Design Principle | All players (colorblind baseline) | Visual structure | v12.43 |
| §58.2a Visual-Enhanced Mode | Hearing-impaired + preference | Visual intensity | v12.43 |
| §58.3 Resonance Timing Assist | Motor-constrained | Timing precision | pre-v12.25 |
| §58.3a Reaction-Assist Dodge | Motor-constrained | iFrame forgiveness | v12.43 |
| §58.4 One-Handed Control Preset | Motor-constrained | Input layout | v12.43 |
| §15.2a Haptic Feedback Layer | Hearing-impaired mobile | Tactile | v12.38 |

Each mechanism addresses a specific constraint on a specific channel. Combined use produces cumulative accessibility — a hearing-impaired mobile player with motor constraints can enable Visual-Enhanced + Haptics + Reaction-Assist + One-Handed simultaneously, getting amplified visuals + tactile confirmation + iframe forgiveness + single-thumb layout without any mechanism conflict.

**Light architecture touches — Architecture bumps v2.16 → v2.17.** Three accessibility preference flags extend the PlayerPreferences schema following the v12.38 `collision_glow_enabled` + `haptics_enabled` pattern:
- `visual_enhanced_mode: 'off' | 'on' | 'stronger'` (default `'off'`)
- `reaction_assist_dodge: 'standard' | 'assisted' | 'relaxed'` (default `'standard'`)
- `one_handed_mode: boolean` (default `false`)

No new Durable Objects. No schema migrations. §58.1 shape-first discipline is an authoring constraint with no persistent state. §58.2a Visual-Enhanced is purely client-side rendering amplification from the preference flag. §58.3a Reaction-Assist applies a server-side iframe-offset computation from the preference flag during dodge-resolution, bounded by the tuning-constants range (DODGE_IFRAMES_ASSIST_OFFSET_ASSISTED max 0.15s, DODGE_IFRAMES_ASSIST_OFFSET_RELAXED max 0.30s). §58.4 One-Handed is purely client-side input-routing from the preference flag. The preferences-flag pattern has now shipped through v12.38 and v12.43, maturing into a repeatable architectural discipline for opt-in player preferences. See architecture §9.5.11 extension for the full PlayerPreferences schema detail.

**Companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.26 → v5.27 with three accessibility-specific creative direction additions: (1) §1.1.1b DO/DON'T Quick Reference extension with a seven-row shape-first UI authoring rules table (element identification, quality tiers, status effects, directional indicators, state differentiation, resistance reads, monochrome-preview verification); (2) new §1.9.11 One-Handed Auto-Attack Feel covering the creative direction for the One-Handed Control Preset — character handles routine rhythm so the player can focus on movement and swipes, attack visual cadence identical to manual attack, auto-lock as character initiative rather than system automation, block long-press as committed stance; (3) new §8.53 Accessibility Modes — Creative Direction establishing the tonal register across the Accessibility settings panel (observational labels not medical framing, per-mode creative direction for Visual-Enhanced/Reaction-Assist/One-Handed/Shape-First, privacy defaults, unifying thesis of accessibility as universal UX quality). The three additions together formalize the accessibility-as-baseline creative discipline — a player using any §58 accommodation experiences the same character presence and combat register as a player who doesn't need the accommodation.

**Why ship this now.** Third design ship past v12.40.1 hygiene (v12.41 + v12.42 + v12.43 in the new 4-ship cadence block toward v12.44.1). v12.43 is a compilation bundle matching the v12.29, v12.36, v12.38, v12.42 precedent — four self-contained entries bundled thematically rather than a single substantive ship. The accessibility theme is thematically coherent (all four entries extend §58 Accessibility), zero invariant risk, and the four entries compose cleanly at the creative-reference and architecture levels. The ship produces a complete accessibility vocabulary statement — post-v12.43, the game's accessibility discipline is documented across six mechanisms covering color perception, hearing, motor, and input-layout constraints.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.43): The accessibility compilation is load-bearing for the game's inclusivity thesis. Conventional ARPG design treats accessibility as a late-stage polish concern addressed through toggleable modes that transform the UI for "those players who need them." v12.43 rejects this framing. The baseline UI is designed to work for all players — shape-first element palette, visible-to-all combat events, self-regulating economic systems — and accessibility settings are preference additions rather than corrective modes. A colorblind player doesn't need to enable anything to read the game's UI; a hearing-impaired player can amplify visual cues optionally; a motor-constrained player can adjust timing forgiveness optionally; a single-handed player can reconfigure input layout optionally. The game meets players where they are rather than requiring players to meet the game where it is. The six-mechanism vocabulary (shape-first, visual-enhanced, resonance-assist, reaction-assist, one-handed, haptic) operates across independent channels and composes cleanly for players with multiple constraints. Each mechanism is a specific preference, not a mode; each mechanism is privacy-preserving (no public indication); each mechanism preserves the game's identity (players with accessibility preferences play the same game, not a simplified variant). The multiplayer fairness trade-off for Reaction-Assist is the clearest statement of principle: the game explicitly accepts ~100-200ms iframe variance for accessibility over perfect competitive symmetry. This is the correct priority order. Competitive Arena play already absorbs network-latency variance at the same order of magnitude; accessibility variance is indistinguishable in practice and morally mandatory. The shape-first principle strengthens the game's UI design for all players — a shape-first UI reads better at low resolution, in glare, through motion, and across display hardware — so the accessibility-first discipline produces universal UI quality benefits. The v12.43 ship is small in mechanical footprint (three preference flags, one new subsection each) but significant in design discipline (six mechanisms becoming the game's canonical accessibility vocabulary, with the shape-first principle becoming a constraint on all future UI authoring).]`

### GDD v12.42

Design-ship — Combat Legibility Compilation Bundle #4. Three entries from the experience-first integration plan, bundled as a coherent "combat legibility" theme: Entry 4 (§5.9 Progressive Combo Posture), Entry 11 (§2 Tier-Gated Stat Derivative Visibility), and Entry 20 (§11 Per-Party-Member Aggro Outlines, REVISED to directional arrows). All three entries extend the legibility thread established by v12.27 Combat Feedback Visibility and v12.33 Surfaced Mastery Ladder into new specific surfaces: the character body as combo-state indicator (Entry 4), the inspect card as stat-investment visibility (Entry 11), the monster sprite as threat-direction signal (Entry 20). Each entry makes previously-implicit information legible through peripheral-vision-friendly signals.

**New GDD canonical content.**

(1) **§5.9.8 Visual Combo Posture.** The weapon-glow combo indicator (§5.9.5) extends at count 2 and count 3 into a full-body character posture shift. Count 2 produces a subtle ready-stance lean (~5° forward, weight shift); count 3 produces weapon-specific flourish poses: Greatsword plants blade tip in ground with weight braced, Sword raises blade to shoulder-ready with off-hand extended, Bow draws arrow to full tension at anchor point, Staff suspends gem above staff tip with visible energy buildup. Posture transitions interpolate over ~0.2s (snap on weapon swap). Purely visual — no hitbox, collision, movement, or mechanical changes. Visible to all players per existing §5.9.5 glow rule. Clean composition with §49.3 Quiet Archetype Emergence (archetype affects baseline pose, posture overlays combo-tier modification) and §9.2.1 Weapon Edge Glow (weapon-edge signal for collision compatibility, posture signal for combo tier — both fire independently). Toggleable reduced-motion mode disables posture animations while preserving §5.9.5 glow as sole count indicator.

(2) **§2.7 Progressive Derivative Visibility.** Tier 2 stat derivatives (defined in §2.1-§2.6) are progressively revealed on the inspect card as underlying stat grows. Primary effect always visible for every stat; secondary and tertiary derivatives appear at per-stat thresholds. Ferocity: Crit Damage Mult at 100, Crit Soft Cap at 300. Elemental Mastery: Status Duration at 50, Status Damage at 150. Fortification: Partial Block Floor at 50, Poise at 100, Counter Res at 150. Restoration: Healing Output at 50, Collision Mana at 150. Agility: Attack Speed at 50, Dodge iFrame Bonus at 150. Influence: Debuff Potency at 75. **The hiding is UI-only; every derivative applies mechanically at all stat values regardless of visibility.** Reveal animation fires next inspect-card open after threshold crosses (0.3s slide-in + 0.5s brief highlight, one-time per derivative per character). Viewing another player's card shows *their* threshold state, not the viewer's — each player's inspect card reflects their own depth. No opt-out toggle — the reveal is structurally pedagogical rather than preference-based. New `derivatives_revealed: Set<DerivativeId>` field on PlayerState extending the v12.33/v12.37 Set-field pattern.

(3) **§11.1 Threat Direction Arrows (REVISED variant).** Each aggroed monster displays a small directional arrow (8px default, muted orange `#D68A3C`, ~70% alpha) above its sprite, pointing toward the specific player it is currently targeting. Arrow tracks monster and target positions in real-time. Aggro-transition fade behavior (0.3s) makes threat shifts visible as events. Scope covers 4-player parties AND 50-player overworld AND solo play. No aggro = no arrow. **The plan's original color-coded outline spec is explicitly not shipped** — the revised directional-arrow approach is strictly better because it answers the question players actually ask ("is that enemy coming at me?") rather than "which party member is this assigned to?", scales cleanly to all combat contexts, and requires zero color-decoding. Server-authoritative aggro state (existing per §11); arrow is purely client-side rendering from existing broadcast data. Zero architecture impact.

**Three new tuning constants.**

- `AGGRO_ARROW_SIZE` (8px default, 4px-16px range) — arrow size at standard camera zoom. In §11.1.
- `AGGRO_ARROW_FADE_ON_TRANSITION` (0.3s default, 0.1s-0.6s range) — fade duration on aggro-transition events. In §11.1.
- Per-derivative visibility thresholds are content-data in §2.7 (not in §20 tuning since they're pedagogical thresholds rather than balance tuning).

**Zero invariant surface touched.** All three entries are pure legibility/visibility layers over existing systems. No combat formulas, damage math, stat formulas, economic bands, or mechanical tuning changes. No new derivatives added (progressive visibility reveals existing §2.1-§2.6 derivatives); no posture hitbox changes (§5.9.8 explicitly no hitbox modification); no new threat mechanics (§11.1 is purely a visual signal on existing §11 threat state). All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged:
- INV-16 Damage Ceiling — unaffected. No damage multipliers added or modified.
- INV-22 Gold Equilibrium — unaffected.
- INV-03 Kill Time Range — unaffected.
- All per-stat-derivative invariants — unaffected (derivatives continue to function at all stat values; only their inspect-card visibility is gated).

**Legibility thread extension.** The peripheral-vision combat-awareness vocabulary established by v12.27 (Combat Feedback Visibility), v12.33 (Surfaced Mastery Ladder), and v12.38 (Combat-Sensory Compilation) gains three new specific surfaces in v12.42:

| Channel | Signal | Ship |
|---------|--------|------|
| Visual focal | Active Block, Dynamic Reticle | v12.27 |
| Character body | Combo posture (v12.42 new) | v12.42 |
| Weapon edge | Collision-compatibility glow | v12.38 |
| Above enemy | Threat direction arrow (v12.42 new) | v12.42 |
| Inspect card | Progressive derivative visibility (v12.42 new) | v12.42 |
| Resource ledger | Resonance streak badge, entropy overlay, grace tick | v12.38, v12.39 |
| Notification band | Mastery Level crossings | v12.33 |
| Tactile | Haptic Feedback Layer | v12.38 |
| Audio | Atmospheric layers + combat cues | v12.26 |

v12.42 adds three surfaces to a vocabulary already operating across six other channels — combat is now legible at roughly nine distinct sensory registers simultaneously, with each register reading a specific kind of state.

**Light architecture touches.** (a) `derivatives_revealed: Set<DerivativeId>` field on PlayerState for §2.7 progressive visibility tracking. Server writes to the Set when a stat crosses its next threshold; client reads the Set when rendering inspect card. Extends the v12.33 `certifications_earned` and v12.37 `archetype_encounters_seen` Set-field pattern. (b) Combo posture rendering is purely client-side animation from existing broadcast combo-count data — no new state or network messages. Client animation configuration file `combo-posture.json` holds per-weapon flourish pose specifications (content data, not runtime state). (c) Threat arrows are purely client-side rendering from existing broadcast aggro state — no new state or network messages. Architecture bumps v2.15 → v2.16 for the `derivatives_revealed` PlayerState field extension. See architecture §9.5.14 for the integration spec.

**Companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.25 → v5.26 with three additions: §8.21 Combo Chain extension covering the per-weapon flourish posture register (what each weapon's count-3 flourish should *feel like* beyond the mechanical description); §1.9.3 Inspect Card Hierarchy extension covering the progressive derivative reveal animation and the "earned visibility" tonal register; new §1.9.10 Threat Direction Arrows visual direction covering the muted-orange color choice, the peripheral-not-focal rendering discipline, and the fade-on-transition feel.

**Why ship this now.** Second design ship past v12.40.1 hygiene (v12.41 + v12.42 in the new 4-ship cadence block toward v12.44.1). v12.42 is a compilation bundle matching the v12.29 and v12.36 precedent — three self-contained entries bundled thematically rather than a single substantive ship. Compilation bundles are well-suited to cadence-block middle positions where multiple small legibility improvements can ship together without competing for design-doc weight. The "combat legibility" theme is thematically coherent (all three entries extend existing visibility systems into new specific surfaces), zero invariant risk, and the three entries compose cleanly at the creative-reference and architecture levels.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.42): The legibility thread through v12.27/v12.33/v12.38/v12.39 established a design discipline: information the game already has server-side should be legible to the player through peripheral-vision signals that don't compete with focal attention. v12.42 extends this to three specific surfaces where the gap between "server has the information" and "player can read it" was noticeably large: combo tier (server knows the count; previously signaled only through weapon glow), stat derivative structure (server applies all derivatives; previously visible only through tutorialization), and threat direction (server knows which player each monster targets; previously invisible to players). Each entry places the information where peripheral vision can catch it: the character body for combo tier, the inspect card for derivative structure, the monster sprite for threat direction. The three entries share a structural property — they make implicit information explicit without adding new mechanics. A player's combat outcomes are identical pre- and post-v12.42; only their ability to read what's happening has increased. This is the correct direction for a mature ARPG: mechanical depth stays constant, legibility surfaces accumulate. The combat experience at v12.42 is readable across roughly nine sensory channels (visual focal, character body, weapon edge, above-enemy, inspect card, resource ledger, notification band, tactile, audio), each carrying a specific kind of state without overlap. Expert players perceive all channels simultaneously through peripheral attention; new players perceive what they need and learn the others as they progress. The pedagogical curve matches the investment curve. Progressive derivative visibility is the clearest example: a new Ferocity investor sees one derivative (Crit Chance), a committed Ferocity build sees two (Crit Damage Mult unlocks at 100), an endgame Ferocity optimizer sees three (Crit Soft Cap info at 300). The inspect card grows with the character. This is what legibility looks like when it matches progression — not tutorialized up front, but revealed as earned.]`

### GDD v12.41

Design-ship — Communal Gem Pool at Convergence Shrine. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 40, REVISED variant — the revision supersedes the original NPC Trader draft). Adds a population-independent fallback economy to the Convergence cross-alignment gem trade system: a neutral-aligned shrine at the physical boundary between the Shadow and Radiant halves of the Convergence map, accumulating gems from Convergence deaths (`CONVERGENCE_POOL_DROP_RATE(0.30)` transfer chance per equipped gem on death) and making them available for purchase at a `CONVERGENCE_POOL_PRICE_MULTIPLIER(2.0)` gold premium to any alignment.

**The revised design principle — emergent economy, not failure-indicator NPC.** The original v12.25 draft proposed an NPC called "The Trader" who would appear in the Convergence when cross-alignment trade volume fell below a threshold for 48 hours and vanish when population recovered. The revision converts this into an always-present shrine with population-adaptive behavior through its own economic dynamics: at high population the pool churns rapidly (many deaths, many purchases); at low population the pool accumulates slowly into a reserve (fewer deaths, but gems persist until purchased). No appearance/disappearance logic, no population-threshold metric, no NPC speech signaling server health. The mechanic is self-regulating.

**New GDD canonical content.** §56.5a Communal Gem Pool — one new subsection with: shrine structure spec (neutral altar at Shadow/Radiant boundary, always present, non-combat infrastructure), pool accumulation mechanic (30% death-transfer rate per equipped gem, remaining 70% drops normally per §23.7), pool inventory display (element-grouped sortable grid with tier distributions and buy prices), purchase pricing rule (2× marketplace price for all alignments), contribution-credit mechanic (soft social acknowledgment, no economic transfer), pool-inflow-vs-draw-rate population-adaptive analysis, alignment-neutrality spec, no-inventory-cap specification, gold-sink integration with §23 economic model, INV-22 Gold Equilibrium preservation analysis, INV-24 Wealth Concentration preservation note (contribution credits are identity-only, not gold-transferring), anti-cheat discipline via existing pipelines, "why pool instead of NPC" design-decision framing. Full design intent block articulating why the revision is genuinely different (not a refinement) and why it's better across atmospheric, mechanical, and economic axes.

**Two new tuning constants.** `CONVERGENCE_POOL_DROP_RATE` (0.30 default, 0.10-0.50 range) governing the gem-to-pool transfer chance on Convergence death. `CONVERGENCE_POOL_PRICE_MULTIPLIER` (2.0 default, 1.5-3.0 range) governing the pool-purchase gold premium relative to §23 base marketplace price. Both constants live in §56.5a per the zone-mechanic-local placement pattern.

**Not shipped from the v12.25 original.** `CONVERGENCE_TRADE_MINIMUM` (5 trades/day population threshold for NPC Trader appearance) is explicitly not shipped — the pool has no threshold because it is always present. No NPC appearance/disappearance logic in the v12.41 implementation.

**Zero invariant surface touched directly — two invariants analytically preserved.**

- **INV-22 Gold Equilibrium:** The pool's 2× purchase premium produces a new gold sink proportional to pool engagement, which is itself bounded by Convergence death frequency × 0.30 transfer rate × purchase rate. New sink's gold-flow rate is strictly smaller than existing §23 sinks (Gem Cutting Service, NPC Enchanter Dust Buyback) at all reasonable population scales. No recalibration required. At scales where the pool's gold sink becomes large, it is functioning correctly — absorbing surplus gold from endgame players via voluntary fallback-economy participation.
- **INV-24 Wealth Concentration:** The contribution-credit mechanic is *not* an economic transfer. Contributors get identity acknowledgment when their gem is purchased; no gold is transferred to the contributor. Purchasers' gold is burned, not routed to the contributor. No new peer-to-peer concentration vector introduced.

All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged.

**Integrates cleanly with existing systems.**

- **§23.7 Death Scatter:** Pool-transfer is an opt-in alternative fork of the existing death-scatter resolution path — 30% of equipped gems transfer to pool, 70% drop normally per existing mechanics. The existing drop-and-recover loop continues to function; the pool captures a minority of gems rather than replacing the recovery system.
- **§56.5 Convergence Gem System:** The "game's ultimate power literally requires opposites cooperating" thesis is preserved — direct Hero↔Sovereign trades remain the best-value path because the pool's 2× premium makes direct trades strictly cheaper. The pool is genuine fallback (available but more expensive), not alternative (equal-value substitute). A Hero logging into a populated server still prefers direct trade; a Hero logging into a sparse server can still complete the Tier 7 dual-gem build through the pool, just at higher gold cost.
- **§23 Economic Model:** Pool gold sink integrates as an endgame-tier sink that scales with engagement. High-population servers with active direct trade produce smaller pool-related sinks; low-population servers produce larger ones. The incidence is correct — players most affected by low-population trade friction pay the gold premium that compensates for the friction.
- **Convergence lore:** "Two alignments meet here and both leave traces" extends mechanically — deaths leave gem traces, and those traces become shared resources. The pool is atmospherically consistent with the zone's existing identity rather than imported machinery.

**Light architecture touches.** New server-side persistent state: Convergence Communal Gem Pool DO tracking gem inventory, contribution credits, and purchase transaction log. Integrates with existing death-resolution pipeline (30% transfer fork) and new shrine-interaction endpoint. Architecture bumps v2.14 → v2.15. See architecture §9.5.13 for the full spec.

**Companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.24 → v5.25 with one new §8 entry: *§8.52 The Convergence Communal Pool Shrine — Creative Direction*. Covers the shrine's visual identity (neutral stone altar at alignment-boundary midpoint, faint elemental motes drifting around the accumulated gems, no directional lighting favoring either alignment), contribution-moment register (the dying player's gem transfer as a quiet trace rather than a ceremonial event), purchase-moment register (a brief acknowledging line when a contributed gem is purchased: the contributor sees a soft notification that someone took up their gem), and the tonal discipline of the shrine as *persistence-through-trace* rather than *transaction-hub*.

**Why ship this now.** First design ship past v12.40.1 hygiene pass; begins the new 4-ship cadence block toward v12.44.1. v12.41 is a medium-complexity single-entry ship that opens the Convergence zone as a design surface — the zone was referenced in recent ships (sprite sizes at v12.40, existing §56 spec) but has not received focused design-ship attention in the v12.25-v12.40 integration sequence. Completes an underdeveloped endgame economy vector (population-adaptive fallback for Tier 7 cross-alignment trade) that had been flagged since v12.25 but not addressed. The revised emergent-economy design is genuinely elegant — converting a failure-mode NPC into an always-present shrine with self-regulating behavior — and the integration with existing §23, §56, and §16 systems is clean.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.41): The Convergence's endgame power curve requires cross-alignment gem trades to reach the peak Tier 7 dual-gem build — this is design-intentional (§56.5's "opposites cooperating" thesis). At high server populations, this works naturally through direct Hero↔Sovereign trades; at low populations, direct cooperation thins and the system's power curve becomes inaccessible. The plan's original draft solved this with an NPC Trader appearing during low-population windows, which works mechanically but signals failure atmospherically — an NPC whose presence means "the server is struggling" is essentially a failure indicator in character form. The revised Communal Gem Pool design converts this failure mode into an emergent feature: the Convergence itself becomes the fallback economy, deaths leave gem traces at a shrine, and those traces accumulate into a population-adaptive supply. The shrine is always present, always behaving correctly for the current population density, without any threshold logic or NPC state-switching. The 2× gold premium keeps direct trades the preferred path when available (preserving §56.5's cooperation thesis) while making the fallback path reliably available. The contribution-credit mechanic gives pool contributors soft social recognition (their gem helped another player) without economic transfer, which preserves INV-24's wealth-concentration discipline and matches the game's recurring "identity acknowledgment, not mechanical reward" pattern (v12.25 crafter attribution, v12.30 weapon history, v12.33 Mastery certifications, v12.36 title showcase). Gold burned on purchase integrates naturally with §23 gold-sink modeling — the pool produces an endgame-tier sink proportional to engagement, with incidence falling correctly on players who benefit most from the fallback economy (low-population endgame players with surplus gold). The zone's existing "two alignments meet here and both leave traces" lore extends mechanically into the pool's accumulation behavior — gem traces from both alignments commingle in shared shrine inventory, available to any alignment, physicalizing the thematic convergence the zone's name promises. Every aspect of the design improves on the NPC Trader alternative: atmospheric consistency, mechanical elegance, population adaptiveness, economic incidence, thematic alignment. This is what "revision supersedes original" means at its best — not incremental refinement but structural re-approach that produces a better outcome across every axis.]`

### GDD v12.40

Design-ship — Non-Linear Monster Size Jumps. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 13, REVISED variant), rewriting §6.8 Monster Sizes to concentrate the visual-growth transition at a single dramatic depth 8 jump rather than smooth linear scaling. Small but far-reaching presentation change — depth 1-7 retains smooth 16→32→48→64→80→96→112 progression, then jumps dramatically to **176×176 at depth 8** (~57% increase, the largest depth-over-depth jump), with depths 9 and 10 plateauing at the same 176×176 endgame-scale sprite size.

**New GDD canonical content.** §6.8 Monster Sizes rewritten with the revised progression table, per-depth notes calling out depth 4 (player-width match) and depth 8 (endgame-entry visual transition), design-decision framing for why single-jump beats two-jump, INV-03 Kill Time Range preservation note, hitbox continuity disclosure, content authoring scope (no new art required — existing sprites re-exported at updated sizes).

**Cross-reference updates — four locations touched.**

1. **§-Reference Map Monster sizes row** — updated range to `16×16 → 176×176` with depth-8-jump callout.
2. **§6.3a Archetype Death Behaviors (Brute Collapse)** — Brute size reference updated from `128×128 at Depth 8` to `176×176 at Depth 8`.
3. **§14.1 Hub and Spoke depth table** — depths 8-10 monster sizes all updated to `176×176` (previously 128, 144, 160 respectively).
4. **§55.2 Guardian Zone Structure** — all 8 Guardian zones updated from `160×160` to `176×176` (matching endgame-scale plateau).
5. **§56.2 The Convergence access table** — Convergence monster size updated from `160×160` to `176×176`.

**Companion CR updates.** CR v5.23 → v5.24 with three surgical reference fixes in existing content: §1.1.1 Visual Identity sprite-size range updated to the depth 8 jump framing; §1.1.2 Imp visual evolution updated from `128×128+` to `176×176 endgame scale`; §2 Brute collapse audio scaling reference updated to match; §8 Brute Collapse creative direction updated to match. Pure reference-consistency edits across existing creative content.

**Zero invariant surface touched.**
- **INV-03 Kill Time Range** — explicitly protected per the revised §6.8 "size is purely presentation" note. Kill-time audit gates at each depth are tied to HP and damage formulas, not sprite size. No §6.5 audit threshold adjustments required; the v12.40 ship changes visual progression without touching mechanical progression.
- **INV-16 Damage Ceiling** — unaffected. Sprite size does not factor into damage formulas.
- **INV-22 Gold Equilibrium** — unaffected. Drop tables are unchanged.
- **INV-28 Quality Drop Distribution** — unaffected.
- All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged.

**Hitbox continuity caveat explicit.** The depth 8 jump means depth 8+ hitboxes are larger than depth 7 hitboxes, so swipe and auto-attack arcs connect to depth 8+ monsters more generously. This is acceptable and intentional — the endgame-scale transition includes the gameplay side-effect of "bigger targets are easier to connect to," which pairs appropriately with the harder-to-kill HP scaling at endgame depths. Documented in §6.8 so the side-effect is not a surprise during tuning.

**No new tuning constants.** Monster sprite sizes live in `zones.json` per-depth configuration and in the monster art pipeline's per-depth size variants — both content-data locations. No §20 additions required.

**No architecture impact.** Sprite size is content data; the v12.40 change is a re-export pass at updated sizes in the existing art pipeline. No new Durable Object, no schema migration, no runtime infrastructure. Architecture stays at v2.14.

**Content authoring scope is bounded.** For an existing sprite rendered at 128×128, upscaling to 176×176 is handled by the art pipeline's existing per-depth size variants. The same sprite design scales to the new size. No new sprite designs, no new animation frames, no new rigging work. The art work is a re-export pass at updated sizes — one task per existing sprite, automatable.

**Extends the zone-as-pedagogy thread into presentation.** v12.32/v12.33/v12.35/v12.37 established the zone-as-pedagogy infrastructure (Codex curricula, Mastery Ladder, late-depth hybrid zones, progressive archetype introduction). v12.40 extends this thread into the *visual* progression curve: depth 8 isn't just mechanically harder (the v12.37 Hexer archetype is fully introduced, the v12.35 hybrid-zone secondary-element content is active), it *looks* harder at a glance because the sprites are dramatically larger. The visual-progression curve now matches the emotional-progression curve — smooth growth through depths 1-7, a transition moment at depth 8, then endgame plateau through depths 9-10. This is a small addition in doc-weight but a meaningful one in play-feel.

**Why depth 8 specifically.** Depth 8 is the endgame-entry depth in the canonical progression (per §14.1) — the depth where Tier 5 legendary resources become available, where the v12.37 Hexer archetype is introduced, where the v12.35 late-depth hybrid zones begin. It's the correct depth to reserve for *the* visual signature moment because it's already the mechanical threshold where the game's endgame content activates. Concentrating visual emphasis at this specific depth reinforces the existing mechanical transition rather than creating a new arbitrary threshold.

**Why one jump instead of two.** An early v12.40 design candidate (reflected in the integration plan's original spec) proposed two visual jumps — one at depth 4 (monsters match player size) and one at depth 8. The revision to a single jump came from the observation that two jumps dilute each other: a player who already experienced one "monsters got huge" moment at depth 4 finds the depth 8 version less striking. A single emphatic jump reserved for the endgame-entry moment produces one genuinely memorable visual transition instead of two medium ones. The depth 4 "player-width match" moment is still called out in the §6.8 table (as a note), but it's a continuous-curve milestone rather than a discontinuous-jump moment. This leaves the discontinuous-jump emotional weight unspent until depth 8, where it lands with full force.

**Depths 8-10 share the same sprite size.** Depths 9 (Darkness) and 10 (Light) both plateau at 176×176 alongside depth 8. This turns the endgame into a visually-unified zone register rather than continuing the linear growth curve. Dark and Light monsters are *the same size* as standard-element depth 8 monsters, which matters because Darkness and Light are thematically the culmination of all elements — not "even bigger than the elemental endgame" but "the elemental endgame itself, at its peak visual scale." Plateau-at-peak is the correct semantic read.

**Why ship this now.** Four design ships past the v12.36.1 review pass (v12.37 + v12.38 + v12.39 + v12.40). The 4-ship cadence is hit; v12.40.1 hygiene pass becomes due next. v12.40 is a natural capstone ship for this cadence — small, self-contained, thematically coherent with the zone-as-pedagogy thread, zero invariant risk. Landing it before the review pass keeps the design momentum moving while giving the hygiene pass one more ship's worth of accumulated cross-reference drift to catch.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.40): Entry 13 sat in the open-entries queue because its surface-level description ("monster sizes") reads as cosmetic rather than experiential. The revision ("single dramatic jump at depth 8 instead of two") is the insight that makes the entry worth shipping — not because two medium jumps are bad, but because one emphatic jump is better. The v12.40 ship concentrates visual emotional weight at the specific depth where mechanical transition already occurs (endgame-entry at depth 8, where Tier 5 resources, v12.37 Hexer archetype, and v12.35 late-depth hybrid zones all activate). The ~57% sprite size jump from 112×112 to 176×176 is genuinely peripherally noticeable — a player doesn't have to be looking at the sprite size to register that the enemies are *different* at depth 8. Depths 9 and 10 sharing the same 176×176 scale turns the endgame into a visually-unified register: once the player crosses into endgame, they stay there through Darkness and Light. This presentation change extends the zone-as-pedagogy thread into the visual layer — v12.32 made zones teach through the Codex curriculum, v12.33 made the Mastery Ladder visible, v12.35 completed zone-in-zone curricula at late depth, v12.37 made monster archetypes a progressive teaching curve. v12.40 makes the *depth progression itself* legible through visual scale: growing smoothly from depth 1 through depth 7, transitioning decisively at depth 8, then plateauing through the endgame. The visual curve now matches the emotional curve. For a small content-data change (sprite re-export pass, no new art), this produces a disproportionately large play-feel improvement — which is the signature of the best experience-first ships.]`

### GDD v12.39

Design-ship — Visible Mana Entropy + Grace Period. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 21, REVISED variant), completing a long-standing deferral from the v12.25 Social Layer bundle. Entry 21 was originally deferred pending INV-02 Mana Sustainability simulation verification of the hold-swipe-vent mechanic; the revised grace-period mechanic sidesteps that concern entirely by bypassing entropy per-collision within a 2-second rhythm window rather than resetting entropy via a separate input gesture.

**The revised design principle — rhythm as the vent.** The original v12.25 draft required players to hold swipe for 0.5 seconds outside combat to manually vent entropy at a 20-mana cost. The revision replaces this entirely with an automatic grace window: collisions triggered within `ENTROPY_GRACE_WINDOW`(2.0s) of the previous collision's status-consume are **grace collisions** that ignore entropy, producing full mana restore regardless of recent collision count. Outside the window, entropy applies normally per existing §3.5 formula. No new input gesture, no separate resource, no mid-combat management — the mechanic is taught through combat rhythm itself.

**New GDD canonical content.** §3.5a Entropy Grace Period and Visibility (v12.39 CANONICAL) — one new subsection extending §3.5 with: revised resolution pseudocode including grace-window branch and v12.38 Resonance Streak integration, two-channel composition analysis (entropy multiplier vs streak multiplier at distinct ledger positions), red entropy overlay on mana bar spec, green grace-tick countdown-arc spec above mana bar, explicit rejection of the not-shipped hold-swipe-vent mechanic, INV-02 analytical verification, upper-bound analysis (`COLLISION_MANA_CD(3.0)` caps collision frequency regardless of grace state), single new tuning constant `ENTROPY_GRACE_WINDOW` (2.0s, range 1.0-3.0). Explicitly notes that `MANA_VENT_HOLD_TIME` and `MANA_VENT_COST` constants from the original v12.25 draft are NOT shipped.

**Zero invariant surface touched — INV-02 analytically preserved.** The grace period **improves** mana sustainability vs baseline (a rhythm-paced player restores more mana per collision than a non-rhythm player). INV-02's failure mode is mana starvation during sustained combat; grace period pushes the system *away* from that failure mode, not toward it. The existing INV-02 bound `regen + collision at entropy floor < swipe spam ceiling × 3.0` still holds — under grace, per-collision restore is higher than entropy-floor restore, so the inequality holds more comfortably. No Monte Carlo re-derivation required; structurally unchanged. The original v12.25 concern was specifically about hold-swipe-vent's unbounded recycling potential (20-mana cost with no cooldown enabled aggressive mana cycling); the grace-period mechanic has no such recycling path because collision frequency is capped by the existing COLLISION_MANA_CD cooldown regardless of grace state. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged.

**Mana-flow design surface completed.** Together with v12.38 Resonance Streak, v12.39 establishes the full mana-flow design system:
- **Streak (§5.7.7, v12.38)** amplifies mana *gain* for resonance-timing skill (up to 1.50× at 5 streak steps, 10s window)
- **Grace (§3.5a, v12.39)** bypasses mana *loss* for pacing rhythm skill (entropy ignored within 2s window)
- Both operate on mana restoration but target distinct multiplier slots (streak bonus vs entropy multiplier), reward different skill dimensions, and compose multiplicatively without double-counting.

A player sustaining both — rhythm-paced AND resonance-timed collisions — experiences the fullest mana-flow affordance the system offers. The mana-bar behavior becomes the flow-state surface visible through play: a rhythm-precise player watches their mana bar stay fuller than baseline during extended combat, which is the authentic ARPG flow-state feel ("tireless," "never running out").

**Pedagogical infrastructure pattern preserved.** The red entropy overlay shows the mechanic's cost to spammers; the green grace tick shows the reward window to rhythm players. Neither state requires explanation; both are legible through play. This matches the v12.29 Quiet Surfaces and v12.33 Mastery Ladder discipline — teach through structure rather than through tutorial. A new player sees the red overlay accumulate when they spam collisions and naturally slows down; a skilled player sees the green tick and learns to chain rhythm-paced collisions for the bypass.

**Resolves long-standing deferred item.** Entry 21 was flagged in the v12.25 Social Layer changelog as deferred pending INV-02 verification: *"Entry #21 (mana entropy grace period) was planned for v12.25 but has been deferred pending an INV-02 simulation verification. Entry #21 will ship as a standalone v12.33 after the simulation confirms or rules out the impact on mana sustainability bounds."* The plan-document note identifying v12.33 as the ship slot was an estimate; actual integration waited until v12.39 because (a) the v12.38 Resonance Streak work this session established the analytical framework for mana-side mechanics preserving INV-02 via skill-reward differentials, and (b) the grace-period revision sidesteps the original vent-mechanic concern entirely. The deferral is now closed.

**One new tuning constant.** `ENTROPY_GRACE_WINDOW` (2.0s default, 1.0-3.0s range). The constant lives in §3.5a per the resource-system-local placement pattern. No additions to §20 required. The v12.25 original's `MANA_VENT_HOLD_TIME` and `MANA_VENT_COST` are explicitly NOT shipped.

**Light architecture touches.** Single ephemeral field addition on player combat state: `grace_window_start: number` (server monotonic time of last collision consume). Resets on death/zone transition along with other combat state, same ephemeral-storage pattern as v12.38's `resonance_streak` field. The existing `entropy_tracker` field is unchanged — entropy still accumulates during grace collisions but is bypassed at mana-restore resolution time. Architecture bumps v2.13 → v2.14 for the combat-state field extension.

**Companion bumps.** Creative Reference v5.22 → v5.23 with two additions: §1.4 UI extension covering the red entropy overlay and green grace-tick visual direction (typography, intensity scaling, opt-in clarity), and §2.4 extension (within the v12.38-authored Haptic Feedback Design section) adding a grace-collision haptic note — a subtle additional tick on grace collisions to communicate the mana-restore bypass through the tactile channel alongside the visual. Architecture v2.13 → v2.14 for the `grace_window_start` combat-state field.

**The combat-sensory recognition thread extends.** v12.38 opened the thread with three sensory channels (mana-flow via Resonance Streak, peripheral-visual via Weapon Edge Glow, tactile via Haptic Feedback). v12.39 extends the mana-flow channel with the grace-period bypass and visibility layer, producing a more richly articulated mana-flow surface. The two systems (v12.38 streak + v12.39 grace) share the same flow-state register: tireless rather than devastating, quiet rather than celebratory, legible through system behavior rather than through announcement.

**Why ship this now.** Three design ships past the v12.36.1 review pass (v12.37 + v12.38 + v12.39). The 4-ship cadence suggests one more design ship before v12.39.1 hygiene pass becomes due. v12.39 is a natural continuation of v12.38's mana-flow work and closes a long-standing deferred integration debt. The thematic coherence between streak and grace makes shipping them close together produce stronger design surface than shipping them separated by other work.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.39): Entry 21 sat deferred for 13 design ships since v12.25 because its original mechanic (hold-swipe-vent) risked breaching INV-02's mana sustain bound through unbounded recycling. The revised grace-period mechanic is strictly better along every axis. It requires no new input gesture — the player's combat rhythm is the vent. It has no recycling path — the COLLISION_MANA_CD cooldown caps the upper bound regardless of grace state. It improves sustainability monotonically vs baseline — no INV-02 breach is possible. It teaches through play — the red entropy overlay shows cost to spammers, the green grace tick shows reward to rhythm players, neither state requires a tutorial. Paired with v12.38's Resonance Streak, it completes the mana-flow design surface: streak amplifies gain on resonance-timing precision, grace bypasses loss on pacing rhythm. The two systems compose cleanly because they target distinct multiplier slots — streak is a post-entropy bonus multiplier, grace is an entropy-multiplier bypass. A player who invests in both skill dimensions (rhythm pacing + resonance window timing) experiences the fullest flow-state surface the mana system offers. The mana bar's *behavior* becomes the flow-state signature — a rhythm-precise, resonance-accurate player watches their mana bar stay fuller than baseline during extended combat, reading the flow state textually through resource dynamics rather than through any explicit game-state indicator. This is what ARPG flow actually feels like in the hand, and v12.38 + v12.39 together produce it through two complementary mechanics that share a single design register (tireless rather than devastating, quiet rather than celebratory). The v12.25 deferral is closed, the mana-flow surface is complete, and the analytical framework established in v12.38 for mana-side mechanics preserving INV-02 is now applied to a second mechanic — which strengthens the pattern as a repeatable design heuristic for future mana-side work.]`

### GDD v12.38

Design-ship — Combat-Sensory Compilation Bundle #3. Three entries from the experience-first integration plan, each small and self-contained, bundled as a coherent "combat-sensory recognition" theme: Entry 17 (§5.7 Resonance Streak, REVISED to mana-restore variant), Entry 5 (§9.2 Collision-Opportunity Status Hint, REVISED to weapon edge glow), and Entry 29 (§15 Mobile Haptic Feedback Layer). All three entries express combat state through distinct sensory channels — mana-flow rhythm, peripheral visual signal, and tactile vibration — without adding new mechanics or interrupting gameplay. The thread matches the observational-warm-without-ceremony voice established across v12.27-v12.37 while operating at the per-second combat-feedback layer rather than the per-milestone layer.

**New GDD canonical content.**

(1) **§5.7.7 Resonance Streak (mana-restore variant).** Consecutive resonance-timed collisions within `RESONANCE_STREAK_WINDOW`(10s) compound a mana-restoration bonus. Each streak step adds `RESONANCE_STREAK_MANA_BONUS`(0.10) to the mana restored by the next resonance-timed collision, capping at `RESONANCE_STREAK_CAP`(0.50, 5-step cap). Non-resonance collision or window timeout silently resets streak to zero. UI feedback: small badge next to combo-count display, fades near window expiry. **No damage bonus** — the mechanic is mana-side only, fully avoiding INV-16 Damage Ceiling interaction. The plan's original damage-amplification draft is explicitly not shipped; the v12.38 ship integrates only the revised mana-restore variant. Per-player, not per-party. Stacks cleanly with the §3.5a mana entropy grace period (shipped at v12.39; grace period reduces mana loss rate; streak increases mana gain amount — two ledger sides, no double-counting).

(2) **§9.2.1 Collision-Opportunity Weapon Edge Glow (weapon-edge variant).** When a nearby enemy (within 20u) carries a status the player's equipped weapon would trigger a collision against, the weapon sprite's edge displays a subtle peripheral glow in the setup element's color. Glow intensity scales with the most-urgent compatible status's remaining duration (stronger glow as status approaches expiry). Maximum 60% alpha — peripheral signal, never attention-grabbing banner. The plan's original status-icon-arrow draft is explicitly not shipped; the v12.38 ship integrates only the revised weapon-edge-glow variant, which avoids battlefield marker clutter and keeps information density constant regardless of combat density. Toggleable via Settings → Accessibility → Combat Hints → Collision Opportunity Glow, with no auto-retirement. Colorblind-safe via the §10.1 shape-first palette's shape-edge rendering.

(3) **§15.2a Haptic Feedback Layer.** Mobile-device haptic motors receive tactile feedback for 12 key combat events: auto-attack hit, critical hit, collision triggered, status applied (by / to player), Grand Slam, Perfect Block success, Godly reveal Stage 3, Hardness 5 success, Death, Mastery Level crossing, Act-transition acknowledgment. Each event has a canonical pattern specification (pulse sequence, duration, intensity) authored in `haptic-patterns.json`. Combat haptics under 120ms each; rare cinematic haptics (Godly reveal) up to 3.5s. Duration budget prevents haptic fatigue. Device detection via Web Vibration API (Android) and WebKit haptic APIs (iOS); devices without haptic hardware silently skip all haptic output. Toggleable via Settings → Controls → Haptics; default on. **Explicitly accessibility-first** — hearing-impaired players gain tactile counterparts for every audio-critical signal (Perfect Block timing, resonance pulse, Godly reveal motif).

**Zero invariant surface touched.** No combat formulas, damage math, stat formulas, economic bands, or mechanical tuning changes. Entry 17's mana-restore revision was specifically designed to preserve INV-16 exactly — the damage-stack ceiling at 3.2× is unchanged, with no Monte Carlo re-derivation required. Entry 5's weapon-edge-glow is purely informational (no damage effect, no timing window change, no collision mechanics adjustment). Entry 29's haptics are sensory output only. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged:
- INV-02 Mana Sustainability — requires verification under streak-active play. See "Invariant verification note" below.
- INV-16 Damage Ceiling — unaffected by any of the three entries.
- INV-22 Gold Equilibrium — no new gold flows.
- INV-24 Wealth Concentration — no new peer-to-peer transaction vectors.
- INV-28 Quality Drop Distribution — unchanged.

**Invariant verification note (INV-02 Mana Sustainability).** The streak mechanic amplifies mana restoration at high skill, which *improves* sustainability rather than degrading it — a chained-resonance player restores more mana, not less. INV-02's failure mode is "mana runs dry during sustained combat"; streak pushes the system away from that failure mode. No Phase-1 validation flag required: the mechanic cannot cause INV-02 to fail at any streak level (a 5-step streak restores 1.5× the baseline mana, which strictly exceeds baseline sustainability; non-streak play restores baseline amounts exactly as pre-v12.38). Structurally unchanged; no simulation re-derivation needed. The mechanic does create a skill-reward differential — players who never hit resonance get baseline mana sustainability, players who chain resonance get super-baseline sustainability — but skill-reward differentials are within INV-02's explicit design tolerance (the invariant protects against absolute mana starvation, not relative mana equality across skill levels).

**Three new tuning constants.** `RESONANCE_STREAK_WINDOW` (10s default, 5s-20s range), `RESONANCE_STREAK_MANA_BONUS` (0.10 per step default, 0.05-0.15 range), `RESONANCE_STREAK_CAP` (0.50 max cumulative default, 0.25-0.75 range). Constants live in §5.7.7 per the resonance-system-local pattern established by §5.7.6. No additions to §20 required.

**Light architecture touches.** (a) `rpg._combat.resonance_streak` field on player character state — small state object `{ count: number, last_resonance_timestamp: number }`, server-authoritative update on every resonance-timed collision, rides on existing combat state payload. (b) Haptic patterns authored in `haptic-patterns.json` content file; no runtime architecture impact. (c) Settings flag `collision_glow_enabled` (default true) — one boolean field extension on player preferences. Architecture bumps v2.12 → v2.13 for the combat-state streak field extension; haptic patterns are content data and the glow toggle is a preferences-schema minor addition.

**Companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.21 → v5.22 with three additions: §8.19 Resonance creative direction extension covering the streak "tireless flow state" register (how the streak badge and mana-restoration feel should read — quiet confidence, not excitement); §1.9.8 Collision Opportunity Glow visual direction (edge-glow rendering pattern, status-timer intensity coupling, shape-texture for colorblind palette); and new §2.4 Haptic Feedback Design covering the 12-event registry with per-event tonal rationale (why Perfect Block is a double-pulse "click" rather than a single thud, why Godly reveal is wave-modulated over 3.5s, why Death is a single heavy pulse followed by silence).

**Continuation of the combat-sensory recognition design thread.** The v12.27 Combat Feedback Visibility ship made combat inputs legible through visual feedback (Active Block, Dynamic Reticle). The v12.38 compilation bundle extends combat feedback into three additional sensory channels — mana-flow (Resonance Streak), peripheral vision (Weapon Edge Glow), and tactile (Haptic Feedback). Together, the two ships establish a full sensory vocabulary for combat recognition: what the player sees at the focal point (v12.27 Dynamic Reticle), what they see peripherally (v12.38 Weapon Edge Glow), what they feel in their tools (v12.38 Haptic Feedback), and what they feel in their resource ledger (v12.38 Resonance Streak mana flow).

**Why ship this now.** Two design ships past the v12.36.1 review pass (v12.37 + v12.38). The 4-ship cadence suggests two more design ships before the next scheduled hygiene pass at v12.38.1 or v12.39.1, depending on how aggressive the ship sequence runs. The v12.38 compilation is a natural pre-review ship: low invariant risk, three self-contained entries, strong thematic coherence, no cascading cross-doc updates beyond the companion CR extensions. The bundle pattern (v12.29 / v12.36 / v12.38) is now established as the compilation cadence alternative to single-entry substantive ships.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.38): Three small entries, one unifying thread — combat recognition expressed through sensory channels beyond the visual focal point. Resonance Streak: the mana system becomes a flow-state channel. Players who chain resonance-timed collisions feel their resource ledger refill faster, a positive-sustain loop that captures ARPG flow-state's actual emotional register ("tireless," not "devastating"). The mana-side framing preserves INV-16 entirely, which is the load-bearing reason for the revised spec — the original damage-amplification draft would have forced INV-16 recalibration, and the revision solves that structurally by operating in a different part of the combat ledger. Weapon Edge Glow: the player's own tool becomes the information display instead of the battlefield. A glow on the hand in the foreground reads as "my tool is ready"; a marker on an enemy reads as "the enemy has been identified for you" — the weapon-glow framing is more empowering and scales better with combat density. Haptic Feedback: the third sensory channel most games neglect, given proper accessibility framing. Hearing-impaired players gain tactile counterparts for every audio-critical signal; all players who opt in get combat's recognition moments felt in their fingers. The three entries share a discipline: additive sensory channels, never required, never interrupting, always peripheral or ambient. Each entry expresses what the game already knows (resonance timing, collision compatibility, combat events) through a new channel without changing the underlying system. This matches the v12.27 Combat Feedback Visibility thread but operates at the sensory-layer layer rather than the mechanic-legibility layer. v12.27 taught the player what their inputs *mean*; v12.38 gives the player more *ways* to feel what the game is already communicating. The cumulative effect across v12.27 + v12.38 is a combat feel that reads across visual, peripheral, tactile, and resource-ledger channels simultaneously — a sensory layering that makes flow state feel genuinely textural rather than abstract.]`

### GDD v12.37

Design-ship — Progressive Archetype Introduction. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 12), extending §6.3 Enemy Archetypes with a per-depth archetype-availability schedule. The seven archetypes defined in §6.3 (Fodder, Brute, Swarm, Sentinel, Volatile, Stalker, Hexer) were previously all available at all depths, scaled by zone-and-level weighting. v12.37 converts this into a progressive teaching curve: each depth 1-7 adds one additional archetype to the zone's standard spawn pool, with depth 8+ containing all seven. Depth 1 teaches Fodder basics, depth 2 introduces Brute (patience-teaching HP), depth 3 adds Swarm (AOE), depth 4 adds Sentinel (element swapping), depth 5 adds Volatile (positioning against death-AOE), depth 6 adds Stalker (lock-on as surprise-counter), depth 7 adds Hexer (attunement matching).

**New GDD canonical content.** §6.3b Archetype Introduction Schedule — one new subsection with: per-depth archetype availability table, renormalization rule (§6.3 relative weights preserved by scaling the available-archetype subset), progressive-pedagogy rationale, first-encounter Codex reveal spec using the v12.33 non-blocking notification format, boss and zone-unique monster exception preserving hand-authored encounter design, explicit interaction with §14.4 Late-Depth Hybrid Zones (v12.35), §6.5 Kill-Time Audit Gates compatibility note, and §25 quest-system interaction flag. Includes full design intent block articulating v12.37 as the capstone of the zone-as-pedagogy design thread started by v12.32 Zone-Paced Collision Discovery.

**Zero invariant surface touched.** No combat formulas, damage math, stat formulas, economic bands, or mechanical tuning changes. Archetype stat multipliers (HP, Dmg, Speed) are unchanged from §6.3. The archetype-distribution percentages (40/15/15/10/10/5/5) are preserved through the renormalization rule — relative weights are unchanged, only the available pool at each depth is restricted. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged:
- INV-16 Damage Ceiling — no new multipliers, no new attack patterns
- INV-22 Gold Equilibrium — same drop rates per archetype; renormalization maintains effective drop values within existing bands
- INV-24 Wealth Concentration — no new peer-to-peer concentration vectors
- INV-28 Quality Drop Distribution — unchanged; quality drops are per-monster, not per-archetype
- §6.5 Kill-Time Audit Gates — audit baseline is Brute at each depth; Brute available from depth 2+; depth 1 (Fodder-only) uses its existing depth-1 pacing target

**No new tuning constants.** The depth-to-archetype mapping is content data (per-zone `archetype_availability` field in `zone-config.json`) rather than §20 tuning, matching the v12.35 precedent where hybrid-zone content data lives in config files rather than §20. The renormalization rule is deterministic (sum available archetype %, divide each by total) and needs no tuning parameters.

**Pedagogical-infrastructure arc capstone for the monster system.** The pedagogical-infrastructure thread across v12.27 + v12.29 + v12.32 + v12.33 made combat inputs legible (Active Block, Dynamic Reticle), discovered-content atmospheric (Encyclopedia Flavor, Quiet Archetype Emergence), the Codex a pedagogical map (Zone-Paced Collision Discovery), and the learning curve visible (Surfaced Mastery Ladder). v12.37 extends this thread specifically to the *monster archetype* system — the seven-archetype roster is now structured as a teaching curve matching the player's depth progression, each new archetype getting its own one-sentence Codex reveal moment through the v12.33 notification infrastructure. Together with v12.32 (zones as curricula) and v12.35 (late-depth hybrid zones completing in-zone curricula), the zone system is now a fully articulated pedagogical environment for teaching combat, elements, and monster archetypes.

**Interaction with §14.4 Late-Depth Hybrid Zones (v12.35) is clean.** The v12.35 hybrid-zone system adds 16 secondary-element variants at depths 7-8 as art reskins of existing recurring forms. The v12.37 archetype schedule governs *archetype availability*, while §14.4 governs *elemental variants*. A depth 7 Ember Fields dungeon can spawn a Basalt-reskinned Hexer (Hexer archetype introduced at depth 7, Stone-element hybrid visual per §14.4) — the two systems compose naturally.

**Interaction with §49.3 Quiet Archetype Emergence (v12.29) preserved.** The v12.29 Quiet Archetype Emergence system governs the player's own visual archetype drift as their combat behavior accumulates. This is orthogonal to the monster archetype system — §49.3 describes how the player's avatar visually shifts toward a Berserker/Mage/Rogue archetype based on their actions; v12.37 describes which monster archetypes spawn at which depths. Both systems use the word "archetype" but describe different things, and §6.3b is explicit about the monster-spawn scope. No renaming needed.

**Companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.20 → v5.21 (new §3 Zone Personality extension notes per zone — which archetype is newly introduced and the thematic framing for its arrival at each depth). Each zone's creative direction in CR §1.2a Zone Mood Guides retains its depth-1-through-depth-8 base identity unchanged; the §3 extension adds a small per-zone table identifying which archetype appears at which depth, with tonal notes on how each archetype arrival reshapes the zone's combat feel.

**No architecture impact.** Archetype availability is content data (`zone-config.json` per-zone archetype_availability field), and the first-encounter notification state rides on existing character-state storage with a new `archetype_encounters_seen` Set field. The Set can live in the same player-state transient storage as v12.33's `certifications_earned` Set (the two fields use identical patterns). Architecture stays at v2.12.

**Quest-authoring constraint flagged.** The v12.37 schedule creates an implicit constraint on future §25 capstone quest authoring: capstone quests that require killing a specific archetype must require it at or above the depth where that archetype is introduced. Spot-checked existing quests at v12.37 integration; no conflicts. Flagged in §6.3b and reiterated here as a standing rule for future quest-content work. Not a mechanical change, just a discipline — existing quest system infrastructure is unchanged.

**Why ship this now.** Three design ships since the v12.36.1 review pass (v12.35, v12.36, and now v12.37) — the 4-ship cadence suggests one more design ship before the next scheduled hygiene pass. v12.37 is a natural fit for the pre-review rhythm slot: low-risk (zero invariant surface, single canonical subsection), strong thematic fit (capstone of the zone-as-pedagogy thread), and self-contained (no cross-doc cascades beyond the companion CR extension). The remaining 27 plan entries include several medium-complexity items (Entry 40 The Trader, Entry 46 Minigames) that would produce more cross-doc cascade work than fits well in a pre-review slot.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.37): The pre-v12.37 archetype system had a tuning gap: an archetype that produced good combat at late depths (Hexer's status application against the player's attunement-carrying armor; Stalker's invisibility against the player's lock-on skill) could also spawn at low depths where the player didn't yet have the gear or skills the archetype's combat design assumed. The per-zone, per-depth tuning workaround would have required extensive manual curation at every zone × depth slot with no clear rule for what to weight where. v12.37 converts this into a system-level pedagogical structure: the archetype roster *expands monotonically with depth*, each new archetype's arrival is a teaching moment (Codex notification), and the teaching curve matches what the player's gear and skills can answer at that depth. Depth 1 is Fodder's power-fantasy zone — teach attack and movement. Depth 2 is Brute's patience zone — teach commitment against big HP. Depth 3 is Swarm's AOE zone — teach spatial awareness. Depth 4 is Sentinel's resistance zone — teach element swapping. Depth 5 is Volatile's positioning zone — teach reading death-AOE telegraphs. Depth 6 is Stalker's lock-on zone — teach pre-emptive target selection. Depth 7 is Hexer's attunement zone — teach armor matching. Depth 8+ combines all seven. This is the curriculum. The first-encounter Codex reveal makes it legible. The renormalization rule preserves the §6.3 archetype weights so each depth still feels like a zone with proper archetype flavor (depth 2's Brute at 27% is a real Brute presence, not an occasional rarity). Together with the v12.32 zone collision curriculum, v12.33 Mastery Ladder, and v12.35 late-depth hybrid zones, v12.37 completes the zone-as-pedagogy design: every depth of every zone teaches something specific, the teachings are visible through the Codex, and the player can track their curriculum progress across all three dimensions (collisions, archetypes, elements). This is experience-first pedagogy at its clearest: teach through structure, acknowledge through notification, never through tutorial.]`

### GDD v12.36

Design-ship — Small-Entry Compilation Bundle #2. Three entries from the experience-first integration plan, each small and self-contained, bundled as a coherent "recognition moments" theme: Entry 38 (Specific Act-Transition Acknowledgments), Entry 39 (Title Showcase), and Entry 53 (Supporter Pass Ferryman Acknowledgment). All three entries add single in-world moments where the game quietly acknowledges something the player has done — an act crossing, a title combination, a first-time Supporter Pass activation — without ceremony, tutorial, or fanfare. The thread matches the observational-warm-without-ceremony voice established across v12.27-v12.35 creative-direction work.

**New GDD canonical content.**

(1) **§53.6 Act-Transition Acknowledgments.** Mayor Bro delivers one brief ambient line on each act transition (6 transitions across the seven-act structure) referencing the specific accomplishment that crossed the threshold. Line pool ~37 lines across 6 transitions, with variant selection keyed to trigger-context (which zones visited, which dungeon cleared, which capstone choice made, Eclipse vs Umbra, Ascendant first-visit, specific Apex boss defeated). One line per transition per character, permanent after delivery, preserved in Journey Log (§27). Queues if player is in a zone at trigger time; delivers on next town visit to Mayor Bro. Mayor Bro chosen as canonical speaker because his §33.7 tone-progression system already trains players to listen to his dialogue shifts; act-transition acknowledgments layer on this without introducing a new narrative surface. The feature is invisible to players who never notice it and textured for players who do — consistent with the v12.29/v12.32/v12.33 pedagogical-through-structure design thread.

(2) **§54.1 and §54.2 Title Showcase slot.** Players can pin one additional earned title as a secondary display alongside the active title. Example displays: "Hero · The Unlikely One", "Sovereign · Gladiator", "Arbiter · Forgemaster", "Ascendant · Voice". The showcase slot is one-and-only-one (not a loadout of 3+), opt-in (players can leave it unset), freely swappable (no cooldown, no cost), and must differ from the active title. Data model extends `rpg._titles` with a nullable `showcase` field; server-validates that the pinned title is earned and is not the same as active. Visual treatment: active title in primary weight, showcase title in smaller secondary weight, separated by middot when horizontal or on a second line when vertical space allows. Migration-safe — all pre-v12.36 characters have `showcase = null` at release, no automatic backfilling.
