(3) **§32.2 Ferryman Supporter Pass Acknowledgment.** On first Supporter Pass activation per account, the Ferryman delivers one unique line on the player's next encounter: *"Thank you. The lights stay on because of you."* Fires exactly once per account — never on renewal, never on subsequent activations. The line does not queue indefinitely; if the player never visits the Ferryman after activation, the line never delivers, consistent with the Ferryman's established register as an NPC who never seeks the player out. Why the Ferryman: he is already the game's sole acknowledger of external forces per §8.5a (*"The gods have left a mark on this. I do not touch what they have touched."*), and that positioning extends naturally to acknowledging real-world support of the game itself. The monetization-to-lore bridge is consistent with his established role rather than invasive; Mayor Bro or Bro Dealer delivering the same line would feel wrong.

**Resolves dangling §53 cross-reference.** The v12.26 §14.3.3 Town Time-of-Day subsection referenced "§53 act-transition acknowledgments" as a sibling NPC dialogue system, but §53 had no corresponding subsection until now. The v12.36 §53.6 subsection fills that reference — it was always planned as part of the §14.3.3 context, and is now authoritative.

**Mayor Bro as canonical act-transition speaker — framework placement.** Mayor Bro joins the designer-authored NPC roster's dialogue-specialization pattern: Mayor Bro handles tone-progression + act-transitions, the Ferryman handles death-fee + gods + Supporter-Pass-gratitude, Bro Dealer handles Gambling Den modes (§28.3). Each designer-authored NPC has a distinctive dialogue register specialization. The v12.36 work confirms this pattern without introducing new named NPCs — all three entries extend existing NPCs' dialogue rather than adding new voices to the town.

**Zero invariant surface touched.** No combat formulas, damage math, stat formulas, economic bands, or mechanical tuning changes. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged. The three entries together add ~80 lines of dialogue content (35 act-transition pool + Ferryman single line + internal spec work), one new player-state field (`showcase` on titles), and one cross-reference fix. No new Durable Object, no schema migration, no balance work.

**No new tuning constants.** Act-transition content lives in `act-transition-dialogue.json` as content-authored data, not §20 tuning. Title showcase has no tunable values — the slot either holds an earned title or is null. Ferryman Supporter acknowledgment is a single trigger event with no tuning. No §20 additions required.

**Light architecture impact — single field addition.** Title data model gains a nullable `showcase` field on `rpg._titles` (§9.5.6 PlayerState extension). Act-transition queued-line state is ephemeral (trigger-time to first-town-visit) and can live in existing player-state transient storage. Ferryman one-time activation line uses existing `first_activation_timestamp` on the Supporter Pass data (already stored per §32.7). Architecture bumps v2.11 → v2.12 for the single `showcase` field extension; the ship is architecturally minimal for the content it unlocks.

**Companion bumps.** Creative Reference v5.19 → v5.20 with three additions: §8.51 Act-Transition Acknowledgments — Creative Direction (tonal specification for Mayor Bro's register — specific, observational, never congratulatory), §1.9.3 Inspect Card Hierarchy extension for the dual-title display typography, and §8.5a expansion covering the Ferryman's Supporter Pass line alongside his existing Godly-weapon line. Architecture v2.11 → v2.12 (§9.5.6 PlayerState extension with `showcase` field).

**Continuation of the "recognition moments" design thread.** v12.33 Surfaced Mastery Ladder gave the game mechanical recognition (Mastery Level tiers + 14 skill certifications). v12.36 extends this into narrative recognition (act-transition Mayor Bro lines), social recognition (dual-title showcase), and emotional recognition (Ferryman Supporter Pass line). Together these four entries produce a consistent "the game notices what you did" design voice, each register tuned to a different kind of doing. No ceremony anywhere — just observation, acknowledgment, and continuation. The player keeps playing. The game keeps noticing.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.36): Three small entries, one unifying thread — the game acknowledges specific player moments without celebrating them. Act-transition acknowledgments: the seven-act structure from §53.1 has existed as an invisible design framework since early versions; v12.36 gives each transition one brief in-world moment through Mayor Bro's voice, respecting the player's capacity to feel progression without having it narrated at them. Title showcase: titles have been single-slot display since §54 was authored; v12.36 allows one companion title as a secondary identity line, which produces stories ("Hero who also walked the unlikely path") rather than résumés. Ferryman Supporter Pass acknowledgment: monetization in most games is either aggressively visible or hidden entirely; v12.36 finds a third path by routing real-world support gratitude through the Ferryman, the one NPC whose established role makes acknowledging external forces narratively diegetic. All three entries share a discipline: the moment happens once, it's specific, it's observational, it's small, and it's done. No fanfare audio, no achievement popup, no quest entry, no banner. The game notices what the player did; the player keeps playing. This matches the pedagogical-infrastructure thread (v12.27 + v12.29 + v12.32 + v12.33) and the atmospheric-variation thread (v12.26 + v12.34) — the game teaches and acknowledges through structure rather than through announcement. v12.36 is the compilation ship that demonstrates how small, focused additions to existing systems produce meaningful texture without introducing new systems. Three entries, zero invariants, one recognition-moments theme, integration complete.]`

### GDD v12.35

Design-ship — Late-Depth Elemental Hybrid Zones. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 16), completing the zone-pedagogy design that v12.32 Zone-Paced Collision Discovery established. Where v12.32 classified every collision pair to signature zones and displayed the full zone curriculum in the Codex, v12.35 extends each zone's late-depth content with secondary-element integration so that the full curriculum becomes discoverable through in-zone play. A player whose preferred zone is Ember Fields can now encounter Flame+Stone collisions entirely within Ember Fields (at depths 7-8) rather than having to find Stone-element applications in Deep Hollows first.

**The hybrid treatment applies to the 8 elemental spokes only.** Shadow Sanctum (Dark primary) and Radiant Heights (Light primary) are deliberately excluded — their alignment-endgame identity depends on primary-element clarity, and hybrid treatment would dilute the thematic weight the Dark/Light endgame depends on. Dark and Light zones remain single-primary through all depths.

**New GDD canonical content.** §14.4 Late-Depth Hybrid Zones — 9 sub-subsections covering: (§14.4.1) element mapping per zone using existing §14.2 primary/secondary pairings; (§14.4.2) visual palette shift spec with Ember Fields worked example (volcanic lava joined by basalt/ashen browns at depths 7-8); (§14.4.3) hybrid monster variants — 16 canonical variants (2 per hybrid zone) implemented as art reskins of recurring Imp/Skeleton/Eye forms with secondary-element visual language, mechanically equivalent to the secondary-element's standard monster behavior; (§14.4.4) shared-dungeon rule — hybrid content lives within existing depth 7-8 dungeons, not separate instances; (§14.4.5) ambient audio layering — thin secondary layer over primary soundscape; (§14.4.6) gem drops remain primary-element only, protecting §4.13 inter-zone gem economy; (§14.4.7) level progression unchanged (depths 7-8 still cover levels 61-80); (§14.4.8) transition to depth 9/Darkness is a clean reset, not cross-zone blending; (§14.4.9) content authoring scope — reskins of existing sprites rather than new art, bounded within art budget.

**Zero invariant impact.** No combat formulas, damage math, stat formulas, economic bands, or combat tuning touched. Hybrid variants use the secondary element's existing status/collision infrastructure; no new runtime mechanics. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged:
- INV-16 Damage Ceiling — no new multipliers
- INV-22 Gold Equilibrium — same drop rates per monster; new variants inherit existing Imp/Skeleton/Eye drop tables
- INV-24 Wealth Concentration — no new peer-to-peer transaction vectors
- INV-28 Quality Drop Distribution — same weapon drop distribution per monster type
- All zone-specific invariants (INV-19, INV-20, INV-21) unchanged

**No new tuning constants.** Hybrid is pure content classification layered on existing mechanics. Monster drop rates, gem drop rules, collision damage formulas, zone level progression — all use the existing §20 tuning constants unchanged.

**Extends the zone-pedagogy thesis.** v12.32 established zones as pedagogical curricula (Codex shows what each zone teaches); v12.35 makes the full curriculum encounterable through in-zone play. Together, v12.32 and v12.35 produce a complete zone-as-pedagogy design: the Codex maps the curriculum, depth-graded encounters teach the zone's primary-element collisions through depths 1-6, and late-depth hybrid content at depths 7-8 teaches the secondary-element collisions that complete the zone's signature list. A player no longer needs to cross-zone grind to complete their zone's Codex section — depth progression within one zone is sufficient.

**Inter-zone economy protected.** The gem-drop-primary-only rule is load-bearing for preserving §4.13 Gem Cutting's zone-specialization design. Stone gems remain the specialty of Deep Hollows; a player farming late-depth Ember Fields gets Flame gem drops regardless of the Stone hybrid content they encounter. The inter-zone supplier-specialization dynamics that the gem marketplace depends on are not touched.

**Companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.18 → v5.19 (§1.2a Zone Mood Guides extended with per-zone hybrid palette-blend specifications, ambient audio layering direction, and thematic framing for each of the 8 hybrid zones).

**No architecture impact.** Hybrid variants are content-data additions (monster-config.json gains 16 new entries with reskin references to existing sprite templates; zone-config.json gains `late_depth_hybrid` field per zone with secondary-element and variant references). No new Durable Object, no schema migration, no runtime infrastructure. Architecture stays at v2.11.

**Content authoring follow-up (v12.35.1).** The v12.35 ship specifies the infrastructure, thematic mapping, and authoritative 16-variant hybrid list. The sprite art reskins, zone palette shader layers, and ambient audio layer authoring are a v12.35.1 content-authoring follow-up. Each hybrid variant is a recolor of an existing recurring-form sprite; the art budget is bounded because reskins reuse animation sets. Zone palette shifts are shader/tint layers on existing environment art.

**Why ship this now.** v12.35 completes the zone-pedagogy thread. The pedagogical-infrastructure arc (v12.27 + v12.29 + v12.32 + v12.33) made combat inputs legible, discovered-content atmospheric, the Codex a pedagogical map, and the learning curve visible. v12.35 extends the Codex-as-map idea into the *world* — the zones themselves now teach their full curricula through depth-graded encounters rather than requiring cross-zone knowledge. This is the last major piece of zone-design infrastructure the integration plan calls for; remaining zone-related entries are smaller atmospheric additions.

**Exclusion decision — Shadow Sanctum and Radiant Heights.** Both zones have "Various" as their secondary element per §14.2, which doesn't fit the single-secondary hybrid treatment. Two options were considered: (A) no hybrid treatment for these zones, or (B) cycling secondary elements through different palettes at depth 7-8. Option A was chosen because Dark/Light are alignment-endgame zones whose identity depends on single-primary clarity; hybrid blending would dilute the endgame's thematic weight. This exclusion is deliberate and canonical.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.35): The zone system has been a pedagogical backbone since v12.32 — zones teach signature collisions, and the Codex maps the curriculum. But depth 1-6 of any zone only naturally produces collisions involving the zone's primary element. A player could see their Ember Fields curriculum lists 17 collisions but experience only ~9 in natural play, with the Stone-involved ones requiring visits to Deep Hollows or Thunder Peaks. This undermined the zone-as-curriculum thesis: the zone *promised* to teach its full curriculum but actually required cross-zone supplementation. v12.35 closes the gap. Depth 7-8 of each elemental spoke naturally produces secondary-element encounters, making every collision in the zone's signature list encounterable through pure in-zone play. A player who wants Ember Fields to teach them the Flame+Stone collisions can now get those teachings from Ember Fields. The zone keeps its pedagogical promise. The 16 hybrid variants as recurring-form reskins is the right implementation choice — each variant is a recolor with new particle effects on existing sprites, keeping art budget bounded while delivering real content-depth increases. The primary-element-only gem drop rule is the second load-bearing constraint: if Ember Fields' hybrid content dropped Stone gems, the inter-zone gem economy would collapse (Ember Fields would become the self-sufficient supplier for Flame+Stone needs), undermining §4.13's zone-specialization design. By keeping gem drops primary-only, we get the combat variety of hybrid content without the economic damage of gem-economy disruption. Shadow Sanctum and Radiant Heights are excluded deliberately because their identity depends on Dark/Light clarity for alignment-endgame weight; hybrid treatment would muddy that. This completes the zone-pedagogy infrastructure. Together with v12.32 (Codex as map), v12.35 (zones complete their curricula at depth), and the v12.26/v12.34 atmospheric work (zones have temporal texture), the zone system is now a richly articulated pedagogical and atmospheric environment. The remaining zone-related entries in the integration plan are smaller atmospheric additions; the infrastructure is complete.]`

### GDD v12.34

Design-ship — Gambling Den Time-of-Day Events. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 35), extending §28 Gambling System with four time-of-day event modes that coincide with the §14.3.3 Town Time-of-Day tonal phases established in v12.26. Where v12.26 gave the town four distinct tonal atmospheres across the server day, v12.34 gives the Gambling Den four distinct *activity* modes keyed to those same phase boundaries — morning (Quiet Individual Gambling), afternoon (Dealer's Hour with Bro Dealer), evening (House Tournament with multiplayer elimination pot), and late night (The Quiet Room 1v1 high-pressure format). The four modes preserve the existing 60/40 house edge through different distribution structures; no invariants touched.

**New GDD canonical content.** (1) §28.3 Time-of-Day Event Modes — full authoritative subsection with seven sub-subsections: §28.3.1 Morning Quiet Individual Gambling (preserves base Double or Nothing), §28.3.2 Afternoon Dealer's Hour with Bro Dealer (named designer-authored NPC + 4-challenge rotation pool, each calibrated to 40% house edge), §28.3.3 Evening House Tournament (multiplayer elimination pot with 4-20 participants, 40% house cut, 70/20/10 payout split to top 3), §28.3.4 Late Night Quiet Room (1v1 Stone/Paper/Shears high-pressure format with 20% house cut producing 40% per-participant edge), §28.3.5 Cross-mode rules and economic integrity, §28.3.6 Tuning reference, §28.3.7 Named-NPC classification note (preserving the §8.12a framework boundary). (2) §20.9 Gambling Event Modes tuning subsection with 7 tuning constants, all calibrated to preserve the 40% aggregate house edge invariant.

**Phase boundary coherence with §14.3.3.** The v12.34 mode transitions happen at exactly the same server-time boundaries as v12.26's town tonal-phase transitions (06:00 / 11:00 / 17:00 / 21:00). This is load-bearing: a player in town at 17:00 experiences the golden-hour tonal shift simultaneously with the Gambling Den transitioning into evening House Tournament mode. The town's four atmospheres and the Den's four activity modes share the same temporal grid, producing shared-atmosphere transitions rather than disjoint schedules. The Den becomes a place with the same rhythm as the town, not a separate system with its own clock.

**House edge invariant preservation.** The 40% aggregate house edge from §28.1's original DESIGN INTENT is preserved across all four modes through different distribution structures:
- Morning: direct win/lose at 60/40 (base Double or Nothing, 40% house edge)
- Afternoon: Bro Dealer's 4 challenge formats each calibrated to 40% expected-value loss
- Evening: tournament with 40% house cut on the aggregated pot
- Late night: 20% house cut on 2-player pot = 40% per-participant expected loss

The math is verified per-mode in §28.3. Any future tuning adjustment must preserve this invariant; §20.9 flags this requirement explicitly.

**Bro Dealer classification — §8.12a framework preserved.** Bro Dealer is a designer-authored NPC comparable to Mayor Bro, the Ferryman, the Tavern Keeper, and the Blacksmith. The §28.3.7 subsection explicitly classifies Bro Dealer as **not** a player-emergent mythology figure — the §8.12a framework (Godly recipients, Hardness 5 owners, Transcendent craftsmen, Tempered-weapon crafters, high-History weapons = six player-emergent categories) is preserved at six layers. Adding a new designer-authored NPC does not extend the player-emergent framework; these are distinct content categories. This prevents category drift that would have happened if Bro Dealer were framed as a "seventh-layer mythology" addition.

**Zero invariant surface touched.** No combat formulas, damage math, stat formulas, or any combat tuning affected. INV-22 Gold Equilibrium is preserved by the constant house-edge rule. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged. The §28 Gambling System's economic sink property is identical to pre-v12.34; the v12.34 addition is purely atmospheric variation in how the sink is experienced across the server day.

**Light architecture impact.** No new Durable Object. The Gambling Den's server-side state already exists (Weekly Jackpot pool, gambling stat tracking). The time-of-day mode state is derived from server clock — no persistent state needed; the current mode is computed as `current_mode(server_hour)` on every Den interaction. House Tournament pot state exists only for the duration of an active tournament (5-90 minutes); persisted to existing gambling-system storage. Quiet Room matchmaking uses a small in-memory queue on the Gambling DO. Architecture stays at v2.11 — the v12.34 ship is pure content + UI expansion over existing server infrastructure.

**Companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.17 → v5.18 (new §8.50 Gambling Den — Creative Direction covering Bro Dealer's wryly observational voice register, the four-mode atmospheric specifications, the 1v1 Quiet Room high-pressure register, the named-NPC roster context). Architecture unchanged at v2.11.

**No new content-authoring follow-up required.** Unlike v12.26's anniversary rituals (per-year thematic assets to author) or v12.32's collision classification (90-pair signature-zone tagging), v12.34 specifies the four event modes and the 4-challenge Dealer's Hour rotation pool as authoritative canonical content in the ship itself. Future content ships may add additional Dealer's Hour challenges or House Tournament variant formats (e.g., themed tournaments on Arena match days); these are additive extensions, not required follow-up.

**The Den joins the town's temporal texture.** v12.26 gave the town four tonal phases; v12.34 gives one specific building within the town — the Gambling Den — its own activity variation keyed to those phases. This is the first building to gain this treatment post-v12.26. Other buildings (Marketplace, Blacksmith, Enchanter) remain mechanically identical at all hours; they inherit only the town's ambient tonal shifts, not their own mode variations. Whether additional buildings should gain time-of-day activity modes is an open design question — the Den's high-atmosphere-low-mechanical-complexity profile makes it an ideal test case; other buildings might or might not benefit similarly.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.34): A gambling den that operates identically at 6 AM and 6 PM is a service counter; a gambling den that shifts with the server day is a place. The v12.26 Town Time-of-Day work gave the town this property; v12.34 extends it to one specific building in the town. The four modes are calibrated to four player moods: morning's quiet solitary gambling for early-riser players who want quick resolution without social overhead; afternoon's Dealer's Hour for players who want a named presence and themed challenges; evening's House Tournament for the prime-time crowd who want multiplayer stakes and competition; late night's Quiet Room for the specific kind of player who stays up to gamble with one other person in hushed tension. The 40% house edge preserved across all four modes is load-bearing — the economic gold-sink property of the Den is identical to pre-v12.34, so no balance work is required, and INV-22 Gold Equilibrium holds automatically. The categorical distinction between designer-authored NPCs (Bro Dealer) and player-emergent mythology (§8.12a six-layer framework) is also load-bearing; blurring these categories would weaken both. Bro Dealer is a colorful service NPC, not a player-emergent mythology figure, and §28.3.7 says so explicitly. The phase-boundary coherence with v12.26 is the subtle structural achievement: a player visiting the Den at 16:58 sees the afternoon mode; at 17:00 sharp, the Den transitions into evening House Tournament at the same moment the town's golden-hour ambient lighting begins its crossfade. The Den and the town share a rhythm. This is experience-first atmospheric design — the player doesn't need to know the schedule; they feel the day's texture by being in its places.]`

### GDD v12.33

Design-ship — Surfaced Mastery Ladder with Skill Certifications. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 15), integrating both the base spec (continuous Mastery Level ladder, tier-crossing notifications with opt-in hints) and the ADDITION (discrete Skill Certifications layered on the ladder). Extends the pedagogical-structure thread started by v12.27 Combat Feedback Visibility and continued through v12.29 Quiet Surfaces Compilation and v12.32 Zone-Paced Collision Discovery. Where v12.32 made the collision system's depth legible in space (zones as curricula), v12.33 makes the game's mechanical progression legible in time (Mastery Level acknowledges what the player has just demonstrated).

**This IS the onboarding.** The load-bearing design choice for v12.33 is that the Mastery Ladder replaces forced tutorials rather than layering on top of them. There is no mandatory walkthrough, no "press X to learn about collisions" dialog, no tutorial mode. A new player starts at Mastery Level 0 and advances as they naturally encounter each tier's mechanics through play. The game acknowledges what they've done after they've done it and offers deeper learning on opt-in. Players who prefer pure discovery can disable notifications entirely via Accessibility settings; the Level progression continues in the background and remains visible on the Inspect Card for identity purposes.

**New GDD canonical content.** (1) §12.1 Surfaced Mastery Level — full authoritative subsection defining tier advancement triggers for each of the 8 learning curve tiers (0, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5, 3, 4), the tier-crossing notification spec (upper-left, 4 seconds, non-blocking, never interrupts combat), the opt-in hint register, the Inspect Card and Inventory display spec, the Accessibility notification-off toggle, and the server-authoritative data model. (2) §12.2 Skill Certifications — 14-certification starting set distributed across tiers, certification earning triggers (all tied to single canonical events), the "what certifications are NOT" list (not unlocks, not rewards, not rankings, not reversible), the Inspect Card certification count display, the content-authoring scope (minimal — no new anti-cheat surface, hooks ride on existing event paths).

**The 14 certifications.** First Grand Slam, First Status Applied, First Collision, First Lunge, First Retreat Shot, First Combo Burst, First Perfect Block, First Combo Spread, First Volatile Collision, First Resonance-Timed Hit, First Combo Chain to 3, First Attuned Defense, First Apex Defeat, First Endgame Capstone. Each ties to a single canonical trigger event (combat resolution, collision firing, gear-state evaluation, etc.) so earning them requires no new validation pipeline.

**Zero mechanical impact, zero invariant surface.** No combat formulas, damage math, stat formulas, economic bands, or tuning touched. Mastery Level and certifications do not unlock content, do not affect combat, do not change player stats, do not gate access. They are pure informational infrastructure — the game notices what the player has demonstrated and displays it for identity and pedagogical purposes. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged.

**No new tuning constants.** The notification timing (4 seconds), notification position (upper-left), and dismissal behavior are fixed UX specifications, not tuning. Each tier advancement trigger is either a binary event (first collision, first Lunge) or a derived event (first attuned-defense requires matching attunement element to incoming damage element) resolved in existing event pipelines. No §20 additions required.

**Light architecture impact.** Character-state schema gains two new fields: `mastery_level` (decimal, one of 0/0.5/1/1.5/2/2.5/3/4), `mastery_level_timestamps` (map of tier→timestamp), and `certifications_earned` (set of `{cert_id, timestamp, context_string}`). All three ride on existing character-state persistence (the same storage that holds player gold, inventory, quest progress). No new Durable Object. See architecture §9.5.10 for the schema specification and architecture v2.10 → v2.11 for the companion bump.

**Companion bumps.** Creative Reference v5.16 → v5.17 (new §8.49 Surfaced Mastery Ladder — Creative Direction with the notification-as-acknowledgment register, the distinction between tier-crossing notifications (bigger events) and certification notifications (smaller marks), the "respectful, brief, never mandatory" tone specification). Architecture v2.10 → v2.11 (§9.5.10 character-state schema extension for mastery_level, mastery_level_timestamps, and certifications_earned fields).

**Why ship this now.** v12.33 completes a four-ship pedagogical arc: v12.27 Combat Feedback Visibility made combat inputs legible (Active Block, Dynamic Reticle), v12.29 Encyclopedia Flavor Text added atmospheric discovery content, v12.32 Zone-Paced Collision Discovery structured the Codex as a pedagogical map, and v12.33 surfaces the game's learning-curve progression as informational acknowledgment. Together, these four ships form a complete pedagogical infrastructure that teaches through structure rather than through tutorials. A new player entering the game now experiences: clear combat feedback (v12.27), atmospheric discovery of encyclopedic content (v12.29), a zone-clustered Codex that shows what each zone teaches (v12.32), and Mastery Level acknowledgment of their progression (v12.33). None of these require forced onboarding; all of them teach through structure.

**Privacy/social identity.** The Mastery Level and certifications appear on the Inspect Card for social signaling — other players in town see *"Adept · Mastery 2.5 · 9 certifications"* when inspecting the character. This is identity-level information comparable to level, class, and guild affiliation. Mastery Level does not identify specific certifications earned (requires tapping through), and no certification earning generates server broadcasts — the information is available to those who specifically inspect but is not pushed to the server at large. This matches the §8.12a "anonymous accomplishments stay anonymous" design principle.

**The ladder plus certifications together.** The Mastery Level alone would be too coarse — only 8 tiers, too big to reflect granular demonstration. The certifications alone would be too granular — 14 discrete badges with no sense of overall progression. Together they form the Duolingo/RPG hybrid: continuous progression (ladder) plus discrete accomplishments (certifications), both visible at different zoom levels on the Inspect Card. A player at Mastery 2.5 with 8 certifications has a visible identity — "I know the collision system, I've done the timing-based stuff, I've been through the Perfect Block mechanic." A player at Mastery 0.5 with 1 certification has a different visible identity — "I'm new, I've just started applying statuses." Both identities are legible without forcing either player through a scripted tutorial.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.33): Onboarding in most games is a gauntlet the game forces on the player before "real play" begins — tutorial islands, hand-holding tooltips, "press X to continue" dialogs. Experience-first design rejects this model entirely. The game's mechanics should be experienced first, named after, and explained only if the player wants the explanation. The Mastery Ladder and Skill Certifications are the infrastructure for this approach: the game observes what the player has done, acknowledges it in a small non-blocking notification, and optionally offers a one-sentence next-thing-to-try hint. That's it. No mandatory dialog, no gating, no "you must complete the tutorial to proceed." Players who want scaffolding get it; players who prefer to discover continue to discover. Both paths remain valid. The 14 certifications corresponding to single demonstrated actions keep the design lightweight — each one is a single server-side trigger on an existing canonical event, so content-authoring work is minimal. The Inspect Card summary gives other players a quick legible identity read on each character without making it a ranking surface: "Adept · Mastery 2.5 · 9 certifications" says "this is what this player has demonstrated," not "this player is better than you." The four-ship pedagogical arc (v12.27 + v12.29 + v12.32 + v12.33) now produces a complete infrastructure for structural teaching — combat feedback, atmospheric discovery, zone-clustered Codex, and Mastery progression — none of which require scripted onboarding. This is experience-first at its most explicit: the game teaches its depth through structure, and the structure is pedagogical infrastructure rather than hand-holding.]`

### GDD v12.32

Design-ship — Zone-Paced Collision Discovery. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 14), integrating the REVISED spec (zone-paced discovery replaces the original hidden-tier discovery framing). The original plan proposed dividing the 90 collision pairs into three discovery tiers gated by hidden conditions — Tier 1 obvious, Tier 2 cryptic conditions, Tier 3 unknown conditions. That framing would have required players to guess or stumble into Tier 2/Tier 3 pairs, creating invisible progression that players couldn't plan against. The revised design eliminates hidden tiers entirely and uses zones as the pedagogical axis — each zone teaches a specific cluster of collisions involving its primary/secondary elements, and the Codex shows the cluster openly (including undiscovered entries as placeholders) so players can see what each zone is trying to teach and track their curriculum progress.

**This is content-design structure, not a runtime mechanic.** No gating, no hidden conditions, no locked entries. Every collision still fires whenever its setup+trigger combination produces it. The v12.32 change is how the Codex *presents* the 90 pairs — zone-clustered rather than flat — and how players understand where each pair is most naturally discovered.

**New GDD canonical content.** (1) §10.5c Zone-Paced Collision Discovery — full authoritative subsection defining the signature-zone classification rule (a collision belongs to a zone's curriculum if either element of the pair matches the zone's primary element, per the §14.2 zone→element table), the Codex display structure (zone headings with discovery fractions, expandable discovered entries, placeholder undiscovered entries), the multi-zone overlap rule (collisions involving two zones' elements appear in both curricula, discovery counts credit both), the discovered-elsewhere annotation, the flat-list Accessibility toggle, the capstone collision handling (Eclipse and Umbra gated at the Convergence). (2) §35.2 Codex Tab updated with a zone-clustered presentation note pointing to §10.5c.

**Zero mechanical impact, zero invariant surface.** No combat formulas, damage math, stat formulas, economic bands, or combat tuning touched. No runtime mechanic changes — collision resolution logic in §10.3, collision damage formula in §10.6, collision ground marks in §10.7, and all 90 collision pair entries in §10.5a/§10.5b are unchanged. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged. This is pure pedagogical-structure work — a reshaping of how the Codex presents existing content.

**No new tuning constants.** The zone-paced classification is content-authored data (new `signature_zones` field per collision in `collision-config.json`), not runtime tuning. No §20 additions required. The content authoring follow-up is a one-time classification pass over all 90 pairs; trivial by integration-work standards.

**No architecture impact.** Codex presentation is entirely client-side on the existing `discoveredCollisions` localStorage data. The zone-cluster grouping is a rendering layer atop existing state; no server changes required. Architecture stays at v2.10.

**Companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.15 → v5.16 (new §8.48 Zone-Paced Collision Discovery creative direction — the "Codex as map, not checklist" framing, undiscovered-entry teaser register, zone heading typography, multi-zone overlap presentation).

**Content authoring follow-up (v12.32.1).** The v12.32 ship specifies the infrastructure and classification rule; the actual zone-tagging of all 90 collisions is a v12.32.1 content ship. The classification is determined by the signature rule (collision's setup-or-trigger element matches a zone's primary element) and is mechanical rather than creative — a Steam collision (Flame+Frost) belongs to Ember Fields (Flame zone) and Frozen Shore (Frost zone); the classification is unambiguous. Eclipse and Umbra are explicitly classified to the Convergence zone per §10.5c.

**The Codex as map, not checklist.** The design intent block in §10.5c articulates the thesis: the pre-v12.32 Codex was a flat list that revealed itself gradually but offered no pedagogical structure — players who stumbled into Steam learned Steam, players who didn't never discovered it existed. Zone-paced discovery changes the Codex from "list of things you've found" to "map of what each zone teaches," letting players play with intention rather than by accident. The 90 collision pairs haven't changed; what's changed is that the game now tells you what it's trying to teach, zone by zone. The collision system is the game's signature combat depth mechanic, and players who don't understand the depth don't engage with it. Zone-paced discovery makes the depth legible without adding mechanics.

**Why ship this now.** v12.32 completes a pedagogical thread across the integration sequence: v12.27 Combat Feedback Visibility gave combat inputs clear feedback (Active Block, Dynamic Reticle), v12.29 Encyclopedia Flavor Text added atmospheric layer to the encyclopedia's discovered content, and now v12.32 extends the encyclopedia's presentation structure to make the collision system's depth legible before discovery. These three ships together form a loose "player-education" arc — the game teaches its mechanics through structure rather than through tutorials.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.32): The game has 90 collision pairs, which is a lot of depth. Players who find and engage with the collision system experience combat at a fundamentally richer level than players who don't. The v12.32 work is entirely about making the depth accessible to players who want it without forcing it on players who don't. Zone-paced presentation is the load-bearing idea: zones are already pedagogically meaningful (each zone's element is already in its identity), and tying collision curriculum to zones exploits that existing mental model rather than inventing a new one. A player who visits Frozen Shore already understands "this is Frost territory"; showing them the Frozen Shore collision curriculum leverages that understanding. The Codex presentation — showing undiscovered pairs by name but not effect — is calibrated: it reveals *what exists* without revealing *what it does*, preserving the discovery moment while giving the player a map to pursue it. This is the opposite of the original plan's tier system, which hid both the existence and the conditions of the pairs. Revealing existence is the right choice because it makes the player's learning intentional; hiding existence makes learning accidental. The zone pedagogy is experience-first in its purest form — the game teaches its own depth by structuring itself around what the player already knows.]`

### GDD v12.31

Design-ship — Marketplace Price Guidance and Flip Disclosure. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 31), completing the three-ship marketplace arc after v12.28 Temper marketplace integration and v12.30 Weapon History marketplace integration. The v12.28/v12.30 ships added new *filter dimensions* (Temper tier, Weapon History counters) that buyers could use to find weapons matching their criteria; v12.31 adds the *informational context* layer that helps buyers evaluate whether a listing's price is fair for its spec. Together the three ships produce a marketplace with dramatically richer decision-making context than the pre-v12.28 marketplace offered.

**New GDD canonical content.** (1) §39.9 Price Guidance and Flip Disclosure — full authoritative subsection with four sub-subsections: §39.9.1 Price Guidance (data and display format, recency window semantics, matching-key precision with fallback chain for rare items, factual-framing-never-editorial discipline), §39.9.2 Flip Disclosure (detection rules, meaningful-state-change carveout distinguishing value-added resale from opportunistic flipping, explicit not-a-flip case table, flip-flag lifetime, successive-flip behavior, anti-exploit discussion of the 24-hour waiting-out strategy), §39.9.3 UI Integration (inline display without navigation, summary-level rendering), §39.9.4 Tuning reference. (2) §20.8 Marketplace Guidance tuning subsection with PRICE_GUIDANCE_SALES_WINDOW_N (30), PRICE_GUIDANCE_SALES_WINDOW_DAYS (7), FLIP_DISCLOSURE_WINDOW (24h), FLIP_HISTORY_DELTA_THRESHOLD (10), all flagged for Phase-1 calibration against real marketplace behavior.

**Factual-framing discipline — the load-bearing design choice.** The original plan framing included editorial labels ("good deal" for sub-average listings, implied "overpriced" for above-average). The shipped spec explicitly removes editorial labels in favor of factual numeric deltas. A listing 20% below average reads *"This listing is 20% below recent average."* — not *"Good deal!"* This discipline is load-bearing: a marketplace that picks winners is not a neutral order book, and the long-term health of the marketplace depends on buyers trusting that guidance is information rather than advocacy. Factual framing respects player judgment and keeps the marketplace neutral between buyers and sellers.

**Flip detection — meaningful-state-change carveout.** The spec distinguishes *opportunistic flipping* (buy, list without changing the weapon's state) from *value-added resale* (buy, meaningfully improve the weapon, then list). The carveout rules check Hardness level, Temper value, applied composite state, enchanted state, and Weapon History counter delta. If any meaningfully changed between purchase and listing, the listing is not flagged as a flip. A player who purchased a weapon and successfully hardened it from Level 2 to 3 is genuinely adding value; the marketplace should not flag their subsequent listing as a flip. The explicit not-a-flip case table in §39.9.2 documents the full set of recognized value-add scenarios. Failed hardening attempts (Temper incremented but no Hardness gain) are intentionally included in the flip-flag set — a Temper-1 Hardness-3 weapon is functionally equivalent to a Temper-0 Hardness-3 weapon under the §4.6c.6 failure-reset rules (both face the same next-failure behavior), so a seller who ran one failed attempt between purchase and listing hasn't added meaningful value.

**Zero invariant impact — pure information layer.** No combat formulas, damage math, stat formulas, economic bands, or marketplace-mechanic changes touched. Fees and taxes unchanged (5% listing fee, 2% trade tax per §23.4 still apply). Order-book matching unchanged (§39.2). INV-22 Gold Equilibrium untouched (no new gold flows). INV-24 Wealth Concentration unaffected beyond what v12.28/v12.30 already flagged for Phase-1 validation (price guidance doesn't create new concentration vectors; if anything, it may modestly reduce concentration by helping new players avoid overpaying). All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged structurally.

**Light architecture impact.** The Marketplace Durable Object (§9.5) gains a per-matching-key recent-sales cache (see architecture.md §9.5.9 for the extension) and a purchase-to-listing flip-detection lookup keyed on `(player_id, item_id)` with 24-hour expiry. Neither is a major addition — both are small aggregations riding on existing marketplace DO storage. No new Durable Object; no schema migration for existing listings.

**Companion bumps.** Creative Reference v5.14 → v5.15 carries UI creative direction for the guidance and flip displays (§1.9.7 Price Guidance UI, §1.9.8 Flip Indicator). Architecture v2.9 → v2.10 extends the Marketplace DO specification with recent-sales cache and flip-detection lookup.

**Completion of the marketplace arc.** v12.28 established Temper as a marketplace filter dimension. v12.30 extended listings with Weapon History counters as additional filter/sort dimensions. v12.31 closes the loop by giving buyers price context for evaluating what the earlier ships' dimensions are worth in the market. The marketplace now has: (a) rich filtering by multiple weapon attributes, (b) biography-level information on individual weapons, (c) price-context overlays helping buyers interpret listing prices relative to comparable recent sales, and (d) flip disclosure for transparency about recent flipping activity. Each layer builds on the previous without redundancy.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.31): Marketplaces mature not by adding more items but by adding more *context* per item. The pre-v12.28 marketplace was mechanically competent but informationally sparse — buyers saw prices, had to intuit from browsing, and routinely overpaid or missed opportunities because they lacked comparison data. The v12.28-v12.30-v12.31 arc progressively enriches the information per listing: Temper tier as a first-class attribute, Weapon History as a biography layer, and Price Guidance + Flip Disclosure as the comparative-context overlay. Each ship is independently useful; together they transform the marketplace from "a list of things for sale" into "a structured trading environment where buyers can evaluate listings against comparable recent activity." The factual-framing discipline is what keeps the marketplace neutral. "15% below average" is information; "good deal" is advocacy. A neutral order book presents information. The flip disclosure follows the same discipline: disclosure, not accusation. A flipped listing displays its flip status; buyers decide what they think of that. Some buyers will avoid flipped listings on principle; others will consider flips reasonable market behavior and buy anyway. The marketplace doesn't take a side. It just tells the truth.]`

### GDD v12.30

Design-ship — Weapon History. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 34), the natural thematic companion to v12.28 Temper. Where Temper gave weapons a record of *failed attempts* (persistence through adversity), Weapon History gives weapons a record of *accomplishments* (kills, bosses defeated, Godly reveals witnessed, Arena wins, Clan Wars, and crafter attribution). Together, Temper and Weapon History form a parallel pair — both per-weapon, both persistent through trades, both visible on the inspect card, both contributing to the "weapons accumulate story" design thread that started with v12.25 crafter attribution and has been extending across subsequent ships.

**No Durability deletion required.** The integration plan described Entry 34 as "replacing Durability," but the current GDD has no Durability mechanic — the word does not appear anywhere in the doc before this ship. v12.30 is therefore pure additive, with no deprecation work required. This differs slightly from the plan's framing but does not affect the mechanic itself.

**New GDD canonical content.** (1) §4.11b Weapon History — full authoritative subsection with counter fields (`total_kills`, `total_damage_dealt`, `arena_matches_won`, `clan_wars_won`) vs log fields (`bosses_defeated`, `godly_reveals_witnessed`, `hardness_successes`, `first_crafter`). Inspect card display with compact 10-item top-line view plus expandable full history. Player-curated starring mechanism with star persistence through trades. Salvage-destroys-History rule (consistent with §18.3 attribution and §4.6c.6 Temper). Orthogonality with Godly lineage (both coexist on Godly weapons). Size management estimates (~100KB for heavily-used weapons). (2) §17.4.3 Weapon History Ledger — anti-cheat subsection documenting the server-authoritative write paths for every counter and log field. Append-only audit. No new Durable Object; rides on existing gear-state validation path, commensurate with Weapon History's economic significance (ordinary-rare rather than transcendent-rare like Godly).

**Critical privacy rule.** The `godly_reveals_witnessed` log field records `{zone_id, timestamp}` only — **no `revealing_player_id`, no weapon name, no identifying information about whose Godly dropped.** This preserves the §8.12a anonymous-accomplishments principle: a weapon records that it was present at a Godly reveal, but does not out the player who received it. The privacy rule is load-bearing and was not explicit in the plan; it emerged necessarily from cross-system coherence with the v12.25 Social Layer design intent.

**`hardness_successes` cross-references §17.5 HardeningLedger** rather than duplicating storage. The v12.28 HardeningLedger already records every hardening attempt per-weapon; Weapon History projects successful entries from it for display. No duplicate writes, no storage bloat from parallel logging.

**Zero mechanical impact.** No combat formulas, damage math, stat formulas, economic bands, or combat tuning touched. Counter increments and log appends happen in existing event-resolution code paths without modifying the resolution itself. A kill is still resolved identically; a boss defeat is still computed identically; only the per-weapon metadata write is new.

**Invariant impact summary.** INV-01 through INV-33 confirmed unchanged structurally. **INV-24 Phase-1 validation flag, consistent with v12.7 and v12.28 precedent.** Like Godly trading (v12.7) and Temper marketplace (v12.28), Weapon History introduces a new price-discovery dimension in the marketplace — high-History weapons may command premium prices, creating a new concentration vector. At modeled volumes, the expected Gini coefficient impact is small but nonzero. The "all invariants unchanged" framing is correct for mathematical invariants, but the real-data validation dependency is explicit: INV-24 should be re-validated formally during Phase 1 playtesting with actual Weapon-History-marketplace transaction data. The pattern is now established: each ship that adds a new peer-to-peer transaction dimension to the marketplace inherits this flag rather than forcing a pre-Phase-1 band adjustment.

**Player-curated starring — subtle design detail.** Stars travel with the weapon through trades. A weapon sold at Temper 80, Hardness 3, with 8,000 kills and 4 starred history entries arrives at the new owner with all four stars intact. The new owner can edit freely (add stars, remove stars, re-star), but the default state on acquisition is the previous owner's curation. Over successive ownership, a high-History weapon accumulates layered curation — each owner's sense of what mattered about the weapon during their time with it. This is a small but important piece: the weapon becomes a collaborative artifact across successive players, not just a tool that accumulates metadata. This design emerged from the integration plan's ADDITION block and was preserved in v12.30's spec as authoritative behavior.

**Companion bumps.** Creative Reference v5.13 → v5.14 (new §8.47 Weapon History — Creative Direction with the "weapons accumulate meaning" framing; §8.12a extension to add Weapon History as the sixth layer of player-emergent content). Architecture v2.8 → v2.9 (§9.5.8 Item schema extended with `history` field containing counters + log arrays + star state; parallel to the v12.28 `temper` field extension pattern).

**Architecture impact.** Minor. The Item data model gains a `history` field (structured object containing counters + log arrays + per-entry star flags). No new Durable Object, no migration required — existing weapons default to `history = null` at v12.30 release time, meaning their inspect cards simply don't show a History section until events begin accumulating. Weapons with accumulated History carry approximately ~100KB metadata at heavy-use peaks; typical active weapons carry <20KB. Storage impact well within practical budgets.

**Six layers of player-emergent content.** With v12.30, the player-emergent mythology framework (CR §8.12a) extends to six distinct categories: Godly recipients (blessing), Hardness 5 owners (commitment), named blacksmiths (forging), named gem-cutters (gem mastery), Tempered-weapon crafters (process mythology via Temper — v12.28), and high-History weapons (biography through accumulated metadata — v12.30). All six scale with the player base for free. The new layer is distinctive because it lives *in the object*, not *in the player's relationship to the object* — a high-History weapon carries social weight independent of who currently wields it, and that weight persists across ownership changes.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.30): Temper made weapons remember their failures; Weapon History makes weapons remember their victories. Together they produce a weapon that has a biography — an object accumulating metadata through play, legible to any player who inspects it, valuable on the marketplace in proportion to its accumulated story. A player who wields a single weapon through their full character lifetime sees that weapon acquire increasingly dense identity — kills, damage, bosses, Godly witnesses, Hardness climbs, Arena and Clan-War wins. The weapon becomes, in a small but real sense, a record of the player's own life in the game. When that player retires the weapon (trades it, sells it, or leaves it in the bank), the weapon carries their record forward. A buyer acquires a weapon whose metadata tells them something about who wielded it before. The starring mechanism is a quiet authorship layer: most players will never touch it, but players who care about their weapons' stories get a small curation surface that makes each weapon's highlights *theirs*. Over years of server life, high-History weapons accumulate layered curation from successive owners — each one starring what they felt defined the weapon during their time with it. The weapon becomes a collaborative artifact. This is player-emergent mythology at the object layer — not "the named player who owns this weapon" but "the weapon itself, carrying its own story across multiple lives." The sixth layer of the lottery-layer mythology framework, extending naturally from the previous five.]`

### GDD v12.29

Design-ship — quiet surfaces compilation bundle. Three small entries from the experience-first integration plan, chosen for deliberate thematic coherence rather than mechanical relationship: Entry 36 (§35 Encyclopedia Flavor Text), Entry 37 (§49 Quiet Archetype Emergence), and Entry 58 (§51 Feedback Author Credit). Unifying thesis: **content that accumulates quietly through engagement rather than arriving through announcement.** This thread extends the v12.25 (accumulated lived-in presence), v12.26 (atmospheric layered ambient), and v12.28 (quiet mythology surface) thematic sequence. The ship is deliberately low-risk after the high-stakes v12.28 Temper ship plus the v12.28.1 review pass — chose a compilation bundle specifically to warm the integration pattern back up without immediately tackling another invariant-touching single entry.

**New GDD canonical content.** (1) §35.5 Encyclopedia Flavor Text — cross-cutting subsection covering all four encyclopedia tabs. Flavor text is discoverable via meaningful engagement (10 kills for bestiary, 3 visits for zones, 20 harvests for materials, 5 triggers for collisions). Authoring pool ~1,900 entries across all categories, phased across three content ships (monsters in v12.29.1, zones/collisions in Phase 2, materials in Phase 3). Narrative register calibrated to Fracture-lore voice — fragmentary, suggestive, never explanatory. JSON schema extension with `flavor_text` and `flavor_threshold` fields. (2) §49.3 Quiet Emergence — archetype visual transformation fires during the next combat action after threshold crossing, interpolating over 0.4 seconds, with a single Journey Log entry as the only explicit acknowledgment. No popup, no HUD flash, no audio cue, no ceremony infrastructure. Archetype drift handled with smooth transition back (no log entry on drift-back). Re-emergence after drift fires the visual transition again without duplicating the Journey Log line. (3) §51.4 Feedback Attribution — feedback items display author names by default with opt-in anonymity. Voice title (added to §54.1) awards at 100+ upvotes threshold. Implementation credit messages fire when developers mark feedback as having shaped a specific change. Anti-cheat rides on existing §17 pipeline. §54.1 gains the Voice row in the Title Hierarchy table.

**Zero mechanical impact, zero invariant surface.** No combat formulas, damage math, stat formulas, economic bands, combat tuning, or invariant calculations touched. INV-01 through INV-33 all confirmed unchanged. This is the lowest-mechanical-risk ship in the integration sequence to date — every change is additive presentation, content, or social-recognition infrastructure. No new tuning constants beyond the content-authoring thresholds (KILLS_FOR_FLAVOR, ZONE_VISITS_FOR_FLAVOR, etc.) which live in `encyclopedia-flavor-config.json` rather than §20.

**No new architecture.** The three entries ride entirely on existing infrastructure: encyclopedia flavor reveal is client-side localStorage extension (existing `bt_encyclopedia_engagement` pattern), Quiet Emergence is an if-check in the existing §49.2 render cycle, Feedback Attribution uses existing §51 Durable Object plus §54 Title data model plus §17 anti-cheat. `architecture.md` stays at v2.8.

**Companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.12 → v5.13 carries the tonal register work for all three entries. New §8.44 (Encyclopedia Flavor tonal register — Fracture-lore-adjacent, voice-less, compressed, oblique), §8.45 (Quiet Emergence felt moment — the peripheral-vision noticing, the Journey Log as quiet record), §8.46 (Feedback Culture atmosphere — named contributors, Voice as recognition not reward, implementation credit as developer-side acknowledgment).

**Content authoring follow-up (v12.29.1).** The v12.29 ship specifies the infrastructure and tonal register for all three entries; content authoring ships separately. The monster flavor pool (~1,500 entries) is the first and largest content ship, prioritized because bestiary engagement is the highest-volume encyclopedia interaction. Zone flavor (~220), collision flavor (~90), and material flavor (~80) follow in subsequent phases. The Voice title award is infrastructure-only at v12.29 — no Voice title can be awarded until real feedback items accumulate real upvotes, which is a post-ship emergent process.

**Why this bundle, why now.** The three entries share the "quiet content" thesis but are otherwise mechanically independent. They could have shipped separately, but bundling them into a single compilation ship is cleaner because: (a) they collectively represent ~150 lines of canonical content, which is below the volume that typically warrants individual ships; (b) the shared tonal thesis benefits from coordinated creative-reference work in a single CR bump; (c) shipping them together documents them as a coherent "quiet surfaces" theme in the changelog, making the design pattern visible for future ships to extend. Compilation bundles are a valid ship shape when the entries are related thematically even without being related mechanically.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.29): The first four ships in this integration sequence (v12.25-v12.28) produced substantial canonical additions touching combat mechanics, economy, mythology, and anti-cheat. v12.29 is deliberately lighter — a bundle of small entries that share a thematic thread but require no invariant work, no architecture work, no cross-system coordination. The bundle's value is the thread itself: across v12.25's ambient social visibility, v12.26's atmospheric layering, v12.28's Temper as the "quietest mythology surface," and now v12.29's encyclopedia flavor + quiet archetype emergence + named feedback, a consistent design voice has emerged — *content accumulates through engagement, not through announcement*. Players who spend time with a monster unlock its flavor text; players who train specific stats see their archetype emerge during combat; players whose feedback resonates earn a title through peer recognition. All three paths are earned by playing the game rather than by completing a ceremony the game presents. The design intent of this ship is less about any single entry and more about making the thread itself visible in the doc — this is what "experience-first" looks like in compound form. Not every ship needs to be high-stakes. Some ships exist to extend a voice.]`

### GDD v12.28

Design-ship — Temper. Single entry from the experience-first integration plan (Entry 33), but the single most consequential entry in the whole plan. The integration plan's original draft for Entry 33 proposed a Temper mechanic that *boosted success rates* at high Temper values (e.g., guaranteed Hardness success at Temper 200+). The plan's revision pass replaced that approach with a fundamentally different mechanic: **Temper softens failure penalty without modifying success rates.** The v12.28 ship integrates the revised mechanic exclusively; the original Temper-boosts-success-rate draft is not shipped in any form.

**The mechanic, in one paragraph.** Every weapon has a `temper` field that counts failed hardening attempts. At Temper 0-19, failed attempts reset Hardness to 0 (the canonical pre-v12.28 behavior). At Temper 20-49, failed attempts reset Hardness by −2 instead. At Temper 50-99, failed attempts reset by −1. At Temper 100+, failed attempts preserve Hardness entirely (the weapon does not lose Hardness on failure, though gold and materials are still consumed). A successful hardening attempt resets Temper to 0. Temper persists through all trades, sales, and the weapon's lifetime; salvage destroys the weapon including its Temper. Raw success rates at every Hardness step are unchanged (80/20/5/1/0.5%).

**Why the revision was the right call — the design reasoning.** The original Temper draft had an intuitive appeal: reward players who commit to a weapon through many attempts by giving them a better shot at eventually succeeding. The problem is that "eventually guaranteed" is mathematically indistinguishable from "grindable," and Hardness 5 as a grindable endgame achievement is a completely different mechanic from Hardness 5 as a server-rare lottery. Under the original Temper, a player with enough gold and patience would be able to reach Hardness 5 with near-certainty after ~200 failed attempts at the 4→5 step. That destroys the named-individual mythology the Hardness ladder is designed to produce. A Hardness 5 weapon that anyone with enough gold can eventually achieve is cosmetic pretense, not mortal lottery.

**The revised mechanic preserves the mythology completely while still delivering the experience-first intent.** The problem the integration plan identified — that Hardness 4→5 attempts feel psychologically brutal because a 99.5% failure rate combined with full-reset-to-0 produces a catastrophic-loss experience — is addressed by softening the *failure penalty*, not the *success rate*. A player who has accumulated Temper 100 on a weapon can keep attempting indefinitely without watching their Hardness cascade back to 0 on every failure. Each attempt still costs real resources, each attempt still has only 0.5% raw chance, but the catastrophic-loss failure mode is softened. The mythology — that Hardness 5 is rare, earned by a handful of players through real persistence or real luck — stays intact.

**What preserves what:**
- **Lottery euphoria preserved.** Raw odds unchanged. Most Hardness 5s will still come from early-attempt lucky hits rather than from grinding Temper.
- **Named-individual mythology preserved.** Hardness 5 remains as rare as v12.24 specifies. 5-10 per year per 1,000-player server cadence holds exactly.
- **Grit-expressed-as-persistence preserved.** A player who fails 100 times on a weapon has accumulated Temper 100+ and their weapon no longer catastrophically resets. Each additional attempt costs resources but doesn't lose ground.
- **Tempered-weapons-as-marketplace-category preserved.** A weapon with Temper 60 at Hardness 3 is more valuable than a fresh weapon at Hardness 3 — the partial-reset protection has real gold-cost value. Buyers pay a premium for risk management, not probability manipulation.

**What changes vs. the original:**
- **No guaranteed success via grinding.** There is no Temper threshold at which an attempt is certain to succeed. The lottery is sacred.
- **The 200+ tier becomes "attempts never cost you hardness," not "this attempt is free."** The difference is the entire design.

**Invariant impact — the revision's best property.** INV-27 (Hardness 5 Creation Rate) is **unchanged** under v12.28. The original Temper draft would have shifted the creation rate projection from 1.25-2.5 per 90-day window to 5-10 per 90-day window (because guaranteed-at-Temper-200 creates a bulk path to Hardness 5), requiring the cap to move from ≤10 to ≤12 or higher. The revised Temper leaves the creation rate mathematically identical to v12.24's projection. The ≤10 cap holds with the same headroom it had pre-v12.28. **No Monte Carlo re-derivation required.** The revision was specifically designed to produce this outcome — a more forgiving mechanic that does not require retuning the rest of the system. Every other invariant (INV-01 through INV-33) is likewise confirmed unchanged; Temper does not touch combat math, damage pipelines, stat budgets, economy bands, mana sustainability, or any other system with an invariant.

**INV-24 Phase-1 validation flag.** Like the v12.7 Godly marketplace addition and the v12.6 gem-cutting service economy, v12.28 Temper marketplace (§39.8) introduces a new peer-to-peer transaction category with potentially-premium pricing for high-Temper weapons. Gold flows involved are peer-to-peer (INV-22 Gold Equilibrium is untouched — gold neither generated nor destroyed), but the concentration vector is new: high-Temper weapons may concentrate in specific hands if certain players specialize in accumulating them. The theoretical pattern is the same as existing weapon-marketplace concentration (wealthy buyers acquiring high-value items), amplified modestly by the new filter dimension. At the currently-modeled volumes (~5-10% of weapon listings carry Temper >20 after first year of ship), the expected Gini-coefficient impact is small but nonzero — similar in magnitude to gem-cutting services. **INV-24 should be re-validated formally during Phase 1 playtesting with actual Temper-marketplace transaction data.** The model's tentative finding is that INV-24 holds with thin margin but the ≥Phase 1 validation is required to confirm. Consistent with v12.7 and v12.6 practice, this is flagged here rather than forcing a pre-Phase-1 band adjustment.

**Marketplace integration.** §39.8 adds Temper as a searchable/sortable/filterable field on weapon listings. Temper is inspect-only visibility (not broadcast like Hardness 5's prismatic numeral); but marketplace listings show it prominently, and the listing text includes a human-readable tail computing the effective failure behavior ("next fail preserves Hardness at 2"). Buyers can filter by `temper_min`, `temper_max`, `temper_tier`, or `failure_depth`, all through the existing §39.2 order-book index with no new infrastructure. Price discovery emerges from the market naturally — Temper 100 weapons at Hardness 4 attempting 4→5 may become the most-valued weapon category in the economy, but the marketplace is allowed to price them as it will. No Temper-specific price floors, caps, or validation.

**Named-craftsman mythology extended.** CR §8.12a gains framing for Tempered-weapon crafters. The mythology shifts from "Bro Mason's weapons fail less" (which would have been the original draft's framing) to "Bro Mason's weapons fail more gracefully." The crafter's persistence is visible in the Temper value — the weapon carries the record of attempts in its metadata. A Transcendent Blacksmith known for sustained work on a weapon produces weapons with genuinely high Temper values when they come onto the market. This is named-craftsman content expressed through persistent state on the item itself.

**Anti-cheat integration (§17.5).** The `temper` field is server-authoritative; clients cannot fabricate, modify, or spoof Temper values. The HardeningLedger append-only audit log records every successful and failed attempt with full weapon state before and after, so any disputed Temper value can be reconstructed. Temper rides on the existing gear-state anti-cheat path — no new cryptographic infrastructure like Godly provenance. This is "mortal systems for mortal lottery" by design.

**Architectural impact.** Minor. The Item data model gains a single `temper: UInt16` field (architecture.md §9.5.5 schema extension, bumping architecture to v2.8). No new Durable Object, no migration — existing weapons default to Temper 0 at v12.28 ship time. The HardeningLedger is a scoped addition to existing weapon-state persistence, not a new DO instance.

**Companion doc bumps.** Creative Reference v5.11 → v5.12 (§8.12a extension with Tempered-weapon mythology framing; new §8.43 creative direction for the Temper visual register on inspect cards). Architecture v2.7 → v2.8 (§9.5.5 Item schema extension with the `temper` field). Both companion docs update in sync with GDD v12.28.

**New tuning constants (§4.6d).** TEMPER_THRESHOLD_T1 (20, range 10-30) — partial-reset threshold, failed attempts reset to `max(0, current - 2)`. TEMPER_THRESHOLD_T2 (50, range 30-80) — near-preservation threshold, failed attempts reset to `max(0, current - 1)`. TEMPER_THRESHOLD_T3 (100, range 80-200) — no-reset threshold, failed attempts preserve current Hardness. TEMPER_RESET_DEPTH_T1 (−2, fixed). TEMPER_RESET_DEPTH_T2 (−1, fixed). All five are below-thresholds that gate the softening behavior; the Hardness ladder success rates themselves remain at their v12.24 values and are not touched.

**Deprecated from original draft:** TEMPER_BONUS_T1/T2/T3/T4 (the success-rate boost constants) and TEMPER_THRESHOLD_T4 (the guaranteed-success threshold) do **not** exist in the shipped mechanic. They were part of the original Temper draft and were deleted during the revision pass. Any doc reference to Temper success-rate bonuses is pre-revision and does not represent shipped behavior.

**Why ship Temper as a solo bundle.** Temper is the most design-loaded entry in the integration plan — it touches combat mechanics (§4.6c, §4.6d), invariants (§22 INV-27 confirmation), economy (§23/§39 marketplace), anti-cheat (§17.5), creative direction (CR §8.12a/§8.43), and architecture (§9.5.5 schema extension). Shipping it bundled with other entries would obscure the Temper-specific reasoning in the changelog and make rollback harder if something about the mechanic surfaces a problem in Phase 1 playtest. Temper deserves its own commit with a full design-reasoning trail.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.28): The Hardness ladder pre-v12.28 was mathematically sacred (rare lottery producing server-rare content) but psychologically brutal at the endgame tiers. A player attempting 4→5 faces a 99.5% chance of watching four successful upgrades evaporate in one click, and the catastrophic-loss failure mode is the reason Hardness 5 pursuit often feels like masochism rather than commitment. Temper threads the needle cleanly: raw odds are preserved entirely (the mythology is sacred), while the failure penalty is softened for players who have actually invested in the weapon. Each tier of the failure-reset reduction (0 → −2 → −1 → no-reset) is earned through actual attempts, at actual cost, on a specific weapon. Temper is not "failure forgiveness" in the sense of mercy — it is "commitment credit" in the sense that the weapon remembers. The marketplace implication is that Tempered weapons are a legitimate economic category, priced for risk management rather than probability manipulation. The mythology shift — "Bro Mason's weapons fail more gracefully" instead of "fail less" — is both more honest to the system and richer as content: a high-Temper weapon carries the visible record of the crafter's persistence without changing the crafter's mathematical advantage. Two players attempting Hardness 5 on fresh weapons have the same odds; the difference emerges through investment over time, not through hidden success-rate multipliers. This is the v12.28 commitment: Hardness 5 remains a mortal lottery, and Temper is the mechanism by which mortal persistence becomes visible in the object itself.]`

### GDD v12.27

Design-ship — combat feedback visibility. Two revised entries from the experience-first integration plan: Entry 3 (§5.3 Perfect Block, REVISED to Active Block) and Entry 18 (§5.8 Pre-Swipe Interpretation, REVISED to Dynamic Reticle). Both revisions replaced their originally-drafted specs with fundamentally-different mechanics: Active Block converts block from a held stance into a tap-at-impact committed action with three timing outcomes (perfect/standard/miss); Dynamic Reticle converts the contextual dodge teaching system from a touch-down radial diagram into a continuous real-time reticle that follows the drag direction. Both reshape combat-UX meaningfully — v12.27 is the first ship in this integration sequence that changes how combat inputs *feel*, not just how the world looks around them.

**New GDD canonical content.** (1) §5.3b Active Block — new authoritative subsection with the three timing outcomes, mechanic pseudocode, eligibility rules (direct attacks yes, swipe triggers yes, reflected damage yes; DoT no, collision no, environmental no), perfect-block full-threat preservation for Tank identity, PvP symmetry, one-tap-one-block consumption rule, BLOCKING state deprecation. (2) §5.8.7 Dynamic Reticle — new subsection with three reticle states (red-on-target for Lunge, blue-arrow-away for Retreat Shot, grey-tick for standard dodge), continuous drag-time updating, release-commits-to-current-state, auto-retirement threshold of 100 successful contextual dodges, accessibility via shape+position encoding beyond color alone. (3) §20.1 Timing and Input gains four new constants: PERFECT_BLOCK_WINDOW (0.15s), BLOCK_COMMITMENT_WINDOW (0.4s), PERFECT_BLOCK_STAM_REFUND (10), CONTEXTUAL_DODGE_HINT_AUTO_OFF (100).

**Cross-section updates integrated.** §2.3 Fortification Derivatives block formula note updated to clarify that `block_reduction` applies to the Standard Block outcome tier only; Perfect Block (§5.3b) produces full damage negation rather than scaled reduction. §5.4 Player States table deprecation note for BLOCKING state (the pre-v12.27 held-stance state). §8.2 Damage Pipeline P10 Block interceptor logic implements the three-tier resolution.

**Invariants confirmed unchanged.** INV-02 (Mana Sustainability) — block doesn't touch mana. INV-16 (Damage Ceiling) — Perfect Block reduces to zero, which is strictly better than existing block-reduction cap; no ceiling violation possible. INV-29 (Godly Integrity) — Perfect Block works identically on all weapon rarities including Godly; no Godly-specific behavior. All 33 invariants confirmed unchanged.

**Backward compatibility.** The deprecated `BLOCKING` player state value is retained in the Appendix A enum registry so that pre-v12.27 serialized save data doesn't break on load. The game never enters this state during play; no systems key on it. Appendix A treats the value as a deprecated placeholder.

**Tuning sensitivity flag.** PERFECT_BLOCK_WINDOW at 0.15s is tight. Phase 1 playtest may need to calibrate within the 0.10s–0.20s range. Too tight → perfect blocks feel impossible (discourages Active Block engagement). Too loose → perfect blocks become reliable rather than skill-expressive (removes the "committed decision" design intent). Suggested playtest protocol: 50+ players × 2 hours of combat × variance on 0.12 / 0.15 / 0.18 / 0.20 → measure perfect-block hit rate distribution; target is that moderately-skilled players land 20-40% of attempted perfect blocks and highly-skilled players land 60-80%.

**Triggered companion bumps.** Creative Reference v5.10 → v5.11 for Active Block and Dynamic Reticle creative direction (perfect-block metallic chime, gold flash on weapon, felt moment of committed choice; reticle visual register of subtle/informative/never-distracting). Architecture unchanged at v2.7 — both mechanics are client-side input handling flowing into the existing server-authoritative damage pipeline.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.27): v12.25 shipped social layer visibility. v12.26 shipped atmospheric presence. v12.27 shifts to combat inputs — the first bundle in this integration sequence that changes what the player's fingers do rather than what their eyes see around them. Active Block is the single highest-leverage change: block converts from a passive stance into an active decision, and the three-tier outcome (perfect/standard/miss) gives defensive play the same skill-expressive shape dodge already has. A Tank reading a Brute Collapse and perfect-blocking it produces the most heroic single-input moment in the game's combat — damage negated, threat held, party saved, stamina refunded. Dynamic Reticle is a teaching layer that auto-retires — it shows new players the interpretation rules during drag, then disappears once the player has internalized them. Both mechanics preserve existing systems rather than replacing them: §2.3 block_reduction still applies (for Standard Block), §8.2 damage pipeline still owns resolution, §11 threat still generates (at full amount for perfect blocks), §5.8 contextual dodge system still exists (Dynamic Reticle is feedback on top, not a replacement). The combat layer becomes more skill-expressive without being rebuilt. This is v12.27's shape.]`

### GDD v12.26

Design-ship bundle — environmental persistence + atmospheric polish. Nine entries from the experience-first integration plan, spanning what the plan called "v12.28 Environmental Persistence" (Entry 7 ground mark tiered lifetimes, Entry 9 zones leave marks on players, Entry 10 fracture lore ambient audio, Entry 28 town time-of-day variation) and "v12.29 Atmospheric Polish" (Entry 2 distinct resource bar behaviors, Entry 6 Bless heal wave animation, Entry 8 resistance damage tiered visuals with audio addition, Entry 19 Thorns pack-flash, Entry 32 anniversary ritual). The v12.26 ship consolidates both bundles into a single sequential version bump since they share the "the world feels more alive" thesis and have zero invariant surface in common.

**Note on bundle numbering.** The integration plan at `/docs/plans/experience-first-integration-plan.md` referred to these as bundles v12.28 and v12.29. The actual canonical version is v12.26 — sequential from v12.25's social layer ship. The plan's bundle labels were provisional planning names; actual ship version numbers advance sequentially with the document's history. Future bundles in the plan (v12.30 collision discovery, v12.31 monetization texture, v12.32 convergence sharpening) will similarly map to sequential version numbers during integration. The mapping between plan bundle labels and actual ship versions is tracked in `CHANGELOG.md` pointer entries.

**Zero mechanical changes, zero invariant impact.** No combat formulas, damage math, stat formulas, economic bands, or combat tuning are touched. INV-01 through INV-33 all confirmed unchanged. The bundle is entirely additive — GDD changes are either brief references to creative-reference.md for visual/audio specs, or expansions of previously-stub sections (§50.1 Anniversary Ritual) that were explicitly awaiting specification.

**GDD changes:** (1) §3 adds the resource bar distinct behaviors reference (HP shake on damage, stamina breathe rhythm with movement, mana ripple on collision restore; 60% intensity dampening when 2+ bars animate simultaneously). (2) §8.3 adds the Thorns Pack-Flash reference (green aura + spike particles when 3+ enemies hit a Thorns-active target). (3) §9.4 adds the Bless heal wave visual + audio reference (gold radial wave, warmer heal numbers, distinct chime; visible to all PvP observers). (4) §10.7 Cosmetic Ground Marks is rewritten with tiered lifetimes — Tier A Casual (10 min) for standard collisions, Tier B Volatile (60 min) for volatile pairs with preserved crackle visual, Tier C Capstone (6 hr) for Eclipse and Umbra only. (5) §13 adds the resistance damage tier reference (standard/resisted/immune visual + audio differentiation, symmetric for player-taken damage). (6) §14.3.3 adds Town Time-of-Day Variation (four tonal phases — morning, afternoon, evening, late night — using server time for cross-player shared memory).
