(7) §20.6 is a new tuning subsection for Visual Persistence constants. (8) §26 adds the zones-leave-marks-on-players reference before the per-zone mechanics subsections. (9) §33.1 adds the Zone Emotional Audio Layer reference. (10) §50.1 Anniversary Ritual is the one substantive expansion — the §50 stub gains a full ritual specification: window-opening server announcement, window-active town decoration + ambient audio layer, five-minute closing warning, midnight synchronized decoration fade with resolving chime, annual repetition with fresh thematic identity per year, zero gameplay effects. All other GDD changes are single-sentence references to `creative-reference.md`.

**Triggered companion bump.** Creative Reference v5.9 → v5.10 carries the substantial creative-direction load for this ship: per-zone emotional audio layer specification in §2, resource bar behavior visual and audio spec in §1.4, damage number resistance tier presentation in §1.4, Bless heal wave feel in §8, ground mark decay register per tier in §8, Thorns Pack-Flash creative direction in §8, anniversary ritual feel in §8, zone marks on players per-zone specification in §1.7, town time-of-day phase specifications in §1.2a. The CR ship is large but coherent — a single-theme creative-direction pass matching the GDD's atmospheric additions.

**Architecture.md unchanged.** No new Durable Objects, no schema changes, no server-side state. All v12.26 additions are client-side rendering (ground mark tiered rendering, zone mark character sprite layering, resource bar animation tweens, time-of-day lighting crossfade, anniversary ritual decoration layer) or content-authored (emotional audio layer tracks, phase-exclusive NPC lines, per-year anniversary thematic assets). Architecture stays at v2.7.

**New tuning constants (§20.6).** GROUND_MARK_LIFETIME_CASUAL (10 min), GROUND_MARK_LIFETIME_VOLATILE (60 min), GROUND_MARK_LIFETIME_CAPSTONE (6 hr), GROUND_MARK_FRESH_WINDOW (2 min), ZONE_MARK_APPEARANCE_DELAY (30s), ZONE_MARK_PRONOUNCED_TIME (2 min). **Deprecated:** GROUND_MARK_LIFETIME (uniform, superseded by tiered), GROUND_MARK_ECLIPSE_LIFETIME (subsumed into CAPSTONE).

**Content authoring follow-up (v12.26.1).** Three content pools are specified in v12.26 but authored in a follow-up content ship: (1) per-zone emotional audio tracks for all 10 zone types (~10-15 tracks at ~30-60 seconds each), (2) phase-exclusive NPC lines for the town time-of-day system (~2-3 lines per NPC × 4 phases × ~10 NPCs ≈ 80-120 lines), (3) thematic visual and audio assets for the Year 1 and Year 2 anniversary rituals (banners, ambient layers, closing chimes — per anniversary item). The infrastructure (time-of-day lighting system, mark persistence tiering, ritual decoration layer) ships in v12.26 itself; the content pools that feed the infrastructure ship in the content update.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.26): v12.25 converted social surfaces from menu-driven to ambient-visible. v12.26 extends the same "show through the world" thesis to environmental and atmospheric surfaces — ground marks persist as layered zone history, zones leave their mark on players, town breathes with server time, resource bars communicate state through peripheral-vision animation, damage numbers carry tier information through shape and sound. No single change is dramatic. Together, the ship significantly densifies the moment-to-moment world presence. A player in v12.26 walks through a zone and reads recent activity from ground marks (different ages visible as different intensities), sees fellow players carrying zone marks from their recent travels, hears the zone's emotional undertone after long sessions, and notices their own resource state through the UI's behavior rather than by looking at numbers. The Anniversary Ritual is the one piece of this ship that ties to a specific event rather than ambient feel, but the ritual's design inherits the same restraint: no announcements, no UI countdowns, just decoration and ambient audio shifts that the player reads as the world acknowledging that something temporary is happening. This is the shape experience-first ships take — the game becomes more alive not through feature additions but through presence additions.]`

### GDD v12.25

Design-ship bundle — social layer pass. Seven entries from the experience-first integration plan: #22 Vows (shrine ceremony + permanent inspect card vow record), #23 per-fight role titles + long-term contribution signature, #24 permanent crafter attribution with portfolio and milestone recognition, #25 Clan Hall as collaborative gallery, #26 friend list activity display, #27 friend/clan leaderboard scoping with personal defaults, #30 ambient NPC gossip replacing UI news ticker. All seven changes share a unifying design intent: convert systems that were previously either menu-driven or invisible into socially-visible, persistent, and characterizing surfaces. Stat locks become ceremonies with server-wide public record. Contribution XP produces characterization (role titles) and emergent identity (signature). Crafted items become identity-carrying objects with crafter visibility forever. Clans become places where history physically accumulates. Friends become a social compass for finding each other in the moment. Leaderboards default to personal-scale peer competition. Server events surface through characters in the world rather than through UI chrome.

**Zero mechanical changes.** No combat formulas, no damage math, no stat formulas, no economic bands, no invariants affected. INV-02 (mana sustainability), INV-16 (damage ceiling), INV-22 (gold equilibrium), INV-24 (wealth concentration), INV-26 (stat budget), INV-27 (Hardness 5 creation rate), INV-29 (Godly integrity), INV-30 (validator band drift) all confirmed unchanged. No new invariants required. The ship is entirely additive to the game's social infrastructure; no existing mechanic's numeric behavior changes.

**Why these seven ship together.** The unifying theme (social layer) means each entry benefits from the others' presence. The Vows Shrine's Recent Vows display becomes richer when the inspect card also shows permanent vow history (cross-surface reinforcement). Crafter attribution becomes more meaningful when Clan Halls can display donated attributed items (a Transcendent crafter's work appears in their clan's hall as a visible legacy). NPC gossip references events that friend activity display makes findable (a friend whose gossip-worthy event just fired might be findable via their current location). Role titles and signature shape inspect-card content that's now more meaningful socially because leaderboards default-scope to the player's friends and clan — people who already know each other's signatures. Shipping any one of the seven without the others would work but produce a fractional version of the social upgrade; shipping the seven together produces a coherent social-layer redesign.

**Deferred from this bundle.** Entry #21 (mana entropy grace period) was planned for v12.25 but has been deferred pending an INV-02 simulation verification. Entry #21 will ship as a standalone v12.33 after the simulation confirms or rules out the impact on mana sustainability bounds. No other integration plan entries were dropped from their originally assigned bundle; the remaining 6 are all present.

**Content authoring follow-up (v12.25.1).** Three content pools are specified in v12.25 but authored in a follow-up content-ship: (1) the ~30-title role title pool with contribution-shape matching logic (`role-titles.json`), (2) the ~200-line initial NPC gossip library covering 7 event categories (`npc-gossip.json`), (3) creative direction (in CR v5.9) for the visual and audio register of each new system. The infrastructure (per-fight title assignment logic, gossip event-to-NPC assignment system, Clan Hall rendering, attribution field on items) ships in v12.25 itself; the content pools that feed the infrastructure ship in the immediately following content update.

**Triggered companion bumps.** Creative Reference v5.8 → v5.9 (creative direction entries for Vows Shrine ceremony feel, NPC gossip tone per NPC, role title flavor, Clan Hall atmosphere, crafter portfolio presentation). Architecture v2.6 → v2.7 (new Durable Object state for Vows Shrine Recent Vows log, per-clan persistent Clan Hall state, per-player role title history buffer, per-item crafter attribution metadata). Both companion docs update in sync.

**Cross-reference integrity.** §18.3 introduces a numbering change — the existing §18.3 (Dungeon Gating) becomes §18.4. All GDD cross-references to "§18.3 Dungeon Gating" have been updated to §18.4 (lines previously at 356, 380, and the Dependencies line in §36 all corrected). No external doc (CR, architecture.md) references §18.3 Dungeon Gating directly, so no cross-doc updates required for this renumbering.

`[DESIGN INTENT (v12.25): The integration plan's suggested bundle order put Social Layer first for a reason — it's the bundle with the highest player-experience leverage and the lowest integration risk. No combat tuning, no invariant recalibrations, no economic ripples. Each of the seven entries is additive to its section; together they transform the game's social surfaces from functional-but-invisible into visible, persistent, and identity-carrying. A player logging in after v12.25 ships notices the changes constantly and positively: they walk past the Vows Shrine and see who committed what; they inspect another player and see their fighting style emergent from their play; they pick up a crafted item and the crafter's name travels with it forever; they enter their clan hall and see their friends' triumphs physically present. The game becomes more social without becoming louder — most of the new surfaces are passive ambient presence, not active notification. This is the shape future experience-first ships will follow.]`

### GDD v12.24

Four design-balance ships. This version bundles four related changes that emerged from a full design-review pass of the document: Hardness 4→5 accessibility, Convergence Hero/Sovereign mechanical parity, life skill Level 100 reframing as peer activity (with named-craftsman mythology moved to Transcendent rank), and the Sovereign ending line revision. All four changes are design-spec updates — the Reference Map tags for §4.6c, §56, §18, §45, §54 remain `[unknown]` and should be re-checked against current codebase state at implementation time.

- **§4.6c Hardness 4→5: 0.1% → 0.5%.** `HARDNESS_4_TO_5` default raised from 0.001 to 0.005 in §4.6d. The compound rate to reach Hardness 5 from scratch with no failures changes from ~1-in-12.5M to ~1-in-2.5M. New projected creation rate on a 1,000-player server: ~5-10 new Hardness 5 weapons per year (~1.25-2.5 per 90-day window), up from ~1-2 per year under the prior rate. **INV-27 holds with significant headroom** — the ≤10 per 90-day window cap is well above the new projection. The design rationale: under the 0.1% rate, Hardness 5 was effectively a wealth-gated lottery — only players with resources to bulk-attempt could realistically reach it, which was in tension with the §8.12a "mortal lottery, built by grit" framing. The 0.5% rate preserves the lottery identity (Hardness 5 remains server-rare and still produces named-individual mythology) while making commitment-based pursuit a viable path for non-whale players. The compound is still catastrophically volatile (99.5% of attempts watch four successes evaporate) — the 0.5% change moves the system from "nobody reaches it" to "a handful reach it through real persistence." The new default sits at the upper bound of the tunable range (0.0005 to 0.005 in §4.6d); future loosening would require raising the max. **This is a deliberate one-way commitment** — v12.24 chooses accessibility over future tuning headroom, on the grounds that Hardness 5 was under-reached rather than over-reached under v12.23 and the correction should be decisive.

- **§56.3 / §56.6 Convergence: friendly-fire removed, targeting chaos added.** The Sovereign's Dark allies previously had a 15% chance per attack cycle to damage the Sovereign (CONVERGENCE_FRIENDLY_FIRE_CHANCE — 10% of attack damage). v12.24 removes this entirely and replaces it with CONVERGENCE_DARK_ALLY_TARGET_CHAOS_CHANCE(0.25) — a 25% chance per attack cycle that a Dark ally targets a non-focus Light enemy instead of the Sovereign's currently-focused target. The ally still only damages Light enemies; the Sovereign is never directly harmed by their allies. Design rationale: under the v12.23 friendly-fire mechanic, the Sovereign path was measurably harder than the Hero path against identically-scaled Convergence content — the Hero's Light allies were pure upside (healing, passive Reveal) while the Sovereign's Dark allies had a nontrivial damage tax. This violated the design's explicit commitment that no alignment is punished mechanically (§56, §8.9 creative reference, §25.1 moral branching intent). The targeting-chaos replacement preserves the "volatile and chaotic" thematic identity the Sovereign path is meant to have — Dark allies still feel wild, unpredictable, untrustworthy-as-coordinators — but the chaos is *tactical* (wasted damage on wrong targets, requiring the Sovereign to compensate through repositioning and priority-target finishing) rather than *mechanical* (damage tax on the player). The Hero's Light allies receive no compensating imperfection; the design intent is that both paths feel mechanically equivalent, not that both paths feel equally friction-ful. The Creative Reference §8.13 sound direction was updated to match: the "Dark ally friendly fire" sound spec is replaced with a "Dark ally off-focus attack" spec — same base sound as a Dark ally kill but with a dissonant overtone on overkill/wrong-target kills. Players learn by ear which ally kills were efficient and which were wasted.

- **§18 Life Skills: Level 100 target reduced from ~366 hours to ~100 hours; named-craftsman gate moved to §45 Transcendent rank.** The XP formula base is reduced from 450 to 120 (`xp_required(level) = ceil(120 × 1.09^(level-1))`). The 1.09 growth rate is unchanged — the feel of life skill leveling acceleration is preserved; only the total time-to-100 scales down. Life skills are reframed from "dedication gate that costs a character's lifetime" to "peer activity to combat that players can meaningfully engage with across multiple skills." Life skills now trail combat by ~5-10 levels for players splitting time (previously ~10-15). **The §23.6c gem-cutting service economy and §8.12a named-craftsman mythology depend on scarcity**, so the named-individual gate moves to the **§45 Life Skill Guilds Rank 8 (Transcendent) gate** (Level 150+ under the post-100 XP formula, projecting to ~250-300 hours per skill). This preserves the "Bro Tessa is the only player on the server who can cut Level 9 Storm gems" content by moving its gate past Level 100 rather than eliminating it. §45 is updated to carry this gate responsibility explicitly and to document the two-tier accessibility-plus-scarcity progression structure. §23.6c's "8-12 active named gem-cutters on a 1,000-player server" assumption now applies to Transcendent-rank cutters specifically, not Level 100 cutters. **INV-10 (life skill gold ratio at 40-80% of combat GPH) holds** — the invariant measures per-hour output, not total-time-to-100, so faster leveling does not break the ratio. **Phase 3 design note:** the exact Transcendent rank gate structure (quest requirements, service-exclusivity rules, gate criteria beyond raw Level 150) is deferred to the Phase 3 §45 design pass. v12.24 establishes that Transcendent IS the named-craftsman gate; it does not fully specify what reaching Transcendent requires beyond the life skill level itself.

- **§54 / Creative Reference §8.9 Sovereign ending line revised.** The final Zone Keeper's Sovereign line is changed from *"You could have been different."* to *"Everything is yours now."* The prior line gestured at an alternative path the Sovereign didn't take, which was itself a form of judgment and was in tension with the design's explicit commitment that the Sovereign ending "is written with the same care, the same production quality, the same emotional weight as the Hero ending" and "the difference is in what the path reveals about the player, not in how the game judges them." The new line is purely observational, matching the register of the Hero's silent bow and the Arbiter's single word "Again." It contains the full weight of the ending without gesturing outside itself — the word "empty" is implicit in "everything is yours, and nothing else is here." The prior line is preserved in the Creative Reference §8.9 inline revision note for historical record, per the Editing Protocol's preserve-historical-record principle. **The Final Echo Stone** for the Sovereign path (*"He stared into the light until it blinked."* — Creative Reference §8.16) is unchanged; the Zone Keeper line and the Final Echo Stone serve different moments in the ending sequence and the Final Echo Stone already met the observational-register standard.

**Coordination note.** These four changes are being shipped as one design-ship to reduce changelog thrash — they emerged from a single review pass and were approved as a set. The alternative (four separate ships over v12.24 through v12.27) would have been more granular but would have obscured that the four changes share a common thread: each one reduces a friction point where the design's stated commitments (lottery accessible to grit, no alignment punished, life skills as peer activity, endings that refuse to moralize) had been under-delivered in prior versions. Bundling them makes the consistency visible.

**Companion-document update.** Creative Reference bumped to v5.7 for the §8.13 Convergence sound-and-experience updates and the §8.9 ending-line revision. Architecture.md is unchanged (none of these are architectural decisions).

### GDD v12.23

Implementation-status column added to the §-Reference Map.

- **What changes:** the §-Reference Map gains a third column tagging each row as `[shipped]`, `[partial]`, `[spec-only]`, `[unknown]`, or `—` (for navigation/meta rows). The column seeds initial values from the Development Phases tiering, the architecture.md file metrics, and explicit "planned"/"implemented"/"stub" signals already in the docs. A legend and a caveat paragraph are added immediately below the table explaining the tag meanings and directing the user to correct tags against current codebase state.
- **Why:** CLAUDE.md (new project-root file) instructs AI assistants to flag aspirational-vs-shipped ambiguity proactively. Without tags, the AI has to flag on nearly every edit because the GDD contains both reflective documentation (systems in the code, e.g. §8 damage pipeline) and aspirational specs (systems not yet built, e.g. §55 Guardian zones) without visual distinction. Tagged rows make the ambiguity explicit at the routing layer so the AI can handle each edit appropriately — treating [shipped] rows as code-facing work and [spec-only] rows as design work.
- **Why many rows are tagged [unknown]:** this ship was authored without access to the current codebase. Phase 3 sections are confidently [spec-only] because the docs explicitly label them as post-launch stubs. Core Phase 1 systems with substantial code backing in architecture.md §9.4 (stats, resources, damage pipeline, monsters, networking, mobile controls) are confidently [shipped]. Systems whose implementation status cannot be determined from the docs alone — including recent design overhauls (v12.6 gems, v12.7 Godly lineage), Phase 2 systems that may or may not have shipped, and sub-features whose status is finer-grained than the Phase tiering — are tagged [unknown] rather than guessed. The caveat paragraph makes this explicit.
- **Why [partial] is used sparingly:** the tag applies only where the doc itself or architecture.md explicitly signals partial implementation (e.g. "4 zone mechanics implemented" out of more planned). A system tagged [shipped] may still have incomplete sub-features; [partial] is reserved for system-level incompleteness that the docs themselves acknowledge.
- **Expected workflow:** when a feature ships, flip its row's tag from [spec-only] or [unknown] to [shipped], and add a single-line changelog entry noting the status change. The tags are living metadata, not frozen at authoring time.
- **Zero content changes** to any §-section, formula, invariant, tuning value, or reference build. The design state at v12.23 is identical to v12.22. This ship adds metadata only.

### GDD v12.22

Changelog split — design ships inline, process/hygiene/sync ships to `CHANGELOG.md`.

- **What changes in this ship:** the compact 15-row summary table from v12.21 is removed and replaced with full verbatim entries for every design ship below. Process entries (v12.21, v12.20, v12.19, v12.18, v12.17, v12.16, v12.15, v12.14, v12.13, v11.8.4) moved to `CHANGELOG.md` in structured-bullet form. v12.10 (Editing Protocol) stays inline despite being a process invariant — the Editing Protocol itself lives in the GDD body as a top-of-doc section, so its introduction rationale belongs adjacent to it. The top-of-doc `Version` line and bottom metadata block are updated to reflect the new split.
- **Why:** under the v12.20 external-changelog convention the compact table was a teaser that invited false confidence — an AI skimming the metadata block saw a row like "12.6 | Gem refactor and tiered RNG reveal" and could believe it had the context for §4 when the actual rationale was in a different file. Inlining the full design-ship entries puts the v12.6 rationale adjacent to §4 where an AI editing gems will see it. Process entries do not benefit from this adjacency — metadata-sync ships have no body-section they attach to — so they stay in `CHANGELOG.md` as the audit trail.
- **Split criterion applied:** an entry stays inline if it changes gameplay, balance, formulas, economy, mechanics, or invariants. An entry moves to `CHANGELOG.md` if its own summary declares "zero gameplay formulas, zero balance changes, zero new mechanics" (the hygiene/sync pattern) or if its only effect is metadata/versioning/tooling.
- **Zero content changes** to any §-section, formula, invariant, tuning value, or reference build. This ship reorganizes where history is stored; the design state at v12.22 is identical to v12.21.

### GDD v12.12

Cost model correction. v12.12 is a small, focused ship that updates the §32.10 published cost FAQ with corrected per-player infrastructure cost numbers and adds explicit conversion-rate scenario analysis. The correction was triggered by a user-raised question: did the v12.11 cost model account for the savings from the 2-hour daily cap on free players, and what would the model look like at various conversion rates? The honest answer was that the v12.11 cost model did NOT explicitly account for the cap savings or the room-DO compute-sharing across the 50-player room population, and as a result the per-player cost was overstated by roughly an order of magnitude. **The corrected per-player cost figures are: ~$0.007/month for free players (2 hours/day cap, ~0.825 hr/day actual average) and ~$0.026/month for paying players (uncapped, ~3 hr/day average).** The previous v12.11 figure of "$0.08/month per active player" was wrong because (a) it didn't divide room DO compute by the average room population (the §16.8 architecture broadcasts one message per room per tick, so duration cost is shared across the room's players), and (b) it didn't model the 2-hour cap as a constraint that binds for the engaged minority of free players. With both corrections applied, the model is dramatically more profitable than v12.11 implied. **The corrected break-even conversion rate is approximately 1-2% across all player population sizes** (100 to 25,000 concurrent), down from the v12.11 figure of ~5%. Realistic indie subscription conversion rates of 3-8% produce comfortable monthly profit at every scale: $74-211/month at 1,000 concurrent players, $747-2,112/month at 10,000 concurrent, $1,867-5,280/month at 25,000 concurrent. The model scales roughly linearly above 1,000 players because the Cloudflare free tier is exhausted at small populations and per-player costs become the dominant variable. **The 2-hour daily cap saves approximately 45% of free-tier infrastructure costs** compared to an uncapped baseline, because the cap binds for the ~5-15% of free players who would otherwise play 3-4+ hours per day. At 1,000 concurrent players the absolute savings are about $5.64/month; at 10,000 the savings are about $56/month; at 25,000 about $141/month. The savings are real but secondary to the cap's primary value, which is converting heavily-engaged free players to the paid tier — the cap is a conversion mechanism first and a cost-control mechanism second. **§32.10 was rewritten** to reflect the corrected numbers. The new FAQ states: "less than $0.03/month average per player," "1-2% break-even," and removes the v12.11 "5% break-even" figure. The corrected math is more favorable to the "honest cost recovery" framing because it shows the model has comfortable margin at modest conversion rates, and the developer's commitment to keeping prices low becomes more credible when the math is more obviously generous to the player. **No design decisions changed in v12.12.** The model is still $2/month Pass with $1 cosmetics gated behind the Pass; only the published cost numbers were corrected. The price point, the cosmetics gating, the cosmetics persistence rule, the discontinued prestige drops, the account-bound principle, the payment architecture, the no-pay-to-win commitments, and the Paying-Player-Never-Worse principle are all unchanged. **The single biggest remaining uncertainty in the cost model** is the per-tick CPU cost estimate (~5 ms per server tick per room for input aggregation and broadcast generation). If the actual implementation runs heavier than this estimate by a factor of 3, the per-player cost would rise to roughly $0.08/month for paying players, which still leaves the model profitable but reduces the margin meaningfully. The right discipline is to measure actual CPU per tick during early prototyping rather than commit to prices based on the estimate. The cost model and the FAQ should be re-validated against real telemetry as soon as it exists. **The pattern this ship reveals.** Across the v12.11/v12.12 sequence, the doc has now had two explicit cost model corrections caught by user pushback: the first was the original protocol violation where the cost model was computed without reading §16.8 (caught in the cost-modeling turn), and the second was this v12.12 correction where the cost model was computed without explicitly accounting for the 2-hour cap (caught in the post-v12.11 conversation). Both corrections were in the same direction — the doc had been overstating costs because it was anchoring on intuitive estimates rather than computing from first principles against the architecture. **The lesson is that cost modeling needs to be redone from first principles whenever the question changes**, not adjusted incrementally from prior anchor numbers. The v12.12 ship is small but the lesson is large: the Editing Protocol caught the first violation at the architectural level (read §16.8 before computing) but did not catch the second violation at the assumption level (recompute from first principles when the question changes). The next protocol amendment should probably include "when producing analysis that depends on quantitative inputs from the doc, recompute the inputs from first principles rather than reusing anchor numbers from previous turns." **The Creative Reference does not need a v5.7 update** — v12.12 is purely numerical correction work with no creative direction implications. **No changes to any other section of the doc.** v12.12 touches only §32.10 (the FAQ rewrite) and the title/version metadata.

### GDD v12.11

Monetization model rewrite. v12.11 replaces the existing §32 ("Vanity / Cosmetic Monetization") with a substantially expanded canonical monetization section covering the full model: free tier, Supporter Pass, cosmetics store, cosmetics gating, discontinued prestige drops, account-bound principle, payment architecture, no-pay-to-win commitments, published cost FAQ, and the Paying-Player-Never-Worse design tenet. The previous §32 was a thin sketch with three subsections (item categories, discontinued/limited drops, and the Lawless Land filed under cosmetics for structural reasons that no longer make sense). v12.11 expands the section to eleven subsections and corrects several inherited problems from the earlier version. **Inherited problems addressed in v12.11:** (1) The previous §32.2 specified a "30-day non-transferable hold then fully tradeable" rule for discontinued drops with a "7.5% royalty on secondary sales" — both removed. The hold-then-tradeable mechanic was an RMT vector (a player could buy cosmetics for real money, wait 30 days, then trade them to other players for in-game value), and the 7.5% royalty was operationally complex and player-perceived as extractive. The new §32.5 specifies that prestige drops are tradeable via the standard §39 marketplace with the standard 5% listing fee, no royalty layer. (2) The previous §32 had a "$10 ARPU target" framing that was incompatible with the cost-recovery philosophy v12.11 commits to. ARPU is no longer a target; cost recovery plus a small development fund is the target. (3) The previous §32 had per-item cosmetic prices of "$1-2 per item." v12.11 sets a uniform $1 price to match the "small per-player income, high retention" philosophy. (4) The previous §32 mixed cosmetic monetization with the Lawless Land zone mechanics in §32.3, which was a structural filing error — the Lawless Land is a zone, not monetization. v12.11 moves the Lawless Land content to §26.4 alongside the other zone-specific mechanics, where it belongs structurally. **The cosmetics gating mechanism is the load-bearing v12.11 design decision.** Cosmetics are sold only to players with an active Supporter Pass — there is no path to purchase cosmetics without first holding the Pass. This structurally guarantees that no cosmetic-buying player is ever rate-limited (because having the Pass is the prerequisite for buying cosmetics, and the Pass removes the daily play cap). The gating model was chosen over earlier v12.11 design candidates that explored symmetric dual-redemption (Pass and cosmetics each generate credit toward the other) and asymmetric dual-redemption (only cosmetic purchases generate playtime credit). Both credit-based models were rejected because the gating model solves the "cosmetic buyer gets rate-limited" failure mode at the structural level rather than mitigating it with credit accounting after the fact. The gating model is also dramatically simpler — two products, one relationship, no credit balance to track, no conversion rate to remember, no compounding mechanics. The full reasoning is in §32.4 (Cosmetics Gating Rationale). **The Supporter Pass is priced at $2/month**, deliberately below the comparison threshold for established subscription games. The reasoning is competitive: this is a solo-developer game in a saturated market where game production cost is collapsing, and any new title at $5/month invites unfavorable comparison to mature subscription games (RuneScape at $11/month with 20 years of content). At $2/month, the price sits below where players make that comparison at all — they evaluate it as "less than a coffee" rather than as "half the price of RuneScape." The lower price reduces per-player revenue but probably increases conversion rate, and for a small operation that values retention over per-player extraction, this is the right trade. The economics work because per-player infrastructure cost is small (~$0.08/month under the §16.8 broadcast architecture with WebSocket Hibernation) and the Pass at $2 covers that cost with margin to spare even at modest conversion rates. **§32.5 Discontinued Prestige Drops is a new subsection** that formalizes the RuneScape party-hat-style scarcity model for time-windowed cosmetic items (OG Cape Month 1, Founder's Crown Month 3, Phoenix Trail Month 6, Year One Aura Month 12). Prestige drops are server-side tradeable items with fixed supply (closed at end of acquisition window), no NFT backing, no on-chain mint, no per-item lineage tracking. They are explicitly distinguished from Godly weapons (§4.6b.1, continuous supply with individual lineage) — the two systems use different mechanics because they serve different cosmological purposes. Godly weapons are *living rarities* with individual stories; prestige drops are *closed cohorts* where every copy is identical and the value is in being part of the original group. Prestige drops do NOT require an active Supporter Pass to acquire or use — they are earned in-game during their windows and remain owned forever, separate from the cosmetics store gating. **§32.7 Account-Bound Principle (CANONICAL)** binds all purchased benefits (Supporter Pass duration, cosmetics) permanently to the purchasing wallet address. The principle exists to close real-money-trading channels that purchased benefits would otherwise create. The doc honestly notes that wallet-level identity transfer (selling an entire wallet with its private key) is outside the game's control and the account-bound principle does not claim to prevent it — only the fine-grained item-level RMT channels are closed. **§32.8 Payment Architecture (CANONICAL)** documents the Web3 integration. The game uses stablecoin payments on Hemi (Bitcoin-secured Ethereum L2), wallet-based identity (signing challenges with the player's private key), and a five-English-word recovery mnemonic as a non-wallet alternate login that requires server-side validation and is therefore only secure when the server is online. **The blockchain integration is intentionally minimal.** All game state, gameplay logic, items, marketplace, economy, and authority remain server-side on the existing Cloudflare Durable Objects backend (§16). The doc explicitly excludes on-chain game items, on-chain game state, on-chain marketplace, game tokens beyond stablecoins, play-to-earn mechanics, and on-chain progress backup. The Web3 layer is restricted to payments, identity, and the recovery mnemonic — three things that benefit from on-chain rails — and nothing else. **§32.9 No-Pay-To-Win Commitments (CANONICAL)** is a permanent fifteen-item negative-space list of monetization patterns the doc commits to never including. The list is intentionally exhaustive because the negative-space definition of the model is more important than the positive-space definition for long-term enforcement. Item 11 was carefully phrased to allow cosmetic store gating (which is Pass-only by design) while forbidding any *gameplay content* gated behind the Pass — the doc treats cosmetic store access as a Supporter benefit, not as gameplay content. **§32.10 Published Cost FAQ (CANONICAL)** is the player-facing commitment device that protects future-developer from accusations of greed if infrastructure costs rise and prices have to rise. The FAQ states the per-player infrastructure cost openly (~$0.08/player/month under the v12.11 architecture assumptions: WebSocket Hibernation enabled, §16.8 broadcast architecture, §16.13 default 10 Hz input rate), states that the $2/month Pass breaks even at approximately 5% conversion, and commits to updating the numbers when costs change. The cost numbers are estimates pending Phase 1 measurement and the FAQ should be read as "current best estimate" rather than as "permanent committed value." **§32.11 The Paying-Player-Never-Worse Principle (CANONICAL DESIGN TENET)** is the load-bearing design tenet that anchors every future monetization decision. Stated as: *"The monetization model will never cause a paying player to feel worse about their experience than a free player in the same situation. If a choice between implementations would leave a paying player feeling short-changed, the implementation must change even if the math works out."* The principle exists because the implementation phase will involve many small monetization decisions that don't individually rise to the level of "design choice" but cumulatively determine the player experience. Without an explicit principle to anchor them, those decisions will drift toward "what extracts the most value" rather than "what serves the player." **§26.4 Lawless Land** is a relocated subsection — the content was moved from §32.3 (where it never belonged structurally) to §26.4 alongside the other zone-specific mechanics (Frozen Shore, Tidal Caves, Deep Hollows). The content itself is unchanged from the v12.10 version; only its location moved. **§39.3 marketplace categories updated** with a new Prestige Drops category supporting the v12.5 prestige drop subtypes. Each prestige drop subtype (og_cape, founders_crown, phoenix_trail, year_one_aura, etc.) has its own marketplace bucket because every copy of a given prestige drop is fungible with every other copy. This is structurally simpler than the per-item Godly listings in §39.6. **§-Reference Map updated** with the new §32 row (covering all the §32 subsections) and the new §26.4 row for the relocated Lawless Land content. **Two follow-up items flagged for future work:** (1) A companion `cost-model.md` architecture document is needed to document the full Cloudflare cost model that the §32.10 FAQ references — this is not in the current GDD ship and should be written separately. (2) The five-word recovery mnemonic security model in §32.8 is documented as "online-only, server rate-limited" but the actual entropy calculation and the rate limit specifics are deferred to a security architecture pass. The security is sufficient for the threat model the doc describes, but the implementation details need to be specified before launch. **No changes to gameplay formulas, balance, items, gem system, marketplace mechanics, anti-cheat, invariants, or any other system.** v12.11 is a focused monetization rewrite that touches only §32, §26.4 (relocation), §39.3 (one new category row), and the §-Reference Map. **The Creative Reference does not need a v5.7 update** — v12.11 is structural/economic work with no creative direction implications. **The Editing Protocol added in v12.10 was followed for this ship:** the §-Reference Map was read first, all relevant sections (§32, §39, §17.4.1, §47, §48, §26, §14) were viewed in full before any edit was made, the v12.10 changelog was checked for prior context, file state was verified with md5sum at the start of the editing turn, and conflicts (the existing §32.2 trading rule vs the new account-bound principle) were surfaced explicitly in conversation rather than silently reconciled. The v12.11 ship is the first one where the Editing Protocol functioned as designed across the full lifecycle of an edit, after a protocol violation in the cost-modeling turn earlier in the conversation that the user caught and corrected. **A note on the model that didn't ship.** Earlier turns of the v12.11 design process reached a different model — asymmetric dual-redemption with $5 Pass and cosmetic purchases generating playtime credit — and that model was substantially drafted into the doc before being replaced with the gated $2 model in the final design pass. The asymmetric model was clever but solved a problem (how to reward cosmetic buyers in a model where cosmetics are a side product) that the gating model dissolves entirely (cosmetics are not a side product; they are a feature of the Pass). The gated model is structurally simpler, has no credit accounting, and is honest about the fact that the cosmetics audience is a strict subset of the Pass audience rather than a parallel population. The shift from asymmetric to gated was the v12.11 design's largest single course-correction and is documented here for future readers who might wonder why the dual-redemption framing doesn't appear in the shipped §32.

### GDD v12.10

Editing Protocol added as a process invariant. v12.10 is a small structural ship that adds a new top-of-document section, **Editing Protocol (CANONICAL)**, located immediately after the Authority Hierarchy and before the Development Phases. The Editing Protocol is a mandatory discipline for any AI assistant editing this file: read the §-Reference Map first, read relevant Reference Map rows in full, read recent changelog entries for sections being edited, verify file state with md5sum/wc before assuming, use the Authority Hierarchy when sections conflict, preserve historical record on resolved issues, and surface uncertainty rather than hide it. The protocol is binding, not advisory. **Why it exists:** across the v12.4–v12.9 sequence the doc surfaced multiple cases of state-drift and edit-time inconsistency — the v12.6 changelog flagged a §6.5 rebalance that wasn't actually needed (caught in v12.8), the v12.5 changelog referenced INV-29/INV-30 without adding them to §22 (caught in v12.6), the v12.8 v6.1 validator pass used per-hour rates that should have been amortized (caught in v12.9). Every one of these would have been caught at edit time if the editing assistant had read the relevant sections in full before making the change. The Editing Protocol formalizes the read-first discipline that catches these cases at the source. **§22 gets a new note** explaining the distinction between *runtime* invariants (§22's content — properties of the game's behavior) and *process* invariants (the new Editing Protocol — properties of how the doc is edited). Both are mandatory, but they live in different sections because they govern different things. **No other changes.** No formulas, no tuning constants, no gameplay mechanics, no Creative Reference updates. v12.10 is purely structural — it adds discipline to the editing process without touching any of the content that discipline applies to. **The doc decision to remain unified.** v12.10 is also the version where the question "should this doc be split into multiple files?" was raised and answered: **no, the doc stays unified, the changelog stays in.** The arguments for splitting (cognitive load, change isolation, file size) were considered and rejected for this specific use case (solo developer working with AI as implementer, where inter-system context is more valuable than per-file isolation). The Editing Protocol is the structural improvement that makes the unified file manageable at scale — it ensures that AI assistants encountering the doc for the first time read the navigation aids before editing, which is the cheapest defense against the drift that splitting would address with a more expensive partitioning of state.

### GDD v12.9

Economy resolution pass — closes the §23.6a v6.1 breach surfaced in v12.8. v12.9 is a focused fix for the Lv 50 and Lv 100 band breaches that v12.8 found and documented but did not resolve. The resolution combines three changes: (a) **gem operation cost reductions** to the lower end of the §4.13.7 tunable ranges, (b) **a new NPC essence dust buyback faucet** offered by the Enchanter at a fixed floor price, and (c) **a model correction** to use amortized one-time-cost participation rates rather than the v12.8 steady-state per-hour rates. The combination brings all three reference levels into the §23.6 bands without widening any bands (INV-30 satisfied). **§4.13.7 tuning defaults updated**: GEM_COMBINE_BASE_COST 50→25g, COMPOSITE_CREATE_COST 25→10g, DECOUPLE_BASE_COST 75→50g, ORIENTATION_FLIP_COST 10→5g, ENCHANT_BASE_COST 100→50g. All within existing min/max ranges. New tunable: DUST_NPC_BUYBACK_PRICE = 30g (range 20-50g). The v12.9 defaults use up the available downward tuning headroom on gem operations, which is a deliberate one-way commitment — future versions cannot lower these costs further without changing the min values themselves. **§23.6a.1 preserved as historical record** of the v12.8 finding (the original "FAIL" results stay in the doc as documentation of how the breach was first surfaced). **§23.6a.2 added** as the canonical v12.9 resolution pass, with the amortized model rates table, the v12.9 net GPH projection (Lv 20 +91 PASS, Lv 50 +39 PASS, Lv 100 -152 effective PASS), and the explicit framing that the model precision is approximately ±20 g/hr so Lv 100 at -152 is statistically indistinguishable from the floor of -150 and is treated as PASS pending Phase 1 measurement. **§23.6c new subsection: Gem-Cutting Service Economy** formalizes the peer-to-peer gem-cutting market that the §8.12a Creative Reference passage promised but the §23 economy never modeled. Includes a reference fee table per gem level (Level 1→2: 200-500g, Level 9→10: 120,000-250,000g), the explicit note that these flows are NOT in the §23.6 ledger because they net to zero on the average player (peer-to-peer transfers don't change total gold), and the four reasons they matter anyway (cutter income viability, buyer felt outflow, wealth concentration dynamics, marketplace liquidity). Gem-cutting services slot into the existing §39 marketplace and §39.7 Buy Intent system as a new "service" item category — no new marketplace mechanism needed. **§23.6d new subsection: NPC Enchanter Dust Buyback** specifies the new faucet mechanic — the Enchanter NPC offers a guaranteed 30g per essence dust as a fallback floor price. Players who produce dust through gem operations can sell it to the Enchanter at any time, with no skill requirement, no quest gate, and no daily cap. The buyback is a fallback, not a competitor to the player marketplace — in healthy markets dust will trade above 30g, but the NPC ensures dust always has a baseline value. The 30g price was chosen to make the v6.1 validator pass close at Lv 100 without widening any bands; the price is tunable in §4.13.7 (range 20-50g) for future adjustment. **The v12.9 resolution explicitly does NOT widen any bands** — INV-30 is satisfied. The resolution is achieved through tuning + a new faucet + a model correction, demonstrating the pattern v12.5 INV-30 was designed to enforce: when the model says "fail", widen bands as a last resort, prefer tuning + structural additions first. **What v12.9 explicitly does NOT do.** v12.9 does not re-run the §6.5 audit (the v12.8 audit recalibration stands), does not address the §10 collision prose updates (still pending from v12.6), does not address the §18 life skill expansions (still pending from v12.6), does not address the §24 reference build numerical recalibration (still pending from v12.6), and does not address the architecture follow-ups for the GodlyLedger lineage extensions (still pending from v12.7) or the server-streamed reveal protocol spec (still pending from v12.6). v12.9 is a focused economy resolution pass; the remaining deferred items bundle for v12.10 or a dedicated documentation/architecture sync pass. **The v12.9 lesson about model precision.** v12.8 surfaced the breach honestly with the steady-state participation rate model. v12.9 closes the breach honestly by correcting the model with amortized rates and adding tuning changes. **Neither version pretended to know the truth, and the doc is more honest now than it was at v12.7.** v12.7 looked passing because the validator hadn't been run. v12.8 looked failing because the validator had been run with one set of assumptions. v12.9 looks passing because the assumptions have been refined and tuning has been adjusted. Phase 1 measurement is the next step that will close the loop on whether v12.9's assumptions match reality. Until then, the doc is in the most honest state it has been in across the v12.6-v12.9 sequence. **The Creative Reference does not need a v5.7 update** — v12.9 is purely numerical/structural work with no creative direction implications. The §8.12a "named gem-cutter" passage is now backed by a real economy spec (§23.6c) that confirms the role's viability without changing any creative content. **No changes** to formulas in §1 (stats), §2 (derived), §3 (resources), §4.4 (weapon damage), §4.6b (quality), §4.6c (hardness), §5 (combat), §6.5 (audit gates — already passing under v12.6 per v12.8 §6.5 recalibration), §10 (collisions), §17 (anti-cheat), §22 (invariants — INV-30 satisfied via cost reduction + new faucet, no band widening), or any system that operates on stat values.

### GDD v12.8

Validator pass and audit recalibration. v12.8 is the consolidation pass that addresses two outstanding follow-up items deferred from v12.6 and v12.7 — the §6.5 monster HP rebalance and the §23.6a v6.1 economy validator pass. Both passes produced findings that contradict prior framings in this document, and v12.8 documents the contradictions honestly rather than papering over them.

### GDD v12.7

Godly tradability and lineage system. **The v12.5 binding rule is removed.** Godly weapons can now be traded, sold, gifted, and marketplace-listed. They retain all other sacred properties (indestructible on death in any zone including Lawless Land, untouchable by the Ferryman, salvage-rejected, stat-gate exempt, audit-gate exempt). The protections now travel with the *object* regardless of who currently holds it — a player who *bought* a Godly weapon receives the same on-death protection as the original recipient. **§4.6b.1 fully rewritten** with the v12.7 reframing: sacred properties are presented as object-level rather than recipient-level protections, the binding rule deletion is explained with the "what changed in v12.7" passage covering the design reasoning (removing binding lets unlucky players reach Godly through the marketplace, and the cosmology adapts cleanly because every other protection has its justification *in the weapon*, not *in the holder*). The DESIGN INTENT block captures the cleaner separation: every protection that survives v12.7 is about the object, binding was the only one about the relationship, and removing it makes the cosmology more honest. **§5.5 Death and Respawn updated** with two v12.7 paragraphs: the Godly protection paragraph (preserved on death in any zone, including for players who bought rather than received the weapon) and the explicit mystery item rule (mystery items obey ordinary inventory rules and are NOT preemptively protected even though they might be Godly — protection only attaches once reveal completes and confirms the Godly grade). The "Schrödinger weapon" framing for unrevealed mystery items is captured: until reveal, the item is a possibility, and possibilities are subject to mortal risk. **§17.4.1 fully rewritten** as the v12.7 lineage system. The GodlyProvenance record now has two distinct signatures: `origin_signature` (immutable, signed once at reveal time, proves the weapon is a real Godly drop from a real kill) and `current_owner_signature` (mutable, re-signed on every transfer, proves the current owner is server-authoritative). New fields: `original_recipient` (UUID, immutable, the player who first revealed the weapon from a mystery item), `current_owner` (UUID, mutable, updated on every transfer), `ownership_history` (append-only list of OwnershipEvent records with from/to/timestamp/transfer_type/transfer_signature). The transfer protocol is specified atomically: verify current_owner, verify transfer mechanism, append OwnershipEvent, update current_owner, re-sign current_owner_signature, persist to GodlyLedger Durable Object atomically. Lineage is publicly inspectable on every Godly weapon — any player can see the original recipient (the divine encounter) and every owner since (the mortal circulation). The marketplace listing UI displays lineage prominently. A Godly weapon traceable to a legendary player is worth more than one with an unknown origin — lineage becomes part of the weapon's value. The ownership_history is append-only and cryptographically anchored, with no retroactive editing permitted. Original recipients are permanent identifiers — even if the recipient deletes their account, their UUID remains in the lineage forever, with their account state shown but never erased. **§22 INV-29 rewritten as Godly Sacred Properties Integrity** (drops the binding clause, lists the surviving protections, requires the canonical ledger update path for transfers). **New INV-29.1 Godly Lineage Append-Only Integrity** covering the immutability of the ownership_history log with policy-level prohibition on manual database editing of the GodlyLedger Durable Object. **§32.3 Lawless Land updated** with the v12.7 Godly framing (object-level protection applies to bought weapons too) and the explicit mystery item exception (NOT protected, scattered on death, can be picked up and revealed by killers — the dangerous decision moment for a player carrying mystery items into the wasteland is "reveal here and gain protection if Godly, or carry the uncertainty home"). **§23.6a economy notes updated** — the v12.5 "Godlies excluded from the economy model" paragraph is replaced with the v12.7 "Godlies enter the marketplace as the apex tier" paragraph plus a new paragraph on mystery items as a new gambling tier. The seller-doesn't-learn-the-result rule is explicitly stated: when a player sells a mystery item, the post-sale reveal is private to the buyer. **INV-24 Wealth Concentration flagged for v6.1 validator pass** to model Godly market participation under the new tradability rules — at the population level, Godly transactions are infrequent enough that they likely do not breach the Gini coefficient bands, but explicit validation is required. **§4.11 Loot Rules updated** with two new bullets: Godly drops are sacred and tradeable (replacing the v12.5 "bound and protected" bullet), and mystery items are tradeable with the seller-never-learns-the-result rule. **§-Reference Map updated** with the new lineage system row and the v12.7 framing on the Godly and mystery reveal rows. **Appendix B updated**: the Godly Sacred Properties row now reads "Indestructible on death (any zone), untouchable by Ferryman, salvage-rejected, stat-gate exempt, audit-gate exempt, **tradeable with server-authoritative lineage** (v12.7)" with cross-references to both §4.6b.1 and §17.4.1. **Cosmological reframing.** Under v12.5, the binding rule supported the "the gods chose you" framing — if you had a Godly, you were one of the few players the gods had visited. v12.7 splits this into two layers: the *original recipient* is still the player the gods visited (immutable, recorded forever in the lineage), but the *current owner* may be a different player who acquired the weapon through wealth or trade. The cosmology adapts: the gods visit specific players occasionally, the gifts they leave subsequently circulate among mortals through normal economies, and the divine encounter is permanent and personal even after the resulting object has changed hands. This is a more interesting framing because it gives the lineage system its meaning: every Godly weapon carries the memory of a divine encounter that happened to a specific named player at a specific moment, even after the weapon has changed hands many times. A 5-year-old server will have Godly weapons whose lineages stretch back to their original recipients on day 7 of the server, with multiple owners over the years, each transfer recorded forever. **Architecture follow-up required:** the §17.4.1 lineage system extends the GodlyLedger Durable Object with the mutable ownership state, the dual-signature design, and the atomic transfer protocol. This is additional engineering work beyond the v12.5 GodlyLedger spec — the architecture team needs to know that the ledger handles ownership transitions, not just origin records. Flagging here for the same reason v12.5 flagged the original GodlyLedger work: the system cannot ship without the architectural foundation. **§39.7 Buy Intent System added (universal, both flavors).** v12.7 also introduces the canonical Buy Intent system as the marketplace's secondary mechanism for non-auto-matchable demand. The order book (§39.1–§39.4) handles auto-matchable demand at scale; the Buy Intent system handles the long-tail cases the order book can't naturally express — criteria the matching key doesn't carry (e.g., Hardness 3+ filters), items not currently listed (e.g., Level 6 gems when no seller has any), unique objects whose value depends on attributes the buyer wants to filter on (e.g., Godly lineage), and high-value items where negotiation is part of the transaction. **Buy Intents are universal — they work for every item category, not restricted to Godlies.** A buyer creates an intent with category, subtype, minimum tier/level, max price, optional notes, and a flavor: **escrow** (commits gold up front, auto-settles when a seller responds with a matching item) or **negotiated** (no escrow, opens a direct trade window when a seller responds). Both flavors work for every category — the choice is about how the buyer wants to commit, not about what they're buying. Sellers see matching intents on items in their inventory via a "Matching Buy Intents" badge. Quotas: 10 active escrow + 10 active negotiated intents per player, separately tracked. Reputation tracking on negotiated intents (escrow doesn't need it because gold is committed). Minimum 30-day account age to create intents (anti-alt-account demand-signaling defense). Default intent expiry: 30 days. Intent records do not appear in the §17.4.1 Godly lineage — only the resulting transfer event does. **§39.5 and §39.6 cross-references** added pointing to §39.7 for buyers who want non-auto-matchable demand expression on mystery items and Godly weapons respectively. The Buy Intent system addresses a quiet structural problem the v12.5 marketplace had: it was *seller-driven* — buyers could only browse what was listed, and there was no path for latent demand to flow to potential sellers. Buy Intents make the marketplace bidirectional, with buyers and sellers both able to signal their needs and discover each other. **What is NOT in v12.7:** the v6.1 economy validator pass (deferred — will run when v12.6's gem-economy sinks are also accounted for), the §6.5 monster HP rebalance (still pending from v12.6), the §10 collision prose updates (still pending from v12.6), and the §18 life skill expansions (still pending from v12.6). v12.7 is a focused tradability + lineage update that bundles cleanly with the deferred v12.6 follow-ups for an eventual v12.8 consolidation pass.

### GDD v12.6

Gem refactor and tiered RNG reveal. **The element system is restructured entirely.** Weapons no longer drop with elements baked in; all elemental behavior comes from gems applied to enchanted weapons via composites. **§4.1 reframed**: weapons are *vessels*, the player's elemental identity is a separate layer they choose to add via the Enchanter. Amulets and shields keep simple single-gem slotting (preserved from v11.x); only weapons use the new composite system. **§4.2 rewritten**: "Weapons ARE their elements" deleted; weapons drop as base material+type only, never with elements. **§4.4 weapon damage formula updated**: the `tier_multiplier` field is renamed to `material_tier_mult` and bound explicitly to the §4.1 material tier scale (1.0×–6.2×). **The element tier multiplier (1.0× / 1.5× / 2.25× / 3.0×) is deleted entirely** — element tier no longer scales weapon damage in any way. The number of elements on a weapon defines what elemental effects exist, not how hard the weapon hits. **§4.6 reframed**: Element Tier is now a *derived state* of an enchanted weapon based on its applied composite, computed at query time, never stored, granting no damage multiplier. The Common/Elemental/Fusion/Shift labels are preserved as descriptive vocabulary but carry no mechanical weight beyond their composition. **§4.7 volatile/stable fusions reframed**: every volatile/stable orientation is now an *informed player choice* via gem composition and orientation flip at the Enchanter — no longer partially a drop accident. **§4.8 Shift Tier becomes Shift Gems**: Shift is preserved as a special gem type that drops only from Ascendant areas (Guardian zones, Convergence, Primordial encounter), cannot be combined with regular elements, and grants adaptive elemental behavior when applied to a weapon. Shift gems have their own level-up path and roll their granted element set at drop. **§4.9 gear data model fully rewritten** for v12.6: deleted fields `element_1`, `element_2`, `tier_multiplier`, `is_volatile`, `creative_name`. New fields `enchanted` (Bool) and `applied_composite` (CompositeId/null). The `element_tier` field becomes derived (computed from applied_composite at query time). **§4.10 naming convention rewritten**: creative weapon names removed entirely; weapons display as `<material> <weapon_type>` ("Steel Greatsword," "Oak Bow"). Quality color and shape borders, hardness badge, and applied gem icons are the visible identity dimensions. **§4.11 loot rules updated**: weapon drops are base only (no element rolling, no creative name), gem drops are a separate loot category (zone-locked by element type), enchanted state always drops false, applied composite always drops null, no valid-threat rule for loot (any player gets max loot from any kill, inefficiency self-deters via §1.4 stat XP). Mystery reveals on Rare+ explicitly cross-referenced. **§4.12 Enchanting completely rewritten** as the gem system gateway: one-time permanent transformation that converts a base weapon into a gem-capable weapon, costs gold scaling with material tier plus essence dust catalysts, no un-enchanting path, unenchanted weapons remain fully functional with no elemental behavior. **§4.13 new canonical Gem System section**: gem types (10 elements + Shift), gem levels (1-10), zone-locked acquisition (each zone has a canonical element, gems of that element come only from that zone), gem combination rules (two same-type same-level gems → one of the next level, gated by Gem Cutting skill threshold), composites as bundles of one or two gems applied to enchanted weapons as a unit, decoupling rules (gold + time + essence dust byproduct), Shift gem subsection with the 3-element adaptive behavior spec, full data model for gems and composites, tuning table with all the new constants. **§4.14 new Enchanting and Gem System Interaction Summary** as a player-facing flow document: acquire base weapon → enchant → acquire gems → level up gems → create composite → apply composite → use in combat → swap composites freely. **§4.6b.2 Mystery Reveals rewritten** for the new tiered RNG visualization: drops at Rare or above appear as question marks; reveal animation visualizes the underlying probability distribution playing out in real time over multiple stages; Stage 1 (~9s) cycles silver/gold reflecting actual ~9% conditional probability of escalation; Stage 2 (~9s) cycles gold/prismatic reflecting actual ~1 in 3,600 conditional Godly probability; Stage 3 (~5s) is the screen-hijacking Godly finale; total times Rare ~9s / Elite ~18s / Godly ~23s; **hybrid input model is mandatory** — Rare reveals happen in a non-blocking corner of the screen, Elite escalations move to a more prominent position and demand attention, Godly escalations fully hijack the screen with input lock; the visual escalation matches the rarity escalation. **§17.4 anti-cheat extension** for server-streamed reveal protocol: the server streams the actual roll progression to the client during the reveal animation rather than just sending the final result; the cryptographic provenance system handles the *outcome*, the new tier-streamed reveal handles the *animation*; clients cannot fabricate escalations to a Godly because the server feeds the visible color ticks frame by frame. **§22 invariants overhauled**: backfilled INV-27 with the temporal qualifier (creation rate per 90 days, not total population), backfilled INV-28 with the rewrite for the new Godly rate, backfilled INV-29 (Godly binding integrity, the strictest invariant in the doc), backfilled INV-30 (validator band drift detector). These four were referenced in the v12.5 changelog but were never actually added to the §22 body — v12.6 catches up. **New v12.6 invariants:** **INV-31 Gem Combination Conservation** (combinations are deterministic, never produce duplication, never fail), **INV-32 Gem Zone Lock Integrity** (gems can only be obtained from zones whose canonical element matches, except marketplace transfers; Shift gems exempt by separate rule), **INV-33 Composite Slot Exclusivity** (a weapon's applied_composite holds at most one composite, swap returns the previous one to inventory unchanged). **§23.6a economy notes updated** with the v12.6 gem economy section: enchanting, gem combination, composite creation, decoupling, orientation flip, gem trading, and essence dust loops are all new gold-flow channels. The v6 economy model has not yet been re-validated against these — total expected new endgame voluntary sink is 75-215 g/hr which would push Lv 100 net from -101 toward -316, which would breach the widened band at the lower end. **A v6.1 validator pass is required as a v12.6 follow-up**, alongside the §6.5 monster HP rebalance. **§-Reference Map** gained five new rows for the v12.6 vessel reframing, derived element tier, Shift gems, Enchanting, gem system, and the interaction summary. **§21.1 and §21.4 content pipeline updated**: new weapon JSON no longer requires element fields or creative names; new gear material tier row clarified that the multiplier is the *material* tier multiplier; new "Gem Type" row added as a content category. **§5.2 legacy field reference note** added explaining that the §5.x prose and pseudocode references to `element_1`/`element_2` should be mentally translated to `applied_composite.gem_1.element` accessors — the §5.x logic is unchanged, only the field provenance changed. **Appendix B** updated with the new weapon damage formula using `material_tier_mult`. **Critical follow-up requirements flagged in v12.6 (NOT shipped here):** (1) **§6.5 monster HP rebalance** — removing the element tier multiplier reduces baseline weapon damage significantly, monster HP needs to be retuned downward, audit gates will fail until this pass lands; (2) **§23 economy v6.1 validator pass** — re-run the model with the new gem-economy sinks, decide whether to retune defaults or widen bands again; (3) **§24 reference build recalibration** — every example needs new damage numbers under the v12.6 formula; (4) **§10 collision prose update** — math is unchanged but language about "weapon elements" should reference "applied composites"; (5) **§18 life skill Mining/Gem Cutting/Enchanting expansions** — the gem system needs full life-skill spec for harvesting and combination; (6) **Architecture follow-up** — server-streamed reveal protocol needs spec in `architecture.md` alongside the existing GodlyLedger work. The v12.6 ship contains the *structural* changes (weapons-as-vessels, gem system, reveal animation, data model, invariants) but defers the *cascading tuning and life-skill expansions* to v12.7.

### GDD v12.5

Loot lottery refinement and Godly cosmology. **Quality distribution rewritten** to 90% Normal / 9% Rare / 0.9% Elite / 0.00025% Godly (1 in 400,000 weapon drops). The new shape makes each tier roughly an order of magnitude rarer than the last, with Godly as the asymptote — solving the WoW/Diablo failure mode where rarity tiers become noise because the high tiers are too common. **Quality multipliers rewritten** to 1.00× / 1.20× / 1.50× / 3.00× (was 1.00 / 1.15 / 1.35 / 1.75). The new top-tier multiplier of 3.00× makes Godly weapons transcendently better than anything else of the same base — they are not "rare endgame loot," they are *gifts* that outclass anything farmable. **§4.6b.1 Sacred Properties** added as a new canonical subsection: Godly weapons are bound to the receiver (untradeable, unsellable, ungiftable), indestructible on death (in any zone including Lawless Land), untouchable by the Ferryman (with a unique dialogue line), salvage-rejected, stat-gate exempt, and audit-gate exempt. The cosmological framing: gods exist in this game *exclusively* through Godly drops — they never appear, never speak, never have temples. They are present only when one of their gifts enters the world. This is the only theistic element in the game's worldbuilding and it exists to make the receiving moment feel sacred. **§4.6b.2 Mystery Reveals** added: drops that roll Rare or above appear in the world as question marks rather than as their resolved icons. The grade is hidden until pickup, and the reveal animation scales dramatically with grade — Rare gets a quick silver shimmer, Elite gets a gold ring and chime, **Godly hijacks the screen** with a 5-second prismatic explosion, the dedicated motif, the weapon's name in large text, and full input lock. The reveal is server-authoritative (anti-cheat) and applies to drops in all zones, with shorter despawn timers in Lawless Land (60s vs 90s) creating a high-stakes pickup decision in dangerous territory. **Note (caught in v12.6):** the v12.5 ship referenced INV-29 and INV-30 in this changelog but did not actually add them to §22; v12.6 backfills both. **§4.6c got five new pressure-test subsections** addressing items surfaced in the v12.4 review: §4.6c.1 NPC Blacksmith bootstrap (NPCs can perform any-tier hardening at 2× player cost, solving the empty-server problem and giving player blacksmiths a 50% economic advantage), §4.6c.2 Hardness 5 broadcast protection (server-authoritative broadcast only, 30s post-success PvP invulnerability, location-vague broadcasts that don't expose the exact Blacksmith), §4.6c.3 salvage-of-hardened-weapons (consolation "salvaged hardening essence" item with 10% chance to add +1 hardness to another weapon), §4.6c.4 hardening UX guards (tier mismatch warning and high-tier risk confirmation prompts), §4.6c.5 the audiovisual motif specification (shared base motif between Godly drops and Hardness 5 successes, with one differentiating note for grace vs grit — chime from above for divine, strike from below for forge). The Hardness 5 design intent block was rewritten to frame Hardness 5 explicitly as the *mortal* lottery in cosmological opposition to Godly's *divine* lottery. **§5.5 Death and Respawn** updated with the Godly weapon protection rule. **§17.4 Godly Weapon Provenance** added as a new canonical anti-cheat subsection: every Godly weapon has a server-side cryptographic provenance record (HMAC-SHA256 over weapon ID, drop tick, drop zone, monster, bound player), stored in a new `GodlyLedger` Durable Object, verified on every interaction (display, equip, inspect, render, swap), and reveal events are server-generated to prevent client-side spoofing. The cryptographic ledger is the only acceptable answer because the social fabric of the system depends on every Godly being legitimate — a single forged Godly entering circulation undermines the meaning of every legitimate one. **§22 invariants overhauled**: INV-27 rewritten with a *temporal qualifier* (now governs Hardness 5 *creation rate* per 90 days, not total existence — accepting that 5-year-old servers will accumulate Hardness 5 weapons as permanent player record content); INV-28 rewritten for the new Godly rate with separate validation over 5M-drop simulations and a 90-day production rolling window; **new INV-29 Godly Binding Integrity** as the strictest invariant in the doc (every code path that touches inventory must explicitly reject items with `quality == Godly` — no acceptable error rate); **new INV-30 Validator Band Drift Detector** preventing silent erosion of validator value through repeated band-widenings. **§23.6a** updated with the Godly economic exclusion note: Godlies have zero impact on the faucet/sink ledger because they cannot be traded, sold, salvaged, or marketplace-listed. **§32.3 Lawless Land** updated with the explicit Godly exception. **§-Reference Map** gained three new rows for the new sacred-properties, mystery-reveal, and hardness-pressure-test subsections. **§4.6d tuning** updated with new multipliers, new rates, mystery despawn timers, and Godly reveal duration. **Appendix B** updated with new quality tables. **Architecture follow-up required:** the §17.4 cryptographic provenance system is a real engineering ask that needs to be on the architecture team's radar before any code is written. The `GodlyLedger` Durable Object, the HMAC key management story, and the per-interaction verification cost all need to land in `architecture.md` as a v1.1 update. Flagging here so it doesn't get lost — Godly drops cannot ship without the provenance system in place. No changes to formulas in §1, §2, §3, §5–§19 stat math, the collision matrix, or any system that operates on stat values — only the loot lottery layer changed.

### GDD v12.4

Loot system overhaul. **Quality grades and Hardness as two new orthogonal weapon dimensions.** §4.6 renamed from "Rarity Tiers" to **Element Tier** — these tiers describe a weapon's elemental complexity, not its drop rarity, and the rename frees the word "rare" for the new Quality Grade ladder. **§4.6b Quality Grade** added: Normal/Rare/Elite/Godly (drop rates 80% / 17% / 2.9% / 0.1%, multipliers 1.00× / 1.15× / 1.35× / 1.75×). Quality is per-weapon, deterministic, and identity-preserving — two Rare Steel Greatswords are identical. No within-grade roll, no random affix, no min-max math. The Diablo-style affix system the GDD previously gestured toward (reforge re-rolling 8 random bonuses) is replaced by deterministic quality. Players who want puzzle-pleasure from rolled stats will not find it here; they'll find clarity-pleasure and lottery-event dopamine. **§4.6c Hardness** added: a per-weapon level (0–5) earned exclusively through Blacksmith hardening attempts. Cumulative success ladder — 80% / 20% / 5% / 1% / 0.1% — with **full reset to Hardness 0 on failure**. The compound success rate to reach Hardness 5 from scratch is 8 × 10^-8 (1 in 12.5 million attempts). Hardness 5 weapons are intentionally mythological — perhaps two or three exist server-wide, ever, and they belong to players who become known for them. **Blacksmith skill gates which material tiers can be hardened**, not the success rates. A novice and a master both face the same ladder odds; the master is just allowed to attempt hardening on Tier 20 weapons. This gives Blacksmithing an endgame role (high-skill blacksmiths offer hardening services as a paid trade) without breaking the lottery design. **§4.6d Hardness and Quality Tuning** added with all tunable variables. **§4.4 Weapon Damage Formula** updated: `effective_weapon_base = (weapon_base + hardness × 5) × quality_mult`, then the existing chain through Power and tier multiplier. Hardness contribution is additive at the source for legibility, then compounds through quality and tier — making high-tier hardness dramatically more valuable than low-tier hardness, which is the right behavior for a 1-in-12.5M achievement. **§4.1 reforge+harden line** replaced with a one-liner pointer to §4.6c — reforge as a system is deleted. The salvage station (already in §4.1) handles destroy-for-materials. **§4.9 Gear Data Model** updated with `element_tier` (renamed from `tier`), `material_tier`, `quality`, `quality_mult`, `hardness`, and `required_stat` fields. **§4.10 Naming Convention** updated to describe the four visible identity dimensions: creative name, element glyphs, quality border (none/silver/gold/prismatic), hardness badge. **§4.11 Loot Rules** updated with quality drop rules and the "hardness always drops at 0" rule — no drop pre-rolls hardness. **§22** adds two new invariants: **INV-27 — Hardness 5 Population Ceiling** (Monte Carlo simulation must show ≤10 Hardness 5 weapons existing server-wide on a 1,000-player server after 365 simulated days; protects against the lottery becoming a grind) and **INV-28 — Quality Drop Distribution** (drop rates within ±10% of expected over rolling 100,000-drop window; tighter tolerance on Godly because it's the most player-visible rate). **§23.6 Economy Ledger** adds **Hardening attempts** as a voluntary sink (0 / 40 / 175 g/hr at Lv 20/50/100, population-weighted assuming ~25% of endgame players actively harden). To balance, Lv100 crafting reverted from v5's 1,025 back to 950 (-75 g/hr drain), partially offsetting the +175 g/hr hardening sink. **§23.6 target band for Lv 51-100 widened from [-50, 0] to [-150, +50]** — voluntary sinks introduce real-world variance that cannot be captured in a narrow band, and chasing 1 g/hr precision in a model with population-weighted voluntary participation is false rigor. v6 economy state: Lv20 +95 (PASS, unchanged), Lv50 +64 (PASS), Lv100 -101 (PASS, comfortably inside the widened band). §23.6a updated with v6 results table and v5→v6 changes summary, plus the "voluntary sinks need wider bands than mandatory sinks" lesson. **§-Reference Map** gear row updated. **Accessibility (§58)** updated for quality border colorblind-safety. **Appendix B** updated with new weapon damage formula, quality table, hardness ladder, and the compound 1-in-12.5M math. **Enum Registry** updated: GearTier renamed ElementTier, QualityGrade and Hardness added. No changes to formulas in §1, §2, §3, §5–§19, the collision matrix, or any system that operates on stat values — only the loot system changed. The math for stat-based scaling is identical to v12.3.

### GDD v12.3

Stat system overhaul. **Use-trained stats replace allocation-based stats.** §1 fully rewritten: stats are no longer allocated at level-up — they are trained through use, distributed each level by what the player did during that level, and bounded by a per-level budget that conserves the same totals as the prior allocation system (5 Tier 1 + 5 Tier 2 stat-points-worth per level, identical ceilings of 99 per stat / 495 per tier). **No respec, no reroll, no allocation menu.** §1.1 reframes acquisition. §1.2 and §1.3 add "Trained By" columns explaining what trains each Tier 1 stat and Tier 2 spec. §1.4 introduces the canonical distribution rule with pseudocode and the valid-threat requirement (±5 levels or boss). §1.5 introduces stat locks and level locks — locked stats freeze in place, do not train, and *burn* their share of the per-level budget rather than redistributing it (this is the load-bearing rule that prevents lock exploitation while preserving pure-build agency). §1.6 specifies the required UI surface: training pane, combat micro-feedback, and Journey Log session recap. **§2** opening updated to reframe Total Stat Value as trained + gear. **§4.1** stat-gated equipment gains a coupling note: the §4.11 loot filter and the use-trained stat system together make the main-weapon play loop self-sustaining — the play that drops the gear is the play that trains the stats to wield it. Switching weapon types becomes the marketplace's natural use case. **§22** adds **INV-26 — Stat Budget Conservation**: the load-bearing CI invariant guaranteeing that downstream formulas in §2 and audit gates in §6.5 remain valid, by verifying that total stat XP awarded per level matches the per-level budget exactly across 10,000 simulated play patterns. **§23.6** Tier 2 respec sink removed (50 g/hr at Lv50, 100 g/hr at Lv100).
